Chapter1:Afterlife Chapter Notes note 7/26/2025: hello. apparently people are selling copies of this fic on TikTok and Etsy. my god, i despise these people. if you are somebody who scrapes fanfiction off the internet and sells it, i hope you know that you're a parasitic growth on the entity that is fanspace and i feel deep contempt toward you. may you make no money and wake up every morning feeling ineffably wrong. THAT SAID. these platforms do not respond to my reports because i am not the IP holder, and i'm not in fandom anymore. soîif you've sent me an alert about this, thank you, but please don't send me more links. i can't stop it and at this point it just stresses me out. thank you for caring about keeping fanspace free. xx Hi and welcome! A few notes, so we all know what we're getting into here! 1. This is a DH rewrite focusing on a Draco redemption arc, with an eventual Draco/Hermione pairing. It's plot-heavy, so the first several chapters (and occasional chapters throughout) will read more like a gen fic than pure romance. 2. The rating is a VERY light M. A Caution M, if you will. If you are looking for sexy fics, this is not the one! Levels of swearing and violence will be canon-comparable. 3. I'd class the fic as a rewrite, but it's not a fix-it fic. It's meant to feel in line with the spirit of canon, not act to correct it or bash any particular elements. ("Remix" would also be a good descriptor.) Basically, my hope was that this could be read immediately after HBP as a "what-if?" alternate book 7 and blend in stylistically. OK, that's it for notes! I hope you enjoy it, and thank you very much for reading :) <3 If you would like to listen to Disappearances on audio, ETL Echo has a podfic here on AO3 and on Spotify! Translation list: Brazilian Portuguese: complete here on AO3 and in progress on Wattpad. Thai: in progress on Readawrite. Ukrainian: in progress on AO3. Vietnamese: complete on Wattpad. See the end of the chapter for more notes ØI can help you, Draco,Æ said Dumbledore. ØNo, you canít,Æ said Malfoy, his wand hand shaking very badly indeed. ØNobody can. He told me to do it or heíll kill me. Iíve got no choice.Æ ØHe cannot kill you if you are already dead. Come over to the right side, Draco, and we can hide you more completely than you can possibly imagine. What is more, I can send members of the Order to your mother tonight to hide her likewise. Nobody would be surprised that you had died in your attempt to kill meîforgive me, but Lord Voldemort probably expects it. Nor would the Death Eaters be surprised that we had captured and killed your motherîit is what they would do themselves, after all. Your father is safe at the moment in Azkaban. è When the time comes we can protect him too. Come over to the right side, Draco è you are not a killer èÆ Malfoy stared at Dumbledore. ØBut I got this far, didnít I?Æ he said slowly. ØThey thought Iíd die in the attempt, but Iím here è and youíre in my power è Iím the one with the wand è youíre at my mercy èÆ ØNo, Draco,Æ said Dumbledore quietly. ØIt is my mercy, and not yours, that matters now.Æ Malfoy did not speak. His mouth was open, his wand hand still trembling è With every passing second, the wand in Dracoís hand seemed to grow heavier. Do it, hissed Bellaís voice in his mind. Kill him, Draco è the filthy Muggle-lover è look at his ruined hand, look at how he stands, how he breathes. He is as good as dead already! Kill him now! Draco had been hearing Bellatrixís voice all year. In the days after his assignment, her fanatical energy had felt like a gift. She knew as well as he did that the Dark Lord had given him this mission to punish his fatherîand yet, sheíd said, think, think of what you might achieve, Draco! It is a chance that any faithful servant of the Dark Lord would die for, to serve him beyond all others! Draco had repeated the idea to himself so many times that it had become a liturgy. This wasnít a death sentence at all. It was an invitation to the Dark Lordís right hand, and if he could only kill Dumbledore, he would cross the finish line, ensure his familyís status forever, and win power and glory beyond imagining. Kill Dumbledore, and end the dark year at last. But now, as the night wind stung his eyes, as he stood shivering upon the cusp of victory, Draco allowed himself to imagine it fully. He saw himself sitting beside the Dark Lord as his most honored deputy. And he saw the truth, glowing steadily and ominously like a faint red light behind everything else. He thought he might have known it for months already. This was not a finish line. It was the starting gate. Kill once, and he would need to kill again and again to survive. And even then, even if he gave the Dark Lord decades of loyal, absolute service, he wouldn't be safe. He could be brutally punished at any time for a single error, as his father had been. He thought wildly of his parents, then of Crabbe and Goyle, Pansy and Blaise. They would suffer for his failures the way heíd suffered for his fatherís. His life would be the dark year drawn out forever into the future, a lifetime spent beneath a knife that hung by a thread. Draco clutched harder to the wand, telling himself to actîto say the incantationîto make the choiceîbut the world seemed to be dissolving around him. Everything was coming apart into incomprehensible patches of texture and sensation. There was this: the pale green light that shimmered down from the Dark Mark overhead, undulating over stone and flesh and rampart, like standing in an underwater place. And this: the tacky stick and reek of cloth in the damp pit of his right arm, where his robes had bunched; he hadnít showered in three days, sleepless with preparation. And this: the hiss and whip of the wind at the top of the world. This. The depth of the lines in the old manís face. Draco was standing close enough to see where the silver hair joined to the ancient skin, like a thousand silk threads coming out of old, soft fabric. Bellaís voice faded, replaced by an echo of Dumbledoreís gentle words. It is my mercy, not yours, that matters now. The old man was right. There was no mercy anywhere else. His hand trembling more violently than ever, Draco lowered the wand. A resounding bang came from the stairwell behind him, followed by the distant crash of stone hitting stone. The voices below that had been growing louder were suddenly shut away, leaving the ramparts silent, as if they were miles away from the rest of the castle. Draco didnít even react. He was swaying, lightheaded. ØWe must move at once,Æ said Dumbledore. Seeming to draw strength from a place it caused him great pain to access, he grasped the ramparts and pulled himself slowly, excruciatingly upright. ØTime is very short. Your wand, Draco è as quickly as you can, please.Æ Draco handed the wand to Dumbledore, but his eyes were fixed on the flagstones. He couldnít watch his own body act, couldnít fully understand it even as he did it. Dumbledore aimed the wand over the ramparts and whispered, ØAccio!Æ A momentís silence. Then Dumbledoreís own wand flew up out of the darkness, cut through the night winds with a thwip, and landed in his waiting hand. Dumbledore aimed both wands at the door to the stairwellîDraco flinched backward at the motionîand the door flew shut, sealing away new sounds of shifting rock. Draco heard the lock snap into place. Dumbledore returned Dracoís wand and indicated the brooms that still leaned against the ramparts. ØTake the faster of the two,Æ he rasped. ØFly to Hagridís hut and wait there. We will speak soon enough.Æ Draco was jarred back to his senses. Hagridís hut? Was that supposed to be the security Dumbledore had promised? ØButîIîyou saidîÆ ØYou will know the full plan soon, Draco,Æ Dumbledore insisted, his brilliantly blue eyes meeting Dracoís over the rims of his half-moon spectacles. ØFor now, there is no time. I must ask you to fly to Hagrid è to trust, if not my judgment, my rather prodigious skill.Æ Dumbledore managed a feeble smile. Draco hesitated. Dumbledore was visibly weakened, and the Death Eaters were on their way, and Snape had made the Vow to his mother. If someone else finished Dumbledore è if no one knew Dumbledore had promised to protect him è Then a muffled shout issued from the stairwell. Draco flinched, swallowed, and jerked his head in a reluctant nod. ØGood,Æ Dumbledore said. ØThe final touch, then èÆ He rapped Draco on the head with his wand. Something cold trickled down Draco's back as the Disillusionment charm erased him. ØGo, now,Æ Dumbledore whispered. As Draco seized one broom, Dumbledore summoned the other. Draco glanced back as he mounted and couldnít help but pause at the sight. Under Dumbledoreís wand, the second broomís handle was swelling like an arthritic finger, bulging outward first at random, then to mimic what were unmistakably hips, ribs, and shoulders. The wood softened, seeming to melt until it looked like pale skin, and the handle split into two legs, draped with the soft black cloth of a fallen robe. The bristles shortened and shone until they had become white-blond hair, framing a sphere of wood that elongated into a human faceîDracoís own face. Within seconds, another Draco Malfoy lay before them, motionless, quite convincingly dead. Dumbledore pulled up its left sleeve to reveal the skull and the snake intertwined there. Draco stared into the face that had stared back at him in the mirror all year, the body he had wished he could escape. Even he couldnít discern a difference between his own self and the thing he was about to leave behind, the corpse with the mark written upon its pale forearm. Dumbledore looked up. ØGo,Æ he said. Draco kicked off, hard, into darkness and wind. He didnít know how much time had passed. The shock of what heíd done was still beating slowly through him, distorting his perspective. It might have been ten minutes or an hour since heíd lowered the wand. He sat in silence at Hagridís rough wooden table and watched the fire crackle. He refused to look at Hagrid, who seemed to loom in the corner of his eye no matter which direction Draco turned. To say the gamekeeper had been surprised to see him was an understatement. Thankfully, the oaf hadnít asked for information. Heíd just grunted, his obvious dislike mingled with suspicion, and yanked out a chair for Draco to take. Vaguely, it occurred to Draco how ridiculous it was that someone of Hagridís size lived in a place like this. It was the kind of thought he might have shaped into a joke last year to make Crabbe and Goyle laugh, before things like making his friends laugh had become unimportant. He tried not to think where Crabbe and Goyle might be now. An unwanted memory resurfaced: the moment that Fenrir Greyback had muscled his way out of the Vanishing Cabinet into the Room of Requirement. Draco hadnít expected him, hadnít wanted him. It was only supposed to be Yaxley, Gibbon, Rowle, and the Carrows, all dangerous, but all at least reliable. The sudden appearance of Greybackîthe way he towered over them, the size and the rancid smell of him, everything about him an invasionîhad made Dracoís mouth go dry. Crabbe, miraculously, had stood his ground as Greyback bore down on them, but Draco and Goyle had shied back a few steps, which had made the werewolf roar with laughter. Draco had collected himself almost at once, his cheeks hot. ØI didnít ask you here, werewolf,Æ heíd said through gritted teeth. ØGo back. Are you listening to me? Go back in!Æ But Greyback and the others were already stalking toward the exit, speaking in low, excited voices, ignoring Draco. Crabbe and Goyle looked to Draco, expecting instructions. For an instant he could only look back at them, wondering with a rush of panic where Blaise and Pansy were. Draco knew Greyback would attack them as he would attack anyone in the castle, indiscriminately. ØYou two stay here,Æ he ordered Crabbe and Goyle. ØKeep the Room open so we donít waste time getting back in after Iîa-after itís done.Æ Then Draco strode through the towering aisles of forgotten objects to meet the others. He would not run. Running was a mark of desperation and would make him look like a child, and he wasnít a child. Heíd planned this entire attack, hadnít he? At its end, he would be not just a man but one of the most feared men in the countryîand when he had the Dark Lordís favor, he would make Greyback pay for ever dismissing him. The thought had heartened him. It had made him feel powerful. Now Draco stared into the fire, his palms sweating. He felt ill. In a matter of hours, Crabbe, Goyle, Pansy, and Blaise would all think he was dead. Tomorrow Dumbledore would make some grave speech about it to the school, probably about how his death had been the Dark Lordís fault, and just one more reason to stand together and fight him. Draco gritted his teeth. He didnít want to be used like that. His advice to the other Slytherins wouldnít be to take up arms against the Dark Lordîit would be sit down and stay silent. If someone in power tells you what to do, do it, whether thatís You-Know-Who or Dumbledore. Donít be a hero. Donít try to figure out what you believe. Survive. Disappear. He told himself again that he had made the right decision. Dumbledore was a fool when it came to things like his trust of Snape and his worship of Potter, but the headmaster had defied the Dark Lord for decades. He could hide them, surely. Draco and his parents could flee the country, change their names and keep their heritage boxed away. They would be safe and obscure, no one. Draco closed his eyes. The flames danced dully in abstract shapes behind his eyelids. If someone had told him two years ago that he, the heir to the House of Malfoy, would ever hope to fade into insignificance, he would have laughed in their face. Of course, a lot of things had seemed funny before. Hagrid broke the silence after what must have been hours. ØThaís Professor McGonagall cominí now,Æ he rumbled, peering through the window. Minutes later, McGonagall strode into the hut. Her face was smudged with rock dust, her temple bruised, and a thin red scrape was drawn across her jaw. She closed the door and checked that the curtains were drawn before facing Draco. He avoided her eyes. McGonagallís gaze was always unforgiving, but it felt even more penetrating than usual tonight. ØWhaís happened?Æ said Hagrid, staring at McGonagall. ØYehíve been hurt!Æ ØDeath Eaters, Hagrid.Æ Her eyes were still boring into Draco. ØThey gained entry to the castle. You didnít see the Dark Mark, then?Æ ØI was sleepiní until Malfoy turned up! Ruddy hell, are they still here? Do yeh need me ter fight?Æ ØNo need. Professor Dumbledore returned to the castle in time to turn the tide, very fortunately.Æ ØAníîaní everyoneís all right?Æ She managed a thin smile. ØWe have all our limbs, Hagrid.Æ ØOh.Æ Hagrid huffed out a long breath. ØGood. Well, then. Yeh è yeh want a cuppa, Minerva?Æ ØNo time, Iím afraid.Æ She turned to Draco fully now. ØMr. Malfoy. Professor Dumbledore has explained the circumstances.Æ Draco still didnít look up, but he could feel Hagridís curious stare joining McGonagallís accusing one. ØIím sure you will be relieved to hear,Æ she went on, Øthat none of your classmates were injured by the Death Eaters tonight. As for the Vanishing Cabinet, it has been dismantled, and the passage between them closed.Æ Malfoy didnít answer. If McGonagall was waiting for him to weep with joy for the Death Eatersí failure and repent upon his knees, she would be waiting a long time. He could hear the judgment in her voice, the poorly disguised anger, even disbelief of what heíd done. She hadnít really understood the circumstances at all, then. He found himself thinking, begrudgingly, of how Dumbledore had listened to him. Dumbledore, at least, had acknowledged the danger he faced. Still è his friends were unhurt. A knot eased inside his stomach. ØWhat about my mother?Æ he said, more belligerently than heíd intended. McGonagallís lips thinned. ØRemus Lupin and Nymphadora Tonks have already been sent to retrieve her from your home.Æ He looked up at that. ØThey think theyíre just going to walk in, do they? My cousin, whoís been an Auror for about seven seconds, and a patched-up werewolf whoîÆ ØThat will do,Æ McGonagall barked. ØI assure you, Mr. Malfoy, our Order members are quite capable of doing the task assigned to them. And, as you yourself are meant to have attacked Albus Dumbledore tonight, signs of a struggle at your home will only send a clearer response to the Death Eaters.Æ ØSigns of aîyouíre notîÆ Draco struggled for words. ØThey wonítî?Æ McGonagall paused. For the first time, she seemed to soften slightly. ØYour mother will not be harmed, of course. Lupin has taken along a vial containing Dumbledoreís memory of tonightís events. Narcissa will be shown that you are safe and well, and urged to come and meet you.Æ She paused. ØAs for a struggle, I mean that Remus and Tonks will break any protective enchantments at your home and lay evidence of a fight to match our story.Æ Draco looked away. ØAll right, then,Æ he muttered. ØAnd the Death Eaters? Have you killed them yet?Æ A momentís ringing silence. When he glanced back, both teachers looked shocked by the question. Or maybe they were shocked by the way heíd said it, dully, as if it cost him nothing. Draco couldnít keep the contempt from his expression now. Did they really think he felt any affection for the other Death Eaters, who had stood back and laughed while Draco was threatened, his father slandered, his mother mistreated? Kill them all, he thought savagely, what does it matter. ØNo, Mr. Malfoy,Æ said McGonagall with a deep, concerned frown. ØOne Death Eater was killed in the crossfire, but the others are being transferred to Ministry custody within the hour. We intend to allow one of the Carrows to make a narrow escape, so that You-Know-Who will learn the details of your death tonight. Otherwise he might be inclined to investigate your motherís disappearance too closely.Æ She pursed her lips. ØIt goes without saying that we would rather not allow any Death Eater to go free. But it is certainly better for Alecto or Amycus to escape than Greyback.Æ ØI didnít let him in here,Æ Draco said under his breath. His face felt hot and full, as if his blood had turned to boiling water. ØGreyback wasnít supposed to come.Æ If McGonagall heard him, she made no sign. She was rummaging in a bag. He raised his voice. ØWhere are you taking me? Whatís this safe place Dumbledore says youíve got?Æ ØYou will be sheltered at Order headquarters. Weíll go up to the castle now; youíre to use the Floo Network. You come too, Hagrid. Albus would like to fill you in. Ah, yesîhere.Æ Something soft and silvery spilled out of McGonagallís bag. An Invisibility Cloak. ØUp, now, Malfoy. Put this on.Æ He rose automatically on legs that still felt unsteady and took the Cloak. As they slipped out onto the dark grounds, McGonagall went on. ØProfessor Snape is attending to Professor Dumbledore in the HospiîÆ Draco stopped dead, half-under the Cloak. ØSnape? Heîhe doesnít know about this, does he?Æ ØOf course he does.Æ Draco stared back at her aghast. He could hardly believe that they could have been so stupid. ØThen youíre going to get us killed!Æ ØMerlinís beard,Æ Hagrid said, Økeep yer voice down.Æ ØIím telling you,Æ Draco hissed, looking frantically between the two teachers, Øyou have to listen to me. Snape works for the Dark Lord. Heís been trying to help me get to Dumbledore all year. If youíve told Snape the plan, Iím as good as dead already.Æ ØMr. Malfoy, please,Æ said McGonagall sharply. ØSeverus will be no more involved in your concealment than any other member of the Order. Professor Dumbledore has sworn to see to you and your parentsí wellbeing personally. I assure you he is up to the task.Æ Draco hardly heard her. The only thing that mattered was that she wasnít listening, she didnít believe him. Fear rushed through him, making his whole body cold. Heíd trusted Dumbledore to think up something advanced, something unbeatableîand instead the old fool had had gone right to Snape. The moment Snape was unattended, he would tell the Dark Lord the truth. Draco could see it all playing out in his mind: the Death Eaters would beat the Aurors to his mother. They would torture and kill her, and then his father. Draco would be responsible. His thoughts raced madly into the past, back to his own idiotic choice. Heíd had Dumbledore powerless in front of him! He should have killed the old man, yes, he saw that now è should have killed him and found some way to fake his own death, to make his own escape è but it was too late now. Heíd succeeded where nobody had thought him capable of success, and in the end, he and his parents would die in pain and disgrace anyway. Unless è A glimmer of hope appeared. He tried to swallow and couldnít. The lump in his throat was as large and sharp as a clump of broken glass. Unless. Could Snape choose to lie for them? Draco knew he was Snapeís favorite student. Heíd always been best in the class at Potions, besides the Granger Mudblood, anyway. And hadnít Snape tried to help him all year? Hadnít Snape, unknown even to the Dark Lord, sworn an Unbreakable Vow to help Draco? Maybe there was a chance. If he or his parents had posed a threat to the Dark Lord, it would have been Snapeís sworn duty to reveal them. But Draco had been entrusted with no secrets, his father had been locked in Azkaban for a year, and his mother was not a Death Eater. Was it so impossible that Snape would show mercy, simply allow the Malfoys to evaporate? Besides, he considered with a fresh surge of hope, double agents have to think tactically. As long as Dumbledore lived, Snape had to keep the secret, or his loyalties would be revealed. Draco loosed a slow breath. Yes. That was good, solid reasoning. He had some time, then. Snape would surely seek to kill Dumbledore soon, to fulfill the Vow, but Dumbledore didnít need to survive forever, only long enough to hide Draco and his parents somewhere not even Snape knew. As long as Dumbledore lived, he was safe. ØFine,Æ he said. ØLetís go.Æ He let the Invisibility Cloak fall over him and followed McGonagall up the long, sweeping lawn. Draco hadnít seen the castle so empty all year. Sneaking up to the Room of Requirement, heíd had dozens of near misses with inconveniently placed Order members on patrol, or teachers looking sleepless and harried, their wands held loosely in their hands as if they were always expecting attack. Now they didnít pass so much as a ghost. Most of the portraits were sleeping in their frames, although occasionally a figure would stir and watch McGonagall and Hagrid, seemingly alone, pass down the corridors. The torchlight shone through Dracoís invisible body as he followed the teachers up a long staircase. He felt a kind of exhaustion beyond physical tiredness. The morning seemed as if it had happened a year ago, when heíd jerked awake in the Slytherin dormitory with the same fear that had been closing in on him for months now, the feeling that his time was running out. He felt mostly numb, now, and yet as he gazed blankly around at the halls of Hogwarts, knowing he could never come back, his insides seemed to coil and twist like a nest of serpents. ØAh, Minerva. Lock the door, please,Æ said Dumbledoreís voice as they entered the Hospital Wing. It was so late that he was unattended. Even Madam Pomfrey had gone to sleep. ØProfessor Dumbledore!Æ Hagridís eyes widened at the sight of the headmaster lying in the infirmary bed. In his haste to get to Dumbledoreís side, he clipped an empty bed with one of his enormous knees, sending it flying aside with a resounding clang as if it were made of straw. Draco hardly noticed. He had stopped in his tracks. A second person lay motionless in a nearby bed, a figure with Weasley-red hair. Draco couldnít tell if he knew him, because the face had been mangled and torn so violently that his features resembled a red blur. Draco felt as if heíd been kicked in the stomach. He wanted to look away, but he couldnít. Hadnít McGonagall said that none of the students had been hurt?îbut of course, most of the Weasleys had left Hogwarts è Draco remembered how the twins had left last year, flying out of the Entrance Hall, Umbridge raging after them. He remembered feeling a reluctant sense of amusement, even admiration. Heíd had to wipe the tiny smirk off his face when Umbridge had rounded, seething, on the Inquisitorial Squad, her face so shiny and purple that sheíd looked like a peeled onion. Dracoís mouth was slightly open. He realized his vision was blackening at the edges with the speed of his breathing. He finally managed to tear his eyes from the deep gashes, from the skin that had been arranged delicately back into place like a hideous puzzle, but his thoughts felt disorganized. The Hospital Wing seemed too bright. He knew without needing to ask that the wounds were from Greyback. Had Dumbledore asked him here so he would be forced to see this, the consequences of what had happened tonight? So was he meant to feel guilty, even responsible? No, Draco thought with a kind of furious panic. He refused to feel responsible. What good was it for him to feel that, or even to see the destroyed face? Heíd turned his back on the Death Eaters. What more could he do? Anyway, heíd told Greyback to go. He seized on that fact and held it tight. Go back, heíd said. Go back è ØDraco,Æ said Dumbledore. ØPlease, show yourself.Æ Still breathing hard, Draco realized he didnít want to take off the Cloak. He didnít want Dumbledore to see him again, not standing feet from the mauled body, where the line could be drawn so obviously between them. What if Dumbledore changed his mind and decided he didnít want to help him anymore? He knew he couldnít last on his own. ØNow, Mr. Malfoy,Æ said McGonagall impatiently, holding out her hand. ØThe headmaster needs rest, and you need to leave as soon as you can.Æ Draco swallowed, took off the Cloak, and dropped it into McGonagallís waiting hand. Dumbledore looked concerned at the sight of him. ØAre you all right, dear boy?Æ he asked. Draco stared at the old man. All right? Was that a test? Was he supposed to compare himself to the horrible injuries of the figure in the bed, and to realize that he was all right, but only at the expense of whichever Order member it was? Was that the psychological game Dumbledore was playing? Draco realized his face had twisted up. ØNever been better,Æ he forced out. McGonagall and Hagrid looked irritated, but Dumbledore was as serene as ever, twinkling away in his bed. Draco didnít want to look at themîall Gryffindors, he realized, standing unified against him. Instead he glared at Dumbledoreís blackened hand lying against the white sheets. ØWell?Æ he said. ØWhen are you going to get my father out of Azkaban?Æ Hagrid let out a furious, strangled noise. ØGetîget åisîwhat?Æ Even McGonagall couldnít restrain an odd little sound that sounded like a cat choking on a hairball. Dumbledore did not look at either of them. He met Dracoís eyes, and almost at once, Draco felt an inexplicable sense of safety settle over him. He hated himself for the feelingîso far, hadnít Dumbledore done little more than hurl him back into danger with his idiotic trust of Snape?îand yet there was something in the ancient face that still radiated power, and therefore reassurance. ØAs I said earlier,Æ Dumbledore said calmly, ØLucius is safe enough in Azkaban for the time being. There is no need to fear for his life, especially now that Lord Voldemort will consider him, after the apparent death of his son and wife, more than sufficiently punished. In fact,Æ he added mildly, as if commenting on the weather, Øif we act out his death immediately after your motherís, it will make both deaths rather less convincing, I think.Æ ØBut you know how youíre going to do it? You have a plan?Æ Dumbledore inclined his head. ØWell?Æ Draco said impatiently. ØWhat is it?Æ ØMalfoy,Æ barked McGonagall, who clearly thought he was being too demanding with a convalescent man over a century old. But Dumbledore silenced her by lifting his healthy hand, which, Draco noticed, was not trembling nearly so badly now as it had on top of the tower. ØSometime in the coming weeks,Æ said Dumbledore calmly, Ømembers of the Order will visit Lucius in Azkaban under the guise of informing him of your death. They will, of course, tell him the truth instead. They will also provide him with a dose of Draught of Living Death to drink soon thereafter.Æ One corner of Dumbledoreís mouth lifted. ØAfter his burial in the family plot, I daresay we might find him under slightly less surveillance. Then he can be fetched back to headquarters, too.Æ Hagrid couldnít seem to contain himself. ØBut Professor Dumbledore, sir,Æ he burst out, Øhow are we ter know Lucius Malfoy wonít run righí back to You-Know-Who after gettiní out of Azkaban?Æ Draco snapped. Part of him had been waiting for something like this, waiting to vent some tiny fraction of his fear and anger. ØBecause,Æ he snarled, Ømy fatherís smart enough not to waltz back to the Dark Lord while your lot have my mother and me all but captive at your headquarters. Thank God Iím not relying on your brains to keep us alive.Æ The expected maroon color flooded Hagridís cheeks. Draco wanted him to retort, wanted an excuse to fight, but before Hagrid could respond, Dumbledore broke in. ØTo your question, Hagrid,Æ he said, voice slightly raised, ØI do not believe that Lucius would risk his wife and son for anything, especially for a loyalty to Lord Voldemort that wavered so soon after his master first lost his body.Æ Now he looked toward Draco, stern-faced for the first time since Draco had said the word ØMudbloodÆ on top of the Astronomy Tower. ØDraco,Æ he said with a touch of steel. ØNot just here, but while you are at headquarters, I must ask you never to speak to a member of the Order that way again. To protect you puts them all at risk. You owe them, if not your gratitude, your respect.Æ Draco clenched his jaw. All sorts of thoughts flew through his head, namely that he hadnít asked for the oafís help, that he wouldnít trust Hagrid as far as he could throw the great lump, thatîfar from protecting himîif Hagrid didnít wind up compromising his familyís safety by sheer carelessness it would be an absolute miracle. But he knew insulting Hagrid would do nothing. It was Dumbledore he needed to keep happy, so he twitched his head in the smallest nod he could manage. Dumbledore didnít look wholly convinced. ØYou will promise to treat everyone who enters Order headquarters with respect?Æ Draco closed his eyes. He could only imagine what would come pouring into that place come summer. Harry bloody Potter, he supposed, and the Weasel King, and the Mudblood Encyclopedia. Not to mention werewolves and blood traitors and Aurors and generally no one he wanted to see under any circumstances ever. ØWhat does it matter?Æ Draco ground out. ØOh, it matters greatly, Draco.Æ Dumbledore sighed and lifted a goblet from the bedside. He sipped the potion inside, grimaced, and then straightened against his cushions. ØTo respect each other, no matter our differences, is the most fundamental distinction between what the Order practices and what the Dark Lord espouses among his followers. We will have much to accomplish this summer, andîÆ ØIím not working for you.Æ The words were out of Dracoís mouth before heíd planned them. Dumbledore looked at him with polite interest. ØOh?Æ Draco felt both Hagridís and McGonagallís disapproving looks hot on the side of his face, but he lifted his chin defiantly. ØYou heard me,Æ he said coldly. ØI said Iím not working for you. Snape might keep quiet if he thinks my parents and I are just hiding, but if he thinks weíre working against the Death Eaters, thereís no chance he wonít tell the Dark Lord.Æ ØProfessor Snape has been instructed not to reveal your survival toîÆ ØHe isnít working for you! What do I have to do to get that through yourîÆ ØThat is enough, Malfoy!Æ Professor McGonagall snapped. ØReally!Æ Before Draco could snap back, Dumbledore intervened again. ØPlease, Minerva. Have patience. Draco has suffered a dreadful ordeal, and Severus does, after all, play his part convincingly enough to fool even Lord Voldemort. It is perfectly natural for Draco to be concerned for his and his familyís wellbeing.Æ Draco just stared at Dumbledore. He had no idea what he was supposed to feel at this collection of sentences. Were they condescending? Yes. Mollifying? Also yes, somehow. Draco couldnít understand the old man at all. He seemed so understanding of everyone and everything that it was like he wasnít a person at all, just a ghostlike entity who drifted between a thousand points of view, acknowledging them all to be perfectly natural without ever really feeling anything of his own. He even spoke about the Dark Lord with a bizarre kind of understanding. Draco wanted to decide it was pathetic, but he couldnít get beyond the fact that it was completely incomprehensible. What, did Dumbledore never judge anyone? Did he never prefer anyone or dislike anyone? How could he even be on his own side and say that Draco had Øsuffered a dreadful ordealÆ? Draco would almost have preferred Dumbledore to look at him with loathing; at least that would have made sense. Anyway, heíd shut McGonagall up, so there was that small blessing. ØI do not expect you to assist the Order of the Phoenix, Draco,Æ said Dumbledore lightly, as if nothing had interrupted his original speech. ØIt is enough of a victory, in terms of our aims, for three people close to the Dark Lord to cease working against us. And it will be difficult enough for you to leave behind everything you know, to be thought dead.Æ Draco had no response to that, either. Dumbledoreís expression was a bit too knowing, as if heíd guessed what Draco was imagining. His friendsí faces when they learned what had supposedly happened to him. The silence in their compartment on the Hogwarts Express back home. ØWhat are you going to tell them?Æ he muttered, looking at the Hospital Wing's scrubbed floors. ØI will announce to the school that you had been sworn into Lord Voldemortís service and ordered to kill me, or be murdered yourself. I will tell them, too, that you admitted to the attacks that hurt Katie Bell and Ron Weasley. Yes, Draco,Æ he added more quietly, ØIím afraid the school deserves to know the truth about that. The more of your behavior this year they understand, the more they will be able to understand the kind of future that awaits them under Voldemortís rule.Æ ØSo, youíre going to use me as a warning, are you?Æ Draco couldnít quite keep the sneer out of his voice. ØBe careful or youíll wind up like Draco Malfoy. Join up now, and resist the Dark Lord, so we donít have any more Draco Malfoys in the world. Except youíre still getting them killed that way, arenít you?Æ Dumbledore did not reply for a moment. When Draco looked up, he felt an uncertain lurch at the expression on Dumbledoreís face. The old manís eyes were bright and full of pain. ØYes,Æ Dumbledore said quietly. ØFor those like you, Draco, who come of age in times like these, Iím afraid that is the only choice left to make: whether to stand against evil, at risk of being cut down by its many terrible weaponsîor to come quietly, and be led, voiceless, into its center.Æ Draco couldnít answer. His throat had constricted. ØIs there anything else you wish to ask me before you go?Æ Dumbledore asked. ØWho è whoís that,Æ said Draco, unable to look at the other bed. ØBill Weasley. He was Head Boy here in his time, and a Prefect like yourself. I believe you know his youngest brother, Ron, and his sister Ginny. He will recover, though his life will, of course, be different.Æ Dumbledore paused. ØIf that is all, then please read this.Æ He handed Draco a slip of parchment. It read: The Headquarters of the Order of the Phoenix is located at Number 12, Grimmauld Place. Hardly had Draco read the words when the parchment burst into a heatless flame. In seconds it had gone. ØGoodnight, Draco,Æ Dumbledore said, removing his spectacles. His face looked strangely and soft and naked without them. ØThank you for what you did tonight.Æ Mad, Draco thought. ØNight,Æ he muttered, and then he let McGonagall steer him away, through the halls one last time, and into her office, where he cast a pinch of glittering green powder upon an empty grate, spoke the address, and left his life behind. Chapter End Notes Notes for Fic Journal of the Plague Year: This chapter was drafted during social isolation and edited during Shelter-in-Place, an escalation on restrictions in Chicago that happened a few days ago. The moment I lost one of my jobs to the virus situation, I decided I wanted to start a new long fic project for the first time in years and years. HP fanfiction is maybe the most comfortable space in the world for me. Iíve met so many wonderful people through it, which makes everything feel even warmer and cozier. For all that people constantly Discourse about fandomís toxicity, I have barely ever felt a personal bad vibe off the whole experience. And I wrote fandom-related songs in like 2012 that talked about SuperWhoLock and stuff. Youíd think Iíd be a prime target for cringe humiliation efforts. Anyway. For me this particular fic will probably be mostly escapism, although maybe Iíll find myself writing some additional oneshots or something that look the quarantine mood directly in the face. I donít know. For now I just want some stability, so here I am. Chapter 2: TheFall and theFlight Two weeks later It was lucky the house at Grimmauld Place had several storeys and a basement still strewn with Dark artifacts, because if it had been smaller or duller, Draco thought he might have surrendered himself to the Dark Lord out of sheer boredom. ØDraco,Æ said his mother one evening, when he made the mistake of saying this to her. ØThat is nothing to joke about.Æ Her eyesîicy blue and slightly feline, as if his own had been saturated with colorîflicked nervously to the door. Draco knew she was remembering the manor, which for nearly a year had housed a steady stream of Death Eaters, all monitoring each otherís words for hints of weakness or disloyalty. Draco yawned and sank down in his ancient leather chair. ØPlease, Mother. You know how hard I worked to get us this exclusive reservation. Iím not going to leave it all to you.Æ Her expression softened, and her lips pulled briefly in a near smile. She returned to the Evening Prophet. Draco's mother looked healthier than she had in a year. Her long blonde hair, which had been lank and dull every holiday, was brushed and clean now, and though her eyes still had the red tint of sleeplessness, her movements were less nervous. Her posture had regained the rigid perfection that Draco associated with black pearls and silk robes, the lavish parties of his childhood. In general, she looked the way Draco felt: as if the previous year had been physically siphoned out of his body, leaving him lighter, able to breathe. Draco ran his fingers over the chairís cracked, faded arms and experienced a rare moment of contentment. It was mid-July and pleasantly hot, and theyíd pushed up the windows of the drawing room to let in a breeze. The Wizarding Wireless in the corner was humming with the Clantham Cricketsí Symphony, and his mother was, if not happy, at least safe and comfortable. To top it all off, theyíd received word two nights ago that Lucius had been smuggled the Draught of Living Death in Azkaban and was to be freed this weekend. There was no owl post to the house, but the Prophet and any messages came through the Floo twice a day, ejected unceremoniously onto the kitchen hearth. The most eventful bit of news in the Prophet so far had been their shared obituary, which had run the week after their Ødeaths.Æ Draco had read the piece out loud in a somber, priestlike tone that had made his mother smile with teeth, which she never did; there was a sharp canine she didnít like. ØAnything worth reading today?Æ he asked, propping his feet up on an ottoman whose ivory legs were carved out of troll tusks. ØNot particularly. The Ministry is conducting an internal investigation of the Department of Magical Games and Sports. They suspect someone there has been compromised.Æ ØMagical Games and Sports?Æ Draco snickered. ØOf course. All part of the Dark Lordís master plan to take over the International Association of Quidditch.Æ One corner of his motherís mouth twitched. ØThey think itís a side door into the Auror Office. Lax security in one department could mean a chain of Imperius Curses, et cetera, et cetera.Æ ØOh.Æ Draco paused. ØAnd? Has someone been Imperiused?Æ His mother arched one thin eyebrow. ØI donít think itís appropriate to discuss such things while weíre here, Draco.Æ She turned a page pointedly. Draco kept watching her, amused. His parents always withheld information from him for about twenty seconds. They seemed to regard it as a good exercise in patience. On cue, his mother sighed and looked over at him. ØI didnít hear of any plans for that department. Of course, the Dark Lord will have jettisoned anything I did hear. Heíll think I was interrogated thoroughly before my death.Æ ØOf course. If Dumbledoreís known for anything, itís his brutal interrogation tactics.Æ ØHe is an accomplished Legilimens.Æ ØYeah, well, that didnít stop me from getting Death Eaters into his school, did it?Æ Draco stood and stretched. ØI think Iíll have the elf make me a cup of tea. Do you want any, Mother?Æ ØNo, thank you, Draco. è Iíll call him if I need him.Æ She reached up absentmindedly to touch Dracoís arm as he passed, as if to remind herself that he was real. In the end, Draco headed down to the basement kitchen to make the tea himself. He wasnít fond of the elfîCroucher, or whatever his name wasîwho had a bad habit of popping up suddenly, looking like a demented gnome. Two days after their arrival, the hideous old thing had shown up, sent by Dumbledore and apparently delighted to see both of them. Since then, Dracoís mother had been giving the elf orders, and under her instruction, the house had grown cleaner and cleaner. There was only one type of mold left on Dracoís bathroom ceiling, for instance. Or, rather, Regulus Arcturus Blackís bathroom ceiling; heíd been sleeping in the manís bedroom. ØMaster Draco!Æ yelped a deep, throaty voice when the kettle was halfway to boiling. Draco flinched and looked back to see the elf scurrying into the room, looking stricken. ØKreacher did not know Master Draco was in need of tea è Kreacher would have been honored, honored to serve the noble son of Malfoy è Kreacher knows he takes his tea strong, as befits his pure blood, yes èÆ Draco moved back with disgust. Mercifully, the elf had replaced his old rag of a loincloth with a more presentable towel, but he still reeked with the scent of decay. Probably it was all the mold. Then Draco registered the words. ØHow do you know how I make my tea? Youíve never made me any.Æ The elf shifted guiltily, his bloodshot eyes sliding back and forth. ØKreacher watched è that is to say è Kreacher was forced to watch Master Draco last year, at Hogwarts, underîÆ his expression souredî ØMaster Harryís orders. Kreacher was made to supervise Master Draco at all times, and to tell Master Harry information, yes, he was, though he didnít want to.Æ Draco stared at the elf, repulsed. ØAt all times? What, even when I was asleep?Æ Kreacher looked like he greatly regretted coming into the room. ØKreacher felt such remorse,Æ he gasped, his eyes swiveling violently now, Øsuch remorse, to pry and spy on a Malfoy è such shame èÆ But before Draco could ask any more questions, footsteps and whispers came from the front of the house. Then the door was swinging open, and Ron Weasley and Hermione Granger were walking into the kitchen. Øîif even he doesnít know who it is, thenîÆ Granger was whispering to Weasley, but as the door swung shut behind them, she broke off. Motionless, they stared across the kitchen at Draco for a long moment. He stared back. The last time heíd seen Weasley, theyíd been near the entrance to the Astronomy Tower during the fight. Weasley and his sister had been shooting hexes at the Death Eaters, mysteriously sliding under every counterattack at just the right time. Draco could still see the flashes of multicolored light reflecting across his freckled face. After two weeks of sitting in this silent house and doing nothing more exciting than Banishing a centipede down the drain, the memory of the battleîthe yelling that echoed in a thousand directions, the taste of rock dust in the air as the castle took the marks of their angerîfelt unreal. It was even stranger to remember the last time heíd seen Granger: the day before the fight, waving her hand in the air during one of Slughornís Potions lessons, making him suffer from secondhand embarrassment as always. It didnít seem possible that anything so normal could have been happening only two weeks ago. The creak as Kreacher slunk through the door brought Draco back to himself. ØDonít mind me,Æ he drawled, though his voice didnít sound as nonchalant as heíd wanted. ØIíll be gone in a moment. Donít want to interrupt your è what is this, are you eloping, or something?Æ He eyed Grangerís fingers, which were wrapped around Weasleyís arm. ØActually, I donít think I want to know.Æ ØYouíve got some nerve,Æ Weasley snapped as Draco went back to poking through various boxes of tea. ØEnjoying your holiday, are you?Æ ØIíve had better. Doubt you have, though, Weasley.Æ He shot a smirk over his shoulder. ØThis place does have multiple bathrooms, so I suppose to you itís practically a five-star hotel.Æ Before Weasley could retort, the door opened again. Four more Weasleys poured into the kitchen, whispering among themselves: the twins, followed by the parents. Øîdonít care what Remus thinks youíre ready toîÆ Mrs. Weasley was hissing at one of the twins under her breath, but as the party spotted Draco and stopped in their tracks, she lost her voice just as Granger had. Silence fell again, even more uncomfortable than before. The kettle began to whistle. Draco turned his back on them and poured his tea, suddenly aware that his mouth was dry. As he poured, he imagined spilling the boiling liquid over his fingers, imagined skin scalded red, injured skin, like Bill Weasleyís. In the two weeks heíd been in this place, heíd listened to Order members come and go from the first floor landing. Heíd heard the Weasley parentsí voices during meeting nights, but he hadnít seen them yet. He didnít want to be in a room with them, or to know what they wanted to say to him. With the steaming cup in his hands, Draco strode for the door, brushing past the mother, a dumpy little woman who smelled of cooking oil and cheap cleaning potion. He didnít look at her. He only spared the rest a glance, but he thought, oddly, that none of them looked especially angry except for Ron. Even Granger was giving him a wary, critical look rather than her usual vehement glare. Draco didnít exhale until the kitchen door was shut behind him. He climbed up the short stairwell into the front hall to find other figures amassing in the dusty light of the foyer: Dedalus Diggle, bouncing on his tiptoes; and Kingsley Shacklebolt, a head taller than the rest; and his cousin Nymphadora, her hair an outrageous shade of tangerine. Tonight must have been a meeting of the full Order. ØDraco, hello,Æ said a weary voice. Remus Lupin had emerged at the front of the group. Draco couldnít help eyeing his robes, which had been patched so many times that they looked practically quilted. Draco jerked his head in a nod of greeting and moved away from the kitchen door, but Lupin didnít go through. His old professor sidestepped with him, instead, allowing the other Order members past. Mad-Eye Moodyís magical eye tracked around to stare at Draco as he clunked by on his wooden leg. ØHow have you and your mother been doing?Æ Lupin asked, voice lowered. ØI know this canít have been easy for you. This house is èÆ His tired eyes traveled over the peeling wallpaper. Øè not the most welcoming place.Æ Merlin, spare me, Draco thought with irritation. Lupin obviously hadnít changed since their third year at Hogwarts. He had been like this then, too, annoyingly serious beneath his mantle of exhaustion, treating everyone like they were also nursing some private injury. Well, Draco wasnít. He was alive and safe and had no interest in being addressed as if he were on the verge of breakdown. Even at the worst of things, heíd never needed pity, and especially not from a werewolf. ØWeíre both fine,Æ he said coldly. ØIím going up to her now.Æ ØOh, thatís nice,Æ said Tonks indignantly as he stalked past her. ØWe did bring her here, you know, your mum. Youíd think sheíd have been less rude to people saving her life. I sípose it runs in the family.Æ Dracoís steps faltered. It was the first time his cousin had ever spoken to him. With a Mudblood for a father and a mother estranged from the family, Tonks had never been invited to the family reunions, or the Christmas parties, or the summer gatherings that fell all the way down the manor lawns in cascades of pastel umbrellas and platters of choux pastry. Draco had never wondered about that side of the familyîwhy would he be curious about anyone whoíd married a Mudblood?îbut now it occurred to him that his mother and Tonksís mother had probably visited this house together growing up. He didnít know why, but the thought made him feel oddly young. The rest of the Order had filed into the kitchen now. He avoided Lupinís and Tonksís eyes and didnít answer her, starting up the long, carpeted steps instead. But as he reached the landing, the front door opened again. He glanced back and saw two final Order members: Snape, impassive and hook-nosed and greasy-haired as ever; and Dumbledore, who stepped slowly, deliberately, as if every motion caused him a hint of pain. Draco watched them pass down the hall and into the kitchen. Dumbledore clearly hadnít recovered from whatever had happened to him the night of the attack, but the old man was still sticking close to Snape, still clearly trusted Snape. It was a miracle Snape hadnít found an opportunity to kill him yet. Of course, in this weakened state, the headmaster probably had to delegate everything to other Order members; he was probably surrounded all the time. Maybe Snape was trying to do it in absolute secrecy, so that he could stay in the Orderís ranks even after Dumbledoreís death. Draco glanced back down the hall to the drawing room door. He thought of his mother reading inside, thought of her look of anticipation when theyíd gotten word about his father. Suddenly the rescue didnít feel like as much of a guarantee anymore. With Dumbledore in this state, it looked like one weak poison could finish him off. Draco made a decision. He fetched the book about blood magic heíd been reading, sat on the top step, and waited for the kitchen door to open again. He knew it was no good trying to listen to the meeting; heíd tried it several times with smaller gatherings, but there was always a Silencing Charm on the door. This time, the meeting lasted hours. Draco had finished both chapters about blood as a potion ingredient by the time the door cracked open again. He strode downstairs as several Order members reached the front door. Green flashes were emanating from the kitchen as others took the Floo out; he hastened forward, not wanting to miss Snape and Dumbledore. He was in luck. When he reached the threshold, Snape and Dumbledore remained, along with Granger, Weasley, and Mrs. Weasley. Dumbledore was listening patiently to Mrs. Weasley, who was saying, Øîyounger Order members as little more than bait, Albus è!Æ Dumbledore raised a hand. Mrs. Weasley glanced over, saw Draco, and shut her mouth. ØGood to see you looking so well, Draco,Æ said Dumbledore with a small smile. ØHow may I be of use?Æ ØI wanted a word with Professor Snape." Snape glanced at Dumbledore, who inclined his head. ØI will wait for you here, Severus è Molly and I have more to discuss, clearly èÆ Draco felt unnerved. Even Dumbledoreís voice was noticeably weaker than usual. Draco wanted to watch the headmaster for any more warning signs, but soon Snape had crossed the kitchen and ushered Draco out into the now-empty hall. ØYes?Æ said Snape. His black eyes were, as usual, unreadable. ØIím not going to try to stop you,Æ Draco said quietly. Snapeís expression remained unchanged. ØI have no idea what you mean.Æ Draco lowered his voice further. ØI know you made the Vow to my mother. I know youíre going to kill the old man. Iím not going to tell anyone.Æ Snape pushed the kitchen door all the way shut. ØThen why say anything?Æ His lip curled. ØIs this a threat?Æ ØNo. It's è Iím asking you to wait until the Order gets my father out of Azkaban. None of the others will want to help us after Dumbledoreís dead. And Iím asking you not to tell the Dark Lord weíre alive.Æ He paused, then added reluctantly, ØPlease.Æ Snape looked coldly back into Dracoís face. It occurred to Draco, oddly, that he was an inch or so taller than Snape now. In his mind, Snape was still the towering presence heíd been during their first Potions lesson, the one professor in Hogwarts with the kind of power and mystique that Draco had wanted to learn himself. ØIf you had accepted my help early on,Æ said Snape in his cool, sibilant voice, Ølast year would have been a great deal easier for you, Draco. è You said you didnít want me toîwhat were the words?îah, yes è to steal your glory.Æ The syllables were crisp, scathing. ØClearly something changed.Æ Draco looked down at the grimy old carpet. ØFine. I couldnít,Æ he ground out. ØIs that what you want to hear? I couldnít kill him.Æ ØYet you would allow me to do it.Æ Draco couldnít help a bitter laugh. ØLike I could stop you?Æ He shook his head and spoke more urgently. ØBut even if I could stop you, I wouldnít. Thatís what Iím telling you. Just because Iím here, it doesnít mean Iím working against the Dark Lord. My mother and father wonít, either. Weíre not a risk to him. You can let us live and itîit wonít change anything, do you see?Æ There was a long silence. Draco held eye contact, daring Snape to use Legilimency on him, to see he was telling the truth. But he didnít feel the familiar probing sensation. He wondered, with a sudden lurch in his gut, whether Snape had already revealed the truth to the Dark Lord, if, even now, the Death Eaters were waiting for Dumbledoreís death to close in on Draco and his mother. But then Snape said, ØVery well.Æ He spoke so quietly that his lips barely moved. ØAs long as I know you are no threat to the Dark Lord è you may remain among the dead.Æ Draco took a deep breath, relief flooding him. ØThank you, sir.Æ He dipped his head. ØYou know weíll repay you when we can.Æ ØI know,Æ Snape said. Then the Potions Master turned and swept back into the kitchen. Soon there was a flash of green light. He and the headmaster were gone. Moments later, Molly Weasley stormed past Draco toward the front door, muttering something to herself about ØrecklessÆ and Øso young.Æ He heard the muffled crack as she Disapparated on the front step, and when he headed down into the kitchen to return his teacup, he found Granger and Weasley still standing at the edge of the table, talking in low voices. Again they stopped at the sight of him. They exchanged one of those looks that they and Potter were always trading, as if the three of them could read each otherís minds. Draco wondered how it felt to be so readable, so predictable to another person. It seemed like it would be boring. The silent transmission of information took only an instant. Then Granger seized Weasleyís upper arm in a viselike grip. ØRon,Æ she said in a warning tone. ØRon, donítîÆ ØGet off, Hermione.Æ Weasley tugged his arm free and took a step toward Draco. His ears were already red again. ØI know what really happened,Æ he said darkly. ØOh?Æ said Draco idly. ØWell, that might sound a bit more threatening, Weasley, if I had any idea what you were talking about.Æ Weasleyís face reddened. It was too easy with him, actually. ØIím talking about the Vanishing Cabinet,Æ he said furiously. ØThe Room of Requirement. Dumbledore mightíve told everyone else a version of things that makes it sound like you didnít have any choice, but we know you did. Youíre not a hero just because you got cold feet at the last second.Æ Dracoís eyes slid onto Granger, whose face was a web of shadows in the weak electric light of the kitchen. She didnít add anything, but she didnít contradict Weasley, either. She was studying Draco with the same hard, critical look as before, the kind heíd seen her direct at difficult assignments. The pair of them had probably discussed all this at length already. Anger filled Draco as he pictured it: these two debating with Potter, deciding how best to judge himîas if the last year of his life had been some sort of theoretical moral argument. Sanctimonious little toerags, he thought viciously. What was the hardest choice the three of them had ever had to make? Whether to go with each other to the Yule Ball? Whether to keep their mouth shut in front of Umbridge so they wouldnít get detentions? What a struggle. Draco spoke only when he could be sure his voice wouldnít betray his anger. ØYou think I need your approval, Weasel King?Æ he said, softening his words to the same cold sneer that Snape had just used on him. ØYou think I give a damn whether you think I made the right choices or not?Æ ØNo,Æ Weasley snapped. ØI donít think you give a damn. I think you only thought about yourself, like always, and thatís why my brotherís going to be scarred for the rest of his life!Æ His voice had risen to a yell. He took a deep, shaky breath and tamped down the volume, though his words still trembled with fury. ØIt all just went away for you, didnít it, Malfoy? The second youíre out of danger, everythingís back to normal, eh? Well, it didnít go away for me and my family. My brother nearly died for you Death Eaters to get a second chance you didnít deserve.Æ Draco just looked at him. The accusation had not hurt him. Actually, hearing the words, all of the heat in Dracoís body had seemed to drain, and now he felt as if he were standing several paces away from himself, watching himself be lectured, watching his own blank face. It was like the feeling of Occlumency: the numb remoteness, the total closure of himself to external force, and the strange accompanying hyperawareness of what was happening inside his own mind. Inside, odd fragments of memory were surfacing. He remembered the Quidditch pitch in second year, watching Weasleyís curse backfire, the slugs that had oozed out of his mouth. He remembered laughing so hard at Weasleyís embarrassment and discomfort that no sound came out. And during sixth year heíd guffawed over the Slytherin table at the way heíd broken Potterís nose. He remembered the satisfaction heíd felt as the bridge crunched beneath his foot and blood streamed down over Potterís face, Potter getting what he deserved, pain and humiliation, the arrogant fool. He remembered, too, the Christmas holidays last year, when the Dark Lord had come to the manor accompanied by a Muggle man from the neighboring town who had drunkenly mocked his robes. Draco remembered the Muggle manís glassy look, and how it had broken when the Dark Lord lifted the Imperius Curse, and how all the other Death Eaters had laughed to see him try to get away. Draco remembered the way the manís naked feet had slipped and scrabbled comically on the shining parquet floors. The syncopated rhythm of his ragged breaths. The pain and terror on his crimson face as he spun, asphyxiating, into the air, directed by the Dark Lordís wand. Draco had tried to make himself laugh, told himself it was funny, just a filthy Muggle getting what he deserved; that was the phrase that went through his head, cold and clear and mechanical, as if it were something he had memorized rote out of a textbook. Anyway, when commingled with the othersí laughter, the sounds he forced out of his throat sounded mostly natural, and Bellatrix looked at him like she was proud. All of this flickered and died in his mind within an instant. He was back in the kitchen and legally dead. Weasley was still flushed, but the redness of his skin had lost its humor. Draco left his teacup on the counter and walked out of the kitchen. Two weeks later Hermione looked into Ronís eyes as he told her everything was going to be fine. Doubt must have shown on her face, because he repeated, ØIt will.Æ ØYes,Æ she said. ØIîyes, Iím sure it will. Youíre right.Æ She didnít sound convincing even to herself. Ron sighed. ØWell, if you donít feel safe with Shacklebolt, Mad-Eye, Lupin, and Dumbledore there, Iím not going to be able to talk you into it. You ready to go?Æ Hermione nodded, feeling ill. Itís for Harryís sake, she told herself. Youíre going to see Harry in a matter of minutes. Then she shot a guilty look at Ron. Maybe she was imagining it, but she felt as if heíd started acting odd whenever she brought Harry up of her own accord. Sheíd been at the Burrow for most of July now. Maybe sheíd been stupid to expect it at a house filled with Ronís family, but sheíd thought è she didnít know what sheíd thought. That Ron would make a passionate declaration? No, maybe not, but she had hoped that without Harry there, Ron might move in that general direction. She felt as if she were going mad. Whenever they found time to discuss their hunt for the Horcruxesîdiscussions that required them to be hidden in Ronís roomîshe felt the tension in the air between them, and in her own body, pleasurably tense, like a string about to be played. And sometimes she could swear she felt Ron looking at her from across a room, or the crowded dinner table, but when she looked toward him, he was always in the middle of turning away. She supposed it was reasonable to wonder why she hadnít tried to move things along. Ginny had asked that precise question when Hermione had fretted to her one evening about Ronís behavior. ØIîI donít know,Æ Hermione said, taken aback. ØI suppose èÆ ØIs it because Ronís a boy?Æ Ginny said, flicking through a page of Quidditch Quarterly. ØNever thought youíd be so old-fashioned, Hermione.Æ ØNo, itís not,Æ she said hotly. ØItís because è because è well, this thing that we have to do with Harry è if Ron and I are seeing each other, it could strain the whole situation.Æ ØOh, right, and itíll be much less strained like this,Æ Ginny chortled. ØThe both of you looking like youíre constipated whenever you finish a completely average conversation.Æ Hermione sighed and lay back on Ginnyís bed, while Ginny settled herself deeper in the pouf by her window. Unfortunately, Hermione couldnít be entirely open, because a secret part of her dared to ask the question that neither Ginny nor Ron ever would: did she feel something for Harry? It felt almost impossible to separate the tangled threads of what she felt for her two best friends. A feral kind of protectiveness, affection and tenderness, even jealousyîshe felt it all for the both of them, so strongly that she wondered whether she might be mistaking it for romance with Ron, or missing it with Harry. She wondered whether, if Harry had been here instead of Ron, and they had been having these private and intense discussions, she would have felt a similar tension. She even wondered whether, if it had been Harry who had been poisoned last year, Harry whose bedside sheíd attended for weeks on end, her feelings might have developed that way instead. Or had she attended Ronís bedside precisely because sheíd been so hurt by his relationship with Lavender? She couldnít tell, and her logical mind knew perfectly well how ridiculous it was to try and cross-examine previous possibilities as if they changed anything, and also they were fighting a war, and all of this seemed pathetic and trivial and still somehow like the most important thing in the world, all her feelings amplified by the danger they faced as if the constant possibility of disaster were a drug. Now or never, and more likely never. Of course, she couldnít tell Ginny any of this. The idea of revealing that she might have feelings for Ginnyís ex-boyfriend, with whom she was about to undertake an undercover mission, would have destroyed Ginny. Hermione knew that. She also knew that if she felt anything for Harry it would destroy Ron, too, his fragile self-confidence. These threads of guilt spun another indecipherable dimension into what she felt. So Hermione had been keeping it all inside. And now, on the brink of seeing Harry again, she was feeling a kind of relief that made everything even more confusing. Why relief? When Ronís presence made her feel fluttery agitation, when sheíd become this attuned to his voice and his glances, why on earth was she relieved that theyíd failed to start things while they had the time and privacy? Was it because, secretly, she didnít want to be with Ron? Or was it because it would have been too much to start a relationship when they would soon be in such danger? Was stasis reassuring because this kind of stasis was already too much to feel? Well, if she knew one thing, it was that if sheíd tried to talk about any of it with Ron, he would have stared at her as if she were speaking another language. So she kept quiet. When the time came, she, Ron, Bill, Fleur, and Mr. Weasley gathered in the yard around five brooms. Mrs. Weasley kissed her husband, Ron, and Bill, then hugged Fleur and Hermione. She performed their Disillusionment Charms for them before retreating, checking her watch. ØYouíd all better go now,Æ she said breathlessly. ØIíll see you soon. Very soon.Æ ØWeíll be back before you know it, Molly,Æ said Mr. Weasley. The last thing Hermione saw before she kicked off was Mrs. Weasleyís weak, unconvincing smile. ØHarry!Æ Hermione threw herself into his arms. When she drew back, he was grinning. ØHi, Hermione. Hi, Ron, mate.Æ Ron clapped him hard on the back, and the anxiety of the previous month eased off Hermioneís shoulders. She could see in Ronís ear-to-ear grin that he felt it tooîthe comfort of being together again, a perfectly assembled puzzle. Dumbledore let Mad-Eye explain the plan: seven Potters, seven guardians. Harry protested, as she and Ron had known he would, but eventually relented, as he had to. Hermione shuddered through the hot, melting process of Polyjuice transformation. When they were all dressed, Dumbledore began to pair them off. Harry was matched with Hagrid, Ron with Tonks, and Hermione with Dumbledore himself on a Thestral. Hermione felt a nervous flutter. ØWouldnít you prefer to guard Harry yourself, Professor Dumbledore?Æ ØI donít think so, Miss Granger,Æ he replied with a warm smile. ØIn the case of an attack, it will be best to distribute the Death Eatersí attention. They will likely expect Harry, skilled flier that he is, to take a broom; he will, instead, be on a motorcycle. They will also likely expect him to be with me or with our Aurors; he will, instead, be kept safe by Hagrid.Æ ØI can go with Dumbledore instead,Æ Ron said quietly. ØI donít want you to be a target.Æ ØI donít want either of you to be targets,Æ Harry muttered, eyes flashing. ØItís all right, you two,Æ Hermione said, trying not to sound worried. She saw Harryís worry just beneath the surface of his anger. ØYou know how I am with brooms. Iíd rather be on a Thestral.Æ Soon, too soon, the brooms had been distributed, the Thestrals mounted, Harry tucked into Hagridís sidecar. Hermione had a gut feeling of foreboding. She didnít want to take off; she wanted to wait. She wanted them all to be safe so badly that she felt as if she were holding her palm above a rising flame, waiting to feel a burn, praying it wouldnít happen. Dumbledore, at her back, began to count down. ØThree,Æ he called, Øtwo è one!Æ Feet hit the ground. Thestral wings snapped out. A motorcycle engine roared. Seven Potters and their protectors shot up into the night sky. Hermione felt a snap as if of surface tension as they broke out of the protective enchantments. She screamed. Dozens of Death Eaters were shooting toward them from all directions, black bullets out of the dark. ØHold on!Æ Dumbledore ordered. Hermione bent low over the Thestralís neck, and the winged horse flung itself forward, letting out a strange wild cry of alarm as a streak of light shot over its head. Hermione threw a look back, trying to see any of the others, but all she could see were multicolored lights bursting in clusters like roman candles, each a pocket of battle. Already they were so far away from each other. Harry had gone due North, Dumbledore had mentioned that. She squinted that way, eyes watering as the wind buffeted her, and saw that Dumbledoreís instincts had been good. The lights there were fewer and thinner. Clutching to the shred of reassurance, Hermione turned her eyes forward again, only to realize that they hadnít been nearly so lucky. Death Eaters were pelting toward them. They had drawn half a dozen, maybe more. A Stunning spell spiraled toward her out of the black. ØProtego!Æ she cried, before remembering all the practice she and Ron had done to master nonverbal Shielding at the Burrow. The instinct had gone out of herîit felt safer to scream, to let out the fearîbut at the next burst of light she forced herself to think, Protego! and the Death Eaterís curse rebounded. She felt Dumbledore moving behind her, blocking several curses with a single long sweep of his wand. ØParasalvus!Æ he cried, and a shivering translucent wall curved around Hermione, blocking everything that came near. She could feel Dumbledore shaking. Itís the cold, she thought wildly, heís only shivering from the cold, and yet she was horribly aware of his dead hand, and the thinness of his other wrist. But Dumbledore was casting spells of such immense power now that the hair on the nape of Hermione's neck stood on end. A stream of silver light hummed toward Bellatrix Lestrange, making her and her broom freeze in midair. Another Death Eater he encased in a web of purple electricity, and the man dropped like a stone, disappearing almost at once. We're going to be all right, Hermione told herself, sending a Stupefy of her own rocketing toward a Death Eater. Then a familiar face rose up before them. In the center of a cadre of Death Eaters was Severus Snape, his face twisted, shocks of light cartwheeling across his sallow features. Hermioneís stomach plummeted. Why was Snape here? And why, when Snape was supposed to have fed Voldemort incorrect information, had there been Death Eaters here at all? There was only one answer to both questions. Snape raised his wand. Hermione knew he was going to say the words before he said them, knew he was going to snarl, ØAvada Kedavra!Æ She knew the shield around her would break. She knew Dumbledoreís body would topple off the Thestral behind her, and yet the sudden absence of his reassuring weightîthe way his robes brushed her knee as he plummetedî was so horrible that she nearly let her wand fall out of her nerveless hand. Her mouth was wide open but hardly any sound was coming from it, only a high keen that hit her own ear like the sound of an animal in pain. Horror reverberated through her. She couldnít lift her wand, couldnít breathe. She could only wait for Snape to kill her, too, or to take her, as Harry, back to Voldemort. Then the Thestral lurched. One of the Death Eatersí curses had struck the creature across the flank. Hermioneís grip on the Thestral had loosened. She was off-balance. Still in shock, she grappled too late for the Thestral's neck. She toppled, the scream finally tearing free of her, into thin air. ØThat isnít Potterís wand,Æ she heard Snape roar as she fell. ØLeave that one! We must find the real èÆ It was terrifying how quickly his voice evaporated. Hermione was plummeting like a stone. She had fallen a hundred feet, maybe more. The Death Eaters were no longer even in sight. Then she caught a glimpse of something in the night air, falling through a wisp of cloud: Dumbledoreís body, fifty feet beneath her, falling toward the Earth. Something in her ignited. Some of the madness cleared out of her head. She clutched her wand, spreading her arms to stabilize her body, Harryís body, and screamed, ØAccio!Æ Dumbledoreís body slowed in its descent, then, in an almost graceful motion, lifted toward her like a piece of ash, as if he were weightless. Her hand closed around his wrist. His bones were as brittle as a birdís. Then a voice above her roared, terrifyingly close, ØPetrificus Totalus!Æ Hermione flinched. The spell missed her by a centimeter. She twisted around to see the contorted face of Antonin Dolohov. He clearly hadnít believed Snape. And now she was tumbling head over foot again, the wind snatching the duplicate of Harryís glasses from her face, the houses below transforming into a blur. Dolohovís vague form was rocketing toward her on a broom, stretching out a dark shape that must have been his armî Hermione focused with all her might on her destination and turned in mid-air. Crack. ØIf weíre sent to the States,Æ said Dracoís mother, lifting a bite of pie to her mouth, ØI know an excellent school where Draco could finish the last of his education. Itís quite exclusive. The headmaster is a friend of my fatherís.Æ ØNarcissa,Æ said Lucius, Øby the time it would be safe for us to use that kind of contact, Draco would be past school age.Æ He sipped a glass of elf-made wine that Kreacher had pulled from the cellar. Draco tapped his fork against the stem of his own wineglass. ØDonít either of you want to know what I think about my own education? I am of age.Æ His father opened his mouth to reply. Then there was an almighty crack. Three people exploded out of the air, falling in a pile of limbs to the kitchen floor. Narcissa let out a scream and leapt to her feet. Lucius, still weak from Azkaban, staggered out of his seat and had to clutch to the counter to remain upright. Two of the figures on the ground were already writhing up to their feetîone a boy with untidy jet-black hair, the other a man with a familiar twisted face. Draco was on his feet, too, though he couldnít remember standing. He stared with horror at Antonin Dolohov, who was raising his wand. Then Dolohov saw the Malfoys and froze. His face went white. ØYou,Æ he croaked. ØYou!Æ Narcissa reacted first, slashing her wand up, but Dolohov ducked her curse. It rebounded off one of the pans hanging from the ceiling, forcing Lucius to throw himself flat. Draco looked back to see Potter raising his wandîand to see Dolohovís finger colliding with his left forearm. ØStupefy!Æ Draco yelled. But even as his spell struck Dolohov, causing the man to fly back into the wall and crumple, Draco felt the Mark burn black on his own forearm. Silence dropped, except for the heaving of breath. Only then did Draco register the third figure: a crumpled body with long silver hair and beard, motionless. Draco looked up at Potter, who was gasping, an uncharacteristic wild panic on his face. Heíd lost his glasses. ØDraco,Æ his mother cried out. ØDraco, we must go! Now!Æ But Draco was still staring at Potter, whose face had begun to change, melting, softening. For a second Draco thought he really had gone mad, that all of this was the result of a psychotic break. But a moment later the hair was growing bushy and wild, and the green eyes were turning brown. The chin lifted. The lips grew smaller and fuller. Draco blinked hard, and when he opened his eyes, Hermione Granger was standing before him, panic written across her face. Lucius strode to the mantel and seized a jar of Floo Powder. ØWhere?Æ He rounded on his wife. ØSomewhere safe. Where?Æ Narcissa just shook her head, looking like she was about to faint. ØThe Burrow,Æ gasped Granger. ØGo to the Burrow!Æ Narcissa seized a pinch of green powder and flung it on the grate, stepped over the massive hearth, and said, ØThe Burrow!Æ Nothing happened. The green flame died around her. ØThe opposite grate is closed.Æ Narcissa turned back. Her composure had cracked completely now, her blue eyes wild. ØTheyíll be coming now. He touchedîtheyíll be here any minute, LuciusîDolohov, he sawîÆ ØHere, then!Æ said Granger. ØHurry!Æ All three Malfoys stared at her for a moment. She was holding out a hand. ØOh, for Godís sake,Æ she yelled, Øwould you rather touch a Mudblood or die? Take my hand now!Æ Draco was the first to move. He seized Grangerís hand, and his mother and father formed a chain with his other hand. Granger stooped to grasp Dumbledoreís wrist. In that moment, Narcissa let out a little cry, her eyes fixed across the kitchen. Dolohov was stirring again. Lucius slashed his wand downward. There was a blast of green light, and Dolohov toppled, lifeless, to the ground. Draco jumped so violently that he nearly let go of Granger. There was a crack somewhere outside the room, down the hall. A second crack. A third. Loud, indistinct voices. Granger turned on her heel. The crushing darkness of Disapparition closed in around Dracoís head. He couldnít breathe, couldnít shift an inch, could only clutch to his motherís hand in his left, the coldness of her silver rings pressing against his fingers, and to Grangerís in his right, warm and small and holding on so hard that he thought she might crack a metacarpal. They reappeared on a stretch of hardwood floor. Draco let go of his mother, who instantly folded, still gasping, her eyes wild, into his fatherís arms. Granger, breathing hard too, was facing away from Draco. For a long moment she didnít move. Then she knelt beside Dumbledoreís body and slowly, deliberately arranged him into a relaxed position, as if he were sleeping. She moved the silver hair out of his face. The half-moon spectacles were cracked in one lens. Granger lowered her wand to them and whispered, ØReparo.Æ The fissure sealed over, but Granger didnít stand. She stayed on her knees, trembling. Draco watched her with a sense of impossible distance. Heíd known this would happen, had known Dumbledore would die. He just hadnít known he would see the aftermath. He remembered the weight of the wand in his hand as he stood atop the Astronomy Tower, and a terrible, thunderous kind of relief dropped onto him that he hadnít done this. Even so, he heard his own voice saying, two weeks ago, Iím not going to try and stop you. Donít let it be Snape, he found himself thinking now. Donít let it have been him that did it. ØWho èÆ he forced out. ØHow did it happen?Æ ØPro è Professor Snape.Æ Granger was crying, he could hear it in her voice, but she didnít turn around, didnít want him to see. Maybe she thought he would mock her if he saw. Draco looked around in a daze. They'd landed in a large and finely furnished sitting room. A Persian rug stretched out beneath a long, elegant leather couch; a wall of bookshelves faced an empty fireplace.
