On an oak table, across from the couch, stood a strange black box with two metal spindles extending from its top. ØWhat is this place?Æ he said. Granger had wiped her face. She rose to her feet and looked over at him, her eyes red, her cheeks pink. ØItís my house,Æ she answered. Chapter 3: TheMidnight Vigil ØYour house?Æ said Lucius Malfoy, aghast. Hermione didnít bother answering. She fixed her eyes on the bookshelves and breathed, trying to draw some comfort from the familiar scents of home, maple candles and old pages and soft leather. It didnít work. Her eyes had found a photo of herself and her parents. She was six, ready for her first day of primary school, one of their hands on each of her shoulders. And now her mind was summoning the image sheíd ruminated over dozens of times: her parents lying in bed the night sheíd modified their memories, her motherís hair in a nightcap, her fatherís mouth ajar. As sheíd left for the Burrow that night, sheíd wanted so badly to go back and say goodbye. If something happened to her during their hunt for the Horcruxes è if she never had the chance to say it è Sheíd refused to acknowledge the possibilities then, but now her eyes slid back onto Dumbledoreís cold, thin body, and she knew sheíd been right to expect the worst. The knowledge was strangely stabilizing. At least she could stop telling herself that she was being overanxious. Here was proof that the world was as dangerous as it felt. ØThereís nowhere else you could have taken us than a Muggle house?Æ said Narcissa, not bothering to veil her disgust. ØNo. The Orderís safehouses are all in use tonight to move Harry.Æ ØOf course,Æ Draco muttered under his breath. Hermione ignored him. Wingardium Leviosa, she thought, flicking her wand. Dumbledoreís body rose from the ground, and she walked him over to the sofa and let him settle there. She knew it was ridiculous to think that he looked uncomfortable, but she tucked a pillow beneath his head anyway. She glanced back at the three Malfoys. Lucius and Narcissa had edged closer together, eyeing the house as if afraid it might contaminate them. Mr. Malfoy looked different now than he had in the dark alleyways of the Department of Mysteries. Azkaban had followed him out into freedom. The once-faint lines in his pointed face had deepened and set as if his skin were candle wax, aging him a decade. Draco wasnít looking at the house. His colorless eyes were fixed on Dumbledoreís body. ØDo the Order know heís dead?Æ he asked. His voice wasnít the usual drawl but something unfamiliar, closed-off and hard. Hermione shook her head. ØI was the only one with him. The others wonít even know anythingís wrong on our end for another èÆ She glanced to a clock on the wall and was disconcerted by how early it still was. The nightís events seemed to have stretched time out like elastic. ØAnother half an hour, when they start arriving at the Burrow.Æ ØWhat, you canít contact them?Æ said Draco with disbelief. ØYes, I know we have to, but I canít think how.Æ She bit her lip and began to pace across the sitting room. ØI donít know how to cast a messenger Patronus. Thatís well past N.E.W.T. standard. And thatís not an option for any of you, obviouslyÆîshe glanced at the Malfoysî Øin case someone recognizes your Patronuses who shouldnít. But then èÆ She stopped pacing. ØI donít suppose you have a way of sending messages? You wouldnít know where to find anîan owl, orî?Æ ØAn owl?Æ Lucius Malfoy let out a scathing laugh. ØWhat use would an owl be to us when weíre meant to be dead?Æ Hermioneís temper flared. ØWell, if you have any ideas to get us out of this, I would love to hear them.Æ Narcissa drew herself up. ØThere would be no need to åget out ofí anything if you hadnít Apparated into our hiding place with Dolohov hanging off your robes, you stupid girl.Æ Rage filled Hermione like hot tar. It seemed unbelievable that they were blaming her for this. She wanted to say something to defend herselfîit had, after all, been impossible to feel Dolohov seizing her robes in freefallîbut when she thought of the attack, her mind conjured Snapeís face, full of loathing, and she felt anew the horrible sensation of Dumbledore's body sliding off the Thestral behind her, brushing her as if in farewell, and her stomach began to churn, and her eyes began to sting. She could finally appreciate why Harry hardly ever spoke about Cedricís death, even now, years after the graveyard. Draco turned away from Dumbledoreís body. Hermione steeled herself for him to pile on, too. ØThe ones you and that group had.Æ ØOh.Æ She gave her head a little shake to clear it. ØMine is at the Burrow with all my things. Besides, I doubt Harry and Ron would think to check them. We havenít used them since fifth year èÆ But as she thought back to fifth year, and their ill-fated journey to the Department of Mysteries, it hit her. ØThe Ministry!Æ she exclaimed. All three Malfoys stared at her as if sheíd gone mad. ØOh, yeah,Æ said Draco. ØThe Ministry. Brilliant idea, Granger, just brilliant. Weíll stroll in and ask to seeîÆ ØNot you. Iíll Apparate to the visitorsí entrance tomorrow under a Disillusionment Charm. Mr. Weasleyís been on overtime there all summer, so heíll have to be in by seven. Iíll ask him to connect our fireplace to the Floo temporarily, and we can all move to the Burrow tomorrow.Æ No response from the Malfoys. ØThereíll be room for us to stay here tonight,Æ Hermione added. ØMy parents are è theyíre on holiday abroad.Æ Still, nothing. Narcissa seemed to have noticed the television. She was watching it warily as if expecting it to explode. Their looks of distaste wore down Hermioneís patience in seconds. ØWell,Æ she said shortly, Øitís this or go somewhere the Death Eaters will find you within minutes. Take your pick. Iím going to put some protective enchantments on the house.Æ She stalked off, leaving them there. Hopefully they would say whatever disgusting things they were all thinking about Muggles while she was out of the room. Flush it out of their systems. All of July, Hermione had tried not to let her misgivings about the Malfoys overwhelm her, but, good God, they made it difficult. It rankled at her that the Order were sheltering a family who hadnít even pretended to change their minds about pureblood supremacyîpeople who would have gone back to Voldemort in a heartbeat, if theyíd had any guarantee of their safety. Now that Dumbledore could no longer protect the Malfoys, would they take their chances with Voldemort? Would they spin some story about only accepting Dumbledoreís help to infiltrate the Order of the Phoenix? Ron, rather braver than she was at Order meetings, had voiced this idea at the full meeting two weeks ago. Dumbledore had insisted that Mr. and Mrs. Malfoy would never risk returning to Voldemort, out of fear for Dracoís life. ØBut what if Dracoís in on it too,Æ Ron had said, Øand they all pretend they were loyal the whole time? What if they pretend they were just è just going undercover, or something? They could give You-Know-Who the location of headquarters. Theyíve seen all our members going in or out. They have a load of information they could tell him.Æ ØThey canít give him the location of headquarters, Weasley,Æ McGonagall had said briskly. ØWith the Fidelius Charm securing Grimmauld Place, only our Secret-Keeper can divulge that information.Æ ØWell, still,Æ Ron insisted, Øwouldnít You-Know-Who take the Malfoys back if they gave him the names of everyone in the Order?Æ ØAh,Æ Dumbledore had said with his usual serene smile, Øthis is where Lord Voldemort differs from you or I, Mr. Weasley. You, a great player of chess, as I understand itÆîhe inclined his head first to Ron, then to Professor McGonagallî Øare thinking in terms of strategy. Lord Voldemort, on the other hand, thinks in terms of absolute, unwavering loyalty. He detests the idea of seeming any less than omniscient, and so he would be furious to learn that the Malfoys were still alive when he thought them to be dead.Æ ØWhat,Æ Ron said with clear disbelief, Øyou mean heíd give up a chance to learn about the Order, just to teach them a lesson?Æ ØOh, certainly not,Æ said Dumbledore. ØHe would indeed extract any information about the Order that he could from the Malfoys. Some they might give willingly. Names and identities, as you say. Other information he would wring out of them by torture and Legilimency, or by torturing one family member to question another. Then, once he had what he needed, he would kill them for what he would perceive as an unforgivable lapse in the fealty they swore him.Æ Dumbledore sighed. ØDracoís life in particular would be forsaken twice over, as he failed in the mission the Dark Lord set him. The Malfoys, who have lived in the shadow of Voldemort for most of their lives, understand this. I do not think they would accept such a fate merely to further Voldemortís cause.Æ Ron, who had gone rather pale, had not asked any more questions. But, Hermione thought, climbing the steps to the attic, Dumbledore was wrong about Snape. That had cost him his life. Had Dumbledore made other miscalculations? Had he put too little faith in the Malfoysí devotion to the cause, or too much faith in their love for their son? What if Voldemort felt predisposed to overlook Dracoís failure now that Snape had completed the mission for him? As Hermione pushed up the attic window and leaned out to cast enchantments into the summer night, she wished she could talk about all this with Ron and Harry. She wanted to be at the Burrow with them so badly that it felt as if something had stuck in her throatîas if she would not breathe clearly again until they were safe and reunited. ØProtego Totalum,Æ she whispered. They are safe, she told herself, over and over, in a sort of mantra. Still, even ifîwhen!îboth Harry and Ron returned to the Burrow, she could imagine their panic when she didnít come back. They would want to hunt for her, to leave the house, while the rest of the Order would forbid it absolutely. ØRepello Muggletum èÆ She imagined them lying awake in Ronís room later that night, Ron monologuing in a low, anxious voice about how she was with Dumbledore, and nothing could hurt her while she was with Dumbledore. Harry, on the camp bed, would be staring at the ceiling in silence, only letting out the occasional ØYeahÆ and ØMm,Æ but she could imagine the way his brilliantly green eyes would crinkle with worry. ØSalvio Hexia.Æ By morning Ron would be angry and agitated, taking out his mood on Ginny only to feel guilty minutes later, and Harry would turn into that quiet, serious man he sometimes became when things were particularly badîthe man he wouldnít have grown into yet, in a perfect world. Hermione lowered her wand, lowered the sash, and went for the stairs. Draco couldnít sleep. Granger had situated his parents in the master bedroom, where theyíd stood in momentary horror at the array of devices lined up in the bathroom. Granger had said they were meant to stave off tooth decay, but they definitely looked like torture instruments and Draco would not be putting any of them near his face. Sheíd shown him to a second bedroom on the ground floor with a large, soft bed. He hadnít thought it was late enough to sleep, but the moment heíd settled onto the mattress, a wave of tiredness had rolled over him. He should have fought the instinct, he thought irritably now as he turned over. Heíd reawakened at one in the morning. Now it was half-past two, and he was still wide awake, staring into the semidarkness. The curtains werenít quite opaque, so the streetlamp outside the window tinted everything orange: the mahogany desk in the corner, the anonymous white dresser, the brass lamp with its drooped neck and hanging shade. Images kept passing across his vision. Occasionally he saw Dumbledoreís face lit green atop the Astronomy Tower, or Dumbledoreís face pressed into the kitchen tiles of Number 12, Grimmauld Place, as if his cheek were the sole of a shoe, just any other object. Mostly, though, he found himself thinking of Dolohov. Dolohov spinning as Dracoís Stunner had hit him. Dolohov stirring on the floor, only to be struck by his fatherís Avada Kedavra. The way heíd slumped, lifeless, back into place. Something in Dracoís mind was stuck inside that instant, the moment the green light had connected with Dolohovís body. It had happened so quickly. His father had cast the Killing Curse the same way heíd done so much everyday magic at home, summoning books or drawing curtains, conjuring a Transfigured zoo for Dracoís amusement on his eighth birthday. Draco knew his father had killed before, but the idea had always been abstract. Now he wondered how many people his father had killed in total. He wondered too whether heíd disappointed his father by failing to kill Dumbledore. Theyíd so studiously avoided the subject at Grimmauld Place that an objective observer might have thought theyíd all wound up there by sheer accident. His father wouldnít have hesitated to kill Dumbledore. That much was obvious now. Was it a sign of weakness that Draco had hesitated? He pictured his parents one floor above and wondered whether his father was sleeping soundly, or whether he was disturbed at all by killing Dolohov, who had been closer to the family than most other Death Eaters. The man hadnít been friendlyîhardly anyone would be, after nearly fourteen years in Azkabanîbut he had been respectful of Draco, had treated him like an adult where most of the Dark Lordís followers had treated him with condescension. Dolohov had even said, over the last Christmas holiday, that Draco had done well to think up the plan with the Vanishing Cabinet. ØIf this plan of yours does work,Æ heíd said, eyeing Draco with interest, Øthe Dark Lord will certainly want to see more from you in future.Æ Then heíd inclined his head to Narcissa and said, ØYouíve raised your son well, Narcissa.Æ Draco remembered the odd silence that had followed that sentence. Heíd understood the implication: Dolohov would be sorry to see Draco die if he failed. Could we have modified his memory instead? said a quiet voice in the back of Dracoís mind, but he stamped the thought back at once. That sort of thinking would get him killed. The Dark Lord had shattered Barty Crouchís memory charm on Bertha Jorkins in fourth year, and Crouch had set that charm so forcefully on her that it hadnít just held for years but deepened over time. There was no hiding from the Dark Lord. No room for nuance or affection or protection. There was only truth, and death. Draco sat up, his mouth dry, and went to get a glass of water. He moved quietly through the ground floor, glancing around as he went. The house hadnít come as a surprise, exactly, because heíd never been remotely curious about Grangerís upbringing. Still, if heíd been asked to guess what a Muggle house might be like, this wouldnít have factored anywhere into his expectations. First, most obviously and strangely, it seemed that Granger came from some money. The house was large and spacious, with wide halls and clean deep-piled rugs, and many frozen Muggle photographs hung on the walls, each depicting the Grangers on different holidays. Here they were on a French beach, and there, bundled up in their odd clothes on the slopes of the Alps, and over there, laughing in street markets in idyllic towns. And while none of the houseís furniture would ever have been found in Malfoy Manor, it had a clean, distinct style, a coordination between rooms and pieces that made Draco feel as if heíd walked into a world that had existed, and been evolving, for a long time. Obviously Draco had known Muggles couldnít all be mud-covered farmers and witch-burning mob members, like they usually were in textbooks, but his mother in particular had always associated the Muggle world with the total absence of refinement or culture. It was hard not to feel disoriented. Even more uncomfortable was being forced to think about Granger growing up here, probably dashing through the halls carrying stacks of books, reciting facts to her parents. He hadnít asked for a window into her life. He certainly wouldnít have wanted her wandering around his house, thinking about his childhood. The kitchen was a long, silvery room filled with an audible hum that made Draco uneasy. As he passed a large metal refrigerator, it rumbled, making him flinch. Had it been hexed? He checked half a dozen cabinets before he found the glasses, filled one as quickly as possible, and hurried out of the kitchen, glancing back at the refrigerator with narrowed eyes. When he reached the end of the hall, he stopped so suddenly that water leapt over the rim of the glass and slapped against the hardwood. A light had been turned on in the front room. Something was moving there. His stomach clenched, preparing him to flee, until he realized it was only Granger. She was sitting on the floor, facing away from Draco, beside the sofa where Dumbledoreís body lay. Sheíd changed into Muggle clothes, a huge, soft-looking T-shirt and a pair of black leggings. Her feet were bare. Her shoulders were moving strangely, as if she were patting something on the rug where he couldnít see. After several long seconds, though, a gasping noise came from her, and Draco realized she was crying. It wasnít shallow catching of breath, either, but intense, full-body sobbing that radiated through her in waves. Draco froze, certain that if he moved, she would notice. The longer he stared at her, the more he felt as if he had fallen asleep and none of this was happening. Was he, Draco Malfoy, actually standing here in a Muggle house, watching Hermione Granger, the obnoxious know-it-all heíd recreationally loathed for six years, sob over the corpse of Albus Dumbledore? How in Merlinís name had he ended up here? He needed to get out of this somehow. His room was so close è surely he could make it. He took a step toward the door. The floorboard emitted a deafening creak. Dracoís heart dropped. Granger whipped around. ØAh,Æ she said, wiping her cheeks hurriedly, mortification on her shining face. Her nose was abraded and her eyes swollen, so that she looked like a slightly incorrect painting of herself. Draco had a clear, sudden vision of Pansy Parkinson, her eyes filling with tears last year when heíd told her impatiently that he didnít have time for her anymore. Heíd had no idea what to say then, either. ØIîI told him about Snape,Æ Draco said too loudly. ØDumbledore. I said not to believe Snape wasîhe didnít listen to me.Æ Granger didnít respond immediately. She looked back at Dumbledoreís body before saying, ØI know.Æ Draco didnít ask how she knew. It probably involved Potter somehow. Another uncomfortable pause. Draco wondered if it would be reasonable to just go into the bedroom and shut the door. Then Granger took a shaky breath. ØDumbledore was so sure about him,Æ she said, voice small but clear. ØI donít know why he trusted Snape so much.Æ Draco curled his lip. ØBecause he trusted everyone.Æ Granger shot him an annoyed look. ØThereís no need for that tone. Youíre only here because he trusted you.Æ ØYeah, well, maybe he shouldnít have.Æ Draco registered a wary shift in her expression and rolled his eyes, sidling into the sitting room so they could keep their voices down. ØIím not going to blow up your house, Granger, calm down. Iím just saying he never behaved like this was a war, did he? No, he was too busy acting noble.Æ The annoyance on her face hardened into indignation. ØHe wasnít acting,Æ she said hotly, rising to her feet. ØI think itís admirable that he still believed in peopleís better natures in all this.Æ ØOh, well, as long as you and the rest of Gryffindor House think itís admirable. Thatíll be a real comfort to him now.Æ Draco let out a hard laugh and set his glass of water on a side table. ØI hate to disillusion you, Granger, but Dumbledore didnít act like that because he believed in peopleís better natures. God, are you actually that nave?Æ Her hands balled into fists at her thighs. ØJust say what you think, Malfoy, and stop congratulating yourself for having an opinion.Æ ØFine. The old man trusted people because he thought he knew everything about everyone. He didnít think anyone could possibly surprise him. He wasnít being noble, Granger, he was being arrogant. And now look where itís gotten him.Æ Draco cast a contemptuous glance at Dumbledore and, doing so, realized that he was furious with the dead man: furious that heíd died uselessly at Snapeís hands, rather than using his legendary skill to fight the Dark Lord; furious that he had thrown away the safety of Dracoís family; furious that he had somehow coerced Draco into believingîridiculouslyîthat he could keep them safe. And even more than that, Draco was livid with himself for believing it. Snape, at least, had kept his word. The Potions Master had waited ten full days after Lucius's arrival in Grimmauld Place to kill Dumbledore. Draco should have made use of that time, should have demanded an audience with Dumbledore and ensured the old man got them out of the country. If Dumbledore really had made plans for their long-term safety, those plans were lost now, gone from the brain that lay dead inside the skull, a pound or two of useless flesh. When he looked back at Granger, she was giving him that same piercing stare sheíd directed his way in Grimmauld Place. There was a faded shape on the front of her giant T-shirt. Her hair was even unrulier than usual, falling in tangles and puffs and stray curls over her shoulders. ØWhat?Æ he said coldly. She shook her head. ØMaybe youíre right,Æ she said, so quietly and grudgingly that he nearly didnít catch the words. There was another uncomfortable pause. ØWhat is that thing, anyway?Æ Draco asked, nodding at the black box with the glass face, which had been staring at him in his peripherals. She glanced over at it, and her expression smoothed. ØItís a television.Æ She picked up a small black box from an end table, aimed it like a wand at this åtelevision,í and pressed a button on its surface. The front of the box blazed into light. Two Muggles behind the glass were walking down a street, moving like a photograph, and even as Draco watched, the image changed to a profile view of the Muggles, as if they were surrounded by invisible photographers. They were speaking, too, though the noise from the box was too quiet for him to make out the words. ØWhat isîwhat?Æ he said, staring dumbfounded at the box. Was it a kind of Muggle Pensieve? ØWhat does it do? Why is it doing that?Æ ØWell, this is a television show." Granger sounded amused now. ØItís a serialized story that you see a bit more of every week. But it can do all sorts of things. It can tell you the dayís news any time, so you donít have to wait for the paper. And èÆ She pressed another button, and the image on the screen skewed, then flashed into something else entirely, a Muggle man pointing to a large, shiny car, exclaiming something about interest rates. ØThatís an advertisement. There are lots of those. AndîÆ She pressed the changing button again, and now a Muggle was sitting astride a bear the size of a small house. ØThatís a film. A story that takes about an hour and a half.Æ ØBut è butîÆ Draco pointed at the television. ØThat bearís been Engorged! Thatís against the Statute of Secrecy!Æ ØOh, donít be ridiculous,Æ she sighed, Øof course it hasnít been Engorged. Itís a visual effect. Filmmakers use them to make us think weíre seeing something out of the ordinary.Æ Draco didnít answer. Granger clicked the button again, and the screen went dark. ØYouíd know all this,Æ she said with exasperation, Øif you took Muggle Studies. This is exactly why it should be mandatory.Æ That brought Draco out of his trance. He blinked a few times, a dark rectangle still seared into his vision, and scoffed. ØMuggle Studies? Iíd sooner take Divination. Or a slow-acting poison. Or a kick to theîÆ ØAll Iím saying,Æ Granger interrupted, that familiar sanctimonious look on her face, Øis that you wouldnít be asking such embarrassing questions now if youíd been able to ask them in a classroom environment when you were thirteen.Æ Draco gave her a withering look. He didnít need this. ØIím going to bed,Æ he said with as much disgust as he could fit into five syllables. ØFine. Goodnight.Æ She frowned. ØWhy are you awake, anyway?Æ ØBecause taking the night air is good for my delicate humours, Granger.Æ He picked up his water and pretended to be surprised by the sight of it. ØSurely this glass has nothing to do with anything.Æ His hand was on the bedroom doorknob when her voice said behind him, ØHad è had you seen him do that before?Æ Draco stopped mid-step, sure for a moment that heíd misheard her. ØSeenîwhat?Æ ØYour father. He killed that man. Dolohov.Æ She paused. ØYou must have known him.Æ Draco turned slowly on the spot. Granger, standing at the foot of the stairs, looked apprehensive, but she didnít take the question back. Dracoís heart was beating too quickly. He was unnerved. He tried to remember if heíd said anything about Dolohov, anything at all to suggest heíd been thinking about it. He didnít think he had, but then, how could she have known? Then a rush of defensive anger overrode his doubts. It was none of Grangerís business what his father had done, or what Draco had seen him do. Why was she asking, anyway? Trying to wring information out of him that sheíd parrot back to Weasley and Potter, no doubt. And then theyíd start judging his father the same way theyíd judged him. That was all Gryffindors did, really: compare themselves favorably to other groups of people. And they thought they were so saintly. Draco summoned his usual contempt as he looked at Granger. Heíd felt it for so long that it was effortless. Maybe the girl even thought, knowing his failure to kill Dumbledore, that he was too fragile to handle the idea of death. But he wasnít like her, sniveling and bawling over something inevitable. His father had needed to kill Dolohov, or his familyís necks would have been on the line. Draco wasnít sorry about it. He wasnít. ØWhat,Æ he sneered, Øwas Dolohov a friend of yours? Wanted to ask me to leave roses on his grave?Æ Oddly, Granger didnít look angry. She looked almost resigned. ØNo,Æ she said. ØI was going to thank you for Stunning him before he killed me, actually, but I suppose I shouldnít have bothered. Youíre a real pig, you know that, Malfoy?Æ She stalked up the stairs, and he watched the textures of her hair and clothes fade into the blue-grey darkness, feeling irritated, and confused, but mostly irritated. She was misremembering the order of events. He hadnít Stunned Dolohov to save her, for Merlinís sakeîheíd done it because the man had reached for his Mark. He hadnít even known that was her, since sheíd been Polyjuiced at the time. So it wasnít as if heíd thrown her a lifeline. He shut the door to the bedroom a bit too hard. Later, on the edge of sleep, he would think of her hand thrust out to him and his family in the dingy light of the kitchen, and he would think, vaguely, feeling unsettled, that that was what a lifeline looked like. Hermione was hardly out of the fireplace at the Burrow before Harry and Ron were hugging her so hard that the breath was squeezed out of her. ØIím all right,Æ she gasped. ØReally, you two, IímîIím all right.Æ They pulled back. Ron was looking her over as if checking for missing limbs. Harry was just staring into her face, dark circles under his eyes, as haggard and sleepless as heíd looked the day after Siriusís death. Then another body slammed into all three of them, making them stagger. ØOw,Æ complained Draco Malfoyís voice. ØCould you please move the tearful reunion?Æ He lurched away from them, straightening up, and scanned the Burrowís kitchen, which was even more crowded than usual, every surface stacked with decorations for Bill and Fleurís wedding. ØAh,Æ Malfoy said, scorn written in every line of his face. ØOf course, that would require room to move.Æ Ron went scarlet. ØYouîÆ ØLeave it, Ron,Æ Hermione sighed, tugging him and Harry away. She threw an angry look back at Malfoy, who watched them go with glittering malevolence. His gaze skated across hers, and in that instant she remembered him standing with his hand on the guest bedroomís doorknob, looking utterly thrown by her question about his father. She hadnít missed that expression, although heíd pulled that look on quickly to cover it, the sneer he was wearing now. She understood now why Harry had sounded so unsettled when heíd recounted the events atop the Astronomy Tower. It was bizarre to see something other than condescension on Malfoyís face. Harry had tried to describe it, the way Malfoy had wheeled wildly between insulting Dumbledore and showing a bizarre need for his approval and protection. There was something frustrating, yet oddly enthralling, about watching him struggle to keep his persona in place. Mr. and Mrs. Weasley were already having a stiff, strained conversation with Mr. and Mrs. Malfoy, who had come out of the fire and were dusting off the sleeves of their robes, looking disdainfully around at the kitchen. A pot on the stove was bubbling, emitting a delicious smell, as a wooden spoon twirled independently in its contents. ØCome on,Æ said Ron, already heading for the staircase. ØMy room. Youíve got to tell us everything. Dad told us è Dumbledore è blimey, Hermione, what happened?Æ They holed up in Ronís room. By the time she finished recounting the story, tears were running down her face again, though she had exhausted most of her sobs last night. Ronís arm was around her shoulder, and the weight was comforting, although Hermione hadnít missed the uncertain look that Ron had shot across the bed when Harry had touched Hermione lightly on the forearm in reassurance. ØBut this changes everything,Æ Ron said as Hermione wiped her eyes. He glanced at the door and lowered his voice. ØHarry, do you reckon we can still hunt down the Horcruxes without Dumbledore?Æ Harry didnít answer right away. Hermione thought she recognized that look of reluctance. ØHarry," she said, "donít tell us not to come with you, because itís no good.Æ ØDonít worry,Æ Ron said. ØHe tried that already. I talked him out of it.Æ Hermione glanced at Harry for confirmation. He made an unhappy mumbling sound, but didnít contradict Ron. ØAnyway, I mean èÆ Ron hesitated. ØWell, weíve got no help anymore, have we? I thought weíd set out to hunt for a Horcrux trail, the three of us, and weíd sort of è check back in with Dumbledore when we found something, and heíd help us the rest of the way. Or, you know, I thought heíd give us a way to reach him, so if we ever got into real trouble, he could èÆ Ron trailed off, the tips of his ears reddening. Clearly he was embarrassed to have been so reassured by the idea of Dumbledoreís help, but Hermione understood. She, too, felt like a comforting blanket had been stripped away from them, leaving them exposed and vulnerable. ØYeah,Æ Harry admitted, Øthat was what I thought, as well.Æ Ron looked relieved. ØSo, what do we do now?Æ He paused. ØThink we should tell McGonagall?Æ There was a brief silence. The ghoul moaned in the attic. Harry reached over to the bedside table and scooped up the fake locket with R.A.B.ís note inside. ØNo,Æ Harry said quietly. ØI think itís up to us, now.Æ A lump grew in Hermioneís throat. Ron let out a slow breath, looking awed at the enormity of their task. ØHonestly, Harry,Æ Hermione said, Øthis worries me. From what you told us about the cave, weíll be dealing with enchantments and sorcery that are far beyond what any of us can do. Not just beyond what we learn at school, I mean, but the kind of advanced magic that most wizards never manage. Without Dumbledoreís help èÆ Harry met her eyes, and she knew they were thinking the same thing. The Prophecy said that Harry had powers the Dark Lord knew not, but the fact remained that Voldemort could perform feats of wizardry that only Dumbledore, and perhaps Grindelwald, had managed this century. ØOh, lighten up, Hermione,Æ said Ron, with a good try at casual bravado. ØIíve always fancied myself basically equal to You-Know-Who in terms of sheer magical power, so I reckon weíve got a good shot.Æ Harry and Hermione both grinned, but when their smiles faded, the question remained. ØWeíll find a way,Æ Harry said. ØDumbledore didnít tell anyone else besides us. He must have thought we were up to it.Æ He turned the locket over in his hand, then sat bolt upright. ØOh, no.Æ ØWhat?Æ Hermione and Ron said together. ØThe sword. Itís in Dumbledoreís office.Æ Ron swore loudly. Hermione closed her eyes. The day after Harry and Dumbledore had returned to Hogwarts with the fake locket, Dumbledore had told Harry to fetch the Sword of Gryffindor to the Hospital Wing. Theyíd intended to use it on the locket, because, Dumbledore had told Harry, the goblin-forged object had been impregnated with the Basilisk venom it had touched in their second year, making it one of very few objects that could destroy Horcruxes. Then, of course, they had drawn the locket from Dumbledoreís pocket to realize it was a fake. ØWhat if we just ask McGonagall to è to borrow the sword?Æ Ron said. ØYou think Minerva McGonagall would let us borrow the Sword of Gryffindor,Æ said Harry dubiously, Øwithout knowing why? And without insisting that we stay in Hogwarts like good little students once weíve got in touch?Æ Ron grimaced. ØWell, when you put it that way, no, not a chance in hell.Æ ØI donít know,Æ Hermione said thoughtfully. ØProfessor McGonagall might be the Order member whoís most loyal to Dumbledore. ØTheyíre saying thatís where Dumbledoreís funeral will be. It should be safe to go then.Æ ØYes, I think so,Æ Hermione said softly. She wanted to squeeze Harryís hand in comfort, but suddenly felt very aware of how close Ron was sitting. ØDid they mention when the funeral would be, Harry?Æ He avoided her eyes, the same way he always had when sheíd hoped he might talk about Sirius. ØFriday,Æ he said shortly. ØThe day after Bill and Fleurís wedding,Æ Ron said. He let out a short, humorless laugh. ØMerlin, thatíll be a change of mood.Æ They sat quietly for a moment. Then Harry glanced up at Hermione. ØAre you all right?Æ he said. ØYou saw it happen.Æ ØYeah,Æ Ron added quickly. ØIf you want to talk about it, I mean èÆ As Hermione looked at them, warmth spread throughout her. Last night, she would have given anything to speak with Harry and Ron, to be able to spill out some part of what had been roiling and twisting inside her. Now, though, she was happy just to sit with them, knowing that they would worry in her absence, and embrace her on her return, and make their awkward gestures toward intimate conversation, and look at her this way, waiting for her to find words. Maybe in time she would be able to speak. For now the offer was enough. Chapter 4: Breakthrough at the Burrow Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Life at the Burrow was driving Draco mad. He didnít know how much more he could take. Along with Potter, Weasley, and Granger, he had four additional Weasleys to put up with. The parents always looked harassed, and Billís scarred face made Dracoís stomach lurch, and Ginny kept shooting looks of such longing after Potter that it made Draco feel nauseated. Then, on top of all that, was Fleur Delacour, who glided around delivering a never-ending monologue about ze wedding and treating them all like servants. At one point she actually told Draco to help with the streamers. As if he were a house-elf. He just looked at her, appalled, until his mother gripped him by the upper arm and steered him out of the kitchen. Mostly, Draco and his parents spent their time at the foot of the gnome-infested, weed-ridden garden, as far as they could get from the others. There was some satisfaction in this. Draco enjoyed sitting in the shade of a tree as Potter and Weasley shot filthy looks at him, pouring sweat, arranging white chairs under the beating sun. Meals, though, had to be conducted outside, and for decorumís sake, the Malfoys were obliged to sit at the same trestle table as everybody else. These hours were so spectacularly awkward that Draco felt almost delirious with discomfort. Arthur Weasley in particular seemed incapable of looking at any of the Malfoys, let alone speaking to them like a normal person. ØCould someone please pass the potatoes,Æ Mr. Weasley would announce loudly to nobody, staring into the dusk, and when Lucius shoved them into his hand, Mr. Weasley would pretend the bowl had appeared there via some mysterious conjuration. Draco had settled on a kind of silent non-aggression pact with Potter, Weasley, and Granger. Weasley still gave him angry and mistrustful looks whenever possible, but Potter seemed much more preoccupied by Luciusís presence than Dracoís. This puzzled Draco at first, until he remembered that his father had fought all three of them, and Ginny, at the Department of Mysteries. As for Granger, Draco had been suspicious at first that she might act differently toward him after their argument at her house. He found himself considering the possibility that she might corner him and try to make him speak about Dolohov, or about his father; he spent time thinking up responses to increasingly invasive questions that he imagined she would ask. This was time wasted. She never approached him. Actually, of all the people in the Burrow, she was behaving the most tolerably toward him: she was brisk but polite, with no long, searching looks that suggested she was just waiting for him to press his Dark Mark and summon the Death Eaters. This was something all of the Weasleys, Potter, and occasionally even Fleur did. All in all, the atmosphere was less than terrific. Draco hoped he wouldnít have to put up with it for too much longer, and two days after they arrived, his wish came true. Shortly after dinner, Mrs. Weasley beckoned him over. ØYeah?Æ he said, unable to keep a sullen note from his voice. With the stern look on her face, he wondered what heíd done. ØArthur has discussed things with the rest of the Order,Æ she said, Øand Kingsley Shacklebolt has agreed to help transport your family out of the country. Weíll start making preparations after the wedding.Æ ØWhy not now?Æ Draco asked. She let out a short sigh. ØYou may have noticed weíre a bit busy here at the moment. And things at the Ministry have deteriorated very quickly since è since Dumbledore èÆ Mrs. Weasleyís voice quavered, and she shook her head, blinking rapidly. ØWe think multiple members of the Ministry have gone over, and with that being the case, youíre lucky Hermione was able to get you here so quickly. The Floo network is being heavily monitored already, and theyíve put up a blanket Trace on the use of Portkeys. And obviously international Apparition isîÆ Øîimpossible, yeah, I know.Æ Draco paused. ØWhere are you sending us?Æ ØWe donít know yet. Your family is well-known even abroad, which makes it difficult to find a place you can live as yourselves, very difficult indeed. But Kingsley will sort it all, Iím sure.Æ She was scrutinizing his expression. Draco didnít like the look on her face, which was verging on pity. He liked even less the visions that were swimming through his head of himself and his parents alone on a desert island, gnawing rats off kebab sticks. ØDonít worry,Æ she said. To his alarm, she made a strange move with her hand as if to touch his shoulder. Luckily, she stopped herself. ØLike I say, Kingsley will sort it all.Æ ØHeíd better,Æ Draco muttered. ØDumbledore promised to help us. So much for his impenetrable protection.Æ But Mrs. Weasley clearly wasnít listening. Her eyes had narrowed in on a point over his shoulder. Draco glanced back and saw Potter, Granger, and Weasley talking outside a kitchen window, washed in the red light of sunset. ØYou tell your parents, dear,Æ said Mrs. Weasley distractedly. Without waiting for an answer, she strode outside, and he watched her breaking up the trio, sending Granger fleeing to the bottom of the garden. Moments later, Potter and Weasley trudged inside and past Draco, heading upstairs. Draco caught a hint of mutinous conversation: Øînever get a chance to èÆ Draco felt a tug of curiosity. It wasnít like he wanted to spend time speculating about whatever sordid secrets Potter, Weasley, and Granger were keeping from the rest of the world, but there was nothing else for him to do in this godforsaken place. Also, in the 48 hours since heíd arrived here, the three of them had been acting all too obviously fishy. They were trying to talk about something, and Weasleyís mother had been keeping them from doing itîbut what? Draco hesitated, then crept up the stairs after Potter and Weasley. He realized almost immediately that sneaking around in this house would require extra precautions. Each floorboard creaked as if it were about to snap in two. He tugged out his wand, aimed it at his own feet, and whispered, ØSilencio.Æ Soon he was creeping up the stairs without a sound. The stairs branched off into rickety little hallways that divided into cramped bedrooms, but Draco thought he could hear Potter and Weasleyís voices further up. He kept climbing and finally paused at the top of the steps, at a juncture between a water closet and a closed door. Leaning close to the door, he heard Weasleyís lowered voice. Øè back to Hogwarts. Sheís just trying to make you feel guilty, mate.Æ ØYeah, well, itís working,Æ said Potter darkly. ØIf you get caught, then it puts them all in danger, doesnít it? And Ginnyís going to be at Hogwarts, right under Snapeís nose.Æ ØYeah.Æ Weasley sounded grim. ØEasy answer, then, isnít it?Æ ØWhat?Æ ØWe canít get caught.Æ Loud footsteps echoed further down the stairwell. ØOh, blimey, itís her,Æ Weasley said. Before Draco could do anything more than leap back from the door, Weasley was pulling it open, revealing a messy room plastered in Chudley Cannons memorabilia. ØYou!Æ Weasley snarled. He and Potter were both on their feet in an instant. ØWhat did you hear, Malfoy?Æ said Potter. ØIím looking for a bathroom,Æ Draco said coldly. ØYou think I care what you and Weasley talk about during your slumber parties?Æ And he walked into the W.C., closing the door behind him. But the snippet of conversation had spun the wheels in his mind. Ginnyís going to be at Hogwarts, Potter had said. So, he and Weasley werenít going back to school, then. And presumably that included Granger, too. Draco supposed he should have guessed. Hogwarts was no longer under Dumbledoreís protection. Potter would have had to be a real idiot to go back for his seventh year, essentially inviting the Dark Lord into a place that housed all his friendsîand if Potter was going on the run, no doubt Weasley and Granger would trail after him the way they always did. Still, though è why would Weasleyís mother be angry about that? Surely Potter, Weasley, and Granger would be shuttled from Order safehouse to Order safehouse? Surely the Chosen One would be given the utmost protection? No, something about it still wasnít quite right. Draco was jarred out of his thoughts by Mrs. Weasleyís voice. ØRonald Weasley! Havenít I told you a thousand times to clean out the shed? The Delacours are arriving in less than a day, and if they see it in that stateîÆ ØMum,Æ Ronís voice groaned, Øplease, tell me why the Delacours would give a damn what the shedîÆ ØYou-get-down-there-right-now!Æ thundered Mrs. Weasley. Draco looked blankly at the door. Mrs. Weasleyís outbursts were yet another bewildering part of life at the Burrow. His mother would have died before showing her anger like that. Draco remembered being eight years old and shattering a sheet of china with enchanted glazeîa family heirloom, Chinoiserie, very rare. His mother hadnít yelled. Actually, she hadnít made a sound. Sheíd gone very pale, and had looked at him in a way that knifed through him, and hadnít spoken to him for a week. Sheíd never mentioned the incident again. Neither had Draco, but something still squirmed shamefully in him when he thought of it. He heard Ron stomping down the steps, and Potter said something, sounding meek and apologetic. ØYes, thank you, Harry dear, that would be a great help,Æ Mrs. Weasley replied, though she still sounded snappish. Two more sets of descending footsteps later, the top of the Burrow was silent. Draco snuck out of the W.C., glanced down the stairs to make sure he was alone, and slipped into Ronís room. ØColloportus,Æ he muttered, wand aimed at the keyhole. The locked door would give him an extra second or two of warning. He scanned the minuscule bedroom, not knowing what he was looking for. Even if he had known, though, the place was such a bombsite that a cursory scan would have done nothing. Every single surface was layered with clothes, books, or soaring Chudley Cannons players. Then Draco spotted a rucksack beside the camp bed. It didnít look wizard-made, which meant it had to belong to Potter. Draco extracted it from a pile of shirts and started rifling through it. At first the contents seemed normal enough for a rucksackîchanges of clothes, a potion-making kit, an old photo albumîbut when he reached the front pocket, he slowed down. Inside was a shard of mirror, on which he nearly sliced a finger; he placed this gingerly on the camp bed. Beneath that was a carefully folded piece of aged parchment. Draco could tell from its delicate texture that it was decades old, but when he spread it flat on the camp bed, it was blank. ØAparecium,Æ he muttered, tapping it with his wand. Nothing happened. He narrowed his eyes, certain there was something there, hiding just out of sight. A clue to what the Gryffindors were thinking? A long and detailed list of their plans? He would come back to the parchment. He reached right down into the rucksackís front pocketîand his hand met cold metal. He drew out a glimmering golden locket. Draco lifted it up to examine the tarnished metal. He sprung its catch, and his heart beat faster as a scrap of parchment fell out into his palm. To the Dark Lord: I know I will be dead long before you read this, but I want you to know that it was I who discovered your secret. I have stolen the real Horcrux and intend to destroy it as soon as I can. I face death in the hope that when you meet your match, you will be mortal once more. R.A.B. Draco stared at the parchment. His heart had stopped beating so quickly. In fact, it didnít seem to be beating at all, anymore. Horcrux. Heíd never heard the word before, but the rest of the note gave him a good idea of what it meant. Mortal once more è whatever this Horcrux had been was something that gave the Dark Lord power over death. An Elixir of Life, maybe? Draco had heard of derivatives of the Philosopherís Stone, which were not so powerful or effective as the real Stone, but which could prolong life past its intended boundaries. And Potter had this locket. Potter had some information about the Dark Lordís secrets, about how to reduce him back to a mortal man. Whatever Potter, Granger, and Weasley were planning had to be something to do with this. Of course Mrs. Weasley would be terrified of the idea of her son getting directly involved in the Dark Lordís mortalityîbut that begged another question. Why in Merlinís name were Potter, Granger, and Weasley doing this, rather than the senior members of the Order of the Phoenix? Draco had known that Potter had a bond with Dumbledore, but he would never have guessed that Dumbledore would entrust him with this sort of information. It seemed insane, ridiculous, to put this into Potterís hands. Surely there was no way theyíd stumbled across this themselves. Draco only realized how long heíd been sitting there, immobile, staring at the note, when he heard footsteps coming up the stairs. He began shoving Potterís possessions back into the rucksack, but before he could get the note inside the locket, he heard Hermione Grangerís impatient voice say, ØAlohomora,Æ and the door flew open. ØRon, I reallyîÆ She broke off, staring openmouthed at Draco. Her eyes traveled slowly down to the locket and note in his hands, and her eyes grew very round. She moved so quickly that he had nearly no time to react. ØObliviate!Æ she cried. ØProtego,Æ he snarled, and she whirled out of the way as her own spell rebounded into the door, leaving a line of splinters peeling away from the old wood. She was flustered now, and he beat her to the next spell by a millisecond: ØExpelliarmus,Æ both their voices yelled in near unison, but Draco lunged forward onto the camp bed, out of the way, and Granger, walled in by Ronís bed, had nowhere to dodge. The spell hit her, and her wand spun out of her hand and into Dracoís. ØNice try, Granger,Æ Draco panted, rising to his feet. He shut the bedroom door hard, then advanced on her. ØWhat is this? Tell me.Æ He held up the locket. Her look of panic intensified, eyes darting from locket to note. ØI è itís èÆ She took half a step back so that her back was against the wall. ØTell me,Æ he repeated. ØNothing! Itís nothing! Itís just èÆ Then her eyes darted somewhere else. The day was sweltering, and Draco had pushed his light summer robes up to his elbows. Heíd cast a glamour over his forearm, but at certain angles, you could still see it: the red snake and skull, like a brand, ready at any moment to burn black. Grangerís eyes flicked back up to his, and Draco felt a shock of disorientation as he recognized the look on her face. In that moment, she was terrified of him. The idea didnít immediately make sense. This was the girl who had cursed Marietta Edgecombe with near-permanent disfigurement, the girl who had slapped him in the face when they were thirteen, the girl whoîwhen Umbridge had taken them captive at the end of fifth yearîhad managed to worm her way into freedom. Granger wasnít a fearful person. Yet now she was staring at Draco as if she really, truly expected him to hurt her. He moved backward from her so quickly that he nearly tripped on the camp bed. When he regained his footing, they were both breathing hard. Some of the fear had left Grangerís face, replaced by confusion. Draco shoved both of their wands into his pocket, his sense of disorientation growing. The power to induce fear was a Death Eaterís first, simplest weapon. Even when Draco was young, his father had occasionally described that kind of power to him with cool satisfaction. There are more effective methods of persuasion than gold, heíd said, though in this day and age itís not appropriate to use them, Draco. From the way his father had spoken of power like thatîand from the way the Dark Lord enjoyed his followersí terrorîDraco had thought it would feel thrilling to frighten people, to command their fear. But that, just nowîit hadnít thrilled him at all. It felt like accusation. Her look of fear told him what she thought of him, what she thought he was capable of. He spoke to stop his thoughts from overwhelming him. ØW-well?Æ he said, trying for composure. ØWhat is this note? What is a Horcrux?Æ Granger blanched. ØKeep your voice down,Æ she hissed, glancing at the door. ØWhy?Æ ØMrs. Weasley is up and down these stairs all day. She could hear you.Æ That took him aback. ØYou mean she doesnít know about this?Æ "No one does! Itís just Harry, Ron, and me, so you canít tell anyone, do you understand? You mustnít. Itís more important than you can possibly know.Æ Draco lowered the locket. ØExplain it to me, then.Æ Granger hesitated, reluctance written all over her face. ØGranger,Æ Draco said, Øyou realize you have nothing to bargain with here.Æ ØYes, yes, fine.Æ She sighed, shook her head, and sat down on the edge of the bed. ØA Horcrux is a piece of a human soul encased in an object. While that object remains safe, that person cannot die.Æ Draco examined the locket, digesting the information. The pieces were slowly clicking into place in his mind. ØSo, this èÆ But there was more squeaking from the stairs, and soon enough Potter and Weasley were shouldering through the door. Draco was ready for them. ØExpelliarmus.Æ With a sweep of his wand, both of their wands flew from their pockets and into his hand. ØOi!Æ said Weasley, grabbing for his wand as it soared away from him. ØWhat are you playing at, Malfoy?Æ Potter said. ØGiveîÆ Then the color drained from his face. He took a step back into Weasley, who had frozen. Heíd seen the locket, too. ØHe was going through your things when I got up here,Æ said Granger hopelessly. ØI è I tried to modify his memory, but I donít think even that would be enough. Voldemort can break those charms.Æ ØYeah, thatís right,Æ Draco said, Øso donít even think aboutîÆ ØThe memory modification would be for your safety, not ours,Æ Granger said impatiently. ØYou have no idea what youíve stumbled into.Æ Weasley shut the door. ØWhat should we do?Æ he muttered to Potter. ØHow much have you told him?Æ Potter asked Granger. ØNot very much,Æ she said. ØI told him what they are, thatís all.Æ ØMaybe thatís all right, then,Æ Weasley said slowly. ØHeís supposed to go abroad anyway, right? If we just let him disappear èÆ ØOh, Ron, do you really think he wonít tell his parents the instant he gets a chance?Æ Draco looked up at the ceiling in irritation. It was like he wasnít even in the room. ØExcuse me,Æ he said, Øcan we get back to the point, please?Æ He brandished the locket. ØPotter, Granger, Weasley, I donít care which of you does it, tell me what this is.Æ ØI told you,Æ Granger said. ØI already told you what a Horcrux is.Æ ØBut this isnít a Horcrux.Æ ØYes, because someone got to it first,Æ she said with exaggerated patience. ØWe donít know who, but someone was clearly working against Voldemort, and wanted to destroy the Horcrux, so they took the real one and replaced it with this decoy.Æ Draco looked between them, wondering if this was a joke. ØYou è donít know who?Æ ØBlimey,Æ said Weasley to Potter with mock sadness, Øsix years of Hogwarts education and he canít even read.Æ He looked at Draco, dripping dislike. ØWhat, do you think the letters R.A.B. are someoneís full name?Æ This was too good. Draco closed the note back into the locket and laughed. ØYou really donít know, do you?Æ They all looked at him for a long moment. ØWhatís that supposed to mean?Æ Potter said. ØWhat do you think it means, Potter? I know who wrote the note.Æ Weasley and Potter traded incredulous looks. ØCome off it,Æ Weasley scoffed, though he sounded uncertain. But Granger's eyes were piercing into Draco. ØI think heís serious,Æ she said quietly. It was infuriating, Draco decided, to be spoken about as if every tiny thing he said needed verification. But it was worth it to see the frustration on Potter and Weasleyís faces as their eyes traveled from the locket to Dracoís face and back. ØWho?Æ Potter ground out. The single syllable seemed to take great effort. ØWell, well,Æ Draco said, enjoying himself now. ØIf thatís the tone youíre going to take èÆ ØJust tell us,Æ Weasley snapped. ØYou donít know what this could mean.Æ ØThatís what she keeps saying,Æ Draco said, nodding to Granger. ØYou donít know what this could mean, you have no idea what youíve stumbled into, you donít know how important this is. Hereís an idea. ØI thought that was the only thing you cared about.Æ Draco considered for a moment. ØFine, then,Æ he said with a shrug, dropping the locket back onto the camp bed. ØI suppose I will ask my parents about it. Maybe we can figure it out together. Maybe we can enlist your parents, too, Weasley.Æ ØNo,Æ Weasley said, moving to block the door. He looked from Potter to Granger. ØI think weíll have to tell him.Æ ØYes. Tell him,Æ Draco said. ØIím waiting.Æ Granger and Weasley both glanced to Potter, ceding the conversation to him in that uncanny soundless way they had. ØFine,Æ said Potter, though he looked furious. ØWe è weíre tracking down the real Horcrux this year. Weíre not going back to Hogwarts. Weíre going to find it and make sure itís destroyed, so that Voldemortîwell, like the note says. So that heís mortal again.Æ Draco hesitated. He had the sense Potter wasnít telling him everything, but maybe that was just because Potter looked like he was about to punch something. ØAll right,Æ Draco said slowly. ØSo, why you three? Why not someone with, I donít know, any qualifications at all?Æ ØDumbledore left the job to Harry because of the Prophecy,Æ said Granger. ØAnd we canít tell anyone else. The more people know, the riskier it is, because if Voldemort finds out his secretís been compromised, then he can take more steps to protect thîprotect it.Æ Draco caught her mistake. ØProtect them,Æ he repeated. ØThereís more than one, then, is there?Æ Potter threw an exasperated glance at Hermione, who returned a look of apology. ØYeah,Æ Potter said roughly. ØThereís more than one. That all you wanted to know? Can you tell us who R.A.B. is, now?Æ Draco paused, wondering whether to push the point, but Potter and Weasley both looked near violence, so he said, ØWouldíve thought youíd know, Potter. He was your godfatherís brother, wasnít he?Æ As much as he hated the sight of the three of them, Draco had to admit there was something satisfying about watching their expressions transform, lifting out of dislike and frustration into excitement and comprehension. ØRegulus,Æ Potter breathed. ØRegulus ArcturusîÆ ØAll that summer before fifth year we were in there,Æ Weasley said, slapping himself on the forehead, Øand we didnít rememberîÆ ØWhy didnít Dumbledore think of him?Æ said Granger. ØSirius hated talking about his brother,Æ Potter said. ØHe only told me about him once, and he changed the subject pretty quickly, too. He wouldnít have wanted to tell Dumbledore about his dead Death Eater brother, not while he was living in that house.Æ He paused. ØBesides, Regulus was only a Death Eater for a year or two before he died. Sirius probably wouldnít have thought it was worth mentioning.Æ Draco remembered the clippings about Death Eater activity heíd seen in Regulusís room while heíd been sleeping there. He remembered the photographs of the slight Slytherin boy, a Seeker like him, excited by the idea of joining the Dark Lord, a cause of Wizarding pride. And if the clippings suggested the group was too violent, that was only because the writers didnít understand the importance of the cause. ØHe did join the Death Eaters, then?Æ Draco found himself asking. All three Gryffindors looked at him as if theyíd forgotten he was there. ØYeah,Æ Potter said. ØBefore he left Hogwarts.Æ Something sat in Dracoís chest that felt both heavy and empty. ØAnd heîhe turned against the Dark Lord to destroy this Horcrux?Æ ØThatís what the note says, isnít it?Æ said Weasley. ØHow do you know heís dead?Æ Draco said. They all hesitated. Then Potter said, ØWell, he disappeared.Æ ØThen they didnít find a body. He could still be alive.Æ Draco didnít like the way they were all looking at him, suddenly: Weasley and Potter with palpable awkwardness, Granger with the look sheíd worn that night at her houseîthe look that suggested she understood too much. ØHeís dead, Malfoy,Æ Granger said quietly. ØIf he were in hiding, he would have come back when Voldemort lost his body.Æ She was right, of course, but Draco hated her for saying it. For one brief second, he thought heíd learned of a Death Eaterîa Death Eater his age, no lessîwhoíd managed to escape. But then, Regulus Black accepted his death, Draco thought. The note said it all. Black had gone after the Dark Lordís Horcrux, heíd brought it on himself. If heíd just been smarter, maybe he would have survived. ØDo you think the locket is still in Grimmauld Place?Æ said Granger, sitting back down on the bed. ØMalfoy, you lived there,Æ said Weasley. ØWhat díyou reckon?Æ Draco looked resentfully at Weasley, but could think of no real reason not to answer. ØNo. I lived in his room when I was there, I didnít leave that house for four weeks, and I had nothing better to do than look around every inch of the place. There wasnít a locket anywhere.Æ ØRegulus could have stowed it wherever he wanted,Æ Potter muttered. ØThere was no reason for him to leave it in the house.Æ ØYeah, there was,Æ said Weasley. ØHis house was full of Dark stuff, wasnít it? It wouldíve been the perfect hiding place. If I were him, I would have mixed it in with all that junk we took out before fifth year.Æ ØThatís a good point,Æ Granger said. ØIt would have fit perfectly with è with èÆ She trailed off. Her eyes had gone round againînot with fear this time but with epiphany. ØThere was a locket,Æ she whispered. ØWhat?Æ said Potter. ØIn the cabinet in the drawing room. A heavy golden locket that no one could open è do you remember? We all passed it around.Æ Now Weasley and Potter were looking dumbstruck, too. ØBut thenîÆ Potter rounded on Draco, who flinched back. ØMalfoy. You said itís not there now.Æ ØNo,Æ Draco said, unsure how he felt about being roped into this. All the same, it was hard to stop watching the three of them try to solve their mystery, for some reason. ØHang on, though,Æ Weasley said. ØKreacher nicked a load of stuff that summer! Malfoy, youíre sure the locket wasnít in that room in the back of the kitchen? Near the boiler? Heís got a foul sort of nest.Æ ØRon,Æ Granger said reproachfully. ØOh, thatís what that was,Æ Draco said with disgust. ØNo, there was only an old book in there.Æ ØWe have to ask him, though,Æ said Potter, his eyes alight. ØMaybe he saw it at some point, or maybe he knows which load of things it got thrown out with.Æ He looked around, as if expecting the elf to spring out from behind a Chudley Cannons poster. ØKreaî!Æ ØNo!Æ Draco and Granger yelled at the same time. Granger actually lunged out and fastened her hand around Potterís wrist. ØWhat?Æ Potter said, startled. ØWhat is it?Æ ØDeath Eaters got into Grimmauld Place, Potter!Æ Draco said, his body feeling cold from the closeness of the near miss. ØIf you think they havenít put a magical trace on that elf by now, youíre as mad as he is.Æ ØIím sure theyíll be watching him all the time, Harry,Æ Granger said, letting go his wrist. ØIím sorry, but I donít think we can ask Kreacher anything.Æ ØThenîthen what do we do?Æ said Potter furiously. ØSearch every rubbish dump in London?Æ There was a full minute of silence. Weasleyís face was screwed up in concentration, and Granger had closed her eyes, her lips moving slightly. After a long moment, Draco realized he was actually, seriously trying to think of an answer to Potterís dilemma, too. He tried to pull himself out of itîgetting involved with Horcruxes had killed Regulus Black, for Merlinís sakeîbut the suggestion was out of him before he could stop himself: ØThereís the Scavengerís Guild.Æ ØThe what?Æ said Granger and Potter at the same time. Both were regarding him with surprise. ØThe Scavengerís Guild,Æ said Weasley. He sounded wary, begrudging, but there was excitement in his eyes. ØThatís an idea, actually.Æ Potter and Granger still looked nonplussed, so Weasley went on. ØSometimes Muggles pick up magical objects and donít realize what they are, because lots of Wizarding objects are spelled to look like rubbish if a Muggle comes into contact with them. Galleons and Sickles and Knuts are all like thatîif you drop one and a Muggle picks it up, itíll look like an old Muggle penny, wonít it?Æ ØWill it?Æ said Potter, looking startled. ØWell, yeah,Æ Weasley said. ØSo, anyway, Muggles wind up chucking loads of magical stuff in the bin, and it ends up in landfills. The Scavengers go through, rescue anything magical, polish it up a bit, and resell anything good. Theyíve got a stall in Diagon Alley, shows up about once a month. Fred and George told me last year they got a good bit of Lightweight Lead off them.Æ ØAll right,Æ said Potter with rising excitement. ØAll right, then. Weíll try that.Æ He turned to Draco, opened his mouth, and made no sound whatsoever. Draco knew Potter had been about to thank him instinctively for the suggestion, but Potter clearly couldnít make himself form the words. ØWell,Æ Draco said stiffly. ØEnjoy your search.Æ When he was halfway out the door, Granger said, ØMalfoy.Æ He turned back. ØYou è you arenít going to tell anyone, are you?Æ she asked tentatively. He surveyed her anxious expression. The girl sounded so frightened half the time. It was like she was expecting him to bite her nose off for asking a simple question. ØWhy would I?Æ he said. ØThatís not a real answer, Malfoy,Æ Potter said. Draco sighed. ØYes, it is.Æ He rolled his eyes. ØGryffindors. Ask one simple question about motive and youíre all completely lost.Æ With that, he returned downstairs and traipsed into the yard. For sleeping room, Draco and his parents had been given a grubby tent that opened up into a miserable little apartment. The first time theyíd entered it, his mother had been momentarily speechless with horror. ØAmazing,Æ Draco had said. ØThey found a place even less fit for human habitation than the hovel itself.Æ But neither of his parents even cracked a smile. Draco didnít know why he bothered sometimes. Draco had been teasing Ron Weasley about his house for so long that it felt stupid to be really surprised by what the Burrow was like. Still, he couldnít imagine growing up in a place like this, never having any privacy, everything used and used and used to shreds. It did, at least, make him appreciate the feeling of coming outside into the evening, when the sky opened up overhead, and he could breathe. And it was true, too, he thought begrudgingly, that Mrs. Weasleyís cooking was delicious. He felt a bit traitorous for thinking it. Merlin knew his own mother couldnít have done so much as boil a potato. Of course, sheíd never had to, since she wasnít a glorified house-elf. When he entered the tent, his parents were having tea at the spindly table in the main room. They greeted each other quietly, and then Draco, already tired, prepared for bed. As he settled beneath the thin sheet, he missed home. He missed the manorís many large and quiet rooms, its lush, sweet-smelling gardens, its fountains and tastefully decorated wings. He missed Hogwarts, too: the lush velvet and dark leather of the Slytherin Common Room, the library and its imposing shelves, the sweeping lawns. Now that they were halfway through the summer holiday, he was starting to feel the usual itch of wanting to return. He wondered if it would go away after September first. As his eyes drooped shut, he wondered if his friends had adjusted to the idea of his death yet. Had Pansy cried over him? Surely she had; she cried often and vigorously. This would probably have ruined her summer. Normally she couldnít shut up about the summer holidays, she loved them so muchîthe events the Parkinsons threw at their estate, the days spent sunning herself by their swimming pool, the luxurious trips to wizarding outposts near New York and Hong Kong. Her family were particularly close to the Goyles, so sometimes they went on joint trips, and Crabbe came along, too, and Pansy spent the holidays daring the duo into more and more outrageous escapades, and sheíd lavishly recount their adventures on the Hogwarts Express back to school. The memories were bright and clear for a while. Then they faded like old photographs, and Draco found himself thinking of a golden locket, and a Slytherin Seeker, looking uncertainly out of a picture frame, whose body had never been found. By the day of the wedding, Hermione felt as if her skull were filled with a swarm of bees. She liked the Delacours very muchîshe certainly got along better with all three of Fleurís family than with Fleur herselfîbut the Malfoys had already been three too many people to live at the Burrow, and the Delacours stretched the situation to breaking point. Maybe she wouldnít have felt so put-upon if she hadnít been secretly packing all of her, Harryís, and Ronís belongings into her small beaded bag. Sheíd finally perfected the Undetectable Extension Charm the day before, and had lost several hoursí sleep feeding her own possessions into it, library of books, catalog of potion ingredients, and all. The wedding ceremony itself was the first time Hermione had felt relaxed in days. Fleurís radiance seemed to make the whole marquee unnaturally silent, and Hermioneís eyes stung as the couple exchanged their vows, and then, finally, kissed. It was a sort of catharsis, she thought, as the band started up. Tomorrow, they would all travel to Hogwarts for Dumbledoreís funeral, but for now, they had music, and family, and friends. She even let herself have a conversation with Luna about Snorkack conservation efforts. Every so often, she caught sight of Harry, in disguise as Barny Weasley, and the three Malfoys, who had Polyjuiced into a butcherís family from the village. The Weasleys had quietly but industriously circulated the idea that the Malfoys were a trio of standoffish family friends, ones who had been living on a remote island off the coast of Iceland for several years and had lost the ability to socialize. So far, this had held up reasonably well, although Viktor had struck up a brief conversation with Lucius Malfoy about Icelandic sheep herding. It felt strange to see Viktor again. Fourth year seemed impossibly long ago now; the return of Voldemort had separated it out into a previous life that she could never reenter. Hermione could still feel wisps of what sheíd felt for Viktor if she focused hard on drawing up the memory, like closing her eyes and trying to recreate an exact image on the backs of her eyelids. But she knew it was past. When Ron asked her to dance, she didnít think twice about Viktor. This is it, she thought, as they whirled and twirled with the laughing crowd. This is when Iíll understand what I feel. This is when it all becomes clear. Yet when a slow song began, and Ron moved close to hold her waist and take one of her hands in his, she felt a leaden sensation in her stomach, rather than the excited, nervous flutters sheíd felt during the start of the summer. A hard lump formed in her throat. Unable to look up at Ron, she laid her head on his chest instead, looking toward the edge of the tent, where Harry was having a seemingly intense conversation with Ronís Aunt Muriel and Elphias Doge. But she wasnít thinking about Harry, either. She was thinking about Dumbledoreís funeral. About Dumbledore falling from the sky, drifting through cloudsí ragged edges. She was so afraid, and touching Ron like this, being close to him, was only exacerbating it. She didnít want to lose him. She didnít want to lose Harry. In that moment, she worried that she was confusing the fear of loss for love. She worried that she could feel nothing at all except the fear of loss, and that, until all this was over, that was the only emotion she would be allowed, in various states of volume and intensity. Even worse was the feeling of Ronís heart beating fast against her ear, andîwhen the song ended and they pulled apartîthe tender look on his freckled face. From that expression, she knew that while theyíd danced, heíd felt what sheíd wanted to feel: a kind of rapture, of euphoria, the sedative feeling that everything was finally right in the world. But all sheíd been able to register was the looming threat of death and attack. It made her feel impossibly alone to realize how far away theyíd been from one another in those minutes, even as theyíd held each other. Ron must have seen something amiss in her face, because he said, ØSomething wrong?Æ ØIím just a bit thirsty,Æ she managed. ØIíll be back in a moment.Æ It was too late, she thought as she stood on the outskirts of the marquee, folding her warm fingers around a cup of cold pumpkin juice. The idea of Ron taking her aside and kissing her no longer made her feel a thrill, only a new kind of anxiety. Those first golden weeks of July had been their chance, and the chance had passed by. The window had slipped shut. She couldnít tell whether Dumbledoreís death had changed things or clarified them. Maybe his death had shaken her so badly that she could no longer feel excitement about romantic possibilities. Or maybe his death had stripped away the complications, all the guilt and history, and made her realize that what she felt for Ron and Harry alike was the desperate, elemental need for them to stay alive. Either way, she felt what she felt now, and she didnít think she could go back. She settled into a seat without paying attention. ØGranger,Æ said an unfamiliar voice. She looked up to see the Polyjuiced Malfoy at the same table. He was a burly boy with black hair around their age, slightly shorter than Malfoy but nearly twice as broad in the shoulder. ØOh. Hi,Æ she said faintly. ØPumpkin juice?Æ ØCanít,Æ he said sourly. ØIím supposed to stick with this. Another dose in a few minutes.Æ He raised an opaque flagon that held his Polyjuice. He was slouched in the chair, legs stretched out, eyeing the dance floor with evident disinterest. ØI expect youíll be glad to leave when this is over,Æ said Hermione, unable to conceal a note of disapproval. She thought the Weasleys had been remarkably accommodating of the Malfoys, all things considered. ØGlad?Æ said Malfoy, with such disdain that it still somehow sounded precisely like him, even in another boyís voice. ØOh, absolutely. Iíll be thrilled to leave behind every last speck of my life.Æ ØYou make it sound as if youíre being forced.Æ ØDo I have a choice?Æ She sighed. ØObviously you have aîÆ She broke off abruptly. Something silvery had appeared in the center of the dance floor. A messenger Patronus, a lynx, had settled to the ground. As a hush spread among the guests, the lynx spoke with the voice of Kingsley Shacklebolt: ØThe Ministry has fallen. Scrimgeour is dead. They are coming.Æ Screams erupted. Thenîsuddenlyîthe crack, crack, crack of multiple Apparitions. Before Hermione had even fully understood what was happening, spells were flying. A jet of white light flew toward their table. Hermione had barely registered it before something was slamming hard into her side, sending her flying out of her chair. Thenî Bang. She looked up, gasping. Their table was smoking and splintered. Malfoy was sprawled beside her. Heíd knocked them both out of the impact radius, and now he was scrambling to his feet, wand in hand. Hermione followed, scanning wildly for Harry and Ron. She saw them almost immediately, already stumbling toward her out of the dust. And thereîbehind themîthe cloaked figures who had Apparated in. They were wearing masks, immobilizing everyone they could see. Death Eaters, here, under the wedding marquee. Malfoy staggered back, his face drawn in terror. And Hermione, in the same instant, saw his curly hair start to change, to turn white-blond. She sprinted to his side, seized him by the arm, and grabbed Ronís elbow with her other hand. ØHold on, Harry,Æ she yelled, allowing him a second to grip Ronís arm before she turned on the spot. They squeezed into the dark space between places. Then they were bursting out onto a wild mountain path that overlooked Hogsmeade Village. Chapter End Notes tumblr | instagram Chapter 5: At theHogwarts Gate Chapter Notes Hello sweet pals! Within minutes of their trespass beyond the tree line, the air grew unnaturally still and silent, and soon the trees were so thick, towering so high overhead, that it might have been nighttime. Adding to the eerie atmosphere was the fact that they had to whisper, not wanting to attract the attention of anything that lived in the forest. Hermione was sure that if the centaurs encountered them, they would remember Harry and her from their disastrous trip into the forest with Umbridge in fifth year. What had that been about? It had probably been repayment for saving him and his family in Grimmauld Place, she thought as they wended their way between a pair of thorny bushes. He probably didnít like the idea of having a life debt to a Mudblood. She was surprised to find that the thought stung. Hermione had all but stopped feeling Malfoyís insults over the years. She remembered the foul comments, of course, remembered how heíd turned to his mother last year in Madam Malkinís and said, if youíre wondering what the smell is, a Mudblood just walked in. But she knew, had known for years, what Malfoy wasîa bigoted little worm without two scruples to rub togetherîand so nothing he said managed to hurt her. In fact, because of the way Harry and Ron reacted to the comments, they served as a reminder of how lucky she was to have kind, loyal friends who would have died rather than subscribe to that sort of prejudice. But now, the idea of Malfoy thinking those things made her feel oddly sore. Hermione didnít know why. Was it the fact that sheíd saved him from capture by the Death Eaters twice now? Did she feel that, having done him a favor that he frankly had never earned, he owed her his respect? Or was it mere exposure? Certainly sheíd seen more of Malfoy over the last week than she ever had before. Heíd even slept in her house, for Merlinís sake. Yes, that was it. Heíd slept in her house, and had seen the pictures of her and her family that hung in the halls; heíd slept in her guest bedroom, and had drawn a glass of water from the same kitchen where sheíd celebrated birthdays and Christmases; heíd seen the library full of books that her mother and father had built up over the course of her life. He had seen her at the Burrow, eaten meals with her, watched her laughing with Harry and Ron over dinner with that cold, sullen look of his, always quiet, but still there, still watching. It was disturbing to imagine that someone could come that close to you, see your everyday life at close quarters, and still despise you for nothing more than existing. But now that Hermione was mulling over that bizarre night at her house, she realized for the first time that he hadnít made a single crack at her family then. Sheíd spent the whole night silently waiting for him to do it. Certainly his parents had met her lowest expectations right away, whispering to each other about what their options might be rather than staying under a Mudbloodís roof. But Malfoy himself hadnít done it, though heíd had endless opportunity. Hermione didnít know what all this came to, but she forced herself to stop trying to find Malfoyís invisible outline in the dark. Donít give him any power, she told herself fiercely. Donít expect anything out of people like that.
