Letís go.Æ Chapter End Notes tumblr | instagram Chapter 6: TheLostDiadem Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Three-foot-long plume quills as soft and fluffy as trails of smoke, their nibs planted into vases like the stems of flowers. Networks of spiderwebs so thickly draped with dust that they looked like the most finely spun lace. Towers of bookshelves, and dated chairs with upholstered backs, and accordions of curtains falling ten, twenty, thirty feet to the floor, cataracts of brocade. Cool light emanating from a source impossibly high above, from an uncertain ceiling, as if the moon were hidden behind several layers of thick paint. Hermione was wide-eyed. It was impossible to take everything in at once, and soon her neck was sore from how many times her head swiveled upon it. Harry, who had been inside the Room of Hidden Things once before, was striding determinedly ahead, scanning for the Horcrux, but Malfoy, who had spent cumulative weeks in the Room last year, had his hands deep in his pockets, and his eyes were flicking from side to side only reluctantly. ØThis place is unbelievable,Æ Ron whispered. ØI know,Æ Hermione whispered back. It felt wrong to speak at any real volume, like they were in a library full of ancient books. Harry glanced back at them. ØHermione, anything?Æ She leapt. The Marauderís Map was held in her limp right hand. Sheíd been tasked with keeping an eye on Snape, since Harry had seen the Horcrux before and would be more use trying to spot it undistracted. But at the sight of the Room, the Map had slipped her mind. She scanned it. ØAll clear,Æ she said, hurrying to catch up with Harry and Malfoy. ØHe and Hagrid are still in the middle of the Forest.Æ ØI donít get it,Æ Ron said, jogging up behind them too, frowning up at the mountains of objects. ØHow can You-Know-Who have thought he was the only one to know about this place, when itís full of stuff?Æ ØYou can find it without knowing what you've found,Æ Harry said. ØFred and George and Dumbledore all found the Room by accident. Iíd bet all this is from students who only came in here once and then couldnít find the place again.Æ ØThat doesnít explain this,Æ Ron said, pointing at a four-by-four stack of faded yellow sofas that crisscrossed upward like logs at a bonfire. ØOh, honestly, you two.Æ Hermione sighed. ØStudents didnít do this. Didnít you read any of the literature I wrote for S.P.E.W.?Æ Harry and Ron exchanged a guilty look. ØEr,Æ Harry said. ØWell, if you had read the pamphlet I distributed in September of last year, you would know that the Hogwarts house-elves are responsible for repairing, maintaining, and disposing of all objects that have been misenchanted, disenchanted, hexed, or otherwise magically discombobulated, even atîeven at!îgreat risk to their own physical safety.Æ ØRight,Æ said Ron. ØAnd that means è what, exactly?Æ Hermione saw, with a hot jolt of irritation, that he was trying not to grin. Ron had gotten much better about house-elves over the yearsîheíd grown fond of Dobby, at any rateîbut whenever theyíd visited headquarters over the summer, heíd still treated Kreacher little better than Sirius had, and he still acted like this sometimes. ØItís not funny, Ron,Æ she said hotly. ØIím saying that all thisÆîshe waved around at the mountains of detritusîØrepresents hundreds of years of enslavement. Itís just one more way that itís all shoved out of sight, so wizards donít have to think about what theyíve doneîwhat theyíre still doing.Æ Harry looked slightly troubled, Ron, undecided. Malfoy, on the other hand, finally stopped feigning deafness and said, ØGranger, would you stop sermonizing? House-elves donít even want freedom.Æ He jerked his pointed chin at the surrounding piles of objects. ØYou couldnít get this lot to quit if you offered them all the money in Gringotts. They love serving wizards.Æ Ron looked more disturbed by this than by anything Hermione had said, and Hermione knew why. How many times had Ron used that exact line of argument in their fourth and fifth years when they discussed elf rights? She couldnít help feeling even angrier and more disappointed by this. Was that really what it took to see how abhorrent your own complacency wasîto hear it come out of the mouth of an enemy? With a savage sort of relief, she turned her irritation with Ron onto Malfoy instead. ØOh, really?Æ she said, her voice rising. ØHow would you know what elves want, exactly? Have you ever spoken to an elf beyond ordering them around? Tell me: in all the years that Dobby lived at your house, how many times did you ask him anything about his thoughts, feelings, or opinions? Say itís more than zero and Iíll swallow Fiendfyre.Æ ØIîthatís not theîÆ ØYes, it is the point! At least most other wizards can claim ignorance. They can all say their house-elves were thrilled to be doing their bidding, because unfortunately, most elves havenít been given the opportunity to learn what freedom would feel like. But you donít even have that much. Dobby hated being enslaved. He was thrilled to be free.Æ Malfoy stood his ground. ØOh, yeah? And why do you care? Let me guess: itís yet another way to show off how much better than other people you are. I didnít realize your hero complex was even worse than Potterís.Æ Hermione let out a high, strained laugh. ØWhy do I care?Æ Her voice rose. ØWhy do you think I care? Why do you, Malfoy, think I might give a damn about how people treat other thinking, feeling creatures that most wizards think are beneath them? You donít think that might have any personal relevance for me?Æ There was a long, ringing silence. Hermione hadnít expected the words to land with such a palpable thud. Ron looked stricken. Harry, for the first time, had stopped scanning for Horcruxes. They were both staring at her as if theyíd never realized any of this for themselves. Something strange, meanwhile, was happening to Malfoyís face. His curled lip was twitching downward as if he were losing control of his facial muscles. His grey eyes were shadowed, difficult to read, but they kept straying away from her and then flying back onto her, as if it were painful to look at her, yet impossible to look away. ØLetís split up,Æ Hermione said shortly. ØItíll be faster that way. HarryÆîshe glanced at him, the one she was least frustrated withî Øletís go that way. You two go right.Æ Ron and Malfoy didnít even argue. They turned and walked off, several feet apart, not speaking. Harry was looking uncertainly at Hermione. ØIím sorry,Æ she said as they set off to the left, staring down at the Map but not really seeing it. ØI è I didnít mean to waste time, we should have been searching for èÆ ØNo,Æ Harry said quickly, Øitís all right. Er. Itís fine. Really.Æ Hermione swallowed past the lump in her throat, her eyes burning. She hated this tendency sheíd developed over the last two years, this new inability to discuss anything she cared about without wanting to cryîespecially since her tears always panicked Harry, and made Ron assume the somber look of someone attending a wake. ØTheyíre still in the Forest,Æ she said, tucking the Map away. ØPlenty of time.Æ ØGood,Æ Harry said. ØThatísîÆ He stopped in his tracks. His face had gone blank. ØHarry?Æ she said. He pointed ahead. Then he burst into a sprint toward an open cabinet, inside which stood a bust wearing a wig. Hermioneís heart leapt. She ran after him, hot on his heels, and soon they were breaking out of their run together and standing for a moment, staring up at the Lost Diadem. ØIs that èÆ she whispered. ØYeah,Æ Harry said. ØI can see the inscription.Æ She moved up to his side and saw it too: Wit beyond measure is manís greatest treasure. Harry reached toward it, but she grabbed his elbow. ØYouíre just going to touch it?Æ ØWhat else am I supposed to do?Æ ØJust wait one moment.Æ Hermione drew her wand and aimed it at the tiara. ØHexia revelio!Æ The tiara sat, glinting dully, motionless. ØFinite incantatem,Æ she said, flicking her wand, and then, with a complicated twist into a slash that sheíd practiced throughout July, she added, ØSkadus dicoperare.Æ Again, no reaction. ØWhat was that supposed to do?Æ Harry asked. ØIt should have shown if there were any major curses.Æ ØAll right, then.Æ Harry reached out more tentatively, and Hermione held her breath. His fingers met the tiara. Nothing happened. No horrible curse, no scream of pain from Harry. Hermione let out a long sigh, her shoulders slumping in relief. ØDramatic,Æ Harry said. She met his eyes, and they both broke into relieved chuckles. ØHey, you two,Æ Harry said, raising his voice. ØWeíve found it!Æ Ron gave a triumphant shout from the other end of the aisle, and soon he and Malfoy were jogging up to Harry and Hermione. ØIs that it?Æ Ron said, eyeing the diadem. ØDoesnít look like much, does it?Æ ØWell, itís been sitting here Merlin knows how long,Æ Harry said. ØItís got her House motto on, look.Æ He passed it to Ron, who held it for a moment, looking uneasy. ØI can feel it." ØI would hope so, Weasley,Æ muttered Malfoy, eyes fixed warily on the Horcrux. ØNo, you prat, I mean I can feel something inside it,Æ Ron said. ØHermione, look.Æ He proffered the diadem, and their eyes met for an awkward instant. He looked away at once, his ears turning red, and Hermioneís cheeks felt momentarily hot. The Horcrux distracted her. The instant she took hold of it, she felt what Ron was referring to: the sense of life within the diadem, pulsing like a sluggish heartbeat. Hermione shivered. ØI donít like it,Æ she said. ØWhat should we do with it?Æ ØPut it in that bag of yours,Æ Ron said. ØI donít know,Æ Harry said. ØWe canít lose this.Æ ØYeah,Æ Ron said, Øbut what else are we going to do? ØThatís an idea.Æ They all looked at her like sheíd suggested they adopt a Blast-Ended Skrewt. ØYou donít think thatíd be conspicuous at all, Granger?Æ said Malfoy with heavy irony. Unlike Ron, he seemed to have no trouble speaking to her, or meeting her eyes. His face had returned completely to normal, as if the argument had never happened. Hermione was strangely relieved by this. At least she didnít have to tiptoe around him. ØI donít mean wear it to keep it safe,Æ she huffed. ØYou said Ravenclaw designed this to enhance intelligence. We may even have a brainwave about the locket or the cup. Maybe thereís something weíre not seeing.Æ ØI donít know, Hermione,Æ Harry said, sounding uneasy. ØYou remember Dumbledoreís hand? Thatís what happened when he put on the Horcrux ring. He told me he only survived the curse becauseÆîhis expression souredî ØSnape helped him.Æ ØWell, Snape might not have really been trying to help him,Æ Ron reasoned. ØTrue,Æ Harry said slowly, Øbut Iím still not keen on the odds of Voldemort justîÆ Ron and Malfoy both flinched. ØStop saying his name, would you?Æ Ron said. ØNo,Æ Harry said bluntly. ØDumbledore used his name, and so will I.Æ ØYeah, butîÆ ØLook,Æ Hermione broke in, ØI donít think we have much time. Dumbledoreís will would have been executed on Monday. That means there have been three days already for the Death Eaters to realize Dumbledoreís bequests are at the Ministry and take them. If they deliver the sword to Voldemort, weíll never get it back.Æ She drew a sharp breath, another horrible thought striking her. ØAnd he already knows the diary was destroyed! What if he decides to make the sword into a replacement Horcrux, to make sure his soul is still in seven partsîand this time with an object from all four Founders, the way he originally planned?Æ The boys were all looking at her with varying shades of dread. Harry was the first to react. He nodded slowly, his face so pale that his lightning scar stood out like a brand on his forehead. ØI think youíre right, Hermione,Æ he said hoarsely. ØI think thatís exactly what heíd do if he got his hands on it. ØGive it here. Iím supposed to be sick anyway, arenít I? If something awful happens to me, you can è can deliver me back to my family, and we can just pretend the Spattergroit got worse, andîÆ ØNo,Æ Harry said. ØIf anyoneís putting that thing on, itíll be me.Æ ØOh, Harry, donít be ridiculous,Æ Hermione said, Øyouíre the symbolic head of the Order now that Dumbledoreís gone! You could carry on hunting the Horcruxes without me. Dumbledore left this information to three people. If I died, there would èÆ He swallowed. ØWell, there would still be three, wouldnít there?Æ Another extremely loud silence fell. She, Ron, and Harry all hesitated. Then, at the same time, they looked at Malfoy. There was alarm on Malfoyís face. In the room's ghostly light he looked even paler than usual, his thin mouth nearly colorless, as if he were freezing. It was only then that Hermione remembered what heíd said at the wedding: that he wasnít keen to leave the country, that he didnít want to leave his life behind, that he didnít have a choice. He seemed to be realizing before her eyes that there was a choice. ØIím not èÆ he started, but he didnít seem to know where to go with the sentence. After a moment, he said, with a good try at his usual sneer, ØIím not putting that thing on. The three of you can act the hero all you want. You canít make me touch it.Æ It eased some of the tension. ØAh, well,Æ Ron sighed, Øat least heís reliable. Now hand it over, Hermione.Æ He made a grab for the diadem, but Hermione darted backward. She threw her beaded bag to Harry, who caught it instinctively. ØIf there are physical wounds,Æ she said as quickly as she could, Øthereís Essence of Dittany in there. If the symptoms resemble poisoning, Iíve packed a bezoar, too. For anything else, Madam Pomfrey is in the Infirmary.Æ ØHermione, no!Æ Ron yelled, lunging toward her. She was already placing the diadem atop her head. Time seemed to slow to a crawl. Ron broke out of his stride, he and Harry staring at her with nothing less than terror. Malfoy had made a strange, convulsive motion, but now heíd gone still, his eyes fixed on the diadem. Nobody breathed. At first Hermione felt very little, not even the coldness of the diadem, since her hair was cushioning it. But then she felt something. Not pain, but easement, as if a seed of stillness were planted in her mind, flowering outward, cooling the agitated heat in her thoughts. The idea of Voldemort with Gryffindorís sword, the weight of finding the remaining Horcruxes, the hurt and anger sheíd felt toward Ron and Malfoy and even Harry, the anxiety of what might, even now, be happening at the Burrow è these things remained in her consciousness, but they seemed to separate like the ingredients of an antidote in a cauldron, precipitating downward into individual containers, where she could analyze each on its own. Itís a Horcrux, she reminded herself. Itís dangerous. But it didnít feel dangerous. It felt as if her brain had been removed from a clamp for the first time in monthsîmaybe for the first time in years. ØIím all right,Æ she said quite calmly. Ron and Harry unleashed a breath as one, seeming to sink in on themselves with relief, as if someone had let air out of their bodies. But Malfoy kept staring at her, the shock and disbelief in his expression unchanged. ØThe sword,Æ Hermione said. ØWeíre trying to solve two different problems with it. The first is the need to destroy Horcruxes. The second is the possibility of Voldemort transforming it into a new, active fifth Horcrux. We have to think about the two problems separately. They might have separate answers.Æ ØAll right,Æ said Harry. ØWhich one do we solve first?Æ His words sounded far away. Hermione didnít answer. She often felt as if she could read lines out of a textbook in her mind, but at the moment, she felt as if whole mental libraries were reopening to her, regurgitating vast quantities of detail, facts connecting to each other like mapped constellations. Why should they be limited to the sword? Almost certainly there were other goblin-made weapons that had imbibed Basilisk venom, or Callacot blood, which, sheíd read, was also toxic enough to destroy a Horcrux. The Callacot had gone extinct in 1829, but there were at least nine known cases of wizards slaying them. Two of those wizards were prominent enough to have displays of their possessions in Wizarding museums, one in Dresden and one in Nairobi, and although the records hadnít provided detail on how they slew the Callacots, it was entirely possible that they could have done so with goblin-made swords, especially since swords as surrogate wands had been at the height of fashion in the 1700s. ØHermione?Æ Ron said tentatively. She gave her head a little shake and closed her eyes. Nairobi and Dresden were problematic as solutions, since leaving the country would mean serious complications. She withdrew from that particular tranche of information and refocused. Which other avenues might be available? Fiendfyre? Most likely too dangerous, unless they could find a fully sealed and warded test environment, like the ones supposedly housed in the Department of Mysteries or St. Mungoís Severe Isolation Ward. Could the Room of Requirement itself serve as a sealed environment, she wondered? Maybe, but Hermione didnít know the nature of the Roomís enchantments, and risking it would risk the destruction of Hogwarts itself. Not Fiendfyre, then, or at least, not yet. Callacot blood: unless an obsessive conservationist still had a body of the extinct animal, magically preserved, it was an unlikely option. The Curse of Devouring: definitely not ideal, as the caster would suffer a slow, wasting disease. Basilisk venomî A snippet of conversation rose immediately out of her mind. Ron, sitting on his bed during one of their Horcrux discussions, saying, ØOh, well, lucky weíve got such a large supply of Basilisk fangs, then. I was wondering what we were going to do with them.Æ A thrill shot through her. Her eyes opened. She felt as if she had been thinking for a long while, but it couldnít have been more than a few seconds. The answer was obvious. So obvious, in fact, that she laughed. ØWhat?Æ Harry said as she took off the diadem. ØWe donít need the sword to destroy Horcruxes,Æ she said. ØWeíre right above the Chamber of Secrets. The Basilisk is still there.Æ Harry and Ron let out exclamations at the same time that echoed in the Room. ØLetís go,Æ Harry said, already turning for the door. They all sprinted toward it together. ØYou sure youíre all right?Æ Ron said, looking over at Hermione. ØIím fine, Ron. I feel completely normal. Actually, IîÆ She broke off. Sheíd glanced down at the Marauderís Map, and horror lanced through the diadem's residual calm. ØWhat?Æ she choked out, breaking out of her run by the door. ØWhat is it?Æ Harry said sharply. Hermione shoved the Map toward him. The boys bent their heads over the parchment. Empty for the summer, the map of the castle only wore a few black dots. One, Severus Snape, was traversing the grounds at what seemed to be an impossible speed. Four others were nearly at Hogwartsí front doors. Geoffrey Dewhirst, Alecto Carrow, Amycus Carrow, Corban Yaxley. Malfoy was the first to speak. ØWe need to get out of here. Now.Æ ØHow did they know?Æ Hermione whispered. ØYou donít think Hagrid gave something away?Æ Ron said. Harry looked up from the Map, his face drawn. ØIt doesnít matter how,Æ he said. ØWe have to get to the Chamber. We havenít got much timeîSnapeís moving so fast, he must be on a broom.Æ ØThe Chamber?Æ Malfoy said in disbelief. ØWe need to run! We can stay there until theyíve gone.Æ ØWhat, and starve to death down there? Snape lives here, Potter!Æ Hermione clutched more tightly to the diadem, and as if in response, another pulse of blissful calm seemed to issue from it, up from her arm, into her mind. Surely, the instinct said, there was no need to rush. This was no real risk, because if the Death Eaters confronted them, she could put on the diadem and fight them, and she would do so with profound competence. She shook her head, feeling disoriented, and slid the diadem into her beaded bag. Her hands felt empty without it. ØHarryís right. We should try to get to the Chamber while weíre here. Itís going to be a risk to get out of the castle either way.Æ ØAll right, all right, fine,Æ Malfoy snapped. ØBut Iím taking the Invisibility Cloak.Æ Ron let out a disbelieving laugh. ØNo, you bloody well arenít.Æ ØI canít be seen, Weasley!Æ Malfoy hissed. ØIf any of them see me, Iíll have to kill them, do you not understand that?Æ There was the briefest pause. Malfoyís lips were pursed, and he ran one hand through his white-blond hair, andîfor an instantîhis eyes flew over to Hermione, who felt a strange jolt low in her stomach, remembering the way his hand had jerked in hers when his father had killed Dolohov. In the week since, had he been steeling himself to do the same? Malfoy was already looking back at Ron and Harry, both of whom looked repulsed by his words. ØThereís no other way,Æ Malfoy said, his face slightly wild now. ØWhat else am I supposed to do? If the Dark Lord finds out Iím alive, heíll know my parents are alive, heíll find us, heíll make an exampleîÆ Harry thrust the Cloak at Malfoy, who fell silent. Hermione understood: If they gave Malfoy both the Invisibility Cloak and the Marauder's Map, he could sneak off at any time and make sure that he alone got out safely. The moment was uncomfortable. No matter how heíd sneered, and mocked, and played up his reluctance, Malfoy had helped them reach this point. But to trust him with two of their greatest tools? Hermione couldnít help thinking of the loathing on Snapeís face as heíd murdered Dumbledore. Then Malfoyís hand came from nothing and took the Map from Hermioneís grip. ØI know,Æ he said coldly. ØThanks for the reminder, Potter.Æ Three Disillusionment Charms later, they were racing down corridors and leaping down staircases two at a time. Draco was trying his best to direct them to the bathroom on the second floor, but the Gryffindors kept pulling too far ahead, unable to see each other well enough to cluster more closely. It was like herding cats. ØWhereís Snape?Æ Potter asked, breathing hard. ØIn the Great Hall,Æ Draco said. Their Disillusionment Charms held. ØCome on,Æ Draco said, leading them down a secret stairwell behind a tapestry. But after several more tense minutes, as they crept through the second floor hall toward the girlsí bathroom, Draco realized two dots were converging on them. Alecto Carrow and Dewhirst were at opposite ends of the hall, closing slowly inward. ØStop,Æ he hissed. ØWe canít keep going.Æ ØMalfoy, itís right there,Æ Potter whispered. ØI know! On the Map, the black dot reading Corban Yaxley entered a passage through a portrait near Ravenclaw Towerîthe other end of which, apparently, spilled out directly ahead. Yaxley, seeing Dewhirst at the opposite end of the hall, instinctively whirled around and said, ØFinite incantatem!Æ The flash of red-gold light rushed down the hall toward them. Potter was the only one to react in time. ØProtego!Æ he yelled, and the light ricocheted back at Yaxley, who dodged. ØItís him!Æ Yaxley roared. ØItís Potter!Æ He repeated the spell as they sprinted back down the hall, away from the bathroom, toward the tiny corridor that was their only chance. ØThis one, this one,Æ Malfoy gasped, throwing himself toward the door. It burst open, and they piled through just as Yaxleyís spell tore past, down the hall. ØColloportus!Æ Granger panted, aiming her wand back at the door even as she rushed down the tiny hallway with them. ØWhere does this come out?Æ Potter said, no longer bothering to whisper. ØThe dungeons,Æ Draco said. ØSnape still in the Great Hall?Æ ØNo,Æ Draco said, tearing around a corner. ØHeís in the Entrance Hall now, right in front of the door.Æ ØThatís fine,Æ Potter said in something like a snarl. ØLet him try to stop us.Æ Draco couldnít believe him. ØDonít be an idiot, Potter. Snape could take all four of us with his wand hand behind his back. Heís been the Dark Lordís protg since he was our age.Æ And now, behind them, new footsteps were echoing down the corridor, and Draco could hear Alecto, Yaxley, and Dewhirstís voices mixing into a confused swirl. ØWhat do we do, what do we do, what do we do,Æ Granger was saying frantically. It came to Draco in a rush of fear that felt like inspiration. ØMy common room! The Slytherin common room. Theyíll never look in there.Æ ØPerfect,Æ Granger gasped as they burst out into the dungeons. ØWhich way?Æ ØThis way,Æ Potter said, going left. ØIîwhat?Æ Draco tore after his outline. ØHow do you know which way it is?Æ Weasley let out a slightly hysterical laugh. ØNot the time!Æ Soon they were skidding to a halt in front of the patch of stone wall Draco knew so well. ØHydrus,Æ he said, praying they hadnít changed the password in the last few days of school. The stone door sank back and slid out of sight, and relief flooded Draco. Clutching a stitch that pounded painfully in his side, he staggered through the passage and out of the way of the Gryffindors, who followed hot on his heels. The door shut behind them and melted back into a solid wall. The others faded back into view, and Draco took off the Cloak, panting hard. Heíd expected it to stifle him, but air seemed to glide through the fluid material somehow. He let it flow over his hand, studying it. He would never tell Potter, but he didnít think heíd ever seen the Cloakís equal, and Borgin and Burkeís kept an entire rack of Invisibility Cloaks fully stocked. It must have been a brand-new Cloak. Maybe theyíd even bought it for this Horcrux hunt. Once heíd caught his breath, Draco let himself look around the common room. The sight overwhelmed him in a way he hadnít prepared for; he felt a bittersweet rush so strong he could practically taste it. His eyes roved first onto the sofa in the corner, the spot that was his, Crabbeís, and Goyleís, where theyíd spent countless afternoons listlessly trying to do homework, mostly failing, and winding up making fun of their teachers until their sides hurt from laughing. Then there were the intricate engravings around the dark stone fireplace, where Pansy had leaned the first night theyíd kissed, fourth year. He remembered the way sheíd looked at him through her dark eyelashes as if she knew everything he didnít, but that was just Pansy. Her lips had been as soft and yielding as flower petals and in truth neither of them had known what they were doing at all. Theyíd broken apart after a few minutes just to snicker about it together. With a lump sticking hard in his throat now, Draco looked up to the light, to the bank of long, greenish windows. The afternoon heíd taken his first O.W.L., heíd curled up where the sun shone through the lake water, in that far windowsill whose stone was curved like a hammock, and heíd listened to the other Slytherinsí voices drifting cheerfully around the common room as heíd fallen into a well-needed nap. Nobody had disturbed him. Heíd been worried about things like grades, then. These remnants of a normal life felt almost ridiculous, now, like a clichd storybook heíd read in childhood. He couldnít pinpoint when that world had slipped out from beneath him. For a while, heíd blamed Potter for the whole thing, for his fatherís disgrace in the Department of Mysteries, but even before that, there had been transformations, hadnít there? Yes, looking back, he could see the signs. Until the end of fourth year, his parents had written him twice a week when he was at school, keeping him up to date with happenings at the Ministry, international Wizarding news, and any major financial moves either of his parents were considering. But over the course of fifth year, their letters had dwindled to once weekly, then once every two weeks, then once a month. They sounded distracted and vague in writing, and when he came home for the Christmas holidays that year, they made him stay in his room whenever other Death Eaters visited the manor. Heíd hated that, being treated like a child, until the night heíd heard screaming from down the hall. He couldnít remember what heíd told himself to explain it, but he must have made up some story, something to fit with the predestined trajectory of the rest of his life. He must have told himself that person deserved it, and that he would never scream that way, like he was afraid, because he would never fail, because he was his fatherís sonîso what was there to fear. Theyíd all caught their breath, now. ØThis wonít buy us much time,Æ Potter said. ØIf they tell Snape they lost us in the dungeons, he might check here.Æ ØI donít suppose thereís a secret Slytherin way out of the castle?Æ Weasley said. ØJust in case you lot wanted to turn tail and flee?Æ Draco didnít dignify that with a response. Granger was frowning. ØIf we were in Gryffindor Tower, we would be able to summon brooms and go out the window.Æ ØVery useful,Æ Draco said. ØLetís open one of thoseÆîhe gestured lazily at the windows îØand flood the school, shall we?Æ Potter and Granger studied the windows as if actually considering it. ØHow deep do you reckon this is?Æ Potter said. ØThe lightís relatively clear,Æ said Granger. ØAnd the dungeons go much deeper. This canít be more than fifteen or twenty feet.Æ ØThe lakeís not that bad,Æ Potter said. ØThe merpeople are a bit weird, but theyíre all right, really. Hermione, do you think you could do that Bubble-Head Charm that Cedric and Fleur used for the Second Task?Æ ØLet me see if I have it.Æ Granger sat on one of the black leather sofas, took out her beaded bag, and began rummaging inside. ØHold on,Æ said Weasley, Øhow are we supposed to stop the place from flooding, though? Not that Iíd mind giving the Slytherins a surprise, butîÆ ØWeíre not flooding this room,Æ Draco said through gritted teeth. ØYou are not touching this place.Æ Weasley looked at him with surprise, but didnít say anything. ØNo, no, of course we wonít flood it,Æ said Granger, sounding distracted, still pulling endless books out of her beaded bag and somehow feeding them back into its tiny aperture. ØHold on è ah, here it is!Æ Sheíd extracted a book called Adventurerís Charms for the Wild. ØI tried a few of these charms for our O.W.L. è hopefully the Bubble-Head Charm is a derivative of one of them.Æ As she paged through the book, Weasley settled on another sofa, scuffing his shoe against the vast Persian rug that spanned most of the common room. ØI still donít understand how they knew we were here,Æ he said. ØYeah,Æ said Potter. Draco turned away from them. Here it came: they were going to accuse him of bringing the Death Eaters here, either accidentally or purposefully. And when he tried to defend himself by asking why he would summon Death Eaters only to help them escape, they would accuse him of getting cold feet at the last second, the same way heíd failed to kill Dumbledore. He could hear every word of the imagined argument. His heart was beating too quickly, anger and defensiveness building up in him just from the thought of it. But Potter spoke in a quiet, worried voice. ØI couldnít still have the Trace on me, could I?Æ ØImpossible, mate,Æ Weasley said. ØIt breaks at seventeen, thatís Wizarding law.Æ Potter let out a slow breath, and Draco realized Potter had been worried he was endangering them all. Draco thought about that ridiculous argument the Gryffindors had in the Room of Hidden Things, each bidding for the opportunity to put on the diadem, to put themselves at risk. He thought about the fear and defiance on Grangerís face as sheíd nestled the tiara into her flyaway hair. He glanced over at Granger now, paging with some enthusiasm through Charms for the Wild. With a book in front of her, she looked calmer than she had since the wedding. She could have died in the Room of Hidden Things. He couldnít understand how she could have made that decision, how she could have forced herself to put on the Horcrux. To Draco that seemed like the act of someone with nothing left to live for, but hadnít she also seemed terrified by the idea of Potter and Weasley getting hurt? If she cared about them so much, wasnít it her responsibility to stay alive, to make sure they all got out of this unscathed? What good was it to die for the people you loved, if you forfeited living for them instead? ØHere!Æ She looked up and met Dracoís eyes. He looked away immediately, his heart beating too quickly. He knew he was being stupid, knew she couldnít see on his face that heíd been trying to puzzle through her thought process. Besides, even if sheíd wanted to know his thoughts, he could shut her out immediately. Bella had called him a natural. That memory still comforted him, still made him feel proud. When did Bella ever speak like that about anyone? ØGood news,Æ Granger announced, shooting to her feet. ØIt is a derivative of the Clean-Air Charm. I thought it might be.Æ Potter looked up from the Marauderís Map. ØHermione, I think theyíre telling Snape. Youíll have to hurry.Æ Grangerís excitement turned at once, palpably, to anxiety. She bit her lip, her shoulders bunched up, and her fingers tightened around her wand. Draco threw a disdainful look at Potter. What a stupid move, telling Granger she suddenly had a time limit, when she was so obviously someone who performed poorly under stress. Draco made a show of yawning and cast a casual look back at the door. ØDonít be stupid, Potter. Yaxley doesnít know thereís anyone in the castle but you, and why would you be able to get in here?Æ He glanced at Granger. ØNo, I think weíve got ages to watch you bungle this charm, Granger.Æ It worked. Her shoulders dropped, and she glowered at him. Some of the fear had gone out of her. ØIím not going to bungle anything." ØYeah,Æ said Weasley, leaping to her defence. ØWhen have you ever seen her mess up a spell, Malfoy?Æ ØFine, then. ØAenai,Æ she said, and with the second wand motion, a perfect circle traced around her own head, she completed the incantation: ØEnacerus.Æ At once, a glassy, iridescent bubble swelled to life around her head. It couldnít contain her hair, but when she breathed in, she smiled triumphantly at Draco. He shrugged. ØWe all get lucky sometimes, I suppose,Æ he drawled. Granger rolled her eyes and went to repeat the charm on Potter and Weasley. Finally, she approached Draco and raised her wand. He couldnít help it. He flinched. He wondered if he would ever be able to get rid of the instinct, facing a lifted wand. Grangerís annoyingly perceptive eyes lingered on his face, then moved down to his right hand, which had jerked toward the pocket where he kept his wand. But she didnít comment on the motion. ØReady?Æ she said, her voice echoing inside the bubble. He nodded. Sheíd barely cast the spell when Potter said, ØMalfoy, I was right. Theyíre headed this way.Æ He and Weasley were both on their feet, their faces distended by the bubbles that surrounded their heads. Draco's own bubble made the whole common room glossy and skewed, but the air inside was cool and refreshing, in motion, as if he were outside on an early spring day. ØFine, then,Æ Draco said, striding to the window. ØHow exactly are we going to get out?Æ ØBanishing Charm,Æ Granger said, sweeping up her bag. ØStrata duro!Æ Steps erupted out of the wall, leading up to the high windowsill. They all rushed upward, and once they'd crouched on the windowsill, Granger waved her wand again. The steps collapsed with a series of resounding thuds back into the stone. Now Draco could hear dull voices from outside the common room. ØYou three cast the Banishing Charm,Æ Potter said. ØIíll cut a way through. All right? è Now.Æ Draco, Weasley, and Granger swept their wands out in an identical motion. She stuffed it into her beaded bag, which she tapped, whispering, ØImpervius!Æ Then they crawled out of the window and dived into the lake. The Banishing Charm held as they turned back, treading water. ØWingardium Leviosa,Æ Potter said, his voice faint and distant through the lakewater, and the thick square of glass lifted back into place. With a quick ØReparo,Æ it was mended, as if they had never been there. Potter looked back at them and nodded. Draco lifted his wand as Granger and Weasley did the same, and the lake crashed into place against the window of the common room, tugging them all momentarily back toward the castle. Then they were swimming in the opposite direction, kicking hard upward and outward. Draco looked back as they swam away. The Slytherin common roomís bank of windows looked ghostly, shedding weak pale light into the dark water. They looked like a memory. Within a minute they had disappeared altogether. They spent that night in the mountain cave outside Hogsmeade. The downside: it was a cave. The bright side: it had enough room that Draco could pretend he wasnít sleeping in the same place as the Gryffindors. They had settled on one side of their fire, and he on the other. They had Transfigured rocks into pillows and leaves into blankets, and through the entrance to the cave, the night sky was a brushstroke of stars. The moment Hagrid had let them back out through the Gamekeeperís gate, Draco had wanted to Apparate as far away as possible, but Granger had insisted that they stay here, where they knew they could find covered shelter, and Transfigure themselves tomorrow to buy supplies in Hogsmeade. ØYou donít think thatís risky?Æ Potter had said, still shivering from the lake as he performed a Drying Charm on his robes. ØItís less risky than anywhere else,Æ Granger replied. ØThe place is so full of people, I donít think weíll be seen, much less remembered. Whatís another couple of guests?Æ ØFine,Æ Draco said. ØAs long as we contact the Order first thing tomorrow.Æ The others had exchanged an exasperated look, which he ignored. It wasnít until they settled into the cave that Draco realized how exhausted he was. It seemed impossible that the Burrow and the wedding had been only a handful of hours ago. Yet when he lay down and closed his eyes, rather than falling asleep immediately, he became aware of the Gryffindorsí whispers. They probably thought they were too far away for him to hear, but the arc of the cave ceiling was such that the sound traveled up and over to him. ØWish the Cloak were a bit bigger,Æ Potter was saying. ØIt would be useful.Æ ØRemember when all three of us could fit under it?Æ Weasley whispered. ØYes, there seemed to be a lot more room in first year,Æ Granger murmured. Draco could hear the smile in her voice. First year? Draco thought. Six years for an Invisibility Cloak was into old age. He would have expected the charms to have halfway unraveled by that point, but from the condition of Potterís cloak, it could have been woven last week. ØItís going well so far, isnít it?Æ Weasley said. ØDay one of the quest and weíve already got a Horcrux. Keep going at this rate and You-Know-Who will be finished by next weekend.Æ Granger and Potterís stifled laughter shivered up and over the cave wall. ØShame about the fangs,Æ Potter said. ØBut we know theyíre there. If we donít manage to get the sword, we can always try to come back to Hogwarts, or get word to someone at school that we need one.Æ There were murmurs of assent from the other two, and then a pause. Then Weasley said, ØLook, Hermione, we wanted to è Iím sorry. Both of us are, all right? We didnít realize that was what the SpewîI mean, S.P.E.W. thing meant to you.Æ Dracoís stomach dropped. He no longer wanted to eavesdrop. He didnít want to think about the fury and disdain in Grangerís face when sheíd demanded to know how heíd treated Dobby, or about the hurt heíd seen in her eyes, even the shine of tears, when sheíd mentioned personal relevance. And just hours ago heíd been floating across the lake, telling himself sheíd never seemed to care when he called her Mudblood, that nothing heíd said had really made any impact, so why did it matter, in the end. He didnít want to listen, and yet he found himself training his ears harder than ever. I know you two care about me, so itís not about that. I didnít start S.P.E.W. because of how Iím treated, or how Muggle-borns are treated. I started it because we should all care about how everyoneís treated. And sometimes I wish you would take that a bit more seriously, even when itís è when itís èÆ ØWhen itís Kreacher?Æ Potter said. ØExactly.Æ It was ridiculous, Draco thought. Completely ridiculous, caring to the point of tears about house-elves. He did have memories of Dobby from before Hogwarts. The elf had scurried around the house wringing his hands and polishing surfaces and punishing himself, and as a child Draco had found it all hilarious: the look of the elf with his batlike ears, and the tea towel heíd had to wear, and the way heíd had to do anything Draco asked, no matter how ridiculous or excessive. Draco remembered enlisting the elf to play make-believe games with him when Crabbe and Goyle werenít there. He would be the hero, and the elf would be the evil monster encroaching on his territory, and when Draco inevitably conquered the monster, he would order Dobby to do things representing his defeat, like sitting in a closet in the dark for four hours. Draco would go and check on him halfway through just to sit in the strange, weightless feeling of his own control. Now, he realized, the memories gave him an uncomfortable twinge. But he shoved that discomfort back. Heíd been seven, maybe eight years old at the time. How should he have known that the elf was miserable? How should he have known that the elf could even feel misery? If Dobby had ever let on anything other than enthusiasm and cooperation, his father would have ordered him to iron his hands. Really, said a disgusted voice in his mind, are you going to spend time feeling guilty about how you treated an elf when you were a child? After all, if he felt guilty about Dobby, of all things, what was next? Accidentally landing that Katie Bell girl in St. Mungoís, or poisoning Weasley? Draco had never allowed himself to dwell on those things, because what would feeling guilty have done, exactly? He knew they would get better, or die, whether or not he felt guilty. Besides, heíd had his own death to worry about. There were reasons I acted the way I did, he thought with a kind of righteous indignation. Was he supposed to feel guilty for prioritizing his own life? Was that what people like Granger wanted, for everyone to prostrate themselves at each otherís feet, never thinking of themselves? Was that how sheíd put on that diadem? He closed his eyes more tightly, so that strange shadows of polygons erupted on the backs of his eyelids. Clear your mind, said Bellatrixís voice, training him in Occlumency. Clear everything away. Close yourself to the world, to guilt, to doubt, to shame. He succeeded, except for one thing, which lingered no matter what he tried. Grangerís face as she lowered the diadem onto her head, crowning herself, terrified and defiant. Chapter End Notes whew, y'all, when i say slow burn good lord do i mean it tumblr | instagram Chapter 7: TheMinister's Eulogy Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes When Hermione woke up, Malfoy was gone. His absence was the first thing she saw. You shouldnít be seeing him anymore!Æ ØThatís not important,Æ Harry said impatiently. ØHermione, Iím telling you, heís after wandmakers. This means something. The night I left Privet Drive, the night we thought you were èÆ He shook his head. ØWell, I saw him that night, I fought him again, and my wand acted funny, all right? It performed a spell without me. It beat him. And later that night, he tortured Ollivander. He was furious about it. è But donít you see? Heís already got Ollivander, and now heís going after Gregorovitch.Æ ØI donít èÆ Hermione felt overwhelmed. ØFirst of all, your wand couldnít have performed a spell without you. Thatís just notîÆ ØIím telling you, itîÆ ØYou three.Æ They all looked up. Malfoy was standing at the mouth to the cave. ØWhat?Æ Ron said roughly. Malfoy jerked his head wordlessly toward the exit. ØWeíre not ready to leave yet, Malfoy,Æ Harry said. ØItís not that. Youíll want to hear this.Æ They exchanged glances, then stood and followed Malfoy. From the path outside the cave, they could see the crowd gathered across the Hogwarts grounds, a sea of black robes. The voice carrying across the water was audible, even crisp, offered up by the lake. Hermione realized immediately that this wasnít the same voice that had been delivering the eulogy. While that voice had been old and wheezy, this one was younger, more powerful, and flatly nasal. Øè assure you that Dumbledoreís supposed conflicts with the Ministry were invented by opportunistic reporters. Prior to Dumbledoreís death, we were working closely with him to find a path forward for the safety of the Wizarding World.Æ ØWhat?Æ Harry said. Hermione hushed him, trying to listen. ØDevoted headmaster that Albus Dumbledore was,Æ the voice went on, Øhe believed that the safest course of action for our children was to implement a mandatory attendance policy at Hogwarts School. Here, they can be protected by a score of capable teachers, as well as Ministry-regulated enchantments and wards. In the weeks to come, the Ministry will send registration forms across the country to ensure that every child in Wizarding Britain is accounted for, requesting location and basic demographic information.Æ Malfoy made a noise under his breath. Hermione glanced his way, but his eyes were fixed on the tiny figures in black at the front of the funeral. ØMoreover èÆ The announcer sighed. ØWe are loath to bring this news to such a somber occasion, but we do so knowing that the Ministryís immediate and forceful action is imperative in these uncertain times. We are saddened and disturbed to say that our investigation has revealed that Albus Dumbledoreís death was not accidental, as was initially believed. We can confirm that the cause of his death was the Killing Curse. While èÆ Sounds from the crowd drifted indistinctly across the lake, though with unmagnified voices, they sounded ten times farther away than the announcer. ØWhile,Æ he repeated, Øwe have little information about the circumstances of Albus Dumbledoreís death, we do know that on the night in question, he was accompanied by Harry Potter, an unstable individual who has been followed by mysterious deaths and supposed accidents for years èÆ An indistinct cry came out of Hermioneís mouth before she could restrain herself. ØNo,Æ Ron roared. Beside them, Harry had gone as rigid as a board. Hermione rounded on Malfoy. ØWho is that?Æ she demanded. ØWho is that, talking?Æ ØPius Thicknesse,Æ Malfoy said. ØUsed to be Head of the Department of Magical Law Enforcement. Apparently heís taken over as Minister for Magic.Æ Thicknesse was still speaking. Øè famous for his reappearance at the end of the Triwizard Tournament holding the dead body of a fellow student, Cedric Diggory. Potter was also involved in incidents resulting in the death of one Hogwarts teacher, Quirinus Quirrell, and the permanent memory loss of another, renowned author Gilderoy Lockhartîthese incidents being particularly notable for the fact that Potter was only eleven at the time of his involvement with the former, twelve in the case of the latter. ØAt fifteen, Potter and several accomplices broke illegally into the Ministry of Magic to steal a valuable, classified artifact, a intrusion that involved several known Death Eaters and resulted in the death of the posthumously exonerated Sirius Black. We have reason to believe that Potter made a similar raid on the Ministry as recently as this week, and only months ago he was nearly expelled from Hogwarts School because of the violent use of a Dark curse on a fellow student, which nearly resulted in yet another death.Æ Hermioneís fingernails were biting into her palms. Were they talking about the Sectumsempra incident? ØHow did they knowîÆ Ron began. ØSnape,Æ Harry said dully. ØSnape told them all this. How to frame it. Everything.Æ He let out the most humorless laugh Hermione had ever heard. ØPretty good case against me, isnít it?Æ Hermione was so angry that she could hardly breathe. How dare they use Siriusís death against Harry? And Cedric? After everything Harry had been through, everything heíd had to endure, to hear these cruel, evil people twisting it all è ØHarry,Æ she said, her voice shaking, Ønobody with any sense will believeîÆ But Thicknesseís speech had ended, and Harry was already stalking back into the cave. ØCome on,Æ he said over his shoulder. ØWeíre wasting time.Æ Ron and Hermione exchanged a look. ØIíll talk to him,Æ Ron said in a low voice. Hermione nodded and watched Ron jog into the cave. She wouldnít be much consolation to Harry, anyway, with her fists trembling in rage. She couldn't lie in this state, and she knew that the crowd was likely full of people who would buy every foul word out of Thicknesseís mouth. Beside her, Malfoy was still watching the scene across the water. The service had come to a close, and a long, white object had formed, gleaming, at the front of the crowd. Even from here, Hermione could tell that it was a magnificent marble tomb, glowing like fire in the summer sun. Hermione stared into the white spot and felt her rage evolving into a furious kind of yearning, which hurt as badly as if some soft tissue in her was ripping. Only then did she realize how much, exactly, she wished everything were different. She wished she had urged the Thestral forward at just the right time to save Dumbledore. She wished that Scrimgeour had held on for one more day, and that they were sitting in that crowd, able to say goodbye. She wished the path before them did not look so long and full of thorns. Then a sound broke across the water. It was the high, tuneful cry of a phoenix, majestic and yet filled with the ache of loss, or maybe majestic because of the penetrative force of that feeling. It was the sound of the ache that unfolded colorfully in Hermioneís chest like the petals of a flower or a catastrophic bruise. As the lament soared upon the air, something red and gold streaked across the blue sky like a brand, and Hermioneís breaths began to stutter as she watched Fawkesís trail fade, this last bright thing that remained of the Hogwarts, and the life, that she had loved. She thought of her parentsí excited faces in Diagon Alley, and the four-poster beds in the girlsí dormitory, and the dusty smell of the library, and Harry and Ron laughing with her on the lawn, and her cheeks were suddenly wet and hot; she was making small sounds. Malfoy half-turned toward her, but she didnít shield her face. She didnít feel that any of it was meant for hiding. She wrapped her arms around herself and felt her diaphragm jerk, felt her ribs hold hard around her heart, felt her body somehow containing it all. As the phoenix song reached its crest, she found herself looking over at Malfoy. He had turned back toward the white tomb, or maybe toward Hogwarts itself. His lips were parted and he looked discomposed. He was breathing like someone who had run very quickly away from something indescribable. He looked younger. The lament faded. He turned his face and met her eyes, and Hermione had no idea what to say. There was a long moment of silence like the moment between the casting of a spell and an explosive impact. She physically could not break it. Instead she lowered her head by a matter of small degrees. He did the same. Five minutes later, they had Apparated away with the others, and Hermione knew they would never speak about what had passed between them in that instant. It was sealed off in the past like the rest. Crack. They were in a small but luxurious office. Papers were piled high on two identical desks, and behind them, two identical people leapt to their feet with a shout. Draco turned to the windows and flicked his wand. The curtains slammed together, blocking out the sunlight. Potter did the same to the door, which swung shut, concealing the plaque that said: Weasleyís Wizard Wheezes: Fred & George Weasley, Owners. The twinsí shock didnít last long. They leapt onto their brother first, wrapping him in a fierce hug. Then they moved on to Harry and Hermione, clapping their backs, looking them over for injuries. They gave Draco a glance and a grudging nod, and he nodded back unthinkingly, still unsteady from the phoenix song. ØYou four seem all right,Æ said George, sounding impressed. ØGot away from the wedding okay, then, did you?Æ ØDidnít you?Æ said Ron. ØWe stayed,Æ Fred said. ØDidnít want to leave the family, but theyíre all fine.Æ ØThe Death Eaters saw the ghoul,Æ George said. ØYou did a decent job on it, too. They didnít want to get within ten feet of the thing. Disgusting.Æ ØCheers,Æ Ron said. ØWhat about everyone else?Æ said Granger anxiously. Her face was still red, her eyes puffy and swollen. ØWell, they interrogated us all, didnít they,Æ said Fred. ØFor a few hours.Æ At the look on her face, he added hastily, ØNot outright torture, or anything. There was a lot of threatening, and George got a smack for being mouthy, but he deserved that. Idiot.Æ George grinned. ØIn my defense, he did look like a hairless cat tried to breed with a potato.Æ Draco finally found his voice. ØWhat about my parents? ØHavenít we already said that none of our safehouses are safe anymore? Death Eaters smashed up every Order-connected house in the country yesterday. She wonít have taken them anywhere we know, and she didnít come back to the Burrow, so weíve no bloody idea.Æ ØYou donít know?Æ His voice was rising now. ØThen how am I supposed to get to them? Theyíre going to be watched every single second of every day.Æ Draco felt as if a cold cloak had fallen over his shoulders. He saw, again, the image of his Polyjuiced parents scanning the crowd at the wedding, frantically looking for him. Now they were gone. He had no way of finding them, no way of knowing whether they were safe, no way of communicating to them that he was safe. His mother was probably going mad with worry. For some reason, he found himself glancing toward Granger. She was watching him with what he now recognized as concern. It was the same way sheíd watched Potter during Thicknesseís speech. Draco looked quickly away, trying to think of some other plan. For Merlinís sake, heíd figured out a way to sneak Death Eaters into Hogwarts under Albus Dumbledoreís watch. Surely he could solve this. ØHang on,Æ Ron said, though Draco hardly heard him. ØIt sounds like youíre saying the Order is finished.Æ ØNot exactly,Æ said George. ØThose of us who are still pretending to be respectable members of our new Death Eater-led societyÆîhe straightened his dragon-skin jacketîØneed to keep up appearances, true. ØAnd if they catch us at itîwell, when they catch us, canít last foreverîthen weíll go on the run. Weíve got bags packed already, in case. His hand tightened around the wand in his pocket, and a tingling feeling swarmed over his body as if ants were scurrying over every inch of his skin. He felt the wooden floor against his back. He heard his mother screaming out. A taster, said the Dark Lord with displeasure. Ron swore loudly. ØAre they all right?Æ ØWe donít know,Æ George said. ØItíll be hard to communicate from now on. The twins were right. This had been an idiotic decision. From now on they would need to Apparate only under Potterís Cloak, or Disillusioned, at the very least. The thought alarmed him. From now on è was he really going to stay with these three? Potter, the most wanted man in Britain, and his cronies? But what else was there for him to do? The farther from the Order he went, the farther he became from his parents. Surely Tonks would find a way to tell other Order members where they were? Draco felt a mutinous heat in his chest. He had nothing in common with some filthy thief. Draco didnít want to go abroad because he was a coward; he was just a pragmatist. For that matter, he didnít even really want to go abroad, he was being forced to by circumstance, and moreover, he hadnít gone abroad yet precisely because he had family and friends, so Weasley could take his incorrect analogies and choke on them. Footsteps creaked on the hardwoods out in the hall. They all froze. George swore under his breath. ØFred!Æ Fred leapt toward Draco and Ron. He seized them by the arms as George did the same to Granger and Potter. The twins turned on the spot, and with a crack, they all Disapparated, reappearing in a room of identical dimensions, built from the same dark wood. Draco supposed they'd Apparated to another part of the same building. The room itself appeared to be a parlor, stuffed with the flashy, gaudy furniture of someone who had suddenly obtained a large amount of gold and had no idea how to spend it properly. Photos of various Weasleys on the wall told him that the twins had taken them to their flat. ØWhat are you two doing?Æ Ron said, looking from Fred to George. ØWhy are we here?Æ Fred was scribbling with a peacock feather quill on a piece of parchment, while George was pulling a trunk down from atop a wardrobe and taking from it a scarlet bundle of cloth and pins. ØWeíre telling you,Æ said Fred urgently, Øitís not safe here. Anyone could be out there. Here, Harryîthis is how to find the Scavengerís Guild.Æ He pushed the piece of parchment into Potter's hand. George shoved the cloth bundle into Ronís arms. ØOur tent. We were going to use it ourselves, but we can get a new one anytime. You take this one and get out of Diagon Alley.Æ ØOut of London,Æ Fred said firmly. ØThanks, you two,Æ Potter said. ØWe owe you.Æ Draco heard the footsteps on a staircase nearby and hastened toward Potter and Granger, who had already linked arms. Ronís face was strained. The youngest Weasley looked like he wanted to hug his brothers, or say something, but the twins backed away as one and hissed, ØGo.Æ ØNow.Æ ØSee you soon,Æ Ron said, sounding strangled. Draco took Grangerís arm, Ron took Potterís, and there was a knock on the door just before they vanished with a crack. They appeared in a forest clearing and disentangled their arms. ØIím sure that wasnít anything dangerous,Æ Potter said to Weasley at once. ØWhen have you ever heard a Death Eater knock?Æ ØYeah,Æ said Weasley, nodding. ØYeah. I know, mate. Itís all right.Æ ØWhere are we?Æ Draco asked, scanning the glade. ØIs this èÆ ØThe forest where they held the Quidditch World Cup, yes,Æ said Granger, already walking around the clearing in a large circle and flicking her wand so that shimmering patches appeared in the air, like heat. Draco shifted uncomfortably. Why had she taken them here? Had she meant to remind him of how theyíd all met the night of the Cup, when his father had gone out to march? He could only remember bits and pieces of their conversation. Mostly he remembered watching the march from the edge of the forest, hearing people scream as their tents were blown away. He remembered the way heíd felt, looking on. At the time heíd thought the feeling was amusement, or exhilaration, but in retrospect he realized heíd just felt safe, that was all. Other people were in danger, but not him. Heíd been tucked away in the darkened trees, and he knew that even if the marchers found him, he was a Malfoy, and theyíd blow past him, maybe even nod deferentially to him. He was above danger. ØWhy are we here?Æ he said. Something must have shown in his voice, because Granger glanced back at him. ØNo reason,Æ she said. ØIt was the first place that came to mind. Why did you think?Æ ØJust asking.Æ Her eyes narrowed. ØKeep squinting at me like that, Granger,Æ he said, Øand I might start mistaking you for that hideous cat of yours.Æ ØNot your best line, Malfoy.Æ She turned back to the edge of the clearing and flicked her wand again. ØLosing your touch?Æ ØOh, Iím so sorry for not being at my wittiest after spending the night in a cave,Æ he said sourly. She laughed. Actually laughed, a clear, bright thing heíd never heard in conversation with her before. ØWitty?Æ she said. ØIs that what you think you are, usually?Æ ØWell, Iíd forgive you for not recognizing wit when you see it, the sort of company you keep.Æ ØMalfoy,Æ said Potter, Øare you planning to do anything useful at all?Æ Draco looked back. Weasley and Potter were nearly done pitching the tent. ØWhatís my incentive, exactly?Æ Draco said. Weasley and Potter scowled in unison and flicked their wands. The spine of the tent sprang up into place, giving it the shape of a small cottage with a gable. It was silken, crimson, and obviously expensive, althoughîlike the rest of the twinsí possessionsîit was rather tasteless, too, with golden tassels hanging off it and gold thread splattered all over its walls, like a Gryffindor lion had thrown up on it. Draco didnít even want to think about what his father would have said, knowing he was going to sleep in a place like this. Weasley ducked in and let out a low whistle. ØHarry, Hermione, come look at this.Æ ØOh, donít mind me,Æ Draco said to the clearing at large as Granger went in after Potter. ØPlease, by all means, continue to ignore me.Æ No answer, so Draco indulged in a roll of his eyes and slapped open the tent flap to follow the others inside. The tent opened up into a comfortable sitting room with a long, plush sofa and two leather armchairs, glimmering floorboards, and a Wizarding Wireless perched on a mantel, again decorated proudly with Weasley photographs. Every other surface was cluttered with Weasleyís Wizard Wheezes products, designs, or test merchandise. As Draco walked through a kitchen with a large iron stove, he glanced down at several sketchbooks that the twins had left open on the oaken table. The pages were covered with scribbled ideas, each twinís handwriting annotating the otherís: hot air balloon blowing gum? îdunno if we want to be a Drooble competitor îlanding in Honeydukes could be great though pocket hang gliders of some kind? îmaybe. could make them a craft, foldable hang gliders like paper aeroplanes? îdo you think we need to know how an aeroplane works to charm something like this îcould always ask dad îNO Firecracker chewing gum îgood one! murtlap for injury reduction here! îno you prat how do you think that would taste îletís hear your idea then Draco found his mouth twitching, threatening a smile. Before one of the Gryffindors could see, he sidled into one of the bedrooms. It had a four-poster much like the beds in the Slytherin dormitories, though hung with red and gold drapes rather than green and silver. Sunlight cascaded through a bank of French windows spelled into the wall and lit up the bedís crisp sheets, which poured over a king-sized mattress. ØThereís beds for all of us,Æ Weasley called from somewhere else in the tent. ØTheyíve got a spare room. Merlin, I donít even want to imagine how rich those two would be if theyíd opened that shop without a war going on.Æ After packing the twinsí belongings into various trunks, drawers, and dressers and unpacking some of Grangerís things from the beaded bag, they returned outside to eat lunch. It was a glorious summerís day, warm enough that they all discarded their outer robes in dark pools on the grass and rolled up their shirtsleeves. Draco was torn between whether to join them or to eat somewhere else. On one hand, he didnít want any of them to think he actually liked them. On the other, it was boring to eat alone, and it would probably look petty, if not outright ridiculous, to sit elsewhere in the clearing. He split the difference by sitting in their circle, but facing mostly away from the others, as if he were only there to admire the trees. While they ate their tomato soup and sourdough bread, the others reminisced about the Quidditch World Cup. Draco, again, felt torn. At one point, Weasley and Potter were arguing over what the most exciting goal scored by the Irish side had been, and they were obviously forgetting the spectacular Khushk Return that Troy had pulled off in the twelfth minute. If it had been Crabbe and Goyle, Draco would have interjected and recounted it in blissful detail, but the idea of enthusing about Quidditch with these three felt deeply wrong. ØI thought the congregation aspect of it was very interesting,Æ said Granger. ØI mean, what other chance do we have to gather on such a large scale? I wish weíd been able to meet some more people from other countries.Æ ØMm,Æ Weasley grunted. ØI think we met plenty of people from other countries that year.Æ Granger turned red, and Draco realized what Weasley was talking about. He remembered Granger walking down the Grand Staircase at the Yule Ball with Viktor Krum, her hair held back from her face and neck, her blue dress robes seeming to float around her. He and Pansy hadnít been able to have a proper conversation for ten minutes after that. When theyíd tried to make fun of Granger, it was all jabs about Krumís taste that felt stupid even to say, so eventually theyíd just left the subject alone and gone off to find the others. Once their bowls were empty, talk of the Cup came to a coda. ØSo,Æ Weasley said. ØWhatís our plan?Æ Draco angled himself more toward the group. ØWe still need the sword to get rid of the diadem,Æ Potter said. ØBut how are we supposed to find out where the Ministry is keeping it?Æ ØIíve been thinking about that, too,Æ Granger said. ØAnd I donít think the sword is at the Ministry anymore.Æ ØWhy not?Æ ØDidnít you hear what they said during that speech? When they were talking about all the things youíd supposedly done?Æ Potterís expression darkened. ØIt was hard to miss, Hermione.Æ ØNo, no,Æ she said quickly. ØI mean, they mentioned the Department of Mysteries, but then, right after that, they said, åWe have reason to believe he made a similar raid on the Ministry as recently as this week.í And I thought, well, thatís a funny lie to make up, especially since the rest were all things that had happened, but that they were twisting around to cast in a negative light. è But what if they werenít making it up? What if something was stolen from the Ministry just this weekîsomething with a connection to you?Æ Draco felt incredulous. It was one thing to memorize lines out of textbooksîdid Granger also remember every word sheíd ever heard anyone say? ØBut thatís even worse,Æ Weasley said. ØIf someone nicked the sword, and we donít know who, that puts us even farther away from getting our hands on it.Æ Draco shook his head. ØBetter than the Dark Lord having it.Æ ØOh, yeah?Æ Weasley said. ØHow do we know it wasnít a Death Eater who nicked it?Æ ØI donít think so, Ron,Æ said Granger. ØIf it were with a Death Eater, it wouldnít have seemed like a problem to Thicknesse, would it?Æ She bit her lip. ØI think Malfoyís right. Potter, Granger, and the Disillusioned Weasley were already sending spells whizzing into the trees. Jets of light shot back toward Potter and Granger, the only two still visible. ØProtego!Æ Potter yelled, slashing his wand through the air, and a Stunning spell rebounded into the trees, rewarded with a strangled cry. Draco seized Potterís and Grangerís arms. The Dark Lord can call only one of us with it, if he needs to, and I donít know how that works.Æ ØOf course, that variant of the Protean Charm will be Dark magic,Æ Granger said. ØI still think itíd be safer if you all just èÆ ØWeíre not leaving you,Æ Granger said in a low, strangled voice. Potter gave her a sidelong glance, an odd, charged kind of surprise on his face. Draco found himself wishing he were several miles away. ØPotter,Æ he said loudly, breaking the silence, Øthereís something funny about this Cloak.Æ ØWhat?Æ Potter looked over, startled. ØFunny?Æ Draco tossed him the Cloak. ØInvisibility Cloaks donít last that long. My father had one that was all but a rag after fifteen years, and that was the top of the line, very expensive.Æ Potter caught the Cloak and inspected it, nonplussed. Weasley was frowning now, too. ØYou know, I never thought about that. The charms on cloaks are supposed to wear off after a while, and even if they donít, they usually have tears or frays or something. ØWhere are we, Malfoy?Æ ØA few miles from my house,Æ Draco muttered. ØI know, I know, we shouldnít stay here. He could have found his way home blindfolded from here. He made out one corner of the house standing proud atop the hill, and he wondered who was inside it now, and to what use it was being put. He wondered about the figures moving through the halls of his childhood like ghosts of the old life, which felt farther away now, as he stood on its threshold, than it ever had. They settled into a routine, which, while not particularly relaxing, lent some structure to their days. In the mornings, they packed up camp. If anyone had an idea for where they might search for Hufflepuffís cup next, they spent the afternoon performing Transfigurations and traveling there, although they tried not to Transfigure themselves multiple days in a row. Side effects of excessive Transfiguration included muscular twitching, headaches, and the viscerally horrible feeling that their faces were melting for hours on end. Soon they had investigated the orphanage where the Dark Lord had been raised. It turned out to be a dead end; the building had been demolished and replaced by a gray block of Muggle offices. They also visited the village where Helga Hufflepuff had supposedly spent most of her life, Greater Padgley. ØGreater than what, exactly?Æ Weasley said as they picked through the few run-down streets. ØIt was quite an important place in the 1700s,Æ Granger said. After this visit, Potter suggested they go to Godricís Hollow, too. Granger sounded a bit too knowing as she asked why, exactly, he thought it might be a good place to search. ØI donít know,Æ Potter said. On the days that they had no new ideas at all, their time was devoted to the brewing of the Polyjuice Potion, and to Occlumency. The lessons were tense, to say the least. Potter seemed only marginally reassured by his friendsí presence, and Granger and Weasley seemed just as uncomfortable with the idea of reading Potterís mind. Weasley, attempting Legilimency one afternoon, somehow managed to hex Potter into saying all his sentences backward. ØThis spell is well past N.E.W.T. standard,Æ Granger had told him hastily after setting it right. ØThereís no reason you should be able to perform it, Ron.Æ Then she went back to trying it herself. Wasnít this your idea?Æ She gave Potter a hundredth tentative look. ØItís all right, Hermione,Æ Potter said. ØReally.Æ Weasley sat down in one of the armchairs rather harder than necessary, watching Granger and Potter making the necessary intense, penetrating eye contact. ØAll right,Æ Draco said. ØReady, then?Æ At long, long last? he added silently. They both nodded. ØGood. Potter, if you canít clear your mind, be aware of what youíre thinking about.Æ Draco nodded at Granger. She took a steadying breath, then aimed her wand at Potter and said, ØLegilimens!Æ It had finally worked. Draco could tell. Potterís body went rigid, while Granger swayed, her eyes closed, her wand fixed in its outstretched position. After about thirty seconds, Granger wrenched the wand away. Potter staggered, gasped in a breath, and seized one arm of the sofa, while Granger tottered backward. ØAre you a-all right?Æ she said, her voice ragged, as though sheíd just coughed up salt water. ØItís èÆ Potter straightened his glasses with a shaking hand. What did you think about before she cast the spell?Æ ØOh.Æ Potter shook his head, looking disoriented, and Draco remembered Bellatrix rummaging through his own memories, which sheíd done eagerly, as if looking for any hint of disloyalty. Yes, it was like surfacing from a violent current, coming out of Legilimencyî but this was a controlled environment, and Potter had the luck of doing this with one of his best friends, rather than with a deranged aunt. Thought you wouldíve read about this stuff.Æ ØIíve had plenty of other stuff to read about, thank you,Æ she huffed. ØNone of the books Iíve brought have particularly detailed passages on Legilimency, more's the pity.Æ He smirked and leaned back on the sofa arm. ØSure. Well, those kinds of memories are called fissure memories, in Legilimency. They make it possible for the caster to force their way in. Normally he would have needled him just for the fun of it, but Potter was clearly still on edge from having Granger in his mind, and Draco didnít want to sit here for another hour waiting for him to calm down. She aimed her wand at him and said, ØLegilimens.Æ Draco had already let the world slide into soft focus. He looked at a freckle on Grangerís cheek, then at a panel in the wall. He felt the spell flowing over him, glancing off his surface. It was light, and he was ice. It was a stream of air, and he was stone. Residual thoughts of Pansy carried no charge; they stirred with other thoughts beneath his consciousness like wraiths, dissolving and reforming and dissolving again. Soon enough the spell had broken. ØSee?Æ he said, glancing at Potter, who looked reluctantly impressed. ØIt felt different from my end, too,Æ Granger said, turning to Potter with interest, as if they were back in Hogwarts and taking part in an engaging Charms class. This went double when they hadnít eaten for a while. Soon, with nothing to contribute to the Occlumency lessons, Weasley took the hours as an opportunity to work on his Transfiguration spells, and often the time alone made him moody. Draco wouldnít have admitted it aloud, but he didnít hate the lessons as much as heíd expected to. Potterís mental block in the subject was like a kind of common enemy, something they all wanted gone. Then, in the second week, it happened again, this time seemingly out of nowhere. Draco let the cutlery clatter to the floor, seized a fistful of Peruvian Instant Darkness Powder from the mantel, and flung it through the tent flap, scattering it over the clearing with a sweep of his wand. As voices cried out in the distance, they spilled from the exit under cover of the black cloud that had swallowed the woodland. Granger summoned the tentís pegs, and Potter and Weasley collapsed the tent. Draco threw up Shield Charms at every spell that whizzed out of the darkness. Within thirty seconds, they had Disapparated. Though the plan had worked, they were shaken. They moved twice more that night, having pointless arguments about how secure each location was, when the truth was that no location seemed more or less secure than another. Finally, they settled in a mountain cave large enough to set up the tent within. Granger cast a Screening Spell to duplicate the appearance of the rock face over the cave's mouth. The hasty collapse of the tent had spilled many of its contents, including dinner. They spent the better part of an hour putting the apartment back into order. ØIíve been sleeping too well. ØWhy donít we set up our own headquarters?Æ Nobody answered for a moment. ØNothing like this ever happened at Grimmauld Place,Æ Potter went on. ØWhy donít we find a place to cast the Fidelius Charm?Æ Weasley stood up from his armchair. ØHarry, thatís an idea,Æ he said with rising excitement, starting to pace across the sitting room. Surely, after six straight years of immaculate test scores and incessant praise by teachers, Granger couldnít actually be insecure about her magical performance? ØThank you,Æ she said slowly, as if waiting for him to say heíd been joking. Weasley jumped in. ØI can try, of course.Æ And so Grangerís practice of the Fidelius Charm was added to the routine. She spent long hours outside the tent in the afternoons, practicing clockwise and counterclockwise turns of her wand, whispering strings of incantations under her breath. Some evenings, they gathered in the sitting room to listen to the twinsí Wizarding Wireless. The newscasts they heard were growing increasingly suspect: every programme reiterated the idea that Rufus Scrimgeour was on a much-needed holiday, and that Dumbledoreís death was associated with the highly suspicious Harry Potter, and that unity among wizards was paramount. Erratic reports from listeners suggested that the Dark Lord had been seen in dozens of locations across Britain. These frightened listeners were then soothed by instructions disseminated by the Ministryîwhich included, in many cases, orders to spy on friends and neighbors, so as to ensure that they were law-abiding citizens rather than rabble.rousers and violent dissidents, such as the unhinged and recently at-large Dedalus Diggle, etc. One particular radio broadcast had made Potter, Weasley, and Granger all roar with indignation: the announcement that Severus Snape had been selected by the Ministry to take over from Albus Dumbledore as Headmaster of Hogwarts, with Amycus Carrow as the new Defence Against the Dark Arts teacher and his sister Alecto tapped for the Muggle Studies vacancy. Draco wasnít particularly surprised, but he didnít like to think what the Carrows would do to unruly students. He hoped his friends were smart enough to fall in line. Meanwhile, they continued with Occlumency. Draco didnít think he could have imagined a person more temperamentally ill-suited for the discipline than Potter, but by the end of the second week, they were making some progress, at least. Now, if Granger gave Potter a minuteís lead time before casting the spell, he could unreliably fight it off after thirty or forty secondsí Legilimency. That level of resistance would have been useless against Snape or the Dark Lord, who would have been able to find anything they needed within several seconds, but still, Draco sometimes caught himself feeling smug about Potterís incremental progress. Snape had tried to teach Potter for months and hadnít gotten anywhere, after all. In some ways, he almost felt as if they were back at Hogwarts, with a potion bubbling in the corner, scheduled lessons, reference texts strewn across the flat, andîmost of allîthe way Granger could constantly be heard parroting facts. ØThe Fidelius is a twelve-part spell,Æ she explained over dinner one night, Øand the third and ninth incantations are spoken in reverse, and the accompanying thought patterns for the fourth through tenth all require different kinds of memory work. It really is astoundingly difficult.Æ But Draco thought she sounded a bit excited at the challenge. ØSo,Æ he said, Øwhich of you three is going to be Secret-Keeper, anyway?Æ Granger looked at him in mock surprise. ØI was assuming you would volunteer, Malfoy.Æ Potter chortled. Even Weasley grinned reluctantly. Then he took another bite of cauliflower and said, ØEight on a good day, thanks.Æ Draco wasnít expecting it. A laugh startled out of him, and Granger and Weasley laughed, too, and then they were all grinning stupidly down at their plates, none of them meeting each otherís eyes.
