Weasley looked slightly stunned. ØWh-what? Me?Æ ØYes, you, Ron,Æ said Granger, smiling. ØYouíre related to half the Order, for a start, and you still know much more about the Wizarding World than either Harry or I do. That could come in handy if weíre ever trying to recruit, or spread the word.Æ ØBut è but I èÆ ØWhat, youíre not planning on blabbing, are you?Æ said Potter. ØNo, shut up, of course not.Æ Weasleyís whole face had turned red. ØBut è you really mean it?Æ Draco saw that Weasley was sitting up ramrod-straight in his chair. Every trace of woundedness and exclusion had melted out of him. He wore a look of glowing pride, as if heíd just been handed the Quidditch Cup, rather than a job that would make him an infinitely more valuable prisoner, or object of torture, to the Death Eaters. Dracoís throat tightened, and he set his fork down on his plate, his appetite dwindling. He felt the way heíd felt at Christmas last year: suddenly aware of how young he was. He felt as if they were play-acting, all four of them, at adulthood and responsibility. Weasley couldnít understand what was really happening to him right nowîcould he? Would he come to realize the full implications of this decision, in an awful way, or would he, Potter, and Granger somehow slink out of this unscathed, as they always managed to? But è no, Draco thought. They werenít unscathed. Potterís fissure memories during Occlumency showed that much: the deaths of Sirius Black, his parents, Cedric Diggory. The graveyard, where heíd felt the Cruciatus too. And Granger had sent her parents away as strangers, accepting that she might die unknown to them, and Weasley had two brothers now with scars. So it wasnít like the Gryffindors were completely whole. Draco glanced around at them, the trio heíd hated so enthusiastically for so long, and thought about the ways they hurt, and everything they were risking. Foolishly, maybeîbut not without reason. He found he couldnít look at any of them for long. Occlumency was difficult that evening. Draco felt surly and unhelpful, and they scarcely managed an hour before Potter went to bed, making some feeble excuse but obviously suffering discomfort in his scar. Weasley followed a short while later. Draco sank into an armchair while Granger pored over a textbook on the sofa. He turned the silver ring with the Malfoy crest around his pinky finger, wondering about his parents. His mother and father had a habit of not speaking when they were worried, or speaking only in clipped sentences that verged on clichs. They rarely fought, but in times of tension there was a lot of icy silence at the manor, the kind you could feel on your skin like a gentle weight. The gold he would have spent to get a single message to them, telling them he was safe è ØItís getting better, isnít it?Æ Draco looked over at Granger and found her eyes on him. He straightened in his chair, feeling self-conscious, wondering how long sheíd been watching him stare into nothing. He didnít ask her to elaborate. He knew she meant living with the three of them, and she was right. Things were better than they had been. Potter was still awkward, but apparently that was just his personality. Even Weasley seemed occasionally to be able to relax around him now, or at least, benignly ignore him. ØIn fifth year, he wouldnít even practice Occlumency, Snape made him so miserable.Æ ØYeah, well. ØNo offense, Granger, but people who chronically care too much about everything arenít exactly predisposed to it.Æ He expected her to protest, but a small smile tugged at her mouth instead. She flipped the textbook shut and nestled her head into the sofa cushions. ØDid you say Bellatrix taught it to you?Æ He nodded. ØWhen?Æ ØEnd of last summer,Æ he said, not really knowing why he was answering, except that it was late, and he was tired, and dinner had left him feeling uncertain. ØWhen he told you to kill Dumbledore?Æ He let out a soft, derisive sound. ØYeah. Obviously. I mean, everyone knew. The other Death Eaters wouldnít shut up about itîtalking about how theyíd always wanted to see my parents taken down a notch. They thought it was really funny.Æ ØFunny?Æ He shrugged. ØThey all resented us for one reason or another. But mainly it just wasnít happening to them. Everythingís funny when itís not happening to you.Æ Draco began to turn the silver ring around his finger again. A headache had started to pulse deep in his skull. ØAll year,Æ he muttered, ØI was just waiting to get back on that side of it, where I was the one watching and è and, yeah, laughing. I mean, it wasnít like I loved it so much, watching things happen to other people, but if youíre watching, itís not you.Æ He paused. ØI mean, and I felt special at first. è You have to think youíre going to feel that way again, or whatís the point, you might as well just die.Æ Draco didnít even know if he was making any sense. He didnít think he was articulating the last year particularly well, but that was the thing about nightmares. They came out of your grip. He glanced over at Granger, holding the tome in her lap. She looked awkward and uncertain, startled if not altogether surprised, and he felt suddenly older than her, and somehow unclean, as if she were brand new, and he was revealing something horrible about the world to her. He wondered if heíd said too much, if heíd disturbed her. He wondered, too, if she would tell Potter and Weasley. Even a few weeks ago he would have been certain that she would. Now, though, for some reason, he wasnít sure. Heíd never told Pansy these things. And it wasnít as if Pansy hadnít asked, in her wheedling and breathless way. But heíd wanted Pansy to keep looking at him the way she always had, like he was something precious she aspired to cradle in her hands. Granger looked at him like she could lay him open with her eyes, like she wanted to. Even now, unsettled, there was flint in her gaze. ØYou flinch sometimes,Æ she said. ØWhen people go for their wands. Is that because èÆ Pansy wouldnít have said these things. ØOnly twice,Æ Draco said, and then he stood and went to bed. With under a week to go until the Scavengersí Guild came to Diagon Alley, Hermione decided it was time to try the Fidelius Charm for the first time. They still had no real idea where they might place a new headquarters, but it would be vital to perfect every detail of the charm, so Hermione wanted to practice it before the real thing. They cordoned off a patch of woodland: this would be the area bound by the Secret. Harry would stand inside that patch while Hermione cast the charm upon Ron, making him the Secret-Keeper. If all went to plan, Malfoy would then be unable to see Harry, and Hermione would be unable to speak his location to Malfoy. They would perform several tests on the area, like Harry walking through its boundaries, which should be permeable yet undetectable, and Malfoy trying to Apparate into its bounds, which should be impossible. Then Ron would confide the secret to Malfoy to ensure that the information could expand from person to person in the proper way. Hermione was full of jitters that morning. She knew it wasnít the real headquarters, and there were no real stakes to the exercise, but she couldnít help feeling that she was standing in the hall before her Charms O.W.L., running through everything sheíd mentally prepared, full of anxiety that some unforeseen question would arise. She had prepared several sheets of parchment, shorthand notes that she could follow step by step. After lunch, they approached the cordoned-off area. Malfoy leaned against a nearby tree while Harry sat down within the ropes theyíd conjured. Hermione faced Ron, who was holding her notes so she could read them. ØYouíll be fine,Æ Ron told her. She tried to smile, but his words had made no impact on her nerves. They rolled off the jagged edge of what she was feeling, as his and Harryís exam-time reassurances always had. She took a deep breath, raised her wand, and began. During the first attempt, she misspoke a syllable in the first of the charmís twelve incantations. During the second and third attempts, she stuttered over the fifth and eleventh incantations, respectively. On the fourth attempt, her mind strayed for a split instant from the seventh incantationís required thought pattern (a memory that evoked intense and unshakable security). It went on and on that way. She reached two dozen attempts, then four, and with each new failure, Hermione felt more and more as if she were falling into one of her old nightmares. For months during fifth year, leading up to their O.W.L.s, she would startle awake in the middle of the night, drenched in sweat, certain that sheíd just walked into the testing room to discover that sheíd forgotten to study the most important section of her notesîand then all of her supposed potential, her supposed great ability, would fall by the wayside and go unrecognized, and she wouldnít be able to sign up for N.E.W.T.s or have any proper job prospects, and her parentsí disappointment would be coupled with her friendsí surprise and her enemiesí vindictive satisfaction, and the rest of her life would be filled with regret. After half an hour, Harry urged her to take a break. Ron agreed, saying all she needed to do was take a walk and try again. Malfoy just watched her from where he was leaning against the tree. He didnít say anything, but Hermione could tell that he was surprised by what he was seeing. She found herself thinking of how heíd said, When have you ever not been able to do a charm? and it just made her stomach twist up more tightly. With every failure she was rewriting her identity as someone incompetent and unworthy. Even Draco Malfoy had thought of her as someone who, at the base of it all, could do any spellîsomeone who was powerfully magical. But he was wrong. They all were. She did take a break, and a short walk, but it didnít help. After nearly three hours, they stopped. Hermioneís throat was tight, and her eyes kept burning. There had been many attempts when sheíd thought sheíd done everything rightîand yet the spell still hadnít taken. ØIím so sorry,Æ she said, unable to look at the boys. ØIíve wasted your whole afternoon.Æ ØWhat else were we doing, exactly?Æ Malfoy said. She glanced over. He was sitting among the tree roots now, his silvery hair dappled with the sunlight that was peeking through the boughs. Harry stepped out of the cordoned area. ØItís all right, Hermione. Everyone needs practice.Æ ØYeah, exactly,Æ Ron said. ØThatís what this was forîto practice. Remember last year, we thought we were going to be slaughtered at Quidditch? But we practiced every night, even when we felt like rubbish, and everything turned out all right.Æ Hermione nodded, not wanting to tell Ron that this didnít feel like Quidditch at all. It wasnít a team effortîit was just her. This felt like a fundamental verification of what sheíd always feared: that she wasnít really talented, but just good for her age; that she wasnít truly exceptional in any way, and certainly not brilliant enough to override what people would expect from her, being a girl, and being Muggle-born, and being herself. It just wasnít enough. She wasnít enough. If only there were a way for her to transcend herself è to è But there was. She drew a sharp breath, feeling like an idiot for not having thought of it sooner. ØThe diadem!Æ Ron brightened immediately. ØHermione, thatís it! Try it with the diadem on. Youíve already done all the preparation. Iím sure thatís all you need to get it right.Æ As Harry returned to the cordoned area, she fished the tiara out of her bag. The second it was in her hand, she felt a sense of security wash over her, and when she placed it upon her head, she felt several inches taller, and lighter, too, as if she were hovering above the forest floor. How stupid not to think of the diadem before, said a smooth, cool voice in her mind. Without it, she did foolish, forgetful things. Without the diadem, she was fallible and unexceptional, only human. With it, she was so much more. She raised her wand, looked at her sheet of notes, and began to speak the incantations. At first, the uncanny clarity brought her relief and confidence. With every new syllable she spoke, though, the clarity began to elicit a different effect. She started noticing every single moment in the charm that she was failing to perform up to standard. It wasnít just an issue of correcting one little flaw. Her pronunciations were imprecise throughout, her wand movements inaccurate by matters of degrees. Her memories were more focused with the diadem, but they still lacked the propulsive force that, for instance, Harry drew on for his Patronus. Before sheíd even finished the charm, she lowered her wand. ØWhat are you stopping for?Æ Ron said. ØThat sounded great!Æ ØNo,Æ she said. ØIt isnít good enough. Iím not even close. I need to do much more work on it. Weíll try again another dayîIím going to practice by myself.Æ She took the notes from his hand and walked back toward the clearing where sheíd been practicing, the diadem still on her head. Two hours seemed to pass in two minutes. She tried several other advanced charms that implemented the same techniques as the Fidelius, honing her memory recall, refining the incantations upon her tongue, the particular way her lips and soft palate and jaw had to move in tandem to shape the wordsí precise and unusual syllables. These werenít the ordinary Latinate spell roots, but words that had their bases in lost language. With the diadem, though, she was learning precision and perfection; everything was beginning to fall into place for her with a speed and power that she associated with her first year at school, the pure shock and exhilaration of being able to wave her wand and send a feather flying into the air, in defiance of her old life as a Muggle. And really, she thought, wasnít it a blessing to be so far away from Muggle influence now? Wasnít it a relief to be in the world of wizards, who were remarkable and special, the way she wasîto have cast off the dull, comparatively barbaric mantle of her upbringing? ØHowíd it go?Æ said Harry when she ducked back into the tent, the diadem replaced in her bag. ØReally well,Æ she exclaimed. She felt intoxicated, the past two hours almost blurred; she couldnít remember exactly which spells sheíd done, or what sheíd been thinking, really, but she had the feeling of intense productivity, as if sheíd just finished four feet of parchment for Professor Flitwick. Itís a Horcrux, sheíd told herself while wearing the diadem. Itís a Horcrux. But the reminder was only perfunctory, really. Yes, it was Dark magic, but that didnít mean it couldnít be useful. The diademîitís lonely, she found herself thinking, and although it felt strange to ascribe a feeling to an object, she knew that it was true. The diadem wanted to be helpful; it had already helped them. It could help them even more. ØYou know, Iíve been thinking,Æ she said, sitting down at the dinner table. ØI think we should all try on the diadem.Æ Ron, who was stirring a pan of browning green beans, laughed. ØI suppose è you do feel all right, Hermione? Itís not like Ginny, where youíre è I donít know, getting reliant on it?Æ ØIím not reliant on it,Æ she said quickly. ØI forgot about it completely for two weeks, didnít I? I hadnít touched it in ages before today.Æ Harry and Ron exchanged a look, then shrugged. ØAll right,Æ Ron said. ØI donít see the harm. If this one helps us find the rest, then engaging with it is necessary. Itís the best tool weíve got right now.Æ Malfoy examined her face. Hermione didnít know what he was looking for, what he was expecting to see, but as she met his eyes, she felt jarred, shaken awake, as if she were coming back into herself for the first time since sheíd put the diadem on. These days, whenever she and Malfoy spoke, she remembered what heíd told her the night that Harry and Ron had gone to bed early. She couldnít shake the brief, brutal description of his mindset during the previous year. If youíre watching, itís not you. And thenîonly twice, heíd said, as if being subjected to Voldemort's Cruciatus Curse a mere two times was something he should be grateful for. Sheíd half expected him to be furious with her for asking, or, in the days that followed, to shun her, angry with himself instead for telling her. Like he wanted her to laugh at his jokes. She hadnít told Harry or Ron what heíd said. It didnít seem right to tell them, but then it left her with a strange weight in her chest when she looked at Malfoy. Had he told anyone else at all? Godricís Hollow in August was filled with families, many of whom were strolling through the picturesque little square in the heart of the village. Unattended groups of children were laughing and chasing each other down the narrow, winding streets. It was a beautiful place, and Hermione tried not to watch Harry too closely. Words were engraved into it: On this spot, on the night of 31 October 1981, Lily and James Potter lost their lives. Their son, Harry, remains the only wizard ever to have survived the Killing Curse. This house, invisible to Muggles, has been left in its ruined state as a monument to the Potters and as a reminder of the violence that tore apart their family. Around the words were lines of ink graffitiîwords of encouragement. Good luck, Harry, wherever you are. è Long live Harry Potter. Harry ran one hand over the words, and as he read them, a smile grew upon his face, until he was beaming. ØHang on,Æ Hermione said, an idea striking her. She took out her wandînone of the Muggles appeared to see them anymore, now that they were in such close proximity to the cottageî and tapped the sign. ØAparecium!Æ All four of them took in a sharp breath. A handwritten line of script had appeared in the bottom corner of the sign. Return to the site of survival ØThe site of survival?Æ Malfoy said. Ron was frowning at the words. ØI think it means youíre supposed to go there.Æ She pointed to the blasted corner of the cottage, to the remains of the room, just out of sight, where the Killing Curse had rebounded sixteen years ago. ØThere, Harry. The place you survived.Æ Chapter End Notes mood for this chapter: things being unexpectedly okay sometimes! haha donít worry though, things will continue to get worse tumblr | instagram Chapter9:The Queen of the Night Chapter Notes hi friends! as a quick note, i wanted to say that whenever someone compares this fic to canon, i swoon! iíve been making a conscious effort to cleave as closely to JKRís writing style and tone as i can, in hopes that the fic will feel contiguous to the first six books. iím really thrilled that this seems to be coming through for some of yíallîitís exactly what iíd hoped!! much love, and i hope you enjoy another stupidly long chapter, lol See the end of the chapter for more notes ØI still donít think we should follow some unsigned note scribbled in a public place,Æ Draco muttered as they approached the Potter cottage again. ØAnyone could have written it.Æ ØYes, well, thatís why we just went on a miniature goose chase, isnít it,Æ came Grangerís whisper from thin air. ØTo shake them off if they were watching.Æ After reading the note, theyíd drifted away from the cottage like disinterested tourists. Theyíd gone right out of Godricís Hollow, swapped their Transfigurations for Disillusionments, and Potter and Grangerîthe two smallestîhad donned the Invisibility Cloak for good measure before theyíd sneaked back into the village. Now, standing before the cottage again, Draco didnít like the idea of going inside. ØWhat if theyíve set up a ward or alert?Æ ØAs long as itís not an anti-Apparition ward, it doesnít matter,Æ Granger whispered back. ØAnd we can check for that. Everyone remember the plan?Æ ØIf anything fishy happens,Æ Weasley muttered, ØDisapparate to that cave where we stayed after they found us last time.Æ ØGood,Æ Potter said. ØCome on.Æ The ivy upon the iron gate trembled, as if in a breeze, as the Gryffindors climbed up and over it. Draco sighed, but followed. The Potter cottage was made of handsome weathered stone, though it was mostly obscured by the dark ivy that had grown wild over its face. The front door must have once been a bright, inviting crimson, but the paint had dulled in the intervening sixteen years, peeling and flaking between the boards, the door handle rusted. ØLetís look for a side door,Æ Potter whispered. ØI donít think we should just walk in through the èÆ Draco didnít miss the strain in his voice. Draco suddenly imagined himself coming back to Malfoy Manor after a decade to find it overgrown, the gardens a mess of weeds, the windows stained, the upper storey blasted apartîthe picture of neglect, disuse, and outright damage. Weasley had clearly also sensed Potterís discomfort. ØCome on,Æ he said, Øletís try back here.Æ He took the lead, and they fell into step after his faint outline. They picked around the side of the cottage, following the path of cracked flagstones so as not to leave depressions in the earth. At the cottageís back corner was a small overhang, and beneath it, a second red door with black brackets. ØAlohomora,Æ whispered Grangerís voice. The lock whined and scraped as it opened, and Weasley pushed the door wide. They entered a small kitchen with outdated fixtures. It might, at one point, have been a cozy place: Dracoís eyes lingered on nested copper pots atop the cabinetry, and on an enchanted plate mounted on the wall, where a painted rooster pecked eternally at some seeds in a few brushstrokes of grass. But time had worn away the comforts. The air smelled of rot, and the corners of the ceilings were spotted with mould. It seemed that the sign had spoken literally. No one seemed to have touched the place at all since that Halloween night. Dracoís gaze fixed on a detail that felt somehow appalling: a single water glass that stood on the edge of the counter, near the sink. Someone might have just pulled a drink of water from the tap and left the glass there to rest. Weasley shut the door behind them, sweeping a section of the hardwood floor clean. Otherwise, the dust was like thin carpet, undisturbedîexcept for a single pair of footsteps that led forward, into a dark, narrow hallway. ØLook,Æ Draco said. ØSomeîsomeoneís been here.Æ He didnít like that his voice had come out higher than usual. ØWe know someoneís been here,Æ said Granger, clearly trying for a matter-of-fact tone, but she sounded tense, too. ØWhoever left the note.Æ ØLetís go, then, shall we?Æ said Weasley. ØWe don't want to linger.Æ ØYeah,Æ said Potter, his voice hoarse. ØNo Lumos. Someone might see from outside.Æ They entered the dark hallway, where Draco could only just make out the photographs in tarnished frames that hung on the walls. One recurring character looked so exactly like Potter that it couldnít have been anyone but his father; another, a woman with long, dark red hair, must have been his mother. Draco hesitated at a photograph of them waving at a small, black-haired baby. In terms of years, they looked hardly older than Draco, but the love, pride, and concentrated affection in their faces, their intense focus as they looked at their son, made Draco feel a decade their junior. It was a look he associated with his own parents, and in that moment he missed his family so much it was like his lungs had been pressed shut by an outside force. He forced himself to breathe, to walk, not to make a sound. They passed a small reading room with dust-laden sofas, then something that looked like a guest bedroom, before emerging in the front room of the cottage, where a stairwell led to the upper storey. The steps creaked horribly as they climbed, but soon they reached a carpeted landing, and when they turned a corner, they could see a door ahead, at the end of a hallway. Draco felt a draft coming from that door. Natural light spilled through the crack beneath it, a promise of the room that had been ripped apart on the other side. They approached with caution. Two steps away from it, a whispery voice spoke. All four froze. ØHarry Potter?Æ ØHang on,Æ said Granger sharply. She and Potter slipped out of the Cloak, leaving them Disillusioned. She extended her wand and said, ØSkadus dicoperare! Hominem revelio!Æ When nothing happened, she added, ØPeriempta revelio!Æ At this spell, a fine, shimmering mist descended over the door. After several moments, it cleared away, and Granger muttered, ØStrange.Æ ØWhat is it?Æ Potter said. ØWell, thereís nobody here,Æ Granger said. ØThis is an Anti-Apparition ward, but itís odd è itís only a one-way ward. Itíll prevent someone from Apparating into that room, but once weíre inside, it wonít prevent us from leaving. So it looks like someoneís trying to protect the room, or its contents.Æ ØYeah, but what about the voice?Æ Weasley said impatiently. ØIs the voice dangerous?Æ ØI canít be sure. If thereís a curse on the door, itís not a common one.Æ ØWell,Æ Draco muttered, Øthe Dark Lord wouldnít need to resort to common curses.Æ ØExactly,Æ Granger said. ØSo, just remember: if anything happens, keep your head and Disapparate straight away.Æ She sounded nervous, as if she were talking to herself. ØRight,Æ Potter said. He stepped up to the door, straightened his back, and said, ØItís me. Harry Potter.Æ The whispery voice spoke again. ØWhat did Albus Dumbledore tell you he saw in the Mirror of Erised?Æ Draco had no idea what this meant, but Potter drew in a sharp breath. ØHimself,Æ Potter said, voice suddenly tense with excitement. ØHolding a pair of woolen socks.Æ The lock on the door clicked open, but they didnít move to open it. Potterís outline had turned back toward them. ØNobody but me and Dumbledore knew that. You realize what this means?Æ ØHe must have left one of the Order to do this!Æ Granger whispered back. ØIn case something happened to himîhe must have given an Order member this assignment!Æ Yet when Potter turned back to the door, he still didnít move to open it. ØItís all right,Æ Granger whispered. ØI ... yeah, I know,Æ he said. ØI just feel strange.Æ ØDonít blame you, mate,Æ Weasley said. ØHereîwant me to go first?Æ Potter didn't object, so Weasley took the door handle and pushed. Clean, fresh air rushed through into the hallway, whisking away the damp, musty scent that had accumulated over the better part of two decades. The sun was blazing through the white clouds, and there was something beatific about the light that fell in long shafts into the room before them, a chamber opened forcibly to the elements. They stepped inside, onto a floor that was warped by years of rain and littered with shreds of wallpaper, some that were little more than burned rags, others that seemed to have come down in more recent years. A chest of drawers stood against the wall, heavily burned on the side that faced one particular corner, pale pine on the side that faced away. And there, in that corner, stood the barest skeleton of a wooden crib. Charred and worn down by decadesí rain, it nonetheless stood upright. No one spoke for what must have been a full minute. Draco couldnít help himself: his imagination was already overlaying images on the scene before himîof a flood of green light, of the woman in the photographs crumpling onto the ground in front of the crib, her dark red hair strewn over her lifeless face. Draco began to hear strange, hard breaths coming from several feet away and realized, with a shock, that Potter was trying and failing to restrain tears. Still, no voice broke the silence, but the Disillusioned Gryffindors coalesced before Draco like the joining pieces of some strange, half-invisible puzzle, Weasley putting an arm around his friendís shoulder, Grangerís hand taking Potterís. Dracoís throat was very tight. He felt as if he were intruding. He looked away, out through the destroyed wall, onto the street of Godricís Hollow where Muggle children were stillîbizarrelyîrunning, laughing, playing, as though none of this were happening. Eventually there was a sniffing sound, the wiping of a hand against a wet face. ØI donít understand,Æ Potter said gruffly. ØThereís nothing here.Æ ØIíll check the drawers,Æ Weasley said. ØHermione, want to do the shelves?Æ Potterís outline moved toward the crib, and then emitted a startled noise. Granger and Weasley instantly rushed over. Draco, unable to resist his curiosity, joined them. ØThat whisper again! It asked how I caught my first Snitch. è In my mouth,Æ he told the crib. ØI nearly swallowed it.Æ And the air beneath the crib shimmered like a mirage in the desert, folding away to reveal a small wooden box. Granger made a stifled squeak. ØOpen it,Æ Weasley exclaimed. ØGo on.Æ Potter lifted the box and slipped the latch. It came open to reveal three objects: a Golden Snitch, a silver object not unlike a cigarette lighter, and a book whose title read The Tales of Beedle the Bard. ØThereís a piece of paper,Æ Granger said. ØThereîsticking out of the book!Æ Potter withdrew the paper and unfolded it. ØItís his will,Æ he said, scanning it. ØDumbledoreís will. è åTo Ronald Bilius Weasley, I leave my Deluminator, in the hope that he will remember me when he uses it. è To Miss Hermione Jean Granger, I leave my copy of The Tales of Beedle the Bard, in the hope that she will find it entertaining and instructive.íÆ ØAnd the Snitch?Æ Weasley said. ØåTo Harry James Potter,íÆ Potter read out, ØåI leave the Snitch he caught in his first Quidditch match at Hogwarts, as a reminder of the rewards of perseverance and skill, and the sword of Godric Gryffindor, as a reminder of the legacy of courage through every dangerous age.í And at the bottom èÆ Potter ran a fingertip over the bottom of the will. Draco leaned down to read the note scrawled at the foot of the page. The writing was shaky, as if penned by someone half-asleep. 15/11 Å 2 a.m. Å Lillimont Lake ØThat must be to get the sword,Æ Weasley breathed. ØBut why didnít they just hide it here with the rest?Æ ØWould you leave that lying around, Weasley?Æ Draco said. ØI donít know if you can leave it lying around,Æ Potter said, folding the will and replacing it in the box. ØThat swordís not normal. I gave it to Dumbledore, but it came out of nothing when I needed it. I reckon if this person just left it here, it might go back into the school.Æ ØWhat do you mean, into the school?Æ said Granger skeptically. ØI mean the school might sort ofîreclaim it, until someone else does something brave. It came to me because I was rescuing Ginny, didnít it? Where was it before then? I dunno. Maybe weíve even got to do something to earn it, again.Æ Potter rose to his feet, the box clutched so tightly in his Disillusioned hands that the charm encompassed the whole box, making it disappear from sight. ØListen,Æ he said. ØBefore we go, I have an idea.Æ ØYeah?Æ said Weasley. ØI want headquarters to be here. This house.Æ There was a long silence. ØHarry,Æ said Granger tentatively, ØI donít know if thatís a good idea.Æ ØWhy not?Æ ØEveryone knows where it is, mate,Æ Weasley said, sounding uncomfortable. ØEven if itís under the Fidelius èÆ ØEspecially once itís under the Fidelius Charm,Æ Draco broke in. ØPotter, itís an entire house. Donít you think the Death Eaters might notice itís gone?Æ ØYeah,Æ Weasley said. ØOnce they see itís disappeared, theyíll know what we've done. Theyíll be able to line up down the street, just waiting for someone to make a mistake.Æ ØWe wonít make a mistake,Æ Potter said. ØWeíll put the garden, the hedge, everything, within the limits of the Charm. People can Apparate right in and out. It wonít matter that the Death Eaters technically know where it is.Æ More silence. Draco shifted. It felt somehow inappropriate to tell Potter no, standing here in the wreckage of the life heíd never had. ØListen,Æ Potter said. ØVoldeîÆ Weasley made a spluttering sound, and Potter let out an exasperated sigh. ØYou-Know-Who, then. Heís got one thing right.Æ ØYeah?Æ said Weasley doubtfully. ØWhatís that?Æ ØItís important where you put things,Æ Potter said. ØIt means something. You saw what was written outside, on the sign. People have already visited this house because it reminds them thereís hope for the whole thing to end. I donít think thereís a better place to put a new headquarters for the Order.Æ ØHarry,Æ said Granger timidly, and at once Draco wanted to tell her not to say what she was about to say. ØHarry, donít you think you might be feeling influenced by è by how it feels to see all this?Æ ØWhatís that supposed to mean?Æ Potterís voice rose. ØOf course youíre right,Æ she said quickly, Øand it would be really meaningful, but donít you think there could be a more secure, less è less noticeable place? Please, please donít be angry, HarryîIím only trying to think about what would be safest.Æ In the long second of silence, Draco worried that Potter might start yelling, here in the open air, where sound could carry down to the street. Instead, Potter lifted his Disillusionment Charm, standing silhouetted against a crag of wall. He didnít look angry, but Draco had never seen him look so serious. ØI know we want to stay safe,Æ Potter said. ØBut if that was what we cared about the most, weíd give up looking for Horcruxes and go to Siberia. Weíd sit back and let the Death Eaters take over Britain, and those people who wrote on that sign would think weíd vanished. And theyíd think, all right, well, guess weíd better give up too, then.Æ He shook his head. ØThis isnít reckless, Hermione. And itís not any more dangerous than any other place. Weíre not inviting them in. Weíre sending a message that theyíre never going to get in again.Æ He looked around at the ruined room, at the cinder-encrusted crib, and then back at the others, silently and invisibly watching him. ØI didnít ask to be a symbol of all this, all right? But he made me one. That was his message.Æ Potter pointed toward the crib, toward the impact radius that had buckled the walls, toward the ruin of the nursery. ØAnd now Iím of age, and I want to send a message back. My life isnít just a war memorial. And as long as Iím here, Iím not going to let anyone elseís life become one, either.Æ These words were followed by the longest silence yet. Draco felt outside himself. Potter was speaking like a hardened general, like someone who had already accepted death. It was as if Draco had entered another world by stepping over the Pottersí threshold. Part of him was in revolt, wishing to flee, to turn away and forget that he had ever seen any of this. The sight before him felt like an omen. Was this the future that awaited him and his parents, the destruction of home and family? If he was caught with the Gryffindors, it certainly would be. Was this a sorely needed reminder to get away from Potter, Granger, and Weasleyîto disrupt the odd kind of comfort that had developed over the preceding weeks, to escape to safety while he still could? A sniffling sound nearby took him out of his panic. It was Granger. She was trying not to cry. Draco swallowed. There would be time to think about all this later. He let out a loud, somewhat theatrical sigh. ØGod, Potter. Do you always do that to win arguments?Æ Granger released a wet-sounding little laugh, and Potterís mouth twitched, then broke into a reluctant half-grin. ØYeah,Æ said Weasley, Øall right, then. Iím convinced. But letís get out of here, shall we, before someone sees you standing there?Æ The next few days were filled with new purpose, as they experimented with the items Dumbledore had left for the Gryffindors. Between Potter and Weasley performing experiments on the Snitch and the Deluminator, Draco working with Granger to translate The Tales of Beedle the Bard from the original runes, and practice in Occlumency, it was almost a relief that they had no new leads on the Horcruxes. There simply wouldnít have been time. Then, three days before they were due to travel into Diagon Alley to see the Scavengersí Guild, their plans were turned on their heads. ØHermione, Malfoy,Æ said Weasley, hurrying into the dining room that evening, where Draco was poring over Beedle opposite Granger. ØCome and listen to this.Æ ØButîÆ Granger started to protest, quill hovering over her translation. ØNo, straight away.Æ Granger looked up at Weasleyís strained expression, exchanged a glance and nod with Draco, and they all strode into the sitting room. Draco wondered when, exactly, heíd started exchanging wordless nods with Hermione Granger. Potter was pacing before the fireplace as the Wizarding Wireless blared at a louder volume than usual through the room. Øè the back-to-Hogwarts rush, these measures will serve to protect young witches and wizards from impostors. All those who fail to present their papers at the checkpoint will be Flooed back to their point of origin until such time as they can present proper identification. Aurors will be on standby, as well as representatives from the newly minted Muggle-born Registration Commission. Aurelia Smeckworth reports on-site with more. èÆ Weasley tapped the Wireless twice with his wand, and the volume decreased. ØWhat are they talking about?Æ Granger said. ØWhat is this?Æ ØTheyíre installing security measures in Diagon Alley, Hogsmeade, and Platform 9.,Æ Weasley said grimly. ØFor Diagon Alley, youíve got to Floo in through the Leaky Cauldron and present your papers. Iíd bet anything itís to identify Muggle-borns, like we were expecting. You heard themîthe Muggle-born Registration Commission,Æ he said, voice dripping disgust. ØCanít we Apparate into Fred and Georgeís?Æ Granger said. ØOr Floo into their flat?Æ ØTheyíve warded the whole neighborhood shut,Æ Potter said. ØYouíd have to Apparate outside the Leaky Cauldron and go in the front way, which means going through the checkpoint anyway.Æ ØAnd the Flooís a no-go,Æ Weasley said. ØThe Department of Magical Transportationís expanded by three hundred and fifty percent, they said. Everything requires prior authorization.Æ ØBut è but èÆ Granger shook her head. ØThey canít do this! How can they? People wonít want to live in a world where they canít even nip down to the shop or call on a friendís house without having to prove who they are!Æ Draco sank into an armchair. ØIt wonít be forever,Æ he muttered. ØItís like Weasley says. Itís to register people. In a few months, once everyoneís registered, and all the unsavories are identified, theyíll say the threatís passed, and things will go back to normal, mostly.Æ ØYou know what this means, though,Æ Potter said. ØIn terms of the Horcrux.Æ ØYes.Æ Granger sighed and slumped onto the sofa. ØTransfigurations wonít be nearly enough. A simple Finite Incantatem will melt them off, and Probity Probes detect Transfigurations, too. Weíll have to wait for the Polyjuice Potion to finish brewing.Æ They all glanced toward the tentís second, smaller W.C., whose door was permanently shut to contain the foul smell of the Polyjuice Potion. Granger had set up several Stabilizing Charms on the small roomís confines to keep the potion from spilling every time they packed up the tent. Potter let out a frustrated sound. ØI hate the idea of that Horcrux getting picked up by someone and disappearing.Æ ØItís already had over two years to disappear, if it got chucked out in fifth year,Æ Weasley pointed out. ØItís only a few more weeks.Æ ØI suppose.Æ Potter sighed. ØAll right, so, weíll go next month, then.Æ ØSeptember 22nd,Æ said Granger, already turning through a planner that had appeared in her hands. Draco was amused to see it, crammed with writing as if she were preparing for O.W.L.s all over again. ØWho are we supposed to Polyjuice ourselves into, though?Æ said Weasley. ØWe'll want to go in a group,Æ Potter said, Øso, a family really will be best this time.Æ ØHogwarts term will already have started by then,Æ Granger said. ØSo we canít use anyone we know, can we? They should be at school on September 22nd.Æ ØA family with kids under Hogwarts age, then,Æ said Weasley. ØOr kids just out of Hogwarts, who havenít left home yet.Æ ØGood thinking,Æ Potter said. ØAnd weíll want it to be a family who can go through the checkpoint without being asked too many questions. A pure-blood family.Æ Draco sighed. He knew they were all going to look at him in unison even before they did it. And the worst thing was, he already had an answer ready. ØPansy Parkinson,Æ he said, somewhat reluctantly. ØShe has three younger brothers. Theyíre seven, nine, and ten, so, they wouldnít be at Hogwarts.Æ Weasley sniggered. ØWhat?Æ ØNothing,Æ said Weasley. ØItís just funny, knowing that Ms. Better-Than-You Parkinson was an accident.Æ Draco shot him a disgusted look. Actually, Pansyís mother had been pressured into having Pansy when she was nineteen, at her very traditional grandmotherís request, to prove her fertilityîa fact that had caused yearsí worth of bitter conflict between Pansy and her mother. But Draco wasnít about to tell Weasley that. ØMalfoy, youíve been to the Parkinsonsí house?Æ asked Potter. ØCan you get us in there?Æ Draco hesitated. Now that heíd made the suggestion, he was regretting it. Could this endanger Pansy's family? In theory, it shouldnít take more than a Sleeping Charm, or something similar, and a couple hours, and the Parkinsons would wake up thinking theyíd slept in, none the wiser. In practice, however è ØLook,Æ Draco said, Øif you do something to make yourselves stand out in Diagon Alley, youíd better be prepared to give away that itís you and not the Parkinsons doing it. Iím not èÆ Iím not going to be responsible for Pansyís family getting hurt, he thought, but he couldnít make himself say it. He found himself looking at Granger. She nodded, as if sheíd plucked the thought out of his head. He glanced away, frowning. He hadnít said he was comfortable with her using Legilimency on him è but he had looked at her, inexplicably, as if hoping she would do it. ØWe can make sure it's safe,Æ she said. ØWe can take precautions to make the Parkinsons seem like real victims if something goes wrong. Iím sure the Death Eaters will be glad for an excuse to make us seem unhinged and dangerous, like a threat to society.Æ ØTheyíll love that,Æ Potter said with a humorless laugh. ØThe idea of us preying on innocent pure-bloods. Front page of the Prophet, probably.Æ ØAnd thatís just if things go awry,Æ Granger said. ØWe know what day weíre doing it, and we have weeksí notice. We should be able to control the circumstances very tightly.Æ ØHang on,Æ said Weasley, looking at Draco blankly. ØYes?Æ Draco said. ØYou said, if you do something to make yourselves stand out.Æ He didnít elaborate. Draco arched an eyebrow. ØYeah, Weasley. Would you like me to restrict myself to one-syllable words in future?Æ Weasley didnít even go red. ØYouíre trying to get out of coming." ØGet out of it?Æ Draco said. ØExcuse me, when did I ever suggest I was going to come?Æ But now, Draco realized, Potter and Granger were both looking at him like heíd done something horrible. ØWhat?Æ he said indignantly. ØIíd prefer not to go dancing into the middle of Diagon Alley unless absolutely necessary. ØPotter, thatís not my problem.Æ ØMerlinís pants.Æ Weasley let out a disbelieving laugh. ØI knew it. I knew it. ØWhat are you here for, Malfoy? A holiday?Æ ØNo, troll-brain, Iím here to get back to my parents!Æ ØIf that was really all you cared about,Æ Granger said, Øwhy didnít you stay put in one of the places weíve sheltered? Granger was staring down at her planner, now, rather than at him, and she didnít sound angry. Her voice was low and controlled. But she was angry, or if not angry, something close. He could tell. Thatís her fault for making assumptions, Draco thought wildly. Itís her fault for wanting me toîtoî To help them? Well, he had helped them already, so why they couldnít be satisfied? ØWhat, exactly,Æ he said, Ødo you want from me?Æ Weasley sighed. He didnít even look angry or incredulous anymoreîmaybe resigned, which somehow felt a thousand times worse. ØIf you donít know by now,Æ said Weasley, ØIím not going to tell you.Æ He and Potter left the room together. Draco sat in silence, stewing in circular thoughts. All the time, the Potter cottage was at the back of his mind, the blasted edifice, the grim and hollow look in Potterís eyes as he talked around what heíd lost. ØSome sort of declaration of loyalty, I suppose,Æ Draco muttered. ØMm?Æ Granger said, still frowning at the carpet. ØThatís what Weasley wants from me. A declaration of loyalty to the Order of the Phoenix, and for me to renounce everything Iíve ever done, and è and some kind of groveling apology, right? And for me to toss my whole life into fighting the Dark Lord like I have no sense of self-preservation, like the three of you. So, basically, for me to be absolutely nothing like myself.Æ Granger didnít say anything. Her silence only made Draco feel more defensive. ØI told them, I said Iíd help you with the Parkinsons. What, thatís not enough?Æ He shook his head and shoved his hair back. ØI donít even want to do it. I bet Iíll be putting Pansy in danger, her whole family, and for what? For you three and yourîand yourîbut Iím doing it! And thatís still not enough? Whatís going to be enough, then? When I join up? When I put myself in mortal danger for you three and your cause? ØRonís angry youíre not going into Diagon Alley, thatís all.Æ Draco let out a frustrated sound and moved forward in the armchair until he was on the edge of its seat. ØYou donít understand, Granger. Itís all the same thing, donít you get it? There are no degrees here. Going into Diagon Alley has the same risks as hunting down the Dark Lord. Iím not being paranoid, all right? Iím not being dramatic. Itís death, or nothing.Æ He shook his head hard. ØYou don't know the Death Eaters. They donít reserve the Killing Curse for special occasions.Æ She didnít speak for a long time, but when she glanced over, he thought her expression looked softer. Some of the twisted feeling in his stomach eased, and he wasnít sure why. What, he thought with irony, did he really care whether Hermione Granger thought he was spineless? He was startled to realize that he knew the answer to that question. He did care, actually. He didnít want her to think he was a coward. Thatís because Iím not a coward, he told himself, annoyed. It had nothing to do with her opinion and everything to do with accuracy. Granger moved her planner from her lap to the sofa cushion. He eyed the lines of text all the way down its pages, her handwriting miniscule from years of overperformance on essays. ØIíve been wondering,Æ she said. ØWhat did you think of when you put on the diadem?Æ Dracoís mouth closed hard, so that his teeth clicked together. Granger met his eyes. ØStop,Æ he said, instantly looking away from her. ØStop doing that.Æ ØStop what?Æ ØI know what youíre doing.Æ ØIím not trying to use Legilimency on you, Malfoy.Æ She sounded surprised. ØIím not very good at it. I definitely canít do it nonverbally, let alone wandlessly.Æ ØButîÆ He frowned, confused. Hadnít she just seemed to know exactly what he was thinking about Pansyís family? He composed himself and shook his head. The point was that she couldnít see inside his head. Good: everything was still private. So, why was he blabbing to her about the Death Eaters? He didnít have to say anything. ØI told you before,Æ he said. ØNothing important.Æ ØFine,Æ she said, standing. ØIím going to go back to working on the runes.Æ ØFine,Æ he said. ØSo will I.Æ ØI donít need your help.Æ ØOh, stop being a martyr, Granger, youíll be up all night finishing The Fountain of Fair Fortune.Æ She sniffed, but didnít object. So he slouched into the chair opposite her at the dining table, and they worked on their translations, and every so often he thought he felt her eyes on him, but he never looked up. The tent was frosty for a few days after that, but they had so many plans to distract them now that even Ron couldnít seem to hold the grudge properly. Harry threw yet another question into the mix by informing them that Gregorovitch was dead, and Voldemortîwhom theyíd started referring to exclusively as You-Know-Who, as Ronís nerves had reached such an advanced state about itîwas now pursuing a thief. Harry had described this thief to them with nearly no identifying information, though, so Hermione insisted they shelve the question, to his annoyance. Once The Tales of Beedle the Bard was fully translated, they turned their attention to the Parkinson transformation problem. Hermione, Harry, and Ron asked Malfoy every question they could imagine, outlining every detail of the Parkinson estate and what they would need to do to break in, use the Floo to Diagon Alley, and make their exit without being seen. The plan was to deliver a batch of delicate chocolate-dipped madeleines, the Parkinson family favorite, the evening before. These were expensive cookies from Calaphorís Confections, whose icing played and swirled in enticing patterns upon their golden surfaces: fireworks, flowers opening, raindrops colliding with a sugary blue pond. Ron choked on his breakfast and had to be rescued by Harry with a quick Anapneo when Malfoy told them exactly how much a box of five said cookies would cost. They would fill each cookie with a delayed-reactant Sleepiness Solution. Six hours after ingestion, the eater would fall into fourteen hours of deep and dreamless sleep. The Gryffindors would come in through Pansyís bedroom windowîwhich she never locked, in order to sneak out during holidaysîand Floo out from the Parkinsonsí house at eight in the morning. They would, if all went to plan, return to Diagon Alley by ten, with hours to spare before the Parkinsons woke up. Still, there were details to iron out, loopholes to close. The Parkinsonsí house-elf would need to be distracted, and Malfoy had told them that the gardener also came on Thursday mornings, an inconvenient coincidence. They needed to brew the Sleepiness Solution to order, too, to ensure that they could delay its effects by the correct amount of time. Hermione, who was moving into the final stages of the Polyjuice brew, left this other potion to Harry and Malfoyís care, which resulted in the three boys taking a lot of unintended naps. Meanwhile, frustrated that Beedle the Bard was of no immediate use, Hermione had returned to practicing the Fidelius Charm. In her homework planner, she blocked off two hours a day for it: between three and five oíclock, she wore the diadem a short distance from the tent, whether woodland, clearing, or mountainside, and practiced the various elements of the charm. These hours quickly became the best of her day. With the diadem, she finally felt that she was the witch her teachers and friends seemed to think she was. She felt cool-headed and confident; she felt logical, unflustered, endlessly capable. With the diadem on, she was finally deserving of positive regard, and so it was almost maddening that these hours were spent alone, where no one could see this better version of her. In fact, she began to feel, in the hours that she wasnít wearing the diadem, its absence. At first, these feelings alarmed herîwas this a dependency?îbut no, the feelings had nothing to do with the diadem, really. They were about her own innumerable flaws, her own countless imperfections. The diadem, like a light shined into a dark place, had helped her to see what was already horrible about herself, what had always been horrible. And sheíd known it all along, hadnít she, in some quiet, unacknowledged corner of her heart? The truth was that she was incompetent, and only managed to get lucky in front of other people, so she had fooled them into thinking she was competent; the truth was that she was self-righteous, condescending, and insufferable, and Harry and Ron only put up with her because they felt sorry for her; the truth was that her parents were disappointed in her and probably always would be; the truth was that she was deeply unattractive and unable to compensate for it with all the other things sheíd tried to use as compensation, which was why Ron had never done anything resembling commitment and also why Harry seemed embarrassed whenever they had a moment approaching chemistry; the truth was that she was a pathetic little Mudblood who would never belong in the Wizarding World and could only ever hope to belong by riding the coattails of greater wizards, real wizards. Hermione didnít speak about the diadem, or these important realizations, to the others. She knew they wouldnít understand, and she knew that any mention of this kind of thing would alarm them, since it was a Horcrux, and then they might try to take the diadem away from her, and the idea was painfulînot because she was so attached to the diadem, no, she wasnít reliant on it (wasnít she very strictly confining her usage of it to two hours a day?), it was just that it was a useful tool for self-improvement. This afternoon, a week into September, hidden away in her secluded copse to practice, sheíd spent nearly the whole two hours trying other spells than the Fidelius, because she didnít want to lie to her friends, and if they asked if she was ready to perform the Fidelius, she had to be able to tell them no, so that she could keep working with the diadem, working to become a better witch, a better person, no longer a failure. And surely this was proof that everything was normal, and that she was fine, because she was still doing things like worrying about lying to Harry and Ron, which was a normal thing to feel. ØGranger.Æ Hermione whirled around, her wand still at the ready. But it was just Malfoy, standing between the trees. She lowered her wand. He was looking at the diadem with an expression that she didnít like. He had never told her, after all, what the diadem did for him. Was he jealous of her? Did he plan to take the diadem for himself? She would have to make sure to keep it safely in her bag, where he couldnít get at it. ØYes?Æ she said. ØPotterís made dinner. It smells repulsive, so, Weasleyís requesting you fix it.Æ Hermione let out a laugh. It was higher, sharper than her usual laugh, she could hear that. She preferred this new laugh. ØAll right,Æ she said, approaching. ØAre you trying to learn the whole spellbook?Æ Malfoy said. ØYouíve been practicing for weeks.Æ ØNone of your business.Æ Malfoy glanced over at her, one eyebrow raised. She wasnít imagining it: his eyes were lingering on the diadem. She touched it, feeling defensive, her fingertips reveling in the cool texture of the silver. ØHelpful, isnít it?Æ he said. Hermione narrowed her eyes at him. ØYes." ØDo you think you can do the charm, yet?Æ ØNo.Æ They had stopped outside the tent. Malfoy raised his eyebrows. ØYouíre not planning on wearing the thing to dinner, are you?Æ Hermione considered. One side of his mouth was curled, and she couldnít tell if it was a joke or not. Would anyone notice if she wore the diadem to dinner? She didnít want to take it off, really. On the other hand, she didnít want to draw more attention to the diadem than necessary. Malfoy eyeing it this way felt questionable enough; the idea of Harry and Ron doing the same was unappealing. ØNo,Æ she said. ØI suppose not.Æ Malfoy watched her, waiting. She lifted her hands to her head and touched the diadem. She ran her fingers over the silver filigree, over the sapphires. She pressed her fingertips into the diadem and shifted it back and forth on her brow, not wanting to lift it, wanting to leave it there until the coolness and clarity sank in so deeply that they could never be removed. Finally, with an effort that suggested she was lifting something far heavier, she took the diadem from her head. A sudden headache skewered her behind her left eye. Hermione hunched over, pressing one hand to her forehead, the diadem still clenched in the other. ØGranger?Æ Malfoy took a step toward her. She wanted to move back, to hold the diadem close to her chest, to make sure he didnít snatch it. But that wary look on his face told her that she shouldnít be so obvious. No. It was better that he not realize she felt protective about it. Much better that way. She forced herself to straighten up. ØSorry. Headache, itís been bothering me all afternoon.Æ And she dropped the diadem carelessly into the beaded bag as if it meant nothing. Her thoughts were on the diadem throughout dinner, and on Malfoy. She watched him for clues of suspicion, but he seemed perfectly normal, sitting with his rigid posture in his chair, narrow face serious, grey eyes glittering coolly, only contributing when he could sound clever. As dinner wore on, the anxiety began to pull at her again, the awareness of her inadequacy. She wanted to read something, but what good was reading when she would never really come to anything? She felt jittery and sad, and unsure how to alleviate it. ØHermione?Æ Harry said. ØOhîIím sorry, what?Æ ØI said, are you all right? You look a bit peaky.Æ ØYes, Iím fine,Æ she said at once, making herself smile, because he didnít really care, after all, he was only asking because he felt sorry for her; that was the basis of their whole friendship, wasnít it? He and Ron had turned back for the girl crying in the bathroom, because they were brave and good, and she was pathetic. But it was all right. She would put up a good show until tomorrow, when, between the hours of three and five p.m., she could focus again on her self-improvement. Draco tapped quietly on the door. He had cast Muffliato down the hall, and he was fairly certain Granger was asleep, but caution was important. ØCome in, Hermione,Æ called Potterís voice. ØItís not Hermione,Æ Draco said. A pause. Then the door cracked open, and Weasley stuck his face through, frowning. ØWhat?Æ ØI need to talk with you,Æ Draco said, Øobviously, as Iím standing here. Now, let me in, would you?Æ Weasley sighed, then retreated. He hopped up onto his bed, his maroon pajamas clashing gloriously with the red-orange bedspread. Draco shut the door behind himself. This was the largest bedroom of the flat, with two full beds, one for each twin. Draco had initially thought it was odd that two grown men would want to share sleeping quarters, until heíd seen the stacks of notebooks written by the pair of them at all hours of the night. They would presumably have spent half their life going from room to room to collaborate if theyíd separated themselves. ØWhatís up?Æ said Potter from the other bed, sounding wary. ØItís Granger.Æ ØWhat about her?Æ Weasley asked, suddenly alert. ØIs she all right?Æ ØI donít know,Æ Draco said. ØI canít tell.Æ ØWhatís that mean?Æ ØItís the diadem. Somethingís wrong with it.Æ Weasley and Potter exchanged an uneasy glance. ØWhen I went to get her for dinner earlier, she was è she seemed off. I canít describe it.Æ ØShe did take it off, though, right?Æ said Weasley. Draco nodded. ØBut she buckled over when she did. She said it was a headache and obviously thought she was doing really well at lying.Æ ØShe did seem weird over dinner,Æ Potter said doubtfully. ØAnd sheís been quiet during Occlumency lessons the past few days.Æ Draco crossed his arms. ØYeah. Sheís got to stop using it.Æ Weasley was looking guilty now. ØMerlin. I thought it was all right, because sheís seemed so normal. She hasnít lost her memory, she hasnít gone all scared and quiet like Ginny did in second year è and itís only two weeks. It took Ginny ages to get influenced by the diary.Æ Draco thought of the way Granger had touched the diadem before all this, the way sheíd looked at it almost tenderly. ØI donít know,Æ he said again. ØWell,Æ Potter said firmly, Øwe canít let it get any further. Weíll talk to her tomorrow, all of us.Æ ØYeah. Good,Æ Draco said. ØBut donít è donít mention that it was me who brought it up, all right?Æ Weasley looked nonplussed. ØWhy not?Æ ØI donít know,Æ Draco muttered, yet again. ØJust donít.Æ True to their word, neither Potter nor Weasley mentioned his role. Over lunch, Potter said, ØListen, Hermione, Ron and I have been thinking about that diadem.Æ Grangerís head jerked up. She looked shifty. ØWhat about it?Æ she said, sounding more present than she had in a conversation in days. ØWe just reckon it might be good if you took a break from it, thatís all,Æ said Weasley with a shrug. ØDid I do something wrong?Æ This was such a strange response that Draco exchanged a bewildered look with both Potter and Weasley. ØErîno,Æ Potter said slowly, Øitís just to be safe. It is a Horcrux, after all. I know itís useful, but itís still èÆ ØOh, no, Harry,Æ Granger said. ØPlease, not right now. I think Iím really close with the Fidelius Charm. I canít take a break now.Æ Potter grimaced. ØHow close?Æ Granger thought for a second. ØThree days. Three more days, and I really think Iíll have it. We can try it this Friday on the cottage itself. Is that all right?Æ No one answered. She looked around at Draco, Potter, and Weasley, and let out a sigh. ØIím really sorry if Iíve seemed distracted. But Iím so frustrated about that book Dumbledore left me. I donít understand what Iím supposed to do with it. And I keep wishing I could be more help with Occlumency, too.Æ ØOh, címon, Hermione, itís all right,Æ said Weasley bracingly. ØNone of us know what Dumbledore was playing at with the things he left us, do we? And Iíve been useless for Harryís Occlumency lessons for over a month.Æ Weasley was sounding relieved, and as Potter engaged in consoling Granger, he looked less wary, too, but Draco didnít trust anything that had come out of her mouth. Sheíd said it all very convincingly, but he kept thinking of the way sheíd dug her fingers into the diadem, hesitating, hedging, before being able to dislodge it from her head. If this was the diadem trying to play them all, he had to find a way to stay a step ahead. ØAll right, all right,Æ Draco said, interrupting Potter. ØYeah, very reassuring, weíre all really useless and have no idea what the old man was thinking when he wrote his will. The point is, Granger, youíve been wearing the diadem for two weeks. I think we should see some results now.Æ Potter and Weasley both looked annoyed. Granger didnít. For a split instant, there was an odd calculating look on her face that, frankly, unnerved Draco, but he continued to pretend not to notice anything out of the ordinary. ØI donít want to waste another afternoon like the one in the woods,Æ she said evenly. ØIíve told you I need three days, Malfoy, and if you donít like it, youíre welcome to try the charm yourself.Æ Draco gave an extravagant roll of his eyes. ØFine. Have your practice hours, then. But Iím going to watch. I think somebody should be making sure weíre all actually as productive as weíre pretending to be.Æ He gave her his best sneer and left the table before she could argue. That afternoon, just as Draco had expected, Granger tried to talk him out of following her. She tried avenue after avenue of argument, trying to pressure him to clean the kitchen, to help Potter and Weasley with the Parkinson plans, to check on the Polyjuice and the Sleepiness Solution, to draw up a lesson plan for Occlumency going forward, on and on. Draco didnít let a single point take hold. He yawned often, checked his extremely expensive watch more than once, and finally Granger relented, her face rigid as she ported Charms of the Ancient Days and the diadem out of the tent. It was a crisp, sunny September day. Draco sat on a large, flat rock, sunning himself like a cat while he watched Granger practicing. Every so often, she cast an irritated look back at him. The second day was much the same. Granger tried to wriggle out of supervision. Draco insisted, embodying obnoxious obliviousness so completely that Weasley cornered him after dinner to ask him exactly what he was playing at. ØIím keeping an eye on her,Æ Draco muttered, Øand Iím trying to do it without being obvious, so shut up.Æ On the third day, he decided to play his hand. At the end of her practice session, before she had taken the diadem from her head, he told her, ØThe charm looks good.Æ Granger looked suspicious. ØThank you." ØSo, what are you planning on doing after you pretend to try it tomorrow?Æ She froze. Her eyes flicked over to the tentîthey were, yet again, in a patch of anonymous woodlandîand back to Draco. ØWhat è what are you talking about?Æ she said hoarsely. He lifted his shoulders. ØYou want to keep wearing that diadem. Those two in there want you to stop. And if you do the Fidelius Charm correctly, theyíll make you stop. So, youíre planning to throw the attempt.Æ He paused, then added, for good measure, ØItís what Iíd do.Æ Granger swallowed hard. She looked deeply disturbed. As she pushed a straggly lock of hair out of her face, she whispered, ØIím sorryÆînot to him, but to herself. Hearing that whisper, Draco felt a new, cold feeling. Not alarm, not unease, but real fear. What was it doing to her? What was it telling her? Whatever it was, he had to make her think he was on her sideîher side, and the diademís, together. ØSo, you èÆ She looked at him with a glazed kind of distrust. ØYouíre not trying to stop me?Æ ØWhy should I? Iím a Slytherin, Granger. We donít say no to a bit of extra power, because weíre not idiots.Æ A sudden gleam lit in her eye. A smirk pulled at her mouth. Dracoís stomach lurched, hard. That expression wasnít hers at all. But in the next second she seemed back inside herself. ØRight,Æ she said slowly. ØI do want to perform the Fidelius properly, Malfoy. Thatís the reason for all this.Æ ØYeah, sure. Thatís part of it.Æ Dracoís mind was racing, still caught on that gleam, that smirk. The Horcrux was already displacing her inside her own body. For split seconds, yes, but still. ØYou know,Æ he said casually, Øif you get the charm right, though, we could probably convince Potter and Weasley that the diadem isnít dangerous. Theyíre too busy to notice anything right now. If you keep acting normally, the way youíve been acting, I bet they wonít care that you keep wearing it.Æ Granger bit her lip. ØYou noticed. You must have seen me acting differently. I must have done something wrong.Æ ØGranger, Granger,Æ he drawled. ØIíve been paying attention to the diadem. No, youíve been doing really well.Æ ØReally well?Æ she repeated, slightly choked. ØReally well,Æ he said again. ØYeah. Do the Fidelius Charm properly. The more I think about it, the more I think thatís the way to convince those two that you should keep wearing the diadem. Good results, see?Æ Granger seemed lost in thought for a while. She stared off at the distant glint of a lake through the trees. ØWell,Æ Draco said. ØYou know where I stand, now, anyway. Youíd better take that off before the others see you wearing it. ItísîÆ He checked his watch. ØTwo past the hour, after all.Æ Over dinner, Grangerís eyes kept flicking nervously onto Draco. He held her gaze, but didnít let himself seem perturbed by what heíd seen. There was no doubt that they needed the Fidelius Charm performed. If Granger could just execute it before the diadem got a further hold, then they could take that tiara and stick it in a locked box until they had the means to destroy it. But if the diadem convinced her to throw the Fidelius Charm the next day, Draco didnít know what they could do. He didnít dare communicate any of this to Potter or Weasley, either, because Granger watched him from the moment he got up from the dinner table to the moment he retreated into the guest bedroom. He lay awake late that night, unable to sleep, or to stop thinking about Grangerís stricken little whisper, Iím sorry. It was nearly one in the morning when he heard it: the unmistakable creak of a floorboard outside. Dracoís heart dropped. He suddenly understood how the diadem had taken hold so quickly, despite being wornîseeminglyîunder such rigid time limits. Draco slipped out of bed, took his wand from the bedside table, and Silenced his own footsteps. He also Silenced his bedroom door, and its hinges, before opening it. And across the darkened apartment he saw her. Granger, sitting up unnaturally straight, her back to him, the diadem upon her head. He knew without needing to see her face that she was asleep. He knew that the diadem, unable to wrest control from her waking mind, had seeped into her subconscious instead. He wondered how many nights of the past two and a half weeks she had been sent tottering through the apartment, drawn irresistibly to the beaded bag she always left on the mantel, sleepwalking into the grip of its contents. He knew instinctively that if she turned around and saw him, she would attack, and she would do so not with her own dueling acumen but with the lethal skill of Lord Voldemort. He would have exactly one chance to take the Horcrux from her. Draco closed the Silenced door and ducked down so that he could barely see the crown of her head and the point of the tiara. Then he crept through the apartment. Halfway through, he remembered to silence his breaths as well as his footsteps. She was sitting as motionless as stone, as if she weren't even breathing. He emerged from the dining room. She was so close now, her head fixed forward as if she were gazing into a fire that did not burn in the empty grate. The moonlight was glowing on the diademís sapphires. Draco straightened up. He steeled himself, and thenîin one clean motionîhis hand shot out and swept the diadem from Grangerís head. She collapsed as if he had struck her dead. Dracoís heart stopped. He vaulted the sofa, letting the diadem clatter to the ground. ØGranger,Æ he tried to say, before remembering the Silencing Charms. When he'd undone them, he said, ØGranger!Æ and seized one of her shoulders, shaking her. ØGranger?Æ Her eyes came open slowly, and relief flooded through him. She was aliveîalive, if groggy and confused. ØMalfoy? What am è whatís èÆ She looked around the sitting room, and her eyes fixed on the diadem. She still didnít seem to understand. ØIt was controlling you in your sleep, Granger,Æ Draco said. Her eyes traveled downward, and he realized he was still holding tightly to her shoulder. He let go immediately, feeling a strange rush of heat, and sat hard on the sofa next to her. ØWeíre putting that thing away,Æ he said. ØNone of us is touching it again.Æ Granger looked stricken. ØNo,Æ she whispered. ØYou donít understand. I need it.Æ ØYou can do the Fidelius Charm yourself.Æ ØI canít!Æ The sentence burst out of her, and suddenly she was crying. The trance had shattered. ØI canít do it right è I canít do anything right èÆ ØGranger,Æ said Draco, bewildered. ØWhat are youîwhat?Æ ØAll I do is fail over and over againîin third year, all the Dementors were coming and Harry tried to teach me, he triedîand then, in fifth year, in the Department of Mysteries, I stopped paying attention and I nearly died, and Harry could have died too, trying to save meîand DumbledoreîDumbledoreîI was right there, I could have èÆ ØGranger. è Granger?Æ Dracoís heart was beating very hard. He had no idea what to do. She was shaking harder and harder, muttering through sobs so that he could hardly hear her, and he was still shaky himself from the residual panic, the thought that heíd somehow killed her in the moment heíd taken the diadem from her head. ØHermione,Æ he said. She startled and looked over at him. She was wearing the same oversized T-shirt sheíd worn in her house, that Muggle shirt. He could see now that it had the name Granger Dental Studio on it, with an image of a smiling tooth, a logo that had faded so much over time that it was nearly invisible. He couldnít believe that this was the insecurity the diadem had managed to play on. He couldnít believe Hermione bloody Granger was actually, seriously, legitimately insecure about her magical aptitude. ØLook,Æ he said, Ødid you know, last year, that all the first-years knew who you were?Æ ØWh-what?Æ she said, looking startled. ØYeah. Because you got so many åOutstandingí O.W.L.s that the teachers were telling first-years about you, by name. God, even the Slytherin first-years used to talk about you like you were the second coming of Rowena Ravenclaw, and then the added insult that youíre not even a Ravenclaw. Absolutely appalling.Æ Draco shook his head in disgust. ØYou know Goyle talks about you all the time? You know heíd kill to have brains like yours? And itís not just him. ØOh, so I suppose it doesnít really matter that youíve been setting up protective enchantments wherever we go, or that youíve learned those spells to check for hexes and curses, or that you packed ingredients forîapparentlyîevery potion in the world. ØNow youíre just parroting my mother, and sheís never happy.Æ Granger let out a small, surprised laugh. ØWhat?Æ ØWell, you always get so angry when people say anything about your mother.Æ ØYeah, because thatís other people. They donít know what theyíre talking about.Æ Granger sniffled and wiped her face on her shoulder. ØIím just so afraid of not being good enough to do anything. This charm è we really need it, and so of course I canít do it. Itís like the second I know something awful will happen if I fail, all I can do is fail. I want to do important things, but I feel like if I am good at anything, itís things likeîlike quizzes about Gilderoy Lockhartís favorite color, and not whatís really important.Æ Draco kneaded his temple with his fingertips. This person she was describing only seemed tangentially related to herself, but he knew it was useless to say that. His eyes moved back onto the diadem, which glimmered innocently in the pool of moonlight where it had fallen. ØWhat else did it tell you?Æ he asked, his voice quiet and rough. ØIt è it didnít tell me èÆ She still sounded defensive. ØWhat else have you been thinking the past two weeks, then.Æ Granger swallowed and pushed her hands over her hair, moving the mass of frizzy curls back until, for once, he could see her full profile, her ears, the curve of her jaw. ØThat Harry and Ron donít è that Iím not good enough for them, that they think Iím annoying and theyíre just putting up with me. And I could give you lots of evidence for that, too. Andîthat my parents are disappointed in me, which is also true. Iím sorry? What would that do? Disagreement with all of it? If she really believed these things, what would his opinion change? The diadem continued to gleam, and he hated it. The hate felt more productive. ØYou know what happens when I put that thing on?Æ he said with sharp distaste. ØWhat?Æ Granger asked. ØI know all about the people in the room.Æ For some reason, Grangerís cheeks colored. ØYou mean Legilimency?Æ ØNo. I mean, I could always tell Weasley was insecure about his money problems and Potter had a complex about being the odd one out, but with the diadem on, wellîI could rip people wide open with it, if I wanted. It made it so easy to see where the power is, and where people feel powerless. And Weasley looks at you and I can see heís in love with you. Probably has been for years. And Potter looks at you both andîÆ Draco grimaced. ØHeíd just die if anything happened to either of you. God, itís like reading his diary, or something. All three of you are like that.
