Then she asked, ØWhy didnít you want to talk about that?Æ A short, bitter laugh escaped him. ØWell, you see, Granger, it also works on me. And as it turns out, I donít particularly want to intuit what people are thinking or feeling about me, or where I stand in the room.Æ He let out a disgusted noise. ØI put it on and I could see it all so clearly, what everyone è and a couple years ago I wouldíve thought it was great, or funny, I wouldíve loved using it è but I just wanted to è I donít know.Æ He shook his head, looking out the window at the surrounding forest. ØWanted to get out. Not participate. Not have to be thought about, or exist.Æ His voice was scratchy and tired. He wanted to go abroad from himself. That was all. ØIíve felt that way before.Æ Granger settled against the opposite side of the sofa, hugging a throw pillow to her middle. ØI went to a Muggle primary school, obviously, and it was awful. Not the schoolîI was awful. I hate thinking about the way I was as a little girl. Always jeering at other people for getting anything the slightest bit wrong. So, obviously I had no friends, and when I got my Hogwarts letter, I thought, well, this is my chance to leave all that behind me.Æ A smile pulled at her mouth, and Draco knew she was remembering opening that letter, the one heíd waited for so breathlessly the summer before first year, too. ØSo,Æ she went on, ØI prepared and prepared and prepared. It wasnít just a new school, but an entirely new world, so I thought I could justîjust strip it all off and leave everything I used to be behind, and Iíd be so good at magic that everyone would want to be my friend. But then I got to Hogwarts, and I was still really lonely, and I heard Ron saying one day that I had no friends, and it sort of made me realize Iíd brought my old self along with me. I couldnít just unbuckle her and throw her by the wayside. It was still me.Æ She lifted her shoulders. Met his eyes. ØSorry, Malfoy. I donít think you can get out of it. What you used to be.Æ ØI know,Æ Draco said, settling against his own end of the sofa. ØIt was just a feeling.Æ There was a quiet silence. They regarded each other, the diadem discarded, forgotten momentarily. ØAnyway,Æ Granger said, Øif you could intuit what we were all thinking and feeling, then it canít have been all bad.Æ ØYeah? Why not?Æ ØI was in the room, too.Æ Draco considered her. She looked calm. She looked, for the first time in days, like herself. ØYeah,Æ he said. As heíd worn the diadem, heíd analyzed her the way heíd analyzed the others, and the way her body had been angled toward him had meant cautious interest, and the tilt of her head had meant openness and consideration, and the way her cheeks had colored when heíd met her eyes had meant something heíd definitely never have expected from Hermione Granger. Heíd read wariness and lingering hostility all over Potter and Weasley, but in her è ØSort of seemed like you didnít hate me, Granger.Æ Her lips pressed together in one of those suppressed smiles that were starting to feel familiar to him, that were starting to feel satisfying to elicit. ØNo,Æ she said. ØI donít hate you.Æ ØI donít hate you, either,Æ he said. Chapter End Notes tumblr | instagram Chapter 10: The Scavengers' Guild Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Hermione was quiet as they packed up the next morning. Sheíd slept poorly, startling awake again and again with the fear that sheíd find herself sleepwalking toward the door, one hand outstretched. She finally understood the feeling that Ginny had tried to articulate years agoî the feeling of being contaminated. Luckily, no one seemed much in the mood to talk. It was still dark outside, and the moon was low, washing the tentís crimson fabric into a dark gray. Theyíd risen well before dawn, hoping for the village streets to be empty so they could attempt the charm in private. The Potter cottage was on the outskirts of Godricís Hollow, which would help, but it was best to be safe. Hermione had convinced Malfoy not to tell Harry and Ron about what the diadem had done to her. She felt ashamed even to imagine confessing such a glaring errorîthat sheíd allowed the Horcrux to penetrate so deeply into her mind. They would want to know what the diadem had made her do and think, and she didnít think she could recount it all again. Once had been painful enough. She kept glancing up from her bag, where she was packing the tentís stakes, to look at Malfoy. Meanwhile, Malfoyís indistinct outline drew his wand. He and Harry had decided to prepare for a fight, in case they were ambushed. For now, they were inconspicuous enough îRon was holding her notes close to the hedge, to camouflage the single sheet of parchment appearing to suspend itself in midairîbut, of course, the eventual casting of the spell would be unavoidably obvious. Oddly, the thought managed to bolster her where endless reassurances from her friends always failed. Unknown Slytherin first-years discussing her had no motive at all. Hermione took a deep breath of the chilly dawn air. She looked back to her notes and lifted her wand. And then, as she began to speak, something wonderful happened. She spoke the two definitional incantations, and then the third in reverse, which would redouble their strength; she spoke them all as fluidly as she would have spoken her own name. As she cast the bounds of the charm, she fixed the Pottersí hedges in her mind, taking care to define a hundred yardsí safe air above the cottage, too, and a hundred yardsí safe earth below it. Perfectly on time, the memory of security floated to the top of her mind like cream: a blissful, ordinary evening in the Gryffindor Common Room, first year. Hermione had considered choosing any number of Vanishing Spells for this memory, but sheíd settled on something all the clearer for how painful it was. Sheíd used the same memory for this incantation since her very first practice: Hagridís bristly face in third year, kind and sympathetic. It was its absence. A step into nothing, with the hope that something solid would rise to meet you. Sixteen yearsí neglect look like this: The collapse of structure. Broken windows from heavy storms and someoneís old disrespect. The unraveling and slow retreat of wallpaper. The exposure of wooden bones, and warp in those bones. Erosion on the outside, the crumbling of sills and lintels. Rot eating away at the inside. Subtraction. Andîaccumulation. The absorption of water into every element. Growth, in every damp patch, of mold. Growth, on metal surfaces, of the red grit of rust. The appearance of small bodies: rodents, insects. Ancient leavings from scavenging animals. Dust frothing up on every surface. They stood outside and cast Charms that sent air pouring through the whole building, spilling out through the cottageís rear face, whisking away every foul smell. They left the windows wide, inviting in the scents of autumn, as they scoured surfaces, pulled open old drawers, tugged furniture away from the walls, unscrewed rusted doorknobs, peeled off sheets of ruined wallpaper, ripped up the stained carpet in the side room, and pitched buckets of dust into the yard, where it flurried away in rags like ashes. Draco thought Potter wouldnít be able to stomach the work, to see the filth his old home had been mired in, but he was wrong. Granger was slower than the others. She kept pausing to add outcroppings of flowers. Then sheíd stand back and look at her handiwork, smile to herself, or brush her fingertips over the flower petals before moving on. They would be sleeping in the tent, pitched in the backyard, for weeks. There was something to sitting there on the old leather sofa, watching Granger stacking books of defensive spells on the newly polished shelves, watching Weasley massacre Potter at chess for the millionth time. In these moments, Draco felt comfortable. So probably he was going insane. Mostly, though, the days were filled with agitation over the upcoming visit to Diagon Alley. Theyíd decided that Potter was to play Pansyís father. Even with Polyjuice Potion, accents could occasionally peek through, and Weasleyís accent was about as far from Mr. Parkinsonís as a personís could be. Probably because he dreaded Weasleyís reaction if the other two ever actually did get involved. Anyway, Weasley made a terrible child, was the point. And Potter, while a marginally better option than Weasley, was still a thoroughly unconvincing Mr. Parkinson. In order to practice the familyís mannerisms, theyíd tried some Transfigurations to make Potter look more like Mr. Parkinson and Granger more like his wife. Granger didnít do too badlyîshe was able to adopt Mrs. Parkinsonís prim, critical air reasonably wellîbut Potter è anyone who had ever met Mr. Parkinson would think he was Confunded, or more dangerously, Imperiused. Potter just couldnít condescend the way he needed to. Draco imagined scenarios where Potter caved under pressure and started hexing people left and right in Diagon Alley. As September wore on, the news on the Wizarding Wireless grew grimmer. Reports from the Muggle-born Registration Commission now included ever-longer lists of names of people on the run, referred to as dangerous fugitives who should be apprehended on sight. Draco felt a small, disbelieving shock when Dean Thomas and Ted Tonks were read off the list. Thomas had been well-liked at school, milder and more thoughtful than most of the Gryffindors. As for Ted Tonks è Dracoís aunt had been disowned by the family for marrying him. If he died, it would mean Andromeda had lost him, and her entire family, for nothing. Nearly every evening, dry academic voices on the Wireless could be heard delivering new reports about the dangers of Muggle-borns. ØNew studies reveal that magic can only be passed down from witches or wizards through blood,Æ the reporters said, Øand as such, investigations into these so-called åMuggle-bornsí and how they have stolen Wizarding secrets are imperative.Æ They never listened to the reports all the way through. Weasley or Potter always turned them off, glancing at Granger, who would immediately busy herself with her planner or The Tales of Beedle the Bard, though she couldnít hide her strained expression. Sometimes Weasley gave Draco suspicious looks after these reports. At first Draco didnít understand why, but eventually he realized: Weasley thought he might get ideas. There was no denying that Draco did think about the Muggle-born reports. He thought about them while he cleaned the Potter cottage, while he got filth under his fingernails and sweat in his eyes. He thought about the broadcasts as he scrubbed his aching muscles in the shower, and for restless hours before he could get to sleep. The dissonance he felt about them was almost physically painful, because his feelings about blood status were tied up in his parents and his childhood and a world that no longer really seemed to exist. The broadcasts included familiar, comforting phrases like åthe community of wizardryí and åthe extraordinary nature of magical powersí and åwhat separates the magical from the mundane, the spectacular from the Muggle.í Draco had grown up around this kind of talk. Heíd always been taught to take pride in his wizarding heritage. Every time heíd reached a new magical milestoneîhis first use of accidental magic, for instance, and the first time he managed sparks with his motherís wandîhis parents had burst with enthusiasm in a way they hardly ever let themselves show. They had Flooed other families to tell them, and theyíd had traditional Wizarding celebrations for these events, and built into all this ritual was the fundamental idea that nobody else could really understand them, that only pure-blood families expressed Wizarding pride in such a true, legitimate way. So, this idea in the reports that Muggle-borns had somehow ØstolenÆ magic è Draco knew it was propaganda, but at the same time, he still felt in his gut the emotion that the propaganda played on. It was the fear that Muggles were encroaching on the Wizarding World, usurping what wizards had created for themselves. As if it werenít enough to look through A History of Magic and see the history of witch-burnings and wizard persecution, which was the reason theyíd gone into hiding in the first placeînow Muggle-borns were flooding into Hogwarts, like his father had always said, as if they belonged there? As if they didnít have a whole world of their own already? And if the barriers between the worlds continued to erode, who knew what it would mean for wizards and their always-delicate way of life, their reverence for everything magical? Draco had never had a real reason to rethink any of this. All his friends had been raised to think the same way, after allîraised well, as his mother called it. Life with Potter, Weasley, and Granger, though è it had begun to give him pause. He was living with a blood traitor, and someone raised by Muggles, and a Muggle-born. In pure-blood circles, there was deep suspicion about what these sorts of people would do to pure-blood traditions if given the chance: discard them, pollute them, corrupt them. But Draco had lived with these three for nearly two months now, and the strange thing about it was how normal it all felt. The more he thought about it, the more he suspected that they wouldnít actually give a damn if he wanted to carry on the magical traditions heíd been raised with. His whole life, Draco had felt that he, his family, and his friends were battling a threat to their way of life, grappling for power because the alternative to gaining power was ceding it, and to cede power would mean obliteration. But now, having crossed over enemy linesîliving with the three people who were the most emblematic of enemy territoryîhe found himself wondering where the threat was, because if it was here, he couldnít seem to find it. None of these destabilizing thoughts did anything to ease Dracoís nerves about the Diagon Alley visit. Granger kept assuring him that framing the Parkinsons as victims would absolve them of any blame, but Draco couldnít make himself believe it. The fact of the matter was, if the Parkinsons were used to damage the Death Eatersí cause, they would be viewed as a liability. The Parkinsons werenít Death Eaters themselvesîat least, they hadnít been when Draco had left Hogwartsîso at least they wouldnít be personally punished by the Dark Lord, but the Death Eaters could do quite enough damage on his behalf. As the visit drew closer and closer, Draco began to suffer from intrusive visions of Pansyís family under Death Eatersí wands, screaming from the Cruciatus. And the imagined scenarios seemed contagious. Sometimes, while Potter groaned over the chessboard or Weasley made ironic commentary on the Death Eaters lurking outside or Granger told them absentmindedly to shut up, please, as she was trying to read, Draco had sudden, lurid visions of them being tortured, too. Maybe he really was going mad. Three days before the trip to Diagon Alley, there was a loud crack in mid-afternoon. ØLook,Æ she said finally, Øthis evening, I was thinking èÆ ØNot now, Hermione,Æ said Weasley, going back to the path. ØBit busy here.Æ Grangerís cheeks flushed. ØFine, then,Æ she said coldly. She turned on her heel and strode for the cottageís back door. Draco was about to follow when Weasley said, ØMalfoy, hang on. I want a word.Æ Draco hesitated. Weasley wasnít working on the path anymore. Heíd put down his wand and was peering around Dracoís legs to see whether Granger had disappeared from view. ØWhatís going on?Æ Draco said slowly. ØPotter is all right, isnít he?Æ ØWhat? Oh, yeah, heís fine.Æ Weasley got to his feet. ØLook, Malfoy, itís Hermioneís birthday today. She thinks weíve forgotten, but we havenít. Harryís gone to get a cake and some decorations and a bottle of Firewhisky.Æ Weasley glanced at the cottage again and grimaced. ØSheíll be angry with me now for interrupting her, but I think she was about to bring it up, and it would ruin the surprise if she mentioned it herself.Æ He sighed. ØAnyway, point is, Harryís going to get back with all the decorations and I donít want her to see us at it. Weíre going to put away the tent and do the yard up. We only need half an hour or so. Could you keep her in the library between half-five and six?Æ Draco opened his mouth and closed it again. ØWhat?Æ Weasley said. ØNothing,Æ Draco said coolly. ØJust didnít think you had it in you, Weasley. This could actually be considered thoughtful.Æ Weasley reddened. ØOh, shut up. Will you do it or not?Æ Draco shrugged. ØYeah, Iíll keep her. Merlin only knows how Iíll manage to occupy Hermione Granger in a library, though.Æ Weasley barked out a laugh, seemingly before he could stop himself. Then he cleared his throat loudly and rubbed the back of his neck. ØThanks,Æ he forced out. ØEr. Andîthanks for helping with Parkinson as well, we know youíre worried about her, but nothing badís going to happen.Æ He said this last very quickly, as if it would help him to ignore the fact of what he was saying. Draco, totally blindsided now, could not actually form words. He just made a feeble sort of åeennghí sound, jerked his head in a nod that probably looked more like a spasm, and retreated to the cottage, wondering what in Merlinís name had just happened. Had Ron Weasley just attempted to extend an olive branch? The madness was truly spiraling. Granger was quiet that afternoon, while they worked through the books in the Pottersí library. Draco knew that she would like Potter and Weasleyís surprise, but it was hard not to want to say something about it when she had that miserable look on her face. He remembered how sheíd spoken about Potter and Weasley under the Horcruxís influence, terrified that they didnít really care about her, and now they seemed to have forgotten her birthday. But he managed to keep quiet. At six oíclock, he stood up from the mound of books, stretched, and said, ØShall we see whether Weasleyís blown up the garden yet?Æ Granger must have been really annoyed with Weasley, because she didnít defend him, just sighed, stood, and followed Draco out through the kitchen. They came out of the side door. Granger stopped in her tracks. Draco watched surprise, then delight, spread over her face. ØOh, Harry, Ron, you didnít!Æ she cried, running to hug her friends. Theyíd strung fairy lights across the gardenîsomewhat clumsily, Draco couldnít help thinking; he was used to his motherís impeccable dcorîand conjured a table on the small patio. It was laden with a small chocolate cake and a bottle of amber liquid. Sparklers were dashing through the air, forming the words HAPPY 18th BIRTHDAY HERMIONE. Draco watched the three of them for a moment, hugging in this idyllic square of grass enclosed by ten-foot hedges, and he felt as if he were detaching from himself, from the scene. The whole of it began to feel surreal. How could an evening like this be contained in the same world where, even now, the Dark Lordís forces were growing in power and number across the country? The imagined pictures came again, more graphic than ever, forcing Draco to blink hard at the stone wall of the cottageîWeasleyís gangly body twitching and jerking as Bella shrieked with laughter, Potterís glasses shattering as he writhed beneath a curse, Granger è He looked back at them. Granger was beaming so widely that her eyes were crescents, pushed up by her cheeks. She was speaking animatedly to Potter and Weasley, and Weasley was chortling, probably recounting how heíd snubbed Granger that afternoon to divert her, and Potter was laughing, too, the back of his hand rubbing his lightning scar absentmindedly. Three days from now, those three would walk into Diagon Alley, into the heart of things, and they were still woefully underprepared. Draco approached them. Their small circle opened to admit him. ØWere you in on this, too?Æ Granger said, still smiling. ØSort of,Æ he said stiffly. ØThank you. Itís a wonderful surprise.Æ She glanced back to Potter and Weasley. ØI canít believe you two remembered, in all this.Æ ØLook,Æ Draco said, ØIímîIím going to come into Diagon Alley with you three.Æ A brief, stunned pause. The Gryffindorsí smiles turned to expressions of incredulity. Draco crossed his arms. ØYouíre hopeless, Potter, you still have no idea how to act like Pansyís father. And Weasley, itíll be less obvious that youíre a 17-year-old in a 10-year-oldís body if there are two children. Andîanyway, itíll be safer.Æ There was a brief silence. Then Weasley said, ØYeah. All right. So, is this just to get a piece of cake, or?Æ Granger and Potter both started laughing. Draco shook his head, but a traitorous smile tugged at his mouth. ØIíll need something stronger than that, Weasley,Æ he said, picking up one of the glasses of Firewhisky and downing it in a single go. On the morning of September 22nd, they Apparated onto the outskirts of the Parkinson estate. The Parkinsons had their own private Quidditch pitch, as Mr. Parkinson was one of Nimbusís chief engineers. As they passed the pitch, Draco remembered playing three-on-three with Crabbe, Goyle, Pansy, Blaise, and Theo Nott, and a pang went through him. He wondered how the Slytherin Quidditch team was performing this year. It seemed almost ridiculous, the idea of team practices and scheduled matches still going on. There was a side gate at the limit of the Parkinsonsí property. Draco tapped the lock and said, ØCamelliaÆîPansyís grandmotherís name. It clicked and allowed them through. The Parkinson house was a long, rambling property, not as elegant as Malfoy Manor nor as large, but with modern touches like large glass sunrooms at either end. Draco led the way through the grounds in the half-darkness toward Pansyís window. A flowering trellis led up to her window, a romantic touch that sheíd always loved. When they stopped at its foot, Draco looked up and could almost see her there, sliding the window open, leaning one elbow against the sill, a catlike smile on her close-set features. Draco climbed up the trellis, which clacked softly against the wall of the house. Heíd done it a dozen times before, in the summer between fifth and sixth year, and soon the window was open, and all four of them were inside. The others crept out into the corridor. Weasley would find the family house-elf and slip her three drops of the Sleepiness Solution. Granger would use a Script Imitation Charm on something Mrs. Parkinson had written to forge a note to the gardener on the front door, rescheduling his visit. Potter would collect hairs from each family member and ensure that the potion had affected them correctly. It should have done: the cookies had been delivered by owl last night, each one personalized to a different family member, and Draco, spying through the kitchen window, had seen them eaten with relish. Draco was about to undertake his own part of the missionîto find the Parkinsonsí papersî when he hesitated. He took a long, slow breath and brushed the lilac comforter with two fingers. Pansyís light perfume hung on the air, a scent as familiar as his own home. He was startled to feel his eyes prickling hotly. He blinked hard, feeling stupid, but his imagination was getting away from him now. Was Pansy, even now, turning over in her bed in the Slytherin dormitories, about to wake early as usual? She could always be found doing her homework in the common room at the crack of dawn, tapping her wand on her knee rhythmically as if it were a drumstick. He wondered if she was still seeing Theo. The two of them had taken up some kind of relationship after Draco had broken things off last January, though Draco had always suspected its primary purpose was to make him jealous. But maybe it had become something else after his supposed death. He was surprised to realize he didnít mind the idea very much. His relationship with Pansy, like so much else at Hogwarts, seemed to belong to a kind of imagined version of himself now. Still, it took several more moments for him to walk through the door. Draco moved down a hallway laden with expensive art, then down the sweeping staircase that encircled a crystal chandelier, then across the marble foyer. He found what he needed in the kitchen quickly enough: their Floo Powder contained in a sculptural twist of dark glass. Then he took the heavy bottle of Polyjuice from his pocket and divided it between four stone flagons. There was enough in each flagon to last several hours, at least, and hopefully the flagons would still be small enough to remain inconspicuous. Draco checked the ebony-faced clock on the wall, whose Roman numerals glowed brightly, slivers of enchanted opal. So far they were on time. He made for a sliding door by the sunroom. Beyond it was Mr. Parkinsonís study, a long, dark room with a dozen models of broomsticks mounted on the walls: the Nimbus 2000 and 2001, the classic 1940, the old cult favorite 1280. Scale sketches of an in-progress design for a Nimbus 2002 were strewn across the table that took up the center of the room. Draco only spared a glance for the meticulous drawings, hurrying toward the desk by the French windows. The deskís third drawer was locked. ØCamellia,Æ he whispered a second time, tapping it. The lock clicked open, and he pulled it wide. The Parkinsonsí papers were clipped neatly inside a folder that he tucked into his robes. Draco was about to leave when his eyes caught on the deskís surface. Scattered across the cherrywood were letters in Mr. Parkinsonís small, precise writing. He paused. Heíd been wondering for weeks whether the Parkinsons had finally declared their loyalties, whetherîwhen he took the Polyjuice Potionîhe would look down at his left arm to realize Mr. Parkinson had taken the Mark, too. He began to rifle through the letters, searching for Bellatrixís extravagant, looping script, or any other Death Eaterís hand he might recognize. But his eyes caught, instead, on a Hogwarts crest. He extracted the envelope from the rest, frowning, and withdrew the parchment inside. Words in Severus Snapeís handwriting read, Dear Mr. and Mrs. Parkinson, I regret to inform you that your daughter, Pansy, has been given her third disciplinary warning. As such, she has been assigned three daysí worth of detentions with Professors Carrow and Carrow. Her punishment will be at their discretion. I privately advise that you write to your daughter to discuss her behaviour and how she might adjust to Hogwartsís new environment. As her Head of House for six years, I assure you I attempted to make provisions for the recent loss of her close friend, but I can only do so much in the face of this kind of erratic, inappropriate behaviour. Yours, Severus Snape Headmaster of Hogwarts Potions Master Draco's throat tightened. Erratic, inappropriate behaviour? What had Pansy done? His eyes found the date at the top of the letter. This had arrived two weeks ago. Draco started to sift through the other letters upon the desk, giving the lamp a tap with his wand to ignite it. In the flood of warm light, he spotted it almost immediately: Pansyís handwriting, looping and calligraphic, in her favorite color of indigo ink. He snatched up the letter. Dear Mum and Dad, Thanks for your letter, but I didnít do anything wrong. Iím fine after my detentions and Iíd do it again. If you must know, Professor Carrow joked about what happened in June, actually joked about it. Everyone was furious, but I was the only one who said something. You wouldnít have stood for it, either. Besides, everyone knows theyíre only half-bloods anyway. Iíd have thought youíd be proud of me. Youíre the ones who are always talking about loyalty. Write again soon, and I hope youíre safe. Pansy Draco gripped the letter so tightly that the parchment creased. What happened in May. Did she mean è but what else could she mean? Heíd died at the end of May. Had Pansy really spoken back to the Carrows for making fun of his death? ØStupid,Æ Draco whispered under his breath. What did it matter if the Carrows had mocked him? What had they all expected? Heíd died in failure and disgrace. Of course he was an object of scorn to the Death Eaters. But Pansyîhe could feel her fingertips stroking his hair, could taste the honey of her lip balmîof course Pansy hadnít kept quiet. Sheíd been punished for it, too, and from what Draco knew of the Carrows, a punishment from them would be nothing to brush away in a single phrase, Iím fine after my detentions. But that was Pansy. Proud to a fault, scornful of weakness, all sharp teeth and vicious tongue. ØMalfoy?Æ said a voice. Draco shoved the letter back into place. ØComing,Æ he said, striding out of the study and closing the door behind himself. ØDo you have the papers?Æ Granger asked. He nodded. ØLetís go.Æ Weasley and Potter were standing by the hearth, already holding their flagons of Polyjuice. Draco took the one that Granger handed him, tipped it against his lips, and drank. Hermione had spent so long with only Harry, Ron, and Malfoy for company that stepping out of the fireplace into the Leaky Cauldron made her feel an instinctive shock, as if sheíd already done something terribly wrong. The grubby little pub was as busy as sheíd ever seen it, but it wasnít filled with regulars at their usual tables and barstools. Instead, lines of witches and wizards snaked through roped-off walkways, trudging toward the backdoor entrance to Diagon Alley. ØCome onîmove, move,Æ said the harassed-looking official manning the fireplace that Malfoy, Harry, and Ron had just stepped through. ØMake way, there could be more any moment.Æ Malfoy, now the broad-shouldered and dark-haired Mr. Parkinson, gave the man an icy look. ØYes,Æ he said in a rich baritone, Øthank you for your contribution.Æ Malfoy ushered Harry and Ron toward where Hermione stood in line, swatting lazily at the boysí shoulders. This earned Malfoy a pair of glares from Harry and Ron, and Hermione said, under her breath, ØYou two.Æ With obvious effort, they untwisted their sour expressions. ØOur papers, darling,Æ said Malfoy, taking a sheaf of parchment from his robes. Hermioneís cheeks grew warm. She knew it was Malfoy, and she knew it was an act, but having a complete stranger look her in the face and call her darling still felt mortifying. It was actually more mortifying, knowing it was Draco Malfoy saying the words. ØYes, thanks, dearest,Æ Hermione said, trying to remove any hint of irony from her voice. One corner of Malfoyís mouth twitched as she took the papers. While they moved forward in line, Hermione handed Devon and Charles Jr.ís papers to Harry and Ron, making a show of explaining how to present them to the nice witch at the checkpoint. Hermioneís heart beat faster as they approached the terminus. Too quickly, they had reached the sour-looking witch who was checking papers. The woman took one look at Hermioneís face, and her expression transformed into a smile. ØAstrantia!Æ the witch exclaimed. ØItís so lovely to see a familiar face.Æ Hermione's heart plummeted. She broke into an instinctive smile, but when she opened her mouth, no words came out. Malfoy moved past her, and the witchís eyes fell to him instead. ØAnd Charles! Itís been too long.Æ Malfoy offered a large, artificial smile and his hand, which the witch shook. ØFinnida,Æ he said. ØIt has. They have you doing this sort of work, do they?Æ Finnidaís nose wrinkled. ØUnfortunately. Terrible understaffing at the Department these days, Iím sure youíve heard all about it è all my reports have been put on Floo monitoring duty, so here I am.Æ She sighed. ØNot without excitement, though, this post. A Mudblood tried to come through yesterday evening, you know. Bold as brass, saying she needed potion ingredients.Æ She looked from Malfoy to Hermione, clearly expecting commiseration. ØHard to believe,Æ Hermione said, stroking her hair behind her ear in the way that Mrs. Parkinson always did, according to Malfoy. ØAt least you were there to stop it.Æ Harry and Ron shifted uncomfortably at her side. ØDevon, Junior, give Finnida your papers,Æ Hermione said, ushering them forward. Harry and Ron looked up at Finnida with large, dark eyes and stuck out their papers. Finnida made a show of stamping them with a shiny, official stamp, then gave a fond chuckle as she did the same for Malfoy and Hermione. ØSo much taller,Æ she said. ØItís true what they say,Æ Hermione said with a tinkling laugh. ØIt happens too quickly.Æ ØEnjoy it in there,Æ Finnida called after them. ØItís much cleaner than usual.Æ Malfoy aimed a thin, satisfied smile back at the witch as they exited the Leaky Cauldron. They entered Diagon Alley, and Hermione let out a trembling breath. ØYes,Æ said Malfoy at her side. ØBreathe normally.Æ ØUseful, arenít you,Æ she said. ØI try to be.Æ The four Parkinsons walked past Ollivanderís, now shuttered, and the imposing marble faade of Gringotts. Diagon Alley still had its fair share of shoppers, but they were walking more quickly, and, if in groups, moving more closely together. Malfoy and Hermione adopted the stance that most parents seemed to have fallen into: Harry and Ron walked just in front of them, a parent at each of their backs. ØHere!Æ Harry said as they reached a small turn that was marked Acaysian Alley. Øè Mum,Æ he added hastily, with an unconvincing tug at Hermioneís robes. They turned down Acaysian Alley and saw it immediately. It was impossible to miss: the alley came to a dead end fifty paces ahead, and there, the florid material of the stall winked at them, covered in blue moons, fiery red suns, and brilliantly yellow stars. A sign twisted over its entrance in animated ribbon, forming and reforming the words: The Scavengersí Guild. As one, they ducked through a hanging flap of cloth into the stall. ØWow,Æ breathed Harry, sounding every bit the ten-year-old. After weeks in the tent, Hermione thought she could no longer be surprised by the sudden expansion of space, but this was something else altogether. The stall, which from the outside had had no more than seven or eight feetís height, had opened up into a space as tall as a cathedral. It comprised a single narrow passageway, like the alley outside, but every inch of vertical wall space was hung with items: cups and saucers and plates mounted in brackets, old-fashioned lamps and large waxen candles, musical instruments dangling from hooks, a fifteen-foot row of shabby-looking wands, patterned carpets and bolts of cloth, on and on and on. And clearly, the Scavengersí Guild wasnít suffering from Diagon Alleyís new restrictions. The place was packed with peopleîbut rather than milling around on the ground, they were floating around the walls in large wicker baskets, like those that might hang beneath hot-air balloons, each quite unsupported. To Hermioneís immense relief, the items werenít all jumbled in together. The Scavengers seemed to have a category system, and soon enough Harry had spotted the section devoted to jewelry, demarcated by a large, glittering sign with a diamond ring that jutted out from the wall. It was fifty feet off the ground, the jewelry glowing like a patch of shimmering scales high overhead. More baskets lined the base of the wall, each large enough for two people. ØDevon, you come with me,Æ Hermione said, opening the side of one basket, Øand Charlie, you go with Dad, all right?Æ Ron entered the basket with her, and Harry and Malfoy took one nearby. She and Malfoy tapped their respective baskets with their wands, and they rose into the air. Hermione tried not to focus on the increasing height, instead watching the Scavengersí wares pass. They ascended past a sea of rings that shivered on long, bent nails driven into the wall. They passed bracelets and bangles and anklets. Finally, they reached the necklaces, a section of wall six feet across that reached up into the alley's lofty heights. From below, the stallís upper reaches had looked clogged with people, but up here, Hermione felt as if theyíd entered a private room. The nearest person was twenty feet down the wall and thirty feet below. They could speak without fear of being overheard. ØMerlin, thereíve got to be thousands,Æ Ron said, dismayed. ØWell,Æ said Harry from the opposite basket, Øletís start at the bottom and work our way up, díyou reckon? Remember, itís gold, has Slytherinís S on it, and itís set with emeralds. About the size of a chickenís egg.Æ And so they set to it. They rose side by side in their wicker baskets, picking through silver chains and ebony beads, passing over glass hoops and jade carvings. Hermione squinted and touched until her eyes felt strained and her hands smelled bitter, like old silver. ØWhat's the time?Æ Malfoy asked after a while. Hermione startled. She needed to be checking when they were due to take another dose of Polyjuice Potion. She shook back her sleeve. ØFive to,Æ she said with disbelief. Had they really passed an entire hour already? Sheíd thought this process couldnít possibly take more than two, but she hadnít counted on the amount of detritus that the Scavengers had harvested. They surreptitiously took their flagons from their pockets and drank. On and on they rose, foot by slow foot. At one point, Harry seemed oddly dazed by a necklace set with sapphires, and was halfway to putting it on when Malfoy snatched it out of his hand and shoved it back onto the wall. Ron eyed a necklace dripping with diamonds longingly, and said, ØHow much do you reckon that is?Æ ØOh, a Knut or two, Iíd say,Æ Hermione said, scanning a tangle that seemed to be about fifty feet of fine gold chain knotted together. Half an hour later, they reached the top of the section, still empty-handed. ØNothing,Æ Hermione said bitterly. ØNothing,Æ Harry agreed. ØYou donít think they could have found it and realized it was Slytherinís, do you? I mean, in the memory, Voldemort knew what itîÆ ØDonít,Æ Ron said through gritted teeth. ØWhat?Æ ØDonít say the name! Weíre in public!Æ Harry looked around in exasperation. ØThe nearest basketís all the way over there. No oneís going to hearîÆ ØBut if someone did hearîÆ ØOh, never mind that,Æ Hermione said, feeling cross. ØLetís get back down. We need to ask the Scavengers if theyíve seen, or sold, anything like it that they can remember.Æ They returned their baskets to the cobbled floor and hurried toward the back. Fred had told them that the Scavengers could be found in a hidden room, behind a portrait of a king eating a pile of corn twice his height. The portrait didnít take long to locate, as it was at ground level, and the mountain of golden corn was nothing if not eye-catching. Hermione, per Fredís instructions, knocked five times on the kingís stomach. The king let out an earsplitting belch, turned over his shoulder, and called toward a window in the background, ØCeline?Æ ØWhat?Æ yelled a voice from the shuttered window. ØHaggler,Æ the king called back. The portrait cracked open, and out stepped a tall Chinese woman. Her long black hair was braided with bright pieces of cloth, and her arms were laden with glittering silver jewelry and sparkling tattoos. ØWant to haggle, do you?Æ she said. ØNo, it isnít that,Æ said Hermione, drawing herself up. She let her eyes linger in a disapproving sort of way on the womanís tattoos; she didnít think Mrs. Parkinson would have liked them. ØWeíre looking for a specific piece and we canít find it on your walls. Weíre wondering if itís come through your hands.Æ The woman jerked her head, and they followed her through the portrait hole into a dim but comfortable room, littered with poufs and rugs and, apparently, everything else the Scavengers had collected that they werenít keen on giving up. Several others Scavengers were loafing in the room, sprawled on velvet divans, counting stacks of unrecognizable coppery coins, wearing gaudily mismatched clothes. ØIím Celine Shih,Æ said the tattooed woman. She had a low, smoky voice. ØAstrantia Parkinson,Æ Hermione said with a thin smile. ØCharmed.Æ ØCharles Parkinson,Æ Malfoy said. ØAnd our two sons, Devon and Charles Junior.Æ ØItís amazing here,Æ Harry blurted. Celine Shih seemed to soften, looking at Harry. ØThank you, little one,Æ she said, crouching in front of him and smiling to reveal a gold-capped tooth. ØWhatís your mother looking for today?Æ ØItís a family heirloom,Æ said Harry, with a convincingly uncertain look up at Hermione. ØYes,Æ Hermione said. ØA locket that belonged to my great-grandmother. Its clasp was always weak, and we think it detached somewhere in the streets of London when my sister wore it out last.Æ ØMm,Æ said Celine, arching one eyebrow. She slouched into a chair that was elevated like a throne. ØAnd when was that?Æ Hermione pursed her lips. ØMy sister, naturally, has forgotten when she last had it. She always was careless.Æ ØWe kept it in our home until two summers ago,Æ Malfoy offered. ØItís made from pure gold, engraved with an S, for Scilla, and inset with emeralds. Weíre a family of Slytherins, you see. Have been for centuries.Æ He offered another of Charles Parkinsonís broad, artificial smiles. ØThe catch is stuck,Æ Hermione added, Øso it refuses to open, if that helps you identify it.Æ ØEmeralds.Æ Celine tapped her long fingernails on the arm of the chair. ØWe always watch for emeralds, sapphires, rubies, and citrine è Hogwarts parents are fond of the house stones. We put them on the wall and theyíre gone by the next month.Æ She shook her head. ØWeíve only had a few emerald pieces in the last two years. Three rings and a very impressive bracelet.Æ ØYouíre absolutely certain?Æ Hermione said. Celine narrowed her eyes. ØI never forget our wares." ØMy wife didnít mean to offend,Æ Malfoy added smoothly. ØThe locket is important to our family. You understand.Æ ØVery important,Æ Hermione said. ØYou wouldnít be able to describe how you find these objects? In case we wanted to search for ourselves?Æ Celineís eyes roved from Malfoy to Hermione, then to Ron and Harry. She looked suspicious, as though trying to sniff out whether they were trying to compete with the Scavengersí business. But the Parkinsons looked obviously related, and evidently Celine doubted that they would have birthed two children just to con her, because she said, ØItís a spell I developed. You begin high up, on a broom, and narrow down on a trace of magic. It wonít work anywhere like Diagon Alley, or in Wizarding settlementsîso much magic overloads the spell. But in barren areasîMuggle areas, that isîit finds a breadcrumb, and leads you down the trail.Æ She stood, drew a piece of parchment from a small, heavily jeweled case, and used a peahen feather quill to jot down the details. She handed the sheet to Hermione. ØThank you,Æ Hermione said. Celine opened her mouth, but before she could speak, a sound blared from the other side of the portrait. A magically magnified voice was booming, ØPlease descend in an orderly fashion to the ground and prepare your papers.Æ The sleepy atmosphere in the back room disappeared. The Scavengers leapt to their feet. Celine pulled the portrait back open. ØOut,Æ she said. ØOut!Æ They rushed back into the stall, where the wicker baskets were sinking down the walls, their passengers tripping and stumbling out in their haste to obey. Hermione took in a sharp breath. Half a dozen figures had appeared at the entrance. ØWhat is this?Æ Celine demanded, striding through her customers toward the robed figures. ØWhat do you want? All our permits are in order.Æ The figures were dressed in Ministry uniforms rather than Death Eatersí robes, but Hermione recognized the face of Yaxley. ØRandom inspection,Æ his voice boomed. The Parkinsons exchanged looks and ducked back through the portrait into the Scavengersí lounge. ØThereís no way,Æ Harry hissed. ØThis isnít a coincidence. How did they know? My scar hasnít hurt at all! And no one could have overheard what we were saying. They were all miles away.Æ Hermioneís mind raced. Could someone have heard their voices echoing? And even if they had, what in their conversation would have given away their identities, besides è besides è Hermione let out a gasp and clapped one hand over her mouth. Realization struck Ronís and Malfoyís faces as well. ØYou è you said his name,Æ Ron said. ØIs this really the time,Æ Harry said angrily, ØtoîÆ ØHarry,Æ Hermione said, Øyou said it that day in the clearing, as well. And that day when we were making dinner. You said it right before all the protective spells broke.Æ Harryís ten-year-old face gaped up at her. ØWhat?Æ ØThatís how theyíve found us,Æ Ron moaned. ØMerlinís pants, didnít I tell you it felt like bad luck?Æ ØIt makes sense,Æ Malfoy muttered. ØOnly people like you ever said it, after all. You and Dumbledore.Æ ØThey can do that?Æ Harry said. ØYes,Æ Hermione said, Øitís called a Taboo, and I read about it in A History of Magical Surveillance. Itís an archaic form of magicîand itís normally limited to a much smaller scope, within a mile radius or so è to Taboo an entire country would take the kind of ability that only people like You-Know-Who would have.Æ The truth brought Hermione little satisfaction. This might help them avoid problems in the future, but first they had to survive the present situation. Malfoy seemed to be thinking along the same lines. ØWe canít be found here,Æ he said. ØBrilliant,Æ said Ron. ØDid you figure that out by yourself?Æ ØI mean in this room,Æ Malfoy hissed. ØItíll look suspicious. Get out, join the queue, get your papers ready. Go.Æ They exited the backroom and slipped into the crowd, mingling with anxious-looking shoppers clutching their papers. The crowd jostled, shuffling them forward. But the Scavengersí stall hadnít been built to contain hundreds of frightened people all on the ground. Hermione heard a small, high voice say something, and when she looked back, she realized she and Malfoy had been separated from Harry and Ron. She tried to push back, but the tide was too strong. ØWeíll wait for them outside the tent,Æ Malfoy said, seizing her by the forearm so they didnít get separated, too. Hermione checked her watch as they neared the front of the stall. The line was chaotic, but moving quickly, and they still had another twenty minutes before they needed another dose of Polyjuice. They were safe enough on that front, at least. The stall echoed with the other shoppersí frightened voices, creating a turbulent stew of noise. Only when they were feet from Yaxley and the other five Ministry employees could Hermione hear their harsh orders: ØWands and papers out!Æ Panic flooded Hermione as the queue spat her out in front of Yaxley. Wands. Ollivander had been in Voldemortís custodyîand they knew that she and Harry were on the runîand Ollivander could identify her wand, and Harryís. ØAh,Æ said Yaxley, his expression easing. ØMrs. Parkinson. Your papers, and your wand, if you please, and weíll have you on your way.Æ ØYes, fine,Æ Hermione sniffed, trying to stay composed. She turned over the papers, but she had no choice but to hand him her wand, too. He gave her papers only a cursory glance before returning them, but when he placed the wand on a small set of scales and read the slip of parchment that spat out of its side, he froze. Yaxley looked up at Hermione, opened his mouth, and closed it again. The surprise on his face was veering into suspicion, and Hermione didnít know what to do, what to say. She knew she should be formulating an excuse, but all she could feel was panic, blotting out her thoughts like the roar of the ocean. Then a ringing baritone said, ØCorban. What a pleasure.Æ Malfoy had strode up to her side. Hermione had the crazed instinct to seize Malfoyís wand, hex the living daylights out of Yaxley, and make a run for itîbut of course it was a ridiculous idea. Harry and Ron were back there in the crowd, and with five other Ministry officials standing in the entrance, she wouldnít be able to do so much as lift a wand before being overpowered. ØCharles,Æ said Yaxley, blinking up at Malfoy. ØMy wife giving you trouble, is she?Æ said Malfoy, with a self-satisfied chortle. ØHere. Wand and papers.Æ Yaxley checked Malfoyís items and returned them, but he kept hold of Hermioneís wand. Malfoyís smile faded from his square face. ØIs something the matter?Æ ØYes, now that you mention it.Æ Yaxley straightened to Mr. Parkinsonís height. ØYour wife is carrying a wand that belongs to a known fugitive and Mudblood.Æ Hermione didnít know whether she should feign outrage. She glanced up at Malfoy. They needed to present a unified frontîbut how to make sure their stories aligned? After a momentís silence, Malfoy let out a long sigh. ØYou see?Æ he said to her in a slightly patronizing way. ØYou see? Didnít I say?Æ From his tone, Hermione could guess the response he was looking for. She let out a defensive huff. ØCharles, I donít want to hear it.Æ Malfoy turned back to Yaxley. ØItís a new wand. Sheíd been having trouble with her own for months, and I told her we ought to go to a reputable salesperson to have a new one made. But she insisted on an Ollivander reseller.Æ He ran a hand through his thick dark hair. ØTo be fair, weíd heard that with all the wands getting taken off Mudbloods, there were a number of quality wands in circulation. è But you sayîwhat, this one is a fugitiveís wand?Æ ØI find it to be a perfectly good wand,Æ Hermione said sharply. ØAnd we paid good gold for it, Charles.Æ Malfoy made a convincing grumbling sound, throwing up his hands. Hermione felt a sudden wave of relief that he was here to act the pure-blood. They were actually managing it, concocting a story in conjunction on the spot. It even seemed to be working on Yaxley, whose brow had unfurrowed. ØI see,Æ Yaxley said slowly. ØBe that as it may, this wand will be very valuable to some of our current è ah, investigations. Any information you can give us could be useful, too. And of course, Iíll want my Aurors to run a number of tests on the wand itself. Iíll have to ask you both to accompany me back to the Ministry.Æ Hermioneís heart dropped, but Malfoy said, sounding dismissive, ØYes, naturally. It wonít take long, will it? Our sons areîÆ ØTheyíre at home by themselves,Æ Hermione broke in. ØTheyíll be expecting us soon.Æ She exchanged the briefest glance with Malfoy and saw understanding in his expression. It wouldnít help things for Harry Potter to be dragged into the Ministry alongside them, that much was for sure. ØMr. and Mrs. Parkinson,Æ Yaxley said, a note of soft menace entering his voice now, Ølet me be perfectly clear. This is not routine. This wand is of the utmost importance, not just to me, but toÆîhe raised his eyebrows, his eyes glitteringîØmy superiors.Æ Hermione exchanged another look with Malfoy, this time long enough for Yaxley to see. She let her fear show, and Malfoy forced a hard swallow, as if in realization. ØAh,Æ Malfoy said. ØYes. We understand. As long as you need, Corban.Æ ØAnd of course,Æ Hermione added, Øwe would be honored to è to assist.Æ ØGood,Æ Yaxley said, sounding satisfied. ØDrummond, Borrofield! Come with me. You three, stay and finish checking the rest of this lot.Æ Hermioneís heart thumped hard as she folded Mrs. Parkinsonís papers back into her robes. They might have avoided immediate detection, but how long could they maintain this faade? Would Yaxley really believe that, by sheer coincidence, her wanted wand had popped up in a place where Voldemortís name had just been spoken? They had to think of a way to tie the threads together somehowîbut how to avoid incriminating the Parkinsons in the process? Or was that already doomed? Her stomach lurched hard with yet another horrible possibility. If Yaxleyís Aurors performed Priori Incantatem on the wand, would it reveal the information in the Fidelius Charm? As she followed Yaxley out of the stall, she glanced back in time to see Harry and Ron emerging at the front of the queue. Harryís eyes widened, and he made to lunge forward, but Hermione looked to Ron and shook her head violently. Ron seized Harryís wrist and yanked him backward, into the crowd, out of sight. Hermione let out a shaky breath. She trusted that Ron would get Harry to safety, at the least, and theyíd brought their D.A. coins for just this kind of emergency. She would be able to communicate that they were safe, as long as they remained safe. As they walked up Acaysian Alley, a Death Eater leading them and Ministry employees flanking them, Hermione glanced up at Malfoy. For the first time that day, she could see a shadow of Draco Malfoy on Mr. Parkinsonís square face. Something in the way his eyes were fixed ahead, fearful and wary. Fear was beating steadily through her own body, too, her heart pounding against her ribs like a furious fist against a tabletop. Still, she and Malfoy had talked their way out of immediate suspicion, hadnít they? Maybe, just maybe, they could talk their way out of the rest, too. Two things, Hermione thought grimly, were certain: they were walking into the belly of the beast, and she was glad not to do so alone. Chapter End Notes is Celine Shih vaguely inspired by the love of my life, Ching Shih/Shih Yang, the most powerful pirate of all time? yes. yes she is. someday i'm gonna write a series about a pirate queen inspired by her, can't wait tumblr | instagram Chapter 11:MagicisMight Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes The Ministry had changed. Draco had last visited during the summer before fourth year. His father had stopped by, Draco in tow, to visit a few choice contacts in the Department of Magical Games and Sports. ØMy son, Draco,Æ heíd said to innumerable people, and Draco had smiled and shaken hands while his father told them that he flew for Slytherin, and eventuallyîwhat a coincidence!î Cornelius Fudge happened to look in on Ludo Bagman at the same moment they were visiting with him. Theyíd left that day with tickets to the Top Box tucked into his fatherís robes. Draco remembered swaggering through the large, light-filled Atrium at his fatherís shoulder, feeling that every door was open to him. Heíd flipped a Galleon over his shoulder into the Fountain of Magical Brethren as heíd gone. Now the fountain was gone, and the Atrium boasted an enormous statue of black stone: a witch and wizard perched on twin thrones. Only as Yaxley led them past its base did Draco notice that the thrones were made from multitudes of human bodies, pressed and twisted into each other, seemingîthough they were quite stillîto writhe like animals. They were naked, and humiliatingly so, their feet and elbows and clawlike hands shoving into each otherís stomachs and breasts and buttocks. They were Muggles. He had the sudden urge to turn toward Granger, to distract her before she could see it, but she had already stiffened beside him. When he next chanced a glance at her, Mrs. Parkinsonís dark eyes were fixed on Yaxleyís back, her lips parted as if she were measuring her breaths. Sheíd seen. ØThis way,Æ Yaxley said, beckoning them toward the lift. As they skipped to the front of the queue, he added, ØBorrofield, fetch Crabbe from Level Two. Weíll be down in the detention area. Room Four should be free.Æ Borrofield nodded and peeled away. Draco let out a laugh, allowing himself to sound uneasy. ØHold on, now, Corbanîthe detention area? Donít you have an office we could speak in?Æ Yaxley gave a dismissive wave as the liftís golden grilles clattered open. ØAll procedure, Charles, no need to worry.Æ But there was a hint of enjoyment in his voice. Just before the lift doors closed, Draco snuck a last glance at the great golden clock that hung in the Atrium. He and Granger had managed to sneak their third drink of Polyjuice Potion during the walk through London to the Ministry, which had taken far longer than heíd hoped it might. Now, again, they were nearing the hour: ten minutes before they needed to replenish their disguises. Draco wondered if he could uncork his flagon in the wide sleeve of his robes. He saw Granger fiddling in her own pocket and knew that she was altering the coin the D.A. had developed, sliding her fingertip clockwise around its circumference to make the numbers rise. Theyíd developed a simple set of numerical codes to indicate their status: safe, in transit, in danger, in emergency, and in captivity. Now that they were no longer in transit, he wondered which she would choose to describe their current situation. But Draco didnít know what it would do, really, to tell Potter and Weasley that they were in danger. If the two Gryffindor boys didnít act like complete idiots, they would be able to get out of the Scavengersí stall without Potterís wand being spotted: they would simply have to hand over their papers, showing that they were below Hogwarts age, and thus below the age that they should possess a wand. But they certainly couldnít burst into the Ministry, disguised as children, and demand that their parents be handed over. Yaxley was treating them cordially enough now, but Draco knew they hadnít escaped suspicion: Drummond had relieved him of his wand before theyíd left Diagon Alley. Dracoís hands felt very empty, and though he had never spent much time with Yaxley, he still worried that somehow Yaxley would recognize his wand: hawthorn, ten inches, unicorn hair core. The lift clattered to a halt. ØLevel nine,Æ said its cool female voice. ØDepartment of Mysteries.Æ Grangerís motions were noticeably stiff as they exited the lift, walking down a long, dark corridor toward a sealed door. Before theyíd neared it, however, Yaxley detoured them down a stairwell toward the courtrooms. Draco felt the Dementors before he saw them. A clammy coldness crept over his body. His chest constricted, as if iron bands had been fastened around his torso, and any thoughts of the Polyjuice in his pocket, or of excuses that he and Granger might conjure, leaked away. Memory rose up instead, surging in him like magma, burning away reality. He could feel the old fine carpet against his cheek, abrading his skin, as he lay upon it, twisting and twitching, his mouth gracelessly open. The fire was hissing and spitting like a serpent, and the serpent herself was twining between the legs of the velvet armchairs è though nearly blind with pain, he could see Nagini movingè ØYou have displeased me, Draco,Æ said Lord Voldemortís silky voice. ØDid you not promise that you could give me what I asked? Did you not swear it on your life è on your beloved familyís life?Æ He heard himself gasping, ØI è I èÆ ØDid you not promise me the life of Albus Dumbledore?Æ ØYes, but IîI need more tiîÆ ØYes? Yes, what?Æ There was a smile in the Dark Lordís cold voice. ØNow, now, Draco è where is your courtesy? Where is your respect for your master? è Crucio!Æ Draco screamed. The pain was skating over the surface of his skin like a thousand delicate cuts. The pain was inside him, splitting bone, skewering nerve. It was ten seconds, or several minutes, or possibly a lifetime, until the intensity lessened enough for him to form wordsîØYes,Æ he sobbed, Øm-my Lord èÆ The wand lifted. Draco was drenched in sweat, his body limp and still. The pain had gone. His face was wet with tears. He heaved, lying there on the floor, but nothing came up. He heard voices from beyond the doorîhis mother screaming at Bellatrix, and Bellatrix yelling something backî Yaxleyís voice split the memory in two. ØExpecto Patronum!Æ Silver vapor brought the present back to him. Dracoís mouth was still open, as it had been in the memory. A sheen of sweat had covered his clammy forehead; he wiped it with his forearm and felt his fingers twitch. A force was being applied to his other arm. He looked down and saw Granger gripping him just above the elbowîwhen had she done that? He realized with a small shock that he was leaning substantially on her, her body straining with the effort of holding up Mr. Parkinsonís frame. As the silvery cloud continued to issue from Yaxleyís wand, the tightness in Draco's chest eased, and his legs took his full weight again. But Granger didnít let go, a wife holding her husbandís arm in seeming support. Her own face was drawn, but when she moved, she seemed steady. Yaxley seemed satisfied by the vapor, and continued to cast the charm intermittently as they moved into the courtroom halls. The silvery glow reflected off the wallsí dark tiles, and the tall, hooded Dementors gliding throughout the hallway shied away from the light, moving closer to the people huddled on wooden benches outside the courtroom doors. Draco watched their terrified faces in the glow of Yaxleyís incorporeal Patronus. Some had tear tracks glistening on their cheeks. One boy, who couldnít have been older than eleven, had buried his face in his motherís arm, his shoulders heaving. Draco found himself fixating on Grangerís grip, the simple feel of her hand through his robes. She was holding on uncomfortably tightîthat was Granger for you, he supposedîbut the human contact seemed to be anchoring him here, in this time, far away from the dark year. As they moved toward the end of the hall, it occurred to Draco that the other Ministry official, Drummond, was trembling. He had to stop intermittently to lean against the wall, and hardly seemed to be paying attention to them at all. Draco, under the pretense of detaching his arm from Hermione, placed his palm against her pocket, pressing the flagon into her hip. She met his eyes, and he glanced to Drummond. She followed his eyeline, then lowered her head in an almost imperceptible nod. Draco uncorked the flagon in his own pocket. When Yaxley moved ahead of them to knock on a door with a brass number å4í on it, he and Hermione turned away for an instant, under the pretense of looking down the crowded hall, and drank quick swigs from their flagons of Polyjuice. The flagon felt horribly light as he replaced it in his pocket. They would have this hour, but how much longer? Though Yaxley had said it should be free, the door to Room Four opened at his knock, revealing a little witch with flyaway grey hair. ØOh,Æ she said, looking flustered, ØYaxleyî we hadnít expected you back untilî?Æ She moved back, and Draco saw who sat at the table behind her, poring over what appeared to be a family tree. A velvet bow was perched atop the womanís head, she wore a lace ruff at her throat, and a serene smile was spread across her wide, toadlike face. Dolores Umbridge looked up, a silvery cat Patronus circling her ankles. As her eyes passed over Draco, new discomfort coursed through his body. He remembered answering to this woman with the Inquisitorial Squad. At school sheíd been a bit of a joke among the Slytherins, really, one of Fudgeís lackeys, never answering to anyone with real power, but now that the Dark Lord had taken the Ministry, nothing about her seemed very funny anymore. ØAh,Æ said Umbridge, getting to her feet and brushing imaginary dust from her robes. ØAre you ready to begin interviewing the next of the Mudbloods, Yaxley?Æ ØNo, donít disturb yourself, Madam Umbridge. I need to ask Mr. and Mrs. Parkinson a few questions, but I can find a different room.Æ ØOh, no need for that.Æ Umbridge let out her simpering little laugh and rolled up the family trees. ØMafalda and I will simply call for another Auror if youíre unavailable. ØI donít mind seeing the Mudbloods passed out. Less trouble that way, arenít they?Æ Umbridge laughed again. ØYouíre quite right. ØItís usually such a pleasure to see you, Charles, Astrantia, but è it seems this isnít the happiest place to meet?Æ ØWeíre here to give vital information to the Ministry about Mudbloods, thank you,Æ said Draco coldly. His mouth felt strange around the word, as it had in the Scavengersí stall. ØOf course. Do pardon me, Charles; I should have known.Æ Draco allowed a stiff nod. But there was nothing they could do now, wandless, a hall full of Dementors between themselves and freedom. The door closed, and Yaxley summoned more silver vapor. They work on Time and Space, in there.Æ Crabbe glanced upward, to the ninth floor, and the Department of Mysteries. Excitement came over Yaxleyís face. ØYes. Good idea, Crabbe. Perhaps thereís a trace of location on the wand. Merlin knows what they could do. Iíll go up and have a word with Ghosh.Æ He swept Grangerís wand off the table and left. Draco glanced over at her, wondering if she would be willing to sacrifice her wand in order for them to escape safely. If he could only grab his own from where it lay on the table and Stun Crabbe è but the table was too wide to reach it without flinging himself bodily toward Yaxleyís empty seat, and Crabbe had taken out his own wand now, turning it over and over absentmindedly. Several minutes passed before Granger asked, ØYouíre well, Alistair?Æ ØWell enough,Æ Crabbe said. Draco swallowed past the lump in his throat. ØAnd how is Vincent?Æ Draco was startled to see a distinct coldness, even disdain, appear in Crabbeís face. ØDoing better than your girl.Æ Granger sat up straighter in her seat. Pretending like they were friends with the Dark Lordîhah. And that boy of theirs.Æ He shook his head, letting out another short, harsh laugh. ØNever treated my son like much, did he? Because thatís as good as being a filthy blood traitor, isnít it?Æ In the deafening silence, Crabbe started turning his wand over and over again. ØGaltonís worried about Gregory. And he should be. Why arenít you worried?Æ His dark eyes moved from Granger back onto Draco. ØWe notice things, you know. We see you two havenít declared your loyalties. We wonder about that, we do.Æ Draco knew he needed to make an excuse as to why the Parkinsons were being slow to join the Death Eaters. He had to think of something, but his mind was blank. The only thing happening in his head was the slow repetition of Crabbeís words: No real loss there. è Smarmy, preening little idiot. è Never treated my son like much, did he? And he remembered what Weasley had said a month and a half ago. I never thought Iíd sympathize with Crabbe and Goyle, but I donít know how they put up with you. Crabbe himself had told his father he knew Draco would fail. Heíd said, Good riddance. Draco was finding it hard to breathe. Grangerís voice broke the silence. ØAll Iíll say, Alistair,Æ she said with magnificent coldness, Øis that you donít know whether the Dark Lord has even given us the honor of an invitation to his inner circle. Or are you claiming that you have the influence to extend those kinds of invitations yourself?Æ Crabbeís expression darkened, but before he could say anything, Yaxley returned, a scowl on his long pale face. ØNothing from the Unspeakables. Ghosh did perform Prior Incantato on this blasted thing for me, but it was all Disillusionments and protective charms. The remnants faded out long before it got to anything useful. She mightíve ditched it because we have Ollivander. Maybe she guessed weíd try to find her wand.Æ ØHave you tried the Prima Satteranium charm?Æ said Granger. This managed to penetrate Dracoís numbness. He looked at her, trying to hide his disbelief. What was she doing? Was answering questions just so deeply engrained in her by now that she couldnít hear a problem without suggesting an answer? ØPrima èÆ Crabbe repeated slowly. ØSatteranium,Æ she finished. ØYou two do know that I was a specialist at the Assembly for the Development of Experimental Charmwork before I had the boys?Æ Dracoís disbelief transformed into curiosity. Pansyís mother had done no such thing. Granger was up to something. He chanced a glance at Crabbe, worrying that he might remember that Astrantia Parkinson had never been a member of the Assembly. No, thoughîAlistair Crabbe could hardly have named what his own wife had done with her free time, let alone be bothered to keep up with the wives of his acquaintances. ØWhatís this charm?Æ Yaxley asked Granger. ØPrimaîwhat does it do?Æ ØIt measures the association between any magical object and a living being,Æ she said. ØIf the Granger Mudblood had a strong enough association with it, it could be used to track her through that bond. It was, as I say,Æ she added, Øa highly experimental charm. It isnít taught, and even I occasionally had difficulty with it, but I would have thought a wizard like Ghosh èÆ ØBut you can do it, then,Æ said Yaxley sharply, excitement in his voice. ØHavenít I just told you I had difficulty with it?Æ Granger said, her lips pursed. ØAnd that was a decade ago.Æ Crabbe rose slowly to his feet, looming over them all. He squared his shoulders, emphasizing the powerful arms that made his robes strain. ØDo you want to help the Dark Lord,Æ he said with quiet menace, Øor not?Æ Granger swallowed and let the disdain melt from her face. ØI è yes, of course I do. Iíll try. Just è just place the wand there, then.Æ Draco watched with bated breath as Yaxley set Grangerís wand on the table. He and Granger both rose to their feet and moved toward it, and Draco felt his body tensing the way it had once tensed during Quidditch practice, ready to make a capture. ØIt may take a while,Æ said Granger, holding out her hand for another wand as if the gesture meant nothing. ØA few attempts. è But Iím sure I can. Yes. For the Dark Lord.Æ Yaxley placed his own wand into Grangerís hand. Granger took a deep breath and stepped back from the table. Her gaze flickered, for an infinitesimal moment, toward Draco. ØAll right,Æ she said, focusing on her wand again. She drew a breath. She lifted Yaxleyís wand. In that instant, Draco lunged forward and snatched her wand from the table. They moved in perfect concert, Draco aiming at Crabbe, Granger at Yaxley. Both Death Eatersí hands shot into their robes, but they hadnít even drawn their wands when the nonverbal spells struck them. Crabbeís bulk was so formidable that the red jet of light from Dracoís Stupefy didnít even make him stumble. Instead he went rigid where he stood, then toppled to the ground with a resounding thud. Grangerís spell, on the other hand, had been a Full Body-Bind, which smacked Yaxley back into the wall. Rigid as a board, he teetered to one side and crashed to the stone floor, too. Granger was breathing hard. She approached Yaxley, whose eyes were still wide open, his face fixed into an unmoving snarl. She knelt before him. ØIím afraid,Æ she said, Øyouíve underestimated the Mudblood Granger.Æ Her voice shook, but there was determination on her face. ØAs you can see, we arenít afraid to come after your precious pure-blood families, Yaxley. Then she approached Crabbe, not without some trepidation, and took his wand from his pocket. ØLetís go,Æ she said. They exited Room Four and locked it behind them. Hermione glanced over at Malfoy as they climbed the stairwell back up to Level Nine. Theyíd peeked in at all the courtrooms, and Hermione had claimed to be looking for Madam Umbridge, to report sensitive information directly to her. A dark-haired, austere-looking witch had told them that Madam Umbridge had gone to her office to fetch files for court proceedings. Malfoy hadnít made a sound during all this. The words seemed to make little impact. His face was slack, his thoughts clearly a thousand miles away. ØLevel one,Æ said the voice in the lift. ØMinister for Magic and support staff.Æ The grilles clattered open to reveal a finely appointed corridor with thick purple carpets and gleaming wooden doors, each of which bore a shining brass nameplate. Hermione had never been to this part of the Ministry before. Then weíll take the Horcrux and get out of here as quickly as we can.Æ He didnít answer. Hermioneís eyes fell on the rough pamphlets, whose headings read, MUDBLOODS and the Dangers They Pose to a Peaceful Pure-Blood Society. An illustration accompanied the words: a smiling rose being strangled by a weed with fangs and a malevolent leer. The Horcruxes are what can stop all this. The nearest pamphlet-folder glanced up at Hermione and Malfoy and lowered her wand, looking glad for a respite. ØLost, are you?Æ she said. Hermione surveyed her, in her simple uniform, with disdain. ØMadam Umbridgeís office,Æ she sniffed. ØIn there.Æ The worker nodded toward a door at the front of the regiment of desks. Hermione strode forward, gripping her wand in her pocket. Set into its center was a horribly familiar electric blue eye, fixed in a golden ring, swiveling from left to right. Hermione felt a fresh surge of anger, this time mixed with revulsion. The Death Eaters had found Mad-Eye Moodyís body after the escape from Privet Drive, then. They'd raided it for its most valuable contents. Her hand shook with rage as she knocked thrice on the door. Malfoy looked less dazed than before. He was watching the door, and its eye, warily. The door opened, and Hermioneís heart seemed to stop altogether. Their luck was poor: Umbridge was not alone. ØCharles.Æ ØMinister,Æ said Hermione and Malfoy at the same time. ØSuch a pleasure,Æ Hermione added, extending her hand toward him. He took it and brushed it lightly against his mouth, making Hermione feel another wave of disgust. Heís not responsible for all this, she reminded herself. He was Head of Magical Law Enforcement before this. è Heís under the Imperius Curse. Hermione fed The Life and Lies of Albus Dumbledore into the beaded bag in her pocket as they traversed the purple-carpeted corridors of Level One, Pius Thicknesse murmuring in indistinguishable tones to Umbridge, who had a sycophantic smile pasted on her face. Hermione experienced a surge of hope when Thicknesse bade them goodbye at the main corridor. But the locket was so closeè if she could only reach out and grab itè They couldnít start a fight in the open corridor, where everyone could hear. They would have to wait until the lift door closed. This was it: the best chance they would have. A window of seconds. The lift arrived, and they filed in. ØWell?Æ Dolores said, smiling up at Hermione. ØHow has Pansy been managing in herè seventh year, wasnít it?Æ ØYes,Æ Hermione said as the doors clattered shut. ØShe and her pure-blood friends have been îÆ Malfoy moved immediately. Hermione tore her own wand from her robe pocket, ready to cast. But the Aurors reacted with near inhuman speed. Dawlish was already sending a hex whizzing toward Malfoy, and Marten, the taller of the Aurors, flung himself in front of Umbridge, even as Hermione dove for her throat, her hand clawing, scrabbling, desperately seeking the Horcrux. Petrificus Totalus! Hermione thought, trying to aim, but Marten ducked the jet of light, and his counter-jinx hit her squarely in the chest. She let out a hard gasp, and her body slammed backward into the controls of the lift, which lurched to a stop mere seconds after it had started its descent. Umbridge had let out a high-pitched squeal at the first spell, but now her wand was in her hand, too, and Hermione saw, as she flung herself out of the way of Martenís second hex, Umbridge cast a whirling disk of venomous purple light toward Malfoy. ØNo!Æ Hermione yelledîtoo late. Malfoy had lurched backward, slashing his wand out to deflect Dawlishís Stunning Spell, and moved into the path of the whirling disk. It sliced deep into his shoulder. Dark red blood sprayed out in a way that looked lurid, unreal. Hermione screamed. She acted without thinking. Dawlishís deflected Stunner had ricocheted into the controls, and the doors of the lift were shuddering halfway open again, revealing the purple-carpeted floor of Level One a foot and a half above the threshold. It wouldnít hold them longîten minutes, perhaps, until they tried the correct combination of counter-jinxesîbut it would hold them. Hermione spun back toward Malfoy, who was on his knees, one hand clasped over the wound, sucking in hard breaths. ØMalfoy?Æ she panted. ØMalfoy!Æ ØItísîbad,Æ he said, his voice harsh. His black robes were glistening. Blood had already soaked through them down to his biceps, down to his pectoral.
