Umbridge had cast a powerful curse. But Malfoy was lurching to his feet, his face crazed. ØStairs,Æ he said through gritted teeth. ØCanítîbe seen.Æ He was right. Hermione looked up in dread, wondering if anyone had heard the duelîbut no one was coming out of their offices. Supporting Malfoy at his good elbow, Hermione staggered toward the stairwell and kicked the door wide. Scourgify, she thought, flicking her wand back at the red droplets theyíd trailed. She bore as much of his weight as she could as they eased down the steps, stopping intermittently to eliminate their bloody tracks. As they reached Level Two, though, she heard the door open on the floor below them. Someone was coming up from Level Three. She rapped Malfoy, then herself on the head, Disillusioning them, and they darted through the door of Level Two. The Department of Magical Law Enforcement was very different from the plush-carpeted Level One. At the end of a short corridor, a pair of heavy oak doors was flung open to reveal a loud, crowded area filled with cubicles. Voices rang across dark hardwoods, and what looked like a boxing ring had been erected in the center of the cubicles, encased by shimmering enchantments, two Aurors dueling within. ØIn here,Æ Hermione whispered, hauling Malfoyís sagging body toward a narrow, unmarked door. They slipped into an ordinary broom cupboard. ØColloportus!Æ she panted, and the lock clicked shut. ØMuffliato!Æ she added. Then, in the light of the flickering orange orb overhead, she removed their Disillusionment charms. Even as she did so, her skin bubbled, her bones stretching, her robes now ill-fitting on a taller frame. The Polyjuice Potion had worn off. Malfoy faded back into view. He was himself again, tooîand the sight of him terrified her. He had collapsed back into a jumble of wooden crates, agonized sounds coming from his half-open mouth. His lean frame was swimming in Mr. Parkinsonís too-large robes, the soaked area of which had daubed blood up his neck. Every hint of color had drained from his skin, leaving him marble-white, and his arm hung limp, his fingers twitching. In the unsteady orange light, his pale eyes were wild, fixed upon her. She was beside him in an instant, one hand hovering uselessly over the injury. ØDo you know what that curse was?Æ she said frantically. He let out an agonized laugh. ØThink I willîif youîdonít?Æ he gasped, fixing his long fingers over the slippery gash. ØGod, I think itísîgetting worse èÆ Hermione squeezed her eyes shut and tried to think, but her mind was filled with the shock on his face as the spell had lacerated him, and the smell of blood now permeating the broom cupboard. If sheíd had the diadem, she thought wildly, she would have put it on, knowing its effects, knowing everything, just to remember a bit more clearly. Or had she never known the answer to this? How were she and her seven yearsí study meant to compete with Umbridgeís decades of accumulated knowledge? She was too young for this. She was untrained, she was è ØPlease,Æ he panted. Her eyes opened. Malfoy was still looking at her, his face shining with sweat, his grey eyes too bright. ØCome on, Granger,Æ he said, his voice filled with pain. ØCome on. è Justî another è test.Æ She swallowed hard. If this had been a test, it would have been fourth year. ØIdentification strategies,Æ said Moodyís growling voice in her mind. ØSo, youíve been hit in battle. What do you do? Sit around crying, wailing, and waiting for the pain to end? Not unless you want to die, you donít. No: youíve got to know whatís happened first, if youíre going to do anything. Three simple questions, then.Æ The chalk hitting the blackboard violently. Moodyís emphatic scrawl. ØHow did it look? How did it sound? What has it done?Æ How had the curse looked? Hermione closed her eyes and saw it: a disk of purple light. Not pulsating, not bouncing, but spinning fine and exact like a buzz-saw. So it wasnít a self-multiplying curse or a single-striker, but a curse rooted in repetition. It hadnít been indigo, like blood-specific curses, or lilac, like wasting curses, but vivid violet: brute force, physical magic. How had it sounded? Silent. So it wouldnít be a Conscious Curse, or a curse with a natural derivative like burning, freezing, or electrifying. What had it done? ØI have to look at it,Æ Hermione whispered. Malfoyís head twitched in the tiniest nod. ØDo it.Æ His eyelids were moving unsteadily now, hooding his irises. Hermione lifted the shoulder of the too-large robes and aimed her wand as carefully as she could with her shaking hand. ØDiffindo!Æ The seam split and revealed the wound. It was deep into the flesh, and Hermione felt faint, longed to look away, but knew she mustnít. Blood was still coming from the injury, as fresh and bright as that first spatter in the lift had been. It was unnatural. It was regular. It was as if, with every pulse, the wound was being made anew. The answer came to her like a lightning strike: a section in Darkest Curses for the Darkest Foes, which sat on one of the shelves in the Potter cottage, which she had read in June for the express purpose of the Horcrux hunt. The Curse of Perpetual Tearing, notte to be defended, a moste grievous vyolence unto a prized enemy, shall cause pain uppon pain a hundredfolde as if the Curse hadde ben caste ten thousand timesè She knew the counter-curse. She remembered. Hermione drew her wand, placed its shaking tip to Malfoyís shoulder, drew a clockwise triangle above the injury, and whispered, ØAntagra Vertere.Æ A hissing noise issued from her wandtip. Out from the injury came a seam of white light, like a trace of lightning. Malfoy let out a cry through gritted teeth, his tall body rigid. His bloodied hand thrashed out, found her wrist, and squeezed so hard that pain shot up her own arm. ØOkay,Æ she gasped, Øhold on, itís going to be all rightîÆ ØAh,Æ he panted as the last trace of light was jerked from the wound and into Hermioneís wand. The counter-curse had taken, but his hand was still clamped around her wrist, his face still contorted in pain. Hermione fumbled to withdraw the Essence of Dittany again. She clamped her teeth on the cork to unstopper it and tipped three drops onto the wound. Malfoyís body jerked as a puff of greenish smoke issued from his shoulder. When the smoke cleared, the wound was sticky and congealed. But he didnít make any sound of relief. His eyes had fallen shut. His body slackened, and his hand fell limply from her wrist. He was as white as chalk. Hermione froze in place. ØM-Malfoy?Æ she said, her voice high and uncontrolled. ØMalfoy?Æ He didnít move. She scrambled closer, bending over the crates to press a hand to his chest, then to his neck, trying to feel a pulse, but everything was slippery and hot with blood, and her own pulse was pounding so hard, too hard to allow her to feel anything but fear. Had she done something wrong? What could it have been? Her knees were on the crates now, her body hunched low over his, and she thought wildly of Crabbeís words in the detention room, the way the phrase no real loss there had clearly plunged into Malfoy like a drill. She was remembering what sheíd told him: that they wouldnít ask him to die for them. She seized his face between her hands. His skin was slick with sweat. Strands of white-blond hair fell over her fingertips. ØDraco,Æ she said, her voice feverish. ØDraco!Æ For a long moment, nothing. Then his eyelids flickered and eased open. Relief surged through her, as cold and strong as winter wind. She drew a long, shaky breath. Merlin. Sheíd thoughtîsheíd actually thought è but it wasnít true. It hadnít happened. Heíd only blacked out for a moment, probably from shock or pain. Now his eyes refocused. Confusion colored his expression. She felt his breath against her cheek, and all at once, she realized how close he was, how far she had leaned toward him. She could see the delicate webs of his irises, like spun silver, and a tiny scar like the mark of a fingernail upon the curve of his cheek, and every mote of sweat that had beaded upon his brow. Hermione felt suddenly paralyzed. His gaze was moving slowly, almost curiously over her face, lingering first on one cheek and then the other, on the tip of her nose, on her forehead, and then, finally, on her lips, and Hermione felt something quite aside from fear clench in her stomach. She felt as if she were trapped in the moment before the Time-Turner began to spin, when time stretched impossibly between present and past, absolutely immobile like held breath. She realized her hands were still pressed to either side of his face, warm and tan and trembling against the marble of his complexion. She drew back from him so quickly that she nearly tripped on a crate. ØIîyou still need something for the blood loss,Æ she blurted out. ØYes. I-Iíll have to brew a Blood Replenishment Draught at some point. But for now èÆ Why was her pulse racing? The fear, that was it. They werenít safe yet. it had scarcely been five minutes since the elevator, somehow, though time felt uneven and unreliable at the moment. She fished out a stack of Skiving Snackboxes and extracted a box of Nosebleed Nougat and a Blood Blisterpod. She twisted off the purple half of the Nougat, then proffered it and the Blisterpod toward Malfoy. ØHere. The Blisterpod is a low-grade blood replenisher. This half of the Nougat should keep it from feeding new blood into the wound.Æ He took the chew and the pod with a trembling hand and slipped them between his lips, and his jaw worked slowly for a moment. Then he swallowed. Hermione checked her watch. Yes. Time was of the essence. They had to get back down to the Atrium before Umbridge, Dawlish, and Marten cut their way out of that Sealing Charm. The effects of the Nougat and the Blisterpod would take ninety seconds or so to set in; hopefully they would give Malfoy enough energy to move. She wished she could use Rennervate to revive him, but that spell agitated wounds and would almost certainly reopen his shoulder. She got to her feet. ØWhere are you going?Æ Malfoy said. ØIíll be right back,Æ Hermione said. She swigged the last dregs of her Polyjuice Potion. It would hardly buy her five minutes, but hopefully it would be enough. Once transformed, she left the closet and strode into the Auror Office, walking with purpose, eyeing any Auror who looked her way with disdain. She wasnít questioned. She scanned the cubicles, not letting the bursts of light and crackling sound from the sparring chamber distract her, nor the yells from spectators. As she rounded a corner, she saw her: Nymphadora Tonks, hair as blue as an evening sky, frowning down at a stack of paperwork. Hermione hurried up to her cubicle, where several photographs of Tonks and Lupin hung, smiling, among many maps and charts. ØNymphadora Tonks?Æ she sniffed. ØYeah?Æ Tonks glanced up. ØWho are you?Æ Hermione waited for another bang to come from the sparring chamber, and as voices called out in the aftermath, she lowered her voice and whispered, ØItís Hermione Granger. I mean, Grindelwald was You-Know-Whoís forerunner, wasnít he? Maybe You-Know-Who wanted to show how inspired he was by Grindelwaldís example.Æ Hermione bit her lip. ØThat would be bad news for us. First weíd have to leave the country. Then weíd have to find the school itself, and Durmstrang is very secretive, even more so than Hogwarts and Beauxbatons.Æ She felt daunted at the concept, but at the same time, there was something reassuring in turning back to the mystery. It was smoothing away the discomfort of Ronís confession, joining them with their purpose again. ØI donít know,Æ Harry muttered. ØIf Dumbledore already knew about this mark when he left you the book, if he really thought there was a Horcrux at Durmstrang, why didnít he just tell us that?Æ Ron shrugged. ØWhyíd he leave me the Deluminator, and leave you that old Snitch? He hasnít exactly made it easy on us, has he?Æ Harryís expression darkened, and Hermione knew he was feeling the frustration with Dumbledore that had been plaguing him since the headmasterís death. Occasionally, Harry would mention in a loaded sort of way that Dumbledoreís family had lived here, too, in Godricís Hollow, not even a mile from where they were now. Or he would bring up that snippet of Rita Skeeterís now-posthumous biography, about the supposed dark mysteries of Dumbledoreís family è Hermione let out a small gasp. ØHarry, I've just remembered!Æ She opened her beaded bag and fished out the copy of The Life and Lies of Albus Dumbledore that Umbridge had pressed upon her. ØWe got this at the Ministry. If you want to read it èÆ She trailed off. She hadnít expected Harry to look so stricken at the sudden appearance of Dumbledoreís face, twinkling out at them from the cover. She hastened to add, ØItís Skeeter, mind you, so a lot of it is probably rubbish. But I know youíve been thinking about what Muriel said, and maybe this could have some answers. We do need to understand what Dumbledore was thinking, especially if going all the way to Durmstrang is a possibility.Æ She paused, then added more gently, ØYou knew him best. He really cared about you, I know he did. Would you read it, and see if you can tell what he was getting at?Æ Harry swallowed hard. Then he gave a curt nod and took the book. Hermione glanced at Draco. ØYou donít know where Durmstrang is, do you?Æ ØWhy should I?Æ ØWe overheard you once, saying that your father wanted you to go there, but your mother preferred Hogwarts.Æ Draco mulled this over. ØYeah, well, my fatherís definitely visited. He used to be friendly enough with their old headmaster. You know, Karkaroff.Æ A short, unpleasant silence. Hermioneís brain extended the sentence automatically: è before Karkaroff fled the Dark Lordís service, and was murdered for it. Her eyes found the scrap of parchment on the bedside table, the address sheíd given Draco yesterday. Sheíd told Harry and Ron over dinner last night that sheíd found his parents' location. Theyíd finished restoring a majority of the rooms in the cottage now, and Hermione imagined Draco bringing his parents back here, the Malfoys in the tent, she and Harry and Ron in the cottage. Divided into Gryffindors and Slytherins as before, everything back in its preordained place. She glanced at Draco. His lips were thinned, and he was looking at Beedle in Hermioneís lap. ØI donít know where Durmstrang is,Æ he said. ØBut I suppose I could ask.Æ Dracoís whole body ached when he climbed out of bed that afternoon. The pain moved through him like a ricocheting Bludger, bouncing viciously from shoulder to toe and back. He settled on the window seat with two slips of parchment in his hand: one, in Grangerís handwriting, bore the address in New Cathcove; the other, in Weasleyís, said, The Headquarters of the Order of the Phoenix is located at Number 7, Hartbridge Way. He looked down at the information and tried to understand what, exactly, was happening inside his own head. Draco prided himself on his introspection. He could usually understand his own urges with perfect clarity, and it was other people who acted in foolish or inexplicable ways. Yet as he reread the address in New Cathcove, the information heíd hoped for since July, he found a tiny, incomprehensible part of himself wishing that Granger hadnít given it to him. If heíd been in her shoes, he wouldnít even have asked Tonks about it. He was an asset, after all. This was like holding the door open, letting him walk free, when he could be useful to the Gryffindors in the future. Draco found himself feeling a kind of pride in exactly how useful heíd been so far, and he didnít understand that, either. Was it simply that he was naturally driven to complete a mission when he saw one? Heíd always been competitive, of course. He loved to solve a mystery, to win. He loved to catch the Snitch: a feeling of conclusion. No, though. It was more than that. He thought about the locket around Umbridgeís neck and he wanted them to seize it. He thought about the diadem, and he wanted to see it destroyed. So far, he hadnít really let himself consider the possibility that the Gryffindors might actually achieve their goal. But two Horcruxes were destroyed, a third in hand, and they knew the whereabouts of two others. With five of six accounted for, the task was no longer such a vague, unreachable idea. It was starting to take shape as something that the four of them could, possibly, complete. The four of them è Draco frowned. There it was again. He was thinking as if he were one of them. But wasnít he? Hadnít Granger said it yesterday? Being the arrogant little snot of the group doesnít actually exclude you from the group. Heíd given them information over and over, had helped them plan for months now. If safety had been his only motivator, he would have withdrawn from the mission the moment the Fidelius Charm had been cast, then refused to go against the Dark Lordís cause. Instead, heíd only become more involved since theyíd moved into the cottage, training the Gryffindors in their infiltration efforts. For Merlinís sake, heíd nearly died for their goal yesterday. And why? He found himself thinking of the shock of Crabbeís words in the Ministry. The way heíd stopped dead in Pansyís bedroom, unable to leave. The tug in his chest when heíd heard that Goyle was making his father suspicious, that he and Pansy might be shying away from the path that had been laid down for all of them. Draco supposed it was the feeling heíd tried so hard to bury since heíd accepted Dumbledoreís offer. He couldnít let his life go. He wanted to be alive again. And for that to happen, the Gryffindors needed to kill the Dark Lord. This was the only pathway back to his life: the destruction of the Horcruxes. The end of the Dark Order. So let them hunt the Horcruxes themselves, insisted a voice in his mind. He knew that his parents would take that view. They would be absolutely appalled that heíd gone into Diagon Alley; even in retrospect, Draco could hardly believe heíd done that, himself. Wasnít it sheer Gryffindor idiocy to have done it? But if heíd stepped back and allowed the Gryffindors to go by themselves, they would have failed. They might even, all three of them, be in Azkaban, or dead. And now, instead, with his help, they were one step closer to destroying the Horcruxesîone step closer to a world where Draco could live. It was strange, knowing that he'd been pivotal to an Order victory. Stranger still was his lack of guilt. Rather, he felt as if he were prying his life out of the Dark Lordís hands, bit by bit. Last year, the Dark Lord had tortured him and disgraced his mother and consigned his father to waste away in Azkaban after all their devotion, all their faithful service. The Dark Lord had been willing to throw Dracoís life away just to humiliate his parents. In essence, the Dark Lord had thought he didnít matterîand Draco had taken that belief into himself, that nothing he could ever do would stand up against the might of the Dark Order. But heíd resisted the Dark Lord for nearly four months, now. They had one Horcrux in hand, a piece of the Dark Lordís precious soul, and their sights on another. By that math, it could all be over in another year. If his parents stopped him from helping with the mission, on the other hand, who knew how long it might take? What if he merely waited to contact them? Or sent an owl? They were safe in New Cathcove, werenít they? What if he waitedè even just until they had retrieved the locket? But as the idea formed, disgust surged through him. Draco came back into himself as if heíd been pinched awake from a dream. He didnít know where all this was coming from. Granger, probably, and the way sheíd started talking to him lately, like he was an irreplaceable part of this. But his parents would have done anything to keep him safe, and he would do the same for them. If bringing them within the bounds of the Fidelius Charm meant having to pull back from the Horcrux quest, so be it. Draco gripped the pieces of parchment tightly and stood. The Gryffindors would just have to manage without him from here on. He could ask his father about Durmstrangís whereabouts, pretending some kind of unrelated curiosity, and that would be it, the last bit of help he gave them. When Draco knew nothing else, evenî apparentlyîhis own feelings, he knew this much. He was a Malfoy first. The rest of the world came second. He turned on his heel and Disapparated. The pressurised darkness of Apparition clamped down on his shoulder, pain spiraling through him with twice as much force. It wasnít exactly a gentle way to travel. When he reappeared in a small, dark living room, he was gasping, his vision swimming. The Breakwater, the Tonksís beach house in New Cathcove, was perched on the coast. Draco could look out the back window and see tongues of gray water lapping at the colorless sand. But something was amiss, he realised, as he clutched to his bandaged shoulder. The glass coffee table was filmed with dust. There was no sign of a struggle, but the place felt empty and lonely. ØMother?Æ Draco called. ØFather?Æ He moved toward a narrow hallway and pressed the Muggle light switch. A glass bulb ignited down the hallway, revealing two open doors. Draco glanced into two bedrooms: one with a bed large enough for a couple, another with a twin. This must have been where Tonks had slept during her familyís holidays. Draco felt a twinge of disdain. Some holiday, this shack of a place. Yet in the photograph of a teenaged Tonks and her parentsîTonks sporting a mohawk of brilliant greenîall three looked thrilled, sunburned but waving out at Draco as if theyíd just had the day of their lives. It was unusual to Draco, the mixture of Muggle house and Wizarding photos, the collision of the two worlds, but it also felt oddly mundane. Draco retreated down the hall, and as he entered the kitchen, he saw it: a sheet of parchment on the tiled counter. He snatched it up. The handwriting wasnít either of his parentsí, but of course, there were spells to disguise such things, and they would have wanted to avoid that identifying trace, if the Breakwater had been discovered as an Order-connected house. This had to be itîtheyíd gone to a more secure location, and this would lead him there. He read: N, After three weeks without contact we are abandoning the idea that you will, as you assured us, ØCome back to fill us in.Æ We cannot sit idly by while our son is under constant threat. We plan to return to London and reunite our family ourselves. We do appreciate your quick action. That was it. Nothing on the back. No signature. Draco just stared at the words for a long moment. Then he let the parchment drift back onto the counter. Heíd missed them, and not even by a close margin. They hadnít been here for over a month. Draco felt something cold coming through him like a tonic and it was a moment before he identified the emotion. Heart thumping slowly, he glanced around the beach house one last time, then Disapparated. The pain was even worse this time. By the time he reappeared in the tentís guest room, he was hunched over, his hand fastened to his shoulder. The wound must have reopened beneath the bandages. He felt something wet and hot in the injury. When he straightened up and opened his eyes, Granger was there, standing before the window seat with a flask of bright yellow potion. ØI heard you Disapparate,Æ she said, proffering the flask, Øand Iíd just finished the Numbing è the èÆ She hesitated, glancing into the corners of the room, clearly expecting Dracoís parents to appear. As the seconds wore on, realisation showed on her face. ØTheyíre not there." ØBrilliant deduction, Granger,Æ Draco muttered. He climbed into bed, trying not to think about the dark waves of pain that were thudding down onto his shoulder. ØI suppose youíre happy, are you?Æ ØHappy?Æ She frowned. ØWhy would I be?Æ ØBecause you donít have toîÆ His voice was rising. ØBecause now IîÆ He let out a disgusted sound and looked away. A brief pause. Then she said, ØIím sure theyíre safe. If theyíd been caught, it would have been on the Wireless.Æ ØYeah? Youíre sure, are you? Well, thank God for that.Æ ØThereís no need to be nasty,Æ she said testily. ØI gave you that address and thereís no reason I should be pleased that they werenít there.Æ ØOf course there is,Æ he snapped, Øbecause they wouldnít have let me keep doing this.Æ Granger didnít say anything. When he looked up at her, her face had reddened. ØIím right, arenít I?Æ Draco demanded. ØYouíre glad theyíre gone, because it means I still have to be on your side!Æ ØPeople can feel more than one thing at once,Æ she snapped back. ØI can be sorry that you donít know where they are and alsoîand alsoîÆ ØAlso what?Æ ØRelieved that youíre not going to turn back into that person they make you!Æ Draco stared at her. It took a moment for him to find words. ØWhatís that supposed to mean,Æ he sneered, Øthe person they make me? Theyíre my parents, Granger. I am what they made me.Æ ØYouíre not.Æ She was holding so tightly to the flask of Numbing Draught that her hands were trembling. ØYou know youíre not. I think the reason youíre lashing out at me is because part of you wants to keep looking for Horcruxes.Æ ØOh, yeah? Thatís what you think, is it?Æ ØYes! Draco wondered if you could betray something if you had no loyalties. He wondered, in that moment, if heíd ever had loyalties to anything larger than fear and self-regard. Had he ever really believed in the Dark Lordís cause, or only in his own desire for safety and superiority? For Merlinís sake, when was the last time heíd even thought about the fact that Granger was Muggle-born? The disgust? Nothing seemed to make sense. Draco squeezed his eyes shut, and the world was a red pulse, thudding like a hammer against him. The pain was coming hard and hot through his shoulder. Itísîitís being in the stands at a Quidditch game, and getting a bit worried about exams, and going home for the holidays. Itís just sitting there in the Great Hall and having a laugh with our friends. è Itís not all about morals. Or if it is about morals, itís because those are the morals that let us have a happy life. And Voldemort doesnít want me to have that, or any of us. Even you! I mean, youíre a pure-blood! You believe in all those things that he believes in, and he doesnít even want you to haveîÆ ØI donít know.Æ The words were out of Dracoís mouth before he could think about them. Grangerís lips were parted. She looked at him in astonishment for a long moment. ØWhat è what did you say?Æ Dracoís throat was so tight that he could barely speak. He looked away from Granger. ØI donít know,Æ he muttered again. ØWhat I think about è about those things.Æ ØOh.Æ Her voice was very small. ØWell, thatís. Good.Æ He could feel her eyes fixed on the side of his face. He said, ØYeah,Æ not really knowing what he was saying yeah to. ØYour shoulder must really hurt,Æ she said, standing suddenly. He seized on the topic with a kind of desperation. ØYeah. It does.Æ ØSorry, Iím sorry, Iíve just been sitting here holding this!Æ She let out a shaky laugh. ØItís a weaker version of the Numbing Solution we made last year with Slughorn. In February, I think. I donít know if you remember. Anyway, Iíve diluted it with another half-scoop of beetle eyes, so you should at least be able to feel the injury enough to know if itís started hurting more intensely, which could be a bad signèÆ She kept babbling, conjuring loops of bandages upon the bed, then daubing the Numbing Solution onto the white cloth. Draco watched her doing it with a kind of detachment, only half-listening, knowing she was still thinking about what heíd said, too, turning the words over and over in her head the way he was turning them over in his. It felt absolutely mental that heíd admitted his uncertainty aloud. At the same time, he knew it was between them only. He hadnít told Potter and Weasley about the diadem, and he knew Granger hadnít told them what the Dark Lord had done to him, or what Crabbe had said in the Ministry. He didnít know how he knew sheíd kept quiet îhe just did. And this, too, she would keep to herself. He watched her preparing the bandages, still monologuing about the components of the Numbing Solution, and this bossy, overbearing girl seemed nothing more than a cover, for a moment, for the girl who asked him questions that probed all the way into what he was, and never used the answers to injure him. She corked the flask. ØOld bandages off, please,Æ she said. Draco slipped his robes off his shoulders, watching her as he did. She was looking away again, her face turning red. The feeling of flustering her was new. It made him feel a pleasurable kind of relaxation, as if to compensate for her visible uncertainty. He took his wand from the bedside table and flicked it. His old bandages loosened, slid away, and coiled up on the table, stained with blood. He glanced down at his shoulder, the motion causing another twinge of pain. The wound had opened, but not severely. A thin, dark fissure and a single bead of blood working its way down his chest. Granger took a slow breath. She seemed to be steeling herself. Then she leaned forward and smoothed the ointment-dampened bandages over his shoulder. A frizzy lock of her hair brushed against his cheek. Draco took a short, surprised breath and smelled something clean and light, like citrus and pear. The same scent heíd smelled in the Ministry supply closet as he drifted in and out of consciousness. And as her fingers smoothed the bandage into place, the Numbing Ointment took effect, and a fantastic sense of relief washed over his shoulder, muffling the pain to a whisper. His thoughts stopped racing. He took another breath of the light, sweet scent, the smell of her hair. Then she was pulling back and flicking her wand, and the bandages tightened securely into place. ØThere,Æ she said. ØIt should last you the rest of the day. We can change them tomorrow.Æ He nodded, and she headed for the door. When her hand was on the knob, he said, ØThanks.Æ A light smile pulled at her lips. ØOf course,Æ she said. Then she was gone. As the door clicked shut, he heard her voice againîthe way sheíd said, I want my life back, too, with barely concealed yearning. He almost couldnít believe that the Gryffindors were motivated by self-interest as much as he was. He had only ever really thought about them as Dumbledoreís little heroes, fighting the Dark Lord because they were driven by ideas of chivalry and had no concept of self-preservation. He thought of the way sheíd donned the diadem in the Room of Hidden Things, and he thought he finally understood. Heíd done the same thing, he realised. Heíd walked into Diagon Alley, knowing what might happen. They did these things because there was no world for them if they didnít. Chapter End Notes it's my birthday today!!!! a gift from me to you :D tumblr | instagram Chapter13:The Wandmakerandthe Thief Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Draco spent most of the remainder of September in bed. Hermione changed his bandages at first three times a day, then twice, then weaned him off the Numbing Solution. Even when it became clear that he was well enough to change his own bandages, she found herself continuing to come in and do it herself. She supposed it had something to do with the atmosphere around the cottage and the tent. Sheíd hoped that Ron would become accustomed to the idea of staying friends, and that as he did so he would regain his warmth and humor, but he seemed to be sinking in on himself instead, growing more and more dejected. One day he would be able to speak a few stilted sentences to her. The next, his eyes would be reddened, and he would be quiet throughout their attempted planning sessions. Harry, chronically non-confrontational about these things as he was, proved no help whatsoever. And so Hermione found herself in the topsy-turvy world of dreading meals with her two best friends, but feeling almost eager for the hour or so a day she spent with Draco Malfoy. Draco could be annoying, as sheíd known for the majority of her sentient life at this point. He could be juvenile and overdramatic. But his recovery seemed to confirm that the juvenility and dramatics were mostly done for comedic effect, rather than being actual tenets of his personality. It was a kind of character he put on. Hermione had started combing through the Pottersí library to find texts about Durmstrang and Grindelwald for Draco to read, hoping to find traces of the triangular mark, and though he would sigh and roll his eyes and complain about all that he did for them without so much as a thank-you, he did read the books. Whenever she came in, heíd be paging through them, marking them with slips of parchment when he found something of potential use. ØThis one says, åè the very halls of Durmstrang still bear traces of the infamous Dark wizard who once walked the school,íÆ he said one day, sounding disgusted. ØSo, thatís got to be referring to the mark. But they move right on. Donít actually say anything useful about it.Æ He chucked A Modern History of Wizarding Scandinavia to the end of the bed. Hermione sighed, turning through International Wizarding Schools and Assorted Curricula in her usual spot in the window seat. ØThatís the problem with a lot of these Wizarding historians of the early 20th century,Æ she remarked. ØThey seem to think some details are only there to add flavor to their descriptions, rather than being intrinsically meaningful.Æ ØNot to mention,Æ Draco muttered, Øtheyíre all so terrified by the idea of being seen as sympathetic to Dark wizards, they wonít even touch the topic.Æ He scoffed. ØItís like they think writing about Dark spells is the same thing as getting the bloody Dark Mark.Æ Hermione turned her page, though she hadnít finished reading the previous one. Sheíd never heard Draco make that kind of offhand reference to Voldemort or the Death Eaters. ØSpeaking of which,Æ she said tentatively, ØIíve been meaning to ask. That Protean Charm ... I know Voldemort can't use it to trace everyone with the Dark Mark, or Karkaroff wouldnít have been able to run. But if he were to try to summon you, he couldnít tell that youíre still alive, could he?Æ Draco was still looking down at his book, but she could tell heíd stopped reading. When he answered, it was in a forced-casual tone that didnít convince her. ØItís not that different from the usual Protean Charm, Granger. When he touches his own Mark, he changes the Mark on whichever of our bodies heíd like. It doesnít matter whether that bodyís alive or dead.Æ More stiffly than ever, he added, ØWeíre just objects to him.Æ ØBut that still means that the charm would break eventually, when decay intrudes upon the charmed area.Æ Draco looked up from his book. ØYeah,Æ he said slowly, sounding unsettled. ØI suppose.Æ ØWell, then,Æ Hermione said, trying to sound businesslike, as if this were any other logistical detail, Øwe need to break the Charm from this end at a realistic time. Obviously we wonít want to undo it through injury; it would have to be a major wound. But there must be an Unraveling Counter-Charm we can use. Did Dumbledore mention what sort of burial you were meant to have been given? Did they use Embalming Charms?Æ ØOur family donít do that sort of thing. The Lestranges and the Notts, yeah, but thatís so they can have their annual crypt viewings.Æ ØIím sorryîcrypt viewings?Æ Dracoís lip curled in amusement. ØYeah. Theo took me to one in third year. They all queue up and parade through the crypts, and thereís generations of dead Notts lying there on platforms made out of Italian marble. I think the oldest one is from the 15th Century, but he looks like he died about five minutes ago. And they read them each a line from a memorial poem in Blood Most Ancient, Power Most Profane. It takes hours. Iíve never been so bored in my life.Æ Hermione couldnít help an astonished laugh. ØAre you serious?Æ ØDeadly,Æ said Draco, and then they were both laughing, Hermione looking up at the ceiling, Draco snickering down at the pages of his book. ØIt costs a fortune, too,Æ he went on. ØBecause they extend the Embalming Charms every six months for the whole family, and itís not exactly a normal branch of charmwork, is it. Thereís only a handful of specialists in the whole country. è Not that Theoís family hires Brits for it. The Egyptians are the best, obviously. Been embalming people perfectly for millennia.Æ ØItís actually sort of fascinating,Æ said Hermione thoughtfully. ØI mean, I suppose Theo would find cremation to be an insane ritual.Æ When Draco raised an eyebrow, she said, ØA lot of Muggles incinerate their bodies and keep the ashes in an urn.Æ ØThey what?Æ Draco said, gawking at her. ØThey burnîlike on a pyre?Æ ØNo, there are special machines for it, actually.Æ Draco looked deeply disturbed. ØItís better than parading through a museum of dead people,Æ Hermione said, feeling defensive. ØYeah, well, no oneís pretending the Notts arenít weirdos.Æ Hermione smiled and closed her book. ØIíll start looking into Unravelers, in any case. I think itís better to break the charm too early than too late and have Voldemort get suspicious.Æ Draco flinched. Hermioneís smile faded. ØThe Taboo canít get through the Fidelius Charm,Æ she reminded him. ØThey can only hang around outside.Æ ØItís not èÆ He gave his head a little shake. ØNever mind. Go stick that nose in a book, would you?Æ ØYes, you do the same,Æ she said, standing. ØAnd try to finish that one quickly, please. Iíve just found a set of histories of Wizarding Austria that might have something useful; I know Grindelwaldís campaign was particularly aggressive there.Æ ØMerlinís beard,Æ Draco muttered as she went for the door. ØIt never ends with you, does it?Æ But when she glanced back at him, his eyes looked amused. September wore into October, and the trees outside turned glorious shades of russet and gold. One afternoon, Hermione walked through the cottage and realized that it had transformed from a dismal shell into a quaint, inviting place with spotless floors and brightly repainted walls. The photographs of the laughing, waving Potters no longer seemed such a tragic reminder. James and Lily Potterís smiling faces belonged in this place, which had become a home again. The last room they restored was the ruined nursery. Theyíd talked around what to do with this room since theyíd arrived in the cottage. In the end, rather than multiplying the bricks to remake the walls or rebuilding the roof, they installed sheets of glass in every gap and hole, tenting glass ceilings up over the room as if it were a greenhouse. The damage to the cottageís structure remained, but the sun shone through the sites of impact. They collapsed the tent and moved into the cottage on the same day that the bandages came off Dracoís shoulder, revealing a seam of scar tissue a centimeter wide. Harry, Ron, and Hermione moved into the two bedrooms of the upper storey, Draco into the guest room on the ground floor. Headquarters was gold and red, sun and comfort, but the world outside was growing darker and colder. They heard the report on the Wireless a week and a half after their escape from the Ministry. Øè and lastly, members of the Department of Magical Law Enforcement have discovered treachery from within the Ministryís own ranks. The suspect, Nymphadora Tonks, formerly of the Auror Office, is believed to have enabled the violent attack on the Ministry that occurred last week. Aurors visited her dwelling yesterday evening, where she and her husband, werewolf Remus Lupin, resisted arrest, resulting in wounds to the Aurors in question. They are now at large and have been named to positions four and five on the Ministryís Undesirables list. The Auror Office has also issued an alert for the suspectís mother and father, the latter of whom is a Mudblood in illegal possession of a wand. Both are at large and dangerous. All four fugitives should be Stunned or otherwise immobilised on sight.Æ Long after the report had faded from the air, the four of them were silent. It was a chilly night, so theyíd lit a fire in the grate, but Hermione still couldnít feel her fingertips. She couldnít feel much of anything. She had done this, she knew. Her ten seconds of conversation with Tonks had been enough for someone to notice. She kept turning the moment over in her mind. Could she have done anything differently? Sheíd thought about Disillusioning herself, but at close quarters, in a brightly lit office, around dozens of Aurors trained to spot magical concealments è sheíd assumed she would have drawn more attention to herself than by simply walking through under Polyjuice. She had to believe that was true. ØWhere do you think theyíve gone?Æ she whispered. ØI donít know,Æ Harry said, ashen-faced. ØBut they'd better stay hidden. Heíll be fine.Æ Draco wanted to tell Potter he was being an optimistic idiot, but he found he couldnít be bothered. He could still hear Weasleyís voice saying, Youíve been busy keeping secrets with a Death Eater. Heíd been surprised by the way the words had hit him. Why did he care that Weasley still looked at him and saw a Death Eater? It wasnít like it was a huge surprise. And when had he ever given a damn about Weasleyís opinion? He wondered why he cared what anyone thought of him, at this point. Crabbeís father or Crabbe himself or Weasley or the rest of the Wizarding World. He knew what he was, and that should have been enough. He was reasonably sure he knew what he was, anyway. ØDracoís right, though,Æ said Hermione with more strength. ØWe should be prepared for the worst case.Æ ØIím not leaving,Æ Potter said. ØWeíve worked too hard on this place, Hermione.Æ Hermione sighed. ØWell, weíll have to be on the alert all the time, then. Iíll repack my bag with the essentials. The Horcrux, the tent, Polyjuice, some books. You two, pack some robes for me to put in, too. Iíll keep the bag on me, and if we hear anyone Apparate in and Ron doesnít say itís him, we Disapparate right away to that cave we used in our old escape drills, all right?Æ Hermione hurried up the steps while Potter collected the teacups and saucers. But once Hermioneís door had shut upstairs, Potterís motions slowed. ØListen," he said. "Er. Draco.Æ ØYeah?Æ Draco said. ØThanks. For what you did for Hermione.Æ Draco gave a curt nod. Potter looked into the hearth, where the fire had burned to embers. ØI canít believe Ron and I didnít see it. Itís lucky that you did. I mean, not lucky, butîyeah.Æ He hesitated. ØAnd for what itís worth, I donít think Ron meant any of that, what he said. He was holding the Horcrux, and he hasnít been feeling right for weeks. We know youíre not a Death Eater anymore.Æ ØArenít I?Æ Draco said stiffly. ØI still have the Mark.Æ There was a pause. Then Potter shrugged. ØAll Iíll say is, if you are still a Death Eater, youíre doing a pretty shit job of things.Æ Draco let out a short laugh. ØYeah. I suppose I would be.Æ Draco expected Weasley to barge back in within a day, asking for forgiveness, but two days went by, then three, and he didnít reappear. This seemed to wound Hermione and Potter in a way that neither could articulate. They kept shooting looks at the empty seat at the dinner table, and pained expressions crossed their faces whenever they mentioned him. But overall, day-to-day life was smoother without the mood swings that heíd been prone to before his departure. They kept the Wireless on at all hours, now. If Tonks, Lupin, or Weasley was capturedîand Draco was certain that any of the three would constitute a major announcementî headquarters would need to be evacuated immediately. Days passed, though, and the only interesting thing the Wireless mentioned was the official willing of Malfoy Manor to the Lestrange family, after a legal battle including several other claimants: minor cousins of Dracoís, all of whom his parents had loaned large sums to on multiple occasions. The idea of those leeches clawing at his familyís legacy made Draco so furious that he had to pace the garden for a while, scarf wrapped tightly to keep away the increasing chill. Potter checked the Marauderís Map every day, but Weasley never showed up there, either. It seemed Potterís instincts had probably been correct, and Weasley had returned to his family. Draco would have thought that with some distance, Weasleyís recognition of the Horcruxesí importance would have brought him back from the Burrow, no matter his feelings of rejection. But if he hadnít been captured and hadnít returned to Hogwarts, where else could he be than home? ØYou donít think heís trying to hunt down Hufflepuffís Cup on his own?Æ said Hermione one night, without any context whatsoever, long after Potter had gone to sleep. Draco sighed, but he was grateful for the excuse to flip his book shut. His eyes were exhausted. ØYouíve got to stop thinking about it.Æ ØI know. It's just that if I hadnít shouted at himîÆ ØHe wanted you to shout at him.Æ "Well, that's probably true. But I still donít understand why he hasnít come back.Æ Hermione stretched her legs out on the sofa. ØHave you thought any more about where your parents might be?Æ Draco set his book on a side table and scrubbed his hands through his hair. ØNo idea. They said London, and we hardly ever went to London. Bellaís family has a house there, but obviously my parents wouldnít go near it now. They must be trying to contact other Order members. The twins have that shop, and their father works at the Ministry, so èÆ He sighed. ØAnyway, I donít see how I could get word to them, so it doesnít matter.Æ ØOf course it matters,Æ Hermione said. ØAll this worrying about people we have no way to reach, itís just awful.Æ ØYeah. Really makes you consider the merits of the Dark Mark.Æ ØHa ha.Æ She gave him a stern look. Draco smirked and sank down in his armchair, letting his eyes close. ØCan I ask you something?Æ Hermione asked after a moment. ØDepends what youíre asking.Æ ØIíll take that as a yes.Æ ØYes, then.Æ ØDo your parents have any friends who are Muggle-borns?Æ Draco opened his eyes, suddenly feeling much more awake. It had been nearly a month since heíd admitted to having second thoughts about blood status. Some days he still felt angry with himself, embarrassed that heíd said something like that out loud. He was a Malfoy, for Godís sake. Maybe everyone had blood-traitor thoughts at times, but to go so far as to say it out loud è had it been guilt? The pain in his shoulder, maybe? But then, sometimes, he felt something besides anger, something altogether more unsettling. Sometimes he found himself curious, in a wary kind of way, about Hermioneís Muggle upbringing. These moments triggered a kind of panicked repulsion, the need to get the curiosity out of his head. Heíd feel a wash of shame and self-loathing. His parents would be appalled. His whole family would be appalled. So he felt cautious, now, skirting the topic. ØNo,Æ he said curtly. ØNone.Æ ØDo you think they ever did?Æ ØDoubtful. They wouldnít have met any Muggle-borns in Slytherin as kids, anyway.Æ He paused. ØWhy?Æ ØOh, well, I was just wondering. Do you think, if your parents made friends with a Muggle-born, they might think any differently? Even a little bit?Æ She added, clearly trying to sound offhand, ØI mean, weíre friends, arenít we?Æ Dracoís defensiveness faded. He could tell that this had been the actual question sheíd wanted to askîshe really was an atrocious liarîwhich meant the conversation wasnít really about blood status. They were moving out of dangerous waters. Moreover, he was surprised to find that the answer to the question was easy. ØYeah,Æ he said. ØI think so.Æ Hermioneís expression brightened. ØI do, too.Æ He raised one eyebrow. ØDid you think Iíd say no to that?Æ ØWell, I donít know,Æ she huffed, fluffing the sofa cushion behind her head. ØYour mind works in strange ways.Æ ØI donít exactly go around blabbing to anyone aboutîabout the things weíve talked about,Æ Draco muttered. ØActually, most of it I donít tell anyone at all.Æ She smiled. ØItís the same with me. Itís funny, isnít it?Æ ØYeah.Æ There was a long silence. He looked over at her. Her eyes were closed, and so he watched the low red of the dying fire playing over her features. She looked soft and vulnerable in a way she rarely did, lying curled up like that. A lock of hair had straggled over her cheek, shadowing her eye. Draco found himself remembering the moment in the Ministry cupboard that heíd awoken to find her face inches from his, her expression frantic, her hands slipping against his skin. At that moment, heíd felt like heíd never seen her before, like that had been the first time heíd really looked at the rich warm color of her brown eyes, the slightly plaintive curves of her brows, the color of her always-bitten lips. He looked away from her, frowning. His heart was beating a bit too deliberately, like someone knocking at a door to come in. As October wore on, the three of them spent every day in the cottage library. The days began to wash into each other. They would wake up and spend mornings through lunchtime trying to brainstorm ways to get to Umbridge. ØIf we could just contact Kingsley,Æ Potter said often, Øand get him instated as Umbridgeís security detailèÆ But that idea struck a hard dead end when Kingsley was added to the Undesirables list after a run-in with Death Eaters. ØI wish we could do something for the Order members on the run,Æ Potter said fiercely. ØHow could we?Æ Draco said. ØWe havenít even got our Secret-Keeper anymore. We canít share the secret.Æ An uncomfortable silence as thoughts of Weasley hovered in the air. ØHe knows that, too,Æ Potter muttered. ØI donít know what heís playing at.Æ Hermione drew a sharp breath. ØHang on. Thereís another way! Draco, we still have that piece of paper that Ron wrote the address on for your parents. Youíd better give that to me straight away, so I can keep it in my bag and keep it safe.Æ The afternoons felt, if possible, even more frustrating and unproductive. They pored through every single book that might mention Grindelwaldís mark, their research punctuated only by dinner. Many of the texts were so dry that to read a hundred pages in a day was a great achievement. Draco went to bed every night with his head swimming in information about early 20th-century Europe. He felt as if they were moving in endless circles, and that with every day they failed to make a breakthrough, he was endangering his parents, and Pansy and Goyle, not to mention the Order members on the run, who could at any moment be forced to reveal him. Then, one particularly chilly night, Potter came into the sitting room with the bottle of Firewhisky theyíd sipped from on the evening of Hermioneís birthday. ØAll right, you two,Æ he declared. ØNo more reading.Æ ØExcuse me?Æ said Hermione, looking like heíd just blasphemed in a church. ØYou heard me. Here.Æ And he tossed a glass to her, then Draco. Hermione made a quick grab and caught hers. Draco dropped Against the Dark Arts: a History of Wizarding Power Struggles and caught his, too. ØWhatís this?Æ Draco said, narrowing his eyes. ØWeíre taking the night off,Æ Potter said firmly. ØIím going mental. I know you two are, too. Weíll never find anything if we lose our minds in here. Besides, itís Halloween. Theyíre feasting at Hogwarts right now.Æ He filled his own glass, then flicked his wand. The bottle of Firewhisky poured Dracoís and Hermioneís drinks of its own accord, then set itself upon the mantel with a clunk. ØHalloween,Æ said Hermione, with a strange, pensive look. ØIt is, isnít it? Iíd completely lost track of time.Æ Draco knew this was the night that the Dark Lord had found this cottage sixteen years ago. It was no wonder that Potter wanted some distraction. ØFine,Æ he said, setting his book aside. ØBut Iím not going trick-or-treating with you, Potter.Æ ØShame,Æ Potter said. ØI was looking forward to seeing your giant pumpkin costume.Æ ØHe could be a ghost,Æ Hermione suggested. ØHe wouldnít even need makeup.Æ They tittered. Draco did not dignify either of these comments with a response. He sipped the amber liquid, which was hot and bitter, with a sweetly lingering aftertaste. For several long minutes after Hermione and Potterís chuckles faded away, none of them spoke. Draco tried to think of some topic of conversation, and realized it had been so long since theyíd spoken about anything besides the Horcruxes, or the war, or missing persons, or Dolores bloody Umbridge and her bloody security detail, that nothing came to mind. Soon Dracoís glass was empty. When he held it out, Potter refilled it without a word. Hermione broke the silence. ØItís not bad, is it?Æ she said somewhat awkwardly, still nursing her first glass. ØFirewhisky, I mean. I always preferred Butterbeer, but this is nice on a cold night.Æ ØThereís a bottle of Firewhisky hidden in the Slytherin Common Room,Æ Draco said. ØItís a tradition. You canít tell anyone below fourth year where it is, and if you finish the Common Room Bottle, you have to be the one to buy the replacement.Æ ØThatís not bad,Æ Potter said. ØSort of fun, really,Æ Hermione said. ØI wish Gryffindor had something like that.Æ ØWell, we are the superior house.Æ Draco sank down in his armchair so that his hair rubbed against the leather. Out of stubbornness, he hadnít cut his hair since Hermione had compared him to Snape, but she was right. It was practically Pansyís length at this point. He realized his face was tingling. The Firewhisky was hitting him quickly. ØWe did have Fred and George,Æ said Potter, polishing off his own first glass. ØThey used to nick food from the Kitchens whenever we won a Quidditch match.Æ Draco snorted. ØThat wasnít a Gryffindor thing. We did that, too.Æ ØI miss it so much,Æ Hermione sighed, tracing the cracks in her leather chair. ØHogwarts.Æ ØMe too,Æ said Potter. ØYeah,Æ Draco muttered. ØWhat do you wish you could do again most?Æ Hermione said, holding out her glass for a refill. ØHarry, I suppose itís Quidditch for you?Æ ØI donít know,Æ said Potter. ØI donít think so, actually. Visit Hagrid, maybe. Or è well.Æ His voice became low and bashful. ØThere were those couple months that I had with Ginny.Æ ØYou really miss her, donít you?Æ said Hermione softly. Potter couldnít seem to form words. He took another long sip. ØAnd IîI suppose you miss Pansy, Draco.Æ Hermione glanced at him. Color had tinged her cheeks, from the drink, probably, a dusky rose shade. ØWe broke up halfway through last year,Æ Draco said. ØOh. I didnít realize.Æ Draco shrugged. ØWe werenít great together. She let me get away with anything.Æ Hermione looked amused. ØI would have thought youíd like that.Æ ØYou would, would you?Æ Draco said. The words came out lower than heíd intended, and he was looking at her hairline, for some reason, the way her hair looked very soft right there, the wisps of her curls. She gave him a hesitant smile, then looked away, back to Potter. Draco felt an unusual wash of heat over the back of his neck. Feeling confused, he looked down at his drink. ØYeah, anyway,Æ he went on, bolstering his voice back into a confident drawl, Øshe started going out with Theo Nott. Theyíd be good if they kept it going. Theoís sort of an idiot sometimes, but heís actually liked her for ages. I donít even think she realized.Æ ØI wonder what theyíre doing right now,Æ Harry said. ØWhat, Pansy and Theo?Æ ØEveryone. Neville and Seamus and Luna andîand Blaise Zabini, and the Creeveys, and everybody. I donít know. BloodyîErnie Macmillan.Æ They were all grinning, then. ØI know what Ernie Macmillanís doing,Æ Draco said. He straightened up in his armchair and puffed out his chest. ØIíve already drawn up my N.E.W.T. study schedules, of course,Æ he said in Ernieís pompous tones. Potter puffed out his chest, too, blustering, ØIím already getting in twelve hours of study on weekends, myself èÆ ØBut twelve hours is a bad day,Æ Hermione added. ØI canÆîa giggle escaped herîØcan fit in fourteen if I stop eating.Æ ØFourteen and a half, when I stop using the toilet,Æ Potter managed before they all dissolved into laughter. Potter, who had been standing by the mantel, settled to the ground cross-legged, grinning. It was almost easy after that. Theyíd broken out of the dour, pressurized routine, and soon enough they were talking about all the other students, and then reliving their O.W.L.s, and Hermione was badgering Draco to tell them his scores while Draco scoffed and Potter lay back on the woven rug and laughed. They talked about broom models and holidays and troll hunters. Hermione and Potter recounted the bizarre experience that was Muggle primary school, and Draco allowed himself to listen without reacting; the drink let him do it, helped him glide over thoughts of what it might mean that he was listening to stories of Muggle life without jeering, or even really wanting to. And soon the talk turned back to Hogwarts, anyway, and what jobs theyíd considered after school. Dragon wrangling and experimental Transfiguration. The night darkened, the stars outside seeming to brighten, but maybe that was the Firewhisky, too. They drank glass after glass, until the bottle was low and their voices were tired and scratchy, and Draco felt like his body was unknitting, all the advanced knots of tension of the last few months coming loose, and he kept glancing over at Hermione, for some reason, watching the way her hair caught and spun the light like a hypnotic object of focus. Then, halfway through telling a story about his First Magic ceremony, Draco realized Potter had fallen asleep right there on the rug. ØRude,Æ he said. Hermione let out a giggle. ØI was wondering when youíd notice.Æ ØThink we should move him?Æ Draco's words were sloppy. His tongue no longer seemed on exactly the same track as his mind. Also he felt as if his brain were orbiting his head. This was probably normal. ØLet him rest,Æ said Hermione, the edges of her words wandering, too. She gave her wand a nondescript wave, and Potterís glasses slid from his face and folded themselves, and the patch of rug beneath him swelled up into a makeshift mattress. ØItís good heís gotten to sleep. Especially since itís the anniversary.Æ ØHe didnít say anything about it.Æ ØWelcome to Harry,Æ Hermione said. ØNever mentions anything that bothers him. I was shocked he even brought Ginny up.Æ She bobbed her shoulders. ØNot a surprise once you get to know him. His aunt and uncle were really horrible. Really, really horrible, I mean, theyíd barely feed him for weeks at a go, you know. è Sometimes I think heís still not used to people caring what he thinks or feels.Æ Draco doubted she would have said it without the drink. But now she was looking down at Potter with a soft sympathy, even tenderness. It occurred to Draco that this look bothered him. ØAccio,Æ Draco said lazily. The bottle of Firewhisky sped into his outstretched hand. He poured himself yet another glass. Hermione extended her own for him to refill. She wasnít gazing at Potter anymore. Good, he thought vaguely. He let the tip of the bottle rest on the lip of her glass and lifted its body, aware that the simple action was requiring a bit too much focus, and he might possibly, maybe, be a bit drunk. He filled her glass until it was a sparkling column, then glanced up at her. She was watching him pour. She looked like October in that moment, with the tumble-toss of her brown hair and the glowing flush of her tan skin and a bead of amber liquid sparkling on her lower lip, loosely woven orange jumper, firelight. Draco could tell she was drunk, too. When she blinked, her eyelids glided slowly over her brown eyes as if she were halfway to sleep. ØDo you know what?Æ she said with a tiny hiccup. ØWhat?Æ ØYour hair really does look ridiculous.Æ Draco let out a dramatic sigh. ØMerlin, fine. Iíll let you cut it. You couldíve just asked.Æ He expected her to splutter that she hadnít meant that at all, because obviously she hadnít, but she leaned her head back on the sofa and laughed. Then her face grew very serious and she said, ØDonít tempt me. Iíll do it.Æ Draco smiled idly at her. ØYeah? Do it, then, Granger.Æ ØAgain with the Granger.Æ She drew her wand. ØFine. Come on. Up.Æ ØGod. What? Are you serious?Æ Draco got to his feet, swaying as he did. ØYouíre actually going toî?Æ ØYes. You brought it on yourself. Youíre welcome.Æ She pushed him lightly into the hall, her hand pressed against his shoulder blade, gentle but firm. ØYou donít have to manhandle me,Æ he said. ØYes, I know, your life is a parade of endless suffering.Æ Draco didnít look back at her, because, he realized, he was grinning so widely and unevenly that his cheeks hurt. His head was spinning and he felt weightless. ØLook,Æ he said, Ønot to question your credentialsîÆ Cerdentals, he heard himself saying. Cerdentals. Honestly. Øî but have you ever even cut anyoneís hair before?Æ ØYes, I have. My cousinís. When I was nine.Æ ØAnd howíd that go?Æ ØEr,Æ she said, Øit grew back eventually, didnít it?Æ They tripped over the threshold into the downstairs bathroom, one after the other, and as they stifled their laughter, trying not to wake Potter up, Hermione tapped the lamp with her wandtip. A soft golden light appeared. The fixtures of the bathroom came into view, sparkling clean. Everything was glittering off itself, everything reflecting. Draco sat on the edge of the claw-foot tub, which was very uncomfortable, and stretched out his legs. He looked up at her as they set their half-full glasses of Firewhisky on the toilet seat. She was grinning and shaking her head, clumsily pushing up her sleeves, wand in one hand. ØThis is stupid,Æ she told him. ØJust really idiotic.Æ ØI know. Youíd better hurry up before I change my mind.Æ She tripped getting into the tub. Draco turned instinctively to stabilise her, his hand catching her forearm, and even as they broke into a fresh bout of half-suppressed laughter he noticed that her skin was soft and warm under his palm, and that he was surrounded by that scent, citrus and pear. She braced herself against the wall and regained her balance, and with an odd pulse of heat in his palms he let go of her, and she said, ØAll right, itís happening. Sit up straight. Say goodbye to the Sleekeazy-advertisement hair.Æ ØUnbelievable. Bossing me around like,Æ he said, but he forgot to finish the sentence, straightening on the edge of the tub, still feeling on the verge of laughter. Then he glanced up into the bathroom mirror and saw their reflections. Hermione, clearly unaware that he was watching, had lifted one of her hands to his hairîbut her fingertips had paused hardly an inch from him, hovering indistinctly in the dim light. Her smile was fading. As it did, so did the feeling of repressed laughter in Dracoís chest. Something like confusion or curiosity had appeared on her face. She looked like sheíd only just realized she would have to touch him to do this, and as he waited for her to do it, he realized he was holding his breath. Slowly, cautiously, she let her fingers sift into his hair. Her hand was warm, and as her fingertips slid over his scalp, lifting and carefully tugging, her wandtip scything away white-blond wisps, Draco felt an unfamiliar rush of shivery heat, then cold, sensory confusion. Minute by minute his hair began to frame his face in the usual way, rising over his high forehead, clipped closely near his ears. Neither of them was speaking, and the silence seemed to be rising in volume somehow. He became attuned to the small motions of her breaths, which, as she leaned closer, he felt on the back of his head. Her hands had begun to move more tentatively, and he knew she was feeling the atmospheric shift, too, as if the very air around them were being gripped in someoneís hands and drawn taut. Time swam oddly around Draco. It seemed an eternity had passed, or maybe no time at all, when she lowered her wand in the mirror and pocketed it, finished. His hair looked precisely the way it usually did. Apparently her overactive memory extended to this. But she didnít speak. She was looking down at the crown of his head, obviously flustered, her cheeks flushed, like she didnít understand what sheíd just done. Then, quite suddenly, she looked up into the mirror and saw him watching her. Dracoís mouth went dry. They were both motionless. Hermioneís lips were parted, her face very still, her eyes like autumn. Again, that confused curiosity showed in her expression. He watched her hand rise in the mirror. She laid her palm softly against the back of his neck. His mouth opened by a millimeter. A breath issued out, audible in the tiny room. Her hand moved upward, slipping past his ear. Her fingertips grazed the line of his jaw, and the very corner of his mouth, and brushed against his cheekbone, and there her hand rested, against his cheek, which, he realized, was flushed pink. Actually, his entire face had filled with heat. He couldnít focus on any one thought in particular. The sudden stillness of everything inside him. If he turned away from the mirror to face her. If he rose to his feet and touched her chin, her face. These were things that he could, at this moment, do. But then Hermione gave her head the tiniest shake, as if coming out of a reverie, and took her hand quickly away. ØItís done,Æ she said. She did not speak loudly, but the words still ruptured the silence with a kind of violence. Draco was jarred back into his body. He felt wrong all of a sudden. He realized how much his head was twirling. He rose unsteadily to his feet as she climbed out of the tub, and he had the vague sense of wanting to say something besides ØYeah,Æ but he couldnít manage more than the single syllable. Hermione seemed to have forgotten about her glass. She was already opening the bathroom door. ØIímîI should go to bed,Æ she said, moving back into the dark hall, her face half shadow and half light. There she hesitated, maybe waiting for him to reply, studying him like she didnít know what he was. But she did know, Draco thought, vision tilting slowly. She knew everything that had been done to him and everything that he had done to other people. She knew what had the power to hurt or disturb him. She knew how he looked in satisfaction and in pain. She knew his uncertainties. She knew what he was made of. But then he supposed he knew the same things of her. He knew her loyalty and her impatience and that exasperating righteousness. Her venomous streak and her occasional soft uncertainty. Her fondly irritated sighs. The fears she kept at bay. The crinkled shape between her eyebrows when she laughed. He knew how her fingertip felt at the corner of his lips. Somehow this seemed to change the rest. ØGoodnight,Æ she said, and then she was gone. Draco woke up the next day with a splitting headache. Hermione had been involved in his dream somehow, he was nearly sure. He could have sworn heíd awoken with her already in his mind. God, he thought, what had that been, last night? What the bloody hell had they been doing? In the piercing sunlight it seemed insane, so adolescent, the pair of them snickering and lurching down the hall in the dark, their hands brushing each other. Her fingers in his hair. Her hand tracing his face. The memory mortified him. He kept thinking about it as he slapped cold water on his cheeks in the bathroom, the same bathroom where not even hours before, heíd been inexplicably unable to stop looking at her. Hermione Granger, all books and opinions and stubborn earnestness, no poise or elegance or subtlety at all. And him, Draco Malfoy, staring at her like she wasîlike he was actuallyî It doesnít matter, he told himself, irritated. The natural conclusion was that heíd been drunk to the point of complete uncontrol. That had to be it. He knew she was attracted to himîheíd known it for a month now, hadnít he?îand he enjoyed the feeling, that was all, the slight power of it. Heíd indulged a bit in flustering her. Sort of stupid, yeah, but heíd been drunk. It was meaningless. When he came down the hall into the kitchen, though, he found Hermione standing at the cooker, and when she glanced over her shoulder and met his eyes, Draco felt a lurch in his chest. He felt her palm against his neck. Her finger brushing his ear. He felt destabilized. He didnít know where to look. ØGood morning,Æ she said with a small, tentative smile. ØMorning.Æ His voice sounded so stiff. Why? A silent moment, interrupted only by the sizzle of tomato in the frying pan. Color rose in her cheeks. ØItîit doesnít look bad,Æ she said. Her voice was higher than usual. ØYour hair. I was afraid Iíd mucked it up and didnít realize.Æ ØNo.Æ Draco tried to sound normal. ØYeah, itís fine.Æ He realized heíd stopped in the threshold, mid-step. He made himself move to the cabinets, open their yellow-painted doors, and take down plates and cups, clattering around more than was really necessary. ØNext time,Æ he drawled, Øitís your hair on the line.Æ Hermione let out a laugh. There was relief in it. ØSure. Thatíll go well.Æ She turned the slices of tomato in the pan and brushed her hair back from her face. Her cheeks were still pink, her eyes carefully trained on the fry-up. Draco realized heíd paused mid-motion again, looking over at her, his hands halfway out of the cabinet. He didnít know what was happening. Was he still drunk? ØMorning,Æ said a bleary voice. Draco and Hermione both jumped and turned around. Potter was standing in the threshold, a hangover personified. His hair seemed to have found several new, previously undiscovered directions to point in. His glasses were askew. ØH-hello,Æ said Hermione, too brightly. ØHow are you feeling, Harry?Æ ØLike someone hit me with a Beaterís bat.Æ Potterís eyes passed over Draco, and then he did a double-take. ØHang on. Is your hair different?Æ Between the pulses of his headache, Draco managed to say, ØObservant, arenít you?Æ Hermione made a stifled sound. Potterís eyes moved from Draco to Hermione. There was an odd look on his face, but after a moment he just said, ØAll right. Well. I think that night off should last us a while, donít you?Æ Draco and Hermione both heartily agreed. The night had yielded the intended effect, though. Their mental gears had restarted. New plans formed for Umbridge: if they could Confund a friend or family member, maybe they could set up a meeting with her in a less secure location. They began to take notes from the Wireless whenever she was mentioned, building out a web of acquaintances. They were also reading more quickly and with more detail than before. Hermione had borrowed The Life and Lies of Albus Dumbledore, and one afternoon, poring over the letter Dumbledore had written to Grindelwald, she noticed that the åAí in åAlbusí was replaced with the same triangular mark from The Tales of Beedle the Bard. They puzzled over what this might mean. ØWell, first of all,Æ Potter said, Øit means the mark definitely is Grindelwaldís. Itís not just a coincidence, or a student rumor of Krumís, or a Lovegood conspiracy theory, or anything.Æ Draco, who had also been reading through The Life and Lies, turned it all over in his mind. It had been disorienting to learn that Albus Dumbledore, of all people, had thought like this. But in a way it was almost reassuring. So, Dumbledore had been a real person, with a real past and real opinions. He hadnít been as mild and doddering as heíd played at. If he did act like he understood the Death Eaters, and even Draco, it was because heíd once had something in common with all of them. ØI bet he and Dumbledore came up with the mark together,Æ Draco said. ØGrindelwald took åFor the Greater Goodí out of this letter, after all. Iíd say the markís theirs, too.Æ ØThat canít be right,Æ Hermione said with a frown. ØViktor said Grindelwald carved it on the wall at Durmstrang when he was a student there. That would have been before the summer they met.Æ ØThen it must have been something Grindelwald told Dumbledore about,Æ said Potter, looking strained and irritable, as he always did when they acknowledged Dumbledoreís checkered past. ØI still think itís like a kind of Dark Mark. He used it to recruit people, and thatís why Dumbledore signed the letter like that. You know, a badge of loyalty to the cause.Æ ØThat still doesnít explain what Dumbledore meant, leaving it in Beedle the Bard,Æ Draco said. Silence fell in the library. They were sitting on the rug amid a pile of cushions, books strewn all around them. Harry picked up Wizarding Prisons and Rehabilitation Methods, a book of Hermioneís, then lowered it, brow furrowed. Theyíd read in that book several days ago that Grindelwald was still alive, and being kept in Nurmengard, the prison heíd built for dissenters during his own reign. ØI have an idea,Æ Potter said. Draco exchanged a wary look with Hermione. Theyíd privately discussed the possibility that Potter would latch onto this idea, in the way that Potter occasionally became obsessed with very bad ideas. ØHarry,Æ said Hermione in a rush, Øweíre not going to Nurmengard.Æ ØYeah,Æ Draco said. ØNo way, Potter. Even if we could find where it isîÆ ØHang on, what?Æ Potter blinked owlishly. ØI wasnít going to say anything about Nurmengard.Æ ØOh,Æ Draco and Hermione said at the same time. They exchanged another look. Mild embarrassment, this time. This trading-looks thing that they were suddenly doingîit was new, since Halloween, and Draco didnít know what to make of it. He remembered the start of summer, when heíd seen Hermione exchanging these sorts of looks with Weasley and Potter. Heíd thought disdainfully about how predictable it seemed, how dull, for someone to be able to guess your thoughts with a single glance. But it didnít feel like that from the other end. It was like an ongoing silent conversation, a running inside joke. They hadnít spoken about Halloween night. Since then, though, Draco had thought he could feel Hermione watching him whenever he was washing dishes or adjusting the Wireless. Heíd turn back toward her and her eyes would be on a book, but her cheeks would have that tinge to them, her face a bit too unconcerned. And yesterday night, theyíd passed each other in the narrow downstairs hallway, and both of their steps had caught as they moved around each other, and in the instant heíd glanced down into her face, heíd felt for the hundredth time the ghost of her fingertips upon his cheek. And heíd wondered whether she was remembering it, too. He wondered whether the memory came to her whenever they were close enough to touch, like now, sitting on cushions a foot apart, and whether she remembered it with a disorienting lurch before she went to sleep, and over lunch when they were sitting across from each other. Did she consider it a mistake? It had hardly lasted five seconds, the touch, and sheíd made no reference to it. But if it was a mistake, just a stray instinct induced by Firewhisky, why did she keep looking at him like that? Had she wanted to keep touching him? Had she, possibly, even been waiting for him to do what heíd drunkenly thought about in the momentîstand up, turn to face her, take her by the wristî Quite beside all that, did he want to have done those things? Draco felt like he was going insane. Maybe theyíd just been in this house together for too long. Yes, that could be why he was having these thoughtsîthoughts that were inappropriate, actually, because he was a Malfoy; he was his parentsí son; he was the Slytherin ideal. And she wasè well, she wasè Distracting. She was distracting. Draco realized heíd been studying Hermioneís face for long seconds. He didnít know how long. He looked away quickly. Potter was regarding them with that blank confusion again. Hermione cleared her throat. There was that blush. ØWell,Æ she said, Øwhat was your idea, then?Æ Potter gestured to the spread of books. ØI want to talk to Bathilda Bagshot." ØThat old witch who told Skeeter all this?Æ Draco said, flicking a page of The Life and Lies with distaste. ØYouíve already got it all in here. Why bother?Æ ØShe might know more about Grindelwald than made it into the pages of the book,Æ Harry insisted. ØHe was her nephew, wasnít he? And both times we've seen Grindelwald involved with that symbol, he was school-aged.Æ ØI donít know,Æ said Hermione. ØVisiting someone so connected to Dumbledore, with Death Eaters patrolling Godricís Hollow day and night, just waiting for us to step out of line? Itís èÆ She gave Potter an apologetic look. ØItís the same feeling I have about visiting your parentsí graves, Harry. It seems so risky. Besides, didnít Muriel say Bathilda was half out of her mind?Æ Potter grimaced but didnít reply. Draco, however, looked down at The Life and Lies of Albus Dumbledore, and an idea struck him. ØSkeeter,Æ he murmured. ØWhat?Æ said Hermione. ØRita Skeeter. In fourth year, she all but told me and my friends that she uses Veritaserum to get people to talk. Thatís what she must have done with Bagshot; the womanís got to be a hundred and forty. If we could get to Skeeter, then we can find out everything she knows about Dumbledore and Grindelwald, even the things she didnít think were worth putting into the book.Æ Draco flipped to the back and tapped the page. ØYeah. Here.Æ He turned the book toward the others so they could read: Tip-offs, fan mail, gifts, complaints, protests, lawsuits, and death threats can be owled to my assistant at this address: Titania Smethwyck Receiverís Box 320C Office of Magical Postal Receipts Number 48, Pinpitt Lane London Hermione and Potter had both tensed. ØDraco,Æ Hermione said. ØThis is an idea! All we have to do is go to this addressîÆ ØAnd wait for the assistant to check the box,Æ Potter broke in. ØThen we follow her to her house. Skeeterís home address is sure to be there somewhere. And if not, you can use Legilimency, Hermione.Æ Hermione winced. ØIíd rather not, but è yes, in a pinch, I think I could.Æ ØLetís do it tomorrow,Æ said Potter. ØTomorrow?Æ Draco said. ØYeah, why wait? This isnít like Diagon Alley. Itíll be simple. Hereís what weíll do. èÆ Early the next morning, they Apparated a block away from Number 48, Pinpitt Lane. All three stood huddled under the Invisibility Cloak, Disillusioned to solve the problem of their visible feet. Hermione was in front of the two boys, and they were moving so closely together that her hair was brushing Dracoís chin. He wasnít thinking about Halloween. He wasnít. Or the way her hip kept touching him as they moved. Her shoulder brushing his upper arm. The six or seven inchesí difference in their height. He was so distracted when they reached Number 48 that Hermione had to whisper, ØStop,Æ pointing toward the door. From further away, it had looked like a boarded-up frame. Muggles dressed for work were passing by without a second look. But when they stopped before it, the boards disappeared, and an elegant door of black-painted oak appeared, embossed with the golden words, Office of Magical Postal Receipts. They stood back and waited for the first employees to arrive.
