Soon enough a witch in crisp violet robes unlocked the door and strode through, occupied with a bundle of letters that were an inch from blowing away. Harry stuck his foot out to catch the door, and they navigated themselves in. They moved through a short, dark hallway, and then all three stopped in their tracks. Theyíd emerged into a huge interior space the size of the Great Hall. The space was predominantly occupied by a massive system of funnels, tubes, and boxes. Above it whirled dozens of owls, hooting and screeching, dropping letters and parcels into the funnels, which expanded to accept oversized parcels with various groans and bangs. Once theyíd recovered from the shock of the sorting contraption, they sidled to the side. ØCan you see box 320C?Æ said Harry, squinting through the Cloakís silvery fabric. ØThat way," Hermione whispered, indicating the left side of the mass of tubes. Each tube twisted and squiggled and eventually culminated in a box, on which were stamped large black numbers and letters. They shuffled along the wall, sank to the floor, and waited. More employees filed in over the next hour. On an upper level, a clear charmed surface spattered with owl droppings, exhausted-looking sanitation wizards cycled overhead, performing Vanishing Charms, befouled umbrellas held over their heads. On the ground floor, witches and wizards in their violet robes began wheeling carts of boxes, scrolls, letters, and parcels from place to place. One man was levitating a parcel that was some four times the size of his body, calling out importantly, ØOut of the way, please! Priority order!Æ The place was chaosîand that was before the patrons started coming in, clomping up the mess of wooden steps to get to their boxes, complaining to the workers when their mail was damaged. ØWell,Æ said Potter, Øat least we donít have to worry about keeping quiet.Æ The morning crept by as they waited for Titania Smethwyck. Draco eyed one witch checking 317B: the box on its outside was no larger than a shoebox, but when she opened it, Draco could see an entire room beyond, filled with neat stacks of letters. The witch levitated the letters out stack by stack into a large wooden crate, which a violet-robed wizard then wheeled away. Smethwyck arrived in mid-morning. She was a thin, mousy-haired witch who looked miserable. Her hands were bandaged, and Draco remembered the article that had come out about Hermione in Witch Weekly in their fourth yearîhow someone had mailed her Undiluted Bubotuber Pus. He supposed Rita Skeeter probably got about a dozen of those a day. He remembered, with a sudden squeezing feeling, how heíd laughed with Pansy, Crabbe, and Goyle as Hermione had fled the Great Hall in tears. They stood as Smethwyck levitated the contents of Skeeterís Receiverís Box into a carrying crate. It was small enough to fit in her arms, and she headed for the exit, wincing as she shifted her bandaged hands. Draco, Hermione, and Harry hurried after. Just before Smethwyck reached the exit corridor, Hermione stuck her wandtip out from beneath the Cloak and whispered, ØConfundo!Æ Smethwyck slowed to a halt, a dazed look coming over her face. Then she began to walk again, more slowly. They followed her out of the Office of Magical Postal Receipts. She wandered into a small, deserted side alley and began to look around, as if sheíd lost something. Hermione drank from a small vial of Polyjuice. A moment later, she had transformed into a small, dark-haired stranger. She slipped out from beneath the Cloak. ØTitania!Æ she exclaimed. Smethwyck leapt and turned. ØEr,Æ she said, looking at Hermione with alarm. ØItís me,Æ Hermione exclaimed. ØPenelope Clearwater. You donít remember? Oh, itís all right, it was so long ago.Æ She let out a high, airy laugh. Draco was impressed, as he had been in the Ministry, by her acting abilityîespecially when, as herself, she couldnít lie her way out of a Bertie Bottís carton. ØI thought I recognized you,Æ Hermione went on, Øand I just had to speak to you. I read that you were Rita Skeeterís assistant now. Thatís fantastic. Iíve just finished reading her latest book!Æ Draco nosed the tip of his wand out from the Invisibility Cloak and focused hard on the idea of Titania welcoming Hermione back to her home to catch up over tea. Confundo, he thought, and the spell rushed invisibly into Titania Smethwyckís shoulder. ØPenelope,Æ Titania said, nodding now. ØLovely è lovely to see you. Do you have a moment? We could go to mine for a cup of tea.Æ ØThat sounds lovely,Æ said Hermione, beaming. Titania took her arm, and with a crack, they were gone. Not even a minute later, Hermione reappeared before them with a slip of paper clutched in her hands and a set of Titaniaís robes under her arm. ØGot it,Æ she whispered, checking the entrance to the alley. ØWhere are you?Æ They ushered her back under the Cloak. She took their arms, and they Disapparated. They rematerialized on a small country lane. At the top of the lane, perched atop a hill, was a house that had been extended in several garish ways. Most obviously, a large new wing was tacked onto its back end; through its glass walls, Draco could see a swimming pool, and above the swimming pool, a chandelier the size of a small elephant. His lip curled. Honestlyînew money. Hermione took another flagon of Polyjuice from her beaded bag and slipped a hair from Titania Smethwyckís head into it. She sipped it with a grimace, and a moment later, she was stepping out onto the country lane, mousy-haired and icy pale, her hands painfully swollen. She conjured bandages onto them, then ducked behind a nearby bush and returned in Titaniaís robes. ØLet's go,Æ Hermione whispered. Draco and Harry, under the Cloak, followed her up to the house on the hill. She drew a deep breath and rapped three times on the door. The distant clicking of high heels. Soon there was the sound of many locks snapping open, and Rita Skeeter was drawing the door wide, her blonde hair curled in extravagant ringlets, one penciled eyebrow lifted high. ØYou couldnít have Flooed me, Titania, dear?Æ she said with a wide, false smile. ØIím about to go tîÆ Her eyes dropped, too late, to the wand in Hermioneís bandaged hands. Hermione had already flicked it. Then Rita Skeeterís arms were snapping to her sides, her legs flying together in the Full Body-Bind. Draco and Harry hurried inside as Hermione levitated Rita through the door. They sped through Skeeterís bizarrely composited home: the bones were that of a simple, sensible house, but the place had been plastered in too-large, too-bright art and obviously expensive statues that might have belonged in a French garden, but certainly not at the end of a simple pine banister. Soon they came out into the glass wing that Draco had seen from the lane. The swimming pool glittered, steam rising from a smaller, heat-charmed section at its end. Hermione let Rita down on a green velvet chaise at its side. ØHello, Rita,Æ Hermione said, conjuring a chair and sitting beside the chaise. ØI have a few questions for you.Æ She flicked her wand, and Ritaís face was released from the bounds of the jinx. Draco expected her to spit at Hermione, or even to scream, but the reporterís face was drawing, instead. She looked terrified. ØPlease,Æ she gasped. ØDonít hurt me. Is it the current project? Iíll stop writing it, I swear to Merlin, itís only in the earliest investigative stages è or if youíd like it to have a new angle, I can write to whatever heíd like. I can write it however you need! You all loved the Dumbledore book, didnít you?Æ Draco saw surprise on Hermioneís face, then comprehension. ØWhatís Skeeter talking about?Æ Potter breathed, so quietly that Draco could barely hear. ØShe thinks Hermioneís a Death Eater,Æ Draco whispered back. Hermione recovered quickly. ØOh, I donít intend to hurt you,Æ she said with a good try at smooth menace. ØOr even Miss Smethwyck. As long as you tell me exactly what I need to know.Æ ØOf course,Æ Rita said, her eyes bulging. ØAnything. I havenít got anything to hide. Iím a Slytherin. A half-blood. I swear.Æ ØIn that case è you use Veritaserum on your interviewees, I understand?Æ Hermione hesitated, then added, her voice silkier than ever, ØIt will be less painful, I think, than Legilimency, or è other methods.Æ Harry grimaced. ØSheís good at that, isnít she?Æ he whispered. ØI think she got it from Yaxley at the Ministry,Æ Draco muttered. ØYes, itís in the wine cellar,Æ Rita gabbled. ØFar left, top corner. Itís in a bottle that looks like mulled mead, í87.Æ ØGood.Æ Hermione Disapparated, and the moment that she was gone, Rita strained at the Body-Bind, her face stretching, her eyes rolling all around as if searching for help, sweat beading on her brow. Draco felt an odd sinking feeling, something between pity and disgust. Skeeter was terrified of what might come next. Draco supposed this must have been how heíd looked in the manor those evenings, when theyíd welcomed the Dark Lord over the thresholdîso ready to do anything to escape pain or death. There was no dignity in fear. Hermione reappeared with the mead bottle and a glass. ØAguamenti,Æ she said. Once the glass was full, she added three drops of the Veritaserum. The water glowed for a split instant, then returned to its usual colour. ØDrink up,Æ Hermione said, tilting the glass against Skeeterís lips. Skeeter drank, her eyes fixed on Hermione with both fright and loathing. But when she swallowed the water, all the tension drained from her expression, and her eyes went blank. ØAll right, Rita.Æ Hermione sat back down in the chair sheíd conjured. ØTell me when and how you interviewed Bathilda Bagshot.Æ ØBathilda is old and lonely,Æ said Skeeter in a flat, emotionless voice. ØShe lives in Godricís Hollow alone, and when I visited as a fellow writer, flattering her that I was a fan of her history books, she was delighted to ask me in for tea and biscuits. Without her knowledge I dosed her with Veritaserum over four day-long interview sessions this March. Iíd been working on the biography for a year and a half, but naturally my work accelerated in the wake of Dumbledoreís death.Æ Draco saw anger flash in Hermioneís eyes, but she kept her voice level. ØTell me everything Bathilda told you about Gellert Grindelwald.Æ If Skeeter found the question strange, or in any way noteworthy, none of those feelings showed on her face. ØShe was complimentary about her great-nephew,Æ she said, voice eerily blank. ØBathilda picked Grindelwald up from an international Apparition site the summer that he was eighteen. She described him as brilliant and surly, with a feeling of being misunderstood after his expulsion from Durmstrang. She said he was handsome, and charming when he wanted to be. Not even a day of Grindelwaldís stay had passed before Bathilda suggested he introduce himself to Albus down the street. She listed Dumbledoreís credentials and academic accomplishments, and Grindelwald seemed sceptical but impressed. Within two more days they were engrossed in each other. The entanglement was almost certainly romantic. I omitted this from the book because I felt it might soften readers to the young Dumbledoreís motives and distract them from his choice to engage in Grindelwaldís ideals.Æ Draco felt a moment of fleeting surprise, but Rita went on without pause. ØWhen Bathilda had conversations with her great-nephew, they were almost exclusively about Dumbledore. She described Grindelwald as more than smitten, closer to obsessed, even possessive of the young Albus. Grindelwald felt that all the great woes of Albusís life were due to the encroachment of Muggles into the Wizarding World. He fervently expressed to Bathilda that Dumbledore deserved better than a father locked in Azkaban and a dead mother. Bathilda viewed him as misguided, but a protective friend and loved one to Dumbledore.Æ ØTo your knowledge,Æ Hermione said, Ødid Grindelwald ever mention a mark that looked like a vertical line enclosed by a circle enclosed by a triangle?Æ ØNo,Æ said Rita. ØI noticed a similar mark in a letter that Dumbledore wrote to Grindelwald. I asked Bathilda what it meant. She had no knowledge of it.Æ Hermione looked disappointed, but went on. ØDid Bathilda know what Grindelwald and Dumbledore discussed together? Did she ever overhear conversations between them?Æ ØYes. Bathilda would occasionally pause outside Grindelwaldís room, where he and Dumbledore spent most of their time. She heard discussions of traveling to Wizarding outposts in Nairobi and Sydney. She heard discussions of the ideal Wizarding society, which Grindelwald believed should be an absolutist system, and which Dumbledore suggested should be ruled by multiple leaders. She heard enthusiastic discussions about valuable Wizarding objects and legends, such as Felix Felicis, Invisibility Cloaks, the Philosopherís Stone, the Deathly Hallows, Hands of Glory, and famous swords and wands.Æ At the word wands, Draco felt Potter go rigid beside him. ØThe Deathly Hallows?Æ Hermione said. ØWhat is that?Æ Skeeterís eyes were blank. ØA legend of some kind. Bathilda described it as Iíve just described it to you, in a list of conversational topics. I didnít research it. I omitted these topics from the book because I thought this kind of enthusiasm would endear readers to Dumbledore unnecessarily.Æ ØOf course you did.Æ Hermioneís lips pursed. ØYou said famous wands. Did Bathilda ever discuss any of these wands with Grindelwald?Æ ØYes,Æ said Skeeter. ØBathilda was rosy-eyed about both Dumbledore and her great-nephew. A historian, she was thrilled to find two young men with such an interest in the past. She had several afternoon tea sessions with Dumbledore and Grindelwald, each of which she relived to me in detail. Wandlore was discussed during the third of them. They pressed her about such wands as the Deathstick, the Wand of Destiny, and the Godhammer. They asked whether the wands had identifying features or commonalities. They went on to ask about the lives of the wizards who possessed the wands. Bathilda reminded them of the lack of historical fact surrounding these wands.Æ Draco glanced at Potter, who was staring ahead, his eyes wide. Draco looked back at Ritaís slack, emotionless face. Surely this wasnít coincidence? Surely this meant that theyíd actually guessed the reason for the Dark Lordís flight abroad? ØI omitted these discussions from the book,Æ Rita went on, ØbecauseîÆ Øîyou felt they might endear the reader to Dumbledore,Æ said Hermione, unable to hide the edge from her voice. ØYes. I get the picture. è Did you ever think about interviewing Grindelwald himself?Æ ØYes. He lives in Nurmengard Prison, which is located in international waters and co-managed by the magical governments of several European nations. I wrote to the Finnish government, usually the most lenient of the managing nations, to request visitation permission, but was denied.Æ Hermione sat in silence for a moment. Then she said, ØHave you ever interacted with the Senior Undersecretary to the Minister of Magic, Dolores Umbridge?Æ ØShe and the Minister of Magic sent me a complimentary card after the release of my latest book, which I keep on my mantel. Iíve never met nor spoken to her.Æ Hermione stood. ØThank you for your cooperation, Ms. Skeeter.Æ She pointed her wand at the reporterís face. Even in Ritaís stupor, Draco thought he saw fear in her eyes. But Hermione only said, ØObliviate.Æ Skeeterís eyes rolled into the back of her head, and she lay unconscious. ØCome on,Æ Hermione whispered, hurrying toward the entrance of the pool room. ØThis way.Æ Draco and Harry moved after her, and when Skeeter was out of sight, they ducked out from beneath the Cloak. ØThe wand,Æ Harry hissed. ØThe wand, Hermione!Æ ØYes, I know!Æ Hermione didnít stop walking. ØWhat are we doing?Æ Draco said. ØWhy arenít we Disapparating?Æ They passed through the brand-new kitchen, past the old, shabby pantry, past a massively oversized portrait of Ritaís face, smiling and posing for them, brandishing her Quick-Quotes Quill at the camera. ØUmbridge,Æ Hermione whispered back. ØShe mentioned she was a fan when we were at the Ministry, remember? If sheís sent fan mail to Rita, maybe it has a home address for return mail.Æ ØGood thinking,Æ said Potter. ØThere!Æ He pointed into a room with a frieze-topped fireplace and a long table that seemed to be made from solid gold. They hurried to the ten-foot mantel and picked through the many gushing cards proclaiming Ritaís wit, brilliance, and fearless journalistic integrity. ØItís here,Æ Draco hissed, spotting Umbridgeís handwriting. He snatched up the card, but his heart sank. ØThe return address is a Ministry owl box.Æ ØDamn,Æ Potter whispered. ØWell, it was a good thought. Maybe we could è Hermione?Æ Hermione had stopped several feet away, holding another card. When she looked up at them, she was beaming. ØThis is it. This is where weíll find her.Æ Draco strode to Hermione's side. The parchment in her hand was midnight blue. Glittering flakes of snow tumbled down through the background, passing through silver script that read: Dear Ms. Rita Skeeter, The Ministry of Magic is pleased to invite you as a media correspondent to: THE 1st ANNUAL MINISTRY OF MAGIC CHRISTMAS GALA FOR THE CELEBRATION OF MAGICAL UNITY Formal attire is required for entry. We regret that we are unable to accommodate plus-one invitations for media correspondents. The Christmas Gala will be held at 8 p.m. on the night of December the 23rd at Malfoy Manor. Chapter End Notes is this fic about to turn into a rom-com? MAYBE. lmao Iíve been excited to write this arc for weeks nowèèè sheer indulgence. tumblr | instagram Chapter15: LillimontLake Chapter Notes NOTE: this is a new chapter 15! the old one has been deleted, but all of its material has been redistributed between this new version and the next chapter, so if there was a scene or moment you liked, don't worry - it still lives on :) See the end of the chapter for more notes Malfoy Manor was a 17th-century estate with a single point of entry: the elephantine, wrought-iron front gate. The grounds were sealed by anti-intruder wards, which extended underground and created a closed circuit overhead. Now that the estate had passed to the Lestranges by magical law, Hermione knew that they wouldnít be able to sneak past the wardsînot even Draco. The front gate was their only option, and in the days after their visit to Rita Skeeter, they spent every waking hour trying to think of a way through it. The Daily Prophet had printed a long, splashy, very helpful feature about the Christmas Gala îtheyíd been nicking copies of the paper from a poorly disguised Wizarding home on the other side of Godricís Hollow. According to the article, the Ministry was contracting nearly two dozen companies for the gala, all pure-blood-owned, of course. Among them was a private security service called the Greengrass Guard. Draco had described Elinor Greengrass to them: strict, ruthless, and rigorous. There would be no hoping for laxness or luck at the front gates. ØBut,Æ Draco said one afternoon, as they sat on the library floor, poring over the diagram heíd recreated of the Manor, Øthe gates are fifteen feet wide, and we have the Invisibility Cloak.Æ ØYeah,Æ Harry said, Øbut Snape will have told the Death Eaters about the Cloak by now.Æ Hermione sighed. ØIf thatís the case, Iím sure theyíll place a Semi-Permeability Charm on the entrance, so that if you havenít been given express permission to enter, youíll be immobilised if you try it.Æ A brief silence. Hermioneís eyes lingered on Draco. His hand was still resting on the diagram, conflict in his eyes. She tried to imagine how she would have felt if her childhood home had been transformed into a haven for Death Eaters. No, thoughîthat wasnít right. The manor had always been used for the Death Eatersí purposes. He was the one whoíd changed. Hermione wanted to ask him about it, but in front of Harry, she knew Draco would deflect. She could imagine the easy drawl: Yeah, I really miss the place the Dark Lord promised to murder me. So many happy memories. Draco pushed the diagram away and leaned back against the sofa, crossing his long legs at the ankles. The neck of his dark green jumper pulled so that the weathered edge of his scar from the Ministry showed. He thumbed a strand of hair away from his forehead. Hermione forced her eyes away from him. Sheíd waited for this to stop happening after Halloween. But it had been ten days now, and she still kept noticing him: at breakfasts, when he tapped one finger thoughtfully on the back of his fork; or during these brainstorming sessions, when he wrote notes in a quick but fluid hand; or whenever he came within three feet of her, at which point she remembered the feeling of his cheek, flushed and soft under her fingertips, the shape of his cheekbone, the hard angle of his jawline. She knew heíd caught her at it, too. The noticing. Sometimes she even thought she saw him noticing her, too. She looked blankly down at the parchment. Without even blinking, as if an apparition were laid over her vision, she could see Dracoís expression in the mirror on Halloween, both cautious and vulnerable, like in that second she could have done anything and he would have just watched it happen. Even his features, naturally as sharp as edges honed to cut, had seemed to soften in uncertainty. Focus, she told herself. ØI really think,Æ she said too loudly, Øour only option is disguise and infiltration.Æ She laid another excerpt from the Prophet atop their notes. The clipping was a hiring advertisement from the galaís caterers, Lizzie Spizzworthís Finest Feasts and Magical Mixologists. It read: ØNow interviewing service workers for prestigious single event at high pay. Only exceptionally professional and experienced candidates will be considered. Confident use of Culinary Charms, Balancing Enchantments, and Anti-Spilling Spells a must.Æ ØIf weíre service staff,Æ Hermione said, tapping the ad, Øweíll be able to move freely among the guests.Æ ØIf weíre using disguises,Æ said Draco, Øwhy canít we just be the guests?Æ ØBecause then weíll have to deal with the guest list, too, obviously.Æ She gave him an unimpressed look. ØYou just donít want to serve people off those silver platters you grew up taking for granted.Æ ØGranger,Æ he said, placing his hand to his heart. ØThis mention of my wealth and status wounds me deeply.Æ She and Harry both snorted, and Draco looked pleased with himself. ØItís not that, anyway,Æ he went on. ØGuests will have more access to Umbridge. Theyíre looking for traitors even inside the Ministry, so sheís bound to have an Auror guard everywhere. A bathroom or powder room might be the only place theyíd leave her alone.Æ ØWhat,Æ Harry said, Øservice staff canít use the toilet now?Æ ØPlease, Potter. They wonít use the same bathrooms as the guests. Theyíll go to the East Wing.Æ Harry and Hermione looked at each other. Hermione pressed her lips together in a desperate attempt not to grin. ØAhh è the East Wing,Æ said Harry in his poshest voice. ØI do say,Æ Hermione said. ØIíll take supper on the veranda of the East Wing in future.Æ Draco raised one thin, pale eyebrow at them, looking baleful. ØAre you done?Æ ØI mean,Æ Harry said, Øprobably not.Æ Hermione let out one helpless giggle, then cleared her throat. ØAnywayîthe biggest question is still how weíll disguise ourselves. Transfigurationís far too vulnerable to counter-spells, and the Ministry are on the lookout for use of Polyjuice. Maybe some kind of glamour charm? I doubt those would be powerful enough, though.Æ Pensive silence settled. After a full minute, Harry stretched so that Hermione heard his back crack. ØWeíll have the sword by then,Æ he said. ØWhat if we just slash our way through the gate?Æ He spoke casually, but Hermione heard his anticipation. Five more days, and they would make contact with the Order's messenger at Lillimont Lakeîand receive the Sword of Gryffindor. Hermione knew Harryís excitement wasnít just about the sword, either. She knew that he expected Ron to return for this. Hermione wasnít so sure. It had been nearly a month since Ron had gone, with no word. As always, she felt an anxious pang to think about him. The fear that something might have happened to him was only worsened by the fact that they couldnít do anything if it had. The feeling wasnít only worry about his safety, either. Whenever she thought about Ron, who might still be in love with her, who had stormed out because he loved her, she thought of herself and Draco at Halloween, and she felt a powerful rush of guilt. Sheíd been more flirtatious that night than sheíd ever dared to be with anyone. And, drunk as Draco had been, heíd been flirtatious, too. That idle smile when heíd dared her to cut his hair, as Pansy had once doneî Do it, then, Granger. Hermione shuffled together some papers, her face warm. It was irrational to feel guilty. Sheíd told Ron she didnít love him anymore, that it couldnít work. She was allowed to think other people were good-looking. Besides, nothing had even happened with Draco. Haircut aside, it had been one drunken touch. One! Wellîtwo, she thought. The moment sheíd pressed her palm to his shoulder blade to push him into the hall. Three. When sheíd stumbled into the bathtub and heíd turned, caught her forearm instinctually, a bit roughly, and held on just a moment too long. The thing was, she knew precisely how much Ron would have loathed the idea of her attraction to Draco Malfoy. And it wasnít only Ron. When theyíd been at the Burrow in July, hadnít it been a constant, enthusiastic discussion topic whenever the Malfoys were out of sightîhow detestable they were, how cowardly and arrogant, how evil? ØI donít know how you let them sleep in your house, Hermione,Æ Ginny had said with outright disgust. ØIf it were up to me èÆ And sheíd shot a look of such intense loathing at Lucius Malfoy that Hermione had felt it secondhand, like the rush of heat on opening an oven door. But Draco wasnít his father. He was different, and not in negligible ways, but substantial ones. For the past week and a half, Hermione had felt as if she were arguing with a Greek chorus in her own mind, trying to explain to a host of imaginary people that she knew him, maybe more than anyone in the world did, and that in fact, she liked what she knew. She liked how he was protective of his parents and his friends, sensitive and private about their shared lives. She liked that he actually wasîas heíd once claimedîwitty, and that when he made his smart remarks, he glanced at her like he wanted to know if heíd amused her. She liked that he was responsive to interpersonal detail in a way that neither Harry nor Ron nor even Ginny had ever been; she had no other friends who cared about the fact that sometimes a silence wasnít just a silence, sometimes a glance wasnít just a glance, sometimes people felt a dozen things at once. And that responsiveness did make him a defensive person. But she also liked that she knew how to glide past those defenses. She liked that he ruminated on the past like it was a bittersweet taste. She liked that he was doing the right thing. She looked over at him now. Dracoís eyes had returned to the diagram heíd drawn. Malfoy Manor, heíd written at the top in extravagant, looping capitals. And heíd doodled a peacock on the hills of the grounds, which looked more like a turkey. God, it was almost sweet. She looked down at her notes and tried, for the hundredth time that week, to focus. They had no breakthroughs about the Christmas Gala in the lead-up to the 15th. What they did have were a lot of circular conversations about this wand that the Dark Lord was chasing. Draco was tiring of it. Meals mostly consisted of Potter trying to convince Hermione that the wand was important, to which Hermione always responded that even if some wand with powerful properties did exist, it clearly hadnít helped Grindelwald keep power, so why should they worry? The night that they were due to journey to Lillimont Lake, they stayed up late, waiting for 1:45 a.m. to Disapparate. They were upstairs in Hermioneís room, where Potter was steaming laundry with his wandtip. Draco was trying to lounge in a rather uncomfortable, creaky chair in the corner, and Hermione was lying on her bed, wearing that orange jumper sheíd worn on Halloween and dark Muggle jeans. Draco kept glancing at them, for some reason. The material was so strange, the way it ran and fit along her legs, hugging the curves of her calves. She and Potter were having yet another debate about the wand. After a while, Draco picked up their translation of Beedle the Bard from the end table and flipped to the triangular mark almost idly. Then, suddenly, a thought hit him. He stared down at ØThe Tale of the Three Brothers.Æ He didnít understand why he and Hermione hadnít thought of it before. He supposed theyíd been so focused on Grindelwaldís history, on the idea that this book was simply a convenient place to hide something that looked like a rune, that it had been out of focus in the background. ØPotter,Æ he said, Øhave you actually read the story? The one Dumbledore marked?Æ Potter broke off mid-argument, his wand pausing over a pair of half-dry trousers. ØWell, no,Æ he admitted, bumping his glasses up his nose with a knuckle, Øbut I stillîÆ ØThereís a wand in it. One with more power than others.Æ Potter dropped the trousers and his wand. For a moment he just gaped. Then he turned to give Hermione a stunned, disbelieving look. ØAnd you didnít think to mention that?Æ ØHarry, itís a fairy tale,Æ Hermione said impatiently, sitting up on the bed. ØItís about three brothers meeting Death. The wand in the story is supposedly Deathís own magic wand. Itís not a realîÆ But Potter was already crossing her bedroom, grabbing the translation from Draco, and reading it so quickly his eyes blurred. After a moment, he started to read it aloud, indistinctly at first, then more forcefully, with rising excitement. Øè so the oldest brother, who was a combative man, asked for a wand more powerful than any in existence: a wand that must always win duels for its owner, a wand worthy of a wizard who had conquered Death!Æ Potter looked up, his mouth ajar. ØThis is it,Æ he breathed. ØThis is what Voldemortís looking for. ThisîÆ He scanned the page again. ØThis Elder Wand, a wand that can win any duel. For the leader of the Death Eaters, a wand that can conquer Death!Æ ØHarry,Æ Hermione burst out in exasperation, ØGrindelwald couldnít have had a wand that could win any duel, when he famously lost the duel he had with Dumbledore!Æ ØBut the brother in the story loses the wand,Æ Draco said, suddenly very invested in the debate. ØHe gets stupid about it and brags about it in some inn, gets murdered when heís asleep. Someone could have stolen it from Grindelwald at some point. Probably by the time he dueled Dumbledore, he was back to using a normal wand.Æ Hermione narrowed her eyes to slits, the kind of scepticism that could cut. Draco found one corner of his mouth twitching. That expression had become extremely familiar. ØWell,Æ she said, ØI think this is all highly circumstantiîÆ ØHermione,Æ Harry interrupted, Øyouíre forgetting something.Æ ØOh, am I? And whatís that?Æ ØVoldemort has Ollivander. Ollivander knows more about wands than anyone in Britain, and he was being tortured and interrogated. He would have told Voldemort if this Elder Wand were a myth. Voldemort must have heard about the Elder Wand, and Ollivander backed up the idea, and thatís why he went off to look for it!Æ Hermione opened her mouth, but after a long silence, very slowly, she closed it again. For the first time in five days and as many debates, she looked uncertain. It was just like her, Draco thought with some amusement, to believe in something extraordinary only when it was substantiated by Britainís foremost expert. God, she really could be rigid, for someone whoíd been introduced to the concept of magic halfway through her life. Draco glanced back to the translation in Potterís hands, amusement fading into temptation. An unbeatable wand è he would have liked to see the look on the Death Eatersí faces if he could outduel them all at seventeen. He could show Crabbeís father what he was worth. And è well, if heíd had a wand like that last year, nothing would ever have happened to him. ØStill,Æ Hermione said finally, ØI donít know if it changes much.Æ Potter let out a disbelieving laugh. ØHow can you say that?Æ Hermione had regained her composure. ØLook, maybe you are right. Letís say this mark is the sign of this Elder Wand. Iíll concede that would explain why Dumbledore left it for us: because he wanted to warn us that we'd face more danger if Voldemort found the wand.Æ She drew a deep breath. ØBut Dumbledore didnít tell us to go after it. What he did tell us, very explicitly, was to track down and destroy the Horcruxes. Besides, Voldemort could defeat nearly anyone in a duel already; will this wand really make him so much more powerful? And,Æ she added, when Potter seemed about to respond, Ødonít forget, weíre going to the lake inîÆ She glanced at the clock. ØAn hour, and then weíll have the sword. Thatís a massive step.Æ Potter hesitated. ØCome on, Harry,Æ Hermione pleaded. ØThink of the locket. Weíre doing really well. We canít stop now.Æ After a long moment, Draco said, ØSheís right, Potter.Æ Potter glanced at Draco with surprise. ØI would have thought youíd want the wand.Æ ØOf course I would. But I also donít fancy bumping into the Dark Lord on his quest. Besides, say we somehow break into Nurmengard and get Grindelwald to tell us who stole the wand. What then? Itís been decades. The wand couldíve changed hands a dozen times.Æ ØExactly,Æ Hermione said, giving Draco a grateful look. ØIím not saying itís completely unimportant. Look: if we find and destroy all the Horcruxes, and Voldemort still hasnít realized itís Grindelwald heís chasing, thenîfine, letís try and find this wand before he does. But until then, we really, really need to focus.Æ Potter sighed, defeated. ØFine, you two. Iím going to make a cup of tea.Æ Draco listened to his footsteps retreat down the hall, hearing Potterís words echo oddly. You two, heíd said, looking from Draco to Hermione as if they were a unit. Draco realized heíd sort of enjoyed that. Well, why not? he thought with satisfaction, settling back in the horrible chair. He and Hermione had excellent judgment, working together. Theyíd outplayed Aurors and Death Eaters to break out of the Ministry of Magic, starting without so much as a wand between them. Theyíd outplayed the Dark Lordís soul in the diadem, so that sheíd learned the Fidelius Charm and escaped unscathed. Obviously they made an effective team. Draco glanced over as Hermione lay back on her bed with a sigh. Her hair fanned out around her head, and moonlight fell upon it in strips through the blinds. Something seemed to soften in his chest. They hadnít been up so late since Halloween. A light frown settled on Dracoís face. It had been two weeks now, and he was forgetting more and more of that night in a drunken wash. Yet he could still feel the smoothness of her arm, still see the cascades of her hair falling and shifting every which way, still feel the hesitancy of her fingertips, as if it had been five minutes ago. ØHermione,Æ he said. ØMm?Æ She turned on the bed, onto her side. He opened his mouth and realized he didnít actually have anything to say. Heíd said her name for no reason, so that sheíd look at him. For a moment he just looked back at her lying there, moon-brushed, tugging at the sleeve of that orange jumper, which was a bit lumpy. Her shoulders were rounded, surely from many yearsí excessive study, and those Muggle jeans were faded at the thigh. She looked soft to the touch like snow or cotton. Sitting there absolutely sober, broken springs digging into his back, Draco thought about how he could have kissed her on Halloween. He hadnít let himself imagine it that night. And in the weeks since, heíd always stopped himself before the idea could fully form, always hedging, always deferring. Now the image washed suddenly, unstoppably over him. He was back there in that bathroom and rising to his feet, half-blind with drink and sensitive as a nerve ending, and she was flushed and tentative, and he was sliding a hand around her waist, into the dip of her lower back, pulling her into him. Kissing her. ØWhat?Æ Hermione said, looking slightly self-conscious, a small smile on her lips. Draco felt a rush of mortified heat and looked away. ØNothing.Æ His heartbeat was shallow. He felt dazed, disbelieving. God, he thought. Godîhe was an idiot. No, the past two weeks hadnít just been the claustrophobia of headquarters. It seemed obvious now, stupidly obvious, the attention heíd been paying to her words and looks and motions, the way heíd tried and tried not to notice her. And of course heíd had to try, because Hermione Granger was the opposite of everything he was meant to want. Righteous, combative, messy, so earnest it was painful, always trying so hard and so visibly. The consummate Gryffindor. A Muggle-born. Yet even as this list unrolled in his head, questions were proliferating, unstoppable: would she have tasted like Firewhisky, like sugar? And what would she have done if heíd kissed her? Would she have frozen in shock or responded with heat, as vigorous in this as she was in everything else? The idea of her responding, of her pressing up into him, made his stomach drop. Dracoís mind went utterly blank. ØThanks for backing me up,Æ Hermione said. ØBefore.Æ ØWhat?Æ Draco looked at the pile of laundry where Potter had been standing. ØOh. Right. Yeah.Æ He tried to think of something to say. Anything. ØHaveîhave you always been like that?Æ he said, now thinking about her gesticulating at Potter as she made her points, her cheeks flushed, her voice impassioned. This wasnít helping to distract him at all. Now he was thinking about crossing the room, stopping at the end of her bed and leaning down, watching that brilliant colour rise into her cheeks. Kissing her here. Now, even. ØHave I always been like what?Æ she said. ØSo certain about everything,Æ he managed to say. ØOh.Æ She looked surprised. ØWell, Iím not certain about everything. You know that.Æ She glanced at him confidentially, and Draco felt a warm spot like a thumbprint somewhere inside his chest. Yesîhe did know. As headstrong and rigid as she was, he knew exactly where she faltered, where she could be vulnerable and unsure. ØBut Iíve always been opinionated, yes,Æ she went on, smiling. ØMy parents tell everyone about the time I researched the NHSís dentistry policy when I was eight and delivered a plan for comprehensive reform at dinner.Æ ØNHS,Æ he repeated. ØOh.Æ Her smile faltered for a split second. ØItís the Muggle National Health Service.Æ And again, more powerfully than ever, Draco felt that strange, traitorous curiosity. Real questions were forming now, not just vague feelings, building on top of each other. He wanted to ask whether this åNHSí was better or worse than St. Mungoís, which had been understaffed and mismanaged for years. Decades, the way his mother spoke about it. He wanted to ask how ØdentistryÆ was even supposed to work. At the same time, though, he was aware that his posture had grown stiff and awkward, and his fatherís voice was intruding into the back of his mind. Muggles, it echoed with distant disgust, and Draco found himself staring hard at the floor, face tense, something turning in his stomach. His parents è what would they have thought about him wanting to kiss Hermione? What would his friends have thought? The rest of his family? è but no, there was no need to wonder. Draco had heard everything theyíd called his aunt Andromeda. Filthy Muggle-lover, said Bellatrixís disgusted voice, thought our father would die of shameîand his motherís cool, casual tones, A blood traitor like that is no sister of mine. Draco could imagine Pansyís laugh, disbelieving and appalled, could picture repulsion on Crabbe and Goyleís faces, jeering disdain on Blaise and Theoís. He tried to clear his mind, but it wasnít working. He didnít want the curiosity, but he didnít want these echoes, either; he didnít want anything; he wanted to be alone; he wanted to get the voices out of his head and think unaffected for two bloody seconds. His brain felt pressurized. Think of something else, he told himself. Anything else. He closed his eyes. Her fingertips on his cheek, her curious face in the mirror. All else seemed to fade, his head going blissfully blank. Everything else was complicated; everything else was fear and judgment. But that night had been simple, private, elemental, the drink taking them outside choice or responsibility. ØIím going to see where Harry is with that tea,Æ Hermione said, something odd about her voice. Draco opened his eyes, but she was already crossing the room. The door was already closing, leaving him alone with his thoughts. He still hadnít really processed any of it by the time they Apparated to Lillimont Lake. The air smelled like frost, and the moon was a white sickle. Draco was relieved to be out in the cold, with some distraction. The lake was small, hardly larger than the cottageís back garden. ØHello?Æ Potter called, stepping onto its pebbled bank with wand in hand. White mist formed in front of his mouth as he breathed. It hadnít been nearly cold enough to see their breath back at headquarters. The Apparition must have taken them to the farthest reaches of Scotland. Draco stayed in the trees with Hermione, both their wands drawn, too. They didnít really expect a trap, not when this messenger had left them the will and bequests. Still, it was best to be safe. Draco was under the Invisibility Cloak, but his wand hand peeked through its folds. Hermione stood close to a pine tree, one hand spread across the rough bark. She hadnít spoken to him since her room. He wondered if something of his thoughts had shown on his face. He wondered with a kind of humiliation if sheíd guessed his attraction before heíd even known it himself. Motion ahead jerked him from his thoughts. His wand whipped up, ready. But it wasnít an attack. It wasnít even a person. Before them, materializing at the edge of the lake, where the still water had frozen into the pebbles, was a Patronus: a beautiful doe, dazzling silver, reflecting off the icy lake. For the first time in half an hour, thoughts of himself and Hermione dissipated completely. Draco was transfixed by the sight, and so, it seemed, was Potter. It was a long moment before Potter took a step toward the doe, wand hand faltering. The doe stepped away, pausing occasionally so Potter could follow, drawing him around the edge of the lake. Draco and Hermione trailed them, picking quietly through the trees. When Potter came up on a small bluff that overlooked a deeper part of the lake, the doe took several steps out onto the water. Potter hesitated, clearly unsure how he was supposed to follow, but the doe didnít go far. She halted, bowing her head until the tip of her nose brushed the thin, dark ice. There she remained for several long, still momentsîthen vanished. Potter stared into the water at the spot the doe had touched. He lowered his wand. ØWhatís he doing?Æ murmured Draco. Hermione bobbed her shoulders, silent. Draco, looking at the back of her head, felt an odd prickle of worry. Then Potter called, in a carrying whisper, ØThe sword! Theyíve left it here. IîÆ Potter spun suddenly. Draco and Hermione lifted their wands again. Heíd faced a section of trees twenty feet away from them. ØHello?Æ Potter said again. ØSomeone there? Remus? Tonks? è Kingsley? è Lumos!Æ The beam of light fell across the lake's edge and into the trees, but though Potter squinted, he didnít seem to see anything. Neither did they hear the crack of Disapparition. ØLook,Æ Potter said, with the air of throwing caution to the wind, Øif youíre in the Order, come out, would you?Æ The wind shifted through the trees, a forlorn rasp. Hermione tensed. A brainwave seemed to have struck her. ØHarry,Æ she whispered, hurrying out from the tree line. Potter gave her a concerned lookîthis wasnít part of the planîbut Draco wasnít worried. If this had been an enemy, they already would have attacked. Draco left the trees, still beneath the Cloak, and stopped beside Hermione and Potter on the edge of the bank. Draco looked down into the dark, icy water. There it was, glinting, unmistakable: the ruby-encrusted hilt of the sword of Godric Gryffindor. ØHarry,Æ Hermione said in a low voice, Ømaybe this is someone in the Ministry whoís secretly sympathetic to the Order. Maybe thatís how they got hold of Dumbledoreís bequests. And thatís why theyíd want to stay hiddenîso we canít give them away if we get caught.Æ Potterís brows drew together. ØCould be,Æ he muttered. After a moment's thought, he raised his voice again. ØWhoever you are, if you can get word to any of the Order of the Phoenixî Remus Lupin, Nymphadora Tonks, Kingsley Shacklebolt, or anyone else whoís on our sideî tell them to meet us here. On December the 1st, letís say. Thatíll give you some time to spread the message. 11 p.m., all right? AndèÆ Potter finally lowered his wand. ØThank you. For the sword, and è and everything.Æ There was a long, silent moment. Thenîfrom the treesî Crack. Whoever it had been, they were gone. His face set, Potter turned back to the icy lake. The sword lay on the kitchen table, perfect, unmarred by the centuries. Beside it lay the diadem. Harry was seated with his hands wrapped around a cup of tea, his hair still damp from the lake water, a blanket draped over his shoulders. Hermione was facing away from Draco, her head so full of thoughts it was nearly painful. She wished they had a Pensieve. ØWho do you think it was?Æ Harry said. ØCan you think of anyone with a doe Patronus?Æ Hermione shook her head. She had run through all the members of the Order, all the members of Dumbledoreís Army è not a doe in the lot. ØWhoever it is,Æ said Draco, sitting down too, Øwhy didnít you tell them to contact the Weasleys? Or Hagrid or McGonagall? Theyíre the only ones in the Order who are still out in the open.Æ ØThatís why I didnít mention them,Æ Harry said. ØIf this person gets caught, we donít want them to be able to give everyone away.Æ ØYeah, but why should they be able to find anyone whoís on the run any better than we could?Æ Harry threw up his hands, looking exasperated. ØI donít know, all right? Itís just a chance. Weíve got to take every chance we can.Æ Their attention returned to the objects on the table. For the first time since the conversation with Draco in her room, Hermioneís thoughts focused completely. Tense excitement filled the room wall to wall. At last, they had not just a Horcrux, but the means to destroy it. ØDo you reckon heíll be able to feel it?Æ Harry said quietly. ØWhen we get rid of it?Æ ØNone of the books on Horcruxes went into that much detail,Æ Hermione said, Øbut I donít think so. Itís been split away from him completely. Thereís no more connection between them. Thatís what gives the object its power.Æ She didnít want to look at the diadem. Even now, the way it glittered at her was enticing, reminding her of the bliss she had felt to wear itîto abandon who she really was in favor of the cold certainty of Lord Voldemort. That was the thing people never spoke about: how remorselessness could feel wonderful, an untethered sensation, like the power of flight. It was sometimes so blissful not to care. ØI want it gone.Æ The hardness of her own voice surprised her. ØI donít want it to be here anymore. I still è I still have dreams about it sometimes.Æ Harry looked troubled. ØHermione, thatís èÆ He broke off. Draco had stood, taken the sword from the table, and extended the ruby-encrusted hilt to her. There was something insistent about the way he was standing, turned fully toward her, as if heíd noticed she hadnít spoken to or even looked at him since their conversation. And as the sword glittered between them, she remembered him at her bedroom door after the diadem had tried to take her over, locking her in every night to keep her safe. She remembered him defending her to Ron, saying, You donít know what youíre talking aboutî because Draco did know. He knew exactly what the destruction of the diadem would mean to her. But she still couldnít make herself look at him, because lying there in her bedroom, in the horrible spiraling silence after sheíd reminded him she was Muggle-born, sheíd felt so small and alone. She curled her sweaty fingers around the shining hilt without a glance at Draco. Instead, she looked to Harry, who gave a wary nod. She stepped up to the table. The kitchen seemed to blur around her. She forced her eyes to fix on the diadem. Was it her imagination, or was the light moving strangely on its surface? Were the sapphires shining more and more brightly as she lifted the sword above her head, as if they could sense the danger? She heard an echo of that cold, sweet voice in the back of her mind, the one who had told her those terrible and yet seductive thingsîthat she was nothing, had always been nothing, but that if she only chose the right path, she could be rebuilt in an image that was worth everything è she was weak, she was flawed, she was undesirable and unworthy, and how laughably foolish that she had begun to care about Draco Malfoy, how pathetic to think her inferiority would simply fade away in his eyes, how presumptuous, she, born of Muggles, of worthless filthî No. The voice that rang through her mind was her own. No. In a world filled with hatred for what she was, there could be no room for doubt or self-loathing. Again and again, for the rest of her life, it would have to be her voice that declared her worth. She slammed the sword down upon the diadem and cleaved it in two. Hermioneís feet slipped on porcelain, and a warm, careful hand steadied her at the forearm. A low voice was laughing, mixing with her own, ringing against the tile. She was in the Prefectsí Bathroom, but it had changed. The massive bath sunk into the floor had been replaced with a claw-foot tub. The light was warm and dim. It was sixth year, after hours, and hadnít she had this dream a hundred times? Hadnít she spent half of sixth year dreaming of Ron happening into the Prefectsí Bathroom just as she wrapped a towel around herself? Her eyes were closed. The world was a sleepy abstract of black and red. She didnít know why she was standing in the bathtub. There must have been a reason. She was laughing again, whispering, ØQuiet è we have to be quiet, someoneís going to hear us.Æ His hand slid from her forearm up to her shoulder, where she was still holding the corners of the towel together. His thumb teased at the edge of the cloth. ØNo oneís going to hear,Æ he said. It wasnít Ronís voice. Hermione didnít open her eyes. Her body was humming like an electric wire. ØAre you sure?Æ she murmured as his fingertips brushed across her collarbones, taking her hair slowly behind her shoulders, the motions lazy but deliberate. ØIím sure,Æ Draco said. Then the floor fell out from beneath her, and she was falling, plummeting endlessly downward, accelerating toward a surface she knew would shatter her. She woke up with the echo of his voice in her ear. Hermioneís face burned as she sat up, breathing hard. Her hands were still aching with sensation, as if theyíd just unwound from his robes. Outside, the sky was pitch-black. They must only have returned from the lake four or five hours ago, but she felt wide awake. She threw the covers off herself and got out of bed, feeling a mix of panic and frustration and anger. Standing at her bedroom window, which overlooked the cottageís back garden, Hermione closed her eyes. The image appeared instantly: Dracoís face in the mirror. That night, mind loose with Firewhisky, sheíd looked at his uncertain expression and wondered what heíd been thinking. In that instant, sheíd let herself wonder if sheíd changed in his eyes as completely as heíd changed in hers. More than thatîsheíd hoped for it. In the back of her mind, she supposed sheíd been hoping for it ever since. After last night, she had her answer. Itís the Muggle National Health Service, sheíd said. Such a mundane sentence, and yet heíd looked as if sheíd uttered a disgusting swearword. That hard expression on his face had skewered into a soft, vulnerable part of her. God, the way heíd closed his eyes, as if he couldnít bear the sight of her anymore. Hermione slipped into the bathroom and hunched over the sink, staring at herself in the mirror. How had she let this happen? Hadnít she told herself for months not to invest anything in his values? He was a Malfoy, for Merlin's sake. Yes, they'd saved each other's necks multiple times now, but she knew what Draco had believed unquestioningly for seventeen years; sheíd known that there were certain rules she had to follow with him. If it was possible to trust someone with your life, but not your happiness, that was the way she trusted him. It had been safe to develop a rapport, even to appreciate the odd friendship theyíd developed, based on mutual confession. But the unsafe thing was to let herself care, really care, about his opinion of her. That was the line. On Halloween, sheíd staggered over it. She wondered if she was some kind of emotional masochist. After all, sheíd been most interested in Ron when Ron was interested in someone else. And Draco è heíd claimed they were friends, but she knew they didnít have the same concept of friendship. At Hogwarts, heíd been åfriendsí with Blaise Zabini, but Draco clearly thought of his friendship with Zabini as nothing more than convenient association. That was what this was, too. Convenient association. Forced association, really, since he had nowhere else to go. Maybe Draco found her an entertaining diversion, but in all likelihood he had nothing invested in her. Heíd already made it clear that he thought her attraction was funny. Maybe he thought it was a bit pathetic, too. Maybe he was embarrassed to think about it, and that was why, after two weeks, he hadnít mentioned what had happened on Halloween. Or maybe he was disgusted by it. She remembered him staring at the floor last night as if heíd had a toothache. Maybe, despite their supposed friendship, he still thought of her as è as a è Hermioneís throat tightened, and her eyes prickled. The feeling of panic grew, and it shrank her as it went, until she felt tiny. She squeezed the edges of the sink until discomfort shot up her fingers, until she couldnít feel what sheíd felt in the dream anymore. She gritted her teeth and whispered, ØNo.Æ She felt the diadem splitting under her hands. She heard the high-pitched, distant, vengeful scream of Lord Voldemort. She looked at her own furious expression in the mirror. She was finished with feeling scorned and lonely and inadequate. After last year, never again, never for any reason. She wasnít going to give Draco the ability to hurt her. No more dreams. No more noticing. No more speaking to him unless she had to, until she got this stupid, nave preoccupation under control. As if it even mattered when compared to the Horcruxes, and the war. No more. On the first day, Draco tried to convince himself he was imagining it. He told himself Hermione had slept poorly. Theyíd returned home from Lillimont Lake at 2:30, after all, and sheíd destroyed a Horcrux. He told himself that was why she looked sleepless, and why she wasnít really speaking to him. She wasnít speaking to Potter much, either; that was a good sign. Surely it was just distraction. He asked her after dinner, ØSomething up, Granger?Æ ØMm,Æ she said noncommittally, turning a page in So You Want to Be Someone Else: Largely Legal Methods of Wizarding Identity Reformation. But her gaze was fixed on the top left corner of the page, unmoving. He knew that when she was really too distracted by reading to speak, her eyes whipped back and forth across a page like a speed-painterís brush. Potter had lowered his own book. ØIs it the Horcrux?Æ he asked. ØNo,Æ Hermione said, definitely a bit curt now. Potter went back to his book. Potter wasnít the most observant person, Draco thought, but he did try. That was something he had in common with Goyle, although he never would have said that to Potter at risk of being hexed. Draco tried to refocus on Cleaning Jinxes: Befuddle Your Friends and De-foul Your Household! But halfway through a description of a jinx that would transfer dust onto a friendís face, he realized he was rereading the same six words over and over, and also that he was waiting for Potter to get up and go to bed. Potter almost always turned in before Draco and Hermioneîprobably to watch Ginny Weasleyís dot on the Marauderís Map, which heíd drunkenly mentioned doing at Halloween and, blessedly, seemed not to remember confessing. Once Potter was in bed, Draco could ask Hermione again what was going on, and maybe sheíd give a real answer. He knew there were some things sheíd told him that sheíd never told Potter, a fact that gave him a kind of satisfaction. Heíd hardly gotten through two more pages of Cleaning Jinxes, though, before Hermione stood. ØIím going to bed,Æ she said, already climbing the steps. Both Draco and Harry watched her go. ØYou reckon sheís all right?Æ Potter asked, when theyíd heard her bedroom door close upstairs. ØWhy are you asking me?Æ Draco said. ØYouíve been her best friend for seven years, havenít you?Æ Potter shrugged, but he was regarding Draco a bit too shrewdly for Dracoís taste. ØSheís probably just tired,Æ Draco muttered, going back to his book, suddenly in a bad mood. After the next day, he knew it wasnít a figment of his imagination. Hermione looked alert and well-rested. She treated Potter with perfect normalcy. She was focused and put-together, and she brought detailed pages of notes to the library for their brainstorming for the Christmas Gala. What she didnít do, ever, was look at him. And when she spoke to him, it was with the kind of shuttered coldness that he associated with Severus Snape. Soon she began to distance herself from him outright. When he cooked, she would no longer set the table and hum as she went. After dinner, rather than relaxing in the front room, she shut herself into her bedroom. Draco tried to seem coolly unaffected, to deteriorating results. The first day or two, he managed to act normal enough, but soon, during brainstorming, he found himself bringing up obscure facts that he remembered from Transfiguration, or History of Magic, or the Grindelwald books, waiting for her to give him an impressed look. She never did. Over meals, he mused aloud about arcane theoretical sub-branches of Arithmancy, hoping she wouldnít be able to resist the temptation to launch into an excited monologue; he spoke about foreign Wizarding cultures and traditions, knowing they interested her; he even brought up details about his own life, being far more open than he would have liked in front of Potter. Nothing, nothing, nothing. Five days in, he said something to her about the weather, for Merlinís sake, and Potter looked at him like he was out of his mind. Hermione ignored this completely, finished her orange juice, and walked into the other room. It might not have been so bad if his own mind hadnít been in disarray. Since the night of the lake, he couldnít seem to get his imagination under control. Thoughts of Halloween transformed until suddenly he was picturing kissing her against the library bookshelves, on the sofa in the front room, in the kitchen beside the stove. When she bound her hair up in a massive, frizzy bunch, he looked at the wispy curls at the back of her neck and imagined running his fingers over the skin there. Her constant lip-biting had become a kind of torture for him, the soft wetness of her mouth. He imagined kissing her until she came openîuntil she told him what, in the name of Merlinís last rotting tooth, she was doing. But over the course of the week, a seed of suspicion sprouted at the back of his mind. Draco had gone through a million possibilities, and at this point, he didnít know what else it could be. The fact was, sheíd been strange since the night theyíd gone to the lake. Sheíd been strange since the conversation when Draco had first allowed himself to imagine kissing her. He couldnít even remember what theyíd been discussing, only the rush of his thoughts and feelings, the conflicted shock of realizing the attraction heíd felt since Halloween. She must have seen it in his expression. If not through Legilimency, then through intuition, sheíd realized that heíd wanted her. And this was her reaction. Absolute repulsion. Draco didnít understand. Heíd thought she was attracted to him. Heíd started to enjoy that particular tidbit of information. Sheíd seemed pleased to be friends, too. Who felt this way about a friend? Had he somehow misinterpreted every single signal heíd received from her? But a hypothesis had formed for that mystery, too. She was Hermione Granger. Member of the Order, Gryffindor Prefect, founder of an elf rights society and a defense group to resist the Dark Lord. Right from the very beginning, this was who sheíd always been. And he was still the person whoíd tried to murder Albus Dumbledore. He still had the Dark Mark on his forearm. Sometimes, last year, heíd used to look down at the Mark in the shower and wish he could just tear it off himself. Heíd started doing that again, standing in the tub downstairs, in the very spot sheíd stood on Halloween. Maybe this was the extent of Hermioneís willingness to forgive. Maybe, despite all appearances, she still felt a deep-seated repulsion toward him, and the idea of involvement between them had crossed a serious boundary, one so offensive to her that she had to demonstrate how she really felt. Maybe, he thought bitterly one afternoon in the library, that was why sheíd looked so conflicted on Halloween. Because when she was sober, the idea of touching him was morally repellent to her. Draco told himself the theory was wrong. It had to be wrong. When had he ever been this off the mark on reading anyone? And this was Hermione, the worst liar heíd ever met. He knew her. Heíd thought heíd known her. ØAre you paying attention?Æ Hermione said. Draco blinked. ØYes,Æ he lied. ØGood,Æ she said shortly. ØBecause we donít have time to waste, you know.Æ Hermione turned away with unnecessary affront. Itíll be all right.Æ She softened as she looked at Potter. Draco felt a hot stab of irritation and resisted the urge to flick Potterís glasses off the bridge of his nose. Clear your mind, whispered the voice in his mind that always returned at moments of high emotion. And he tried, but spikes of annoyance kept breaking through, shattering his focus. ØThereís always the Imperius Curse.Æ ØHarry,Æ Hermione said, looking shocked. ØWeíre not using it to hurt anyone, are we?Æ Potter said. ØWe just need someone on the inside. If we used it, we might not need to get into the manor at all.Æ Draco reclined on his pile of cushions and leaned his elbow against the sofa. Who wouldíve guessed that Potter had it in him? ØNot an option,Æ he said idly. ØWhy not?Æ ØThe Unforgivables arenít Wingardium Leviosa, Potter. The Imperius gets harder the farther you are from the target, and the longer you control them, and the more naturally you want them to act. Death Eaters practice for years to get the kind of control youíre talking about.Æ Draco picked at one of the sofa cushionsí tassels. ØWeíre trying to break into a Ministry event, remember? Any idiot in the Department of Magical Law Enforcement will know what an amateur Imperius looks like. We wouldnít make it halfway through setup and decoration.Æ ØAll right, all right,Æ Potter said grudgingly. ØI get the idea.Æ ØWe need something failsafe,Æ Hermione muttered, flipping through a thick, cracked, apparently untitled tome. ØA potion would be ideal, but Iíve cross-checked hundreds of potions against the capabilities of Probity Probes, and it looks like our only options would be potions with really rare or expensive ingredients. Our moneyís running low as it is.Æ ØHow low?Æ Draco said. ØWell,Æ Hermione said to Potter, as if heíd been the one to ask, Øthe Galleons Mr. Weasley withdrew for us at the Burrow are three-quarters gone on food. Hermione Granger, the most relentless person in his life, possibly in the world, who demanded more from everybodyîwhen it came to this, she expected nothing else from him. Then, in a moment like a lightning strike, Draco remembered. He recalled the exact words sheíd said the night theyíd gone to the lake, the last piece of their conversation. Itís the Muggle National Health Service. And he hadnít said anything in reply. Heíd just sat there, trying to suppress his parentsí voices, trying to suppress his curiosity, trying not to think about kissing her, straining and straining at himselfîbut she'd had no idea what had been happening inside him. His father had said to Mr. Weasley with cool revulsion, The company you keep, Weasley. è And Iíd thought your family could sink no lower. He hadnít even bothered to insult Hermioneís family directly. This was why sheíd shut him out. This was what she needed to know. And he needed to know, too. He couldnít go on like this. What did he believe? Heat built in Dracoís head like a fever. Heíd been raised to believe in the elegance and refinement of pure blood, its innate superiority. And yet, in practice, what did that boil down to? He wasnít better at magic than Hermione. He knew half-bloods who were richer and more influential than his family. What did any of it actually mean? With a pulse of anger and incomprehension, he thought about how, that day in Flourish and Blotts, he could have interrupted. It would even have been easy. Why had his father needed to say anything about it at all? He thought, if he could live that moment again right now, he would have steered his father out the door. ØYou è you will?Æ She was looking at him as she hadnít all week. Her eyes were the warm, sweet brown of honeyed tea, and her parted lips looked soft. Draco had the sensation of drawing breath after three minutes underwater. In that moment he felt like the library was slipping around him, like he was losing his grip on something. Heíd agreed to go into Muggle London. He tried to relax back against the sofa. ØYeah, well, Iíve just had it on the Chosen Oneís authority that nobodyís going to cut my nose off, so, I suppose.Æ He wanted her to smile, but she didnít even seem happy. She looked disturbed, almost panicked. Heat was moving through Dracoís whole body now, washing over the back of his neck, the undersides of his arms. Iíll go, he thought again, trying to make the thought feel stable, normal. Iíll go, Iíll go. He was going to somewhere new. And to go somewhere new, he had to leave something behind. Heíd left so much behind already, he would have thought the feeling would be familiar, but every time it was the same. Every time it was tying on a blindfold. Every time it was walking into the dark. Chapter End Notes gonna be totally honest here, i love relationships and deep feelings and transformative character arcs and everything, but coming up with ridiculous book titles, company names, and random Wizarding details is my absolute favorite part of fanfiction. iím gonna write a whole fic about Lizzie Spizzworth. tumblr | instagram Chapter 16:The Other London Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Hermione woke up tired the next morning. Sheíd had the dream again last night, headier and more intense. This time, though, her eyes had been open. She hadnít thought, at any point, that it was anyone but him. She lay in bed a while, looking at the ceiling, thinking. The week had been, frankly, awful. Harry had noticed her change in attitude, of course. After three days of her silent treatment, when sheíd been out in the garden for some air, her hands cupped around the bluebell flames she loved to conjure, heíd come to her and said, ØLook, Hermioneîdid something happen with Malfoy?Æ He already sounded suspicious, as if he were ready to fight Draco. Hermione had felt a rush of fondness for Harry then. She could have hugged him, if it wouldnít have set his hair alight. ØNot really,Æ sheíd said. ØI suppose I just è I got my hopes up, thatís all. That heíd really changed.Æ It was a relief to be herself, not the persona sheíd been putting on in the cottage. Harry warmed his hands over the bluebell flame. After a moment, he said, ØHe seems like he has, though, doesnít he?Æ She frowned. ØIn some ways. Hunting the Horcruxes, being friendly with both of us. è But have you ever actually heard him contradict the things he always used to say? All that pure-blood obsession?Æ ØWell, no,Æ Harry admitted. ØI suppose he just doesnít talk about it anymore. Itís still different from how he used to act, though.Æ ØYes, but èÆ She hadnít been able to finish the sentence. But it doesnít feel like enough. Saying it would have shown her handîwould have shown how much she cared, unwillingly, what Draco thought. Still, she thought Harry understood anyway, in that unspoken way he often seemed to. Sheíd told herself over and over that distance was the only answer. Soon it would be easier to hold Draco at bay, and then sheíd stop thinking about his opinions of Muggles with this feeling like teetering on the edge of a cliff. Sheíd told herself this was the only way to keep herself safe. But part of her kept doubting. All week, Draco had seemed unhappy. It wasnít an obvious thing. He hadnít made any mention of her behaviour, though she hadnít really expected him to, proud as he was. Yet there had been a shift in his manner. Sheíd seen hints of confusion, then dissatisfaction. And through it all, heíd kept trying to talk to her, always using the same casual tone, as if everything were still normal between them, as if this time he could break through to her, or this time, or the next. Heíd brought up one of her favourite sub-branches of Arithmancy on Monday, and the effort it had taken not to enthuse about it had been excruciating. Clearly he did feel their relationship was something more than convenience. Thatís not the real point, she told herself, angry about the small, hopeful bubble that had swelled up in her. The point was his beliefs. The point was that she refused, point blank, to argue her own worth. But then, yesterday, Draco had said he would come into Muggle London, and her thoughts had been thrown into disarray. She didnít know what to do with the information. Heíd agreed to go into a sea of Muggles without complaint, without even a sneer or a look of disdain. All this, when scarcely a week ago heíd heard one simple reference to her upbringing and shorted out like a circuit? It doesnít necessarily mean anything, she reminded herself. He hadnít said anything about his thoughts on Mugglesîjust that he would come. And if he wanted to see Malfoy Manor again, then he had to come, didnít he? Holding his nose out of necessity wasnít the same thing as change. Still, she hadnít expected even for a moment that he would do it, let alone without protest. She found it especially hard to be frosty to him over breakfast that day. Maybe it was the new disorganisation of her feelings. Maybe it was because Harry slept late and it was only the two of them, cutlery clinking against their plates, Draco looking tired and wan and a little bit sad. Whatever the reason, she noticed the delicate indigo colour of the circles beneath his eyes, the arches of his brows, the Y shapes in the cartilage of his ears, the way his lower lip looked bitten. His hair was rumpled in a way that made her imagine its texture. ØDidnít sleep well?Æ he asked, his voice quiet and scratchy. ØNo,Æ she said, quiet too. ØNor you?Æ Draco hesitated, clearly surprised at her engagement. ØNo. I havenít slept well all week.Æ Hermione didnít answer. Something quavered in her at the tentative reference to her coldness. It felt so good to speak to him like a normal person again, even for just three words. She missed the ease theyíd had. She missed him. As the silence stretched, the feeling intensified, running through her. She missed him. She missed his little stories about his life and his friends, his reluctant, closed-off nostalgia. She missed the way he said Granger with light, laughing irony, and she missed teasing him back, and seeing him smile. She missed the way heíd look away when he mentioned the previous year, the feeling that she was the only one he trusted with the truth. She missed him reassuring her in casual, nearly invisible ways when she was so deep into her worry about the war that she couldnít see straight. She missed looking at him. The acute point of his chin, the quicksilver eyes. The lips that her dreams told her would be slightly rough, like linen. The occasional pink in his cheeks, when he let on too much and wanted to backtrack. For a wild moment she considered just demanding what he meant by it, by agreeing to come into Muggle London. But if he gave the answer she didnít want to hear? If he revealed disgust, even distaste? The idea still felt like a stiletto knife sliding into a nervous centre. Distance, she thought, drawing a deep, slow breath. He didnít try to speak to her for the rest of breakfast, which made her feel a pang she hadnít anticipated. Itís good if heís given up, she thought at once, now properly angry with herself. Heíd gotten the message. She could avoid without wounding, now. Still, when she left the table, she thought she could feel him watching. That afternoon, with the crux of the infiltration plan in place, they began to work out the specifics. Hoping to target Umbridge in the bathroom, they decided Hermione should be the one who attended as a guest, while Harry and Draco would apply for Lizzie Spizzworthís. Their first thought for Hermione was a false name on the guest listîbut altering the list would mean breaking into the Ministryís Office of Domestic Magical Affairs. Faking an invitation would be difficult, too. When theyíd been at Rita Skeeterís house, Hermione had tried to duplicate or Transfigure the invitation, but it had been charmed to resist both, so theyíd left it behind, not wanting to arouse suspicion by stealing it. It came to them in the evening, as they sat before a merrily crackling fire in the front room. Rita Skeeterís invitation had read, We regret that we are unable to accommodate plus-one invitations for media correspondents. ØFor media correspondents,Æ Harry said triumphantly. ØNot for members of the Ministry.Æ ØWhat are you saying?Æ Hermione said. ØYou want me toîto Confund a Ministry employee into asking me to the gala?Æ ØNo,Æ Harry said. ØWell, good, because that would beîÆ ØIíll Confund him. Or Draco will. ØIíd rather drink Bubotuber Pus! How can you even suggestîÆ ØBut he knows what your voice sounds like, so it wouldnít work.Æ Harry hesitated. ØIt did make me think, though. I mean, he was so keen on feeling like we were getting somewhere with the Horcruxes. è I thought heíd want to know.Æ ØComing back means apologizing,Æ Draco said with cool distaste. We can ask Mr. Weasley at the gala, is the point.Æ Draco was looking at Hermione, a frown sharpening his angular features. He didnít seem to have heard a word Harry had said. ØWe need someone without combat training. Someone who wonít realise that theyíve been Confunded.Æ ØMarcus Flint, then. Department of Magical Games and Sports.Æ Hermione couldnít help but make a face. ØWhat?Æ Harry said. ØOh, nothing,Æ she said with distaste. ØBut è well, heís older. Wasnít he a seventh-year when we were third-years? Heíll be twenty-two now, or something. And heís mean-looking,Æ she added. Draco was looking down at his notes, but Hermione thought she saw his lip twitch. ØHermione,Æ Harry said, his patience clearly wearing thin, Øyou wonít actually be getting into a relationship with Marcus Flint.Æ ØYes, well, Iíll have to see him more than once, wonít I?Æ she shot back. ØOnce the initial Confundus Charm wears off, he canít just decide he felt impulsive that day and cancel by owl. He has to beÆîshe was blushing nowîØactually interested. And Iím sorry, Harry, but the only memory I have of Marcus Flint is when he tried to scare you off your broom by pretending to be a Dementor.Æ ØAhh,Æ Harry said wistfully, aiming a grin at Draco. ØThat was a great match, wasnít it? I seem to recall blasting a Patronus at the four of you.Æ Draco hadnít had many retorts the past few days, but he raised one eyebrow and said, ØYour finest moment, Potter. Howíd it feel to peak at thirteen?Æ ØFantastic,Æ Harry said. Draco settled back on his cushions, running a hand through his hair. ØThereíd have to be a reason why Flint didnít already know a pure-blood girl our age.Æ ØEasy,Æ Harry said. ØHermione, you can say youíve just graduated from an international school, and youíve just moved here, andèÆ Øè and it would be just so helpful,Æ Hermione went on in a low, flattering voice, Øif someone would show me around Wizarding London and introduce me to the right people.Æ ØYeah,Æ Draco said slowly. ØYeah, it could work. è He has a type, though. And Quidditch is basically his entire personality. And his familyís really traditional. The last thing they needed was Marcus Flintís jilted lover poking her nose into who exactly this new girl was. She tried not to think of the jokes Draco would have made about the idea of Marcus Flintís jilted lover. ØA day and a half is plenty of time,Æ Harry went on, gaining steam. ØWeíll go into Muggle London tomorrow morning to make an appointment for our disguisesîI reckon we wonít just be able to stroll in somewhere on the spot.
