Weíll get the disguises done Friday morning and youíll meet him that evening.Æ There was a silence. Hermione realized she was waiting to see how Draco would react. ØYeah,Æ Draco said. ØAll right.Æ He sounded nearly careless. Hermione hated the candle of feeling that still burned in her chest, small and bright, no matter how she tried to blow it out. On Thursday morning, Draco awoke feeling nauseated. Heíd lain awake for hours last night, nerves flaring, running through reams of memories of his parents. Not just all the things theyíd said about Muggles and Muggle-borns, either, but the way theyíd looked at him when he was younger, the constant glow of pride in their faces. Heíd remembered his mother letting out one broken sob when sheíd clasped him in her arms at Grimmauld Place, the night theyíd faked their deaths. Heíd never heard anything like that from her. He expected no one besides his father ever had. By the time the embrace had broken, she was already collected again, her thin pale face rigid with suppressed emotion. Heíd remembered his father coming out of the Floo from Azkaban. His mother had seemed to melt into his fatherís arms, and then, when Lucius looked at Draco, thin and grey-faced from the effect of the Dementors, heíd extended a shaking hand and drawn Draco into the embrace. For the first time in a year, huddled against his parents, Draco had felt safe. Heíd felt like a child again. He never would have admitted that that was something heíd missed. His parents had given him everything heíd ever wanted. Theyíd raised him with a degree of love, attention, and affection that he knew was unparalleled even within his group of friends, who were generally prized and pampered at home. Theyíd never asked him for a single thing îexcept, maybe, that he become the man theyíd raised him to be. Draco had never in his life taken a step that felt so opposed to their wishes. Defecting from the Dark Lord, even hunting the Horcruxes, was one thingîheíd done all that to keep the family safe, to take steps toward restoring their lives. Going to Muggle London was different. He tried to tell himself he was acting out of necessity, but it just wasnít true. He couldnít lie to himself so easily anymore. That curious part of him kept hissing questions in the back of his mind, kept demanding to know more, to see this world where Hermione had grown up, that his family and friends hated so much. Maybe, he thought, he would arrive there and easily see why they acted with disgust. That was a possibility. Maybe he would finally see the evidence for what his family had always said about Muggles being dirty, oafish, like sheep, a disgrace for wizards to associate with. But if not? If thereís no reason? whispered the voice in his mind. For once he didnít know whose voice it was. Maybe his own. And he had no answer for its questions. When he came out of his room, Potter was already in the kitchen, cooking breakfast. ØHi,Æ Potter said. ØMorning,Æ Draco said, squinting through the weak rays of sun. ØWhy are you awake? I thought we werenít leaving for an hour and a half.Æ ØWeíre not. I wanted to talk to you.Æ Draco hesitated, then sat down at the kitchen table, much less groggy all of a sudden. Was he going to find out what Potter had meant by that apology comment yesterday? ØTalk, then,Æ he said. Potter tapped his wand on the stove, and the flames beneath the saucepan shrank to small golden teeth. He turned, his face serious. ØEr.Æ Potter rubbed the back of his neck. ØFirst of all, youíve been a big help the past few months. So, thereís that, and, yeah, thanks.Æ ØYouíre welcome,Æ said Draco, suspicious. Somehow he doubted this was a conversation revolving around gratitude. Potter hesitated. He seemed to be considering how best to phrase whatever it was he meant to say. ØLook,Æ he said finally, ØI canít be sure whatís going on in your head. I donít know if youíve gone back on all that stuff you used to say in school, or whether youíre just quiet about it now, orîÆ Dracoís heart had begun to beat very hard. He wasnít prepared to talk about this with anyone, let alone with Potter. Defensive words came out of him before he could think. ØGood thing I donít owe you an explîÆ ØThatís not the point,Æ Potter cut in. Confused, Draco broke off. ØI donít need to know what you think,Æ Potter went on. ØHonestly, at this point I donít much care. Youíre helping us, so whatever the reason is, fine.Æ He crossed his arms. ØBut I do care about Hermione. And when we go into London today, and tomorrow, and whenever else we need to, just èÆ His eyes bored into Draco, as shrewd as they had been the day Hermione had first started giving him the silent treatment. Am I wrong?Æ Draco hesitated, feeling wary. So all this had been building up in Potterís mind this week. That was unexpected. He moved his head in the tiniest shake. ØRight, then,Æ Potter said. ØIn that case, Iím asking you to justîact normally, would you? Talk about the plan, and Flint, and whatever else. Ask questions. Donít è donít jeer at anything.Æ ØPotter,Æ said Draco stiffly, ØI know how to be polite.Æ ØOh, so you just chose not to be for six years, then.Æ ØYes. Obviously.Æ Potterís mouth twitched in amusement. ØItís not like sheíll answer any questions I ask,Æ Draco muttered. ØShe wonít say anything to me, if you havenít noticed.Æ Potter sighed. ØYeah, well èÆ But at that moment, footsteps sounded on the stairs. Potter lowered his voice. ØIíll answer the questions,Æ he said quietly. ØItíll be fine. Just donít overthink it.Æ He turned back to the stove as Hermione entered the room, poring over a map of London. Draco stood, motions stiff, and began to set the table, though he was still mulling over what Potter had said. I donít need to know what you think è I donít much care. It was strange how much relief those words brought him. With his thoughts about blood status nearly nonstop these days, heíd started to feel like there were no safe thoughts to have. If he questioned his parentsí ideals, he was a blood traitor who would be derided by everyone from his old life. If he tried to cling to those principles, that dubious voice in his head awoke and started hissing at him, and he thought of Hermione hiding her hurt expression. Moreover, the thoughts felt so loud, as if all usual rules of privacy had shattered and Hermione or Potter could see the turmoil swarming all over his face. Heíd needed Potterís reminder. All anyone knew was what he chose to show. He could show normalcy today, even if he didnít feel it. Still, it was with slow panic that Draco prepared for their departure. He still didnít feel ready when they slipped their wands into their pockets, Disillusioned themselves, and Disapparated. Crack. They were standing in a puddle in a small, dim alley, a crack of blue sky high overhead. ØAll clear,Æ Potter said. Dracoís palms were clammy at his sides. They lifted their Disillusionments, walked down the alley, and emerged into a broad, bright street. Draco couldnít remember when, if ever, heíd seen Muggles in these kinds of numbers. Whenever he and his parents had needed to come into London before, theyíd used Floo to reach their Wizarding destinations. Every year, coming to Diagon Alley, they'd taken him by Side-Along Apparition directly into the Leaky Cauldron, and, like all his friends' parents, they'd always paid the substantial fee to Apparate directly into Platform 9., too. The most heíd seen of Muggle London had been the view through the windows at Grimmauld Place. Here, in the centre of some busy neighbourhood, the flood of information overwhelmed him. Everywhere were Muggle clothes: jeans in every shade of blue, oddly shaped hats that fit tightly over heads, clunky shoes striped with neon, coats made of silvery fabric that glistened with recent drizzle. Nearby, a pair of giggling Muggle children were playing with toys of a hard, shiny material, which buzzed and flashed. A man bustled out of a nearby shop wearing what looked like a pair of black earmuffs, which were attached by a long cord to a glimmering grey oval in his hand; he was humming to himself. ØAll right,Æ said Potter, obviously unperturbed by any of this. ØWhich way do you reckon, Hermione?Æ ØWell,Æ she said, Øin the absence of a phone book, I think we should ask for recommendations from the first salon we find.Æ ØSounds good,Æ Potter said. ØLead on.Æ Draco trailed after them up the busy street. Rolling down the road were car after car after car. Draco had seen cars, of course; the Ministry had a fleet of them, and occasionally Ministry officials would glide up to the manor gates in one of those black machines. But his parents had always considered it distasteful to use them, and of course theyíd never needed to resort to the Knight Bus for transportation, so it was all very alien. He glanced through the window of a boxy, bright yellow car and saw a woman gripping a thin leather-bound wheel with one hand, twiddling a series of dials with her other. The next vehicle was as different to the first as a flying carpet was to a broomîa clunky, roaring thing with a fifteen-foot-long metal box affixed to its end, rolling along on an additional dozen tyres. ØThere,Æ Hermione said, pointing across the street. ØCome on.Æ And she stepped onto the road's shiny black materialîtoward a moving car. ØWait!Æ Draco blurted, grabbing her by the elbow. She looked at him with bewilderment. ØWhat?Æ ØItîit wasîÆ He pointed at the blue car, which was rolling slowly past now, the driver also giving Draco a funny look. ØItís still going.Æ ØYes, at about five miles an hour.Æ Draco opened his mouth, then closed it again. ØTheyíll stop to let you pass if trafficís this slow,Æ explained Potter, looking highly amused. ØTheyíre not just going to roll into you. Look.Æ And he jogged onto the black road, lifting a hand at the next driver, who pulled to a halt. Potter crossed safely. ØAh.Æ Draco realized his fingers were still curled around Hermioneís arm and let go at once. ØRight.Æ Hermione made a funny noise that quickly turned into a cough. Dracoís cheeks felt hot as he followed her across the street, and the three of them pushed into a hair salon. Draco felt even more out of place inside. All around the salon were silver machines, some like hoops, others like bowls, several of which were lowered over the heads of seated Muggle women wearing black aprons. ØGood morning,Æ said Hermione brightly to the woman at the front counter. The woman looked up from the magazine she was reading and gave Hermioneís bushy hair a long, startled look. As her eyes slid onto Potterís hair, her expression turned to something like dread, but finally she looked to Draco, and her expression eased. It was to Draco she addressed the question: ØHere for an appointment?Æ she said. ØOr è your friends are?Æ Dracoís whole body tensed. He was acutely aware that this was the first time heíd ever spoken with a Muggle. The closest heíd come had been that man at the Quidditch World Cup, and his parents had done all the talking then; Draco had watched from behind with a kind of morbid interest. He glanced at Hermione and Potter, who were both watching, faces unreadable. He looked back to the Muggle woman. She had shiny dark hair that reminded him of Pansyís. She had a metal stud in her eyebrowîsome kind of Muggle religious symbol, maybe? ØNo,Æ Draco said. ØWeíre looking for a studio specialising in facial prothletics.Æ She blinked. ØFacial what?Æ Hermione cleared her throat. ØProsthetics. Weíre film studentsîwe wondered if you had any recommendations.Æ The womanís look of disapproval faded, and she rubbed thoughtfully at her chin. ØWhat are you trying to look like?Æ She glanced back to Draco. ØVampires?Æ Draco blinked at her, stung. ØNo,Æ Potter said. ØWe donít need monster makeup, or anything. Weíre trying to look like different people for our film.Æ ØGive Leo Cliftonís a go. Itís over in Kensington, right near the Notting Hill station.Æ The second they were outside again, Draco said, ØWhat did she mean, vampires?Æ It was hard not to feel miffed. His father had done some liaising with a vampire enclave in Germany, and none of the vampires Draco had met had looked anything like him. Neither had they exactly been avatars of good looks. ØWell, Muggles donít think theyíre real, obviously,Æ said Potter as they headed back down the street. ØAnd vampires are all really pale in Muggle movies.Æ ØWhy are they pale?Æ Potter glanced at Hermione, who was watching shopfronts pass with studied disinterest. ØI dunno,Æ Potter said. ØI suppose it makes them look like they havenít got blood?Æ ØBut they do have blood,Æ Draco said. ØJust because itís not circulating, doesnít meanîÆ ØYeah, well, how are Muggles supposed to know the details?Æ Draco felt agitated. Just then, another person wearing those odd black earmuffs walked by, holding the same shining grey oval. ØWhat are those earmuffs for?Æ he blurted, unable to stop himself. ØItís not that cold.Æ ØTheyíre headphones,Æ Potter said. ØYou can listen to music through them. It comes out of the CD player. The grey thing.Æ ØCD player?Æ Draco repeated. He was aware of how stupid he sounded, even infantile, but he couldnít seem to stop himself. After weeks of suppressed preoccupation over what the Muggle world really was, how it behaved, how it operated, he was here, in the thick of it. He had this urgent, rushing feeling, like the realization that he was in a dream, with a puzzle that he needed to solve before he awoke. Besides, Potter had told him to ask questions, hadnít he? And wouldnít it seem twice as stupid to pretend like nothing around him existed? At headquarters heíd been able to act like he wasnít thinking about it. Here, with everything pressing in on him, there was nothing else to consider. This was all. ØA CD is something that holds music,Æ Potter said. ØItís been recorded onto it. Like èÆ Potter was clearly struggling for words. Then Hermione spoke. ØImagine an Echo Charm,Æ she said, Øbut cast on a disc. If you put the disc into a player, itíll play the song as many times as youíd like.Æ Draco looked down at her. She hadnít faced him, and her voice had the kind of intense restraint that suggested Occlumency. Itís the Muggle National Health Service, he heard her say. It suddenly seemed so pointless that he hadnít asked his questions then. ØCome on,Æ she said, slipping into an alley. Another quick Apparition later, they were walking into a shop whose sign read LEO CLIFTON: PROFESSIONAL MAKEUP FOR THE STAGE AND TELEVISION. A little bell clattered on the door as they entered. ØGive us a minute,Æ called a voice from the back of the shop. ØThanks,Æ Hermione called back. Draco glanced around. Leo Cliftonís shop was lined with shelves upon shelves of bottles, most filled with paint. Other shelves held makeup, which didnít look so different from the lipsticks and eyeshadow that his mother had once kept in a cabinet at the manor. And posters hung on the wall with those unmoving Muggle faces, advertising what must have been the åmoviesí Hermione had described. Draco found the frozen images uncanny at first, but it was also hard to look away. The photographic images didnít move, but second by second, he felt as if he were deciphering more and more of their stillness, seeing hints of expression in a squinted eye or an open mouth that he wouldnít have noticed if the people in the posters had been moving the normal way. Just then, a small man with combed-back chestnut hair came bouncing out of a back room, pushing up his glasses with an apologetic smile. ØSo sorry for the wait,Æ he panted. ØWeíre shorthanded here today. Iím fixing up a Minotaur and a 3,000-year-old alien as we speak! But the moulds are setting now, so, what can I do for you? Leo Clifton, Leo Clifton.Æ He offered his hand to each of them in turn. Draco hesitated, lifting his hand only halfway before Leo Clifton seized it and shook with great enthusiasm. The manís handshake was cool and dry and confident. ØLovely to meet you,Æ Hermione said. ØIím Penelope Clearwater, and this is Neville Longbottom and Stan Shunpike. Weíre film students, and weíre trying to look like different people for a short film weíre shooting over the next month.Æ ØDifferent people, eh? How different?Æ ØWeíd like to be completely unrecognizable,Æ Hermione said. ØWeíre trying to fool our acting class,Æ Potter added. ØAlso, Stan and I, weíd like to look around ten or fifteen years older.Æ ØHmm.Æ Leo Clifton studied the three of them. Draco shifted uncomfortably. The manís eyes were piercing each of their faces as if they were marble blocks he intended to carve. ØWell, the hair will have to go, for starters,Æ he said. ØI could cover your faces and recognize all three of you from a mile away. YouÆîhe waved a hand at DracoîØcould get away with dye, but you two è well, they wouldnít really fit under wigs, would they? Weíll straighten this èÆ He moved his hand over Hermioneís head as if casting a wandless spell. Øè and shave you, I think, Neville.Æ Potter startled. ØShave?Æ Clifton smiled. ØIt grows back. Bald caps are more trouble than theyíre worth. The rest should be easy enough.Æ His eyes flicked back to Draco. ØYour bone structure will need some disguising, too, so weíll give you a nice full beard, and bronze up that complexion èÆ Clifton took out a notepad and began to jot down notes, looking between them and muttering things like, ØNew nose for you, I think,Æ and ØLight eye bags should do the trick.Æ The man was reminding Draco more and more of Ollivander. Here was a man who evidently loved what he did, who had clearly done it for decades, who spoke his vocation like a second language. As Clifton discussed payment with Hermione, Dracoís eyes fell on a section of the wall he hadnít noticed before. Here were hung not posters but news clippings. THE MAN BEHIND THE MONSTERS, read the first, with a full-page photo of a clearly elated Clifton. THE FACE OF BRITAINíS FACES, said a magazine cover, on which Clifton was holding a statuette and standing at a podiumîagain, beaming. It occurred to Draco he might be standing in a famous personís studio. In fact, with the vastness of the Muggle world, two hundred Muggles for every one wizard, he could well be speaking with a man who was known to more people than any wizard in Britain. Someone more famous than Potter, or Albus Dumbledore, or even the Dark Lord. Draco felt oddly lightheaded. It was as if heíd kicked off from the ground at the start of a Quidditch match but left half of himself behind. He felt as if he were moving farther and farther away, looking down at the entire world, and his smallness within it, and suddenly seeing the impossible breadth of Muggle civilisationsîso many Muggles, billions of them, of whom Leo Clifton was just one. After seventeen years of viewing anything outside the Wizarding World as utterly without interest, virtually non-existent, Draco felt minuscule. He felt like he needed to sit down. ØTomorrow, then! Eleven sharp. Looking forward to it, Ms. Clearwater.Æ Leo Clifton flashed those brilliantly white teeth again and jogged toward the back room, calling, ØYouíd better not have touched those noses!Æ Draco trailed out of the shop after Hermione and Potter. He blinked in the cold November sunlight, dazed. He looked around at all the Muggles passing, and heard snippets of conversation between friends and families hurrying down the street. Øîthe most beautiful car, bloody expensive, obviously, but èÆ Øîsheís always been rude, Jane. Always, always. Didnít I tell you èÆ Øîhand back the reports by next week at the latest èÆ ØîMum, youíre not looking. Mum! èÆ Dracoís daze was turning to numbness, and it felt stupid and trite, but two words were ringing in his head over and over. Just people. They were people like he might hear walking up the street at Diagon Alley. People involved in their jobs and lives and families. Parents as protective as his were, or as disinterested as Pansyís, or as expectant as Hermioneís. He saw tired-looking workers wanting a day off. Men in expensive watches giving orders. A couple holding hands and gazing at each other in the kind of glowing way that Draco could all but feel. People irritated, and laughing, and sad-eyed, and devoted. ØDo you fancy lunch?Æ Potter said, eyeing a shop across the street that advertised chips. Hermione checked her watch. ØYes, letís. Weíve got plenty of time.Æ Draco swallowed, nodded. Soon they were sitting in a booth inside, and Draco was licking salt off his fingertips. His head had stopped whirling. Everything now felt very slow and calm and quiet, to a nearly surreal degree. At the counter, a machine kept letting out a bright, cheerful ding! He watched Hermioneís hands fidget with the chequered, greasy paper in its shiny red basket. ØWhatís this,Æ he said quietly, tapping his own basket. ØWhatís it made of.Æ Potter hesitated, then glanced at Hermione. ØPlastic,Æ Hermione said. ØItís a synthetic material. Not found in nature, that is.Æ Her voice was still guarded. ØHow do you è how is it made?Æ ØThat kind specifically, Iím not sure,Æ she said. ØItís a diverse scientific process. This kind of plastic is different from the kind that makes up these booths, for instance, or the cash register.Æ She pointed to the machine that let out another ding! ØRight,Æ Draco said. He watched the sweaty-looking teenager who was tapping the machine's surface, speaking to customers. At that moment, the present customer, who was complaining in a loud, obnoxious voice, turned away. The teenager caught Draco watching and gave the tiniest roll of his eyes, clearly at the end of his rope. Draco could practically hear him saying, People like that, eh? It was such a minuscule thing. Such a tiny, human thing. But suddenly Dracoís throat grew so tight he thought he might choke. He saw his parents sitting at the end of their glimmering dining table and speaking about Muggles with fear and disgust. He saw the Dark Lord and the Death Eaters standing in a circle around the Muggle from the village, whose body was twitching and jerking. He saw himself and his friends in the Slytherin common room, eleven years old, laughing about the filth of the Muggle world and everything that came from it. It was how theyíd related to each other, the reason theyíd felt special, different, and important. Draco realized he was sweating. All the energy he and his friends and family had poured into hating Muggles, when Muggles didnít even know wizards existed. The one-sided loathing suddenly seemed almost farcical, the behaviour of obsessives. That boy at the register just trying to get through the day, Leo Clifton and his monster masks, the girl in the street trying to get her mum to pay attention to herîthese were the people they all hated? These were the people heíd been told were brutish and inferior, worthless and subhuman, and yet sinister, tooîthe people who would bring about the destruction of pure-blood life? Draco felt out of his own body. He felt as if he were seeing himself from across the grubby little chip shop, a wizard in a plastic booth, small and out of place, plucked from the roots of his family and the ancient ideals of his house, set adrift in a maelstrom of differences heíd thought were irreconcilable. Then he looked away from the counter and saw, with a flood of relief, that Hermione was looking at him. Her gaze was an anchor in the storm. He looked into her eyes; he needed her not to look away. He could see frustration in her expressionîthe kind she usually wore when she was puzzling out seemingly unsolvable logistical issues. Let her look, said that numb voice in his mind. Let her look at him and try to solve him. Let her see the way something in him was collapsing, no longer able to support itself. Draco had the sense of standing upon a threshold, looking out into wild, uncharted territory. If he truly no longer believed what his family lived by, if he really was a blood traitor like his aunt, his old life was lost. Even if the Dark Lord fell, there would be no going back. This new world, unmoored from everything heíd known, would be all he had. It was terrifying. But it was something else, too. It was quiet out there, over the thresholdîas quiet and still as a field with no breeze. In the new world there would be no chorus of voices in his mind, no Bellatrix hissing in his ear, always reminding him what was expected of him. He would be able to think, just think and feel, without the sensation that someone was watching him all the time. He would be able to ask simple questions, and consider their answers, without feeling guilty and disloyal and angry, hating his own interest, hating himself for breaching the contract of his upbringing. He would be able to care openly about Hermione, be friends with her openly, make her smile whenever he wanted, even have his thoughts of kissing her è he could want her, and it could be simple. Allowed. She looked so lovely to him just then, her hair falling in a thousand directions, scarf tossed carelessly over her shoulder, salt and oil upon her lips. He wanted the world that had her in it. Hermione didnít want Harry to leave the house. She fretted and fussed and delayed. She went over their falsified applications until mid-afternoon, when the winter sun began to sink and Harry told her he had to go now or the submission box could be closed for the day. She watched him Disapparate with a feeling like she was stepping over the edge of a high dive. And then she and Draco were alone in the kitchen. She looked determinedly at the sink but saw only him in her peripheral vision, tall and tensed and silent. She felt the week between them like a physical object. She felt, too, the way heíd looked at her in the chip shop in Kensington. The closest thing sheíd ever seen to that expression had been the moment heíd been struck by the curse in the Ministry. ØWell, weíve only got a day,Æ she said, her voice high and thin. ØCome on.Æ She gathered up parchment and quill, and he followed her down the hall into the darkened dining room. She was so aware of how close his steps were behind hers. ØSo,Æ she said, busying herself with lighting the lamps. ØCirce and Clodhna. In case something goes wrong, is it warded against Apparition?Æ ØNo,Æ Draco said, settling at the head of the table. ØGood.Æ Hermione spread a sheet of parchment near the opposite end of the table and made a note. ØYou or Harry will obviously need to come, too, to Confund Flint.Æ She could hear her words speeding. She tried to breathe normally. ØAnd I think two dates should be enough to keep him interested between the initial meeting and the gala, provided he actually is interested. Provided this is even possible.Æ ØItís possible." Draco flicked his wand, and a Warming Charm spread through the dining room. The temperatures were dropping quickly, drafts slipping in around the cottage windows. Hermione sank into the chair before her parchment, trying to focus. ØAll right. Tell me. Whatís Marcus FlintísÆîshe grimacedîØtype?Æ Draco lifted his shoulders. ØWhat youíd expect. Cultured. Alluring. Aloof but flattering. Intelligent, but would never challenge his opinions. Has strong drinks, but not many. You should drink Firewhisky with Madagascan Glassapple, heíd love that.Æ He hesitated. ØBeautiful,Æ he said. ØPoised.Æ ØSo, imaginary,Æ Hermione said. Dracoís lips pulled. Not quite a smile. Hermione felt a rush of frustration. How could he look so collected? After this morning, after this entire week, how could he sit there at the end of the table and look only a bit on his guard? She felt so messy in comparison. ØHere.Æ He levitated an empty Firewhisky glass from a nearby shelf. It landed in front of her with a clink. ØWhatís this?Æ ØPractice.Æ Draco rose to his feet. ØYouíre at the bar. Iím Flint.Æ ØOh. I èÆ She wanted to protest, but è four evenings with Flint. That could be ten hours of interaction she had to fake her way through. She wouldnít be Transfigured or Polyjuiced. She always felt free when transformed into someone else, but with her own voice and bodyî even if her face was unrecognizably made upîit would feel different. This was a logistical exercise, that was all. It would be useful. ØFine.Æ She pushed away her parchment and put her shoulders back, trying to channel cultured and aloof, as Draco approached. He pulled out the chair beside her. ØNo,Æ he said before he'd even sat down. ØWhat?Æ she said as he retreated to his starting position. ØWhat could I possibly have done wrong in point four milliseconds?Æ ØYou looked over like you were waiting for me to sit. You canít look interested.Æ ØYou said he liked to be flattered.Æ ØYeah,Æ Draco said, Øbut if you look eager, thatís not flattering. Itís only flattering if you seem unattainable and then you give him your attention, because then your attentionís worth something, see?Æ ØSlytherins,Æ she muttered. Now he did smile, a small smile that made her look away with a painful squeeze. This time, as Draco slipped into the seat next to her, she kept her eyes fixed on the glass, running her fingertip around its rim. ØEvening,Æ he said, his voice soft and smooth. ØWhat are you drinking?Æ Hermione began to turn toward him. ØSlowly,Æ Draco murmured. Her head froze mid-turn, and she took a deep breath, then began to move again, lazier, more languid, until finally she was facing Draco. Sheíd been so focused on the glass that she hadnít realised how closely the chairs were placed, or that heíd angled his toward her. He was hardly a foot away. Her gaze slipped immediately to his cheek, then to his arms, which were folded on the table, his dark jumper pushed to his elbows. His hands were long and slender. This did not feel like a logistical exercise. She didnít realise her face was flooded with heat until he said, ØIím going to hope Leo Clifton can cover that up.Æ ØI wonít be blushing tomorrow,Æ she said defensively, without thinking. He hesitated. ØWhy not?Æ Because it wonít be this. ØBecause,Æ she said, her throat tight, Øitísîitís just innately silly, pretending.Æ ØWell, that seems like a lack of imagination.Æ ØThis isnít meant for imagination. Itís meant for preparation.Æ After a long moment he said, ØI suppose.Æ In the short pause, though, Hermione did find herself imagining. She pictured herself at a dimly lit bar made from dark, glossy wood, votive candles flickering at intervals, a tall slender cocktail in front of her, Dracoís stool turned toward hers. Looking at her the way he had in Kensington, from the other side of the booth, like if she looked away he would drown. ØAgain,Æ he said, startling the image out of her head. ØWhat are you drinking?Æ She tried to think herself back into character. ØFirewhisky and Madagascan Glassapple. What about yî?Æ ØStill too eager. Donít ask him questions yet. Heíll want to lead.Æ ØFine. Lead, then.Æ A tinge of color appeared in Dracoís cheeks. ØHavenít seen you here before.Æ ØYou wouldnít have. Iíve only just movedîI graduated from the Multinational Wizardsí Academy in Dubai a few years ago.Æ ØInteresting time to come to Britain.Æ ØYes. The rising philosophy here suits my familyís values.Æ A long pause. Draco looked down at his own face in the polished wood of the table. Then, carefully, he said, ØIf you say something like that, youíll need to be ready for the sort of things heíll say. Because heíll expect you to say them back.Æ ØYou mean about Muggles. And Muggle-borns.Æ Draco nodded. ØItís fine,Æ Hermione said. ØI did it as Mrs. Parkinson. Itís è if I have to.Æ A thick silence filled the room. Hermione could have scooped it out of the air by the palmful. Then the words were bursting out of her, unable to repress: ØYou donít say those things anymore.Æ Draco grew very still. ØYou donít talk the way you used to talk,Æ she rushed on. ØAndîand this morning. You came into London, you were surrounded by Muggles, you talked to Muggles. You asked about things. You didnít say a word about è anything.Æ Draco was still regarding himself in the tableís surface. He had never looked more like a statue than in that moment, face of alabaster, impenetrable. It was almost a shock when he reanimated, turned to face her. He looked like he might choke if he tried to speak. Hermione heard the invitation in the silence. She could keep asking, keep pushing, where once he would have deflected. Or she could run. She could stand up, flee the questions and the possibilities, flee from Draco altogether. She didnít move. ØDoes it mean something,Æ she managed to say, Øthat youíre doing these things?Æ ØYes,Æ he said almost at once, as if heíd been waiting, hoping, for her to ask. Hermioneís mouth was as dry as parchment. Something cold flooded through her. Was it hope? Disbelief? Longing? A stronger fear than ever? And maybe he could see her fear, because the intensity of his expression lessened. There was wariness there, but softness now, too. She knew that look. Halloween. ØThat is what was this week was about, then,Æ he said. Words trembled on the tip of Hermioneís tongue. All week sheíd kept them inside, trying to wall herself off, keep herself safe. But she didnít know how. Her entire life it had been this way. Once she cared, she couldnít make it stop. She was wholehearted; she was her whole heart. ØYes,Æ she said. ØIím trying to be sensible. Because I canít be sure that you wonít è that youíre not going to èÆ She heard herself rushing, tripping, but she couldnít slow down. ØI mean, be realistic. Iím being realistic. You of all people should understand that I have to think about myself first, and given our historyîfor years è given everything, I felt I was being too è too èÆ His expression had stiffened. ØToo lenient,Æ he said. ØAbout my past.Æ ØNo. Too optimistic about right now.Æ The words were painful to say. His actions of the last few months, of the last week, of today, hadnít been those of someone who would hurt her. Hermione wanted to believe that. But sheíd been wrong before. ØIím scared,Æ she whispered. Draco looked as if a heavy weight had settled onto him. His expression was unfamiliar, something between pain and humiliation. It was a long moment before she recognised the look as shame. His voice unsteady, he said, ØI know.Æ There was a long, still silence. He watched her like someone awaiting a verdict. ØDo I need to be scared?Æ she asked. She could see his pulse going in his throat, hard and quick. When he spoke, his voice was very hoarse. ØNo.Æ Hermione searched his face and saw almost instantaneous hints of uncertainty, of intent, each there and gone at once. Then he lifted his hand to her cheek, and her mind went blank. He touched the spot where sheíd touched him on Halloween. For a moment his fingertips just rested there, and now, if anything, he looked afraid. Like she might vanish under his hand. Hermione felt like sheíd forgotten how to breathe. Slowly, haltingly, she tilted her face up toward him so that his palm pressed to her cheek. She remembered tipping into the night at the end of July, a thousand feet above the Earthís dark face. It was the same feeling here, absolutely still, in a silent dining room. Freefall. He leaned down and pressed his lips to hers. The silence rang around them like vibration. For an instant neither moved, held in the cold shock of contact. Then Hermione leaned slowly into him. His lips parted. His hand slid into her hair, warmer than sheíd dreamed it, and rougher. Of course his palms would be callused from the friction of a broom handle. But the kiss was frictionless, as easy as breathing. The tip of his nose pressed into her cheek, and his mouth shifted against hers, slipping, then closing upon her lower lip, soft and sure. She was shivery, suddenly overwarm. She drew a shaky breath through her nose and caught some clean, vague, anonymous scent. She wanted to move close enough to know it. Draco reached forward with his other hand at the same time she did, and their wrists knocked clumsily in midair, and Hermione smiled, and felt him smiling unevenly back into the kiss, and they broke for an instant, letting out small, nervous laughs, and she couldnít believe this was happening, after this weekîshe couldnít believe how her heart was pounding, how badly she wanted to keep kissing him. His hand found her waist, and hers the back of his neck, and their lips met again, this time less graceful, more forceful. He took her lip between his teeth, his hand tightening in her hair. Hermione felt something building like a shout in the center of her chest, like magma surging through the heart of a volcano, currents sunk deep in the ocean, everything buried and full of motion. She needed to put it somewhere. She grasped the front of his jumper and pulled, and he made a low sound and stood, taking her to her feet, the collision of their chairs shockingly loud in the whispery soundscape of unsteady breath and the contact of their skin. One of his fingernails scraped against her scalp. His other hand splayed across her back. They stepped clumsily to the wall, and he pressed her slowly against it. His hands came to her face, pushing her hair back as she stood tiptoed, her fists wound into the back of his jumper, holding him into her. He broke the kiss, still close, his breath whispering over her cheek. Hermioneís eyes eased open, and she looked into his with a humming blankness in her mind. The short fine lashes, nearly translucent. The gaze, so often casual or careless, now riveted on her with something raw and open, almost too personal to see. His hair had fallen over his high forehead; his mouth was kiss-swollen and looked sensitive. Her heartbeat struck and struck and struck like a clock stuck on the hour. She was sure he could feel it, his thumb resting upon the pulse point in her throat. He closed his eyes and leaned forward until his forehead rested gently against hers, breathing hard, and one of the breaths was her name, only half-formed and hardly a whisper, ghosted over her lips like a breeze, spilt out of him like he didnít know what he was doing. She thought maybe he hadnít even known heíd said it. Hermione realised how wrung-out she felt, as if she could have laid her head upon his shoulder and fallen asleep. The week had been like a typhoon. Here was its eye. She closed her eyes too and lifted her chin, just barely, until their lips touched again. Light and warm like rain in sun. She couldnít think enough to do more. Inside her was a disbelief like helium, and she was falling slowly upward, dizzied, lifted toward something as unfathomable as the sky. She let the kiss disengage, but then Draco kissed her in exactly the same way, placing his lips gently upon hers for just a moment, like he wanted to tell her, I knowîI knowîI feel it too. Chapter End Notes i tried deleting the previous chapter sixteen in order for this one to send an update, but if that did not happen, sorry!! alas, some people commented on the previous chapter sixteen and those comments disappeared :( your lovely words live on in my heart. thanks for reading as always, and sending my love <3 tumblr | instagram Chapter17: TheFirst AnnualChristmas Gala for the Celebration of Magical Unity Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes ØSo,Æ said Harry, as Leo Clifton led Hermione into the back room. Draco practically jumped. Heíd been watching Hermione smiling at Clifton, making polite conversation, the way her profile caught the bright lights of the studio. Now he looked over at Potter, who was leaning on a display of brushes and powders. ØSo è what?Æ Draco said. Potter raised his eyebrows. ØSo, you two have made up, have you?Æ Draco looked away, cheeks warm, to a poster of a giant alligator. ØI suppose you noticed she was speaking to me in full sentences again. That Seekerís eye doesnít miss a thing, does it?Æ ØI noticed more than that,Æ Potter muttered. Draco stole a mortified glance at Potter, whoíd also gone rather red now. Why had he brought it up if it was just going to embarrass them both? For Merlinís sake. Of course, Draco supposed, it would have been hard to miss the way he and Hermione had looked at breakfast. Sheíd come in to find him making breakfast before Harry had woken up. Fifteen minutes later, everything had been mysteriously burnt. ØI canít take anything you say seriously,Æ Draco said, Øwhen you look like that.Æ ØOh, like you look any better?Æ Potter said. As one, they glanced into the mirror behind the counter and snorted. Draco wouldnít have recognised himself if heíd seen the face in a picture. In the end, Clifton had opted for a wig rather than dye to achieve the proper texture: a nest of black curls like a thunderstorm, which matched the facial hair that had been glued to the lower half of his face. Using cold, slimy putty and rubber fixtures, Clifton had given Draco a snub nose and full cheeks, then swabbed his eyebrows with black paint. ØYou look like a sea captain,Æ Potter chortled. ØYou look like a Viking,Æ said Draco. Potterís shiny bald head glinted in the studio lights, and the film that Clifton had applied to both their faces aged Potter to his late thirties at least, several prominent wrinkles in his brow, deep crowís feet in the corners of his eyes. Potter, too, had a beard; his was shaggy and dark blond, ending in a small braid. Clifton had also, to Dracoís horror, applied something to their eyeballs called åcontact lenses.í Draco would happily have gone his whole life without reliving the process, but he had to admit they were effective. Potterís eyes were now as dark as Snapeís, Dracoís a vivid blue. ØLetís get some lunch,Æ Potter suggested, checking his watch. ØHe said itíd take an hour or two to get through her hair.Æ Draco agreed, and they went for curry in a nearby Indian shop that was ten degrees too warm. Draco asked occasional questions about the paintings on the wall, and the various machines the Muggles were using, which Potter answered without laughing. Mostly. Muggle London felt less overwhelming today, but maybe that was because Draco felt so distracted. Every few seconds, mid-conversation, even mid-sentence, heíd think of Hermione and what they were doing, what theyíd chosen to do. What heíd chosen. He thought of the way she tasted, like mild lip balm and something salty, and the way sheíd looked at him that morningîlike the sight of him made her happy, nervous, excited. It all washed over him again and again like an insistent tide. His mind hadnít fixated this way since è well, since heíd had to think about the Vanishing Cabinet every waking second. But those thoughts had been all fear and panic. He hadnít known it was possible to be equally fixated on something that made him feel like this. He hadnít known it was possible to feel this way at all. It wasnít the smooth, smug satisfaction heíd felt in the days after he and Pansy had gotten together. When he thought about Hermioneîwhen he thought of her melting into him in the dining room, the hesitance and then the heat, the way sheíd angled him against the kitchen counter that morningîhis heart seemed to stutter, and he felt disoriented, and a delirious squeeze of disbelief followed, seeming to saturate everything around him with color. He was preoccupied. As it transpired, Clifton had underestimated the time required to tackle Hermioneís hair. It was four hours before he emerged, grinning sheepishly, an unrecognizable girl at his shoulder. The hair was the most obvious change; it lay over Hermioneís shoulders as if Clifton had poured two bottles of Sleekeazyís into it. But the rest of her face was just as bewilderingly foreign. Heíd built out her jawline and heightened her cheekbones, then given her an aquiline nose and arched brows. With red paint on her lips, she looked exactly as haughty as she needed to. ØWell?Æ Clifton said. ØWhat do you think of the leading lady?Æ Hermione looked at Draco with laughing eyes, bright green now. ØItís brilliant, Leo.Æ ØTake care of that wig, Stan,Æ Clifton called after them as they left. Soon it was half-four, and they had reached the peacock-blue door painted onto a brick wall that was the entrance to Circe & Clodhna. Draco had heard a lot about the place, but had never actually been inside. He stepped directly through the wall, to the unconcern of the passing Muggles, into the bar. It was a long, dark, low-ceilinged lounge, its elegant walnut counter lined with brass trimmings. The only lamps were blue and indigo, giving the few people inside a ghostly cast. Glowing glass shelves hovered behind the bar, bearing liquor bottles of a hundred colors, and booths were tucked against the walls, divided by velvet curtains for privacy. Hermione, who had entered before Draco and Harry, was already sitting at the end of the bar. Draco tried and failed not to look at her. She was toying with her hair interestedly, examining its new texture. There was that disbelief again, making him feel as if he were hovering an inch or two off the black oak floor. Potter ordered drinks, and they situated themselves in a booth in the back corner, which offered a good vantage of the rest of the bar. Half an hour later, a shaft of light widened across the floor as the door opened, admitting fiveîtenîtwenty people in Ministry uniforms, all laughing and chatting. Draco saw him almost at once, blunt-jawed and half a head taller than the rest, with hair as flat-topped as a well-kept hedge. Marcus Flint, talking to a witch with dark red curls. ØThere,Æ he muttered across the table to Potter. ØNow.Æ Potter slid out of their booth and made for the door, muttering apologies as he slid through the crowd. As Potter passed Flint, Draco saw him grasp his wand in his sleeve and turn it subtly toward Flint, who hesitated on the spot, looking dazed. Draco grimaced into his gillywater and lime, hoping Potter hadnít overdone it. The Confundus was a delicate spell at the best of times, flexible enough to plant ideas or to wipe a mind temporarily blank. It was easy to overwork, and the last thing they needed was Flintís associates wondering why heíd suddenly started drooling onto the table. After a moment, though, Flintís haze cleared. He slid into the booth with the other Ministry workers, but his eyes were now fixed at the end of the bar, on Hermione. Her hand rested on the stem of a delicate glass filled with lavender liquid that occasionally spat violet sparks. Firewhisky and Madagascan Glassapple. The lure. Not even ten minutes later, Flint approached her. Draco tried not to watch it play out. He tried to act normal, to keep up with the small talk Potter was offering across the table. But his eyes kept straying. He could make out the low, breathy affect Hermione had given to her voice, and soon Flint was sitting next to her, unable to take his eyes off her. Draco couldnít help noticing that Flint was more muscular than he was, and several inches taller. He noticed, too, that Hermioneís body was inclined almost imperceptibly toward him. Draco felt a hard, unexpected stab of jealousy. He frowned down at his gillywater and lime, taken aback. Heíd never been a jealous person before. Pansy had flirted with other boys all the time, especially Blaise, toying at making him jealous, but heíd always been so certain of her affection that it had wound up feeling like a joke between them. But Hermione è sheíd loathed him for six years. It struck Draco all over again how unlikely this was, even perilous. The last person sheíd had feelings for had been Ron Weasley, for Merlinís sakeîand the things that had once given Draco such confidence, his family and his status and his wealth, had dissolved. He was alone, adrift, supposedly dead. What did he have to offer anymore? The jealousy gave way to frustration, even embarrassment. Draco could half-see his unrecognizable face in the black glass table. He looked so ridiculous in his disguise, his forty-year-old sea captain disguise. He wanted to be himself. He wanted Hermione to be herself. Draco Malfoy was the person making her feel this way, the one who turned immediately when she entered a room and got that breathless, alert look about him, the one who engaged when she was rattling off ideas about chained charms, the one who could, with a brush of his hand against her thigh during dinner, make her heart race. They stayed up late every night in the front room, talking about international Wizarding politics and Muggle politics alike. At first he mostly listened to the latter. Then he started asking questions; then, eventually, he would make commentsîthat certain cities sounded interesting, or similar to Wizarding locations. A bit older, maybe. At 10:45 p.m. on December 1st, they prepared to Apparate to Lillimont Lake. He spoke with bravado, as if heíd built the manor himself. ØYouíve been before?Æ Hermione said idly. ØYeah. I knew the family who lived here before the Lestranges.Æ Flint sounded, for the first time since heíd met her, a bit reluctant to speak. ØThey got mixed up in some è well, no one really knows the details.Æ Hermione raised a brow at him. ØFriends of yours?Æ Flint was quiet for a moment as they crunched over the gravel. The soft golden lights illuminated the fluttering feathers of several albino peacocks, which roamed up and down the hedges. ØYeah, we were friends. And they were loyal to the end, mind,Æ he added quickly, as if to ensure she didnít get the wrong idea. ØThey didnít turn blood traitor when things got è but that was when Dumbledore was still alive, so things were more dangerous. No one could have known what might happen.Æ Flint seemed to have found his way out of the woods. He was nodding to himself now. ØNo one could have known,Æ he said again. Malfoy Manor loomed overhead now, a faade of worn grey stone spread with tall French windows, every pane rippled with age. Its eaves were decorated with statuettes, dragons and chimaeras that seemed to gambol and play forty feet above, and the windows were flooded with inviting light. Hermioneís sense of foreboding only increased. It was all she could do not to grip her wand in her pocket. She thought of Draco and Harry, who had come in with Lizzie Spizzworthís hours earlier, who were now, hopefully, nestled in the heart of the manor. She and Flint swept up the long stairs into a marble foyer that sparkled with decorations. Real, never-melting icicles gleamed from the banisters, and fairies with delicate, gauzy wings fluttered over the lintels of the broad doorways. A twenty-foot-tall Christmas tree reached up to kiss the lowest dangling point of a chandelier, which hung from an intricately molded ceiling. It seemed unreal that this was the place Draco had grown up, that this wasnít a museum or a historically preserved site but the place that heíd played with friends and learned how to interact with the world. She tried to look neither interested nor impressed. ØYouíll have to tell me who all these people are, Marcus,Æ she said in Marileaís low, breathy tones, glancing around. It was a relief that sheíd recognised nobody so far, a parade of unfamiliar faces in early adulthood to middle age. Now she did, however, see Rita Skeeter leaning against a nearby table, sipping a blood-red drink, photographer at her arm and Quick-Quotes Quill skating away on a notepad before her. Flint thrust out his barrel chest importantly and scanned the crowd. ØThose are the Greengrasses,Æ he said, pointing to a couple in their forties. ØThey own the security company. Thatís Algernon Wolflaw, Office of Domestic Affairs è only a half-blood, but well-liked. He had a hand in planning all this.Æ He nodded to a young couple, two red-haired women in dress robes, one in black and deep purple, the other in grey and misty white. He lowered his voice. ØLidia Taylor and May DeRisa, Department of International Magical Cooperation. Some funny business with their family trees, but they wheedled out of supervision somehow.Æ ØCharming,Æ Hermione said, wondering if she should try and approach Taylor or DeRisa. If they ever did need papers forged, perhaps they could be useful contacts. Flint was already pressing forward, though, his big hand too tight on her waist. ØIíll introduce you to some of the others in Magical Games and Sports inside. Come on.Æ Hermione let herself be steered, and they followed the stream of people across the foyer into something like a ballroom. Crisscrossing hardwoods stretched fifty feet to a small stage where a band was arranging a series of eccentric-looking instruments, all with many more strings and pegs and curlicued shapes than Muggle instruments would have had. The ceiling was vaulted, and along one wall, a low fire simmered in a hearth that could have fit a small bus. Above the hearth stretched a banner that read, in sparkling green-and-red letters, 1ST ANNUAL MINISTRY OF MAGIC CHRISTMAS GALA FOR THE CELEBRATION OF MAGICAL UNITY. On the opposite wall, a matching banner read MAGIC IS MIGHT! Hermioneís unfazed expression must have slipped, because Flint was grinning down at her. ØWhat do you think?Æ She allowed a small, knowing smile. ØNot quite the scale we manage in Dubai è but Iíve seen worse.Æ ØNever impressed, are you? I like that in a witch.Æ Flintís hand slid from her waist onto her lower back, then dipped dangerously low. ØWould you care to find me a drink?Æ she said, stepping forward, away from his touch. He looked displeased for a moment, but nodded and stalked off through the accumulating crowd, leaving Hermione at a small table in the corner. She let out a slow exhalation, scanning the room for Draco, Harry, Umbridge, or the Weasleys. Theyíd decided their first task would be to find Mr. and Mrs. Weasley, to show them the address of headquarters. They were certain the Weasleys wouldnít linger at the gala long, but it was unlikely they would skip it altogether, when seeming like theyíd assimilated into this new society was so important. Sheíd tucked the piece of parchment with the information into her bra; it itched whenever she moved. Suddenly voices were murmuring. A kind of ripple seemed to move through the room, and Hermione followed the looks that everyone was sending back toward the grand entryway. A chill flooded her. Hermione had known she would be here, of course, she and her husband. This was their manor, now. Yet the sight of Bellatrix Lestrange, heavy-lidded and imperious, sweeping through the door with her husband Rodolphus, still made Hermione go very still. She remembered Bellatrix in the Department of Mysteries, lashing out with her wand, her spells flying with such mad power that they had splintered through layers upon layers of wood and glass. On Bellatrixís other side stood Yaxley, whoîunless Hermione was much mistakenîlooked thinner than he had at the Ministry. He also appeared to be limping. Vincent Crabbe was walking at his fatherís shoulder, Millicent Bulstrode beside him in dress robes of pale green. There was a defiant look on Crabbeís blunt features, as if he dared anyone to ask why his father might not be in perfect working condition. Hermione knew she was unrecognizable. Yet when the rest of the group followed, she shifted further back into the corner. More and more faces she knew, all of whom could be dangerous. Garton Goyle, pale-faced and pockmarked, muttering something to his son Gregory with obvious irritation. An ethereally beautiful woman with obsidian-dark skin, ushering along Blaise Zabini, who looked prouder and more disgusted than ever. Then there was Theodore Nott, short and slender and sandy-haired, beside an older brother in Trainee Aurorís robes. Pansy Parkinson was gripping Theoís arm, and Hermione saw, with a feeling of unwilling fascination, that a rain of fine cuts were laid upon Pansy's cheek, half-healed. As the Slytherins dispersed out into the room, into many welcoming shouts from Ministry members, a disgusted voice muttered from the table next to Hermione, ØLetís all clap for our ruling class.Æ Hermione glanced over and received another shock. One was a plump, black-haired witch she didnít recognise, but the other she knew: Sturgis Podmore, broad-shouldered, with a thatch of blond hair. Heíd gone to Azkaban their fifth year after being put under the Imperius Curse by the Death Eaters. And hadnít the witch accompanying him just insulted the Death Eaters? What if Sturgis had been in touch with other Order members? Dare she say something? The witchís dark eyes played over Hermione warily. Clearly she was worried Hermione had heard the comment. ØIîÆ He broke off, his eyes settling on Podmore and the witch. ØAh. è Podmore, right?Æ Flint grunted, sounding a bit suspicious but not outright unfriendly. ØWhat?Æ ØCrabbe was trying to flag you down,Æ Podmore said, pointing to the far end of the ballroom, where both the elder and younger Crabbes were in conversation with Zabini. Flint looked momentarily discomfited, and Hermione knew he was aware of the elder Crabbeís status as a Death Eater. ØHe hasnít been to the Burrow?Æ Mrs. Weasleyís kind face slackened further. ØYes, why?Æ ØHis mother found me outside the Ministry. I know where theyíre staying.Æ Draco felt as if he were in a nightmare. Heíd had dreams of home over the past few weeks with increasing regularity. In the dreams, there had always been something off about the manor, something about the angles or the colors. But simply moving through the halls from the East Wing to the main rooms, finding the spacious rooms and familiar furnishings just as heíd last seen them over the Easter holidays, was somehow a thousand times more disorienting. After missing his home for months, it was a kind of agony to be back here. All the former trappings of his life identically remained, as if he hadnít disappeared, as if he hadnít died, as if his absence meant nothing. He hadnít dared go down to the west end of the house, to his room. ØChin up. Lizzie always takes us out to the Leaky Cauldron the night after, her treat. We can down a litre of Firewhisky and swap stories about the biggest arseholes.Æ And she strode down the long green carpet that spanned the manorís main hall. Draco carried a tray of clean glasses after her, past the sculpted bust of Callalya the Catastrophic, past the long painting of the Battle of the Hebridean Blacks. He emerged at the top of the staircase in the foyer, looking down at the plane of smooth white marble, the chandelier, the tremendous Christmas tree. By the time he reached the bottom of the steps, he was sweating again. Heíd worked in the kitchen so far, had managed to avoid going into the room ahead, which his family had called the hearth room. It wonít look the same, he told himself. Theyíll have cleared all the furniture away. That, however, would be worse. With the furniture cleared, the floor would be exposed. He remembered the stretch of old carpet that ran along the hearth, rubbing into his cheek. He remembered the Muggle man at the Dark Lordís wandtip, the way his body had jiggled like a marionette, bare heels splayed upon the parquet floors. The Dark Lord had cut away his shirt, and cut lines into his skin, and the man had screamed, and then heíd been upside-down. And Draco had stood there and laughed, his voice melting into the rest. An hour later the man had been dead. Draco had taken the Mark there, in that room. It had been agony, but heíd done it willingly, thirsty for the chance to undo his fatherís supposed mistakes. In the echo of voices from the gala he could practically hear his own voice now, everything heíd spat at the Death Eaters in these hallsîall the jeering heíd tried to withstand by lashing out with insults at Muggle.borns and half-bloods and blood traitors. All the ways heíd chosen to belong. Draco swayed at the bottom of the steps and looked through the open doors into the night, down the long twin hedges lit with tiny lights. There they were again, the ghosts that chased him. They were everywhere. Spots burst in his eyes. He saw his eleven-year-old self tearing down that gravel path, yelling at Crabbe and Goyle, Hurry up, for Merlinís sakeîyou two are so slow. He saw himself riding a toy broomstick over this balcony, zooming over the marble, and bragging to Pansy that she could never catch up. He saw himself at age fourteen, moping as he slid down the banister, making fun of Hermione to his mother, trying to mask his own resentment thatîyet againîa Muggle-born had outscored him in every test. This place was his past. It was everything heíd ever been. And now he was separate from it, out in the open, in the new and quiet world. And if blood meant nothing, heíd acted that way for sixteen years for nothing. Every word, every thought, every action, had been for nothing. In third year, just after slapping him, Hermione had called him foul. Sheíd called him evil. He saw himself through her eyes now, deriding the Gamekeeper who had comforted her. He saw himself turning to his mother in Madam Malkinís and saying, hardly more than a year ago, If youíre wondering what the smell is, Mother, a Mudblood just walked in. There was a taste in Draco's mouth like bile. He turned left unthinkingly, away from the threshold of the gala. He made for a side corridor, then tried to shoulder through a nearby door. It was locked, but he said through gritted teeth, ØAlohomora,Æ needing privacy, needing to be out of sight, and it came open. The passage beyond was dark and cold, a set of steep steps. He descended a few and whispered, ØLumos,Æ set down the tray of wine glasses, and leaned against the stone wall, letting out hard breaths. The last few months, heíd grown acquainted with self-doubt, but thisîthis was something else. It was a thick, treacly self-loathing, moving through his veins like magma. After learning to trust Hermione, to care for her, to fear for herîafter these past few weeks, which had been an oasis of understanding in the chaos of the last yearîhe hadn't expected what returning to this place would do to him. Draco suddenly couldnít understand how she didnít still loathe him. How could she look at him without seeing all these things that suddenly, in retrospect, made him feel a shame so violent it was like panic? And what was he supposed to do, now that he was seeing his past self this way? Heíd changed his mind, heíd changed his actions, but he was still contained in himself. He couldnít tear his old self out of his body. He would never be able to. Of course McGonagall had looked at him with suspicion. For the rest of his life people would look at him that way. And shouldnít they? he thought wildly, bitterly. Since when had he ever looked at someone and forgiven them everything theyíd ever done? Hadnít he held petty, stupid grudges, a thousand minuscule judgments? Why should he get special treatment? Why should anyone even care if heíd changed? Then a sound made Draco jerk so violently he nearly toppled down the stairs. ØHello?Æ It echoed up from below, small and feeble. For a long moment Draco could only stare down the dark flight of steps to their invisible end, his heart pounding even harder now. His thoughts seemed to have frozen, his mind suddenly blank. ØIs someone there?Æ It was a girlís voice. With a sense of sick dread compounding inside him, Draco began to climb down. Usually they stored extra furniture down here for their summer garden parties. Usually this place was dark and forgotten. Now, as he neared the foot of the stairs, he saw that a heavy door had been conjured into place, sealing off the space beyond. Dracoís heart beat in his throat, and he held his wand aloft as he slowed, casting the light forward. There was a barred window in the door. A face was staring out at him, grimy and starved-looking, eyes wide and pale in the gloom. It was Luna Lovegood. Chapter End Notes tumblr | instagram "hmm," you say, "that dress sounds awfully familiar!" you are Correct, i lost my mind over this piece by elithien and wrote it directly in. Chapter 18:Malfoy Manor Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes ØLîÆ Lunaís name stopped at the tip of Dracoís tongue as he remembered his disguise. ØWho are you?Æ she asked. The wandmaker bore hardly any resemblance to the man who had clapped with delight when Draco, eleven years old, had swished this very wand through the air to produce a shimmering tail of white flame. Fear and disgust pulsed through Draco. As he thought of Bellatrix, his fear redoubled, sending ice over his skin. If Malfoy Manor was now being used for this purpose, his aunt must have taken precautions to ensure the captives wouldn't escape. He didnít know what to do. He had to think pragmatically. He knew that if he told Hermione or Potter about this, they would insist on trying to save Luna and Ollivander, but it wouldnít help the captives if the three of them got chucked into this cell alongside them. Their circumstances would only worsen if Potter, in particular, were killed. It might not be worth the risk to tryîmight be the better option to take the Horcrux, go, and focus on the Dark Lordís fall. And yet even the idea of keeping this from Hermione made him feel that oppressive sense of shame again. They would have made some oblique reference to her torture, and ensured he knew that worse could always happen. Luna seemed unperturbed by Dracoís silence.
