It would be a real help." The hope in her voice was piteous. Merlin, Draco thought, his stomach in knotsîsheíd already resigned herself to living here, like this. Maybe even dying here. He tried to remember the evening of the nineteenth. Had he and Hermione been huddled in front of the fire that night, Draco toying with thoughts of abandoning the plan and staying in headquarters forever? And all the while, had Luna been under Bellatrixís Cruciatus, or fleeing through the woods in the Malfoy grounds wild-eyed, twigs snapping in her blond hair, tripping into the mud? Draco felt as if heíd crashed down to Earth after a hundred-foot fall. Heíd known the war for the Wizarding World had ground onward while theyíd been in their safe haven, but the reports on the Wireless had felt so distant, sanitised as they were by the Ministry. For strategyís sake, the Death Eaters wouldnít kill Luna. Draco knew that. He also knew theyíd do anything and everything else, if not to manipulate her father, then out of boredom or frustration. If this was what she looked like after only four daysí captivity è Draco resisted the image of the girl propped against the wall like Ollivander, months later. Something was coalescing inside Draco, a fragile, tentative knot of determination. Hadnít he found a hidden way into Hogwarts last year? Hadnít he solved that unsolvable problem? And this was the manor. He knew this place the way he knew himself. There must be a way to manage it again. ØGive me ten minutes,Æ he rasped. As he started up the steps, climbing out of the darkness beneath the manor, he glanced back down the steps at Lunaís face, an amorphous smudge dwindling like a dying candle. In truth he had no idea what he was doing, except that he was going to find the others, and then there would be no going back. Draco faced forward again and held his wand aloft and climbed. Somehow the steep stairs looked three times as long as when heíd descended into the depths, and the manor suddenly seemed treacherous, despite the glittering splendour aboveîor maybe precisely because of that splendour. The home of his childhood seemed to turn transparent, hovering like a mirage in a shining layer over reality. The ancient house shared his name. It had shaped him, it had made him, it was elegance and grace and refinement. Hours before, standing at the foot of the sweeping drive, heíd felt relief and even pride to see it again, to remember where he came from. But here, inside and deep beneath, in its hidden places, this was its foundation, after all. Torture and debasement, his own and othersí. Hideous actions, his own and othersí. Lavish finery, concealing the truth that belonged to him and the lies heíd inherited. Dracoís legs were tiring, and he had that whirling feeling, again, of nightmare. Pressing in on him was the crushing sensation of captivity. That was the manor, in the end. Enclosure. But he kept climbing. He could not, would not stop. There would be a way out. There had to be. Hermione waited until Mr. and Mrs. Weasley had disappeared from the hall before returning to the gala. Just inside the door, a blond-bearded someone caught her eye. It was Harry. She flashed three fingers and gave a small nod: the Weasley part of their plan was complete. A triumphant look flashed across Harryís face, and he flashed his pinkie finger, then nodded, too. Hermioneís heart leapt. Umbridge had arrivedîand Harry had succeeded in giving her a drink that contained a crucial element: fast-acting laxative powder. Under the guise of looking around for Flint, she navigated close enough to Harry to whisper, ØHow long ago?Æ ØSeven minutes or so. Sheís in the sunroom.Æ ØAnd Draco?Æ ØKitchen, I think.Æ Hermione peeled away. She scanned the room for Draco with an anxious pangîshe knew this was the room where Voldemort had used the Cruciatus on him, and that heíd hoped to avoid it as long as possible. Still, they needed him here soon, on the off-chance that Umbridge slipped between her fingers. All their dozens of contingency plans involved him. Just then, an inhuman wail split the air. The band broke off, and a panicked surge of voices rose through the ballroom. Hermione pressed herself against the wall, terrified that theyíd somehow been detectedîbut then she saw two members of the Greengrass Guard forcing through the crowd toward one of the windows, which a tall, portly wizard had cracked open. It was a Caterwauling Charm, Hermione realised. ØWhat?Æ the wizard was saying indignantly. ØItís stifling in here! I canít get a bit of fresh air?Æ ØCast a Clean Air Charm, then,Æ the guard said, stony-faced, shutting the window again. ØSonorus,Æ said the other guard. Her magically amplified voice announced throughout the ballroom: ØGuests will please note that Caterwauling Charms have been applied at windows and all unapproved exits to improve event security and prevent any burglarising. Thank you and please enjoy your evening.Æ As they exited the ballroom and the music started back up again, Hermioneís heart was still pattering. She was grateful it had happened. They hadnít planned for Caterwauling Charms, and that bit of security rendered several of the contingency plans useless. Hermione mentally crossed them off the list, but she frowned, suddenly suspicious. Sealing off the entire manor seemed like an extreme measure to åprevent burglarising.í Then she saw Flint scanning the crowd with dissatisfaction. She feigned relief and navigated toward him. ØMarilea,Æ he said. ØThere you are. Thought youíd run off with someone and set off the Caterwauling Charm.Æ She laughed a low, melodic laugh. ØIím sorry. I thought I was leaving you in good company.Æ ØTheyíve all gone off to dance now.Æ He extended one large hand. ØCare to?Æ Hermione hesitated. The entrance to the sunroom was at the end of the ballroom, a set of tall glass doors fogged by the heat of the crowd. She needed to get in there to monitor Umbridge. What if her prey left for the toilets through another exit? But Flint clearly felt that she wasnít paying enough attention to him. She needed as little friction as possible, so that when she left to pursue the Horcrux, he didnít feel suspicious. She decided to play at self-consciousness. Marilea had to have a weakness, didnít she? ØO-oh,Æ she said, faltering as she looked back at the many dancing couples. ØEr è could we have another drink, first?Æ She glanced from side to side, as if on the brink of reluctant admission, then said, ØYou may find it hard to believe, but I have a difficult time loosening up. Sometimes.Æ With relief, she saw Flintís harsh features soften. ØSure,Æ he said. ØLater. Itís still really early.Æ When he placed his hand on her back again, it was in a more respectful location than before, almost reassuring. For the first time, Hermione felt a twinge of guilt about this charade. Flint had been so self-centred and aggressive that she hadnít considered that he might develop legitimate feelings for Marilea. But then they entered the sunroom, and Flint knocked into someone just beyond the door, and Hermione forgot about her guilt. Heíd walked directly into a tall, attractive couple: the man burly and fair, with windswept sandy hair; the woman dark-skinned, with microbraids wound into an elegant bun. They looked older than they had at Hogwarts, ineffably more adult, but unmistakable. Oliver Wood and Angelina Johnson. The silence that dropped between the former Quidditch rivals was excruciating. The band, dulled on the other side of the door, played on; an enchanted fountain burbled in the humid air. Hermione cast an awkward look around the sunroom, which was filled with row upon row of plants that would have made Professor Sprout proud. Flint was the first to speak. ØWood. Johnson.Æ Then, with a smirk, he said, ØMad who theyíll let into these events.Æ ØYes,Æ said Angelina, looking Flint over. ØIt is.Æ Hermione felt the weight of Angelinaís dislike, but it seemed she didnít dare insult Flint openly. Flint's smirk widened into a smug look that Hermione hated. She wished she werenít at his side. It felt bizarre, standing here in front of the two older Gryffindors. Hadnít she partnered with Angelina during DA meetings half a dozen times, Stunned her back into piles of cushions and had Angelinaís formidable Impediment jinx cast upon her, too? And Hermione had listened to Harry complain about Woodís sadistic captaining for three full years è and now here he was, here they all were, in a world run by Death Eaters. ØAngelina Johnson and Oliver Wood,Æ Flint introduced to Hermione. ØWe went to Hogwarts together, though they were in the house with most of the Muggle-lovers. Gotten over that now, have you?Æ He was definitely jeering now; Angelina and Wood were drawn as taut as bowstrings. Hermione could see Angelinaís fingers digging into Woodís wrist. ØThought so,Æ Flint said, with a sip from his glass of Firewhisky. ØWe can all get along now, though. Schoolís over. New rules out here in the real world. è This is Marilea Linhardt, you two. Marilea, Wood plays Keeper for Puddlemere United, and Johnsonís in the Department of Magical Accidents and Catastrophes, athletics division.Æ Angelina and Wood both stuck out their hands, looking reluctant. Hermione avoided their eyes and shook as quickly as possible. ØMarilea went to school in Dubai,Æ Flint went on. ØSheís thinking about being a Quidditch commentator, andèÆ Hermioneís attention slipped. There she was. There, coming out from behind a Flutterby Bush in a vast adobe pot, was Dolores Umbridge. Umbridge wore satin dress robes in fuchsia and her usual saccharine smile, and the Horcrux gleamed upon her chest. She was deep in conversation with none other than Bellatrix Lestrange. Two tall, expressionless Aurors flanked Umbridge. Hermione let out a slow breath. It was a stroke of luck that both Aurors were men. There was nearly no likelihood that they would insist on standing guard in the powder room, where the guests might need to privately adjust their clothing. Draco had informed her and Harry that it was the height of rudeness to watch glamour magic being performed, and that if Hermione saw anyone erasing wrinkles in the powder room, she should keep her eyes averted. Øè simply canít express our gratitude for your generosity, Madam Lestrange,Æ Umbridge was simpering. ØThe Minister was so honoured that you would open your doors, especially after all the wrongs the Ministry has done you over the years. Pius will be here soon, of course èÆ ØMarilea?Æ said Flint. Hermione started. ØPardon me. That womanís quite eye-catching, isnít she?Æ She glanced back at Bellatrix, who looked haughty and disdainful as usual, but not dissatisfied with Umbridgeís flattery. Wood and Angelina exchanged uneasy looks. Flint shifted too. ØThatís Bellatrix Lestrange,Æ he said, his voice half its earlier volume. ØWhat, Flint?Æ said Angelina. ØNot going to extol her virtues? I thought you lot looked up to her.Æ ØShut up,Æ Flint said through his teeth. Hermione pretended not to notice any of this. ØAnd the woman with her?Æ ØDolores Umbridge,Æ Flint said. ØSenior Undersecretary to the Minister.Æ ØWhat lovely dress robes,Æ Hermione said. ØSuch an unusual shade.Æ But Umbridge and Bellatrix had begun to laugh, and such a wave of hatred ran through Hermione at the sight of Umbridge tittering along with Siriusís murderer that her voice came out high and tight, not at all like Marileaís. Flint gave her a surprised look, but it was Angelinaís expression that made Hermioneís stomach drop. Her eyes had fixed on Hermioneís face with the hard focus that the Chaser had assumed every time she threw a perfectly aimed goal. As Flint glanced back toward the fogged-up doors to the ballroom, Hermione met Angelinaís eyes and gave her head a tiny, urgent shake. Angelina looked stunned for an instant. Then she dipped her head in an equally tiny nod. ØIn any case,Æ Hermione said to Flint when his gaze returned to her, ØIím surprised this is only the first annual gala. Wasnít there a call for something like this before? èÆ They carried on an easy, shallow conversation for several more minutes, Hermione keeping an eye on Umbridge. Then Umbridgeís face contracted with surprise and dread. Hermioneís pulse began to speed. She felt the weight of the wand in her pocket again. Soon enough Umbridge was shifting awkwardly in place, and the vengeful part of Hermione filled with satisfaction. She remembered how the loathsome woman had behaved in the Ministry, so excited to tear Muggle-borns away from their loved ones. The more discomfort in her life, the better, as far as Hermione was concerned. Then Umbridge was making her excuses and hurrying toward the door, the Aurors following. Hermione made herself count to ten. Then she winced, squinting one eye until it watered. ØMarcusîI need to dash to the powder roomîmy makeup. è Shall I meet you on the dance floor?Æ He brightened. ØYeah,Æ he said. ØIíll see you in there.Æ Hermione slipped out of the sunroom into the hall. Umbridge was trotting with increasing speed toward the powder room, which served as a sort of antechamber to the bathroom. There was a line of a half-dozen women down the hall, but Umbridge bypassed all of them, to several disgruntled looks, and disappeared into the powder room. The line was inconvenient, but they had a plan for this: either Harry or Draco was supposed to tell the other women about the other bathrooms spread throughout the west wing. But as Hermione joined the line, neither Harry nor Draco appeared. Seconds ticked by, and Hermioneís mouth grew dry. Where were they? What could they possibly be doing? They didnít have forever, and the longer Umbridge took in the bathroom, the longer the line would growîthe more conspicuous they would be. Just as Hermione was about to resort to the Puking Pastille sewn into her neckline, Draco appeared at the entrance to the foyer. He passed the Greengrass Guard who monitored the hall, approaching with purpose. Hermione felt limp with relief. She could just make out his features beneath the makeup, and the sight bolstered her. ØExcuse me,Æ Draco announced, stopping at the head of the line. Heíd turned his smooth drawl into a buzzing, nasal voice. ØAs weíve had a number of complaints about the wait, weíve decided to open up the toilets on the second floor temporarily. There are two more just up these stairs. Follow me.Æ The line in front of Hermione peeled off, hurrying after Draco toward the stairwell heíd indicated. She couldnít help but think that Dracoís eyes had looked panicked as heíd passed. She tried not to think about it, to focus only on the Horcrux. If something had gone wrong, she couldnít do anything about it now, at the crucial moment. She moved to the door and said, rubbing her fingertip against her lower eyelid as if irritated with her makeup, ØI only need the powder room.Æ The Aurorsí eyes moved over her, then off, as she entered. The door clicked shut behind her. The layout of the room was just as Draco had described. To one side was a luxurious counter of Italian marble, where twin mirrors were lit by hovering bulbs. In the corner opposite the counter stood the door to the bathroom. There were three other women in the powder room: two queued against the wall for the W.C., and the last, a squat older witch in velvet whose hands were down her dress, pulling at an evidently problematic bra. Øè know sheís a higher-up,Æ one of the queued women was muttering, Øbut Iíve been waiting for ten minutes, and she just barges past èÆ ØTheyíve opened up the upstairs bathrooms,Æ Hermione said over her shoulder to the queued women as she approached one of the mirrors. The two women glanced over. ØReally?Æ ØYes. Iíd try those, if I were you. I overheard Madam Umbridge saying she didnít feel well. She may be a while.Æ The women exchanged a glance, then nodded to Hermione. ØThanks,Æ one said, and they both exited the powder room. Hermione continued to toy with her eyelid for a moment, but the older witch in velvet didnít seem close to leaving. ØHonestly,Æ Hermione said with a light sigh. ØThey call it Ellwinaís Everlasting Eyeliner, yet somehow it needs fixing every twenty minutes.Æ The other witch let out a hearty laugh. ØSame with this Sticking Charm. Though youíre too young to need that, of course.Æ ØSoon enough,Æ said Hermione with a friendly smile. She drew her wand, leaned close to the mirror, and pretended to touch it to her eyelid. Then she leaned back, as if to admire the effect, and flicked it in the other witchís direction, thinking, Confundo! The other witchís face went momentarily slack. Then confusion passed over her face, and her expression cleared. She tugged her dress back into place before bustling out of the powder room. Hermione was alone. At once, she stopped fidgeting with her eye and assumed her place in front of the bathroom door. For a long moment she waited, her body so full of tension that she thought she might be sick. The toilet flushed. Muffliato, she thought, flicking her wand at the door to the hall. Colloportus! Locking the door was a big risk, probably the most suspicious part of the operationîbut for these thirty seconds, she couldnít have any other guests getting in. She turned back to the bathroom door and drew a shaky breath. ThenîAlohomora. The bathroom door clicked open. Time blurred and jerked. One moment Hermione was flying over the threshold, met with the sight of Dolores Umbridge washing her hands. The next, Umbridge let out a shriek, her hand flying for her wand. But she was too late. Hermione had already castî Obliviate! The spell struck Umbridge hard, and she sank back against the wall, her eyes rolling, momentarily unconscious. Hermione leapt forward and caught her before she hit the ground. She unclipped the locket from Umbridgeís neck and paused for an instant, remembering what she was holding, feeling that aliveness within it, as the diadem had felt alive. A shiver shot down her back. She shook herself back to life, stuffed the Horcrux down her dress into her bra, and slipped a golden bracelet from around her wrist. She Transfigured it into a precise copy of the locket, which she fastened around Umbridgeís ruff. Her heart beat harder. Almost done now. She drew a deep breath and whispered the spell sheíd hated practicing, for it was the same spell sheíd used on her parents. ØNovaria.Æ Umbridgeís eyelids flickered, but she didnít yet wake. Hermione dragged her forward into the powder room, and as she hauled Umbridge upright against the closed bathroom door, she removed the Muffliato and unlocked the door to the powder room again. She turned back to Umbridge. Confundo, she thought, flicking her wand. Rennervate. Umbridgeís eyes flew open. ØI said,Æ Hermione said, frowning, Øyour dress robes are lovely.Æ Umbridgeís wide, pouchy face still looked disoriented. For a long, horrible second, Hermione was worried the false memories hadnít takenîthat Umbridge remembered something other than washing her hands at the sink, opening the door, and, after a brief momentís dizziness, being greeted by a compliment from Marilea Linhardt. Then Umbridgeís expression cleared. ØThank you,Æ she said, with a wide-eyed look at Hermioneís robes that clearly said she didnít feel the same about Hermioneís appearance. Then she trotted out of the powder room. She wobbled for a moment at the door, which alarmed Hermione, but she seemed to shake it right away. She was gone. It was done. Hermioneís mouth opened, and she drew several long, deep breaths. Her heart was beating as if sheíd just sprinted miles, but triumph flooded through her. The plan had gone exactly as theyíd hoped, and the Horcrux was warm against her breast. They had it. The locket was theirs. Now the only thing was to make their excuses and leave the grounds, and no one would be the wiser. But when she left the powder room, Draco was stationed against the opposite wall again. Her muscles tensed. She hadnít imagined the panic in his eyes. Something had gone wrong, beforeîthey werenít meant to have any contact after her encounter with Umbridge, not until they were outside the manor. ØExcuse me,Æ she said to Draco, ØIíve been told this house has a bust of Callalya the Catastrophic. Could you direct me to it?Æ Their code for a private place to speak. ØRight this way,Æ he said. ØIíve got the locket,Æ she whispered as she followed him down the hall toward the foyer. He glanced down at her and nodded once. Hermioneís heart beat harder. He hardly even seemed pleased. As they passed through the foyer, Hermione realised one of the Greengrass Guardsí eyes were following them. Draco was leading her not back into the ballroom, but toward a side hall, where another catering employee was carrying a tray of empty glasses. Guests probably werenít meant to be in this area. But Draco had noticed the guard's attention, too. ØIím so sorry the refreshments werenít up to your standard,Æ he saidînot very convincingly, Hermione thought. He sounded almost sarcastic. Of course, Draco Malfoy pretending to work in the service industry had been a ridiculous mismatch all along. ØI donít need your apologies,Æ she said coldly as they passed the suspicious guard. ØI want to tell Ms. Spizzworth herself that Iíve never had such undercooked crab in my life. Itíll be a miracle if no one gets food poisoning. èÆ Hermione thought she saw the Greengrass Guard give an irritated roll of the eyes, but the woman said nothing as they entered the side hall. ØWhat is it?Æ Hermione whispered. ØWhatís going on?Æ ØHere.Æ Draco led her to a locked door and tapped the handle with his wand. Hermioneís feeling of foreboding increased as she looked down the steep stairs, which melted into total darkness. Then Harry jogged up out of the dark, and even past his disguise, even in the half-light, she could see the panic on his face. ØCome down,Æ he whispered. ØNow.Æ ØWhat are we going to do?Æ Hermione moaned. Her eyes were wide and terrified. Draco shook his head, throat tight. He still hadnít had an epiphany about how to get the captives out of the house, and worse, the heavy door remained locked. Alohomora had failed to open it, as had three other unlocking spells, as had a series of cutting and blasting jinxes. They had, however, managed to pass a trayís worth of hors díoeuvres and two large glasses of water through the barred window, which Luna and Ollivander were in the process of wolfing down. They hadnít told the pair Dracoís identity, which could be plucked out of Lunaís or Ollivanderís heads if an escape attempt went wrong. To their knowledge, he was still Aidan March, sympathiser. However, they had shown Luna and Ollivander the slip of paper with the address of headquarters. Under the bounds of the Fidelius Charm, only Weasley could transfer the secret to a new party in speech, sign, or scriptîso even if Bellatrix used Legilimency on the captives, she wouldnít be able to find the cottage. The memory of the paper would seem obscured and hazy. ØYouíre sure you never heard them using a specific spell to get inside?Æ Potter asked Luna and Ollivander. ØNo,Æ said Luna. ØThey tap the door with their wands, thatís all.Æ ØSo itís nonverbal,Æ Harry muttered. ØWe just need to land on the right spell, then.Æ ØNo,Æ rasped a voice from inside the cell. Lunaís face disappeared from the window, and after a momentís scraping, Ollivander replaced her. He was breathing hard from the effort of standing upright, and every second or two, muscles twitched beneath his thin, wrinkled skin. ØI è believe it to be è a wand-native locking spell.Æ ØOh, Merlin,Æ Hermione whispered. ØA what?Æ said Potter. ØA wand-native locking spell,Æ Hermione said. ØItís a fairly advanced spellîit ensures that a magical barrier can only be unlocked by certain wands.Æ She hesitated, biting her lip so hard that Draco saw her lipstick transferring to her teeth. ØIn this case, the Death Eatersí wands.Æ Draco looked from Hermione to Potter. They couldnít actually be considering this. Absolutely not. ØNo,Æ he said, hating how his voice sounded high and scared. ØWe have to try,Æ Potter said. ØWe canít,Æ Draco said sharply. ØEven if we do steal a wand from the most dangerous people in this place and get the door open, what are we meant to do after that? Weíll get ourselves killed. How does that help anyone?Æ Hermione looked conflicted. ØIt does seem likely weíd all be caught,Æ she said in a near-whisper. ØWe wonít be,Æ Potter insisted. ØThey flew in some equipment on brooms. Weíll Disillusion Luna and Ollivander. Itís dark enough now that they wonít be seen in the grounds. Then we nick a few of those brooms and fly through the gate to avoid a standoff with the guards.Æ ØWeíd still need a way onto the grounds,Æ Hermione said. ØYou saw the Caterwauling Charm go off earlier. This must be the real reason that Charmís in place. è We canít sneak out through a window or side door. Weíll have to leave through the front entrance, and Disillusionment wonít fool the guards in the foyer.Æ ØWhat youíve done is very good,Æ said Luna appreciatively, looking Hermione and Harry over. ØAre you disguised too, Aidan? You donít sound as old as you look. I would never have known Muggles could do this kind of Transfiguration.Æ ØYeah,Æ Draco said, Øbut the man who did it is in London, so heís not much use.Æ But Hermione was frowning into the middle distance in that way she did when she had an idea. ØWell, they arenít checking the list of names at the manor door. So, if Luna and Ollivander look like guests, theyíll be able to walk right out, in theory.Æ Draco stared at Luna and Ollivander, their dirty, stringy hair, their torn and tattered clothes. ØHow are we supposed to make them look like guests?Æ ØThatís easy enough,Æ said Luna. She sounded unnervingly casual, now, chewing a last hors díoeuvre. ØThis house must be filled with elegant clothes. If you can find some for us, we can use your wands to cast Aguamenti and wash ourselves. Then we can get dressed and come up with you three.Æ Draco could already think of a thousand ways this could go wrong. Which Death Eaterís wand could they steal to get the door open? And what if Bellatrix had given the Greengrass Guard a description of the captives in case of an attempted escape? Could they risk Transfiguring Lunaís or Ollivanderís faces? Security were swatting Probity Probes over everyone who entered the manor, and if they passed too close under Transfiguration while exiting, theyíd surely set off an alarm. Moreover, what if the brooms had been moved since the start of the gala? Draco supposed they could try to get to his familyís broomsîtheyíd been told that gala guests were allowed, even encouraged, to enjoy the Malfoysí substantial grounds, which had been spread with nearly fifteen thousand Christmas lights in spectacular formations. But the Malfoy family brooms werenít stuffed into some garden shed. They had a collection kept under weather-controlled conditions in the gatehouse, watched by the groundskeeper, Farlough, and even if Draco had been willing to risk telling Farlough he was alive, he knew the man would never have done him a favour in a thousand years, the way Draco had always treated him. Draco felt another humiliated pang and closed his eyes, aware that every second they waited, Spizzworthís and Marcus Flint were more likely to notice their absence. Potter seemed to be thinking along the same lines. ØWe need to hurry,Æ he said in a tone that brooked no argument. ØDraîAidan, you find some dress robes from the bedrooms. They wonít let guests into the upper floors, and you know the house best. è Hermione, you and I will get a Death Eaterís wand. There are half a dozen of them here. Not Bellatrix, obviously, but Yaxley looks terribleîwe might be able to distract him. One of us can do the spilling trick again, and the other can pick his pocket.Æ No, Draco wanted to say. Itís too dangerous è for Merlinís sake, itís barely even a plan. He glanced back at the window where Luna and Ollivander were watching, though, and then at Hermione, straight-backed and resolved now. He found that he couldnít make himself say the words. But neither could he look away from Hermione, from the rise and fall of her shoulders, from her rapid, nervous blink. The fear heíd felt in headquarters slammed into him afresh, a kind of acceleration, the sensation of flattening himself to a broom handle in a steep dive. If Potter was caught, the Dark Lord would kill him. Probably not without some humiliation, but the Dark Lord would want it to be quick; he wouldnít want to risk Potter slipping away again. If Draco was caught, the same was probably true. But Hermione was a Muggle-born. The Death Eaters wouldnít consider her important enough to call the Dark Lord back to Britain, which meant that if they caught her, she would be Bellatrixís. If Bellatrix had enjoyed toying with Luna as thanks for the Department of Mysteries, she would unleash that tenfold on Hermione. Sheíd tear into Hermioneís mind with Legilimency, searching for information about Potter, until there was nothing left. Sheíd probably invite Yaxley and Crabbe to join in, to get revenge for their humiliation at the Ministry in September. Draco felt suddenly sick. It was all he could do to keep his voice steady. ØI think,Æ he said, trying to sound coolly rational, Øyou should take the thing we came for back to headquarters, Hermione. We should keep that safe, shouldnít we? You go first, and Potter and I can get Luna and Ollivander out alone.Æ When Hermione looked at him, her gaze bright and pained, he knew sheíd seen right through the excuse. ØIím not leaving you here,Æ she said, and the way she sounded as if she were confessing somethingîit terrified Draco even more. What if this was the last time they ever spoke? ØItís not a bad idea,Æ said Potter. ØWe canít risk losing it, Hermione.Æ ØNo,Æ she said. To Dracoís alarm, her eyes suddenly looked wet. She glanced away, blinked twice. ØNo,Æ she said again, determined now. ØWeíll need two people to get the wand. We have to do this together. Weíre all going to get out together. Now, come on.Æ She looked to Luna and Ollivander. ØWeíll be back soon,Æ she said fiercely. But as they walked up the steps, she slipped her hand into Dracoís and squeezed so hard it hurt. He squeezed back, dreading the moment he would have to let go. They parted ways at the foyer. Draco knew distraction was dangerous, but as he climbed the stairs, part of his mind followed Hermione back into the gala. Right now, were she and Potter scanning the room for Yaxley? How quickly would he know if everything went wrong? He returned to the kitchen and fetched a tray of wine, then hurried back toward the manorís centre. As he approached, he saw nobody there. He and his friends had always used this passage to pretend they were Aurors on secret missions in distant countries. Draco slipped behind the tapestry and climbed toward the third storey. If any more guards had been stationed up there, he could claim that one of the Lestranges had ordered him to leave a glass of wine upstairs. A feeble excuse, but they hadnít planned for any of this; it would be a miracle if any part of the plan hung together. Gryffindors, he thought furiously with every step. Merlin, he hadnít realised his cousin could duel like this. We found a letter from Bellatrix on the counter saying they had Luna, and to meet Travers and Selwyn in Diagon Alley for åinstructions.í They mustíve taken him then.Æ An uncomfortable silence settled over the den. It seemed none of the others could think of anything to reassure Luna, knowing what Bellatrix had done to her for little more than sport. Draco privately thought that the outlook for the dotty old man was grim. There was one lifeline to cling to, though. ØThe Death Eaters still want something from your father,Æ he said. Draco had hung back on the threshold to the hall, several paces clear of the others. When he spoke, all eight turned to look at him, and he fought the instinct to flinch. Headquarters suddenly seemed so cramped. ØThatís whatíll keep him safe,Æ he said to Luna, leaning against the wall. ØThey want him to print their stories in that magazine. So, they canít hurt him so badly that he canít go back to work.Æ ØIs that your expert opinion, Malfoy?Æ said Ginny coolly. Dracoís face flooded with heat. There it was. Confirmation that the other Weasleys, just like Ron, would only ever see a Death Eater when they looked at him. He opened his mouth, but before he could find words, Hermione said hotly, ØDracoís part of the Order, Ginny. He nearly died helping us tonight.Æ The Weasleys all looked at Hermione, startled. Ash was tangled in her hair, her lips a nearly invisible line. Ginny glanced at Potter, who nodded. ØDracoís been on our side for a while now, so èÆ He raised his brows at Ginny and the twins. ØLeave it, all right?Æ Fred, George, and Ginny exchanged dubious looks, then shrugged in reluctant unison. Potter turned back to Luna. ØI reckon heís right. As long as they need your dad, heíll be okay.Æ Luna considered. ØItís true that keeping a magazine at such high circulation is very difficult. Much more than most people would think.Æ And as she went on about The Quibblerís humble beginnings, the tension eased. Hermione gave Draco a faint but encouraging smile. The irritated heat faded from his face, and he felt stabilised again. Her words lingered in his mind. Dracoís part of the Order. It had been true for a while, he supposed, but he hadnít yet thought of it in those terms. The night had wrung him out so completely that for a while the fact just looped through Dracoís mind: Iím a member of the Order of the Phoenix. Heíd helped establish a new headquarters, helped retrieve two Horcruxes. è Heíd been part of the underground resistance before any of these five Weasleys. For months, successfully, heíd been working to undermine Lord Voldemort. Dracoís thoughts agitated, like choppy water before a storm, and for a moment he didnít understand why. Then he realised heíd thought the Dark Lordís name. He rolled the name through his mind again, testing it. Something about the battle had numbed him to it. Heíd fought against the forces of Voldemort and survived. For the first time since the start of the dark year, Draco felt a small, quiet pulse of pride. He looked around the front room at the new Order and saw lines drawn between them all. The Weasleys, suspicious and reluctant though theyíd been, had housed Draco. Heíd made the first move to rescue Luna. Luna had helped Ginny, Harry, and Hermione in the Department of Mysteries. Hermione had saved him. Heíd saved her. They were all inextricably linked. The only thing to do was wait for news about the othersîthe people who had protected Draco in the heat of battle, the way the Death Eaters never had. # SEVEN DEAD, DOZENS INJURED IN CHRISTMAS CATASTROPHE Arson Attack Leaves Malfoy Manor in Ruins A joyful Yuletide celebration disintegrated into a nightmare on Tuesday, writes Rita Skeeter, Special Correspondent, when anti-Ministry radicals staged a ruthless attack on the Ministry of Magicís First Annual Christmas Gala for the Celebration of Magical Unity. ØIt was supposed to be an evening of holiday cheer,Æ said the Office of Domestic Affairsí Algernon Wolflaw, speaking from his bed at St. Mungoís Hospital for Magical Maladies and Injuries, where he and 37 others are currently being treated for burns and injuries sustained during the attack. ØI donít understand what kind of person could do this to a party full of innocent people.Æ Readers need not wonder the same. The Daily Prophet can here reveal, based on hundreds of eyewitness accountsîincluding this reporterísîthat the attack was executed by Hermione Granger, the Mudblood who ranks at Number Two on the Ministryís Undesirables list. ØWe believe,Æ said Minister for Magic Pius Thicknesse in an address to a terrified nation early Wednesday morning, Øthat Granger was acting under the orders of Harry Potter, the disturbed individual now most widely known as the murderer of Albus Dumbledore.Æ While Granger is best known for her association with Potter, she has a history of ruthlessness in her own right, only sixteen when she cursed a fellow student at Hogwarts School with semi-permanent disfigurement. Her assault on Malfoy Manor was aided by a dozen members of the subversive organisation known as the Order of the Phoenix. (For full profiles of the attackers, see page 2.) Together, the assailants burned celebratory banners and used defenceless members of a crowd of nearly one thousand to shield themselves from the Greengrass Guard, who had been hired as event security. Aurors attempted to aid the Guard, but while extinguishing fires, evacuating guests, and reviving unconscious victims, they were unable to prevent the eveningís horrifying coup de grce: the burning of Malfoy Manor, a 17th-century dwelling of untold historical importance to Wizarding Britain. Survivors report that Granger cast the Fiendfyre curse ultimately responsible for the irreparable damage. Official estimates place the value of the destroyed home and its hundreds of historic artifacts at upward of 6.5 million Galleons. However, the greatest cost lies in the deaths of seven courageous fighters who dared challenge the attack. Three Aurors and four members of the Greengrass Guard, ranging in age from 24 to 58, tragically lost their lives in the fire. Mediwizards expect the death toll to climb, as nine other victims remain in critical condition at St. Mungoís. Most of the dead are survived by children and spouses. (For full obituaries, see page 4.) While Granger and four accomplices escaped the scene, readers will breathe more easily to know that eight others were captured and interrogated under Veritaserum, to partial success. Some of the attackers had placed Memory Charms upon each other prior to their capture, presumably concealing dangerous plots yet to come. Aurors suspect this was the doing of attacker Sturgis Podmore, a veteran of decades with the Obliviation Squads. However, experts in Legilimency and memory cracking are scheduled to interrogate the assailants over the coming weeks. The attack has also exposed a shocking web of deceit within one of the oldest pure-blood families in the country. Molly, Percy, and Arthur Weasley number among the captured, the latter two of whom are former Ministry employees. When Aurors called on the Weasleysí relations within an hour of the incident, they found that the family had fled, doubtless already aware of their relativesí heinous plans. Now wanted for immediate questioningîand believed to be dangerousîare: Muriel Weasley, former Mudblood-rights activist; Bill and Fleur Weasley, known goblin group affiliates; Ginevra and Ronald Weasley, the latter of whom was last seen highly contagious with spattergroit; and joke shop owners Fred and George Weasley (for an investigation into the unknown sources of Mr. and Mr. Weasleyís funding, see pg. 12). The Auror Office urges the public to Stun any of these individuals on sight and contact the authorities immediately. The Wizengamot voted unanimously in a night-time session, hours after the attack, to convict all but one of the accused on seven counts of accessory to murder, 38 counts of magical injury, one count of aggravated arson, and associated charges of premeditated assault. Each of these convicts earned multiple life sentences in Azkaban. The remaining defendantîMs. Pansy Parkinson, 17îwas convicted of the more minor charge of accessory to mayhem, and is to serve six months, due to a combination of factors: her young age, accounts that her only aid to the attackers was a water conjuration spell, and an apparently deranged mental state. ØYoung pure-bloods like Ms. Parkinson must band together in times like these,Æ advised the Minister at a press conference following his speech, Øto keep from going astray. As we know, Mudbloods have been manipulating pure-bloods for centuries to position themselves as legitimate users of magic. If tonight shows us one thing, itís that Potter and his ilk will manipulate, slaughter, and stop at nothing to destroy the society we hold dear.Æ As the press conference turned to the subject of Harry Potter, the Minister went on to reveal information more shocking, perhaps, than any other. As has been covered extensively in these pages, Potter broke into the Department of Mysteries two years ago, where he destroyed crucial information about his past in the form of a Prophecy. This lost Prophecy was also sought by the wizard known as He-Who-Must-Not.Be-Named. Famous for his prodigious magical ability, He-Who-Must-Not-Be-Named was also responsible for scattered attacks decades agoîthough none so destructive or senseless as Tuesdayís events. Ministry archivists, the Daily Prophet can now report, have discovered that the lost Prophecy did not concern the downfall of He-Who-Must-Not-Be-Named, as was assumed for years. Rather, it foretold the rise of a terrifying new power who would come of age to bring about the destruction of the Wizarding World. Experts now fear that Harry James Potter may be the subject of this doomsday forecast. As such, they suspect that He-Who-Must-Not-Be-Named wished to show the Prophecy to the public to clear his name, proving why he tried to kill the madman once so affectionately nicknamed åThe Boy Who Lived.í ØCertainly,Æ the Minister said in a concluding statement, Øthe days of the First Wizarding War were troubled, and You-Know-Whoís methods were rightfully seen as extreme. However, given researchersí recent discoveries of the insidious nature of Mudbloods, we must consider the possibility that You-Know-Who acted out of protectiveness for the future of magic itself. Now, in the wake of Potterís horrifying attack on hundreds of innocents, the contents of the Prophecy seem more and more foreboding. The Ministry, and Wizarding Britain at large, must re-evaluate what always seemed so certain about You-Know-Whoís role in these famous events.Æ The Minister for Magic left the stage shortly thereafter, assuring reporters that we would remain apprised of the situation, but questions abound in the wake of this bombshell. Could it be that, for decades, the Wizarding World has shunned and feared its only chance at survival? Could it be that He-Who-Must-Not-Be-Named is destined to defeat the Boy Who Lived, ensuring the end of attacks like tonightís? Only time will tell, but for now, the Wizarding World must mourn its losses and pray that none of the victims in St. Mungoís succumb to their injuries. Well-wishers may find addresses to owl cards and sympathies on pg. 11. (For full transcripts of the Minister for Magicís speech and press conference, see pgs. 6-7. For never-before-seen coverage of He-Who-Must-Not-Be-Namedís ground-breaking research in Experimental Transfiguration in the 1960s, see pgs. 8-9.) It was a mark of the severity of the situation that no one gathered in the front room made any sound of protest during Hermioneís reading of the article. Once her bedroom door was shut, however, he realised what this meant. All through December, despite their late-night hours together, theyíd never actually slept in the same bed. Their eyes met for a furtive moment, and even in his exhaustion Draco felt himself tense. Then they both busied themselves, Draco taking his wand from his pocket to set it on the bedside table and Hermione pulling the bedcovers back. They extinguished the lamps and settled into bed with a creak of the old springs. They were both already in pyjamas, soft cheap ones bought from a Muggle department store in a nearby town. The moon glowed between the blinds, and they pulled the covers up, facing each other in the darkness, a foot or so apart. After a moment, their warmth mingled beneath the quilt, and Draco felt himself relax for the first time in what felt like a week. Hermione was all shadow, her hair splayed across the ivory pillowcase like loose cotton dyed dark. ØI have to tell you something,Æ she whispered. ØIím sorry I didnít earlier. ØThey spelled a Muggle businessman into believing they were best friends of his who were tragically bankrupted. Heís rented them a townhouse in one of the most expensive parts of the city and è and waits on them. Does whatever they ask.Æ Draco looked blankly back at her. An unfamiliar sensation was pulling downward at his insides, making the back of his neck feel hot. Heíd never in his life felt embarrassed of his parents. He didnít even really know how to feel it. They would enjoy describing what they wanted and watching him bend over backward, dull-eyed, to acquiesce. In their state of exile, it would comfort them to use a Muggle that way, to know that no matter how low theyíd fallen, they still hovered miles above the Muggle masses. Look at that, Lucius, he could practically hear his mother saying, cool and clear and light. ØJust è we used to have picnics there when I was six or seven.Æ ØOh.Æ Her gaze softened. ØWas it nice?Æ ØYeah. Those slow ones the size of Bludgers.Æ Hermione didnít quite manage the smile, and her expression was a bit too knowing. Of course she could intuit the collisions that were taking place inside himîthe golden childhood memories grating like a striker against the flint of what he felt now. His father, who had laughed and clapped when a six-year-old Draco caught that comically large Snitch, would have slaughtered the girl across from him in the Department of Mysteries. His mother, who had lovingly packaged gifts to him every week for his first two years at Hogwarts, had called Hermione scum last year in Madam Malkinís. Dracoís embarrassment deepened into bitter shame. For seventeen years, heíd wanted nothing more than to emulate his parents: to be as poised as his mother, as influential as his father. Heíd always striven to make them proud, and for the most part heíd succeeded, but now he wondered what theyíd prided in him. All along, had they really been proud of what theyíd shaped, like an artist to a sculptureîproud that theyíd done such a good job of raising a pure-blood with pure-blood ideals? Looking back, he could see how meticulously theyíd planned his childhood so that he never interacted with Muggles. Heíd never even had half-blood friends growing up. By age eight he was already talking about how only real wizards should be allowed to have wands, how witchcraft really belonged to certain families when you thought about it. Everything about him pruned perfectly into place. Draco wondered if they would still be proud of him now that he was a blood traitor. He wondered if they would even love him. In this moment it certainly seemed theyíd never trusted him, not reallyînot in the way Hermione or even Potter had. Hermione and Potter had trusted him to ask his own questions, to find his own wayîand this was the way heíd found. He thought of the manor burning away, exposing its dark skeleton, withering piece by piece into the night. ØYou donít want to bring them here,Æ Hermione said quietly. Dracoís answer stuck in his throat. Of course I do, he wanted to say. Theyíre my parents. He found he couldnít speak the words. She was right. He dreaded their arrival. Merlin, he thought, disgusted with himself. He hadnít seen his parents for nearly half a year, and he was dreading taking them to a safe haven? No matter his feelings of anger or resentment, he knew they would have done anything for him. They would have died for him. What kind of a son was he? But there was so much he wasnít ready for them to know: the role heíd played in the manorís burning, his participation in the Order, and most of all, this. The jump in his chest when he trailed his fingertips along Hermione's collarbone. They held each otherís eyes, and he knew she was thinking the same thing. ØWhen they get here,Æ she said quietly, Øwell è obviously they wonít like the idea of us being èÆ She let out a slow breath. ØIt might be easier to stop.Æ ØEasier,Æ Draco repeated, not understanding. Since when did Hermione Granger care what was easy? She cared what was right, she cared what was just, she cared herself into knots and never stopped to worry about exertion. Then he recognised the guarded look on her face: the same self-preservation that had led her to wall him off in November. This wasnít about ease at all. Their legs tangled, and she fit so perfectly to him, and Dracoís heart was beating harder; he was realising that he didnít know what he would have done if sheíd demanded they end this. He couldnít go back to the way things had been before. It had taken so much for him to get here, to feel the way he felt right nowînot just the elation but all the pain and confusion of it. He took no pleasure in feeling ashamed of his parents, and the crush of guilt at the manor still lapped at the edges of his mind even now, and he held Hermione so tightly because there was so much he wanted to say, so much that he still couldnít articulate, a build-up that felt halfway to asphyxiation. But heíd struggled and fought to feel these things. He couldnít retreat down this pathînot now, when he was finally starting to feel that he knew the way. # Bellatrix set the Daily Prophet upon an end table and gazed into the fire. She was alone in the Lestrange home. Rodolphus had gone to St. Mungoís; he had congenitally weak lungs, and the smoke had weakened them further. Another inconvenience. All in all, however, Bella remained pleased with the dayís events. The burning of the manor and the escape of the Mudblood girl had enraged her at first, and Bella privately suspected that one of her accomplices had been Potter in disguise è but the Dark Lord didnít need to know that particular suspicion. With some time to mull over what had happened, she realised it was all to the Dark Lordís gain, in the end. Presumably the Mudblood and her accomplices had broken into the manor to free the wandmaker and the Lovegood girl. Neither hostage was such a great loss. The Dark Lord had taken what heíd needed from the wandmaker, and the Lovegood man remained to do their bidding, after Selwyn and Traversís persuasion. YesîCrabbe's idiot boy, the one who had burned the manor and killed those people, had given them a gift. Soon enough he would be able to step out into the sunlight, both feared and awed, like a god. Once Potter was dead and the nation in hand, the Wizarding World would finally see the glory of the new age. With the Dark Lord at its helm and she, his most loyal servant, ready to execute his every plan, Wizardkind could finally unite together in the next, greater mission: the conquest of Muggles. Bella had been basking in satisfaction all day. Still, he had infiltrated Hogwarts in a way that no other Death Eater had managed. Bella could always appreciate ingenuity in the Dark Lordís service. The suspicion felt like madness. She had seen the body herself at the funeral. If, by some freak occurrence, her nephew was alive, his deception would tarnish her by association. Rage reared in her. Her blood was pure, her intent was pure, and yet she had already lost one sister as a blood traitor. How was she meant to stand the shame of another betrayal within her immediate family? She knew the Dark Lord would judge her for the faults of her bloodline. So would they all. è No, better to keep her suspicions to herself, to investigate them alone. Bellatrix stood, and with a whirl of her cloak, she Disapparated. Now she was walking through a graveyard, crunching down a path of pebbles that shone in the starlight. No need to worry, she told herself. The Parkinson girlís memory was affected by her feelings, that was all. She had convinced herself of a pretty lie. Yet Bellaís footsteps quickened as she approached the Malfoysí grey marble crypt. She entered. The small chamber was immaculately clean. The prowling dragons and chimaeras cut into the high ceiling shone as if they had just been cut from their blocks of marble. The floor glimmered underfoot as Bella moved to the farthest corner. DRACO LUCIUS MALFOY Beloved son Born 5 June 1980 Died 30 June 1997 Bella flicked her wand, and a fissure cracked the centre of the marble faade. The hush and suck formed to the arches of the grey-dark hallways, a hollow, inescapable echo. Sometimes, even hundreds of miles away from the place, Bella could still hear the ocean shattering itself against the rocks. Many of her fellow prisoners had tried to break themselves on the rocks the same way, trying for the freedom of death, but never Bella. When there had been nothing else, she had clutched to the knowledge that the Dark Lord would rise once more. ØThe new prisoners?Æ she demanded of a Dementor that drifted close to a nearby cell. It turned toward her with a wash of cold, but she had lived so much of her life in that icy emptiness that she did not even shudder. The Dementor led her to the uppermost floorîto Podmore. ØLeave us,Æ Bella commanded, and the creature floated out, though not before inhaling a deep, rattling breath of her excitementîfor she was excited now, her hand playing over her wand. Podmore lay curled in the corner, the half-blood scum who had caused so much trouble with his Obliviation. He was still smeared with ash, his broad face defiant. Bella looked forward to seeing that defiance melt away. He croaked, ØI donít know anythîÆ ØLegilimens,Æ Bella hissed. Podmoreís mind thundered over her, the immeasurable volume of memory and thought, the manís pathetic worries about his Mudblood friends, the fear that his parentsîthe mother a Mudblood herselfîhad been killed. Soon enough Bella was stable in his memory, treading water, able to navigate. She summoned memories of the Malfoys to herself. They passed through her in cold currents like ghosts, hundreds of snippets of conversation: è seen Lucius Malfoy stalking around the place? He made one tiny, stifled sound. ØOh, are we frightened?Æ she said with a wide smile, stooping to his side. Sheíd immobilised him with a favourite curse of hers, not quite the Full Body-Bind. She liked to see the twitching, to hear the sounds that ground out from between their teeth. She liked proof. She met the young Weasleyís blue eyes, which were blank with abject terror. He wore a pair of horn-rimmed glasses, cracked in one lens and twisted in the frame. She enjoyed the way he recoiled from her. Muffled yells were coming from the parents now, blunted by their gags. Bella looked up at them. Sheíd conjured posts to which sheíd bound them, and no matter how they bucked against their ropes, they would not get loose. Even in this stateîlaid low as they should beî she was repulsed by them. The waste, she wanted to howl sometimes, the waste! The pride they could have had in a line of pure blood as ancient as her own, and they rolled around in the slop with Muggles. ØThis need not be painful,Æ Bella said. She did not speak loudly, but they stopped making their pathetic sounds at once. ØI know that you know what I seek,Æ she went on. ØI know that you remember information about the Malfoys.Æ Both Weasleys went rigid. It was practically a confession. Bella rose to her feet. ØI wish,Æ she whispered, Øto speak to my traitorous sister and her treacherous worm of a husband.Æ She circled toward the posts. With a flick of her wand, she Vanished the gags from the Weasleysí mouths, but they seemed too stunned to speak. Anger was pulsing through Bella now. Back there, in the graveyard, it had taken every ounce of her restraint not to blast the Malfoysí sepulchre to pieces. The idea that Snape might control the so-called unbeatable wand è only now had Hermione begun to feel as if they might surmount the obstacles before them, and now this new threat loomed like a thunderhead. But then Hermione remembered something: a movement in the dark è a piece of wood slipping from papery fingers. ØDumbledore is buried on Hogwarts grounds. Snape could stroll out any time andîÆ ØI didnít bring Dumbledoreís wand back with his body,Æ Hermione said. ØIt fell out of his hand in mid-air when he fell off the Thestral. We were thousands of feet up. It probably shattered on impact. There may not even be an Elder Wand anymore!Æ Although neither Harry nor Draco looked entirely reassured, their faces had, at least, reanimated. Colour was returning to Dracoís cheeks. ØIt could be broken,Æ he said slowly. ØIf it really is such a valuable wand, though, itíll have been treated over the years with Resilience Potions. They do that with family wands, historic wands, that sort of thing.Æ Hermione considered this. ØWell, even if the wand did survive the fall, how is Snape supposed to find it? It was the middle of the night, so he couldnít have seen any landmarks. And,Æ she added triumphantly, ØMad-Eye only assigned us our flying directions when we got to the Dursleysí. So, we know that Dumbledore and I were headed west-northwest on that Thestral, but Snape doesnít even know that much. All he knows is that we were somewhere within a fifty-mile radius of Number Four, Privet Drive.Æ ØStill,Æ Harry said, Øheís had six weeks to look for it already. A wand that can win any fight è Snape would keep going until he found it, no matter how long it took.Æ ØThen we have to find it before he does,Æ Draco said. ØMerlin knows how.Æ Hermione went very still. ØWe already know how,Æ she breathed. ØWhat?Æ said Draco. ØWe do?Æ said Harry. A smile spread across Hermioneís face. She tugged her beaded bag out of her pocket and stuck her arm into it, fishing through the detritus of months on the run. ØCeline Shih,Æ she said. ØCeline èÆ Harry blinked. ØWhat,Æ Draco said, Øthat woman from the Scavengersí Guild?Æ Hermione kept rummaging. ØItís a spell I developed,Æ she recited. ØYou start on a broom, and narrow down on a trace of magic. è In barren areas, Muggle areas, it finds a breadcrumb and leads you down the trail. è Oh, Merlinís pantsîAccio!Æ Out from the depths of the bag, into her waiting hand, flew the piece of parchment that sheíd placed into it three months ago, in Diagon Alley. She unfolded the parchment, upon which the Scavenger-in-Chief had written a set of instructions with a peahen feather quill. Narcissa woke early on Sunday morning. All week, she and Lucius had debated going to Halfhold Hill. With the Weasleys in Azkaban, they knew it was a risk. But they also knew that the Order of the Phoenix had been in contact at the burning of the Manor, and if any of them had been able to pass their message to Draco è if there was any chance at all that they might reunite with him è ØWeíll go early,Æ she murmured to Lucius that morning in bed, before the Muggle had made their breakfast. ØWeíll set protective enchantments.Æ Sheíd added caveat after caveat until finally she whispered, ØDraco è Draco.Æ Then, at last, her husband had nodded. Narcissa had pressed her lips to his cheek, to the alabaster skin that was only now starting to regain its glow after his year in Azkaban. They arrived at Halfhold Hill an hour and a half early to set the enchantments, when the moon was still high. It wasnít early enough. They had hardly appeared at the apex of the hill when Narcissa felt herself flung back against the tree that grew there, bound in place, her wand jerked from her hand. She tasted blood, tried to grab for her wand, and found herself immobilised. ØAlways early to engagements, werenít we, Cissy?Æ breathed a voice at Narcissaís shoulder, and when her vision stabilised, Bellatrixís Disillusionment was fading. Fear shot through Narcissa's blood. Her sister was dressed, as always, as if for an evening event, in magnificent black robes. She had always had the dramatic good looks, Bella, and when she gave Narcissa that theatrical pout, her long lashes lowered over her dark eyes, Narcissa could almost see her older sister as sheíd been at Hogwarts: the life of the Slytherin table, viciously protective of her and Andromeda. Neither of them had ever been as vivacious, as confident, as naturally talented as the eldest Black sister. ØWell, this is a shame,Æ Bella said, spinning Luciusís wand in one hand and Narcissaís in the other. Ø8 oíclock isnít for so long yet è weíll have to wait a long while for dear Draco to join us. Whatever will we do to entertain ourselves?Æ She drew her own wand again, that blackthorn instrument that had felled so many. It gleamed in the moonlight like a blade. ØBellatrix,Æ Lucius said. ØNo. NîÆ But Bella had already placed her wand to Luciusís chest. ØBella!Æ Still he twitched and jerked beside her, Lucius, helpless for a year, already shamed and humiliated for so longîØYour own family?Æ Narcissa screamed. The curse broke. ØFamily?Æ Bella hissed, and now her nose was two inches from Narcissaís, dark eyes filled with hatred that Narcissa had never seen directed at her. For the first time in her life, she saw her sister as the rest of the world must. ØYou are no family of mine!Æ Bella spat. ØYou knew what this would do to meîthis betrayal è when we lost Andromeda, I thought Iíd had the worst of it!Æ Bella let out a mad, wretched sound that was half-laugh, half-sob, and Narcissa remembered Bella lying on her bed as they listened to Andromeda expelled from their home for the last time. Bella, who never wept, whose job it was to be strong always, forceful alwaysî Andromeda made this choice, sheíd snarled, it was her own choice. Yet her eyes had been bright with tears. ØYou turn tail and work for the Order of the Phoenix,Æ Bella tore on, Øyou leave me as if I am nothing, and now you use family against meîÆ ØWe did nothing for the Order.Æ Narcissaís voice was a harsh whisper. Lucius still trembled beside her. ØUse Legilimency on me if you must! I am telling you the truth, Bella! Draco was certain that the Dark Lord would kill us all for his failure; he did not trust that Severusís actions would save us. è So, my son accepted Albus Dumbledoreís protection for us all, but we never advanced their cause.Æ ØYour son,Æ Bella sneered, Øhas joined the Order! He battled with them at the burning of your own home! HeîÆ ØDraco has been outnumbered and surrounded for months,Æ Narcissa snapped. ØCan you be certain he was not under the Imperius Curse? You admit that you accepted the protection of the Muggle-lover Dumbledore. You feel no shame in it?Æ ØThe choice was not ours to make. Draco was barely even of age when he trusted his life to Dumbledore. ØYou were with the Order, you were with Potter, you saw Potter! Surely you could have summoned the Death Eaters to him! She had thought, when Bella agreed to cast the Unbreakable Vow in secret for her and Severus, that Bella still valued family above all else. è Had she been wrong? ØNeither Lucius nor I,Æ Narcissa whispered, Øwill invite our deaths while our son lives, Bella. Neither will we invite death within a mile of Draco. I had thought you felt the same of me.Æ Bellaís fury quavered like a plucked string as pain glanced across her face. Her cheeks were patched with red. ØWe can give the Dark Lord the boy,Æ Lucius rasped. His body had finally stopped twitching, although his facial muscles gave a violent spasm with every few words. Bellatrix slowly turned her eyes to Lucius. She did not react. ØBellatrix,Æ he went on, with a hint of his old charm, Øwe know that of the two of us, you are the formidable fighter è we also know I am the strategist. She gave a haughty sniff. ØOur son disappeared in July with Potter,Æ Lucius went on, Øand the Weasley brat, and that Mudblood girl. If he was seen at the manor with the Mudblood, it stands to reason he must be with Potter.Æ ØI know that,Æ Bella snapped. ØWe know where the Order has set their hidey-hole, and yes, your son has been staying there with the worst kind of filth wizardkind has to offer. We will be shunned, ignoredîand so we will be privy to every scrap of information that that pack of lowbloods and half-breeds has to offer. Draco may even have succeeded in gaining Potterís trust over the past several months, which will make luring the boy out from their headquarters exceptionally easy. Potter need only make one misstep, Apparate to one vulnerable location è Draco can assist us in laying this trap.Æ Narcissa did not look at her husband. She didnít know how much of this he was saying in earnest, but while Narcissa knew Bella, the sister, Lucius knew Bellatrix, the lieutenant. They spoke different languages, and Narcissa could see that Luciusís plan intrigued Bellatrix. ØYou have not told the Dark Lord we live, of course,Æ Lucius said smoothly. ØI would do the same myself è we seem debased, I admit. Blood traitor would be too kind a term.Æ A tiny noise that might have been the start of a laugh was snapped off from Bellaís lips, half-formed. ØBut think of what would best serve the Dark Lord.Æ Luciusís voice showed a hint of urgency. ØWould you kill us and throw away this opportunity, Bella? è This chance to plunge not one but three spies into the midst of the Order? Together our family can destroy their last efforts, and give him Potterîand when we do, our ruse will be forgiven as if it were nothing. We will be lifted even above Severus in his esteem.Æ Lucius paused. ØWe will be no shame to you, if you will let us help you.Æ For the first time since they had appeared on the hill, Bellaís face was near calmîher ever-roving eyes the exception. Narcissa could see a hint of longing in her face. She knew that Bella had desired for years to be the one to hand the Potter boy to the Dark Lord. ØYou would be ideally positioned,Æ Bella murmured, Øto relate the Orderís plans to the Death Eaters è yes, to draw Potter into the open è surely he is Secret-Keeper?
