Mirrors, perhaps, like the ones èÆ She trailed off, not wanting to mention Sirius or Andromeda, which might inflame Bellaís temper again. Andromeda had been close to their cousin in their youth; the pair had used two-way mirrors to speak over summers. Perhaps it was this private connection that had started them both down the regrettable roads theyíd walked. After a long, silent moment of consideration, Bella Disapparated. When she reappeared fifteen minutes later, she held a pair of those mirrors, small and tarnished. Perhaps they were even the same ones that Andromeda and Sirius had shared. Finally, Bella flicked her wand, and the ropes that bound the Malfoys to the tree disappeared. Narcissaís knees buckled. She took a deep, full breath until her ribs ached. Then she arranged her fine blonde hair over her shoulders and considered Bella with the twin mirrors in her hands. Narcissa was not nave about her sisterís nature. She knew that Bella was ruthless and violent, that she had killed many in the Dark Lordís service. Still, before this morning, Narcissa would never have thought her sister capable of doing violence to her family, to their own sacred blood. Now she saw that Azkaban had rebuilt Bella from heart to flesh to mind. The ferociously loyal sister of their youth was still there, but she had undergone alchemy, she was a new substance. Her loyalty was now to one thing only. Narcissa knew that if she betrayed Bella in this, her sister would stop at nothing until she murdered Draco, until she murdered Lucius, until she finally killed Narcissa. She knew it to her core, knew it down to the order of the deaths. That would be how Bellatrix carried them out, to make sure Narcissa felt the most agony. The knowledge seemed to change the world around Narcissa, to dull the sun and sky, to allow the winter cold to pierce all the way into her bones. I am the only Black sister left, Narcissa thought distantly. The others had frayed away from the tapestry into their own universes. Perhaps the old world was already gone. Perhaps to try and hold to it was madness. Narcissa felt thin and weary. She wanted to crawl back into bed. Only the thought of Draco kept her posture rigid, as she had learned to hold herself. She would do what must be done for their survival. She extended a hand to her sister, and Bella pressed the mirror into her palm. Narcissa wrapped her fingers over the cold metal, accepting her duty. For a moment neither let go. ØIf you betray me,Æ Bella whispered. ØI know,Æ Narcissa said. Chapter End Notes this world is just full of healthy familial dynamics huh tumblr | instagram Chapter21: A Meeting With theMalfoys Chapter Notes deadline is conquered and so WE ARE BACK! please remember that projected update times can always be found at my tumblr, url batmansymbol, if you click on the left sidebar where it says "disappearances updates!" i have also added chapter titles. we can have alliterative chapter titles, as a treat See the end of the chapter for more notes Draco lay awake for a long time on Sunday morning. All was quiet. At this moment there was no sign that the sleepy Potter Cottage was the epicentre of the nationís rebellion against Lord Voldemort. Headquarters was blanketed in the deep hush of dawn about to come, the only sound Hermioneís deep breathing. Draco watched her frown in sleep, as though even now she were solving a conundrum. He had realised over the past few days how much he enjoyed watching Hermione, at rest or in motion, directing the others or chewing a quill in thought. Even more, he relished the moments she seemed to remember him out of nowhere, pausing mid-step and scanning the room until her eyes caught his. Draco had watched Hermione Granger hurrying through the halls of Hogwarts for six years, and yet somehowîall that timeîheíd failed to see her at all. Now he didnít want to blink, lest he miss a second. Dawn was rising outside, however, and it was nearly time for him to go to Halfhold Hill. In the hubbub of meeting preparations, Draco had managed to suppress his dread for the past few days, but now, looking at Hermioneís sleeping face, a new fear struck. What if his parentsí presence at headquarters changed her feelings toward him? Surely, with his mother and father living alongside Hagrid, Hermione would think more and more about third year. Draco remembered himself in the hospital wing that year, clutching his perfectly healed arm, owling home to say that the injury should be grounds for firing the blundering halfwit responsible. He rose to his feet and began to dress, suddenly feeling the need to put distance between them. A wave of frustration was cresting over him. His parents had been strict when they wanted. They could have sent Draco a return owl telling him to heal quickly and get back to his schoolwork soon è but no. Theyíd seen an opportunity to cleanse Hogwarts of Hagrid. How many times had his father referred to the gamekeeper as a disgrace to the school, a savage, Dumbledoreís pet barbarian? How many times had Draco done the same? As Draco fastened his winter cloak, he tried to clear his mind, but since the Manor, the thoughts had grown inescapable. He had even begun to dream of things heíd said and done before this year, which replayed over and over in his subconscious so vividly that he awoke feeling unrested. Every single action bore undercurrents of discomfort. When he brushed his teeth, he remembered Hermioneís front teeth growing painfully long, her eyes filling with tears as he and Crabbe and Goyle were wracked by paroxysms of laughter. When he ate, he thought of her furious words in the Room of Hidden Things when sheíd demanded he answer for Dobby, beaten near-daily while cooking his meals and cleaning his clothes. Draco told himself the endless rumination was idiotic, that it was functionally useless, thatî in Dobbyís case, at leastîhe had been too young to really understand è but it went on and on. It made him want to push Hermione away, to keep her from being contaminated. It made him want to cling to her, terrified that she would come to her senses and go. Dracoís eyes slipped to the clock on the wall. It was time. He let himself watch Hermione for one last moment. She was stirring, now, in her cotton pyjamas. He Disapparated. Halfhold Hill was freezing and windswept, the sky a tortured purple. Draco looked around, and for a single moment he thought he was alone, that this was another false trail. Then two figures came out from behind the single massive tree whose roots were sunk deep into the hill. His father, tall and still too thin from Azkaban, hair on the silver side of blonde. His mother, her cheeks chapped red by the wind. The Malfoys were not an expressive family. But at that moment, Draco's parents were gazing at him as if they had seen an apparition, their faces betraying love and pain and relief. In that instant, Dracoís fears were overwhelmed by another feeling that bloomed in his chest. He remembered the feeling of pride heíd felt all his lifeîpride that he was a Malfoy. Although he had changed, he was still a Malfoy. He always would be. Malfoys were subtle and assured, resilient and resourceful. Malfoys glided over every obstacle toward each other. ØMother,Æ Draco greeted in a thin, somewhat shaky voice. ØFather.Æ Wordless, they strode forward and embraced him. # ØNo need to be nervous, Hermione,Æ Fred said airily, gesturing with his fork. ØNone whatsoever,Æ George said. ØIf those pasty gits say a word to youîÆ Øîweíll collapse their tent while theyíre sleeping,Æ Fred finished through a mouthful of toast. Hermione tried to smile. ØThatís not what Iím nervous about. Iím perfectly able to handle whatever they say to me.Æ ØThen why are you worried?Æ Luna said with a curious tilt of her head. Buying time, Hermione chewed a mouthful of eggs for far too long, but Professor McGonagall spoke first. ØI will admit,Æ said McGonagall with lips pursed, perched on the edge of her armchair, ØIíve had a number of security concerns.Æ ØY-yes,Æ Hermione said, setting her plate on the coffee table. ØYes, but I think our precautions should hold. Confiscating the Malfoysí wands, that is." She cleared her throat so her voice would fill the cottageís front room. ØAnd remember, everyone, weíre keeping details of our plans with our smaller teams, so that nobody knows all the dates and specifics. Thatíll be safer ifîif anything goes wrong.Æ Amid murmurs of assent, Hermione glanced at the clock. ØTwo minutes. Draco said theyíd be here at half-nine.Æ The front room dissolved into a tense hum of discussion. As Luna and Bill shuttled dirty plates to the kitchen, Hermione pretended to look through a calendar of the next month, which she had already checked four times that morning. She hadnít lied. She wasnít worried about what Lucius or Narcissa Malfoy might say to her. But she couldnít help worrying about their influence over Draco. He had already been tense over the last week, with ten new people to contend with at Headquarters, none of whom were making any effort to pretend they liked him. Who was to say that with his parents in the mix, exuding the same mixture of prejudice and superiority theyíd fed him his entire life, he wouldnít slip back into certain old habits? When Hermione considered the past few months, spent under such tightly controlled conditions, learning to trust each other with their lives è it all suddenly seemed so delicate, exposed to this many new variables at once. Sheíd discussed it with Draco last night at bedtime, but the conversation had felt strained. ØAre you ready to see them?Æ she murmured. ØMostly.Æ Heíd half-smiled. ØBut I doubt Iíll be getting any Son of the Year awards, Granger, having just burned down our house.Æ Sheíd managed a cross between a laugh and a wince. She remembered how heíd looked after Disapparating from the wreck of his home, stricken, ash caught in his brows like dark clusters of snow. ØIím sorry about the manor,Æ sheíd whispered under the covers, realising that in the panic after the battle, she had never said the words. ØYou didnít cast the Fiendfyre,Æ Draco had said. ØI know, of course I didnít. But if Iíd only gotten away from Flint a minute faster èÆ ØYou have nothing to be sorry for.Æ A muscle had worked in his jaw. ØYou donít è you never have to say that to me.Æ Hermione had hesitated, unsure how to reply. Dracoís voice had been strained, but not in the way sheíd expected, with anger or grief about his lost home. He held her gaze like someone forcing himself to look into bright light, the fine muscles around his eyes contracting. Then heíd kissed her and said, ØI need sleep,Æ and that had been the end of it. Now Hermione shuffled her notes again and wished sheíd pushed the subject. They had never even properly defined their relationship. Draco was not her boyfriend; even the term seemed ridiculous amid the war, and yet, faced with the prospect that he might pull away, she found herself wishing that theyíd made some guarantee to each other. What if all this was only a minor detour in the path of his life? It isnít, she thought, glaring down at the calendar with hard resolve. Left to his own devices, this was the way Draco had chosen. This was who he really was. She believed that. Hermioneís head shot up as three muffled CRACK sounds emanated from outside. Then the door was opening, and a hush fell over the Order as Draco entered, followed by Lucius and Narcissa Malfoy. Mr. and Mrs. Malfoy looked well-fed and well-coiffed, but of course they did, having treated a Muggle as a servant since theyíd lived in London. Hermione felt a rush of dislike, and she saw similar reactions from the rest of the Order. McGonagallís eyes were frost, and Hagridís enormous hands clenched into fists as he glared down the man whoíd tried to have Buckbeak executed. Ginnyís mouth was a hard line. ØMr. Malfoy,Æ Harry said shortly but not rudely, stepping to the front of the group. ØMrs. Malfoy. Has Draco told you the conditions of staying here?Æ Lucius Malfoyís lip curled ever so slightly. ØYes, Potter.Æ A shiver ran down Hermioneís spine. Even after all this time, she could still hear the smooth, satisfied voice echoing behind the mask at the Department of Mysteries. Unspeaking, Draco held up his motherís and fatherís confiscated wands. Harry nodded. ØWeíll keep your wands hidden and safe. In the event of an emergency, weíll return them to you straightaway.Æ ØVery reassuring,Æ said Narcissa Malfoy in her cool, haughty tones. Harryís expression tightened. ØLook, we donít expect you to help us, but Professor Dumbledore promised the Order would keep you safe. Weíll keep that promise.Æ After a pause, the Malfoys lowered their heads in slight, disdainful nods. ØYouíll be staying in our new tent,Æ Fred said, sounding as if he would rather have burned the tent than have it suffer such a fate. ØItís kitted out with its own kitchen,Æ George added gruffly, Øso, you can eat in there.Æ Harry nodded. ØWeíll bring you groceries once a week. Or èÆ He hesitated, glancing at Hermione, then Draco. ØOr, you can eat with the rest of us. But that means doing your share of the chores, whenever and whatever Hermione assigns us.Æ Slowly, finally, the Malfoysí eyes moved onto her. Their faces twisted as if theyíd been asked to lean in and smell a clogged drain. Hermioneís back straightened. She could feel her face flooding with defiant heat, her thoughts clouding with the beginnings of anger. ØHave you mistaken us for servants, Potter?Æ said Lucius, voice taut. ØScrubbing dishes on the orders of a MudblîÆ Hermione felt the rest of the Order surging up as one, ready to come to her defence, but they were all too slow. With one long stride, Draco cut in front of his father, blocking Hermione from view. One uneven syllable burst out: ØNo.Æ A deafening silence fell in the front room. Dracoís narrow shoulders were rising and falling. Several of the Order were staring at his back. The Malfoys, too, were looking at their son as though theyíd never seen him before. Draco smoothed back his hair. Hermione could just see the tremble at his fingertips. ØNo,Æ he repeated, breathing still unsteady. ØWe wouldnít be the only ones working, Father. Because HermioneÆîno one missed the slight stress he added to her nameîØdivides it all evenly.Æ The silence continued to ring. Hermione only realised she was biting her cheek when she tasted blood. Lucius had gone the glazed white colour of fine china. She half expected his face to twist into anger and shock, to demand if his son was Confunded or worse. But Narcissa was the first to recover. She adjusted her fur-lined cloak and said slowly, her eyes moving critically over Dracoís face, ØYes, Draco. Weíll take dinner here, then.Æ Lucius glanced at his wife. After a long moment, he dipped his head in a single nod. Hermione exchanged a startled look with Harry. Theyíd extended the olive branch for Dracoís sake, in the hope that there wouldnít be such palpable loathing in headquarters. They hadnít really expected the Malfoys to agree to spend mealtimes with the Order. As Fred and George led the Malfoys down the hall toward the back door, Draco hurried up the stairs with his parentsí wands, and Hermione felt a hint of cautious optimism. Not only had Draco stood up to his parentsîthey hadnít even turned on him for doing so. It seemed that Dracoís word was more important to them than their right to spew whatever foul prejudice they liked. Draco had told her once that his mother and father had never associated with anyone beyond the pure-blood world. Hermione had wondered, if theyíd had different childhoods, whether there would have been a chance for them to change. Now she wondered, as they disappeared out of the back exit, whether Draco could give them that chance after all. # Draco was aware of more stares from the Order than usual that day. ØYouíd think I killed someone,Æ he muttered when he and Hermione finally slipped into the reading room after lunch. ØDonít be ridiculous,Æ she told him with a smile. ØItís the opposite. You defended me to your parents. The others are starting to realise theyíve made some poor assumptions the past week.Æ As she spread out their notes, Draco made himself turn away, pretending to peruse the books in the library. He had heard the pleasure in her voiceîand yet shame coiled in his stomach. It seemed far too generous to call what heíd done a defence. It had started that way, certainly. Heíd strode in front of his parents intending to tell his father with his iciest tones never to say that word to Hermione, never even to look at her that way again. But then heíd looked into his parentsí faces and seen their alarm at his intervention. In their moment of disorientation, he hadnít wanted to embarrass or abandon them, leave them isolated in a room of people who already loathed them. But I should have done it, Draco thought, a bitter taste in his mouth. Any other Order member would have told his father never to say the word, to treat Hermione with some respect. And yet he, Draco, who alone of the Order needed to atone, had failed at the last minute. Heíd even tortured himself with the thought that Hermione might have been better off with the Weasel King after all, who at least would have made a stronger retort to his father. His parents hadnít mentioned it at lunch, of course. The meal hadnít been entirely unbearable. Heíd strategically guided his parents to sit near Fleur and Luna, who seemed to feel the least outright hatred for Lucius and Narcissa. At the end, heíd told his parents somewhat stiffly that he would be in the library for the day. But that passing fact had contained a lie by omission, too. He hadnít mentioned heíd be with Hermione and Potter in the libraryîstill hadnít admitted heíd joined the Order. Heíd made no real gestures toward the person he thought he had made himself. And if he was hiding that person, hiding his choices, didnít it invalidate them? Draco stared hard at a peeling volume of counter-jinxes. Coward, whispered a voice in the back of his mind. Soon enough Potter arrived from washing up, and Draco tried to focus as they passed the afternoon in slow frustration. They tried a long list of hexes and counter-curses to try and open Slytherinís locket, to no avail. Gryffindorís sword waited uselessly on the sofa behind them. After a few hours of this, Potter turned the locket over and over in his hands. ØDo either of you know what the locket was meant for?Æ ØMeant for?Æ Hermione repeated. ØYeah. Gryffindorís sword appears to the brave, the Diadem made you cleverer. Does the locket have a story around it the same way?Æ ØYeah,Æ Draco said, Ønot that itís much use to us now that itís a Horcrux. It was meant to hold a secret.Æ ØA secret?Æ Hermione said, looking curious. ØYou mean like a Secret-Keeper with the Fidelius Charm?Æ Draco nodded. ØSome people think Salazar Slytherin was the one to come up with the Fidelius Charm, and it was based on the locketís enchantment.Æ He shrugged. ØIf the wearer was a Slytherin, theyíd be able to put a secret inside the locket, and itíd never open, and the secret could never be repeated.Æ Potter considered the locket. ØWhat if we whispered a secret into the locket? Maybe weíve got to bribe it with information, or something.Æ But they each, in turn, took it into the corner of the library and whispered to the locket, and nothing happened. The locketís impenetrability was so discouraging that they wound up abandoning it to talk about Hufflepuffís Cup instead, although they had not a single lead. Even with no information on the cup, though, the weeks to come looked busy. As the evening approached, Hermione spread Celine Shihís instructions out and explained what they would need to do to find the Elder Wand. ØItís a spell-directed compass,Æ she said. ØIíd hoped it would be a simple spell that we could learn right away, and we could start looking for the wand now è but weíll need to steep a compass in a particular potion that stews for three weeks, and during that time, weíll need to master a rather tricky spell in order to control the compass once itís finished.Æ ØSo for three weeks, we just hope that Snape doesnít find the wand first?Æ said Potter, scrubbing his hands through his hair. ØAndîwhat, we just hope that he doesnít hurt Ron while heís at it?Æ ØPotter,Æ Draco sighed, Øwe still donít even know that Snape has Weasley, or that Snape knows heís the master of the Elder Wand.Æ Hermione gave a determined nod, although when she spoke, she sounded as if she was convincing herself. ØYes. Snape may not even know the Elder Wand exists. è Itís an arcane story.Æ As evening fell, Harry left to check on the rest of the Order, leaving Hermione and Draco alone in the library. ØOne more thing,Æ Hermione said as she slid Gryffindorís sword back into her small beaded bag. ØYeah?Æ Draco said, his stomach dropping. Her voice sounded guarded. Maybe she had been thinking about his interaction with his father. Maybe she, too, had decided it wasnít enough. ØIíve found a lead on an Unraveling Charm that I think will be able to undo the magic in your Dark Mark.Æ Dracoís mind went temporarily blank. She turned to him, face set with determination. ØI know itíll be an unpleasant process,Æ she said in a rush, Øand unfortunately weíll have to experiment, which could be risky, but I really think that with multiple Order members in Azkaban and what you said to Pansy, we should prepare for your secrecy to be compromised, and ifîÆ ØGet it off me,Æ Draco said. He was not prepared for how low and ragged his voice sounded. Hermione broke off. ØYouíre sure?Æ ØIím sure. Whatever you have to do.Æ Dracoís jaw was clenched. ØI want it off. Every time I look at it, I think of èÆ ØOf what Voldemort did,Æ she finished. Draco looked at her and felt another wrench inside him. Of what I did, he thought, but he couldnít say it. Hermione narrowed her eyes. ØYouíve been strange all afternoon. Is it your parents?Æ ØYeah.Æ She sighed. ØYes, well, we always knew it would be complicated when they arrived, didnít we?Æ She paused. ØIn some ways I thought it seemed promising.Æ ØPromising?Æ A slightly deranged laugh startled out from Draco. ØWhat about that little introduction was promising?Æ Hermione didnít laugh, just lifted her chin in a dignified way and said, ØThey listened to you.Æ Draco hesitated, the twisted non-smile falling from his features. Heíd been so focussed on his weak defence that he hadnít really thought about his parentsí reaction to it è but Hermione was right. He had never flouted his parentsí authority in public before. If he had disagreements, he made them in private, where they wouldnít tarnish the familyís image as an unshakable unit. Yet they hadnít reprimanded him for doing so. ØThatís true,Æ Draco said slowly. He and Hermione regarded each other for a long, tense moment. Draco hadnít ever let himself consider the idea that his parents might accept themîaccept this. It seemed beyond too good to be true, out of reality. And yet theyíd never spent so long in Muggle London before. Was it so impossible that they had softened, even the tiniest bit? Maybe not enough for full acceptance, or even tolerance è but enough for Draco to keep his parents in this new life? He needed to talk to them. # Dracoís mouth was dry as he stooped to enter his parentsí tent. The Weasley twinsí second purchase was as gaudy and overdecorated as the first, although this tent was, at least, spared the Gryffindor colour scheme. ØDraco,Æ said his mother with a small smile, and it washed over him againîthe surrealness of the fact that he could simply enter a tent and see them, after so many months wondering if they were even alive. His mother and father were at the head of the chiselled mahogany table, casually elegant, as if sitting for a portrait. The sight reminded Draco of the moment heíd brought his O.W.L. results to the Manorís twenty-foot banquet table to share with them. His parents had not been outright displeasedîachieving nine O.W.L.s, with four Outstanding results in Potions, Transfiguration, History of Magic, and Astronomy, was nothing to sniff at. Still, he knew they had hoped for a prodigious result, and their disappointment had radiated coldly over the table for the next week. Dracoís mouth was now so dry that his throat had begun to itch. He stepped closer to the table. As much as heíd chastised himself all day for not saying more in Hermioneís defence, right now, standing across from his parents, it felt pathetically impressive that heíd managed to say a word. His parents exchanged a look. His father gave his mother the tiniest shake of his head, as if in warning. Draco narrowed his eyes, but before he could ask what theyíd obviously been discussing before heíd arrived, Lucius said, ØCome sit, Draco.Æ His father slid a tumbler across the table with a thumb of Firewhisky in its depths. Clearly they had expected him. Clearly, too, they had no qualms about drinking from the Weasley twinsí well-stocked cabinet. Draco took the tumbler and settled into the chair at his fatherís left hand. ØThank you,Æ he said, steeling himself. ØFather, I wanted us to talk about what happened in the cottage, earlier.Æ ØThere is nothing to explain, Draco,Æ his father said smoothly. ØWe understand.Æ ØYouîyou do?Æ ØNaturally. Trapped in this place, with all this èÆ Lucius let his eyes travel toward the tent flap, distaste lifting one corner of his lip. ØOf course you need to play to their rules. We all must, for the time being. è So, we surrender our wands. We speak civilly to the half-breeds and the rest. I forgot myself.Æ ØYou have been who youíve needed to be to stay safe,Æ his mother said, laying one delicate hand over Dracoís. ØAnd youíve done well besides, Draco,Æ said his father, rising to his feet. ØYouíve stayed unharmed, youíve brought us hereîyouíve even remained close to Potter.Æ Lucius strolled to the enchanted window, which showed a dark, moonlit slope covered in snow. He took a sip of his Firewhisky. ØPerhaps you could have made more decisive moves, but under the circumstances è assimilation was the best strategy.Æ Draco didnít reply, nor move. His heart was falling with every sentence. This, then, was why theyíd accepted his interruption: his parents thought heíd been acting to fit in with the Order. A question of image. They were just as rigid, just as decided, as they had always been. When he told them the truth, he would meet every ounce of resistance he had imagined. Draco slid his hand out from beneath his motherís to raise his glass of Firewhisky to his lips. He didnít allow his hands to shake. ØThe è the Manor,Æ he managed to say. Another evasion, and the thought ran forcefully through his head again: Coward. ØWe know youíre not to blame,Æ said Narcissa at once, although her face contracted. Lucius let out a short, icy laugh. ØI suppose we shouldnít be surprised that our home was made a sacrifice to the cause. Why should Dumbledoreís followers care about sites of Wizarding history?Æ ØBut that was Crabbe,Æ Draco said quickly. ØThe Fiendfyre. The Prophet reported it differently. Skeeter wasîÆ Lucius waved Dracoís uneven explanation away with one simple gesture, his eyes still fixed coldly on the tent flap. ØIf the people in that cottage hadnít decided to enter that gala, Draco, our family would still have its ancestral seat. They are to blame. Always remember who is to blame,Æ he added confidentially to Draco with a short nodîvolumes of trust in the tiny motion. Draco closed his eyes and saw the Manor burning. So, if he revealed he had joined the Order, his parents would place the loss of their home on his shoulders after all. His stomach churned. How was he supposed to say any of it, to take their willingness to believe the best of him and rip it to shreds? But intermingled with his fears were resentment and frustration: how could they be this way, apparently unchanged after last yearís humiliations? Draco took another sip of Firewhisky. He had to start somewhere. Pragmatism: his parents were always open to what was most advantageous for the family. It was as good an initial angle as any. ØIíve been thinking, Father. Mother.Æ Draco felt his voice rise higher. ØAnd I think itís safer for our family to stay with the Order than go abroad.Æ His parents traded another one of those inscrutable looks. Then Lucius set his tumbler on a bookshelf and said, ØDraco, come. You must think more ambitiously. è Think of the best option. True, our place in society is not abroad, but nor is itîÆ Lucius waved a hand around at the tent, contempt hardening his narrow face. ØHere. You may have some superficial comforts with the Order, but it is the Dark Lordís vision that has always been greatest and most comprehensive.Æ Draco looked into the grey eyes so like his own, not understanding. His father was speaking as if the last year and a half hadnít happened. As if, somehow, he expected them to join with Voldemort again. ØWait,Æ said Draco, trying to conceal his rising suspicion. ØIn summer, we were talking about leaving the country. America, New Zealand. We thought that was our only chance.Æ ØCircumstances change, Draco. We mustnít settle for a lesser option, whenîÆ ØBut itís better here than it was at the Manor,Æ Draco said loudly. His hands were clasped tight around his Firewhisky. His heart beat hard in his ears. What was it about speaking to his parents that made him sound like such a child? ØLast year was a nightmare, we all know it. We canít to go back to that. Everything was loads better for our family when the whole country still thought he was dead.Æ Lucius blanched, and Narcissa twitched. His motherís eyes even flicked to the sideîthat old habit, prepared for someone to overhear their disloyalty. Then Lucius said, with the air of a politician making a passionate speech, ØDraco, you have never seen the Dark Lordís world at its best. Youíve seen only the turmoil of the transition. Before you were born, we were free in a way you cannot even imagine. Pure-bloods were respected in the way we deserved, not ground down by this parade of lesser wizards, even part-humans, sub-humans, demanding more and more.Æ Lucius strode back to the table and gripped the back of the upholstered chair at its head, the lines in his face set there by Azkaban more obvious than ever. ØThe world of the Dark Lord is the world you deserve. Itís what this family and our descendants deserve. Once his reign settles, with us on the right side, any door will be open to you.Æ Draco set his glass hard on the table. ØThatís what Aunt Bella told me before I started school last year. Just do this, and weíll have everything. But it didnít turn out that way, did it?Æ Dracoís pulse was racing now, and he was halfway to shouting, but before he could say anything else, his mother rose to her feet, radiating icy poise in the way she always could. ØI was afraid that this had happened,Æ she said, a note of urgency in her voice. ØBeing around these people has taken its toll on you. You must stop speaking this way, Draco.Æ ØWhy? Who cares?Æ Draco let out a laugh, standing too. ØWhat, are we planning to sign back up if the resistance collapses? You really think the Death Eaters will take us back after we faked our deaths to get away from them?Æ His father stepped to his motherís side. Narcissa wrapped her fingers tightly around Luciusís arm, and they traded one more look. ØThere is a way,Æ Lucius breathed. ØWe are here, Draco. We must watch and wait, as always. Potter has clearly grown to trust you. è We must be open to opportunities. Do you understand?Æ Draco tried to swallow, but the lump in his throat was like concrete. He knew what all those euphemisms meant. He cursed his own foolishnessîhow had he failed to plan for this? Of course his parents, welcomed into the headquarters of the Order, would be planning to use the valuable information within. And with their wands gone, his parents were surely thinking of him as the linchpin of the plan. They would rely on him to attempt contact with the Death Eaters. His parentsí shoulders were tensed. They were waiting for some sort of cue. But before Draco could speak, a voice sounded outside. ØDraco,Æ called Potter, Øare you in there?Æ Draco startled. ØY-yeah.Æ Potter stuck his head through the tent flap. ØYouíre needed inside.Æ Draco could feel his parents bristling at the idea of his being summoned by Harry Potter, but they didnít speak. He left the mahogany table and walked stiffly toward Potter. But as he reached the edge of the Persian rug, he slowed, then stopped. He turned on his heel and looked back at his parents. What he had come to do poured through his mind. Draco could even see it, hear himself telling them, I wasnít acting to fit in with the Order. Iíve joined them. Iím finished with the Death Eaters and everything they told me to think. The Muggle-born girl you tried to insult earlierîshe trusts me more than you ever did, she cares about who I really am, not who she thinks I should be. Iím working with her and Potter to defeat the Dark Lord, and itíll be better for our family in the end, and you arenít going to stop us. Would his mother break into panicked tears, weeping, My own sonîa blood traitor? Would his father spit at him to Get out with the loss of composure Draco had only ever seen a few times, livid and terrifying? Would they close the tent to him forever? And even if the Horcruxes were destroyed, the world returned to its old self, would his parents bar him from their lives? Would Draco be penniless and alone, his parents relaying his betrayal to every family that had once invited him lovingly into their homes? ØMy bedroom is in the cottage,Æ Draco said to the rug, his voice carefully blank. ØSo Iíll see you at breakfast.Æ He could feel their eyes on his back as he left. The word thudded through his blood. Coward. # The tent was silent for a long while after Draco had gone. ØWell,Æ Narcissa said eventually, settling back down at the dining table, ØI see no evidence of a Confundus or Imperius.Æ ØNo,Æ said Lucius, Øbut he was speaking about the Order far too leniently. Potter seems to have gotten to him.Æ He shook his head, looking troubled. ØIt was best, after all, for us not to tell him right away. è He may have given us up out of fear.Æ Narcissa reached beneath her robe, tugged Bellatrixís mirror from the concealed pocket there, and turned it over and over in her hands. ØDid Potter sway him, Lucius,Æ she said quietly, Øor was it the last year? Draco is right. The Manor was a dreadful place as the Dark Lordís base of operations. The Dark Lord himself as good as made an attempt on Dracoís life.Æ ØNarcissaîÆ Lucius sat at the head of the table and cradled her hands in his. ØWe have no choice. To flee abroad is to invite your sister to track us to our deaths. And our time is short. With Order members in Azkaban under interrogation, we may not be able to stay hidden for very much longer, even with Bella suppressing the information. Our only real chance is to get our hands on Potter, or information equally vital to the Dark Lord, and return to the Death Eaters before we are exposed.Æ Narcissa avoided his eyes, but her husband leaned closer until she could see the fear in his faceîthe fear that he might be returned, again, to the custody of the Dementors. ØWhat is the alternative?Æ Lucius said in a harsh whisper. ØJoin the Order? Associate ourselves withîwith that throng of part-humans and werewolves?Æ His face was twisted in disgust. ØHave we sunk so low?Æ ØNo,Æ Narcissa said quietly, but she was still turning over what Draco had said. It was not so surprising that her son would be sympathetic to the Orderís aims, even if he found the group itself repulsive. If the Dark Lord was defeated, after all, the world would return to the one that he had grown up in, where he had been so comfortable. We were comfortable, too, Narcissa thought. They had been respected, although it was true that they had needed to hold their tongue about Mudbloods and the like, with the shift in the prevailing culture. Still, Lucius was right. They were the union of the Malfoy and the Black line, the culmination of two great houses. The thought of allying with the unsavouries in the Order made her feel delicate. Even to spend mealtimes with a half-giant and that Mudblood girl and a family of blood traitors felt unbearable enough, although the purpose was, of course, to pick information from the Orderís whispers. Narcissa thought of what Bellatrix would have said. Scum, she thought vaguely, disdainfully, but at the same time she remembered that her Mudblood brother-in-law had gone into hiding. She wondered, with a prickling in her nerves, whether Andromeda would come to this place îthe fallen Black sister, singed away from the portrait, lost to her decades ago. When Narcissa closed her eyes, she tried to remember her sisterís face, but there was nothing there except darkness. # Draco followed Potter into Hermioneís bedroom. She was standing by the bed, upon which lay the locket. The Sword of Gryffindor gleamed silver beside the golden chain. The second the door closed, Hermione flicked her wand toward the door and said, ØMuffliato.Æ Draco unfastened his winter cloak and flung it over the armchair. Then he came with Potter to stand before the bed, before the Horcrux. ØYouíve figured out how to open it?Æ Draco said, looking down at the locket. Hermione nodded. ØWe havenít tried it yet, but weíre quite sure.Æ ØYeah,Æ Potter said, looking sheepish. ØItís stupid, how simple it seems. We were talking about how Slytherin would want to keep his secrets safe, and then I thought of the Chamber, andîÆ Draco let out an exasperated sigh. ØParseltongue." It was twice as embarrassing that he, a Slytherin, hadnít thought of it immediately. ØWe think you should destroy it,Æ said Potter. It was a moment before the words registered. ØSorry, Potter,Æ said Draco, ØI couldíve sworn you asked me to destroy my own House relic with Gryffindorís sword.Æ ØYeah, and thatís exactly why. Youíre a Slytherin. A pure-blood, a Malfoy. Maybe it could help somehow.Æ Draco considered for a long time, his eyes travelling from the sword to the locket. Hermione was eyeing him shrewdly. ØWhat?Æ he said to her, raising one brow. ØI know what youíre thinking,Æ she said, and if she had been anyone else, he would have challenged her. Instead he looked back to the objects lain before him. She went on. ØWeíre not asking you to do it because we want you toîto renounce Slytherin, or something like that. Thatís not what this is. We know Slytherin is a part of you.Æ Draco met her eyes for a furtive second, some of his doubt fading. It was, in fact, exactly what heíd been thinking. Slytherin had given him a community, a value system, a simultaneous feeling of pride and persecution. He could never be anything elseîand moreover, he didnít want to be. He didnít want to abandon this part of himself. Draco knew what the rest of Hogwarts stubbornly refused to admit: that the ancient house of cunning and ambition, though they showed a unified face, were complex and individual. Slytherin may have been Crabbe and Millicent, allying themselves with power and not even loyal to their own friends è but it was also Pansy and Goyle, defending Draco to the Carrows while clinging to their disdain for the rest of the world; it was insecure Theo and detached Blaise, waiting to see which way the cards would fall. Slytherin was the murky fog of not having every answer innately. Slytherin was the long conflict before the resolution. Slytherin was Draco himself, and every day he decided what that meant. He reached out with a nerveless hand and picked up the sword. ØAll right,Æ said Potter, suddenly business-like. ØIíll open it, and you be ready for whatever comes out. Yeah?Æ Potter was already scooping up the Horcrux and placing it upon a patch of floor enchanted to resemble stone. Draco jerked his head in a nervous nod, then stepped up, curling his hands around the sword hilt. A hiss flowed out from Potterís mouth. The locket snapped open. Its interior surface was mirrored, and in each half of the locket gleamed one dark eye. The pair of eyes were clever and devouring. Though Draco had never seen them so young, untinged by madness, he knew at once that they were the eyes of Tom Riddle. But as Draco raised Gryffindorís sword, the eyes flashed red, and up from their crimson irises flowed a ghostly grey substance that formed into a silhouette. Draco staggered backîbut the figure that materialised was not an enemy. It was not Aunt Bella or even Lord Voldemort. The face looking back at him was his own, snivelling and stricken, the image of his sixth year. A voice that was half his, half the sibilant hiss of Riddle, issued from the locket, while the ghostly Draco wept and heaved, terrified and desperate. ØDraco Malfoy è sworn to eternal loyalty in my service è I see all that you long for.Æ Draco blanched. The voice, while half-his, was as terrible as the one that still occasionally shocked him awake in nightmares. The sword in his hand dipped. ØYou wish for an end to fear è you wish for freedom. è But if you continue down this path, you shall never be free. When you look in the mirror you shall always see a coward. è Never healed, never whole.Æ The ghostly Dracoís face contorted. He looked around in obvious panic, a defensive panic that verged on hatred, and Draco knew immediately what he was seeing in the invisible distance: accusation, the loathing of thousands. Someone was calling his name from very far away, but Draco couldnít hear. He dimly remembered that he had been in a bedroom, but the sight was fading away, leaving only the locket glowing on the ground. ØThe others will never truly forgive,Æ the voice hissed on. ØThey will never truly forget. è You seek a new path, but you chose your road long ago.Æ And now two new figures were issuing from the locket, flanking him: Lucius and Narcissa Malfoy, one at each shoulder, looking down at him with glowing pride. And the ghostly, panicked Draco was straightening, growing taller, his face untwisting, an air of smug certainty settling over him. The ghostly Draco opened his mouth and spoke the words himself. ØWhen I return to the service of the Dark Lord,Æ said Riddle-Draco, Ømy parents will love me as they always have è I will be self-possessed and proud è I will be true at last to myself, to my familyís wishes, to what I have always been è I need never grovel beneath the hatred and judgments of lesser creatures.Æ And Draco felt it, then: the seductive rush of relief it would be to set down the whole endeavour, to be exactly who he was expected to be, forever. The destructive shame of the last weekîweeks, really, since he had started feeling all this guilt è it could be wiped away. The whole world was dark but for the locketís gold and the mist of the apparition. The apparition extended its hand, and Draco began to lift his own toward it. But then, from very far away, Draco heard two voices echo. The echoes were distant, faint, and yet something in them compelled him to listen. Draco, he heard. Stab it! Draco! And he realised there was no hatred or judgment in those voices. There was fear for his safety. There was urgency and trust. As he looked up into the ghostly Dracoís face, he thought, No. His hands tightened around the sword hilt. His mother and father had made these promises in the tent, and if he had not accepted them from his parents, he would never accept them from Voldemort, his torturer, no longer his master. He would never believe the lie again. No, he was not a coward. He was a Malfoy, and a Slytherin, and a Seeker who would plunge always after what he desired, and what he desired was the world that didnít yet existîthe one shining in some deep recess in his mind. The word pushed up at his throat, struggling, until finally it came through in a strangled cryîØNO!Æîand Draco thrust the sword in one vicious motion through the heart of the locket. The Horcrux screamed as it shattered. The apparition came apart, and the light of the bedroom and the solidity of it rushed back into him, and Draco staggered back, forehead trickling sweat, until the backs of his legs met the bed and his weight gave out. The sword clattered to the ground from his grip, and he felt two small, firm hands grasping his shoulders so hard that it ached, but he kept his eyes tight shut. He couldnít make himself look up at Hermione. He knew that she and Potter had heard every word. There was a long minute of silence, until his breath steadied, until he had wiped away the sweat. ØThatís what it was about,Æ said Potterís voice. ØWith your parents.Æ Hermioneís hands released him. Draco finally cracked open his eyes as she asked Harry, ØWhat? What happened with his parents?Æ ØNothing,Æ Draco said under his breath. He could feel his cheeks colouring. ØItís not nothing,Æ Potter said. ØAnd if youíve got into a row with them, or theyíre planning to say something about you twoÆîhe glanced between Draco and HermioneîØto everyone else, thatís going to be my problem, so youíd better tell me.Æ ØI said itís nothing,Æ he said through gritted teeth. But they werenít going to let him drop it. ØWhat did they say,Æ Hermione said in a tone that told him she was bracing for the worst. Draco looked between them, his heart pumping hard. In the wake of the Horcruxís assault, he felt beyond nakedîhe felt flayed. Every inch of his body stinging with exposure. All his life, heíd been told that his family affairs werenít just secret but sacred. The thoughts and feelings of the Malfoys collective were never to be shared. He never would have told Crabbe or Goyle what had happened in the tent. But then, Crabbe and Goyle had never been privy to any of his thoughts or feelings. Draco felt the world reorient itself yet again as he realised that Harry bloody Potter, simply by being present as his feelings for Hermione had grown, knew more about his interior life than Crabbe or Goyle ever had. Before Draco knew it, he was letting it burst out of him. He was spilling out his frustrations about his parentsí devotion to the Dark Order, repeating every word they had just told him. ØAll that,Æ he snarled, Øafter the Dementors fed off my fatherís body for a year on the Dark Lordís orders. All that after the Dark Lord threatened to kill me. And I can tell what they want! They want us to collect information from the Order, and Iím supposed to try and sneak it back to the Dark Lord somehow. They think thatíll make everything better. But it wonít, and if they think it will, theyíreîtheyíreîÆ But Draco had reached the end of his anger, and at its end, he found hopelessness. He spread one hand over his face, teeth clenched so tightly that pain throbbed in his temples. His parents were never going to change their minds. And he could never change his back. Draco saw that, now, more clearly than ever, because outside of the Horcruxís surreal universe, none of it seemed seductive or even appealing anymore. The promises of power and superiority seemed as false as foolís gold, shining the way unnatural light had always danced upon the locket and the diadem. Draco lowered his hand, staring down at the shattered locket, this fragment of the Dark Lordís power. ØIíll tell them,Æ he said hoarsely. ØIím going to go back down now and tell them everything.Æ # Hermione steeled herself for Draco to go downstairs and confessîbut he had hardly risen from the bed when Harry said sharply, ØNo.Æ Hermione and Draco both looked at him. ØMaybe èÆ Harry said quietly. ØMaybe you shouldnít tell them the truth. Maybe itíd be good for you to have a è a sort of cover story.Æ Hermione began to understand what Harry was suggesting, and she felt a wash of dread. ØPotter,Æ Draco said. Hermione could hear the immense effort it was taking him to keep his voice controlled. ØThink about it,Æ Harry rushed on. ØWe donít have a spy. We never really did, come to that. But what Snape was pretending to do, you can actually do it. Your dad said it himself, didnít he? Youíve stayed close to Potter. You could tell your parents they were right, and then later îyou could convince the Death Eaters youíve done all this to kill me, or turn me in, and that gets you close to the snake.Æ ØHarry, no,Æ Hermione said, her heart beating hard. ØThis is too dangerous. Draco is talented in Occlumency, but he isnít Snape! You canít actually expect him to go back to Voldemort?Æ Harry let out a frustrated sound. ØI know itís dangerous, and if I were any good at Occlumency Iíd do it myself, but we have to get close to Voldemort in the end. What other way is there? We need to get to Nagini, and the snake is always near him!Æ Dracoís voice was shaking with anger now. ØPotter, Iím already dirt to every person in this place. If I go back to the Death Eaters and pretend all this was a lie, the Order will all think they were right.Æ ØBut this is more important than your reputation,Æ Harry said impatiently. ØItís not about what people think, itís about getting to the HorcruîÆ ØNot important, is it?Æ Dracoís voice rose. ØThen maybe youíd like everyone in the world to think whatís being said about you is true, everything the Ministry is coming out with? But then,Æ he added venomously, Øin your case, they have one thing right. You did use a Dark curse on a fellow student, which is more than Iíve ever done to one of our class, by the way, but you donít hear anyone mentioning that. You donít even hear me mentioning it.Æ Guilt glanced across Harryís face, and Hermione knew he was remembering the moment heíd cast Sectumsempra on Draco in that bathroom. ØI è look, I am sorry about that,Æ Harry said, his impatience run through with guilt now, Øbut nowís not the time to è I didnít know what that curse was supposed to do! Iíd seen it written down in Snapeís book and IîÆ ØThatís not the bloody point, Potter!Æ Draco said through gritted teeth. ØMy point is, all your life, youíve been the Boy Who Lived, Dumbledoreís favourite. So when you cast that curse, no one at Hogwarts blamed you. They assumed you made a mistake. For Merlinís sakeÆî Draco let out a hard laughîØeven I assumed it was some stupid mistake! We didnít all look at that curse and say, Of course, thatís the way Potter is. But I è Iím not èÆ Dracoís voice grew high and strangled. Hermione felt a cold shock as his gaze strayed to her. Sweat was still gleaming on his brow from the Horcruxís attack, and his eyes were so wide that he looked nearly manic. ØIím never going to be able to live any of it down! Any of it, anything Iíve done, for the rest of my life! I canít add more to the list, do you understand?Æ A silent moment. Then he sat back on the bed, breathing hard, face turned away from them. Hermione traded a stricken look with Harry. It was a long moment before Harry mustered a response. ØI didnít think about that,Æ he said. Draco didnít even retort. ØBut è still, consider it, would you? You know how important it is.Æ Draco jerked his head in a nod. Harry glanced at Hermione. She nodded, and he slipped out the door. When the door was shut, Hermione approached Draco. She sat on the edge of the bed beside him and slipped her hand into his. He tensed as if he was considering pulling away. For a silent moment, they both looked down at the shattered remains of the Horcrux before them, lying beside the Sword of Gryffindor. ØSo this is where youíve been going,Æ Hermione said quietly. ØThis is why you let McGonagall say what she said at the lake, and è and this past week è itís been worse, since the manor.Æ After another long, silent moment, Draco drew a shaky breath, then nodded. His hand tightened on hers. ØBeing there,Æ he said hoarsely, Øit made me think about everything. I keep thinking about the way I used to è the things I said to you. Everything.Æ ØDracoîÆ ØIím sorry.Æ Hermione looked over at him, but he couldnít seem to meet her eyes. His voice was low, shakyîpractically frightened. There was a quiver in his pointed chin, and he was holding to her hand so tightly now that she could feel his wrist start to shake with the strain. ØIím sorry for what I called you,Æ Draco forced out. ØWhat I did to you. And for standing there doing nothing outside Flourish and Blotts when my father was è and for the Hippogriff, and forîfor things I didnít even remember until I started feeling like this. I want to undo all of it and I canít. I canít even convince myself you should forgive me. Or even look at me.Æ Hermione squeezed his hand back until his grip relaxed the tiniest bit. She supposed she should have prepared for this. Yet she had no idea how to respond. Hermione had never really known what it felt like to forgive someone. She wasnít a person to let things goîthe opposite. She tightened her fists until she could use them to deal blows of retribution. Always it had been this way. And when she thought about the things Draco had said and done, she did feel repulsion. But it was no longer the galvanising repulsion of righteous anger. It was a kind of repulsion based in dissonance, like two magnets of the same pole held close together. That was the way she could feel the man diverging forcibly from the boy. ØIf I still felt that I needed to forgive you,Æ Hermione said softly, ØIím not sure that Iíd be able to. But itís been a long time since I looked at you and saw someone to forgive.Æ She touched his jaw, his neck, his shoulder. ØI see someone else now.Æ Draco did look at her then, and Hermione saw his fear and exhaustion and splintered prideî but she also saw desire, and painful hope, and abject gratitude. It was the look of someone setting down a burden that weighed as much as a past life. It was the look of someone who, in confessing his shame, had finally taken the first, most painful step away from it. She smiled and said, ØI see you.Æ Chapter End Notes sometimes i go back through stuff iíve written and iím like, oh yeah, itís really really obvious that i was raised catholic huh on a more serious note, thank you to everyone who either commented or reached out with concern for my family bc of my previous chapter note. my grandfather wound up passing away last summer after a short, sudden illness (non-covid), and it was harder than usual to live in the US when all my family live abroad. but he had a long and extremely awesome life. he was an architect who did a lot of tai chi and also he once killed an 8-foot-long king cobra with a pair of broomsticks when it tried to attack my sister. RIP to the coolest. Chapter 22: TheResistanceRising Chapter Notes Please note that I have changed the ficís rating to M! It had been six months of researching and planning, eating Potterís surprisingly competent cooking, quietly appreciating that he kept his nose out of Draco and Hermioneís business. Theyíd fought together, laughed together over Ernie Macmillan and Sybill Trelawneyîchatted in idle moments of clean-up about Quidditch and N.E.W.T.s and which jobs at the Ministry sounded best or most boring. And Draco hadnít just grown accustomed to Potterís hero complex. With the Wireless and the Prophet and the Dark Lordís forces conspiring to draw him into the open by any means, Draco now understood why Potter felt that the world and all its casualties were laden upon his scrawny shoulders. After all this, was Draco really supposed to stand there and watch Potter die at the Dark Lordís wandtip? ØIíd say åanytimeí, but letís call this a one-off, shall we?Æ Potter grinned, and Draco smiled back as he slipped out into the hall. # The first weeks of January passed in a rush of Order activity. Hermione, Harry, and Draco were still trying to brainstorm locations for Hufflepuffís cup, but Headquarters was now so busy that they often found themselves distracted by the othersí goals. Firstly and most excitingly, supplies had begun to trickle in from anonymous donors. Apparently, despite Aberforthís gruffness, he was managing to contact sympathisers in Hogwarts, Hogsmeade, and beyondîgenerous sympathisers. Every day, parcels of gold or food appeared at their drop location several miles away. Occasionally they also found owls given over to the custody of the Order, and one memorable morning Hermione found a dozen live chickens inside a handmade coop, which she and Harry had to dismantle before Apparating it back to Headquarters. Soon they had so much money that Fred and George began to manage an Order Treasury in the attic alongside their Wizarding Wireless project. The twinsí radio station was on track to begin broadcasting soon; as a retort to the Prophet, they had named it The Daily Potter. Meanwhile, Ginny and Luna were mapping out flyover distribution routes and drafting leaflets. But as they still had neither broomsticks nor the supplies to produce pamphlets on a mass scale, Ginny and Luna spent half their days collecting materials for Mr. Ollivander, whoîknowing Muggle-bornsí wands had been confiscatedîhad agreed to craft new ones in his time at Headquarters. Harry, Hermione, and Draco had slipped into Ollivanderís room one afternoon, when the wandmaker had recovered somewhat, to ask about the Elder Wand. Even after all the evidence, Hermione had half-expected Ollivander to laugh that the wand was a childrenís story, to confirm what sheíd always believed about wandcraft. It felt surreal to stand at his bedside and hear him speak, instead, about the Elder Wandís bloody path through Wizarding history, as if this were something anyone would know if they simply flipped open A History of Magic. They hoped to search for the Elder Wand at the end of January. In preparation, Hermione had found a compass at an old Muggle garage sale, which was steeping inside the potion mentioned in Celine Shihís instructions, a Magical Sensitivity Draught. Hermione had never tried this potion before, and she found it both interesting and challenging. At first sheíd lamented the potionís reek of rotten eggs, which had filled the upstairs cupboard where they were brewing the potion. But a week into the stewing process, Draco had taken a dropper of lime juice from her potion kit and added three drops to the cauldron. ØDraco! What are you doing?Æ sheíd squeaked, snatching the dropper from his hand, then going back to the instructions, her hair full of foul-smelling moisture. ØIt doesnít say anything about lime juice!Æ ØGranger,Æ Draco had said slowly, looking incredulous, Ødonít you remember?Æ ØRemember what?Æ she said somewhat snappishly, still poring over the text. ØLime juice acts as a nonreactive scent neutraliser in any potion with porcupine quills.Æ At once, Hermione remembered the chapter in Advanced Potion Making that had mentioned that property of lime juice. Sheíd read the Optional Variants chapter less intensively than the rest. After all, if it wasnít in the instructions, was it really necessary? Hermione snapped the book shut and cleared her throat. ØYes. Right. Well. Donít do it again, please; you nearly gave me a heart attack.Æ Dracoís incredulity turned into amusement. ØIíll do it twice a day, if it means I get to see that look on your face again.Æ ØWhat look?Æ ØThe one that said I remembered something from school that you didnít.Æ ØI do remember it,Æ she huffed. ØThe second you said it, I remembered it was in chapter sixteen.Æ ØOf course,Æ Draco said, flicking his wand to banish the foul scent from the cupboard, their clothes, and their hair. The only odour left was the circulating steam from the potion, which now smelled pleasantly like cleaning solution. ØYou remembered it after Iíd already done it. That would have been really useful on your N.E.W.T.s.Æ Hermione drew herself up, not knowing whether she wanted to pick an argument, laugh, or possibly kiss him. Draco was looking down into the potion now, the peach-coloured light of the liquid glancing off the tip of his nose. She wasnít surprised, exactlyîto have signed up for N.E.W.T.-level potions, he must have scored an åOutstandingí on his O.W.L.îbut there was something undeniably attractive about the fact that heíd known something she didnít. Maybe it was just the novelty. It was certainly a relief to see him making jokes again. The change in Dracoís mood since he had apologised had been slow but visible. Gone was the hunch in his shoulders, gone the pain undergirding his expression when their eyes met. He was regaining his old poise, and while he was still closed off to the others, there was even a kind of ease in that, now. It was like the innate confidence heíd used to wear at Hogwarts, except that this confidence did not manifest in sneering. Every day, he seemed to have less to prove. The previous afternoon, Hermione had even spied him speaking to Hagrid in the garden, near where they had tethered Buckbeak, who had joined them under Disillusionment. Draco had turned to the Hippogriff, said something, and lowered his torso in a deliberate bow. After a long moment, Buckbeak had bowed back, while Hagrid looked on with obvious surprise. Draco looked up and met her eye over the cauldron. ØYes?Æ he said. She felt her face grow hotter. Holding his gaze through the swirls of steam, Hermione realised how long it had been since theyíd had a moment alone together. She was beginning to envy Harry and Ginny, who had, inevitably, been unable to stay apart. They were now publicly, happily together. Harry could rest his hand upon Ginnyís absentmindedly while planning; Ginny could kiss Harry on the cheek before leaving the room. But Hermione couldnít acknowledge any connection with Draco. He spent meals with Lucius and Narcissa, then returned to their tent in the evenings. With Hermioneís organisational duties, and not wanting to make Harry uncomfortable during planning sessions, theyíd had to settle for glances shot across rooms, for brushes in darkened hallways. ØColloportus,Æ Hermione said, aiming her wand at the cupboard door. The spell had hardly left her lips when Draco was tugging her into him, kissing her hungrily, his hands tangling in her hair. They did not emerge from the cupboard for half an hour. She missed him more than usual that night while she prepared for bed. She imagined him in the tent in the gardenís back corner and wondered if he was thinking of her, too, or if he was too distracted with the front he had to put up for his parents. Although Draco didnít seem pleased about lying to his parents, Hermione also suspected that part of him was relieved not to have to tell them the whole truth. He still spoke about Lucius and Narcissa with a conflicted expression. ØWhat have you told them youíre doing all day, exactly?Æ Hermione asked one morning, when they and Harry were up in the nursery, lounging in the sunbeams with books spread around them. ØYeah,Æ Harry said, Øwhy do they think youíd spend so much time in Headquarters, if youíre not part of the Order?Æ Draco shrugged. ØI said it was part of the conditions of our staying here. I said you all expect me to be available to answer questions about Death Eaters, and of course I donít tell you anything important, but itís necessary to keep up appearances, et cetera, et cetera.Æ ØDo you think theyíre coming around?Æ Hermione asked. ØTo the idea of staying hidden here, I mean.Æ ØNot really,Æ Draco said, flipping a page back and forth in agitation. ØThey keep trying to get me to commit to sabotaging the Order. Theyíre being more and more obvious about it, too, which is èÆ He shook his head. ØIt doesnít make sense. They were fine with the idea of going abroad last summer.Æ But there was no time to dwell on his parentsí behaviour. With the help of Dobby, Winky, and several secretly freed Hogwarts house-elves, the Order conducted their first real bit of sabotage: they destroyed the Magic is Might statue in the Ministry of Magic, replacing it with a version of the Fountain of Magical Brethren whose subjects were prepared for war. Bill and Fleur were receiving letters day and night from goblin groups, detailing how goblins were being profiled and mistreated, probing their possible interest in Order plans. Fleur, too, was hearing whisperings of Death Eater influence in France and Germany, with Viktor Krum sending a hastily scrawled message about the troubled climate in Bulgaria. Charlie Weasley îto Hagridís delightîhad written back with a mention of dragonet-riders. But the first major breakthrough came halfway through the month, when Fred slipped into the reading room and announced to Harry, Hermione, and Draco, ØAs newsmen, we have some news we think will interest you lot.Æ ØGo on, then,Æ Harry said. George slipped in after Fred. ØThis morning, we had our first-ever broadcast of The Daily Potter.Æ Harry mumbled something inaudible. ØAnd whatís more,Æ George went on, Øwe heard something back.Æ Harryís eyes widened, and Hermioneís heart leapt. ØWhat?Æ Draco said. ØHow?Æ said Harry. ØWhen we were designing the station with Lee in seventh year,Æ Fred said, Øwe had all sorts of features in mind.Æ ØNo one else would know how to access the response spellîÆ George said. ØExcept for Lee,Æ Fred finished. ØAnd he heard the broadcast. Said heís been waiting for us to set this up ever since we disappeared after Christmas.Æ ØAnd,Æ George said, now grinning so widely that pockets of freckles disappeared into his dimples, Øhe had company. One Remus Lupin and one Kingsley Shacklebolt were just about to leave his parentsí place, see?Æ ØThey were on the list of temporary safehouses,Æ Fred added. ØTonks mentioned it at the Manor,Æ Hermione breathed, her heart racing. ØOh, my goodness. Does this meanîÆ ØWeíll be able to get them here?Æ Harry said, voice rising. ØIíll do you one better, mate.Æ Fred tugged the door of the reading room wide. Hermione let out a small scream and leapt to her feet. In the hallway were Lee Jordan, his locked hair bound back, Kingsley Shacklebolt, so tall his bald head reached up into the shadowed heights of the hall, and Remus Lupin, his robes exceptionally shabby, his kind face spread in a smile. That night, they utilised half the tentsí kitchens to cook a welcome feast for the Orderís newest arrivals. How much magic could there be? A lot of magic, was the apparent answer. He, Hermione, and Potter spent three long weeks picking up stray Knuts and other discarded items. When they spent an entire day locating an ordinary drinking glass that someone had spelled with a Self-Cleaning Charm, Potter was so frustrated that he shattered the thing on a nearby brick wall, causing several massive dogs to erupt out of the underbrush in the garden and bark furiously at them until they fled. Their story to the rest of the Order was that they were scouting new locations for safehouses in the middle of the wilderness. His parents also believed the story, to Dracoís relief. They had been protective verging on paranoid since the attack on Aberforth. Indeed, they seemed to be more frightened by the attack than Aberforth himself; the man had survived, though his left hand now hung limply by his side, the damage to his nerves irreparable. He spent most afternoons grousing about being stuck away from his goats. Dracoís parents, on the other hand, had quite a different view.
