Canít you see it?Æ ØHarry,Æ Hermione said, Øplease donít take this the wrong way, but I donít think youíll be able to kill Snape just that simply.Æ ØWell, if I canít kill Snape, then I definitely wonít be able to kill VolîÆ Potter broke off, remembering they were not under the protection of the Fidelius Charm. ØYou-Know-Who.Æ ØWeíve got Ollivander,Æ Draco reminded him. ØWe can ask him if thereís some other way to win the wand than killing the person who got control over it last.Æ Potter brightened. ØYeah, all right. Letís get back and ask him right away. Here, Hermione.Æ He picked up the Cloak, and Hermione passed the Elder Wand to Draco while Potter and Hermione slid the broomsticks one by one back into her small beaded bag. Draco looked down at the wand in his hand. He couldnít believe what he was holding. All the wands that the Slytherins had used to debate over, insisting which one was better than the otherîthis was every wand of legend, rolled into one, here in the palm of his hand. It felt good in his hand, with a reassuring weightînearly warm. ØDraco.Æ He startled, looking up. Hermioneís arm was held out for Apparition. Draco tucked the Elder Wand into a deep, secure pocket, then took Hermioneís arm. When they Apparated into the garden and opened the door of headquarters, Draco expected to hear the pleasant bustle of the Order preparing for dinner, spreading cutlery over the cottageís various surfaces. He expected to hear Fred and George and Lee joking from the sofa that had become their domain. Instead, when they returned, Draco thought someone else had been attacked. The Order were knotted up in the centre of the room, gathered around something. But when the door closed and the Order broke apart, looking back at Draco, Hermione, and Potter, the looks on their faces were pure surprise. They were not gathered around an injured body but around a copy of the Evening Prophet. ØWhat is it?Æ Hermione demanded as the three of them broke for the outstretched copy of the paper. ØWhatísîÆ The headline at the top of the page read MASS REVOLT AT AZKABAN. There was an accompanying photograph, blurry and distorted, which had clearly been taken in a great hurry. It looked as though the photographer had been fleeingîand there was no question from what. The photograph moved through several images. First, the dark, plunging motion of Dementors on the attack, so many Dementors that the image appeared solid black. Then, from nowhere or everywhere, from all four corners of the photograph, came a blast of light. The Dementors swirled as they scattered, and then they regrouped, reformed è but not before Draco caught a single glimpse of what was beyond. A massive crowd was gathered in a prison yard, and standing at forefront of the blur of faces, yelling something with his fist raised into the air, was Ron Weasley. Chapter End Notes the next chapter is called The Secret-Keeperís Tale and i have wanted to write it for like the better part of a year now. cheers! tumblr | instagram Chapter 23: TheSecret-Keeper's Tale Chapter Notes Hi all! A small note: in this chapter Iíll be transforming a piece of the world from canon. So far, in the spirit of a DH rewrite, Iíve tried to stay as close to canon worldbuilding as possible. Obviously this is an AU, but I havenít done anything like, say, making Stupefy a spell to turn things inside-out, or that sort of retcon. This chapter is the first time I am retconning something of JKRís and substituting in my own stuff. So I am dropping a note bc it may be jarring in the context of the fic so far. But I feel pretty strongly about this element and I think (hope??) it feels true to the spirit of the books! Thanks as always for reading!! See the end of the chapter for more notes Mid-October ØI canít do this,Æ Ron said hoarsely. ØIím done.Æ He turned on his heel. Suffocating darkness closed in, and then Ron Weasley was speeding away from the Potter Cottage, away from his best friend and the girl heíd thought would be his future, away from the hot, sickly feeling of rejection. Home, he thought, holding the destination desperately in his mind. Ron didnít care about anything else just now, not Grindelwald or some powerful wand, nor the locket in Umbridgeís possession. His only thought was escaping the mortification on Hermioneís face, the stunned discomfort on Harryís, the cold anger on Malfoyís. He couldnít take one more day in that place. Ron burst out of the darkness in a copse of trees near Ottery St. Catchpole. This was the usual spot for local wizards to Apparate. Too far from town for Muggles to hear, the woods were also laced with Muggle-Repelling charms to deter anyone who might be out for a stroll. It was a shock, then, that Ron appeared not in a dark, quiet stand of woodland but in the flickering light of a campfire. He was not greeted by night-time silenceîbut by a triumphant shout: ØImpedimenta!Æ Ron had no time to reach for his wand. He was blasted off his feet. His back slammed into a tree, his head knocking into a branch so hard that stars burst in his closed eyes. ØWhatíve we got here, Scabior?Æ said a wheezy, gleeful voice. As Ronís vision cleared, he discerned half a dozen grubby men and women circling toward him in the reddish firelight. He tried to worm his hand into his pocket, but ropes had already snaked around him, holding him so securely to the tree that he could not move a muscle. ØLooks like åe could be åogwarts age,Æ said the man called Scabior, sidling closer. ØGet the truants list, Smith.Æ As a woman rummaged through a knapsack at the campsite, Ron spotted a huddled line of figures on the other side of the fire, chains glinting at their hands and feet. Wild theories of this being a trap laid for Harry dissipated. Bounty hunters, he realised. They must be looking for Muggle-borns and Order sympathisers on the run. ØWhatís your name, ginger?Æ said Scabior, stepping closer to Ron. His breath smelled foul, as if heíd been camping here without cleaning his teeth for weeks. ØSt-Stan è Shunpike,Æ Ron gasped out, still winded from impact. ØLike åell it is,Æ said Scabior. ØWe know Stan Shunpike, åeís put a bit of work our way.Æ Ronís vision whited out as Scabiorís ringed fist slammed into his jaw. His companions chuckled as Ron sagged against his ropes. Ronís mind was racing, cycling through all the foulest swearwords he knew. Why hadnít he expected something like this? How could he have left Headquarters without his wand already in his hand? Headquarters è Ronís stomach dropped like a stone. If they sent him back to Hogwarts as a truant, Snape would read the secret of the Potter Cottage right out of his head. In that instant, Ronís wounded feelings toward his friends were blacked out by blind panic. He couldnít let Snape get to them. ØIím notîon that list,Æ he panted. ØFinished Hogwartsîages ago.Æ The bounty hunters sized him up. ØHeís tall enough,Æ one of them grunted. ØCould be a fair bit older. è Mudblood on the run, then?Æ ØNo, Iím g-going home, thatís all è I live in Ottery St. Catchpole è took a business trip èÆ ØOh, really?Æ sneered the one with the lists. ØWhatís your name and address, then? Course, weíve got every wizard on the local registry marked down here.Æ Ron gaped at the stack of papers in her hands. ØI èÆ The bounty huntersí faces all resolved into leering satisfaction. ØMudblood, this one,Æ said the woman with the papers, to murmurs of agreement. With an icy rush, Ron realised that this alternative was even worse than Hogwarts. If they thought he was on the run from the law, theyíd bring him to the Ministry. Someone there would recognise Arthur Weasleyís son, one of the six who had broken into the Department of Mysteries. Theyíd realise heíd been on the run with Harry. He wouldnít be turned over to Snape but to Bellatrix Lestrange. For the first time, Ron really felt the weight of being the Secret-Keeper. He remembered how proud heíd felt the night heíd agreed to take it on, when Hermione and Harry had looked at him with such confidence that heíd felt it glowing in himself like a heated chunk of gold. Now, struggling madly against his restraints, Ron pictured them back at Headquarters. Were they waiting for him to return? Would they go to sleep expecting him to be back by morning, then awaken in the middle of the night to see Bellatrixís face full of mad delight in the darkness, the woman having torn the secret out of Ronís mind? ØI still åavenít åeard a name, ginger,Æ said Scabior softly, stepping closer. Ronís mind was a panicked roar. He needed time. He needed a plan. He had neither. ØGo to hell,Æ he gasped, wrenching hard at the ropes. Scabior punched him again. This time his knuckles hit Ron square in the nose. A nasty crack sound. Pain shot up into Ronís head like a breath of freezing air, and he did the only thing he could think of to buy himself more time. He slumped forward and pretended to fall unconscious. There was the sound of Scabior spitting at his feet. ØPathetic,Æ said the bounty hunter. ØSearch åim and put åim with the others, you three. Weíll question åim properly in the morning.Æ Hands groped into his pockets, worming between the ropes. One yanked his wand free, but as they reached his trousers, something curious happened. One of the hunters was feeling around in the front pocket where Ron always kept the Deluminator. He knew heíd had it before Disapparating from Headquarters è and yet the hunter said nothing, seemed to feel nothing. As the bounty hunters cut the ropes, Ron let himself drop like a rag doll. Someone half-caught him, then kicked him roughly to the ground to laughs from the others. Ribs aching, Ron considered trying to grab one of their wands, making a go of fightingîbut there were too many, three assigned to him now, three others who would join in instantly. When Ron had been searched, the hunters dragged him across the campsite and slung him against a log. He had to force himself not to groan as his spine hit a hard knot in the wood. Then magical chains were snaking around him, footsteps retreating. It was a long time before Ron dared to sneak a look through his cracked eyelids. His eyes widened inadvertently. Staring down at him were Ted Tonks and Dean Thomas. Tedís fair hair was matted with dirt and sweat, and his big belly was hanging slack as if it hadnít been properly filled in a while. Dean was downright gaunt, his dark skin burnished by the firelight. Ron opened his mouth, but Ted breathed, looking down at the ground, ØNot now, son.Æ So Ron let his eyes sag shut again. The pain in his face was spectacular, pulsing from his jaw to his nose and back. After a long while, Ron heard the last rustles of the Snatchers entering their tents. The prisoners were left outside to the elements. Only then did they risk conversation. ØTed,Æ Ron whispered, sitting up. ØDean.Æ His eyes strayed over the other captivesîtwo young women who looked like they might be sisters, huddled together at the end of the log, and a man on Deanís other side who looked vaguely familiar. ØIím Dirk Cresswell,Æ whispered this man. ØYouíre Arthurís son, arenít you?Æ ØYeah, thatís right,Æ Ron whispered back. He remembered meeting Dirk at the Ministry once îone of the understaffed Goblin Liaison office, with circles under his eyes from too many nights preparing goblin defence cases in the courts. He looked twice as tired now, his curly dark hair rumpled and his robes torn. ØWhatís happening?Æ Dean whispered. ØI thought you were sick at your parentsí place.Æ Ron hesitated, licking the blood off his lips. ØI canít say much. Safer that way.Æ ØRight. But Harry? Hermione?Æ Ron felt a pang of shame. ØTheyíre all right,Æ he mumbled. ØLook, Iíve got to get out of here. Reckon you canîcan help me with my chains, orîÆ Ted Tonks shook his head. ØIf there were any way out, donít you think weíd have done it ourselves?Æ he said, not unkindly. Ron tried to reach his front pocket, where he could feel the Deluminator, but the chains were unforgiving. Eventually he gave up. What was the use, anyway? In the months since finding the small silver tool, it hadnít revealed a single use besides turning out the lights. Typical, Ron thought with a bitter taste in his mouth. Dumbledore had bequeathed Gryffindorís sword to Harry. To Hermione, the legendary wizard had left a secret message about Grindelwald. And what had Ron been given? A parlour trick. ØListen,Æ he whispered, ØI mustnít be turned in to Snape or the Death Eaters. Itís important.Æ He gave the others a meaningful look, hoping they would understand it was about the Order without him having to give them too much information. ØThereís no other option,Æ said Dean hopelessly. ØSince Iíve got no proper Wizarding family tree, theyíll send me to the Ministry to put me on trial.Æ Ted nodded. ØItís the same for me,Æ he whispered with a comforting nudge to Deanís leg. ØWeíll probably get a year or less in Azkaban for carrying wands, and after that, theyíll release us è expect us to live as Muggles.Æ ØIíll warn you two èÆ Dirk Cresswellís serious face looked sunken in the faint glow of the smouldering embers. ØItís not much of a trial.Æ ØYouíve already been tried?Æ Ron said. Cresswell nodded. ØHad my family tree forged. I managed to escape on the way to Azkaban. Theyíll bring me right back, I suppose.Æ A hard lump rose in Ronís throat. At once he saw the strategy laid out before him. It felt instinctive, as strategies always did to him. Of course, he thought, feeling numb. He remembered being twelve years old and gazing up into the blank face of the white queen. Now, again, he was to play the sacrificial piece. ØTheyíll bring you back to Azkaban,Æ he said quietly, Øno questions asked?Æ The other three all looked at him with sombre understanding. Cresswell nodded. ØNo questions asked.Æ ØDid you escape with anyone, Dirk?Æ Ron asked, trying to sound braver than he felt. ØOther convicts, I mean?Æ ØTwo others,Æ Cresswell said, licking his cracked lips. ØAn older witch from the Obliviation Squads, and a younger bloke, Samuel Gittyburrough. 23 or 24. Assistant to someone in Accidents and Catastrophes. You might be able to pass for him.Æ ØDoes he look like Ron?Æ Dean whispered. Ted twitched his nose, trying to push his small spectacles up into place. ØThat wonít matter, Dean. The Snatchersí lists donít have descriptions. No room, with thousands of Muggle.borns to find. è Just name, age, and blood status.Æ ØAnd the Dementors wonít care who theyíre taking in,Æ said Cresswell, mouth twisted with contempt. ØTheyíll be pleased to have a warm new body.Æ The hair on the back of Ronís neck lifted. As they listened to the hiss of dying embers, Cresswell considered Ron. ØOur sentences were all five years, Weasley. Are you sure you know what youíre èÆ ØYeah,Æ Ron said shortly. ØIíve got no choice.Æ But as he turned over to try and sleep, fear cocooned him like long, cold fingers. # Ron stood on the edge of the rocky island, staring up at the wizard prison with his heart in his throat. Azkaban was a dark monolith reaching up to the flat grey sky. There were no seabirds, no signs of life whatsoever. A pall hung over the place as if over a deathbed. Even from here, the Apparition point at the farthest tip of the island, Ron felt coldness radiating out and down, moving through him. At his shoulder, Dirk Cresswell shuddered. Ron began to see them as the Snatchers shoved them up the path: hooded Dementors, drifting over the surface of the prison in their uncanny way, dwarfed by the blunt rock face. Remember how Sirius kept his head in there, Ron told himself. Remember, you havenít done anything wrong è But then his eyes fixed on the door ahead, that black notch in the side of the impenetrable wall. With a sudden wave of terror he considered trying to break away from the Snatchers. He considered the edge of the island, the drop-off into churning ocean. He kept walking. # Bang. The cell door slammed shut as Ron staggered in, the tendrils of cold knotted so tightly inside his lungs that he scraped breaths like an asthmatic. He scrambled away from the Dementorís hooded face looming beyond the bars. He was dimly aware that heíd told himself to remember something outside, some kind of reassurance è but there were so many Dementors. The halls had seethed with them like a dark tide. Remember, he told himself. Remember è No memory came. There was only the numb horror of being inside this minuscule cell, wandless, hopeless, dressed in ragged prison clothes. Theyíd stripped him of his robes. Ron had watched them flung away by the scant few human guards, even the tiny comfort of the useless Deluminator gone now. There was nothing left except the threadbare blanket on the rank mattress beneath the window. The window itself was barely a two-inch slit in the wall, allowing nearly no light. Still, Ron scrambled up onto the cot and put his eye up to the slit. He looked out on the endless ocean, trying to catch his breath. He did this for hours, until the sun set and the night began. It was a night more awful than any other. Ron sank back onto the cot, gasping, as the cold of the Dementors sank into his marrow. The darkness pressed down against his body, amplifying the thoughts that had engulfed him all day. Of course youíd wind up here, Ron è havenít you always been useless? è Really, this is the best thing you could do, rot in here è Harry would never have run è Hermione would have thought to be prepared with her wand è Ron stared at the ceiling, jaw slack. How had he ever helped his friends, really? Heíd been trapped by the cave-in at the Chamber of Secrets, unconscious while Harry and Hermione saved Sirius together è attacked by brains in the Department of Mysteries, reduced to vomiting slugs when heíd tried to stand up for Hermione in second year è He could see it so clearly now. He was nothing beside either of them. So heíd won a few Quidditch matchesîso what? Harry was the real star of the team, everyone knew that. Fred and George had outshone Ron before theyíd left, and Ginny had begun to outshine him even though she was younger. At the thought of his family, Ron curled into a ball. When had his mum or dad ever really seemed to think he was special or capable? When heíd gotten the Prefectís badge, maybe è yes, that had been one good evening è Ron tried to cling to the memory of that night, the party in his honour è but even that was soured, because hadnít Hermione assumed it would be Harry? Sheíd been stunned when sheíd seen it was him. Probably Hermione had realised over the last year, while Ron was humiliating himself with Lavender, how much better she liked Harry. If Ron ever got out of here, it would be to find them together. The prodigy and the Chosen One. They would laugh at the idea that heíd ever tagged along beside them as if he belonged. He tried to cling to memories with Harry and Hermione, the comfortable evenings they had spent in Gryffindor Tower, but even the happiest images were tainted by the fact that he was least of the three friends, least of the seven siblings, never impressive or desirable. And his own desires è his feelings for Hermione, his desperation for acclaim and approval è childish, humiliating. A fresh wave of cold washed into the cell as a pair of Dementors passed outside. Ron turned over, freezing, shivering, gaspingîand felt a lump in the pocket of his prison clothes. Confusion disrupted the waves of despair. He slipped his hand into the front pocket and somehow, impossibly, felt the warm metal of the Deluminator. Hope surged in Ronís throat. He yanked the Deluminator out of his pocket, not understanding how it had returned to himîbut surely there would be help within è He depressed the silver catch. Nothing happened. ØNo,Æ Ron moaned, wiping cold sweat from his face. ØNo, no èÆ He clicked it over and over, but there was no light in the cell to collect. The Deluminator held nothing for him. He collapsed back onto the cot and stuffed the useless Deluminator through a tear in his mattress, hiding it deep in the filthy insulation within. Let it stay there forever, buried, this reminder of his own unimportance. Without an ounce of strength left, Ron collapsed into sleep. # Every day was the same in Azkaban. Ron awoke from nightmares and ate the hard bread and water that were left inside his door, chewing and swallowing mechanically. For one hour each day, lower-security prisoners were allowed to walk around the yard, a grim gravel plain enclosed by Azkabanís interior walls. Ronís hour was spent with others from the same prison sector, including Dirk Cresswell, who looked more wan than ever. The first few days, Ron dimly thought he recognised a few other faces around the yard. Someone who might have been a shop assistant at Florean Fortescueís ice cream parlour. A parent he might have glimpsed at Platform 9.. Then Ron stopped caring about faces, names, identities. The only thing left was pointless habit. He walked with Dirk Cresswell, but most of the other prisoners were so weak and hopeless that they merely huddled against the walls. Every other hour was indistinguishable. Ron lay in his cell, dreading the rattling approach of Dementors on their rounds, who would occasionally hover outside the door. When they were near, Ronís feelings became as raw as wounds. He saw himself with brutal perspective, as if seeing an unflattering candid photograph: Ron Weasley, a boy with no real accomplishments except to be in proximity to Harry Potter. Around nightfall, a second meal. Then hours of impenetrable darkness. A feeling of such aloneness that Ron felt as if he were being peeled out of himself little by little. # Time passed. Ron was not sure how many days, then not sure how many weeks, but surely it had been weeks. He could hardly see Dirk Cresswellís face in the yard when they met to walk. There was the dim recognition one day that Dean Thomas and Ted Tonks had, indeed, been convicted tooî for there they were in the yard. They trudged, the four of them, around in a circle. It did not occur to Ron to speak. All curiosity had gone from him. The world was rolled out flat. No colour. Eventually he could not walk anymore. He sat against one of the walls as so many did, and it seemed to make sense to him, fading into obscurity once and for all. # Ronís eyes slid open. Trying to fall into sleep, he could have sworn he had heard a voice. Not a moan or a cry echoing down the hall, but a tiny, muffled voice within the cell. So, he was going mad. Not such a surprise. It was becoming harder and harder even to force down food. Of course his mind would go next. When he lifted his heavy head, he could hear nothing, but when he lay back down to sleep again, his ear pressed to the mattress, he heard the faint whisper: Ron. The sound was coming from inside the mattress. Ron slipped his hand into the rip, felt around until his hand touched metal, and withdrew the Deluminator with trembling fingers. Harryís voice echoed inside, sounding determined: Ron. And within moments, Hermioneís had joined it, full of concern. Ron è Uncomprehending, Ron depressed the Deluminatorís silver catch and peered down into its chamber. Inside he saw two wisps of light, hanging there like tiny unicorn hairs, ethereal, seemingly weightless. One for each utterance. He clicked the catch again and again, but the wisps were too feeble to issue out into the air, as other lights once had. They merely stirred within. ØHarry?Æ he croaked. ØHermione? Can è can you hear me?Æ But there was no response. Only the faint echo of their voices saying his name. A cloak rustled beyond his door. Ron let the Deluminatorís catch snap shut again, but once the Dementor had gone, he pressed the button again and looked down into the infinitesimal glow. For weeks, with the Deluminator muffled in the mattress, maybe he had been missing whispers like these. The wisps barely cast enough light to see the outline of his finger in the darkness. The fog of misery was too thick to think properly, but the voices eased his mind in an animal way. Theyíre alive, he thought. Somewhere, theyíre alive. # Soon afterwardîor maybe a long time, it was impossible to tellîother voices joined the cohort, one by one. Ron began to keep the Deluminator in his pocket again, and so he heard them when they arrived. Ron, spoken by his mother in a choked whisper. Ron, his fatherís voice thin. Ron, Ginny and Neville and Luna. The twins. Charlie and Bill and even Fleur Delacour. The whispers collected in the Deluminator, and Ron looked down into the tiny glow of its chamber after nightfall. The light couldnít fix anything, but Ron found that he could make himself walk in the yard again. One afternoon, trudging along beside the others, he had his hand affixed around the Deluminator in his pocket. Even holding it gave him some comfort. Childish, maybe, but heíd decided he was not above childishness. Through the usual dark fog, he remembered that last night he had heard Seamus Finniganís voice saying, in a bracing sort of way, Ron. He rasped out, ØDid you see Seamus over the summer?Æ The words were such a disruption of the hollow, sea-swept silence that all three of Ronís walking companions stopped in their tracks, looking at him with blank, hopeless eyes. Dean, Ted, and Dirk looked nearly unrecognisable, so sunken were their faces. ØSeamus,Æ Dean repeated in not much more than a whisper. ØYeah. è I went to his for a week in August.Æ ØHad you been before?Æ Dean had to think a long time, as though he were paging slowly through a book of memories. Then he said, ØYeah. Before the Quidditch World Cup. è I went with him and his mum.Æ ØThe Cup èÆ Colour migrated across Ronís vision. Tents with shamrocks upon them. Bulgarian Quidditch robes plummeting through a cheering stadium. ØWe didnít see you there.Æ ØSeamus mentioned seeing you.Æ Dean paused. ØYou and Harry and Hermione.Æ ØYeah,Æ Ron managed. They did not speak for the rest of the walk. After sunset that night, Ron huddled under his threadbare blanket and clicked the Deluminator. He peered down into the chamber, and for an instant, his muscles eased. The edges of the painful feelings were filed away by the light. He wondered, vaguely, if the light seemed stronger than it had last night. Ron held the Deluminator away from his eye. Yes. There was no mistake. There was now enough light within to illume the colour of his freckles beneath the blanket, and suddenly he was reckoning with the fact of his body, his existence, when he looked down at his bony hands and arms. He realised how very hungry he was. Ravenous. Ronís mind felt slow and stupid, but actual thought was forming for the first time in weeks, thought outside the crushing repetition of his own worthlessness: Why is the light stronger? Heíd spoken to Dean in the yard. Heíd been holding the Deluminator then. Ron leaned forward and whispered into the Deluminator. ØThe Quidditch World Cup èÆ He clenched his eyes shut, brow furrowed, searching for something to give the light. ØThe Quidditch World Cup è I l-liked going.Æ He could feel the press of Azkaban all around him, tearing at the memory, reminding him how heíd made a fool of himself over the Veela there è reminding him that at the end of that night, the Death Eaters had attacked the site è begun their reign of terror, which had led to all this è But he didnít take his eyes from the Deluminatorís light. ØWe were in the Top Box,Æ he croaked. ØDad was proud he could take us. è Harry loved it, we got him away from the Dursleys, Hermione liked reading about Quidditch for once. è Best seats in the stadium, and they said Ireland was the best team in decades èÆ The words left him shaking, almost nauseated. They took so much from him. And yet Ron could see, before his eyes, the light pulsing below, the wisps coalescing into a brilliant bead the size of a fingernail. He watched it as long as he could, but his strength had been sapped, and he fell into unconsciousness. # The days that followed were agonising in fresh ways. The coldness of the air, Ronís omnipresent hunger and sadness, the ugliness of the prison, the pain in every face in the yard è these things were more acute than before, and yet Ron knew it was because he was becoming lucid again. Every night he whispered a happy memory into the Deluminator. The effect of the Dementors was such that the memories seemed to want to turn to dark pockets of fear or anxiety, but Ron gritted his teeth and stared into the light until it bored spots in his vision, and he made himself speak only the good. And so he could count time again. The first night was the World Cup. The second, his victory in Quidditch in fifth year. And so on. After a week of depositing memories into the Deluminator, he holed up one night under the blanket and clicked the catch. Light rushed out. It hung under the blanket, shapeless, the size of a walnut, and Ron took in a gasp of air as warmth sheathed him like a comforter, the first moment of relief heíd had since entering Azkaban. Suddenly thoughts began to form, frantic, one upon another, building. He clung to the Deluminator and whispered to it, ØIímîIím doing the best I can. Iím keeping them safe.Æ The hovering light grew in diameter, swelling by a millimetre. He leaned close to it and heard Harryís and Hermioneís voices again. Ron. ØIím keeping them safe,Æ he gasped out. ØAll this time Iíve been hereîIíve kept them safe.Æ The light pulsed and grew. Ron held the Deluminator right up against his lips. It was warm. It fogged with his breath. He realised how firm his grip was. He felt the beat of his heart and the ragged edge of his nerves. ØIím here for a reason,Æ he said. # The next day, Ronís trip to the yard was different. For the first time, he took note of which way the Dementors led their shuffling band through the prison. He did the same the next day, and the next, until he remembered the system of corridors and could retrace them in his head. In the yard, he walked alongside his three companions. He wished he could let the light out to give them strength, too, but the Dementors who stood along the yard would surely detect the disturbance. Instead he whispered, ØDonít stop walking, donít look at me. But I got something into the prison.Æ Their steps faltered, but Dean, Ted, and Dirk continued on. ØHow, son?Æ Ted whispered. ØIf they catch you èÆ ØThey wonít. Here. Take it.Æ He slipped the Deluminator into Tedís hand, and Ted drew a small gasp. Ron felt the absence of the warmth at once, a sagging of his spirits. ØHold your hands up to your mouth like youíre breathing into them,Æ he whispered. ØAnd say something happy. Whisper a happy memory into it.Æ Tedís kindly face was ashen. Ron heard him straining as he whispered, ØNymphadora è my Dora è the day she was born. Dromeda was in labour six hours. When we held Dora è we both cried, we were so happy, and Dromeda never cries.Æ For the whole hour, they walked and passed the Deluminator, whispering memories under the guise of warming their hands. And as they walked, Ron noted that the courtyard was shaped like a pentagon. He noted the five exits and the sheer, windowless walls. That night, with the force of the othersí memories, the Deluminatorís light grew even stronger. Ron didnít dare let the light out for long, with the Dementorsí ceaseless patrols. But day after day, the four of them walked and spoke; night after night, Ronís happy memories were coming more easily. Now he could whisper hoursí worth into the Deluminator, and when he recounted Quidditch matches, it was not so tempting to focus on Harryís prodigious talent, Ginnyís star quality, the twinsí zany reliability. These were Ronís own memories. In these moments, to the Deluminator, he was the one that mattered most. One night, he was recounting a memory from third year, Snape calling Hermione an insufferable know-it-all and Ron snapping back, You asked a question and she knows the answer. Why ask if you donít want to be told? Heíd received detention for it, of course, but the happy memory was that moment of defensive pride in Hermione, the impressed hush that had fallen over the class, the way heíd felt nothing but defiant clarity. And then, quite without meaning to, Ron was whispering to the Deluminator, ØI stand up for my friends.Æ The Deluminator burned hot in his hand. Startled, he clicked it. The light that issued outîthe size of a tangerine nowîpulsed fiercely. Ron flinched back against the dripping wall. The glow seemed to push out some of the sickly darkness that had seeped so deeply into him that it lined his organs. I stand up for my friends, Ron thought again. Yes, heíd had arguments with Hermione, envied Harry his fame è but so, too, had he sworn that he would stay by their side until the end, knowing that death might lie at the end of that road. He would fight to save them always. And it was not something to brag about, as he had once boasted about his Quidditch saves by the lake. It was simply a fact about himself thatîRon realisedîhe felt proud of. He looked into the light, swallowed, and whispered, ØYeah. Thatís what I do. I stand up for my friends, and my family, too.Æ The light pulsed, and Ron realised he could no longer look into its centre, for it had grown blinding there, too bright to see with the naked eye. Then there was a noise down the hall. Ron startled, clicked the Deluminator out once more, and toppled back to the cot, cold and fear drenching him. Had the Dementors sensed the light? Worse, had they discovered his real identity? He felt many Dementors on their way up the hall, a tide of ice è No. It was a new prisoner. A tiny voice was begging, moaning, ØNoîlet me out è please, let me out èÆ When he heard the clang of the cell door beside him, the echoes didnít cut off. In fact, the voice seemed to be piercing through the wall, only weak cries now. ØPlease èÆ Ron crept to the source of the voice and realised there was a deep crack through one of the stones. It was scarcely millimetres wide, but it admitted the sound of the prisoner in the cell beside him. He waited until he was sure the Dementors had gone. Then he pulled his cot into the corner, leaned against the wall, and whispered against the fissure, ØHey.Æ No response except the crying. As loudly as he dared, he said, ØCan you hear me?Æ The cries broke off. There was the sound of bare feet on damp stone. Then a ragged whisper. ØYes. è Who are you?Æ ØMy nameís Ron. And you?Æ There was a long pause. Ear pressed to the stone, Ron thought he could hear the sticky sound of a hand wiping a nose, a soft thump like sheíd rested her head back against the wall. ØE-Elaine,Æ she whispered. ØRight. Elaine. Are you allîÆ Ron bit his tongue, grimacing; of course she wasnít all right. Ron had never been much good at reassurances. He could manage silent, physical comfort all right, but when it came to talking about feelings è how many times had Hermione told him he was hopeless? Hopeless, echoed her imagined voice in his mind as the fog and the drear crept back in. Clinging to the warm metal of the Deluminator, Ron said, ØLook, I know itís awful, but youíve got to hang on as best you can.Æ Elaine sniffled. ØAnd how am I supposed to do that?Æ ØThey let us out every day for an hour. Iíll find you in the yard tomorrow.Æ But the next day, there was no one new in the yard. Ron spent the hour with Dean, Ted, and Dirk as usual, whispering memories. His walking companions seemed half-roused from their stupor these days, too, strengthened by the daily touch of the Deluminator. That night, after bolting down his meagre dinner, Ron clambered back onto his cot and leaned close to the crack in the wall. ØHey,Æ he whispered, Øare you there?Æ That sound again of feet on the grimy floor of next doorís cell. Then Elaine whispered, ØIím here.Æ ØWhy werenít you in the yard today?Æ ØI canít come out.Æ ØWhy not? Itíll help.Æ ØHelp?Æ she said in a hollow whisper. ØNo. Itíll make everything worse. è They èÆ She was crying again. Ron glanced to the door. He heard no noise from the hall, so he clicked the Deluminator, then tried to push the glowing ball of light through the crackîbut it merely hovered by the stone, unable to penetrate into her cell. A soft, menacing rattle from outside. Ron extinguished the light and sagged against the wall, gloom creeping back into every corner of the room. ØWhat was the p-point,Æ Elaine choked out. ØWhat was the point in any of it è and now Iím as good as èÆ Ron was beginning to feel panicked now. ØDonít cry, all right? Please donít cry.Æ No answer. Just the sound of sobs, and no one was going to come and help her, no one would help either of them è The Deluminator pulsed in his hand. ØTell me a happy memory,Æ Ron whispered. Her sobs petered out. ØAîa happy èÆ ØMemory, yeah.Æ Ron held the Deluminator up to the crack in the wall. ØGo on. Anything you like.Æ There was a long pause. Then she rasped, ØOnce, on holiday with my family è we went to Greece, the six of us, and there was one day when it rained. S-so everyone else stayed inside and I got to go out by myself, no one cared. I sat by the seaside and it was still sweltering, even though it was raining. And I felt like è like I could do anything. Just for myself.Æ ØYouíve got a big family,Æ Ron said. ØSo have I. Five older brothers and a little sister. Iím second-youngest.Æ He paused, then admitted, ØI never used to feel like I could live up to the rest of them.Æ He wasnít even sure why he was saying it, but he had to fill the silence, keep her from dissolving into tears again. He had to remind himself of the past, or the present would overwhelm him, too. ØItís worse being the oldest,Æ she whispered. ØThe second you go to Hogwarts youíre old news.Æ And at the mention of school, Ron realized he remembered an Elaine a year above them, in Ravenclaw. ØHave you finished Hogwarts?Æ he asked. ØYeah.Æ ØWere you any good at anything?Æ he asked. At once he wondered if that was insensitive. He was sure Hermione would have thought so, but Elaine just gave a dead-sounding little laugh. ØI had friends,Æ she said, Øwhich was all I really cared about. Or I thought I had friends. A war gives it away, I suppose, whoís a real friend and who isnít.Æ Ron thought he heard bitterness in her whisper. Muggle-born, he thought, heart sinking. Clearly Elaineís friends hadnít felt she was worth the trouble. Suddenly he felt angry at them. ØItís rubbish,Æ he whispered. ØAbsolute bollocks, all of it. That youíre here, and that you donít have better friends. I reckon now that youíre è well, now they can see what idiots theyíve been.Æ She sniffled, but whispered back, ØHowís that for a happy thought,Æ and this time, surprised, Ron was the one to laugh. # Ron loathed every stone in his cell with a passion. He loathed the arch above the narrow door. He loathed the flagstone floor whose grit stuck between his toes. He loathed the tiny arrow slit, through which he sometimes stared at a rare blue sky. He loathed all but the cracked stone through which Elaineís voice issued. At first they spoke every night, collecting happy memories. Then they began to speak through most days, too. Ron soon found that the simple presence of her voice helped keep the darkness at bay as much as the ambient power of the Deluminator. It helped that Elaine was mercurial, hard to predict. She sometimes seemed wounded or defensive, like a skittish animal; she loathed speaking about her parents so much that Ron learned quickly not to ask. She could turn back clever remarks but never sounded pleased with herself. Her happiest memories mostly took place in isolation, and she wanted to do something artistic with her life, something with finesse. She never spoke above a hoarse whisper, and when Ron pressed his ear to the stone, he sometimes closed his eyes and tried to imagine her. He thought about asking her what she looked like, then told himself he was being an idiot. He was nearly always the one to instigate conversation, though. This began to worry him. One afternoon, sitting with his knees hugged to his chest, he struggled to quell a painful memory of his fight with Hermione in fourth year, after the Yule Ball. He hadnít been able to tell what she wanted then, or in sixth year. What if Elaine, his one bright spot in this miserable cell, wished he would leave her alone? Hermione had called him insensitive, Fred and George had always called him an idiot, a prat è what if his presence was just one more part of the torture of this place to Elaine? ØDo you mind me talking to you so much,Æ Ron managed to force out. ØNo,Æ she whispered back. Then, quickly, as if she were ashamed, ØI like it.Æ ØAll right. Good.Æ Ron let out a shaky laugh. ØI like talking to you, too. Even if you fall asleep when Iím talking.Æ ØThat was once,Æ she said. ØAnd I slept better than usual that night.Æ Ron hesitated. ØI could do it again. If youíd like.Æ ØWhat?Æ ØI could talk you to sleep.Æ He felt his cheeks growing warm. ØForget it. Stupid idîÆ ØYes,Æ she broke in. A momentís silence. ØAll right.Æ Ron swallowed. ØWhat shall I tell you?Æ ØYour oldest brother,Æ she whispered back. ØThe one who works for Gringotts è tell me more about him. How you got on when you were younger.Æ And this went on. Each night, one would talk the other to sleep. When it was Elaineís turn, she would veto topic after topic until Ron picked one that was apparently inoffensive to her. She liked describing places: the feeling of tropical air on her skin, the interiors of old buildings. But when it was Ronís turn to lull her to sleep, Elaine always asked him to tell her simple things, like his childhood Quidditch experiences or his favourite foods, things that made him say, ØOh, blimey, where do I start.Æ Eventually Ron realised heíd never described the Deluminator to her. He did so. ØAnd I can see your memories making it stronger,Æ he said. ØWhen you tell me è well, all the things youíve told me, I can see it growing brighter. If youíd just come to the yard, I could show it to you. Itíd make you feel better.Æ But Elaine didnít reply. Ron sighed, pulling away from the wall. Sheíd taken to doing thisî going quiet whenever he tried to coax her out of her cell. He understood wanting to limit contact with the Dementors, but he couldnít help wanting to meet the person on the other side of the wall, the only comfort in the long nights when the Dementors were passing outside their doors. Sometimes, when patrols were frequent, they would whisper back and forth the only words they could manage: ØIím here.Æ Although Elaine never appeared in the prison yard, Ron began to notice other new arrivals. There had been a slow uptick in the number of prisoners. ØWhy do you reckon there are so many?Æ he said to Dirk, Dean, and Ted. ØOne way to find out,Æ said Dirk, approaching one of the new arrivals, a small, balding man. What he told them hit Ron like a sack of bricks. A fortnight prior, the Order of the Phoenix had attacked Malfoy Manor when the entire Ministry had been gathered there. As a result, the Ministry was raiding homes even more aggressively, resulting in leaping numbers of arrests. The Ministry è Umbridge, Ron thought. Harry and Hermione must have been trying to steal her Horcrux at that Christmas event. To remember their quest was jarring. Even more jarring was the idea that heíd been in Azkaban for over two months. ØDid they catch anyone?Æ whispered Ted to the balding man. ØDid the Death Eaters catch any of the Order?Æ The man nodded, looking back with that bleary hopelessness that was so familiar by now. ØThey're in maximum security,Æ the man murmured. ØTop floor.Æ He spoke the names. Then he drifted away, leaving them to stare up at the windowless walls, behind which lay Molly, Arthur, and Percy Weasley. That night, Ron huddled in front of the light, letting its strength and clarity wash over him. He felt more like himself than he had since the outside world. This light è it gave them solace. Could it possibly do more than that? He felt the creeping cold of a patrol. This time, he did not extinguish the Deluminator. He steeled himself and let the blanket fall away. Light flooded his tiny cell. The Dementor outside shied back, confused. It tried to approach the cell door, then shuddered, repulsed, and fled down the hall. Ron hurriedly stashed the Deluminator within his torn mattress. He lay down, pretending at sleep, just in time. He heard the rattling breath of multiple Dementors. They had come to investigate the anomaly. Please, he thought as coldness curled around his heart, all plans and plots fading from his mind, please, let them go è let it go è make it stop è Eventually, they did, leaving him filmed in sweat. He fished the Deluminator out from its hiding place and clutched it to his chest, gasping. The Dementor had retreated from the light. The Deluminator had been powerful enough, then, to scare away one, possibly two è could it be enough to help his family and the Order break free? Dean, Ted, Dirk, and Elaine? Ron remembered how the light had intensified when he had spoken confidently to it. He gritted his teeth and breathed, ØI-I donít give up on people. Thatís another thing.Æ He pictured his mum and dad floors away. Percy, who had apparently chosen to do the right thing. ØI wonít give up on helping them.Æ As the Deluminator burned hot, Ron closed his eyes, pushing pieces in his mind. There could be no sacrifices this time. He needed a perfect game. He looked down at the silver piece in his hand and saw the winning move. # Dementors were blind creatures. They could only feel the essence of human emotions shifting through space. To human guards, there might have been something suspicious in the way Ron moved throughout the prison yard in the days that followed, speaking to new clusters of huddled prisoners. But the Dementors could not distinguish between acquaintances and strangers. They knew nothing of strategy or empathy. Their only impulse was to feed, and Ron would take advantage of their ignorance. He coaxed memory after memory from the prisoners. He heard stories of weddings and births, of golden days with old friends and high academic marks. The stories came in agonised strains at first, then in frantic confessions. When each prisoner whispered into the Deluminator, Ron could see it buoying them up, reminding them of something within that was beginning to stir once more. They made another valuable ally in the prison yard: Augusta Longbottom, who had swept into the place her first day as if Azkaban were a court and she were nobility, her body trembling but her eyes flinty. As Augusta had only been under the Dementorsí influence a matter of hours, she was able to see a number of holes in the plan. ØThere are four human guards on duty,Æ she reminded them. ØWhat do you plan to do about them?Æ She had addressed this to Ted and Dirk, but both older men faced Ron. ØAsk Weasley,Æ said Dirk. ØItís his operation.Æ Augusta Longbottom scanned Ron up and down with a critical eye. ØYes, my grandson has told me about you è Prefect, arenít you? Gryffindor Keeper? Fought with Neville at the Department of Mysteries?Æ Ron found he was standing straighter than usual, Deluminator held in hand. ØYeah, thatís right. As for the guards, their wands might be useful èÆ But while the plan built in secret, Elaine was deteriorating. Ron had told her every detail of their escape plot, yet she still refused to come into the yard. Ron had no chance to try and pull her out, for when he was released for the hour, the Dementors stood sentry at his door, making sure he followed the hunched, shuffling crowd. In isolation this way, she was succumbing to the Dementors. Ron could hear it. She couldnít speak him to sleep anymore, couldnít muster the life. Her happy memories were fewer, feebler. Her voice was turning hollow, losing all inflection, and occasionally she would fall into quiet, keening sobs halfway through a sentence. Sometimes she spoke not to Ron, apparently, but to people in her mind. ØIím sorry,Æ sheíd whisper over and over, nearly insensible. ØIím sorry for everything è I thought it mattered èÆ One night, he whispered her name through the crack in the wall, and she did not respond. ØElaine,Æ he hissed. ØElaine! Are you there?Æ Ron felt as if he were falling headlong into a cold pool. Had she been eating? Heíd forgotten to ask, the past few days. Surely she couldnít be dead? ØPlease be there,Æ Ron moaned. ØPlease be alive.Æ ØOr else what?Æ came the tiny shred of a whisper in response. Ronís heart missed a beat. He whispered back, ØOr else Iíll never get to meet you.Æ An unformed sound that might have been the start of a laugh. ØWeíre escaping soon,Æ he pushed on. ØThis week. Iíve got memories from everyone in the yard. I canít even open the Deluminator anymore, itís painful to look at. Youíve got to be ready.Æ Elaine didnít acknowledge this, didnít say yes or no. ØTonight.Æ # Ron made his move an hour after dinner. Dirk Cresswellís window slit looked out at the Apparition point, and according to his information, the night guard appeared after dinner each evening. So, Ron waited until he was certain the guard had changed, and they would have eight uninterrupted hours on the island. He waited until a Dementor patrol approached his cell. Then he clicked the Deluminator. He hadnít dared press its catch for weeks. Heíd expected a forceful display, but he was unprepared for the brilliance that erupted from its silver lip. The whole room was erased by bright white light, making Ron cry out and fall back. He heard a hoarse rattle at the distant end of the hallîthe Dementor had been thrust back as if by the charge of a Patronus. He clicked the Deluminator again, reclaiming the light into its silver chamber, and bundled into his cot, waiting for them to check on him, the way they had before. Elaineís voice came from the corner. A hoarse whisper. ØRon,Æ she said, as if testing his name. ØYeah?Æ he breathed back. ØGood luck.Æ There was no time to respond. The Dementorsí keys were scraping in the lock of his door. Clunk. The door creaked wide. Ron jumped to his feet, held the Deluminator forward, and let the light blast out. The force of the glow made the three Dementors rocket back like motes of sand caught in a surf. Ron sprinted out after them, down the hall, surrounded by light. The force of the Deluminator was such that luminescence rang all the way up and down the hallway, making the damp stones glitter painfully. Its power was sinking into Ron, every memory and confident assurance he had accumulated, filling his body with a type of surety he had never felt in his life. But there was no time to revel in the feeling. Even now, the Dementors that had unlocked his cell would be searching for their kin. Ron raced down the hall, retracing the way toward the yard, and sprinted down one of the endless spiral stairways to the ground floor. Near the prison entrance was a chamber where the guards forced prisoners to change into uniforms. In that room, Augusta Longbottom had recalled, were a set of steps. ØThey must be to the guardsí quarters,Æ sheíd said. ØWhere they can hide from the Dementorsí effects.Æ It was this chamber that Ron needed to find. He dashed through the first floor, Deluminator outstretched, cries coming from inside the cells as dazzling light roused the sleeping prisoners. Ahead, Dementors were fleeing down side corridors, wheeling and billowing wildly like shadows cast by a strobe. Ron burst into a wide hall. At its opposite end was a door; through the window of that door, rain-lashed night. He sprinted down the hall and into the search chamber, then saw the steps Augusta had described. He barrelled down them, and when he burst through the door into the guardsí quarters, four voices cried out. Ron had one moment to take in the scene. It was a comfortable lounge with a flickering fire in the hearth, an Exploding Snap deck laid upon a long table. A pair of Patronuses patrolled the perimeter of the room, insulating the guards from the misery above. Ron realised the light must be even more blinding to the guards than it was to him, for they were doubled over, sending spells ricocheting off the walls. He threw himself forward, out of the way of a jet of light from the nearest guard, and wrested anotherís wand out of her hand. ØStupefy,Æ he yelled, dropping a third guard. The fourth had fallen to his knees, moaning, weeping, his arms folded over his eyes. Ron sprinted forward and seized the wands from the third and fourth guards, moving erratically to dodge any further attacks from the first. But when he wheeled around to stun the first guard, the man was gone. Ron swore loudly and flung himself up the steps after the man. The guard couldnít be allowed to escapeîcouldnít be allowed to raise the alarmîbut by the time Ron reached the main hall, the prisonís front door was hanging open. The guard was tearing across the islandís rocks to the Apparition Point. Ron slid down the rain-slicked steps of the prison, taking aim at the manís back. ØStupefy!Æ he yelled, the Deluminator casting light through the rain like an immense floodlamp. ØImpedimenta! STUPEFY!Æ But the man was at the Apparition point, the single spot on the island that anyone could enter or exit. Then he was gone. Now phrases that would have appalled his mother were falling freely from Ronís lips. They were meant to have all night to do this, with the guards sealed beneath and the Dementors at bay, all night to free everyone in this accursed place, but that guard would travel to the Ministry and raise the alarm. Ron was sure he had fifteen, maybe twenty minutes. He bolted back toward the stairwell. Dirkís cell was closest, then Tedís, then Deanís. Ron handed them each one of the three wands, and as the corridors of Azkaban rang with confused cries, they pelted up toward the 17th floor, the Deluminator scattering bands of Dementors before them. Dirk did a clever bit of charmwork that made the stairs spin, accelerating them up toward their destination. The maximum security hall. ØOpen them!Æ Ron yelled. ØAll of them!Æ ØAlohomora!Æ The hall rang with Dirkís, Tedís, and Deanís voices, and cell door after cell door burst wide. Ron hurried forward, looking into each cell, huntingîhopingî And there they were. His parents, frighteningly thin, staggering out of their cells, their faces streaked with tears. ØRon,Æ they gasped as one, and then they were throwing themselves upon him in an embrace, making him stagger. Ron clenched his eyes shut and let himself squeeze them for one second before breaking back. ØWhereís Percy?Æ he panted. But when he wheeled around, he saw Percy there, his glasses cracked in one eye. ØHowîÆ Percy stuttered. ØHow?Æ ØNever mind how,Æ Ron said. ØWeíve got to get to the Apparition point. Weíve only got a few wands, and èÆ He turned back. The hallway was filled with people. Augusta Longbottom, Oliver Wood, and Angelina Johnson; Sturgis Podmore andîto Ronís shockîNymphadora Tonks, hugely pregnant and weeping into her fatherís shoulder. The thought of the other prisoners tore at him. Elaine, he thought. There was no time to go back for her. She would die here. Just then, he heard a distant sound that made his heart plummet. It was the crack of Apparition. They all rushed to the windows and peered through the slits. Ron could see wandlight at the tip of the island. More crack sounds pierced the roar of the storm, and the number of lights increased. ØThe Ministry are here,Æ Ted breathed, clutching to his daughterís shoulders. ØLooks like theyíre staying down at the Apparition point for now,Æ Tonks said. ØProbably waiting for us to make a break for it. But they wonít stay there forever.Æ ØWeasley! What do we do?Æ said Dirk, wand held in his hand like a knife. There was a hard expression on his face that told Ron he would fight to the death. Ron looked around and realized they were all facing him, awaiting his direction. A nervous shiver ran through him, but then the Deluminator throbbed in his hand. You can do this, he thought. ØWe canít fight them with three wands,Æ Ron said. ØWe need something else. Some other way to èÆ He looked back through the slit in the wall and heard panicked yells from the Apparition point. The Ministry were trying to conjure Patronuses, but swarms of Dementorsîapparently maddened by the Deluminatorîwere beginning to swoop down on them, attacking their own masters. ØYes,Æ Ron whispered. Their best weapon was the very force that had been sent to imprison them. ØWe need to get everyone into the yard,Æ he called. ØDirk, Ted, DeanîPatronuses.Æ Without hesitation, they did as heíd asked. Three silver animals burst forth, shining brilliantly within the daylight of the Deluminator. ØDirk,Æ Ron said, Øget to the front entrance and block it as best you can. Ted, take everyone here down to the yard. Everyone stay close to your Patronusîit wonít be able to hold off these numbers of Dementors for long. Dirk, once the front door is walled up, meet them in the yard. Dean, you and I will go and start letting people out floor by floor. Letís go!Æ The prison was an immense fortress, but over the past weeks, Ron, Dean, Ted, and Dirk had formed a rough estimate of exactly how many prisoners it held. Before their hour in the yard, there was a low gong. This gong sounded four times a day, which, Dirk reasoned, meant there were four different groups who came to the yard. Their own group had a hundred and fifty prisoners, so theyíd guessed there should be six hundred total or so. This seemed to hold true. On each of Azkabanís seventeen floors, Ron and Dean found a few dozen prisoners scattered throughout the hundreds of cells. Dean cast mass Alohomoras with a sweep of his wand while Ron called to the prisoners staggering out of their cells to stay within view of the Deluminator. Always, at the edge of the light, he could see the Dementors seething, writhing, trying to break through to their quarry. But the Dementorsí numbers were dwindling. When Ron glanced out through the slit windows into the driving rain, he could see more and more furious Dementors fleeing the prison and the Deluminator to attack the arriving Ministry members, whom they seemed to think were easier prey. By the time Ron and Dean had shuttled half the prison out into the yard, an all-out battle was being waged on the edge of the island. They needed every minute they could get. Many of the prisoners were so weak that they hobbled along at an agonising pace. Dean thought to try Transfiguring a quicker way down to the yardîØCould we turn the walls into slides?Æ he suggestedîbut when he tried to cast spells on the walls, they extinguished immediately. The fortress had been reinforced against all magical attack. The biggest risk was the growing number of prisoners in the yard, whose fear and anticipation was becoming an increasing magnet for the Dementors. Ron often had to pelt to the slit windows during the evacuation to shine the Deluminator out into the yard, assisting Tedís and Dirkís Patronuses, forcing the Dementors up and out of the yard. Soon they were nearing Ronís sector of the prison. His throat was beginning to tighten. Would Elaine refuse to come with them? Surely the power of the Deluminator would rouse her, as it had the rest of the prisoners è but sheíd seemed on deathís door this entire week è Ron rounded the corner onto his hall. ØAlohomora,Æ Dean yelled, and the doors burst wide. People began to pour out, dozens, but Ronís eyes were fixed on Elaineís cell door, halfway down. No one was coming through. ØTake this a moment,Æ Ron said, pushing the Deluminator into Deanís hand. Dean nodded, calling to the prisoners while Ron darted through the crowd and into the threshold of Elaineís cell. ØElaine,Æ he panted. ØCome on, itís time! Weíve got to go!Æ She was shaking, her blanket pulled over herself. ØElaine!Æ Ron dashed forward. ØCome off it, you canítîÆ Finally, she lowered her blanket. Ron stopped in his tracks. He had imagined Elaine hundreds of times at this point. Heíd reasoned with himself about the possibility that she wouldnít be lovely, the way heíd pictured her. Shallow, heíd heard in both Ginnyís and Hermioneís disgusted voices when he thought these things, but Ron couldnít help it. His imagination ran away with him. But Elaineís looks werenít the problem. The problem was that she wasnít Elaine. She was someone else. She was Pansy Parkinson. Ron took an inadvertent step back, gaping. ØI told you,Æ Pansy rasped. Her face was gaunt. Her dark hair, always shining around her shoulders at school, was lank and knotted. Her voice, which he remembered as a derisive shriek, was unrecognisable in this hoarse whisper, and with her tattered prison clothing, Ron could hardly picture her in her expensive Twilfit and Tattingís robes, which sheíd seemed to replace every break. It was Pansy Parkinson who had spoken Ron to sleep, Pansy Parkinson who had eased the darkness. Pansy Parkinson who felt least-loved of her family. Pansy who had begun, this past week, to whisper apologies to him. ØI told you,Æ she said again. ØIt would have been worse to go out there with all those people, your people. Didnít I tell you, Weasley?Æ ØDonít call me that,Æ Ron said without thinking. For an instant they just looked at each other. Then, behind him, Dean burst out, ØRon!Æ He had skidded down the hall. ØThis floorís done, weíve got to get to the nextîÆ As the Deluminatorís light fell onto Pansy, making her shudder, Dean broke off. ØPansy?Æ he said. Ron came back to life. What was he doing, standing motionless when they still had four more floors to evacuate? ØThis way. Weíve got to get you out. This way!Æ he repeated, for Pansy hadnít moved. She was staring at him as if she had no idea what he was saying. Finally Ron flew forward, and his hand closed around her wrist. ØCome on,Æ he said. Her face was smeared with grime and sweat, her eyes liquid. She nodded. Ron and Dean had nearly evacuated the ground floor when Ron heard it. A boom. A thud. The Ministry had reached the doors of the prison. They were trying to get in. ØCome on!Æ Dean called, while Ron raced forward, coaxing the last few prisoners out of their cells. Finally, the last group reached the prison yard. It had stopped raining; they could see stars overhead. ØThatís everyone,Æ Ron yelled to the Order, clustered nearby. At once, Ted and Dirk raised their wands, Transfiguring some of the yardís gravel into stone barricades that blocked out the five doors. The panicked prisoners in the yard had all turned as one to the presence of the Deluminator, which Ron held aloft, casting its luminescence over them. He saw fear transform into defiance, pain into hope. For a split instant he saw Pansy in the crowd, straightening up into that posture like a dancerís, which heíd always thought so snobby at school. Then cries rang throughout the prison yard. Hands thrust up into the night. Ron looked up. Now that the Ministry had breached the entrance, the Dementors were returning in force. Up on the prisonís battlements, there were showers of wandlight, Ministry wizards looking down into the courtyard, pointing, yelling. They were directing the Dementors downward. Screams of terror from the other prisoners. Dread filled Ron as a dark shadow knit itself shut over the courtyard, blocking out the starlight. For a mad second he thought he saw the flash of a camera. How long had this siege been going on? Surely upward of an hourîhow far had the news spread? Once the Death Eaters realised it was a full-scale revolt, they would come themselves. Ron thrust the Deluminator up from his side, and the light blasted upward, scattering the Dementors high. Again the Dementors fell upon the Ministry wizards on the battlements. There were screams, blasts of distant light. Ron could see dozens of Patronuses flitting around the battlements. There were cries from within the prison, too, and Ron knew the horror that the Ministry was facing within, trying to find their way through the maze of corridors. The Order turned to Ron. His mother was squeezing his arm with an agonising grip. ØWhatís our plan, Weasley?Æ said Dirk. ØHow are we going to get to that Apparition point?Æ Tonks said, still out of breath. ØWeíre not,Æ Ron said. ØThereís too many of us. But then I thoughtîitís just good sense, isnít it? If we can just undo the Apparition wards, then we can all Apparate out to Order headquarters using these wands.Æ ØUndo the Apparition wards?Æ Percy spluttered. ØButîhang on just a moment. That kind of magicîeven to begin weíll need an Arithmancer to do reams of diagnostic charmwork èÆ Dirk Cresswell gestured at the prison yard. ØThen itís a good thing weíve got six hundred people to pick from, isnít it?Æ He turned back to Ron. ØWeasley, should I use Sonorus to get their attention?Æ ØNo,Æ Ron said, looking up at the battlements. ØSomeone could hear and figure out what weíre up to.Æ He looked among the Order. ØEveryone except Dean, Dirk, and the Tonkses, go through the crowd and find an Arithmancer who knows what theyíre doing.Æ The Order dispersed into the crowd. Ron turned to Dean. ØMate,Æ he said apologetically, Øgive your wand to Tonks, would you? Tonks, Dirk, Ted, we need some cover from above, in case the Ministry start firing spells down on us. I reckon this thingîÆ Ron pointed to the umbrella of light that hung over the courtyardî Øcould ward off minor hexes, but we need more protection. Reckon you can conjure a decent shield for us?Æ The night deepened. Azkabanís impenetrability became clear. As Ron had anticipated, the perfect place to keep prisoners in was also an ideal place to conduct a siege. Though they could hear blasts inside the sheer walls of the fortress, nothing broke through. And although the Ministry made occasional attempts to rain spells down upon them from above, they were so soon beset by the Dementors that hovered over the battlements that they fled for cover. The diagnostic charmwork elapsed as Percy had promised. An Arithmancer named Suresh Madan paced back and forth in a corner of the yard, muttering spells and twitching one of the wands in squiggles that Ron could in no way connect to Hermioneís scrolls of homework from Professor Vector. Meanwhile, the prisoners, tense and fearful, huddled against the wall, waiting, occasionally crying out in warning when Ministry wizards appeared in bands on the battlements. Ron walked among the yard, holding the Deluminator aloft, spreading its light evenly among the crowd. He spoke to people he recognised from his hours in the yard, saying bracing words. He couldnít believe the way they looked at him. Not just with admiration, but with faith. Like Ron was someone who could stand on his own two feet, who could achieve something like this on his own. And he had, Ron supposedîbut not completely on his own. He saw Pansy once, leaning against a wall. Sheíd moved far away from where heíd seen her earlier, as if she hadnít wanted him to find her. A lump rose in his throat when their eyes met. Ron didnít understand the feelings clashing together inside him, so he moved along quickly, not getting close enough to speak, trying to stay focused on the dangers at hand. Ron didnít know how long the siege had been goingîan hour? More?îwhen he heard a noise that made the bottom of his stomach drop out. Shouting from behind one of the five entrances to the yard. Tedís and Dirkís reinforcements had put the doors out of sight, but their Transfigured barricades would not hold in the same way that Azkabanís mighty foundations would. Most of the prisoners had shied back from the door. Ron and the rest of the Order surged forward. Sturgis and Ted, who had both taken over shielding duty, stood at the front with wands raised. ØSuresh isnít done yet,Æ hissed Percy, bobbing anxiously at Ronís side. ØHe needs a few more minutes, and then weíll know how deep the rune anchor is buried, and then we need to search for it before we can destroy it.Æ Ron swallowed. ØWeíll just have to hope there arenít many Aurors behind that door. If we can battle them backîitís our only chance.Æ They steeled themselves as the shouts increased in volume. Ronís fists tightened. Was there a chance? The doorway was only wide enough to fit one at a time, really, which meant that Sturgis and Ted would have an advantage. If they could just stop the Ministry from spilling out into the courtyard è ØReducto!Æ yelled a voice from inside. The stone reinforcement exploded into rock dust. As Sturgis and Ted began to shout hexes, Molly Weasley cried out, ØStop!Æ and flung herself in front of them. ØMolly,Æ Sturgis bellowed, Øget out of theîÆ But Ron knew why his mother had run forward, for he, too, knew the voice that had yelled the Reductor Curse. The first thing to burst over the threshold, through the smoke of rock dust, was a zoo of silver animals. A cantering horse, two flittering birds, a massive, prowling lynx. A tabby cat and a wolf on its heels. An otter. A stag. Then the freckled faces of Fred and George Weasley appeared, and into the inner yard of Azkaban spilled the rest of the Order of the Phoenix, gasping and sweating, bruised and bloodied, their wands held high. Chapter End Notes hooray! annnd weíre all caught up. i estimate that i will have fooled approximately thirty percent of people with the Pansy reveal. i accept my fate. please also note that I have added a chapter count!! Ron gave a shout of recognition. Then the three collided in a crushing embrace, and tears burned Hermioneís eyes as she clung to her two best friends. Behind them, the Order was already walling up the door again. As Kingsley had led them through the secret Apparition point, as they had battled their way through the ground floor of Azkaban, Hermione had been terrified that they would arrive to find the revolt overwhelmed, Ron deadîor worse, Kissed by the Dementors. Now, when the three broke apart, Ronís gaunt face was split in a wide grin. ØThat you, Harry?Æ he said, for Harry wasîas usualîunder Polyjuice. ØAbout time you two showed up.Æ Ronís eyes fell on an approaching Draco, also under Polyjuice as a mousy-haired man in his mid-twenties. Azkabanís halls had taken a heavy toll on him; he looked bloodless, his brow shining with sweat. ØWeasley,Æ he said with a good try at his usual drawl. Recognition spread across Ronís face. ØMalfoy. Canít Polyjuice out the way you talk.Æ ØApparently,Æ Draco said. Then, after a pause, he and Ron exchanged a brief handshake. Hermione traded a relieved look with Harry. There was no time for grudges here. It had been difficult to determine the situation within Azkaban. On their way through the dark, dripping halls, theyíd fended off bands of Dementors and fought two clusters of Ministry workersîbut the bulk of the Azkaban guards seemed to have chased the Ministry into the upper floors. The screams echoing down through the stairwells had been enough to turn Hermioneís blood to ice. Kingsley had visited the prison enough times to know exactly where to find the nearest entrance to the yard, and with Professor McGonagallís Transfiguration to aid them, theyíd been able to uncover the concealed door. Now, those wounded in their push through the prison halls were being tended to. Luna had taken a hex to the side that looked like a third-degree burn, and Fleur was sprawled on the gravel, Bill and Kingsley treating a deep slash down her arm. Elsewhere, though, reunions were taking place. A huge cluster of Weasleys had formed, which Ron and Harry now joined, and Lee Jordan had tackled a skeletal Oliver Wood and Angelina Johnson. Lupin was embracing a massively pregnant Tonks near a man whom Hermione assumed was Tonksís father. Despite the Orderís summer meetings, Hermione had never met him in person. She was studying Tedís kind face when his eyes suddenly went round. Hermione followed his sightline. A figure had drifted out of the crowd of prisoners, attracted by the new commotion. For a horrible instant Hermione thought it was Bellatrix Lestrangeîthe dark hair, the haughty, high-boned features è but no. This woman had a softer, kinder face, a mouth that had not spent years twisted in disgust. ØIs that èÆ Hermione breathed to Draco, who had stiffened at her side. ØMy aunt,Æ he said slowly. ØAndromeda.Æ Now Lupin, Tonks, Ted, and Andromeda were huddled together. ØHow long h-have you been here?Æ Ted was weeping, unable to stop big tears from running down his cheeks. ØMonths. They found me just after we were separated.Æ Andromedaís eyes were bright, but she did not cry. Hermione glanced up at Draco. ØYouíve never met her, have you?Æ ØNo. Iíve only ever seen her in one or two family photos. The ones that werenítÆîhis lips thinnedîØfixed.Æ ØShe looks like you.Æ Draco didnít answer, his eyes riveted on his aunt, uncle, and cousin. It struck Hermione then how similar their circumstances were to Tonksís parents. Tonks had told Hermione that Andromeda had been disowned before sheíd turned twenty, on the cusp of the first Wizarding War. In marrying Ted, sheíd been left penniless, friendless, adrift. It was the same fate that awaited Draco, when they admitted what they were to each other. And yet as Hermione watched Ted embracing Andromeda, her face grew warm, because she found herself thinking, Iíd be there for Draco the same way. Just like that. Hermione cleared her throat. ØThat reminds meîhere.Æ She ushered Draco to re-join Harry and Ron, who had just finished hugging his siblings. ØRon,Æ she said, Øhave you seen Pansy Parkinson and her parents? Are they all right?Æ For some reason, splotches of colour appeared in Ronís cheeks. ØYeah. That way.Æ He pointed. Draco and Hermione turned. Through the crowd, Hermione glimpsed a ghostly, bedraggled Pansy beside her parents, whose faces were still intimately familiar from the Polyjuice. Draco let out a short breath of relief. Just then, two other prisoners jogged up to Ron. ØWeasley,Æ panted the taller of the two, who had copper skin and an ash wand in hand. ØIíve calculated the arithmantic depth alignment.Æ ØPerfect, Suresh,Æ Ron said with apparent understanding. Hermione goggled at him. Why did Ron know anything about arithmantic depth alignment, which Professor Vector had only mentioned briefly in their sixth year? She exchanged bewildered glances with Harry and Draco, but before they could speak, Ron lifted the Deluminator high and called, ØEveryone, gather round, fast as you can.Æ The Order formed a rough circle. Hermioneís chest tightened at the sight of so many of them, shoulder to shoulder, backed by hundreds. ØWe know we canít get everyone back to the Apparition Point,Æ Ron explained. ØSo, weíre going to hit the Apparition wards themselves. This is Suresh Madan. Heís found out how deep theîwhatís it called, mate?Æ ØWard anchor,Æ Suresh replied. ØRight. How deep the ward anchor is buried.Æ ØWhatís a ward anchor?Æ asked Dean. Hermione answered before she could stop herself. ØA ward anchor,Æ she recited, Øis a magical lodestone inscribed with Ancient Runes, which casts a protective or blanket enchantment over a designated area.Æ Suresh gave an approving nod. ØYes. As for this anchor, my diagnostic charms show that itís submerged within the island at a depth of six-point-six to six-point-eight metres.Æ ØWhat,Æ said Fred, scanning the yard with dismay, Øyou mean the anchor could be buried anywhere on this island?Æ ØThatís the thing,Æ Ron said. ØI donít think so. They say Azkabanís resistant to flying, tunnelling, swimming, and every other way of escaping, donít they? So, outside the prison walls, the whole island has got to be warded against digging or blasting charms, in case someone was going to try and break a prisoner free from the exterior. And that means, if the surface of the islandís got no weaknesses or access points èÆ ØThey must have buried the anchor inside the prison,Æ said Bill with a nod. ØYeah.Æ Ron gave the yard a critical scan. ØIt could be here in the yard, but I reckon weíll find it in a basement, or some other spot weíll reach through the building itself. Extra protection, see?Æ ØWell, if weíre going to searchîÆ began Lupin, but before he could finish, shrieks rang throughout the yard, cutting them off. The Order looked up as one. Hermioneís heart plummeted. Hanging over the battlements was the eerie green glow cast by a tremendous, hovering Dark Mark. Draco made a convulsive motion at her side. Hermione wanted to take his hand, but she stopped herself. They had concealed their feelings from the Order this long; even here, she reminded herself that word must not get back to the Malfoys. ØThatíll be Lestrange and the rest,Æ said Kingsley grimly. Minerva, Remus, and I cast some tough spellwork on the front entrance, but that wonít keep the Death Eaters at bay for long.Æ ØThen we get in there and find that anchor now,Æ said Tonks, tensing for the fight. What díyou think, Dirk?Æ A lean, hard-faced man with dark flyaway hair spoke from Ronís other side. ØSheís right, Weasley. ØWeíve been relying on the Dementors to keep the Ministry off the battlements, but with more and more reinforcements è well, your Deluminatorís good, but it canít keep Death Eaters from shooting Killing Curses down at these people.Æ Professor McGonagallís gimlet eyes roved from one prison wall to another. ØTo cover an area of this size, weíll need a dozen conjurers at least.Æ ØOoh, this will help, then,Æ said Luna, unslinging a bag from her back, moving gingerly from her burn. Ron looked dubious, but when she brought out a fistful of wands, he lit up, taking the proffered bag. ØLuna, where in Merlinís nameîÆ ØTheyíre from Ollivander,Æ Luna said happily. ØDirk, Ted, and Sturgisîpass these out to the prisoners whoíve got strong Transfiguration backgrounds, or who are trained fighters.
