She seemed so surprised to see her youngest son obeyed by men twice his age that her mouth stayed open, wordless. ØBlimey,Æ muttered George, trading a startled look with Fred. ØIckle Ronniekins not so ickle anymore.Æ And they were right. Hermione noticed for the first time how tall Ron was standing, with everyone in the circle looking to him for direction. She didnít think sheíd seen him so sure of himself since first year, when theyíd stood upon the giant chessboard, Ron directing Harry and her to safety. Draco must have noticed, too, because he murmured to Hermione, ØWhat exactly happened to Weasley in here?Æ ØI have no idea,Æ she whispered back, smiling. Soon the prison yard was dotted with wands held aloft. Directed by Professor McGonagall, streams of spells rose overhead, forming a stone barrier between the yard and the battlements. Dean held the Deluminator high, its light mingling with the eerie waves of green that pulsed down from the Dark Mark, while Lee and Ginny enlisted the crowd to search the yard for a concealed trapdoor. The rest of the Order congregated around the door into the prison. Hermioneís pulse was speeding again, her hand sweating around her wand. ØShall we split up to search, Ron?Æ Harry asked. Ron nodded. ØGood idea. Letís say two groups. If thereís too few of us, it gives the Dementors an easier go, but too many people running around, and we wonít have space to get out if weíre attacked.Æ Together, Ron and Kingsley divided the Order. Within a minute, Kingsley was leading the second group to another exit door. Their own group formed ranks. Hermione felt reassured by Lupin, Tonks, and Augusta Longbottom at the front. Tonks had transformed her stature, well clearing six feet, in order to reduce the effects of the pregnancy on her stride and fighting speed; meanwhile, Nevilleís grandmother held a wand like a knife. Harry, Ron, and Draco stood at Hermioneís side. ØOne, two è GO!Æ The doors banged open, and the Order streamed back inside the prison. The first thing they did was barricade the door to the yard behind them, Transfiguring it to look like an unremarkable wall to any Ministry members. At once, sealed away from the light of the Deluminator, Hermione felt the difference in the air. The temperature plummeted ten degrees. Her nerves wound tight again, her stomach knotted with dread. But the nine Patronuses swirling around them bolstered her. She lifted her wand so that her otter gambolled around her and Dracoís heels, shedding light upon the eerie prison hallway. ØAll right?Æ she said to Draco. He nodded, although his face was strained; he hadnít been able to conjure a Patronus, only wisps of silver vapour. Farther away in the prisonís maze-like halls, they could hear cries, moans, even explosions overhead. ØThe Death Eaters could break through the front door's enchantments at any moment, Weasley. They may already have done so.Æ There was another dull BOOM overhead, and the Order flinched, looking up at the ceiling. I canít believe I didnít think of it beforeîÆ She fumbled in her robes, then fished the magical compass out of her pocket, which had directed them to the Elder Wand what felt like long days ago, although in fact it had only been a matter of hours. ØThis will point to the most magically saturated item in an area, which must be the ward anchor, mustnít it? Those anchors undergo months of enchantments and potion submersion every year, and this oneís been around for centuries!Æ ØWonít there be interference from the Deluminator?Æ said Harry. ØOr fromîÆ Draco cut himself off, hand straying to his robes, but he, Harry, and Hermione exchanged a look of understanding. Before the Order had left headquarters, Draco had grabbed Hermioneís beaded bag and put in the destroyed Horcruxes, as well as the Elder Wand, in case something went wrong and the Potter Cottage was compromised. ØNo time to wonder,Æ said Tonks impatiently. ØGive it a go, Hermione.Æ Hermione tapped the compass. ØLocarium revelio!Æ The needle spun. At first Hermioneís heart sank, because Harry seemed to be right. The needle was pointing back toward the yard. But then it swung back around, moving like a clockís pendulum toward another attractive point. Back and forth the needle moved, trying to decide between the yard and a spot northwest of them. ØThat way,Æ Hermione exclaimed. ØItís thatîÆ Several things happened at once. The leftmost fringe of the group yelled and slashed their wands out, sending their Patronuses flying toward a dark, rattling mass of Dementors. A wave of cold blasted over the group, and Hermione staggered back, gasping, as ice pierced her to her core. At the same time, a cluster of Ministry workers burst into view at the opposite end of the hall. Every wand rose. Spells shot so fast and thick toward the Order that hardly in time did Hermione scream, ØProtego!Æ ØThis way,Æ Ron yelled, leading them forward into the stairwell. Even as she pelted down the hall, she craned her neck back, making sure that Draco hadnít fallen behind in the stairwell. Yesî there was his Polyjuiced figure at the back of the group. But as he ran faster, both of them making to manoeuvre toward each other, she realized something was itching in the back of her throat. Then Harry and Remus began coughing on either side of her. What was it? She felt something cold and small brush against her cheek. A fragment of gritty stone. Hermione realized what was going to happen a millisecond before it did. In that instant, the only thing she could do was twist toward Draco again. They were perhaps five feet apart. His eyes met hers. With a great tumbling roar of black rock, the ceiling caved in directly above their heads. An arm wrapped around Hermioneís waist and yanked her backward. Tonksís voice yelled, ØREDUCTO!Æ and Hermione, eyes shut, felt rather than saw the effect: a shock wave that pulverized a crashing weight of falling stone. She hit the ground hard and tasted blood. There was a ringing in her ears, voices everywhere. Harry was yelling, ØExpecto patronum! Stupefy! STUPEFY!Æ Hermione opened her eyes. Lupin, who must have yanked her out of the way, was at her side, scrambling to stand as he took aim through the gaping hole in the ceiling. His Impediment jinx struck one of the faces there, but another appeared immediately to take the place of the first. Clouds of black dust billowed through the hallway. Rocks from the cave-in were piled high. Staggering to her feet, hacking, eyes watering, Hermione realized she couldnít see the others. She couldnít see a hint of Draco. Terror flooded her. At her side, her silvery otter flickered and died. All other thought fled her mind. There was only one question left: had he been hit? She had been inches away from death; heíd been only feet from her. Had Draco been hit? ØHermione, come on!Æ Tonks yelled, helping Lupin drag her backward. ØWeíve got to run!Æ ØNo,Æ Hermione cried out, not know what she was saying or doing, trying to wrest free of their vicelike grip. She needed to see the rockfall. She needed to know that there wasnít a pale wrist extending from the tons of stone, that there was no glimmer of blond hair emerging as the Polyjuice faded from his body. His body. The words echoed horribly in her mind. Dracoís body. Dracoís dead form, limp and bleeding è Then she felt the cold washing over her. Lupin and Tonks had pulled her halfway down the hall, and now another clot of Dementors spilled down from the hole in the ceiling, their scabrous hands outstretched. With one last searching look into the impenetrable cloud of dust and spell light, Hermione sprinted after Harry and Ron, tears burning her eyes. By the time they managed to escape the Dementors, they had returned to the ground floor and ducked into a cell for cover. She looked up at him, but he was addressing Tonks, not her. Tonks was wild-eyed, looking just as petrified as she felt. ØThe others know we have Hermioneís compass,Æ Lupin repeated, clearly trying to keep his voice calm. ØThat means your parents know we will find the anchor, Tonks. They wonít risk these halls any longer than they have to.Æ ØYeah,Æ Ron said, still gulping for breath. ØYeah, thatísîright. Theyíll probably goîgo back to the yard and help the others.Æ Hermione swallowed hard. There was some sense to it. Draco was with Tonksís parents and three others. They had the larger group, which meant they had a stronger chance against the Dementors. Remus had managed to pull her out of the way è surely someone had done the same for Draco. Then è then the faster we get rid of the anchor, the faster they can Apparate out.Æ ØExactly,Æ said Lupin. ØGo on, Hermione,Æ said Harry. Hermione pulled the compass from her pocket, its face mercifully intact. How had she managed it? He wondered where she and Ted Tonks had met. Hogwarts? Sometime after? Draco didnít even know their occupations. Having seen Potterís little display during his practical O.W.L., Draco had wanted to prove to himself that he could do anything Potter could do. Heíd practiced through all of June. Then, in July, focusing on the moment heíd been sorted into Slytherin, heíd managed it. A glittering peacock had burst forth from his wandtip and spread its fan there in his bedroom. Two weeks later, Voldemort had decided Malfoy Manor would be the Death Eatersí provisional headquarters. After that, the bird had never come again. # The compass needle was spinning. Ancient runes had been carved into its surface centuries ago, words of protection and stability. Hermioneís mouth tasted of blood, she had bitten her lip so hard. His wand was in his hand as he burst out onto the battlements, into the night air. The wind raked through him. It was freezing, and there was no trace of another wizard out here. There was only the stone, and the tremendous Dark Mark looming overhead in the sky, and as he ran he realised a mad laugh was tearing from his aching throat, because he would die as he had been supposed to die last year: against a battlement, beneath the sign of Lord Voldemort. Behind him, the Dementor was pursuing, and Draco could feel others gathering interest, floating down from the night sky. His aunt and uncle were gone, and with them his single chance at acceptance from his family. Every effort had been for nothing. Draco remembered standing upon the Astronomy Tower, gazing into Dumbledoreís face, able to relyîone last timeîon the power of someone older and wiser. That world was no more. There was nothing, anymore, except for the Dementor slowing before him. The terror coming from his body must be a feast to it. Dracoís arms were hooked over the battlements as he felt his nerveless wand slip from his fingers. He imagined he could hear her voice. ØMannaz eihwaz èÆ Dracoís eyes eased open. He couldnít help it. He could see Hermione speaking the runes aloud, the furrow in her brow, the tremor in her lips, and he realised he had been wrong. She was alive. Fevered hope tore through him, and on its heel came a crash of memories. Draco remembered the feel of her lips the first time theyíd kissed, sitting at the table in headquarters, the way some delicate glass inside him had seemed to shatter, releasing a flood of impossible heat. He remembered laughing with her on Halloween, her fingertips upon his cheek. He remembered realising in Muggle London, sitting in a grubby little chip shop, that he would do anything not to let go a world they might share, and all this seemed to form together until Draco could see it clearly. He was in love with her. He loved Hermione Granger beyond all else, in a way he had never loved anyone or anything. He loved her, and to the last, he could not let go. And now more memories were coming to him, not so much happy as rich with hope, full of a promise of the new world. The surprise on Hagridís face when Draco had made his stiff apologies and bowed to Buckbeak. The Order listening to his suggestions at their meetings, their surprised faces when heíd defended Hermione to his parents. His handshake with Weasley, his shared laughter with Potter, the way Pansy had lied to keep him safe even after knowing he had fought for the Order. All this was not meaningless. This was a promise of what was nextîif he could only survive to see it made real. The Dementor was lifting its hands to his hood, others spilling down like ink out of the starry sky, blotting out all light. Draco drove his hand into the depths of his robe and felt Hermioneís bag, and as his fingers closed around the beaded exterior he felt another giddy wave of love pulse through him for her brilliance, her foresightîhe was pulling the bag free îplunging his hand into itî Dracoís hand met the handle of the Elder Wand. But the silver animal that exploded from his wandtip was not a birdîor was it? It seemed to have wings è The creature charged the Dementors away from him, keeping them at bay. As it cantered back around, battling wave after wave of the creatures, Draco, near delirious, saw it for what it was. It landed on four strong hooves. It beat its great wings, repelling Dementors. It pawed at the dark stone and lifted its strange, sleek head high. It was a Hippogriff, proud but brittle, sensitive and sharpîthe fusion of two beings into one. Hermioneís voice had risen in a crescendo, and now reached its fever pitch, and with the speaking of the last syllable, a crack like a whip rang out that could surely have been heard a country away, the breaking of the wards. Then the sounds of Disapparition followed all throughout the prison, ringing up from the yard, out from the distant hallways, crack after crack of escape, building into a sound like cleansing rain, as Draco turned on his heel and left Azkaban behind. Chapter End Notes i would like to offer a personal apology to everyone who wanted an andromeda/narcissa reunion tumblr | instagram Chapter 25: WandofElder Chapter Notes the angst is strong in this one, kids See the end of the chapter for more notes Draco had not Apparated into the front garden of headquarters. ØYouíre alive,Æ she gasped, holding him so tightly that he could feel the pounding of her heart. ØAre you hurt?Æ She pulled back and began to examine him, face drained of colour. ØIím all right,Æ Draco panted. ØIím è yeah. Iím here.Æ Her panicked expression didnít ease. He pointed to the door to the hall, where Lucius and Narcissa Malfoy had appeared. The sight of his parents only served to make everything feel less real. Draco had never seen them look so afraid. His fatherís cheeks were stained red as if by sherry, and a copy of last nightís Evening Prophet was clutched in his motherís shaking hand. ØWhere is he?Æ demanded Narcissa. ØWhere is my son?Æ ØIím here,Æ Draco said hoarsely. ØIím under Polyjuice, Mother. Iím all right.Æ Tonks and Lupin, who hadnít noticed him upon the steps, whirled his way. ØDraco,Æ said Lupin sharply. ØWhat happened? Were the others captured?Æ A hush spread throughout the whole room. Draco held tightly to the banister. Suddenly he felt sick. He even felt that it would have been easier to fall in Azkaban than to stand here, the one who had to speak the words. ØTheyíre èÆ He tried to stop his voice shaking. ØThey didnít èÆ ØWhere are my parents?Æ Tonks burst out, forcing her way through the crowd. Lupin caught her by the door, holding her at bay. ØTheyíre dead,Æ Draco rasped. A terrible scream tore from Tonksís throat. She sank downward, Lupin half-bearing her weight. ØHow?Æ Lupin said over her sobs. Draco recounted as much of the last half-hour as he could. Throughout, there were stifled noises throughout the front room. When he described Woodís and Longbottomís futile heroism, Angelina Johnson began to weep into a pale George Weasleyís shoulder. Lupin let out a low, agonised sound when he learned how Ted and Andromeda had fallen. ØWho d-did it?Æ Tonks demanded, her eyes wild. ØWho cast the curses? W-was it Crabbe or Greyback? Rowle?Æ Draco shook his head. ØDonít know. è I couldnít tell.Æ Something made him look to his parents, then. To anyone else, they might have looked unaffected, but Draco could discern the tightness of his motherís grip on the doorjamb. ØBut Crabbeís dead,Æ Draco went on, numb. ØOr as good as. A Dementor Kissed him è itís the only reason I got away.Æ The words were no comfort to Tonks. She sagged lower in Lupinís grip. Draco could feel his own knees shaking, too. Hermione, several steps above him, made a little movement toward him. He apportioned more weight to the banister. ØThey were trying to get back to you.Æ His voice had shrunk to a jerky mutter. ØIím sorry.Æ He braced for his cousin to look at him with hatred, to accuse him of not trying hard enough to save her parents. But Tonks met his eyes and nodded, and Draco knew it was in thanks. He nodded back, and at the same time that her knees gave out, so did his. # HISTORIC BREACH AT AZKABAN The insurgent group known as the Order of the Phoenix, writes Rita Skeeter, Special Correspondent, has struck another blow to Wizarding societyîthis time at Azkaban Prison. Near midnight on Friday, saboteurs orchestrated the violent breakout of Azkabanís full population, more than 600 prisoners. The Daily Prophet can exclusively report that the revolt was spearheaded by convict Ron Weasley, 17, a close associate of notorious madman Harry Potter. Other criminals now at large include those responsible for the massacre at Malfoy Manor in December. Now an already grieving Wizarding community must contend with further losses of life. Eyewitnesses report that the Dementors, who have guarded Azkaban faithfully for many years, were driven out of their senses by the prisonersí revolt. Between the merciless attacks of the Order and the Dementorsí confusion, 32 casualties have been tallied so far. Long famed as an impenetrable fortress, Azkaban had not seen a mass breakout until two years ago, when several prominent members of Wizarding society, wrongfully imprisoned, were liberated. At the time, articles by this paper failed to acknowledge the flawed charges that led to those citizensí unjust sentences (for a full retraction, see pg. 7). In a Saturday morning address to the nation, Minister for Magic Pius Thicknesse said, ØWith the destruction of Azkaban Prison, so long a bastion of a lawful Wizarding Britain, we fear that the next goal of the Order of the Phoenixîand of its unhinged commander, Harry Potter îis a full-scale coup.Æ Minister Thicknesse went on to announce a number of new national safety protocols. Firstly, Diagon Alley has again been put under a no-Apparition mandate, in order to increase security at Wizarding Londonís most historic site. Additionally, as Potter and his cohort can be expected to wield Dark magic without hesitation, the Ministry of Magic plans to release instructive pamphlets covering the basics of Dark magic, which are presently being taught at Hogwarts School to prepare the next generation to protect themselves. Despite these measures, many across the country remain uneasy about national security. Startling reports within the Ministry suggest that Minister Thicknesseîwho has been spotted in public dissolving into unexplained fitsîmay be buckling under the strain of these high-profile attacks. ØThicknesse isnít up to it,Æ reported one plain-spoken source in the Ministerís office, whom the Prophet interviewed under the condition of anonymity. ØI give him three months at most. As for whoíll succeed himîWizarding Britain doesnít need another bureaucrat or Department Head in fancy robes. We need someone who guessed the danger Potter posed and tried to stop it. At this point, we need a bloody saviour.Æ # The Order held a memorial that night. All evening, they shared stories and memories of the five fallen. Draco knew better than to ask his mother if she would attend, despite the fact that she had neither slept nor eaten since heíd brought news of her sisterís death. All day she had moved around their tent like a ghost, hand clasped around a cup of cold tea. Draco didnít know what to make of it. His mother had never shown a hint of emotion toward her lost sister besides disgust or shame. But when he returned to the tent from the memorial, he found his parents gazing into the fire without speaking, and he could practically see recollections shuttered away behind his motherís blue eyes. ØSit, Draco,Æ said his father, lips barely moving. Draco did. ØWe feared the worst this morning,Æ Lucius murmured. ØYou could have been killed. You must never allow Potter to drag you into something like this again.Æ Draco cast about for some excuse. ØI didnít realise how far it would go,Æ he said mechanically. ØI thought theyíd ask me to be a lookout or something. è I was trying to blend in.Æ ØYour life,Æ said Narcissa, Øis more important than the keeping of appearances.Æ They were the first words she had spoken since morning. Draco looked into the fire and felt resentment simmering within him. It made him feel slightly more alive. ØYou canít have it both ways,Æ he muttered. ØYouíre the ones who want me to spy on the Order, or whatever it is. Get information back to the Dark Lord somehow so we can re-join. è If I was going to get information out of the Order, theyíd need to trust me, which means doing things like this.Æ ØThen youíre considering the possibility,Æ said Lucius. Dracoís resentment bottomed out into exhaustion. He couldnít care about his parentsí maddening nostalgia for the cause just then. He couldnít even be angry with his mother for concealing everything about Andromeda from him. He had scraped a few hours of shallow sleep throughout the day, but the Dementors had sapped so much of his strength. è He didnít want to think anymore, didnít want to feel. He just wanted to be unconscious. ØIím considering every possibility,Æ Draco said stiffly. He stood and stalked down the hall to his bedroom. In the confused aftermath of the battle, he hadnít had time to change. Now he stripped off his robes, still gritty with rock dust, and his sweaty clothes beneath. He rinsed himself in the shower and fumbled his pyjamas onto clumsy limbs, swaying. He had barely lain his head upon the pillow when he was asleep. He was sitting in a compartment of the Hogwarts Express. Crabbe and Goyle sat on either side of him, Pansy, Blaise, and Theo Nott seated opposite. ØReally, Draco,Æ Blaise was sniffing, ØI donít see what the problem is. Iíve bought our sweets the past six years.Æ ØI just donít want to go out there, thatís all,Æ said Draco dismissively, glancing toward the compartment door. ØBut I want an Everlasting Gobstopper,Æ said Pansy with a scowl. ØYeah,Æ said Theo, Øand you owe me about a dozen Pumpkin Pasties.Æ Draco was beginning to feel agitated. The compartment was swaying. Outside, the countryside was darkening, but the lights inside were growing strangely bright. ØI suppose you two are going to say I should buy you Cauldron Cakes?Æ he said, shooting looks at Crabbe and Goyle. They laughed their usual grunting laughs. ØYeah,Æ said Crabbe, Øthatís right.Æ ØFine,Æ said Draco, standing. All his friends rose with him. Their faces blurred strangely. ØGo on,Æ said Goyle, folding his arms. They closed ranks. Draco faced the compartment door. He didnít want to go outside, but he knew he had to. He drew the door open and stepped out into a dark stone corridor. The instant he recognised the halls of Azkaban, he whirled around, but the compartment door had gone, replaced by a single torch flickering in a spiky bracket. He began to walk, faster and faster, until he broke out in a run, trying to find an exit, any door at all è ØHelp! è Draco, help!Æ cried a voice behind him. He turned. Andromeda lay before him in the corridor, clasping Tedís hand. Their eyes were filled with pain and fear. Their wounds pooled upon the flagstones. Draco tried to move for them, but when he looked down, his feet had sunk into the stone. Even as he watched, the stones swallowed his ankles. He was being submerged, little by little, and the dark pools beneath Ted and Andromeda were creeping toward him, glittering like garnet in the torchlight. Now he was sunk in to the knees, the stones as cold as icy water è A rattle at his back. He twisted around and saw a Dementor filling the end of the corridor. It lifted its skeletal hands to its hood. Draco tried to grab for his wand, but his hands were trapped in the stone. It was then that he saw four limp bodies sprawled around him. Longbottom, Cresswell, Wood, and the nameless Muggle man. Their faces were all turned in his direction, their unseeing eyes levelled at his own. Andromeda was struggling more feebly now. Ted had gone still. His aunt reached for him, accusation in her eyes. ØDraco èÆ she said in a voice that had begun to rattle. Then the Dementor bent over him. There was nothing but the scabbed grey skin stretched over its hollow eye sockets. Draco woke up screaming into his pillow. He twisted and thrashed in his sheets, trying to free himself, his body drenched in sweat. He was freezing, his mind filled with such blank terror that when he stared around the room he didnít understand where he was, or even that he had been asleep. He tore out of his bed and stumbled into the bathroom, knocking his elbow painfully against the sink. His heart beat hard against his ribs. Only then, staring at his face in the mirror, did reality begin to come back to him. It was just a dream, he told himself. Just a dream è But tears were finally burning their way free. A harsh sob worked its way out of his throat, and he held his right forearm over his eyes so he couldnít see his face. More sobs came, racking his whole frame. No lucid thoughts existed in his mind, only a mix of dark colours. A sickly, violent pulse beat in his stomach. Before he knew what he was doing, he was fleeing his room, moving through the twinsí tent and out into the moonlit garden. Within a minute he was back inside the kitchen of the Potter Cottage. As he shut the door behind himself, Draco drew slow breaths. There was the enchanted plate on the wall, where the painted rooster still pecked at its seeds. There was the table where he and Hermione and Potter had spent months planning in safety. He wiped his face clean, his hands steadying. The cottage gave some reliefîit had become as much of a home as he had anymoreîbut now that he was inside, he knew, too, why he had come here. Doubts coiled around him. He didnít move for the hall. He imagined knocking on Hermioneís door, interrupting the peaceful sleep that she sorely needed. When she saw him in this state, she would worry. She might even pity him. Did he really want her to see him like this? He caught sight of himself in the glazing of the windows. His dishevelled hair and tear-oiled eyelids, the pallor of his skin. Draco tasted something bitter. Was he really so weak, so childish, running to her for consolation after a nightmare? He was sure Potter and Weasley would never have done it. He slipped back out into the garden. The moon was nearly full overhead, the starry dome of the sky immense. He told himself nothing was closing in on him, but his heart rate remained elevated, the dream still hovering over him like a hand about to grasp his shoulder. When something creaked behind him, his heart plummeted. He whirled around, nearly slipping as he took a step back. Then his frantic thoughts subsided. Hermione stood upon the steps, her eyes red and puffy. He understood without a word exchanged. Heíd pictured Hermione sleeping peacefully in her bedîbut heíd been wrong. Sheíd been lying awake in tears. Sheíd heard him come into the cottage and known it was him. She had wanted him near, too. They moved for each other at the same time. It was not a relief or a kindness, the kiss. It was a painful, clumsy press of mouth to mouth, like resuscitation. Hermione gripped him so tightly that his ribs hurt, and when she bit down on his lower lip, he felt a jolt down to his fingertips; he wished she would do it again, more forcefully. He steered her away from the door, away from the patio, into the dark little strip of garden alongside the cottage, where the shadows swallowed them completely. Draco sank against the brick wall and held her close, breathing in the scent of her still-damp curls, allowing his mind to empty. Their hands fumbled, their breath mingled, and somewhere in him, he felt the seed of hope from which had grown his Patronus. It couldnít make him forget, nor even enable him to speak, but it filled some part of the space carved out inside him. For now, that was all he could ask. # They began to meet each night. At times, Draco felt that the prospect of his next stolen hour with Hermione was the only thing keeping him afloat, because the nights were filled with variations on the same dream, and the days were nothing short of chaos. Following the breakout, the Potter Cottage had become something of a tent city, both its gardens quilted with fabrics. Luckily, the number of tents diminished as the week wore on and Order members relocated to safehouses. A disused Muggle resort, a mildewed hotel, a crumbling fortress, and even an old monastery had been transformed into places that could hold scores of their allies. Now, each site teemed with Azkabanís former inhabitants. The procurement of enough food and water to sustain nearly 650 people was a mammoth task, but the Orderís network of supporters proved up to it. Before Aberforthís exposure, he had disseminated the Orderís drop location to a few trusted allies among the networkîand the breakout at Azkaban had triggered an explosion of support. Every day, the cave that served as the drop location overflowed with well-stocked hampers and sacks of staples, which then required distribution to the safehouses. ØThe question,Æ said Kingsley during a meeting one night, Øis how to organise ourselves. Weíve finally got real numbers. Now, weíve got to move these forces against Death Eaters across the country.Æ He glanced to Ron. ØYou coordinated them in Azkaban, Weasley. What do you think of them?Æ Ronís ears went red at Kingsley asking his opinion, but he answered readily. ØI doubt everyone will be up to that. Theyíve just come out of prison, havenít they? A lot of them will be too weakened to fight, or too scared. Then thereís others who arenít even for the Order, but landed in Azkaban because they crossed the Death Eaters. Like è like the Parkinsons. Theyíre not about to run out onto the front lines for us, even if we did just save their necks.Æ Arthur Weasley looked troubled. ØI must say, I have to wonder if itís secure, keeping people like that in Order safehouses.Æ ØYes,Æ agreed Fleur. ØWho is to say zey will not give ze location away?Æ Broad-shouldered Sturgis Podmore lifted a hand. ØIíd be willing to Obliviate their memories of the safehouses. We could return them to London. Could be theyíd reintegrate into society.Æ ØNo,Æ said Draco sharply. ØThatíll get them captured and interrogated.Æ ØHereís an idea,Æ said Ron. ØWhat if we sorted them all?Æ The Order looked at him nonplussed. Ron reached for a piece of parchment and scribbled four symbols. ØWeíve got four safehouses, donít we? I dunnoîmade me think of the Sorting Hat. What if we assign each safehouse a purpose, and move people into them depending on what theyíd do for the Order?Æ ØThat could be a good move, Ron,Æ said Lupin, extending a hand for the piece of parchment. Flanked by Kingsley and a sleepless-looking Tonks, Lupin considered the symbols for a while, then spread the parchment flat on the coffee table so that the whole Order could gather around to see. ØWell,Æ Tonks said, tapping the symbol of the fortress, Øthis oneís obviously a good shout for the people who want to fight. Extra protection in case they draw counterattacks.Æ ØI thought that, too,Æ said Lupin. He pointed to the hotel. ØThis more comfortable location could be for those who wonít assist our efforts, either because theyíre injured, recuperating, or unsure of their loyalties, but whom we still need to keep safe.Æ Professor McGonagall peered critically at the parchment, then pointed to the monastery. ØThis one is centrally located. That makes it the best choice for those who wish to write letters to Order sympathisers or assist with supply collections.Æ Kingsley finally touched the resort. ØThen this one should be for the gathering and interpretation of Death Eater intelligence. We can send word throughout the network that any tips on vulnerabilities or enemy activities should be sent here.Æ So it was decided. Each safehouse was assigned two or three Order guardians who could return to headquarters immediately if an attack were to occur. Draco was glad for it. While the Potter Cottage was still busy after this thinning of their numbers, it was no longer so full of people as to be in danger of collapse. That gave him, Hermione, Potter, and Weasley time to return to their own endeavours. When they finally had a full afternoon to themselves, they locked themselves into the nursery upstairs. Sitting in the long, thin shafts of wintry light, alone together for the first time since October, Draco felt unexpectedly tense. Weasley cleared his throat. ØLook, before you say anything, Iím sorry for running out last autumn. What I said, just before I leftîI didnít really mean any of it.Æ ØYou donít need to apologise, Ron,Æ said Hermione, her cheeks pink. ØYeah,Æ said Potter, Øyouíve made up for it about a hundred times since then.Æ Ron glanced uncertainly to Draco, who shrugged and said, ØYou can grovel a little more if youíd like, Weasley.Æ The others laughed, and the tension in Dracoís chest eased. After that, the air felt clearer, and they filled Weasley in on every Horcrux-related event of the previous months. It was a relief to focus on their shared goal againîand Draco had to admit that Weasley proved an excellent audience. He gasped, exclaimed, and swore through their tales of infiltrating Rita Skeeterís house, stealing Slytherinís locket at the Christmas gala, and destroying both the diadem and the locket. But Weasley was most interested in the silver doe Patronus. ØYou still donít know who sent it? All these people at headquarters, and it doesnít belong to any of them?Æ ØWe think itís someone whoís still undercover, working for the Ministry,Æ said Hermione. ØThat could be how they got hold of the bequests, you see? If only we could get in touch with themîthey could be a big help. Clearly Dumbledore really trusted them.Æ ØAnd speaking of things Dumbledore didnít tell us èÆ Potter added, nodding to Draco. Draco had hardly thought of the Elder Wand in the frenzy of the past few days. Now he withdrew it from his pocket and showed it to Weasley. When they explained, Weasleyís jaw dropped. ØNo,Æ he breathed. ØThatís not è? No way.Æ ØIt is,Æ said Hermione, eyeing the wand with mistrust, Øbut as long as itís loyal to Snape, itís dangerous.Æ ØDangerous? Itís brilliant!Æ Weasley crowed. ØIf Harry can just beat Snape, then heís got a secret weapon that none of the Death Eaters know about!Æ A faraway look came over him. ØIf we can orchestrate something to get Harry into Hogwarts è surprise Snape somehow èÆ But Draco had ridden that train of thought dozens of times at this point. ØYouíll never surprise Snape. Half the Death Eaters and the Order thought he was a double-crosser for years. Heíll have been preparing for attacks from any direction all that time.Æ He ran his fingertips over the Elder Wandís engravings. ØBut I think there must be a way to win the wand without beating Snape in a duel.Æ ØYou do?Æ said Hermione, looking surprised. ØWhy?Æ ØBecause I used it at the breakout,Æ Draco said. ØOn the battlements, I lost my wand, and I cast a Patronus with the Elder Wand. A real Patronus.Æ Draco felt heat creeping up his neck. Even after several nights of their clandestine meetings, he hadnít told Hermione about the Patronus, nor about the feelings that had enabled him to conjure it. He had no idea how to tell Hermione he loved her. He had no idea if he even should. They had been together for scarcely three months. Was it too quick? And would it show too much? Draco had never been so open with anyone as with her, but the idea of telling her he loved her and receiving silence in return was excruciating. They already had enough to contend with, now that Weasley was back in the mix. They hadnít yet decided how or when to tell Weasley about their relationship, but they knew they needed to do it before he found out himself. Weasleyís voice derailed these thoughts. ØWhat was your Patronus, then, Malfoy?Æ he said, breaking into a grin. ØPlease tell me it was a ferret.Æ ØVery funny. It was a Hippogriff.Æ ØOf course,Æ Hermione said with a knowing smile that made Dracoís palms tingle. ØNot bad,Æ said Potter, grinning too. ØAll right, thenîletís talk to Ollivander.Æ They found the wandmaker in the downstairs bedroom, as always. He had made a full recovery from his imprisonment at Malfoy Manor, except that he was rather jumpier than before, and he had transformed the bedroom into a wandmakerís workshop. Slowly revolving metal tools were situated on a long table beneath the window, and baskets full of wandwood stood lined along the wall. Before the battle, Ginny and Luna had been Ollivanderís assistants in wandmaking. Now, though, Ginny had turned her efforts back to the pamphlet campaign, the plans for which had expanded tenfold after the Azkaban breakout. Their re-information flyover, scheduled for late March, now included many smaller towns with substantial Wizarding populations, although the riskiest drops would still take place at the Ministry, Hogsmeade, and Diagon Alley. As for Luna, she was still recuperating from the burning curse. Ollivander, who seemed to have taken a liking to Luna during their shared captivity, had insisted that in her waking hours she could continue helping with the wands if she wished. When Draco filed into Ollivanderís room after the others, he saw Luna dozing on the bed. They all traded a look. Draco wondered if they should come back when Luna was not present, but she rarely left the wandmakerís side these days. Asleep was likely the best they could hope for. The others seemed to decide the same. Potter spoke quietly so as not to wake her. ØMr. Ollivander, we have some questions to ask you.Æ The old man surveyed them, lowering a glimmering strand of unicorn hair. Since they had first questioned him about the Elder Wand shortly after his rescue, he had seemed wary of them. ØMore questions about the topic we previously discussed? Miss Granger has scheduled me to assist with kitchen duties this evening èÆ ØWe wonít take much of your time,Æ said Potter, pointing his wand at the closed door. ØMuffliato.Æ ØVery well. What is it you wish to know, Mr. Potter?Æ ØIs there some other way to win the Elder Wandís allegiance than by killing its previous owner?Æ Ollivander looked surprised. ØYes, of course.Æ He settled into a chair, his white hair glowing in the light of sunset. ØThe Elder Wandís allegiance has coincided with murder, but that is likely because of the thirst that it arouses in its pursuers. Among those of us who study wandlore, it is a commonly held opinion that an act of force, of mastery, is sufficient to bend one wizardís wand to anotherís will.Æ ØRight,Æ said Weasley, Øbut we mean, youíd definitely need to beat the previous owner somehow? You couldnít just èÆ His eyes strayed unhelpfully to Draco. ØJust hold it for a while, or try to cast spells with it until it warms up to you?Æ Ollivander stroked a wisp of hair from his wrinkled forehead. ØThe connection between wand and wizard is a complex one, Mr. Weasley. Ephemeral connections are forever forming between wands and wizards, mapped along the lines of power, desire, ownership, and surrender. This is not only true of the Deathstick.Æ He sighed. ØBut if a wand is to recognise a new master, yes: that new master must, in some way, best the old.Æ ØBut thenîÆ Weasley said, looking with even more obvious bewilderment at Draco. Potter broke in. Clearly he didnít want Ollivander to suspect too much. ØThank you, Mr. Ollivander. We appreciate your help.Æ ØOf course.Æ Ollivander rose, looking pleased that the conversation was at an end. ØExcuse me è my dinner duties èÆ The frail old man slipped out the door. Something was stirring in the back of Dracoís mind è facts beginning to swirl together, to form into some new truth. He brushed the cool wood of the Elder Wand in his pocket. The new master must best the old è yet the wand had worked for him è But before he could speak, someone else broke the silence. All four of them leapt and whirled toward the bed. Draco had completely forgotten Luna was there. ØWere you talking about the Elder Wand?Æ Luna was lying on her side, her protuberant eyes half open, her hair straggly from several days in bed. They all exchanged startled looks. ØWhy, have you heard of it?Æ said Weasley. ØOf course,Æ she said with a bit more strength, almost conversational. ØBut I have to admit, Iím surprised that you four have heard of the Deathly Hallows.Æ ØThe what?Æ Weasley asked. Draco stared at Luna. He knew theyíd heard the term beforeîbut where? Had they come across it in a book somewhere during their research? Hermione beat him to it. ØThe Deathly Hallows,Æ she whispered. ØRita told us è Bathilda heard them talking about it.Æ In a trice, all four of them were crowding around Lunaís bedside. Potter crouched to her eye level and said urgently, ØLuna, what is that? What are the Deathly Hallows?Æ ØOh, the quest for the Hallows is a very ancient one. My father is a longtime Quester.Æ She took her wand from the bedside table. ØIíd like to join someday, too.Æ With her wandtip she traced a fiery symbol in the air, one they all knew well. It was a line enclosed by a circle, both enclosed by a triangle, resembling a triangular eye. ØQuesters believe that the Hallows are described in the Tale of the Three Brothers,Æ Luna went on. ØIím sure you heard the story growing up, Ron, Draco. è The Elder Wand is at the centre, you see?Æ She pointed to the line. ØThe circle represents the Resurrection Stone, and the triangle is the Cloak of Invisibility. Brought together,Æ she said with dreamlike finality, Øthey make a wizard the Master of Death.Æ # As Draco followed the others back upstairs into the nursery, none of them spoke. Even after the door was locked, the nursery placed under Muffliato, the silence lingered. ØSo,Æ said Potter after several minutes. ØDumbledore wasnít just trying to tell us about the Elder Wand. This was what he meant. This was what he and Grindelwald were after. è The Deathly Hallows.Æ ØCome off it,Æ said Weasley, looking horrified. ØYou think he wanted us to find all three, on top of the Horcruxes? Blimey, not asking much, was he?Æ ØHold on,Æ Hermione said with open incredulity. ØAll three what? You two canít really believe that thisîthis Resurrection Stone exists?Æ Potter went on as if she hadnít spoken. ØDumbledore didnít need us to find all three Hallows,Æ he said quietly. ØHe knew we already had one.Æ ØWhat?Æ said Draco sharply. ØWhat do you mean?Æ ØDumbledore borrowed my parentsí Invisibility Cloak before they died,Æ said Potter. Iíve been wondering for months what it means, why he would have left it to me. It says I open at the close, but what could fit inside it that could do any good? I thought maybe a dose of Felix Felicis, or something è but it all makes sense now.Æ Potterís voice was rising, the fire in his eyes burning more brightly. ØDumbledore showed me a memory last year of the Horcrux he destroyed, the ring. There was something engraved there, and Riddleís grandfather was bragging about it. It was the sign of the Hallows. Iím certain. è The stone in the ring was the Resurrection Stoneîand after Dumbledore destroyed it, he left the Stone to me. In here.Æ Potter held up the Snitch. ØHe accounted for two of the Hallows.Æ Draco pictured Dumbledore penning his will. He pictured the old manís dying hand laid beside the parchment è the ancient fingers inscribing the sign of the Hallows into The Tales of Beedle the Bard. Cloak è Stone è Wand. The last piece clicked into place in Dracoís mind. Time seemed to stop. His nerveless hand slipped inadvertently into his pocket and closed around the Elder Wand. ØYouíre wrong, Potter,Æ he said, voice low. ØHe accounted for all three.Æ The Gryffindors faced him, Hermione and Weasley incredulous, Potter alert. ØHow?Æ said Potter sharply. ØThat night on the Astronomy Tower,Æ Draco said. ØWhen you and Dumbledore got there è I disarmed him.Æ He drew the Elder Wand from his pocket. ØI disarmed Dumbledore.Æ All the air seemed to have gone from the room. The others didnít move, didnít even seem to breathe. Draco looked down at the glimmering stick of wood in his palm. This was why the wand felt so correct in his hand, as much as his own ever had. It was why the Patronus had come. They didnít need to win the wandís allegiance from Snape. The convoluted path of the Elder Wand had already brought it back to its master. ØAnd Dumbledore knew that,Æ Potter whispered. ØSo, all we had to do was find the wand. It would have been easy, if heíd been buried with itîand you and your family were supposed to be kept safe at headquarters è he all but left us a map.Æ Draco nodded. He should have felt victorious, triumphant, excited. Yet as he regarded the Elder Wand, he only felt distant from himself. Strange collisions seemed to be happening in his mind, hypotheticals drifting into and out of being. If heíd only known the unbeatable wand was under his control, he would have used it at Azkaban from the start. Would things have turned out differently? Might he have been able to cast some obstruction that had prevented Oliver Wood and Augusta Longbottom from throwing themselves into the fray? Could he have charmed the whole group to run more quicklyîto escape? In the final minutes, what if he had been able to overcome Crabbe, Greyback, and that Ministry wizard? Perhaps, with the Elder Wand, he could have cast a shield, grabbed Andromedaís arm, and run, knowing they could survive the battlements. He bit down on his tongue, tasting longing, frustration, bitterness. But then he heard his auntís voice whisper in his memory. Donít look back, sheíd told him. They had been her last words. It took a mammoth effort, but Draco dragged his mind out of the swirling sea of hypotheticals. He was alive. Hermione was alive, the Order, his friends, his parents. He had to turn his mind forward, to focus on the potential of the wand. è It might be the key to the future, even if it could never unlock the past. ØIíve got to get this open.Æ Potterís voice was so hoarse with excitement it was practically a croak. Draco looked up. Potter was turning the Snitch over and over in his hands. The fever in his gaze had intensified; he suddenly looked like he hadnít slept in days. ØThis is the key. Master of Death. Thatís what Dumbledore wanted me to become, so I could defeat Voldemort. This is the only way.Æ ØHarry, no, itís not,Æ Hermione insisted, looking to Draco and Ron for backup. ØPlease, just think about this logically. The way is to destroy the Horcruxes. Maybe he left you the ring in there and maybe he didnít. Maybe Dumbledore even believed it was this è this Resurrection Stone. But nothing can help us beat Voldemort if we donít find and destroy Hufflepuffís Cup, then kill Nagini.Æ The words hardly even seemed to reach Potterís ears. He moved his head in an indeterminate sort of way, eyes still fixed on the Snitch. ØSheís right, mate. Come on,Æ Weasley urged. ØWeíve got to keep our heads on straight. If Malfoyís right, weíve already got the wand, and thatís the most important one, isnít it? We can use it to beat You-Know-Who, in the end. Forget the Stoneîweíve got to finish off those Horcruxes.Æ But Draco saw the way Potterís body had rotated toward the crib in the corner of the nursery, and he knew, then, what Potter meant by all this. ØItís not going to bring them back,Æ Draco said. Only then did Potterís focus seem to jar from the Snitch. His shoulders hunched defensively. ØYour parents,Æ Draco said. ØDumbledore. Sirius Black. è My aunt and uncle.Æ His throat tightened. ØLook, I get it, Potter. I wish there was a way to è if Iíd èÆ He gripped the Elder Wand tight. Donít look back, whispered Andromedaís voice. Dracoís resolve hardened. He pushed on. ØBut theyíre all gone. And if we donít find the next Horcrux, other people are going to join them.Æ Potter gave his head a frustrated shake and went back to the Snitch. ØYouíre not listening,Æ he said with agitation. ØDonít you get it? If we open this, we can ask Dumbledore himself what he wanted us to do! Iíve seen things like this before. Priori Incantatem. It saved my life fourth year. It didnít bring them back, but I could see them again. They were there. è Parts of them are still here.Æ Potterís head shot up again. ØMaybe,Æ he said, voice rising in excitement, ØDumbledore had a guess about where to find the cup, and he wanted us to contact him using the Resurrection Stone!Æ None of them had anything to say to this pronouncement. Draco met Hermioneís eyes and saw the sadness there, and he struggled to conceal his pity, knowing how it would incense Potter. But Potter must have sensed the feeling from all of them. He stood, anger in his face, and shoved up his glasses. ØFine, then. Iíll figure out how to open it myself.Æ With that, he stormed out of the nursery, leaving Draco, Hermione, and Weasley behind. Chapter End Notes thank you for reading! kudos and comments always make my day :) tumblr | instagram Chapter 26: TheDarkand the Dawn Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes In the following weeks, Hermione tried time and time again to shake Harry out of his fixation on the Deathly Hallows. None of her efforts made any impact. Before the battle at Azkaban, the Order had rallied around Harryís passion and leadership, but now he was withdrawn, even seeming preoccupied around Ginny. Hermione could see his mind sliding back to the Hallows in the middle of every conversation. ØI shouldnít have told him about the Elder Wand,Æ Draco said one night when he and Hermione were curled secretly in the library, door locked, Muffliato in place. ØWhy not?Æ Hermione asked. ØBecause.Æ Draco twirled a hazy curl of her hair around his fingertip. He still looked tired, but less haggard than he had in the week after Azkaban. ØIf he thought he had to go through Snape to get all three Hallows, would he be this obsessed?Æ Hermione sighed. ØYes, I think he would. The last time I saw Harry like this was èÆ ØWas when?Æ ØWell, it was last year, when he was convinced youíd joined the Death Eaters. When Harry zeroes in on something like this, he never lets go of it.Æ And while Harry fixated on opening the Snitch, Voldemortís influence clouded over the nation like a thunderhead. The Azkaban breakout had brought the conflict aboveground, so that by late February, the Daily Prophet was reporting open skirmishes across the country. Near daily, the Order received sympathiser intelligence about Muggle hunts. The Ministry, meanwhile, had begun to demolish monuments honouring prominent Muggle-borns, alongside new efforts to shutter gathering places for goblins, vampires, and other magical beings. The Order sent battalions from the fortress safehouse to fight these efforts, but reports from the small battles disturbed Hermione. Most bystanders did nothing to act against the Ministry and the Death Eaters. Some even joined in against the Order. It was becoming more and more clear that the Prophetís disinformation campaign had taken root. Worse, the twinsí Wizarding Wireless programme was forced to change frequencies with every broadcast to prevent the Death Eaters jamming their signals. Hermione doubted that The Daily Potter was reaching even a hundredth of those reached by its Ministry-controlled counterpart. So the Order prepared with ever more anticipation for their pamphlet flyover. Thanks to their allies in the monastery safehouse, devoted to magically multiplying the pamphlets, they would soon have thousands upon thousands to distribute, describing the truth about dozens of events since last year. ØWeíll have to drop them all in one mission,Æ Kingsley said. ØIíll bet within an hour of the first leaflet hitting ground, theyíll push through a law to create aerial patrols.Æ The final piece of the puzzle for the flyover would be obtaining broomsticks. The Bulgarian Quidditch team had sent them a dozen Firebolts courtesy of Viktor Krum, enough for the Order members who would spearhead the operation in London and Hogsmeade. But broomsticks were expensive, and now, with nearly sixty locations mapped out nationwide, they would need not so much a cache of brooms as a fleet. Hermione tried to remind Harry of the urgency of this endeavour, but he couldnít seem to focus on it. Worse, he remained just as disengaged from their discussions of Hufflepuffís Cup îeven when Hermione developed a new theory about the final hidden Horcrux. ØWeíve discovered every hiding place but one,Æ she said one afternoon as they sat in the library. ØBut the thing is, only some of them are hiding places, when you think about it.Æ Ron frowned. ØWhat do you mean?Æ ØI mean that Voldemort didnít really hide the diary,Æ Hermione said. ØHe gave it to Dracoís father to use someday. And he doesnít hide Nagini, either; he sends her to attack people. So, really, only three of the Horcruxes were hidden: the ring, the diadem, and the locket.Æ ØThatís true,Æ Draco said slowly, tapping one finger on A Compleat Historie of the Founders Four. ØSo, youíre asking whether the cup was meant to be hidden or used.Æ ØYeah,Æ said Ron, frowning. ØIt would make sense for it to be three and three, wouldnít it? Three to hide, three to use?Æ ØI thought so, too.Æ Hermione shuffled through her notes. ØAnd I read something about the cup è here. Itís a historical account of Helga Hufflepuffís Revels of Goodwill, written by a witch who attended one of the celebrations. Apparently lifelong friendships started at the Revels, and couples who were married met there. Every guest drank out of Hufflepuffís own cup at the beginning of the night, as a show of trust, loyalty, and good faith. Those who had drunk from the cup seemed to come together in a way that was preternatural.Æ ØSo?Æ said Harry. He had not said anything for a while, and even now was looking listlessly out of the window. Hermione glared at him. ØSo, it sounds like something Voldemort might use, doesnít it? A cup of loyalty that binds a group together?Æ Ron looked blank. ØYeah, but that doesnít tell us where heíd keep it, does it?Æ ØWell è no," Hermione said shortly. "I suppose not.Æ Draco clearly sensed her aggravation, because he stretched his long legs out, breaking the stillness that had hung over them for the past hour. ØI have an idea. How about I, Master of the Elder Wand, use Legilimency on the Dark Lord to find out where the cup is, now that Iím unbeatable?Æ Ron rolled his eyes. ØHow about you, Master of the Elder Wand, stop being such a git about it all the time?Æ A smile pulled at Hermioneís lips, but she eyed the Elder Wand with residual mistrust. She appreciated that it had helped save Draco at Azkaban, but she didnít like the idea of a wand that had enticed wizard after wizard to slaughter. ØYou know perfectly well that bringing that wand anywhere near Voldemort wouldn't be sensible.Æ ØItís the only way,Æ Harry murmured, seemingly to himself. Hermione exchanged annoyed looks with Draco and Ron. None of them deigned to respond to Harry. Given his Hallows obsession, Hermione supposed they should feel lucky that Harry had shown no sign of interest in taking the Elder Wand from Draco. But Harry seemed comfortable with the wand so near, and too absorbed by the Snitch to trouble himself with anything else. ØSo, Malfoy,Æ Ron said to Draco, Øhave you managed to get any information out of your parents, yet?Æ ØBesides this, you mean?Æ said Draco, holding up a sheaf of notes about the Death Eaters that heíd developed in the previous weeks. The notes contained every detail his parents had mentioned, each of the Death Eatersí talents, weaknesses, and histories. Eventually they would use the information to zero in on a contact who could insinuate Draco back into the fold. Ron waved the sheaf away. ØYeah, besides that. I mean, do you reckon they know anything about the cup, or the Horcruxes?Æ ØUnlikely.Æ Draco slipped the Elder Wand behind his ear. ØMy father and I have spoken about the diary a few times now. I can tell he has no idea what it really was. And I finally managed to detour them into a proper conversation about the Foundersí artifacts a few nights ago, but he and my mother seem convinced that theyíve all been lost for decades or centuries, besides the sword.Æ Hermione frowned contemplatively at his notes. ØWell, maybe next time you can ask more vaguely about treasures Voldemort has in his possession, or something. But you should wait a while to bring it up again. ØAlso, have they stopped asking you to spy on us, yet?Æ Dracoís mouth thinned. ØNo. But theyíve been cooped up here for months, now. ØListen, no offence, but I think we should un.invite them from meals. I know we wanted to be civil, but if theyíre still asking you to stab us in the back every other day, thatís not going to happen. Sometimes she had the sense there was something Draco wasnít telling her, something hanging between them like spider-silk, but if she tried to move toward it, it would float away as if dashed by a breeze. And she, too, felt there was something she hadnít expressed after the battle. She hadnít admitted the way sheíd screamed and fought to try to get back to him after the cave-in. Instead there would be a frozen hand protruding from rockfall, the same hand that played with her hair or teased at the neck of her jumper at nights, the hand that was still twirling the Elder Wand now. She had looked too long. With a funny skip in her chest, Hermione turned quickly away, sneaking a glance at Ron to make sure he hadnít noticed anything. But Ron was flipping through her notes, unaffected. Hermioneís nerves settled. She was finally beginning to relax around Ron and Draco. At first, after Azkaban, she had worried that the Dementorsí influence might have worsened Ronís feelings of rejection from October. But to her immense relief, the intervening months had clearly helped him overcome his feelings for her instead. She had always ribbed Ron about being emotionally oblivious, but in truth, he had been hair-trigger sensitive last year, when heíd suspected that she and Harry were trying to steal romantic moments together. So, Hermione had girded herself for the possibility that Ron might still be sensitive to her attentionsîthat she and Draco would need to act cold to each other to escape his notice. But Ron had simply seemed relieved that none of them had held his long absence against him. And while he still gave Draco grief about their shared history, there was no real enmity in it anymore. In general, Ron was too preoccupied with his own Order duties. Impressed by his triumph at Azkaban, Kingsley and McGonagall had recruited him to help plan the safehousesí movements and strategies. Ron could often be seen stepping in and out of the fireplace in the front room, having just returned from one of the four safehouses. And somewhat bizarrely, Percy could now sometimes be seen taking attentive notes on Ronís ideas. There was one thing Hermione couldnít understand, though. Yet when she tried to ask him about what had happened at Azkaban, he avoided the subject with something near embarrassment. Her curiosity was finally sated in early March, when Draco said to Ron during another afternoon meeting, ØWeasley, I need a favour.Æ Dracoís eyes slid briefly to Hermione. ØAnd I think itís time we gave that Unravelling Charm a try.Æ # Pansy returned the next morning to stay in Professor McGonagallís tartan tent, as McGonagall now resided at the monastery safehouse. Ron seemed too flustered by this turn of events to refer to it directly, but during meetings about the flyover, Hermione often noticed his eyes straying to the corner where Pansy sat. ØTwo weeks before he does anything,Æ Draco bet to Hermione under his breath during one of these meetings. Her lips twitched. ØHe canít even say hello when she walks into a room. è What on earth do you think happened in Azkaban, anyway?Æ ØAsk him for all the sordid details,Æ Draco murmured. ØOh, yes, thatíll go over well.Æ Secretly, Hermione was glad she and Draco could joke about it, because the web of former, current, secret, and half-realised relationships was beginning to give her a headache. Sometimes, if she laughed at a joke of Ronís during their Horcrux discussions, sheíd feel Dracoís eyes lingering on them, and the room would suddenly feel too small. Or if Hermione was talking through reports of Snatcher raids and Ron was listening intently, Pansy would walk close by in a way that seemed designed to pull his eyes away from Hermione. It went in reverse, too. When Pansy and Draco sat together at meals, Hermione always found herself watching them, unwillingly picturing the two of them tangled together the way Ron and Lavender had once been. One day, over lunch, Pansyîwho was annoyingly, casually physical with Dracoîhad flicked his wrist with one of her nails. Hermione had heard a loud thunk and looked over to find that Ron had knocked over a tureen of gravy. Ron hadnít spoken to Draco for the rest of the night. Hermione blamed it all on the close confines of headquarters. They were treading on each otherís toes, and no one was admitting their real feelings, and she spent half her time scolding herself that her feelings didnít matter, anyway, in the context of the Orderís work. While most of the Order prepared for the flyover, Hermione was blocking off time to work on Dracoís Dark Mark, which, she reasoned, was important on the grand scale, too. After all, it would eventually be crucial to make Draco a believable double-crosser to the Death Eaters. So, over a week and a half, she and Draco measured the effects of Protean charms and the Unraveller on other organic matter, like an apple and a slice of ham that came out much the worse for the encounter. Once they had finished making modifications to the charm, they prepared to try it on the Dark Mark itself. When Hermione walked into the library that afternoon, Draco went still. She had Lupin and Tonks following at her heels. Hermione drew a shaky breath. ØDraco, Iíd like Remus and Tonks to help us with the process. Tonksís Auror training may help us if there turns out to be a curse aspect to the enchantment we havenít foreseen, and Remus has a lot more practice with physical magic than I have.Æ After a secondís deliberation, Draco nodded. He rolled up his sleeve, and Tonks dropped onto the sofa beside Draco to examine the Mark. ØUgly thing, isnít it?Æ she said, wrinkling her nose. ØIíll put in a word to the designer,Æ said Draco. Remus smiled and drew his wand. ØI think itís best if we perform a few simpler charms before we begin the Unraveller, simply to see if interfering with the Protean Charm will trigger something in the Mark to harm you. ØIf we are successful, you may be the first person ever to be freed of the Dark Markís enchantment.Æ As Lupin moved his wand over Dracoís forearm, Hermione felt a pulse of gratitude toward their former professor. She realised that Lupin alone of the whole Order had never said a bad word about Draco, had never treated him with anything other than patience and respect. It was almost as though heíd been waiting for Draco to change. Hermione knew it was that same kindness of Lupinís that had helped Tonks to manage the weeks since Azkaban. Since her parentsí deaths, Tonksís hair had been limp and colourless, and she had rarely spoken except to discuss the Orderís strategies. What had seemed to come out of her in recent days was a harder, more focused woman. If Fenrir Greyback was ever mentioned, she went still like someone anticipating attack. Hermione supposed her loathing for Greyback was no less because he had been the one to bite Remus as a child. Tonksís wand was drawn, now, at the ready. But nothing happened as Lupin moved his wand over Dracoís forearm. ØWell,Æ said Lupin, looking satisfied, ØI think we may be able to tackle the charm right here. Hermione, are you ready?Æ ØI think so.Æ Hermione drew a deep breath, holding Dracoís eyes. ØIt might hurt.Æ ØIíll be surprised if it doesnít.Æ There was little humour in the words. ØNo. è You do it.Æ Tonksís eyebrows rose, but she didnít comment. Hermioneís face grew hot. These days she felt as if everything she and Draco said to each other was a loaded confession, millimetres away from being correctly interpreted by the people around them. With those four words of Dracoís, could Remus and Tonks now understand why she had struggled so wildly against them in the halls of Azkabanîthat it hadnít been fear for the rest of the group, but for him? ØYouíre all right è itís working, I see it è just a bit longer èÆ Draco panted, making small sounds through his clenched teeth. ØThatís it.Æ He went limp with relief as the last of the raised skin sank back into place. Didnít it work?Æ ØI believe the curse has lifted,Æ said Remus. ØYou can touch it again.Æ Draco ran his fingers over his forearm. ØI think so, too.Æ # Narcissa Malfoy was standing in a hedge. For nearly two months, she and Lucius had spent their days hidden between these evergreen branches that surrounded the Potter Cottage. The lines of their Extendable Ears snaked magically up to the windows. They had managed to glean just enough information to slake Bellaís thirst these past months. Unveiling Aberforth Dumbledore and Augusta Longbottom had given them a few weeksí goodwill; it had provided the Death Eaters with an entire web of Order sympathisers to disrupt. Then, after the breakout at Azkaban, Bella had been too preoccupied to call on them for a fortnight. Narcissa had wondered whether Bella had felt anything, however briefly, at Andromedaís death. Sheíd even wondered whether the fortnightís silence had to do with Andromeda è but when Bella had next spoken to them, sheíd relayed nothing but eagerness for the Dark Lordís return. The Prophet had done its job, sheíd said gleefully. The nation was now primed to accept him as Minister for Magic. Since then, Narcissa and Lucius had managed only minor successes. Expelled from the Orderís mealtimes, they could no longer piece together fragments of timing or location. Theyíd been able to report the Orderís Wizarding Wireless frequency once, enabling the Death Eaters to jam the signal for a week, before the Weasley twins had realised what was happening and begun to shuffle frequencies. They had also reported the presence of Pansy Parkinson, whom theyíd spotted through the windows. Otherwise, though, the Order had been religious about that charm they used, Muffliato, the last thing the Malfoys heard before everything receded into an indistinct buzz. ØWe need more substantial information,Æ Lucius said halfway through March, pacing back and forth before the hearth in their tent. ØOne blow struck hard against the Order, and Draco will see their forces must be overwhelmed, and he will tell us every plan heís heard these past months. He said he was considering it. è He will join us.Æ Narcissa didnít reply. Ever since the battle at Azkaban, Lucius had been losing sleep, re.treading that fragment of a hope from Draco again and again. Her husband seemed to have taken Andromedaís death as a sign, yet more evidence that the only way to save all their lives was to re-join the Death Eaters. He crafted fantasies about their redemption within the Dark Order if they could only deliver Harry Potter to the Dark Lord. Narcissa, however, found no galvanisation in her sisterís death. Yes, Andromeda and her husband had died loyal to the Order. But hadnít Alistair Crabbe, a friend since their Hogwarts days and a loyal Death Eater, suffered the Dementorís Kiss during the same battle? The only thing Azkaban had shown her was that death was everywhere, always encroaching, growing ever closer to her family. Narcissa even found herself thinking about Andromedaís Mudblood husband, these days. Ted Tonks, now dead. She remembered the days before Andromeda had revealed the truth about Ted, when sheíd still been trying to present him to the family as someone of acceptable birth. Sheíd told Narcissa and Bellatrix that he was a bookseller in Flourish & Blotts, a few years out of Hogwarts, interested in preserving old spell texts. But going to those specialised schools in Greece or Japan took serious gold. That was how Andromeda had phrased it. ØItís a bit stupid, really. è And nothing fazes him; I donít think even you could intimidate him, Bella.Æ Theyíd all laughed at that. Andromeda, so obviously taken with him. Andromeda, gone now, her last words a declaration of love to that man. Some part of Narcissa had always waited for Andromeda to wake up, to remember her heritage, to come back to the family yearning for reconciliation. This was exactly the event they had been hoping for. It sounded as if half of the Order would be out in the open, vulnerable to attackîand they had mentioned Dracoís name among those who would be staying behind, uninvolved. Yet here, on the threshold of success, Narcissa felt a sudden, sharp doubt. These doubts had nagged her since Azkaban. Now they redoubled. A force of six hundred were free and loyal to the Order. Were their chances so impossible? Could Draco be right after all, that lying low and staying secret was the familyís best chance at survival? Drifting through the leaves every day, Narcissa had found something like comfort in invisibility. Did she really want to intervene? What if they simply put down the mirror and waited, come what may? But Lucius was already taking parchment from his pocket and scribbling notes. ØA date,Æ he murmured. ØGive us the date èÆ The youngest Weasley boyís voice obliged. The plan was to occur three days from now, March 24th, at daybreak. Luciusís face filled with stark relief. Lucius had always been something near a blessingîa creature of intelligence and ambition, someone more devoted to her than she had ever felt to herself. He would always seek more for their family, where she might have settled for the status quo. ØYouíre certain this is the best way?Æ she whispered. ØYes,Æ he whispered back, and she trusted him. That night, they told Bellatrix every word. # Headquarters were abuzz the night before the flyover. The front room was plastered in maps, contingency plans, and diagrams of evasive manoeuvres that reminded Draco of Quidditch practise. Draco was sitting on a cushion by the hearth, watching the rest of the Order.
