ØIím afraid we have to distribute these pamphlets, no matter the risk.Æ ØBut if they know our plans,Æ Draco said urgently, Øtheyíll be ready to stop the pamphlets, itíll be pointless.Æ ØWeíve got ter try, Malfoy,Æ said Hagrid. ØWeíve come all this way, we caní just turn around.Æ Draco shook his head, his panic mixing with anger now. They werenít listening. They were thinking of him as a scared child, they werenít confronting the possibility that they were flying into an ambush. And there was no time to argue. If others around the country were also flying into ambushes, he needed to act now. Draco grasped the broom handle, unslung the satchel of leaflets from his shoulder, and shot downward. Mrs. Weasleyís surprised voice sounded behind him. Diagon Alley swelled back into existence before him, its tiny people moving in clusters. Still Disillusioned, Draco levelled out the Fireboltís handle in mid-air, bringing himself to an abrupt halt. With a quick motion he passed the Elder Wand over the satchel to Disillusion it, too. Then he swished and flickedîWingardium Leviosa. He guided the invisible satchel 200 meters forward. Then he lifted both enchantments. The satchel plummeted, visible once more. As it fell, it twisted and swayed, loosing its contents. They began to flutter toward Diagon Alley, flurrying, disconnecting from one another, turning into a diffuse white cloud. For an instant Draco thought he had been mistaken, that his panic about his parents really had just been a paranoid instinct. Then a dozen blasting charms rocketed out of Diagon Alleyís rooftops into the sky, toward the place where the satchel had originated. They intersected with a bang like a colossal firework. Even 200 meters back, the shockwave blew Dracoís broom backward. When he righted himself, he saw that the painstakingly crafted leaflets were burning to nothing, intersecting with a Screening Charm before getting anywhere near the ground. Draco swore, turned the broom, and urged it up into the clouds. Robed figures on brooms were bursting up out of Diagon Alleyís perimeterîdozens of them. By the time Draco reunited with the Weasleys and Hagrid, their shock was already transforming into fear. Draco yelled, ØWe have to Apparate!Æ Arthur flew to Hagridís side and seized his elbow. ØHold on tight to that bike, Hagrid,Æ he panted. As coloured jets of light crisscrossed the sky, they all twisted in mid-air and vanished. They had barely reappeared in the launch field when Mr. and Mrs. Weasley were sprinting toward Hermione, Potter, and Ginny. ØHermione, your medallion,Æ yelled Arthur, as Draco and Hagrid bolted after them. ØQuickly. The emergency signal!Æ ØI only hope weíre in time,Æ gasped Mrs. Weasley, staring around. ØWe made good time è all the rest èÆ White-faced with fear, Hermione fumbled the necklace from around her neck. The lead fliers of each group had matching medallions imbued with Protean Charms. In case of just this kind of emergency, they could alter Hermioneís medallion, and the others would grow hot and change to match, signalling an immediate end to the mission. Hermione touched her wandtip to the medallion. A corresponding glow came from the chain around Mr. Weasleyís neck. Almost at once, other flyers began to Apparate back to the field around them. They appeared by the dozenîall seeming bewildered or worried, but not injured. Within fifteen minutes, all 109 flyers had returned. A score or so, assigned to the smallest villages, had already begun their drop when the signal had been issued, but theyíd encountered no ambush. So they didnít know everything, Draco thought, feeling numb. His parents hadnít known everything. Remus gave a short, bracing speech to the gathered fliers about focusing their re-information efforts on undercover distribution methods. Then their allies returned to the safehouse, and the Order assembled in the heart of the field. ØHow did you know, Draco?Æ said Remus. ØI è I donít know.Æ Draco avoided the Orderís eyes. ØInstinct, maybe,Æ he added flatly. ØI started thinking about the attack on Kingsley last night è it seemed too coincidental. è Wanted to make sure.Æ ØItís a major blow,Æ said Bill, tugging his hair out of its ponytail. ØI suppose youíre right, Remus, and we can try to distribute the leaflets hand to hand instead, but Iím worried it wonít be enough to counteract the Prophet.Æ ØWeíll discuss it back at headquarters,Æ said Mrs. Weasley, whose hands were placed protectively on the twinsí shoulders. ØCome on, now, letís all go back and have some breakfast.Æ ØI think Iíll go to the drop location first,Æ said Hermione in a shaky voice. ØRon, Harry, Draco, shall we do a bit of supply distribution before going back to headquarters?Æ They nodded, and while the rest of the Order returned to the Potter Cottage, they Apparated to the cave, which was newly replenished with supplies. Crates of produce rose to the ceiling, baskets full of bread balanced atop them. Rice and potatoes strained at their burlap sacks. Other supplies seemed to have been purchased from Muggle supermarkets. Draco stared into one of their smiling logos as he sank onto a crate. ØIt was my parents,Æ he said before Hermione, Potter, or Weasley could say a word. ØMy parents have been passing information to the Death Eaters.Æ The others didnít speak, just looked at him with dismay. ØI donít know how theyíve been doing it,Æ Draco forced out, Øand I only realised it at Diagon Alley. But they were awake this morning. They tried to stop me from going. Itís because theyíd tipped the Death Eaters off, so they knew thereíd be an ambush.Æ Hermione closed her eyes. ØThen è then Aberforth wasnít betrayed by someone in his network.Æ ØAnd the twinsí signal getting jammed last month,Æ said Potter. ØAnd Kingsley, last night è itís all because of them.Æ Draco nodded. When he lifted a hand to his face he realised his fingers were trembling with anger. ØThatís why theyíve been trying to get me to commit to spying. They needed to know I was on their side before telling me what theyíve been up to. Otherwise, what if I tried to stop them.Æ His voice was rising. ØIíve been trying to keep them out of all this, and the whole time, theyíve been trying to get everyone killed.Æ ØBut they havenít,Æ said Hermione softly. Draco turned toward her, needing to see reassurance in her face. ØThey havenít gotten anyone killed,Æ she said more firmly. ØNo one got hurt today. This morning could have been a massacre, but you stopped that.Æ ØSheís right,Æ said Weasley in a bracing voice. ØLook, Malfoy, even if we tell the Order about your parents, no oneís going to think you had anything to do with it. Youíve been helping us half a year, now. We know youíre not like them.Æ The anger leached out of Draco, leaving a strange, sober feeling. Weasley was right, of course. He was no longer like his parents. And they had lied to him. His family were supposed to be a team; from the first breath to the last, it was supposed to be the Malfoys against the rest of the world. But they had kept something this consequential from him, and he had been concealing something just as important. With all three of them lying to each other this way, what was their family anymore? He pictured his mother and father, younger and healthier, laughing and watching him play at the top of Halfhold Hill. Potter broke him out of his reverie. ØMaybe this isnít what you want to hear,Æ he said slowly, Øbut this could be useful. Weíve been coming up with a plan to get the Death Eaters to trust you again, but we donít need to do any of that anymore. They already trust your parents. When we want to set that trap, the jobís already done.Æ Draco nodded. Potter sounded more present than he had since theyíd learned about the Hallows. That, at least, was some relief. ØI just donít understand how theyíre doing it,Æ Hermione said. ØThey donít have a wand between them. Could there be something in the twinsí tent that theyíve adapted for magical communication? I thought we stripped the place clean.Æ ØIíll find out what it is,Æ Draco said. ØAnd Potter, Iíll need to borrow the Cloak.Æ # When Draco returned to his parentsí tent, he thought his motherís eyes looked reddened, as if she had recently been crying. They both stood abruptly. ØDraco,Æ said his father. Since Draco was looking for it, he could see the relief on Luciusís narrow features, so much like his own. He could see the remnants of the terror that he might have betrayed his own son to his death. Draco even wondered, for an instant, whether they would finally confess what they were doingîwhat theyíd done. The danger that they had put upon him, however unwittingly. He waited. He gave them the chance. But neither of them said a word. # That night, Draco donned the Cloak and crept down the hallway to his parentsí door. There, he crouched and peered through the keyhole. Øè will not be pleased,Æ said his father. ØThis should have been a master stroke.Æ ØWe did all we could,Æ said his mother quietly. ØThe rest was in her hands. Come. Itís nearly time.Æ Draco watched as his father joined his mother at the desk. She drew something glimmering from a drawerîa hand mirror. They had no wands, but there was no incantation necessary. ØBellatrix Lestrange,Æ said his mother to the mirror. His auntís face appeared there, her dramatic features livid. ØCissy,Æ she said. ØExplain yourself.Æ # Draco did little else in the following two weeks than tail his parents, invisible, watching. He could never guess when they might return to the mirror, and the Gryffindors had pointed out that Bellatrix might give away some of the Death Eaters' movements. Draco knew that they were rightîthat secret access to Bellatrix might be a more valuable source of intelligence than any otherîbut it wasnít the only reason he wanted to watch. There was something addictive in seeing his parents unmasked, in piecing together the truth for himself. It took him several days to assemble the basic facts. Before his parents had come to headquarters, Bellatrix had discovered that the Malfoys were alive and cornered his parents. They had promised inside information to bargain for their lives. Otherwise, his aunt would have murdered them and made it her next mission to kill Draco. His parents had also lied to Bellatrix for his benefit. Theyíd told her that he had been acting under Confundus, possibly under Imperius. They told her that every day he was working to gain the Orderís trust in order to position himself favourably. Draco acknowledged, then, that their actions had been to keep the family alive and safe. He clutched to this. He told himself it wasnít so different from the way heíd been raised. But he could not stop seeing his parents differently. His mother was a cypher, quiet in a new way than she had been at the Manor or even last summer at Grimmauld Place. When she followed his father out to the hedges, where they hid most hours of the day and tried to eavesdrop, there was something listless in the way she moved. His father, on the other hand, was more agitated than he had been in Dracoís childhood. His feelings seemed to catch him unawares, his fear of Bellatrix revealing them to Voldemort, his desire for restoration among the Death Eaters. His hopes for Dracoís future. Heíd express these things with jerky suddenness, as if his skin had caught upon a jagged edge and cut the thoughts loose. Bellatrix, on the other hand, had not changed at all. It was bizarre to listen to her again. Draco could remember admiring Bella for her infamy as a duellist, for her high rank among the Death Eaters, for her adherence to pure-blood traditions. Now, striding ever farther away from those traditions, Draco was struck by how insipid and repetitive her words sounded. Everything was an excuse to insult Muggle-borns or non-wizards. She seemed to manipulate every topic to reiterate her own status, to preen, to remind herself how blessed she was to exist in the Dark Lordís reflected glory. All this when Draco knew that Voldemort cared as little for Bellatrix as he ever had for Draco. Voldemort had humiliated Bellatrix in front of other Death Eaters, yet she fantasised about nothing other than his approval, winning his highest favour back from Snape. It made Draco feel a mixture of pity and scorn. That wasnít ambition at all. It was debasement. Draco brought quill and parchment whenever he hunched outside his parentsí door to listen. Day after day, anything that suggested the Death Eatersí plans was jotted down immediately. But in early April, Draco heard something quite apart from anti-Muggle legislation and plans for expansion abroad. It started normally enough, with Bellatrix whispering about the future. ØWe must be ready to deliver Potter when the Dark Lord returns to Britain,Æ she said. ØA secure location, where Potter cannot wriggle free è will Draco be prepared? The Dark Lord has intimated to me that he hopes to come by May to take his rightful place in the Ministry.Æ ØYes,Æ said his father smoothly. ØEvery day Dracoís faith in the Order weakens. And Draco is trusted, now; he is perfectly placed to act. Once the Dark Lord returns, convincing him to turn Potter over will be easy.Æ Bellatrix sighed. Even through the keyhole, Draco could see the relish on her face. ØThen our familyís name will be restored, Cissy. And it will be as it was before, Lucius, among the Death Eaters è we will surpass Snape and retake our place at the Dark Lordís right handî we, his most trusted inner circle, who drank from the cup of the Dark Lordís covenant è we will hold the ranks we are owed.Æ The sentence struck Draco like a slap. He had to press his palm to his mouth to stop himself from making a sound. Go on, he willed Bellatrix. Go on è surely she wouldnít stop there, he had listened to hours of her rapturous speeches in the last weeks è ØSurely,Æ murmured his mother, Øthe Dark Lord has also shared the Cup of the Covenant with Severus.Æ Bellaís face flushed. ØThe Dark Lord prizes old loyalty,Æ she hissed. ØThe covenant was for us alone, his first, his most devoted, who swore him our lives before even the inkling of a war.Æ A smug, prideful note entered her voice. ØNo, Cissy. No Death Eater will drink of it again, not even Snape. That much, I can guarantee.Æ Then they were speaking about the old ranks of Death Eaters, ones long-dead, but Draco was no longer listening. He pelted from the tent into the garden, then up into the cottage. He found the others reading together in Potter and Weasleyís room. ØShe has the Horcrux,Æ he panted. All three shot bolt upright. ØWho?Æ Hermione said. ØBellatrix. He gave my father the diary. He gave Bellatrix the cup.Æ Chapter End Notes some more minor tinkering with canon, in honor of helga's good name thanks for reading - kudos/comments always make my day :) tumblr | instagram Chapter27:A Family Affair Chapter Notes hi everybody!! jsyk, projected chapter count has been bumped to 33, and my posting schedule has been updated to reflect! (just basically means there'll be an extra update next friday.) cheers! See the end of the chapter for more notes ØMother, Father, Iíve made a decision,Æ said Draco. The Malfoysí hands stilled on their wine glasses. ØI think youíre right. From what Iíve seen, the Order donít have a chance anymore. They canít get their message out, theyíre losing all their fights. è I think itís only a matter of time.Æ Draco laid his fork and knife on his plate, face downturned. ØIíll do it,Æ he muttered. ØIíll tell you what theyíre up to.Æ Lucius grasped Dracoís shoulder so hard that it hurt. ØGood,Æ he breathed. ØI knew you would see, Draco. Youíve made the right choice.Æ Narcissa stayed silent. Draco had expected her to exhale through flared nostrils in the way she sometimes did after a great relief, but her expression remained unchanged except for a slant to her thin mouth. His father rushed on. ØWe have news, too. Something that can make everything just as it once was.Æ Draco pretended at wary curiosity. ØWhat is it?Æ ØWe have been communicating with your Aunt Bellatrix. She is eager to help us regain our place among the Death Eaters. Family means more than ever, now.Æ Draco widened his eyes. ØYou è but how?Æ His father fetched the mirror. Then, over the course of hours, his parents explained everything. They told him even the details he hadnít expected them to divulge, like the attacks theyíd orchestrated and the reasons theyíd concealed the mirror from him. ØWe needed to be certain of your loyalties, you understand,Æ said Lucius with satisfaction. ØNow youíve made the choice yourself. The Dark Lord will not punish you for a brief lapse, not when we place Harry Potter into his hands.Æ ØUnless,Æ said Narcissa, Øyou think the Potter boy has some ability that might allow him to overcome the obstacles in his path, Draco?Æ Draco studied his mother. The words had sounded mostly neutral, but had there been a hint of doubt there? ØNo,Æ Draco said. ØPotterís ordinary. Heís always been ordinary. Just got lucky over and over again.Æ ØBut not anymore,Æ said Lucius. Dracoís mouth had gone dry. He took a sip of his wine. ØNot anymore,Æ he agreed. Near midnight, they gathered before the fireplace to contact his aunt. ØNephew,Æ said Bellatrix, peering at him out of the mirror. ØAlive and well. è You have finally recovered yourself, then? Your mind is your own again?Æ His parents tensed on either side of him. Bellatrixís tone was honeyed, but nothing friendly showed in her face. Every word was a demand as to why he had not made contact sooner. ØIíve been biding my time, Aunt Bella,Æ Draco said. He rolled up his sleeve to show her the altered Dark Mark. ØIt hasnít been easy. That girl Potter drags around with him, she figured something out so I couldnít contact anyone. The spellís broken.Æ ØItís true,Æ said Lucius quickly, pushing up his own sleeve. ØShe has destroyed my Mark, too. è The Mudblood is enterprising, Bella.Æ Anger twisted in Dracoís stomach. He imagined piling something cold and dark atop the burning feeling until it extinguished. The suspicion in Bellatrixís face eased. ØNo matter. We will dispose of that scum before we bring Potter to the Dark Lord. The blood traitor Weasley boy, too. When he dies, Potter will know that none of his allies still live.Æ Draco forced himself to lift the corners of his mouth. ØYou have positioned yourself close to Potter, then, Draco?Æ breathed Bellatrix, leaning closer to the mirror. ØTell me è what have they been doing to prepare the little baby boy to fight the greatest wizard who ever lived?Æ A frisson of anticipation ran through Dracoís body. He must be careful, here. ØI canít be sure.Æ He narrowed his eyes. ØThey spend a lot of time reading books that donít seem related to anything, and they wonít tell me why. Top secret even inside the Order. è Still, I think they must be trying to get Potter some extra powers, because theyíre all books about magical history, and theyíve mentioned a few magical items. The Ramís Horn of Uric the Oddball, the Hogwarts Foundersí Artifacts, that sort of thing.Æ Bellatrix went very still. ØWhat,Æ said Draco, eyeing her, Ødo you know what it means?Æ ØNo,Æ she said sharply. ØYou must be right, Draco è they must hope to give the Potter boy false powers, or weaponry, in hopes that he might withstand the Dark Lord. But he will fall.Æ She waved her hand as if the words meant nothing to her, but Bellatrix was one of the worst liars Draco had ever met. Years of evangelical devotion paired with years in Azkaban had made her consummately readable. She was on edge. They had prepared this speech to Bellatrix in detail. Draco knew he couldnít push for answers about Hufflepuffís Cup, nor speak about it too directly. He had to ease into the subject so that she might eventually give a clue to its location, but she mustnít feel so threatened as to think the Horcrux was in immediate danger. ØStill,Æ she went on, Øyou must be watchful. Listen closely to what they are planning and report it to me, Draco. Together, our family will deliver the deciding blow of this war.Æ # At breakfast the next morning, when the rest of the Order were distracted, Draco gave a single nod to Hermione, Harry, and Ron to indicate that their plan had unfolded as theyíd hoped. Potter and Weasley brightened. But Hermioneís eyes lingered, as though she hoped to read the details from his mind with Legilimency. Draco wished he could tell her about his motherís strangely tight-lipped reaction. He had rarely seen his mother hold her cards close to her chest around the family this way. Heíd have paid his weight in Galleons for some of Hermioneís analysis. But they no longer had that kind of time. In the wake of the failed flyover, Order meetings were running deep into the night and beginning long before the crack of dawn. After having to dive into the downstairs bathroom to escape notice by Mr. and Mrs. Weasley, Draco and Hermione had agreed they should stop meeting at night. This would have been bad enough. But to Dracoís immense irritation, heíd realised that most of the Order seemed to think it was only a matter of time until Hermione struck up a relationship with Ron. During dish clean-up one evening, Draco had overheard Fred telling his humiliated-looking brother, ØIím telling you, Ron, youíve got to stop waiting around. Hermioneís smart, all right, but sheís not a mind-reader. Itís the opportune moment to sweep her off her feet.Æ ØI dunno,Æ Ron had mumbled. ØDonít know? Donít know?Æ said George. ØYou just turned into a bloody war hero, didnít you? Iím sure half of Azkaban fancies you now.Æ Fred nodded. ØAnd you could lose an ear any time, remember? Every second counts.Æ Draco realized he was scrubbing a dish so hard that its owl design had fluttered onto the back of the ceramic to avoid his onslaught. He put it up to dry with a pointed clang and stalked out of the room. After that, heíd started seeing the signs everywhere. Mrs. Weasley ushering Hermione into the seat next to Ron at meals. Hagrid asking Ron and Hermione to feed Buckbeak together, a knowing twinkle in his eye. At least Pansy seemed to loathe this behaviour as much as he did. Draco noticed that Pansy had co-opted a seat beside the hearth every evening, the one next to Ronís usual spot by the fire. Weasley always went strangely still when she sat down, and Draco had begun to see them exchanging words every so often, although they spoke so quietly that it was impossible to know what they were saying. It had taken all his restraint not to tease Pansy about having feelings for Weasley, but he managed to restrain himself. Draco began to fantasise about what would happen if, one of these evenings, he simply reached out and placed his hand over Hermioneís as if it were nothing. He imagined the Orderís shock, the flustered look on Hermioneís face. Some days he came so close to doing it that his heart rate began to speed. And surely, even if news got back to his parents, he could now play it off as one more aspect of his cover? Draco imagined himself saying, Iíve come up with the perfect way to get information out of them è this way, sheíll tell me everything. Only one thought stopped him: what Hermione might want. In the minutes they managed to steal in cupboards or during kitchen duty, he sensed that there was something she wasnít saying to him, which made it even more difficult to speak the words heíd been trying to shape since Azkaban. Donít dive in headfirst, Draco kept telling himself. He had to keep some level of control. But it was becoming difficult to imagine any future without Hermione. Heíd envision the end of the war, travelling abroadîand there was Hermione on the flying carpet beside him, keeping well away from the edge and speaking about Stabilizing Charms. Heíd picture himself in five years, working at whatever version of the Ministry remained after all this, and there she was at the desk opposite, working on stacks of paperwork with her lip between her teeth. Or he would imagine the terrible reverse. Heíd imagine Hufflepuffís Cup slipping through their fingers è Voldemort returning to Britain and dealing a death blow to the Order. When Draco imagined fleeing the country, Hermione was there, too, tucked into a rowboat with terrified eyes. She had to be. If she didnít escape, he would not go. He knew that much. Winter faded and died like the embers of an old fire. Draco fed carefully chosen pieces of Order intelligence to Bellatrix, and soon the last traces of suspicion had faded from what Weasley referred to as his Øfamily chats.Æ April rains began to patter on the roof of the Potter Cottage, and with the mists of springtime came a new plan to disseminate the truth throughout Wizarding Britain. ØWeíve figured it out,Æ said Ginny one evening during an all-Order meeting. The safehouse guards had returned, and the front room was full to bursting. ØHogwarts. Itís the one magical hub where weíve still got an advantage. If Dobby, Winky, and the other house-elves can get our pamphlets into the students' hands, they can go home and give them to their parents, and their parents can contact everyone they know, and so on.Æ ØNow thaís an idea,Æ exclaimed Hagrid. ØAní loads of kids will go home for the Easter holidays at the end of April.Æ ØGood thinking, Ginny,Æ said Remus. ØWasnít just my idea,Æ said Ginny with a nod to Lunaîand to Pansy, who had been spending most of her afternoons with the other two girls. Pansy lifted her shoulders wearily, as if it were a great burden to assist with the Orderís problems. But Draco knew that proud set to her chin. Luna was propped in a chair, still frail but mostly recovered. She went on serenely, ØWe thought weíd bind the leaflets in groups of several hundred, then disguise them as extra spellbooks. So, even if the Carrows or Professor Snape search studentsí trunks, they shouldnít see anything out of the ordinary.Æ ØWe will need advanced Transfiguration to escape Snapeís notice, Iím afraid,Æ said Professor McGonagall. ØBut our allies at the safehouse will devote themselves to the effort, Iím sure.Æ So the Order reoriented its efforts, re-energised. This was a relief to Draco, Hermione, Harry, and Ron, who were still pretending to work hard every day on Harryís supposed Occlumency block, and preferred the rest of the Order to be as distracted as possible. For hours each day, they tried to come up with a plan to tease the Horcruxís location out of Bellatrix. It seemed an impossibly delicate manipulation. ØI wish we could just hex it out of her,Æ said Potter one afternoon, spinning his wand in his hand. Hermione was drumming her fingers on a stack of texts about Hogwarts. ØI still think weíll have to risk being more specific about the Foundersí objects. Draco, what if you told her outright youíd gone through our things and found the diadem or the locketîor even both? Then maybe sheíll start to worry that we know where the cup is, too.Æ ØThatís a massive risk,Æ Weasley argued. ØWhat if she decides to tell You-Know-Who? Then heíll know weíre after Horcruxes, and there goes any shot of our getting to the cup.Æ ØIím not sure Bellatrix would tell him,Æ Draco said slowly. ØNo one knows that the Dark Lord was collecting Foundersí objects. è All Bellatrix knows is that she was entrusted with Hufflepuffís Cup, this Cup of the Covenant, and she has to keep it safe.Æ Potter shook his head. ØStill, sheíll want to keep Voldemort updated on what weíre doing, wonít she? That diadem was powerful. She could let it slip to him in a report that weíve got it as a weapon. We canít chance it.Æ ØYes, butÆîDraco massaged his templesîØshe needs to think weíre on the trail of the cup, or sheíll never give anything away.Æ ØNo,Æ Hermione said. ØWait. She needs to think the three of us are on the trailînot necessarily you, Draco.Æ Her eyes were bright with inspiration. ØI have an idea. I think Iíve got it.Æ Draco spent the evening in a state of advanced nerves. They went over and over Hermioneís plan, all three Gryffindors playing Bellatrix until Draco could reroute the conversation back to where they needed it to go each time. After dinner, he stormed into his parentsí tent with his mouth twisted in a scowl. They were sitting near the fire, the mirror in his motherís hand, ready for the discussion theyíd scheduled with Bellatrix. They looked up as he shoved the flap open. ØHas something happened?Æ asked Lucius, voice sharp. ØYeah. I mean, I think so. This is impossible.Æ Draco scrubbed his hands through his hair. ØI think theyíve had some sort of breakthrough, but I canít tell what. Bellatrix is going toîI canít get the Order to tell me the important bits. Sheís going to think Iím not trying hard enough, but Iím doing everything I can.Æ ØCome sit,Æ said his mother. Draco did so, staring bitterly at his feet. Narcissa rested her hand upon his back. ØBellatrix knows that something so important will take time.Æ He forced a laugh. ØYeah, Aunt Bellaís such a paragon of patience.Æ But he allowed a slow sigh. ØLetís just get this over with. Muffliato,Æ he added, aiming his wand at the tent flap. Narcissa raised the mirror and said, ØBellatrix Lestrange.Æ Bella appeared at once. She was walking outside, the moon licking the dramatic waves of her hair, making it look like dark water spilling over itself. ØCissy,Æ she said. ØDraco. Lucius. What news of the Order?Æ ØDraco has been working tirelessly to uncover new information,Æ said Lucius. ØThe Order remains biased against him because of his affiliation with the Dark Lord, of course è they are loath to trust too much in him.Æ Draco couldnít help a sidelong look at his father. Lucius had leapt to speak for him like this in fifth year, too, during the Dark Lordís occasional visits to their home. Heíd thought his father had been talking him up to make him seem like a desirable recruit. Now he also saw that his fatherís intervention was a form of protection. He looked back to Bellatrix, refocussing on the plan, twisting his mouth back into its scowl. ØYeah. Somethingís happened, and I tried for hours to get any details, but they shut me out of the room.Æ He shook his head. ØI think they must have caught the trail of one of those artifacts they want. Thatís got to be it. Or else why would they be so excited?Æ Bellatrix had stopped in her tracks, alone on her wooded path. ØYou say they shut you out. Did you listen at the door, Draco?Æ ØThey cast enchantments against such things, Bella,Æ Narcissa cut in. ØAs you know.Æ ØLet Draco speak, Cissy,Æ Bella snapped. Narcissa drew back in affront, and Bella struggled to compose herself. ØI only mean è you are right, my nephewîthis may be very important è such a weapon could pose a threat to the Dark Lordís aims. You must tell me everything you remember about their conversation.Æ Draco let his scowl fade into a serious expression. ØWell, before they kicked me out, Granger was reading one of those history books. And Weasley and Potter were looking through this book of advanced charmwork.Æ He frowned as if trying to remember something. ØYeah, thatís right. Weasley said something about location spells. Then all three of them got really excited and told me I had to get out right away.Æ He met Bellaís eyes, which were shadowed by their lashes, reflecting no moon. ØIf itís a location spell that did it,Æ Draco said slowly, shifting in his seat as if worried, Øthen they must have figured out where to find one of those artifacts. What are we supposed to do? How am I supposed to find it?Æ Bellatrix had begun to walk again, looking above the rim of the mirror as though to show that her interest had faded, but Draco could see the tension in her generous mouth. Thatís got to be one of the oldest vaults in Gringotts.Æ Hermione was chewing her lip vigorously, excitement coursing through her. The odds were long, of courseîbut they finally knew where the Horcrux was. ØCouldnít we ask Bill? He used to work for Gringotts.Æ Ron snorted. ØYeah, and a fat lot of good thatíll be. They were sitting in Ronís and Harryís bedroom, two on each twin bed. Usually Hermione and Draco were cautious to keep some distance between themselves during these meetingsîbut Ron and Harry had plopped down so quickly on Ronís bed that she now found herself sitting scant feet from Draco on the other. This should not, perhaps, have been as distracting as it was, but Hermione kept remembering the last time theyíd been on a bed together, a fortnight ago, having sneaked up to her room near two in the morning. She had rarely been so grateful for Silencing Charms. Cheeks warm, refusing to let her eyes stray to Draco, she refocussed on Gringotts. Cool rain was drumming at the window. Somewhere below, they could hear Ollivanderís wand-crafting machines whirring gently. Above, Fred and George were clunking around with their broadcasting equipment. ØMaybe the extra security could benefit us,Æ said Draco slowly. ØDiagon Alley is still under No-Apparition. That makes getting out of Gringotts a lot slower, if you canít just Disapparate from the bottom of the steps.Æ Hermione realised what he was suggesting and let out a little ØOoh!Æ ØFill us in, would you?Æ said Harry, looking between her and Draco. Draco lay back on the pillow and stretched his legs out in front of Hermione, his knee inches from hers. ØIím saying, we donít need to get into Gringotts. If we get Bellatrix to take the Horcrux out for us, then we can wait in Diagon Alley and ambush her.Æ Harry frowned. ØHow are we supposed to do that?Æ ØWell, forcing the actual withdrawal should be simple enough,Æ said Hermione, beginning to jot down notes. ØWeíll have to make her feel as though the Horcrux is in danger of being stolen, as if her vault is no longer a safe hiding place. AndîÆ She looked up eagerly at Draco. ØMaybe thatís where Bill can come in! He can give us details that will make it seem like we really know how to break in.Æ ØI doubt we even need that much,Æ said Draco. ØGringotts is run by goblins. Bellatrix thinks about as highly of goblins as she does of house-elves.Æ ØAll right,Æ said Harry, excitement starting to gleam in his eyes, too. ØAnd I wouldnít want to be whichever goblin has to attend her when she gets there.Æ Hermione felt her stomach twist. She loathed the idea of putting innocent goblins in the path of an angry Bellatrix Lestrange è but they had to get the Horcrux, and there seemed no other way. With Gringotts in the mix, its workers would have to be involved eventually. She finished scribbling notes, then drew back from the basic bones of the plan. ØAll right. ØWhat could possibly go wrong?Æ Hermione smiled at him. He met her eyes, his hair tossed against the pillow, and moved his knee so that it brushed hers momentarily. She swallowed and looked away. And although Harry and Ron were in discussion again, Hermione could have sworn she saw Ronís eyes stray, puzzled, to the place that she and Draco had touched. # It was the first time that they had recruited outside help to find a Horcrux. Although it felt strange to make their request to Bill, speaking around why exactly they needed help from the goblins, the alliance drove home to Hermione how close they were to reaching their goal. And it was this feeling of alliance that soon gave them the idea for how to enter Diagon Alley, too. They decided to ask Dobby and Winky, who could get through the Apparition wards, to enter Ollivanderís shop, whose grate had been disconnected from the Floo network. Then the elves would use Professor McGonagallís unregistered Floo method to create a pathway to headquartersîright past the wards. Between these plans, Draco managing Bellatrixís demands, half the Order toiling over the Hogwarts operation, and the other half stretched thin with fights across the nation, Hermione thought headquarters could be no busier. But then, one morning in late April, they were forcibly distracted from the Death Eaters for the first time in months. Tonks had just tested out one of their charmed spellbooks, trying to crack through its appearance to reveal that it was made from leaflets. She hadnít been able to make a dent. ØWe should tell Minerva itís ready for Friday,Æ said Tonks, pleased, lifting her arms. ØAnyone fancy helping me up? Everythingís such aîÆ Halfway to her feet, she gasped, one hand flying to her stomach. ØDora?Æ said Lupin sharply. Tonksís face was screwed up in a grimace for what had to be a full minute, but then, finally, she sighed and straightened up. ØItís nothing. Been happening for weeks. Bodyís practicing, I suppose.Æ But twenty minutes later, as a dozen of the Order sat down for lunch, it happened again. Tonks let out a muffled shout and grasped the edge of the table. ØOh,Æ Mrs. Weasley gasped. ØItís happening!Æ At once everyone was on their feet, voices piling on top of each other. Mrs. Weasley flapped her hands, shooing them out of Tonksís way. ØGive her space to breathe! The lot of you! Here, Tonks, dear èÆ And she escorted Tonks out of headquarters, back to the tent she shared with Remus. Soon Mrs. Weasley bustled back inside to inform them that this could be a long process, and they couldnít all sit around with their breath held, because the baby might not be there that day or even the next è but it was no use trying to concentrate on anything else. Hermione, Draco, Harry, and Ron had meant to go over the map of Diagon Alley to look for hiding places near Gringotts, but they unrolled the sheet, stared at it, and eked out a few half-hearted sentences before hurrying back downstairs. All day, other Order members came in and out from the safehouses, breathlessly asking for updates. Kingsley, who had seen Tonks come up from a trainee, was there so often that he eventually sat down in the front room with a stack of his owled reports and said, ØIíll wait.Æ And wait they all did. The sun sank outside. Hermione found herself shifting around the front room over the hours, moving closer and closer to Draco, until eventually they were seated on the sofa beside each other. No one seemed to notice. ØI hope sheís all right,Æ Hermione said breathlessly, looking for the thousandth time at the closed front door. ØMaybe her Metamorphmagus abilities will make the process less painful èÆ ØMaybe.Æ Draco raised one eyebrow at her. ØYou know what this reminds me of?Æ She lowered her voice to match his. ØWhat?Æ ØLast fall. When we were still living in that tent in the garden, and Remus and Tonks were on the run. You were worrying about them then, too.Æ Hermione smiled. ØAnd one of her cousins, when we were thirteen.Æ Hermione nibbled her lip. Remus was blinking rapidly as if in bright light, moving around the front room and accepting the well-wishes of the entire Order. Ron and Pansy were on their feet in front of the fire, looking equally stunned, as though the outcome were somehow surprising to them. Eventually, after an almighty round of back-slapping and hand-shaking from the Weasley twins, Remus came to Draco and Hermione. Its single room had a small kitchenette in one corner and a bank of enchanted windows opposite. Beneath these windows, Tonks was sitting up in bed, sweaty and exhausted-looking, a bundle of blankets in her arms. ØCome on, come closer,Æ she said with a tired laugh. Why was he smiling? ØI love you, too,Æ he said. Hermione felt her heart stop. As she looked up into his eyes, the entirety of the last year seemed to flow over her, sending a thrill from her toes to the top of her skull, andî Of course I love him, she thought, dazed. Ron and Harry were doubled over, Dracoís mouth halfway open, laugh lines pushed up into his cheeks. Hermione leaned against Draco and laughed until tears touched her eyes, their fingers intertwined. # Bellatrix stalked through the rain with her head held high, the water pouring off her umbrella. She was glad to see the last days of April. The month had brought many storms. Yaxley met her at the usual corner. ØAh. Madam Lestrange. Youíve come. And èÆ He sneered at Wormtail, scurrying along at Bellatrixís shoulder. ØCompany. Delightful.Æ ØYaxley,Æ Wormtail said in his small, resentful wheeze. The three Death Eaters continued down the dark lane, wind gusting around them. A house stood ahead behind a secure gate, its walls made of simple brick. ØAre you sure he cannot be constrained any longer?Æ said Bellatrix. ØQuite sure.Æ Bellatrix drew her wand, letting her nails click down the length of blackthorn. ØThe Dark Lord had hoped to keep him in place until his return è and given the events of the past few days èÆ ØYes,Æ Yaxley murmured. ØTroubling.Æ Bellatrix allowed herself to exchange a look with Yaxley. In Ipswich, posters calling for the capture of Harry Potter had been defaced with graffiti that read LONG LIVE THE BOY WHO LIVED. And reports from Wrexham suggested that locals had started capturing the Daily Prophetís delivery owls. It seemed that the Order had somehow managed to deliver news to witches and wizards across Britain. Bellatrix did not know how such a thing could be; the Malfoys had given no word of a new push for information è and yet she couldnít be imagining such a shift in the national mood. Draco had mentioned a number of safehouses controlled by the Order. This push must have been directed by one of those other locations. Bellatrix seethed as they slipped through the gate, then crunched over gravel to the houseís front door. If the Dark Lord had only returned from the continent two weeks ago, he would have seen the fruits of their efforts, a nation almost completely won. But now the Mudbloods and Muggle-lovers seemed to be lashing out in their death throes. It would make all her work seem less impressive in his eyes. All this on top of the not-insubstantial worry that the Potter boy had fixed his sights on the Cup of the Covenant è but Bella reassured herself that the cup was safe, even if the Order knew its location. In centuries of her familyís reliance on Gringotts, the bankís security had never been threatened. Goblins were inferior creatures, of course, but they could be relied on to serve their purpose. Otherwise there would have been no reason to allow them to continue to exist in Wizarding society. Yaxley knocked at the front door. Travers answered immediately. ØMadam Lestrange,Æ he said, offering Wormtail nothing except a scathing look. ØTravers.Æ Bella swept inside and unclipped her rain-wet outer robe, which she threw into Wormtailís fumbling hands. ØWhere is he?Æ ØThis way.Æ Travers led Bellatrix and Yaxley down the hall, leaving a cursing Wormtail to deal with the robes. The house was in a state of advanced neglect. Dust covered every surface; dead insects lay curled upon surfaces and beneath tables. They entered a modest kitchen, the only room that showed some signs of being lived in. Some dishes lay in the sink, others half-washed on a rack, as though someone absentminded had forgotten to finish cleaning. The Death Eaters gathered around the table. Atop it lay Pius Thicknesse, the Minister for Magic. Thicknesse was, Bellatrix knew, a formidable wizard. Yaxleyís greatest accomplishment in the Dark Lordís service had been to place the initial Imperius Curse upon the former Head of Magical Law Enforcement. Since then, Bellatrix and Travers had maintained the Imperius Curse themselves. Travers had a particular talent for the spell. But she knew the instant she saw Thicknesse upon the table that her fellow Death Eaters were right. It was no good. Thicknesseís dark hair was dishevelled, and though he was immobilised, his eyes slid from side to side. Conscious. Aware. Thicknesse had begun to fight the curse, and if he could fight Bellatrixís and Traversís Imperius Curses, there was no otherís beside the Dark Lord's that could overpower him. As Bellatrix watched, his eyes became fixed again. He had fallen back under the spell. But they could not risk him clawing his way into a lucid moment when addressing the public. Bellatrix looked with distaste around the kitchen. Thicknesse had lived a half-life these past months, drifting home to eat mechanically, then sleeping until he was next called to his public life. His eyes began to swivel again, fixing on Bellatrix. She could see the loathing there, the thirst to break free and duel her. Normally she might have had some fun with him, but she could think only of the Dark Lordís displeasure that their puppet had outlived its use. Bellatrix glanced to Travers. ØI presume you have had him write a letter to his office explaining his coming absence?Æ ØYes. A nervous complaint, Iíve called it. Fits with Skeeterís last article well enough.Æ ØGood.Æ Bellatrix pointed her wand at Thicknesse and said, ØAvada Kedavra.Æ Pius Thicknesse went limp upon the table just in time for Peter Pettigrew to enter the kitchen and freeze at the sight. ØClean that up, Wormtail,Æ Bellatrix said to Pettigrew as she stalked past. ØCome, Yaxley, Traversîwe have much to discuss.Æ Chapter End Notes canít let people laugh without a murder! idk why i am saying this now, but i wanted to say, i don't generally reply to comments because i get hideously embarrassed to see the things that i have previously said. and it takes me like 10 anxious minutes to type out a two-sentence comment. but truly, the nice words y'all have left on this fic have sparked joy that marie kondo herself could not describe!! thank you so much for reading, i am so lucky to write for such kind people <3 tumblr | instagram Chapter 28: Goldas a Galleon Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes It was a time for the spreading of news. Over Easter, Dobby and Winky had smuggled fifty thousand re-information leaflets home with Hogwarts students, divided between a hundred thick hard-cover books. By early May, the Order could see the effects across the nation, with new sympathiser groups grating against the Death Eatersí regime in every major city. This was reason to hopeîbut it was also a more volatile state of affairs. Nearly every day they received reports of those brutalised or murdered for their resistance, and to worsen matters, the Daily Prophet had reported that Pius Thicknesse had taken a leave of absence due to a nervous complaint. There was no question among the Order as to what this meant for Thicknesse. The Order and their allies began to brace for Voldemortís return, and for the stand they would need to make when he stepped, at last, into the spotlight. News was spreading within headquarters, too, though of a very different sort. They had to clutch to lightness wherever they could to make the days bearable. Soon every member of the Order knew about the undefined something between Ron and Pansy, which caused a storm of Weasley-twin teasing that Draco didnít envy. It was also now common knowledge that Draco and Hermione were boyfriend and girlfriend. Draco allowed himself to revel in it, this one bright thing in a sea of danger. It was almost stupid how smug it made him feel, the simple act of sliding into a seat beside Hermione, brushing her hand with his, and seeing the pleased smile upon her face. Every time she looked at him he felt like a cat basking in sunlight. ØYou two are revolting,Æ Ginny had commented one afternoon, looking almost impressed. Of course, the breaking of the news had other implications. It was a week into May before Draco mustered the resolve to feed his parents the cover story theyíd designed. ØI wonít be there,Æ Hermione reminded him. ØWhatever they say, it canít hurt me.Æ ØI know.Æ ØJust recite the twelve uses of dragonís blood in your mind if you want to block it out.Æ Dracoís mouth twitched in a smile. ØWe learned those in first year, Granger. Give me more of a challenge than that.Æ Theyíd joked about distractions a while longer, but nothing eased Dracoís nerves. It was with trepidation that he returned to his parentsí tent that night, and his pulse tapped apace as they sat reading throughout the evening. Finally, when it became clear that no opening into the subject would arise, Draco closed his book and spoke. ØI forgot to mention. Thereís something you two should know.Æ He hoped he sounded casual, but his throat felt thin, as though he were forcing the words through the hollow in a reed. ØOh?Æ said his mother without looking up from The Practical Potioneerís Guide to Ingredient Harvesting. ØIím pretending to be involved with that Granger girl. Theyíll never expect that Iíd do something like that to get information.Æ His parentsí heads lifted as one. Draco braced himself for revulsion, for outraged panic. He received ringing silence instead. His fatherís mouth was ajar. Draco sighed, feigning impatience. ØI know, I know. But Iím telling you, itís a good plan. What better way is there to get in with the Orderís inner circle?Æ Lucius reanimated with a slow nod. ØTrue è yes, there would be few more certain paths to gain their trust.Æ He swirled his Firewhisky, looking disturbed. ØI will admit that even the idea of è but no matter. The circumstances are extraordinary.Æ They both looked to Narcissa, whose eyes were wide. Dracoís grip tightened on his book as he awaited his motherís verdict. He supposed that any moment now the vitriol would come through. He supposed, with a sour feeling coiling in his stomach, that she would revile the idea of her precious pure-blood son besmirched by the touch of a Muggle-born. But then Narcissa said, in a tense, unfamiliar voice, ØThis is significant.Æ She snapped The Practical Potioneerís Guide shut and rose to her feet. ØThis shows that the Order really believes you to be one of them, Draco. I have been waiting for a sign like this for some time.Æ ØYouîyou have?Æ ØYes.Æ She strode to the bookshelf, then back to the mantel. ØAnd I have begun to feel that you and I should begin to make these same inroads, Lucius. Draco has gone into the Orderís ranks to reveal the Potter boy. We will succeed, we must succeed.Æ ØYet there have been plans to do away with Harry Potter before!Æ The retort came like a whip-crack, Narcissaís composure slipping. ØPlans laid by Barty Crouch, a prodigious wizard, and by the Dark Lord himself è and still, here Potter stands. He will survive. If our family is to survive, we must not turn down safety, wherever we find it.Æ Silence filled the tent. Draco rose, too, feeling incredulous. The fact that his mother hadnít said a word against Hermione was enough of a shockîbut the idea of his parents becoming Order informants? After months of trying to break the Order from within, his mother was finally having doubts? Draco glanced to his father. Lucius was turning the Malfoy golden signet ring on his little finger, a match to Dracoís silver ring of the same design. His face filled with reluctance verging on distaste, as though he were being forced to take a bite of a dish he knew would revolt him. Iíve seen that before, Draco realised with a jolt. It was the same way that he himself had joined Hermione, Harry, and Ron. He had reassured himself that by helping the Gryffindors he was only trying to keep himself safe, no matter his disdain for them. It was the first step he had taken down this path: one of apparent necessity. ØI think she has a good point, Father,Æ Draco dared to say. But the words shook Lucius from his trance. ØEnough. Itís a decision for your mother and me to make, Draco. To bed, now.Æ In the weeks that followed, Draco kept careful watch on both of them. The plan to steal Hufflepuffís cup was progressing steadilyîBill had set a date for several goblins to visit headquarters, and throughout May, Professor McGonagall taught Dobby and Winky to open a Floo passage into the deserted Ollivanderísîbut part of Dracoís mind remained always on his parents. He often felt as if he had just missed a conversation when he walked into the tent, and as the weather grew fairer, Lucius and Narcissa began to spend more time on the cottage's back patio. Draco sometimes caught them watching the Order through the window, but not eavesdropping, anymore. His parents simply watched the Order stir inside, lips occasionally moving in conversation. As the end of May approached, he and Hermione decided to tell Harry and Ron about his motherís suggestion. The boys looked as though theyíd been struck in the gut with Bludgers. ØBut we canít let them join the Order,Æ Ron protested, fanning himself with a stray leaflet. The bedroom was stiflingly hot. ØYouíve already let one Malfoy in,Æ said Draco. ØYeah, because unlike your mum and dad, youíre trying to stop Bellatrix killing us all.Æ Draco looked up to heaven. Maybe, the more his parents grew accustomed to his ØpretendÆ relationship with Hermione, the more the sight would appear normal to them. ØYeah, I suppose weíve done all right, havenít we?Æ ØWe have.Æ Harry wasnít smiling, but his eyes were bright with determination. ØAnd weíre going to get the cup. Weíre going to finish this.Æ ØLetís go over the plan again,Æ said Hermione. So they did, again and again, in the days leading up to their bid on Gringotts. While tightened by weeksí preparation, the plan still left more up to chance than Draco really liked. It was true that he had the Elder Wand, but if Bellatrix brought her husband with her to make the withdrawal, it would worsen their odds in an outright duel. They decided that they would take Gryffindorís sword to Diagon Alley, in case they had only limited time with the cup. The last night before Billís goblin friends were due to arrive at the Potter Cottage, they stayed up late, sitting in the front room with Pansy, Ginny, and Luna. They had talked through the plan all afternoon and couldnít see how it could be improved, but none could make themselves sleep just yet. ØSo,Æ Pansy said, her dark eyes moving among them. ØAre you planning on telling us why weíre supposed to stay out of the cottage tomorrow afternoon?Æ ØNope,Æ said Ron. ØSee?Æ said Pansy to Ginny and Luna. ØI told you theyíre plotting something.Æ Ron grinned. ØLike youíve never plotted anything before.Æ Ginny sighed, nudging Harry. ØYou could tell us, you know.Æ ØSorry, Gin,Æ said Harry. ØWhat is it, whatís happened?Æ ØNothing.Æ Harry forced his shaking hand down from his head, although his eyes were still squeezed shut with pain. ØVoldemortís figured out it was Grindelwald who stole the Elder Wand. Heís on his way to Nurmengard.Æ # Three goblins arrived at headquarters the next day, stepping out of the Floo with three emerald flashes. ØGornuk, you can read part A, Griphook, you can take part B, and Bogrod, part C.Æ The goblins frowned down at the pieces of parchment. ØThis suggests,Æ said Bogrod, sounding frosty, Øthat Gringotts would work to hand over a piece of treasure to one not its rightful owner.Æ ØWe know itís against your code,Æ said Harry. The rest of the Order had honoured Harryís request; those who were not at the safehouses were in their respective tents. The Potter Cottage was quiet and empty as it had not been since December, when only he, Hermione, and Harry had trodden these old floorboards. Draco let himself stand, a moment, in the calm before the storm. Then he moved swiftly through the cottage. The mirror was clasped in his motherís hand, Bellatrix hunched so close that her face filled the glass. As Draco eased the back door shut, he slipped his hand into his pocket and grasped the Elder Wand. Flagrate, he thought. Nothing happened in sight, but he knew that beyond the closed door of the dining room, a fiery X would appear in the air, signalling the start to the script. He held his finger to his lips and led his parents down the hall. Voices became audible, echoing distinctly beneath the dining room door. They stopped and listened. ØWhat you ask of us is highly unusual,Æ Bogrod was saying. ØIt may even be unprecedented in our history. We do not often seek to ally ourselves with wand-carriers.Æ ØWeíre not asking for an alliance,Æ said Harryís voice. ØItís just a favour. Draco sneaked a look at his aunt. She had gone the colour of ash, her eyes bulging. ØNo other goblin would help you in this matter,Æ said Gornukîa line Hermione had added in hopes that Bellatrix might not hurt the Gringotts goblins. ØDoes that mean youíll do it?Æ said Hermione eagerly. ØYouíll help us?Æ Bogrod sighed. ØVery well. We will help you retrieve the item tomorrow.Æ ØBut the vault in question is very old,Æ said Griphook. ØOne of the oldest. You know its fortifications. We will have to think carefully about how to procure this è you said it was a small golden cup, yes?Æ If they had really been eavesdropping, Bellatrix would have revealed them. She drew in an audible gasp. Narcissa blanched and clutched the mirror close. ØGo, go,Æ Bellatrix hissed. ØGo!Æ They dashed back down the hall, through the kitchen, and down the garden. They did not stop to speak until they had returned to his parentsí tent. ØWhat is it, Bellatrix?Æ Lucius demanded. ØWhat does it mean?Æ ØWe are in more danger than you can possibly know,Æ she whispered. Her darting eyes were wide, and fury was starting to seep into her expression. ØI must go immediately. This must not è I cannot è I must go!Æ And she was gone. The mirror reflected only Draco and his fearful-looking parents. ØA cup?Æ said Draco, trying to sound blank. ØWhy is that so important?Æ ØI donít know,Æ Lucius murmured. ØPerhaps è it could be the Cup of the Covenant. The Dark Lord would not look kindly on its loss, and yetîthat kind of reaction from Bella èÆ Draco seized the opening. ØIíll go back and keep listening. You two shouldnít be inside.Æ He knew that even now, Hermione, Harry, and Ron would be piling through the Floo, exiting into Ollivanderís dusty shop. Bellatrix would be Apparating into the Leaky Cauldron checkpoint, probably storming past the line to have her paperwork verified. Time was precious. He hadnít taken three steps toward the tent flap, however, when his mother seized him by the wrist. ØNo.Æ ØItís fine, Mother. He needed to Transfigure himself and get through the Floo. There wasnít a minute to waste. He was sorting through excuses when Lucius said, ØOn the same note, Draco, I have been carefully considering your motherís suggestion to keep us all safe. ØBoth of you, youíll do what Iím doing?Æ His parents both nodded. Draco knew he must leave the tent and get to Ollivanders. And yet his feet were fixed in place. He imagined his father sitting stiffly beside the Weasleys in a meeting, worn down over time, beginning to grow exhausted by the effort it took to scorn everyone around him. His father, who so prized intelligence, would have no choice but to see Hermioneís brilliance, however reluctant he was to acknowledge it at first. Draco imagined his mother exchanging clipped words with Tonks, perhaps seeing a glimmer of Andromeda there. Draco squeezed the Elder Wand in his pocket, a mad feeling racing of possibility through him. When he returned, he would break the mirror; he would plead his parentsí case to the Order. They only needed to execute the plan. ØAll right,Æ he said, trying to sound casual. And I want to know what Aunt Bella says if she contacts us again.Æ The second he was out of the tent, he broke into a run. # ØHe was surprised,Æ Lucius remarked once Draco had gone. ØYes, I thought he would be.Æ Narcissa approached her husband and laid her head into the crook of his neck. ØHe must not want his situation disrupted. It is delicate, thatís true. è But heíll see we can do more together.Æ ØYes.Æ Lucius kissed her forehead. ØI suppose we should retire these, if weíre to join the rabble.Æ When Narcissa drew back, she saw that he was holding one of the Extendable Ears. She raised her brows at her husband; she had heard an unfinished sentence there. He gave a thin smile and went on. Øè but perhaps we should use them one last time. I donít want Draco involved, but he was right that there will certainly be more to learn from the goblinsí conversation. I wouldnít mind knowing why Bella reacted the way she did.Æ Narcissa hesitated. ØOnce more, then,Æ she murmured, taking the other flesh-coloured thread from the cup on the mantel. They crept out into the garden, then around the side of the cottage. They stayed low, the tops of their heads beneath the windowsills, moving as quickly as they could. But when they reached the dining room window, they saw that the table was empty. Lucius swore quietly. ØThey must have left in a hurry.Æ Narcissa narrowed her eyes at the open door. ØOr did they suspect an eavesdropper and move rooms? Here, this way.Æ The sunshine beat down upon their robes as they slunk to the front of the cottage. Narcissa came to an abrupt halt as she looked through the front window. For an instant she didnít understand what she was seeing. Then she realised that the dark-haired man before them was Draco, casting spells upon himself, Transfiguring himself into someone moustached and older-looking. As he swept a jar of Floo Powder from the mantel and took a pinch, Narcissa drew a sharp breath. ØLuciusî!Æ Lucius sprang to her side. Draco was already declaring, muffled but still audible, ØOllivanders!Æ He stepped into the blaze of green flame and vanished. Lucius and Narcissa bolted for the door and burst over the threshold. ØWhat has he done?Æ Narcissa gasped. She couldnít catch her breath. Spots were bursting in her eyes. There could be no more dangerous place for any of them than Diagon Alley. ØOllivanders,Æ Lucius said with realisation, striding toward the grate. ØThis connection must be how the Order plans to break into Gringotts. Draco must have seen them leave and given chase.Æ He whirled back to Narcissa, face full of dread. ØThatís where theyíve gone. All of them. Draco must be trying to capture Potter for Bellatrix as we speak.Æ Narcissa touched her hand to her racing heart. She had thought Draco would be pleased at their caution. She had thought that he would continue to put safety before zealotry, the way Lucius had finally agreed to do. But it seemed her son had learned the Dark Lordís messages too well. As she tried desperately to catch her breath, it occurred to her with a burst of agonising clarity that they should never have been such diligent teachers. All along, they should have known better than to press any of this upon their son. ØWe have no time to lose,Æ Narcissa said in a harsh whisper. She swept the jar from the mantel. # ØWhat took you?Æ Hermione asked as Draco slipped out the window of Ollivanders into the side alley. ØMy parents,Æ Draco murmured as she lifted her wand to double-check his Transfiguration. ØIíll explain later.Æ Hermione and Ron had both undergone their Transfigurations, too. Hermione had pin-straight, red-blonde hair, and Ron appeared as a blond man with a bushy beard. They would have preferred Polyjuice, but they hadnít been able to procure the necessary ingredients to brew a new cauldron since February. ØPotter under the Cloak?Æ Draco said. ØHere,Æ came Harryís voice from beside Draco. ØLetís go.Æ They hurried up the side street, then emerged into a Diagon Alley full of blazing sunlight. Draco glanced back at Ollivandersís boarded-up storefront. It looked as abandoned as before. The rest of Diagon Alley, on the other hand, was much changed. Storefronts that Draco remembered as vacant had been resold. The Muggle-born-owned Eeylops Owl Emporium, which his parents had always refused to enter, had now been replaced by Selwynís High-Bred Birds. Draco also noted a new outpost of Borgin and Burkeís nearby, from whose window an array of cursed objects glittered out at mesmerised passers-by. Draco began to sweat as they strode up the street toward the white marble faade of Gringotts. Here upon the cusp of summer, the sunlight was as heavy as water. It seemed impossible that the dozens of people bustling and chatting around them could not simply look at the four of themîwell, the three of themîand see the wanted criminals whose faces were plastered upon every shopfront. ØHere,Æ said Hermione quietly as they neared Gringotts. Dobby, who had investigated Diagon Alley for them under cover of night, had told them about an alcove in an intersection just opposite the bank, occupied by a disused drinking fountain. As they sidled past it, Hermione performed a quick Illusion Charm to make the alcove look like a solid brick wall. Then, one by one, unnoticed by the harried-looking patrons of Diagon Alley, they slipped through the illusory barrier to wait. Huddled on the other side, they could still see the Gringotts steps and entrance quite clearly through the Illusion Charm. ØI hope we havenít missed Bellatrix,Æ Hermione whispered, peering through the tan shadow that hovered in mid-air. ØWeíll want to be certain that sheís gone inside èÆ ØWe havenít missed her.Æ The dread in Ronís voice made Draco follow his gaze down the street. His insides plummeted. Bellatrix Lestrange was striding up Diagon Alley, drawn out just as theyíd planned. But she wasnít alone, and it wasnít her husband sheíd brought along. Striding at Bellatrixís back was a contingent of some sixty witches and wizards. Four, at the front, Draco recognised as Death Eaters. The rest wore Ministry robes. It looked like an entire department, but as for what they were doing there, Draco had no idea. His head spun. How could this be happening? All four of them had been convinced, more than anything, that Bellatrix would want to draw as little attention to the Horcrux as possible. If Voldemort ever found out that the Order had come close to stealing it, she would be punished brutally. She must know this. What could her motivation possibly be for making a spectacle of its withdrawal? They gazed, horror-struck, as Bellatrix and her entourage approached the sweeping Gringotts steps. Two wizards at the bankís entrance, who were holding long, thin Probity Probes, let the metal wands quaver as the force strode up the steps. Ron took in a sudden gasp. ØWait a minute. I know those robes. Thatís the Department for the Regulation and Control of Magical Creatures.Æ ØWhat?Æ Hermione and Harry said at the same timeîbut Ron was right, Draco realised. Hadnít he always seen Walden Macnair in robes lined and cut that same way? ØBut whyîÆ Hermione began. ØOut of my way,Æ Bellatrix snarled with a slash of her wand. The security wizards dived for safety, and as Bellatrixís spell struck the door of Gringotts, a low, mournful tone emitted from the marble, which swelled like a voice. The doors swung open to reveal four goblins in the antechamber. The goblin at the front said, ØMadam Lestrange. What is the meaning of this?Æ ØYou know perfectly well what my meaning is, you horrid little beast,Æ Bellatrix hissed. ØYour kind are no longer to be trusted. We never should have allowed you such free rein, with your leniency to Mudbloods and blood traitors!Æ She raised her voice so that it rang down the street. They moved more quickly than Draco could have imagined, melting back into the bank, out of sight.
