ØWait!Æ ØBut the goblinsîÆ Hermione broke off as Draco pointed. Shouts and flashes of light were flying out from the magnificent doors, and dozens of goblins were now visible fighting in the entryway. Most held long, beautifully made daggers that glowed in the summer sun; others wore finely crafted helms or armour. Though they had no wands, they dived and leapt with such speed that the wizards were placed on the defensive, using deflecting spells to keep away from those deadly blades. ØIf we come near them,Æ Draco said, Øtheyíll assume weíre breaking in, too.Æ ØWe might have to break in,Æ Ron said, sounding sick. ØThis might be our only chance to get the cup, while theyíre occupied. We canít rely on the plan now, can we?Æ ØMaybe we can,Æ said Harry. He slipped the Invisibility Cloak off to reveal his Transfigured self, blue-eyed and baby-faced. ØBellatrix still thinks we know how to get the cup from her vault. I think sheíll want it out of there, no matter what.Æ Ron rounded on Draco. ØMalfoy, how long do you reckon itíll take Lestrange to get to that vault and back up again?Æ ØItíll be deep. Near my familyís vault. It takes fifteen minutes to get that far down, another twenty to get back up.Æ ØAnd weíre just supposed to wait around while the goblins are attacked?Æ Hermione said, voice shaking in outrage. ØMaybe we can get close enough toîÆ Harry let out an agonised sound and clutched at his forehead, staggering, nearly falling. Draco bolstered him with the others; there was barely any room in the alcove. ØWhatís happening?Æ said Ron. ØVolîheís there,Æ Harry gasped out, blinking sweat out of his eyes. ØHeís broken through Nurmengardís protections. è Heís in Grindelwaldís cell, torturing him for answers about the Elder Wand.Æ ØThen heís going to find out it passed to Dumbledore,Æ Hermione whispered. ØHeíll come back to Britain.Æ The four of them stood stock-still, sweating more profusely than ever. The sounds of yells and crashes from within Gringotts rang down Diagon Alley. Draco knew they were all thinking the same thing. If Voldemort returned to the country while Death Eaters and Ministry alike were gathered here, he could well join his servants. It would fall perfectly in line with Rita Skeeterís writings: Voldemort, a supposed fighter for wizardsí society, ØliberatingÆ Gringotts from the possession of the goblins. Hermione broke the silence with a stifled scream, clapping her hand over her mouth. Dracoís gaze flew to the top of the steps. One of the Ministry wizards had fled through the bankís doors, a chest levitated in front of him. But hardly had he taken a step over the threshold when a goblin appeared, clasping a wound on his own arm, and slashed his dagger somewhere behind the wizardís legs. The thief howled, and the chest plummeted, cracked open on the Gringotts steps, and spilled its contents down like a golden waterfall. ØTheyíre looting,Æ Draco said, staring at the spread of Galleons. ØTheyíre not just here to get control of Gringotts. Theyíre emptying the vaults that belong to Muggle-borns.Æ ØOf course,Æ said Harry through gritted teeth. ØIíll bet thatís how Bellatrix got them all to risk their necks. Take whatever you can steal.Æ Ron looked stunned. ØWe canít let them!Æ ØLet them?Æ Draco said. ØWe canít stop them, Weasley! There are only four of us, and if we run in there and start fighting, we might not see Bellatrix leaving. Think of the Horcrux.Æ Ronís face reddened, but he let out a frustrated sound and turned back around to watch. Dracoís jaw clenched. He was no happier about the situation than the others; he couldnít believe the scale of the miscalculation. All this after Hermione had fretted for weeks about how individual goblins might be affected. The fight raged on within the bank. After a quarter of an hour, dozens more goblins pelted up the street, all wearing finely crafted armour, to reinforce the Gringotts numbers. They cut bonds, energised unconscious goblins with glittering potions, and started a new array of battles. But still the wizards were carting hoards of treasures up into the Gringotts hall, stacking chests and crates of stolen gold higher and higher. ØI canít believe we did this,Æ Hermione whispered, her face bloodless. ØI canít believe I didnít think of it. Wizards have tried to force Gringotts out of goblin control three different times; Professor Binns gave us a four-day lecture on the Goblin Rebellion of 1734 èÆ ØI canít believe none of these people are doing anything to help,Æ Harry ground out, gesturing at the scores of Diagon Alley shoppers who had collected around the Gringotts steps, staring open-mouthed into the fray. ØThey donít have a Horcrux to find.Æ ØLetís get out in the open,Æ Ron urged. ØWe want to be nearer, anyway, donít we? Maybe we can find a way to help.Æ They all exchanged looks, then slipped out of the concealed alcove. It wasnít a moment too soon. They were halfway through the crowd when they heard shouts compounding from within. The furious cries of goblins. ØItís her! The witch! Lestrange!Æ ØOut of my way!Æ they heard Bellatrix shriek. ØAway, you brutes! Bombarda!Æ A dozen onlookers screamed. The hex had struck the tower of treasure in the hall. So forceful was Bellatrixís spell that the crates, chests, and sacks of money were propelled forward. The treasure scattered Death Eaters and goblins alike, then toppled through the wide-open doors of Gringotts with a deafening crash. The cacophony multiplied as wooden bindings exploded open and loosed their contents. On and on it went, the clinking, clanking, and pouring of tens of thousands of Galleons, innumerable Sickles and Knuts, slip-sliding down the long marble steps onto the cobbles of Diagon Alley. The fight spilled out afterward. Goblins and wizards came slipping and sliding over the glittering tidal wave of coin, slashing and snarling down the steps. Spells issued up into the clear sky. The clang of goblin-forged metal echoed off Diagon shopfronts. A blasting spell struck a nearby building, and yells came from the apartments within; the beams of the building creaked and groaned. As screaming onlookers began to run for cover, Draco struggled to band together with Hermione, Harry, and Ron. They forged against the current, moving toward the steps. Then they saw her. Bellatrix had emerged into the open, fending off a dozen goblins at once. A leather pouch, bulging with an object the size of a fist. ØThatís it,Æ Draco breathed, gooseflesh rippling up his arms in the sweltering heat. ØThe cup.Æ ØNow!Æ Harry yelled. The four of them sprinted through the last of the crowd and into the melee. They had imagined a scenario like a kidnapping, Draco using the Elder Wand at a key moment of stealth to overcome Bellatrix. They had imagined silence and subtlety, and the ability to sneak back into Ollivanders without being noticed. All that was now abandoned. They clawed their way up the steps through the landslide of Galleons, struggling toward Bellatrix, who paid them no notice, still battling a dozen goblins. And it wasnít just the Ministry and goblins upon the steps now. While many witches and wizards had fled, scores of others had been drawn to the spill, shoving gold and silver into their pockets. Goblins darted over the piles of money, inhumanly light and agile, crying out at those who were pocketing coins. All was a confused mass of limbs and knives, the hot smell of metal baking in the sun. Voices bellowed spells as Draco staggered up the steps. It was through all this, piercing through the mix, that he heard his motherís voice. ØDraco! DRACO!Æ He froze, then flung himself to the marble side rail and grabbed on, scanning the crowd. Had he misheard? How could she be here? Then he saw them. His parents, trying to scramble into the foray at the bottom of the steps. They held wands that he could only assume theyíd scraped from the shelves at Ollivanders, and they had Transfigured themselves. They would have been recognisable to few but him. ØDraco!Æ his father shouted. ØIs he here?Æ Draco didnít know who his father meant, but he threw a wild look back at Bellatrixîhad she heard his name? His heart plunged. Bellatrix had freed herself from the goblins. She was gazing down the steps at his parents, seeming to draw understanding from his fatherís words that Draco hadnít. Bellatrix swept her wand out. ØFinite Incantatem!Æ she screamed. Draco, watching, barely had time to think Protego! and lift the Elder Wand in defence. The spell glanced off, leaving his disguise intactîbut at the bottom of the steps, his parentsí Transfigurations dissolved immediately. Elsewhere on the steps, Hermione, Harry, and Ron reappeared as themselves. Cries of shock ricocheted through the crowd. The goblins who saw Harry slowed in recognition, their daggers faltering, and little by little, limb by limb, a great stillness fell over the scene. Heads craned out of windows all down the street. The crowd of shoppers and shopkeepers were creeping closer once more. Now, without the cacophony, Draco could hear some of the voices. ØHarry PotterîÆ ØLucius MalfoyîÆ ØJust there, itís Granger èÆ Bellatrix had eyes for nobody except Harry. ØPotter,Æ she breathed, moving to the brink of the steps. Several of the Ministry made motions as if to curse Harry, but Bellatrix said, ØNo! He is mine to deliver to the Dark Lord!Æ She pushed up her sleeve, exposing her Dark Mark. In that instant of distraction, Draco took aim with the Elder Wand and thought, Diffindo! The base of Bellatrixís leather pouch split as if scythed open by one of those sharp daggers. Out of it slipped a small golden cup, glowing like the sun itself. Bellatrix let out a panicked cry and grabbed for it, but the cup had already struck the lip of the step and was now bouncing down the pour of coin. ØAccio,Æ Draco yelled, aiming his wand. He heard Hermione, Harry, and Ron doing the sameîØAccio!Æ But the Horcrux must have been charmed to resist, because it flew farther and faster, catapulting base over handles down the steps. Draco threw himself bodily for the cup. In his periphery he saw the others doing the same. Ron was closest; he dove headlong for the Horcrux, while Harry seized the mokeskin pouch from around his neck. He pulled from itîto cries from the onlookersîthe sword of Godric Gryffindor. The cup was feet from Ronís hand. Draco swept out the Elder Wand and yelled, ØAbriarus!Æ There was a high, metallic ping as the cup bounced off the invisible barrier. Ron let out a triumphant shout as it rebounded directly into his waiting palm. Draco reached his side and prepared for them to bolt through the crowd. Bellatrixís voice stopped them all cold. ØVery good. And now, you will return that to me.Æ Ice flooded through Draco as he realised that only Harry and Ron were flanking him. All three revolved on the spot. Bellatrix stood at the top of the steps, holding her wand to Hermioneís throat. ØNo,Æ Draco croaked. Harry and Ron had spoken, too, but he couldnít hear. The entire world had narrowed to the spot where Bellatrixís wand touched Hermioneís skin. She was frozen, her hair spilling over Bellatrixís shoulder, coppery as the spill of Knuts around them. Dracoís eyes locked onto Hermioneís. He could see the thoughts racing through her mind, desperate calculations for escape. His mind whirled with plans, too. Could they frighten Bellatrix by threatening the cup? What other leverage did they have, what other chance? They had made emergency escape plans with Dobby and Winky, but something else must have gone wrong, because the elves were not here è No one moved. Draco stood with the Elder Wand in hand, Harry with the sword of Gryffindor, Ron with the cup of Hufflepuff. All the power in the world, useless. ØWell done, Cissy, Lucius,Æ said Bellatrix, voice silky. She cast her imperious gaze upon the rest of the gathered wizards. ØSeize them.Æ Harry moved before any of the Ministry wizards, grabbing the Horcrux from Ronís grip and holding it against the edge of the sword. ØI donít think so,Æ he yelled. ØLet her go, or we destroy this. I donít think your boss would be too happy about that, would he? Iíve noticed you donít seem too interested in calling him here anymore!Æ The Ministry wizards had halted, looking to Bellatrix for instruction. But Draco could hear the edge of desperation in Potterís voice. If they really did destroy the cup, Bellatrix would kill Hermione in cold blood. Draco knew it was an empty threat. Bellatrix knew it, too. She let out a high, delighted laugh. ØOh, let us not play games, Potter è I know you value your friends. I know you do not wish to see this one killed.Æ Her smile turned to a leer. ØYou wish to make a bargain? Here is my bargain. Give me that cup, and every minute you disobey, I will make a new decoration on the Mudblood, like so èÆ Hermione began to scream. The sound made something tear open in Dracoís stomach. A cut was forming beneath Bellatrixís wandtip, blood trickling down Hermioneís neck. Diagon Alley seemed to warp around him. And in the moment of terror, an idea burst into his mind. ØGod, just give it up, would you, Potter?Æ he said, moving the Elder Wand over himself so that his Transfiguration melted away. He stood exposed under the brilliant sun: alive. Hermioneís scream broke. Surprise had interrupted Bellatrixís curse. ØDraco,Æ said his aunt. More whispers from the crowd. The sound of the words Malfoy, Draco Malfoy floated on the breeze. Draco forced himself not to look at Hermione, not to make sure she was all right, although every instinct in his body screamed for him to do so. He could show no reaction to her pain. ØI told you.Æ Draco worked a smirk onto his face and stepped upward through the spill of coins. ØI said Iíd get Potter for you, Aunt Bellaîand here he is.Æ He cast a scathing look back at Harry and Ron. ØSurprise,Æ he sneered. ØYou really thought I was on your side? Thatís the problem with you Order people, isnít it? You canít imagine anyone doing anything for themselves.Æ Harry and Ron just stared, wordless. He could practically hear their voices in his mind: What do you think youíre doing? He dared to raise his brows slightly in their direction: Trust me. Draco knew that even if he hexed Bellatrix and freed Hermione, they would be immediately beset by dozens of Ministry wizards. But if he could convince Bellatrix to bring the others back to the Lestrange House for interrogation before summoning Voldemort, maybe there was a chance. All he needed was one moment alone with them, one moment to free them. Once they were out of imminent danger, they could deal with the cup. He looked back at Bellatrix as if hoping for her approval. ØYes,Æ said his aunt slowly. ØYes, youíve done well, Draco.Æ ØI know. And we werenít expecting to be able to do it for months. I told Mother and Father that meeting was important. I knew it.Æ ØLucky indeed that you overheard those rats at work.Æ Bellatrix smiled, but she was still watching him too carefully for his liking. Dracoís mouth was bone-dry as he sauntered up toward her, occasionally giving careless glances to the goblins and Ministry workers on either side. He couldnít seem defensive, couldnít let the faade falter. Sweat slithered down Dracoís back. Half a dozen steps away from Bellatrix, he remembered the last time he had stood face-to-face with her. The words she had hissed before lashing out at him with her wand. Clear your mind! He realised what she was going to do an instant too late. ØLegilimens!Æ she cried. Draco, having forced himself to hold the Elder Wand casually at his side, did not lift it in time. But as the spell struck him, his mind went slack instinctively. He dropped to his knees on the steps. Diagon Alley was too bright. He felt Bellatrix scrabbling for purchase on the hard, slick surface of his thoughts. He could not allow himself to think a spell, even to raise his wand, or she would dig in. He let his gaze slide into soft focus. The blue blur of the sky. White marble and white cloud. Everything bright, beautiful, blank, impenetrable. Then he allowed certain thoughts to flurry across the surface, perfectly controlled. He was sitting with his parents in the tent, speaking with them about how to get to Potter. He was storming through Hogsmeade last summer, every move with the Order one of resentment and reluctance. A natural, Bellatrix had called Draco. And he had been, once. No fissure memories for him, no cracks of uncertainty or dependencyînothing heíd cared so deeply about that he would make himself vulnerable. He was not looking at Hermione. He was not thinking of Hermione. He could feel Bellatrixís assault withdrawing. The beating sun and the swimming sky were stilling around him, the effects of the spell lifting è Then Bellatrix wrapped her arm around Hermioneís throat. Hermione let out a choked cry, and Dracoís eyes flew to herîhe couldnít stop himselfîand the sight of her, the smear of blood across her neck, her warm brown eyes, the lips he could picture laughingîthe sudden terror for her life burst across his mind, not so much a fissure as a chasm. Bellatrix dug in and wrenched his mind open. Draco collapsed, screaming, on the steps of Gringotts. In his mindís eye he was reliving the events of the past year, but his watering eyes could still see Bellatrixís vindictive pleasure, her rage and hatred, as her outstretched wand held the connection. Somewhere in his consciousness, he heard his parents screaming. ØBellatrix! BELLA! NO!Æ An instant of distraction lessened the spellís force, and in that moment, some counter-hex left Dracoís mouth, the kind of instinctive magic that comes out of Legilimency. A bolt of light rocketed from the Elder Wand and struck Bellatrix in the shoulder, breaking her hold on him. The force of his own hex threw Draco back. He was slipping, falling, sliding backward down the Galleon-slick steps, Ministry workers and goblins leaping out of the way as though to touch him was to invite death. Two pairs of hands stopped him in his tracks. Harry and Ron helped him scramble upright, and as Draco turned, gasping, aching all over, he nursed the tiny hope that the spell might have dislodged Bellatrixís grip on Hermioneîthat she might have burst free. The hope died. The two women stood as before, although an angry weal had appeared on Bellatrixís collarbone. ØSo!Æ Panting, Bellatrix placed her wand upon Hermioneís neck again. Her mouth was drawn wide, baring her teeth. ØSo this was the truth all along, Draco. You, the scion of the Malfoy nameîyou abandon your own family to side with the Muggles and half-breeds. You debase yourself with this Mudblood!Æ Draco held his fury inside, aftershocks of pain running over his skin. He knew Bellatrixís tactics. She wanted to bait him into some noble speech, then humble him by torturing Hermione. But Draco would give her nothing. He had shown the truth already. He had nothing more to prove. Instead he held Hermioneís eyes. He tried to think of some exit strategy, but there was nothing left. Their last chance was gone. They were surrounded, impossibly outnumbered. He saw that hollow terror in her eyes, too: the absence of hope. ØLook at your parents, Draco,Æ Bellatrix spat. She always hated to be ignored. ØLook! You will see what your filthy blood traitor loyalties have done to one of the oldest Wizarding families in the world. And then you will give me the Cup of the Covenant.Æ She dug her wand deeper into Hermioneís throat. ØDo it, unless you want me to spill all of her filthy blood!Æ Draco forced himself to turn. He saw, near the bottom of the steps, his parentsí shocked expressions. ØIs it true?Æ said his mother faintly. The words barely reached him. He lowered his head in a single nod. Narcissa just swayed, as if the breeze might knock her aside. But Lucius came to life. He forged forward. ØNo,Æ he called out, using the rail to haul himself up the steps through the shifting gold. ØNo, Bellatrix, he is confused! The Order have warped his mind. He doesnít know what heís saying!Æ ØI know exactly what Iím saying, Father.Æ Dracoís voice was hard and clear. ØStop this. You must see reason, Draco.Æ Lucius reached him and seized him by the shoulders. In the glow of the late afternoon sun, the lines upon his face had disappeared. He looked almost young, more like Draco than he had in years. ØDonít you see?Æ he breathed, agitated. ØYouíve done it! Potter is here. Youíve helped bring him here. All thisÆîhe swept out an arm, gesturing to the mountain of goldîØwill be the least of our spoils! When we give Potter to the Dark Lord, we will be elevated beyond all you can imaîÆ Draco raised his voice. ØI said no.Æ Lucius let go of Draco, incomprehension written across his expression. Father and son looked into each otherís faces, and Draco saw Lucius grasping, in that instant, for a shred of comprehension. Then Bellatrixís voice rang down from the top of the steps. ØPathetic,Æ she said coldly. ØThe Dark Lord was always right about you, Lucius. You are weak. Your son could never have been anything but weak. Your line deserves to end in disgrace.Æ Luciusís face contracted. The hesitancy disappeared, his eyes burning harder into Dracoís. ØYou donít see whatís best,Æ he murmured. ØOf course, youíre only a boy. How could I expect you to make these judgments?Æ His voice rose into a fanatical snarl. ØI will tell the Dark Lord what you did to aid us. Everything will be the way it was, yes, youíll see, and you will thank me!Æ Before Draco could answer, his father lunged for Harry. With one hand, Lucius raised his wand; with the other, he reached toward Hufflepuffís cup. In that instant, two deafening CRACK sounds broke through the scene. A pair of small figures appeared. Dobby, with a stricken look up the steps at Hermione, seized Harryís and Ronís hands. Winky seized Dracoís. It all seemed to happen in slow-motion. Dobby yanked Harry backward with disproportionate strength. Luciusís hand stretched out, groping, seeking, and clamped not upon Hufflepuffís cup but upon the haft of Gryffindorís sword. The sword tore free of Harryís grip, and Lucius let out a howl of frustration. But he had dived too far. He staggered upon the spill of Galleons, arms backwatering, mouth wide open, trying to regain his balance. Lucius fell backward. The sword in his hand seesawed, glittering, beautiful beneath the sunlight. And as he struck the spread of gold, the pristine sword-tip, imbued with all the deadly venom of the Basilisk, traced a bright red gash onto his neck. Dracoís mouth was wide open, but no sound came free. He felt a great pressure upon his wrist as Winky used the same elf-spell as Dobby, the escape that they had planned; he understood now that the elves had waited to implement it until the last second, hoping that Hermione might escape Bellatrixís clutches. But they could wait no longer. Draco and Harry and Ron flew backward through the air, away from the scene, faster than the gathered wizards could raise or aim their wands, the Horcrux gleaming in Ronís hand. In that final instant, Draco could do nothing but watch. He watched his mother let out an unending scream, sinking to her knees at the bottom of the steps. He watched Hermione struggle in Bellatrixís grip, her face upturned toward them and shrinking as they left her behind. He watched his father go still for the last time upon a sea of Gringotts gold, sprawled upon the final monument to all he had accrued. Chapter End Notes that's capitalism baby! tumblr | instagram Chapter29:The RevelsofHelgaHufflepuff Chapter Notes Hello all! A small note: for timeline reasons I have edited the events of the previous scene to take place in mid/late afternoon instead of around noon. In practice this consisted of changing about six words. Onward! See the end of the chapter for more notes They landed hard in the front garden of the Potter Cottage. Draco breathed shallowly through parted lips. The air smelled like pollen, felt thick and unbreathable. The only sound was the ripple and flap of the tents around them. No one spoke, although the others were all facing his way. He couldnít meet their eyes. What was there to say? They all knew the truth. There would be no antidote for his father, miraculously procured, to save him from the venom of the sword. Lucius was already losing consciousness when theyíd escaped. By now, he was dead. Draco knew this, and yet it couldnít be true. His father, the cornerstone on which heíd built his identity for so long, couldnít have crumbled away from the world within a number of confused seconds. He couldnít be gone. He found himself looking to Dobby the house-elf first, who was gazing up at him with those tennis ball-sized eyes. ØAh, sir,Æ Dobby said, looking away. Draco heard a hint of sympathy, but saw no sign of regret in the elfís face. Dobbyís words seemed to puncture the shock. Ron swallowed and gave his head a little shake. ØWhat are we going to do?Æ he said in a hoarse whisper. ØHermione èÆ But their arrival had attracted attention. The door of headquarters burst open, and a terrified-looking Mr. and Mrs. Weasley ran into the garden, followed by a dozen members of the Order. ØWhat on earth happened?Æ Mrs. Weasley demanded. ØRonald Weasleyîwhere did you go? What were you doing? You should have been back twenty minutes ago! You said you were going somewhere safe, you saidîÆ She broke off mid-tirade, scanning their group. Everyone gathered seemed to come to the question at the same time. ØWhereís Hermione?Æ said Bill in a quiet voice. ØBellatrix has her.Æ The words came from Dracoís mouth, but his voice sounded like a strangerís. Mrs. Weasleyís hands clamped over her mouth, but she was one of the few who moved. Most of the Order froze in place as though Petrified. Remus was the first to recover. ØBellatrix will not kill Hermione,Æ he said, grave-faced but steady-voiced. ØWe know that much. She will be an immensely valuable prisoner.Æ ØHow did it happen, Potter?Æ said Kingsley. ØWhy were you near Lestrange?Æ Harry explained the broad strokes, omitting details of the Horcrux. The news that theyíd gone to Gringotts caused an immediate babble of questions, but none of it meant anything to Draco. He stared unseeing into the veins of ivy upon the walls of the Potter Cottage. His fatherís body was probably still lying there on the steps. Who would claim it? And even now, Bellatrix might be whisking Hermione or his mother back to the Lestrange House to torture them. His mind had no room for anything else, not even the Horcrux or how they would destroy it. Remus was right, though: Bellatrix would keep Hermione alive. She would want to trade Hermione for the cup, and now that they had stolen it from under her nose, Bellatrix wouldnít dare to summon Voldemort. Another shred of hope that Draco clung to like a drowning man to a scrap of driftwood. Still, none of this would stop Bellatrix from hurting Hermione in ways that only his aunt could devise. Nausea churned in Dracoís stomach. He wanted to run, to yell, to shatter something. He fixed the Lestrange House in his mind. Yesîthey had to storm the place. They had to rally the hundreds of people from the safehouses to Bellatrixís doors. It would invite open warfare upon her grounds, but Draco didnít care. He was ready to burn down all their plans for this one chanceîto save the only two people left in the world that he loved. He was opening his mouth to demand that the Order act when Harry staggered and clasped his palm to his scar. In the next instant, Harryís knees had given out. Draco and Ron caught him, but Harry was twitching, mouth wide open. ØWhatís happening to him?Æ said Fred, striding forward as they lowered him to the grass. The rest of the Order crowded around, Ginny kneeling at Harryís side and taking him hard by the hand. It was a full minute before Harryís eyes came open. ØWhat is it?Æ Draco demanded. ØWhat did you see?Æ ØHeís back,Æ Harry gasped out. He struggled to sit up. ØVoldemort. Heís back in the country, heís just heard about Gringotts. è He heard that Bellatrix was there. Heís worried that èÆ He shot a look to Ron and Draco. ØHeís worried.Æ ØWhatís his plan?Æ Ron said. ØHeís going there,Æ Harry panted. ØTo Gringotts. And once he finds out that weíve gotîheíll go toîÆ He glanced around at the Order and made a frustrated sound. ØTo where the others were,Æ Draco supplied, ignoring the Orderís confused murmurs. ØExactly.Æ Harry swallowed. ØThen Hogwarts.Æ ØHogwarts?Æ said Luna, her voice much less dreamy than usual. ØWhy would he go to the school?Æ Dracoís hand tightened on the Elder Wand. ØThereís something he wants on the grounds.Æ ØYou four,Æ said Ginny quietly. ØYou havenít been working on Occlumency at all. This is what youíve been doing. It has to do with what Voldemort wants, what heís been up to this past year.Æ The look that Harry gave her was apology and affection at once. ØYeah.Æ ØPlotting,Æ Pansy sighed, picking at a fingernail. ØWhy didnít you bloody tell us?Æ George said with an accusatory stare. ØYeah!Æ said Fred. ØYou mean we couldíve been helping you four stop You-Know-Who from getting someîsome secret weapon, or something?Æ ØIt was too much of a risk,Æ said Harry impatiently, pushing himself to his feet. ØAnd Dumbledore told us we mustnítîÆ ØAlbus put this on your backs?Æ said a rough voice from the fringe of the group. Aberforth stood between Dobby and Winky, his brilliantly blue eyes boring into Harry. ØAnd even now that your friendís neck is on the line, youíre still backing up his saintly memory?Æ Harryís fists balled up. ØLook, I havenít got time to argue about your brother. We have to get to Hogwarts right now. Weíve got to beat Voldemort there, because thereís something we need, too.Æ His eyes strayed to the cup-shaped lump in Ronís pocket. Draco knew he was thinking of the Chamber of Secrets. Dracoís heartbeat began to thud in his ears. ØNo,Æ he said loudly. ØWhat about Hermione? Weíre supposed to leave her with Bellatrix, are we?Æ He took a step back from Harry and knocked into someone. He forced his way out of the grouped Order, feeling claustrophobic, needing space to breathe. ØYou can all do what you want. Iím going to the Lestrange House, andîÆ ØDraco,Æ said Harry sharply. ØBellatrix is going to bring Hermione and your mum right to us.Æ Draco stilled. ØWhy?Æ ØBecause weíre going to stand and fight at Hogwarts.Æ Scanning the gathered Order, Harry raised his voice. ØWe need to start sending word. Everyone whoís on our side has to get to the castle. Itís now or never. We know thatís where Voldemortís headed, and if we donít stop him now, his next move is to come out of the shadows and take over as Minister for Magic. This is our chance.Æ Harry looked back to Draco. ØOnce Voldemort realizes Iím waiting for him in Hogwarts, heíll call the Death Eaters there. Bellatrix will be at the top of the list, and sheíll have them with her.Æ Draco tried to wring sense out of the words. He tried to force logic into his thoughts, tried to feel anything besides fear and panic and the need to act. There was some sense in the plan. Draco knew every inch of the Hogwarts grounds and its security measures, whereas the Lestrange House and its protections were unknowns. It might be easier to stage an ambush upon Bellatrixís arrival at the castle, if they could only control where she came onto the grounds, or where she planned to keep Hermione è The final piece slid into place: the mirror. The whole plan unfolded before him. They would get to Hogwarts and take control of the castle. They would destroy the Horcrux, then Transfigure a copy. They would use the mirror to stage a trade with Bellatrixîthe cup for Hermione and his mother. Draco, finally, nodded. But Aberforth hadnít had his last say. ØSo, your plan is to go to that castle and use yourself as bait, Potter?Æ His face was reddening. ØYouíll take all your friends, all these kidsÆîhe swept his hand toward Draco and Ron, toward Ginny and Luna and PansyîØout onto the front lines and watch them die?Æ He rounded on the older members of the Order. ØMaybe the boy doesnít know better, but you saw what happened last time. You know how many made it out alive.Æ ØI can speak for myself, Aberforth,Æ said Harry, his voice hard. ØAnd Iím not going to sit here in headquarters and let everyone else risk their necks for me. I can decide what kind of world I want to live inîor die for. I made that choice years ago.Æ Aberforthís eyes, so remarkably like Dumbledoreís, bored into Harryís. At last he lifted his hand to his forehead and smoothed the wrinkles there with his fingertips. ØAll right, then. You follow my brotherís commands from the afterlife. But when you follow Albus, you follow on faith.Æ Aberforth looked from Harry to McGonagall, then to Remus, then to Hagrid. He shook his head, tugging at his long, scraggly beard. ØSecrets and lies and master plans,Æ he muttered. ØI know where it gets you.Æ ØSo, youíre staying here, then?Æ Ginny said coldly. Aberforth looked weary. He stumped toward the cottage, his immobile arm swinging heavily at his side. ØIíll come to Hogwarts, girl,Æ he muttered. ØItíll be the end of all this, one way or another.Æ The door to headquarters closed heavily behind him, leaving a pregnant pause. Harry stood stiffly, looking after Aberforth. Draco noticed that his hand had fastened around the mokeskin pouch where he kept the Snitch. Eventually Fleur cleared her throat. ØWe must act quickly. Thaís still me, mind,Æ he added with a proud pat to the front pocket of his overcoat, which jangled. Professor McGonagall spoke with a shrewd air. But Harry did not gasp and jerk this time, returning to himself. At last Voldemort knew they were hunting the fragments of his precious soul. And soon he would be here, seeking the wand that Draco held tightly in his palm. Draco looked out from the tree line, past Hagridís hut, across the lake. There stood Hogwarts with its turrets and towers, its ramparts laying geometric shapes across the rolling lawns. Draco could almost see himself strutting up the lawn at his fatherís side, six years old. He had visited Hogwarts often as a child; his fatherís governorship had given him free rein of Hogwarts. Lucius had introduced him to all of it: to the magnificent gates, to the Slytherin Common Room, to the delicious meals in the Great Hall. Draco couldnít stop turning over the hesitation in his fatherís face before Bellatrix had started goading him. For one instant, his father had known the truth and tried to understand. Dracoís throat tightened until he could barely draw breath. He ran his fingertips over the mirror in his pocket. Would he have met with the same shred of understanding if he had simply told his parents the truth months ago? They had been at headquarters together. They had been safe. Could he have used those months to explain, to wear them down, instead of deciding to use them as props for their plans with Bellatrix? He closed his eyes, shutting out the sight of Hogwarts, shutting everything out but the present. He squeezed the handle of the mirror so tightly that he felt its brass filigree making impressions in his skin. He could do nothing for his father. All he could do was focus on Hermione and his mother. Not long after theyíd left headquarters, Bellatrix had tried to contact them through the mirror, speaking Dracoís name, then Harryís, and even Ronís, sounding livid. It had taken every ounce of Dracoís self-control not to answerîbut they needed control of Hogwarts before they told Bellatrix to meet them here, or she would inform the Death Eaters and the Ministry that the castle was under attack. Draco rose and began to pace. He would not picture what might be happening to them. He couldnít. As the afternoon sank into evening and their allies filled the forest, not just from the safehouses but from pockets of resistance across the country, Harry was taken by vision after vision. ØTheyíre looking for you three.Æ Sheíd barely spoken the words when Kingsley, McGonagall, Remus, and Tonks materialized out of the forestís gloom. ØIn his last year, he showed us this really top-notch room in the castle that does whatever what you need it to do.Æ ØSo,Æ George said, Øwe ran a few experiments. Just inside stood Dobby, Winky, and Neville Longbottom. # Sweat burned Hermioneís eyes as she staggered to a stop in front of the Hogwarts front gates. ØImperio.Æ # Longbottom let out a roar that the Gryffindors returned. They piled into the hall and into a hug that nearly knocked Longbottom off his feet. For minutes more, Narcissa continued to study her: her hair, mussed from where it had been rubbed upon the floor; her sweaty face; her robes, pulled askew. Then Narcissa rose to her feet. When she walked, she moved like water over a flat surface, impossibly smooth. One thought surfaced in Hermioneís mind. It circulated over and over: She lied to Bellatrix. It could mean any number of things. It could mean shame or self-preservation. It could mean hatred of Bellatrix or fear of the Dark Lord. But it could also mean that she had more to shield than anyone knew. Narcissa stopped feet away. ØI know he is here,Æ she whispered, Øin the castle. If Draco went with Potter to Diagon Alley, he will have come here, too. I believe you know where they are.Æ Thud. Thud. Each beat of Hermioneís heart was painful. Long fingers wrapped around her elbow, and Narcissa stood, taking Hermione with her. Cramps laddered through Hermioneís legs, but she stabilised herself against the wall and rose to her full height. ØYou care for my son.Æ Narcissaís face was rigid. ØI will stand by him to the end. Any moment he would burst through the door of the Headmasterís office, Bellatrix or Snape in tow. She lurched to the mantel, but there was no Floo Powder there, no chance for escape. Hermione turned toward the door and braced herself. But Pettigrew did not emerge. And if he was not within range to perform the counter-charm, there was only one reason the spell would have broken. ØHe is dead, then,Æ Narcissa said. Fear made her voice hoarse. Hermione bit her lip. What was happening in the castle that Pettigrew had been killed? Had a fight broken out? None of the students would know that Draco was working with the Order. What if heíd been separated from Harry and Ron and discovered, the Dark Mark still burned upon his arm? Worse, what if Pettigrew had led Snape and Bellatrix to the Chamber of Secrets? A terrible image burst into Hermioneís mind: Draco and Ron lying dead upon the floor beside the skeleton of a Basilisk, Harry dragged away to Voldemort. Hermione hurried to the heavy oaken door to examine it. A quick check confirmed her suspicions: there was no manual lock. It required magic to open. ØWe need some form of key.Æ They spent long minutes ransacking Snapeís desk and bookshelves but found nothing. ØThe evacuationís started,Æ said Draco. ØThen theyíll already be forming groups to fight,Æ said Harry. ØCome on, hurry.Æ Harry slipped through the tapestry, but Draco hesitated, his hand on the soft fringe of ancient cloth. After his year in hiding, the idea of walking out into Hogwarts as himself, not under the Cloak or Disillusioned or hidden by Polyjuice Potion, felt bizarre. Goyle, Blaise, Theo, and Millicent might be out there right now, filing up to evacuate. Certainly his friends would soon learn that he was alive. So would the younger Slytherins whoíd used to laugh at his jokes in the Slytherin Common Room, whoíd cheered him on the Quidditch pitch. So would every single Gryffindorîand a fair number of Ravenclaws and Hufflepuffsîwhoíd loathed the boy heíd used to be. And? whispered a small voice in Dracoís mind. Lingering in the shadow of the tapestry, he found his muscles relaxing, his posture straightening. What did he have to prove to any of those people? Draco felt something unexpected, something wonderful spreading throughout him. His old pride was returning. It smoothed his defensive edges. It made him lift his chin, feel the cool confidence of his childhood stirring in him like water in a spring melt. He and Hermione had spoken about this all those months ago, the night heíd snatched the diadem from her head. He could never be made completely anew. He could never leave all of himself behind. And what was more, Draco realised, he no longer wanted to. He had made his apologies. He owed nothing more. He felt the last of the guilt and regret falling away from him, shed like snakeskin. Draco stepped out from the dim passageway into the bright light of the Grand Staircase. Standing beside Harry and Hermione, he saw the students on the moving stairwells stopping to point at the three of them, talk rising higher and higher. Draco glanced to Hermione and slipped his hand into hers. All these people saw where he stood. They might as well know the reason. A small smile appeared on her lips. Then, together, they strode down the steps toward the Great Hall. # They collided with most of the Order in the Entrance Hall. At the sight of Hermione, there were yells of relief. Half the Weasleys pelted toward her, and Draco darted back as she staggered under the weight of their hugs. Draco scanned the Entrance Hall, hoping to see his mother, but he found no sign of Narcissa. Nor was she in the Great Hall, whose four long tables stood deserted. ØLooking for your mum?Æ said Tonks, sidling up to him. He nodded. ØWas she here?Æ ØYeah. Not for long, though. She asked me where you were, and when I told her Iíd no idea, she ran off upstairs looking for you again.Æ Draco nodded, fixing his eyes up the stairs. The castle was, for now, safe. At least she hadnít gone out onto the grounds. ØYou lot.Æ Kingsleyís voice rang through the Order, who finally broke away from Hermione. She was flushed, her hair frizzing from the friction of so many embraces. ØNewcomers,Æ said Kingsley, Øweíve divided our forces into four groups. One is holding the towers and battlements, aerial defence. Others are helping the house-elves prepare supplies, anything we can use inside the castle as weaponry. A third group have gone onto the grounds to set up an external guard, and a fourth have gone with Fred and George to close any passageways into the castle.Æ ØThe four of us,Æ said Ron to Harry, Draco, and Hermione, Øare supposed to go up to the Astronomy Tower. Remus has taken a group that way already. Come on.Æ They left the rest of the Order to divide themselves and dashed up through the halls. As Harry filled Ron in on the state of the Horcruxes, Draco took stock of everything they passed. The portraits were full of clamouring painted people rushing from one end of Hogwarts to another, delivering messages. Ghosts were swirling, speaking urgently about what they might do to assist the effort, and all around them, suits of armour sprang to life and marched down the halls, clanking out to defend their castle. As they reached the upper floors, they nearly collided with an immense cauldron full of bubbling water. ØHarry Potter, sir!Æ squeaked a gleeful voice from behind it, and Dobby poked his head out. The hallway was packed with Hogwarts house-elves, who had emptied the kitchens and supply closets of their contents. Vats of scouring and stripping potions were levitated above the elvesí heads, cauldron after cauldron of boiling water hurried out onto the ramparts. They climbed up into the Astronomy Tower. The air rang with footsteps and grim, fearful voices above; Draco felt an electric tension all around. Finally, they emerged onto the top of the tower. The night was cool, and a chill wind scythed through Dracoís robes. Dozens of figures were stationed around the Astronomy Towerís circumference. Draco couldnít help looking back at the spot heíd cornered Dumbledore last year, the spot his Transfigured body had lain. Then the robed figures were turning to see the new arrivals. Roughly half were students. A great shout rose from a contingent of GryffindorsîSeamus Finnigan, Parvati Patil, Lavender Brownîand Luna gave a cheerful wave from where she stood among Ravenclaws and Hufflepuffs. But Dracoís eyes had fixed near the towerís eastmost rampart, where Pansy stood. Beside her stood Gregory Goyle. Draco stopped mid-step. Heíd assumed that his friends would evacuate at once. Yet there Goyle stood. From the lack of surprise on his face, Draco knew that Pansy must have told him everything already. Draco glanced over at Hermione, who looked as startled as he felt. But she made a little motion, urging him toward the other Slytherins. Draco steeled himself and approached his friends. ØGoyle,Æ he said, extending a hand. After a moment, Goyle smiled and shook. He had grown even taller and broader, looming half a head over Draco. ØWhat are you doing here?Æ Draco said quietly as he let go. ØWhat if your fatherís out there?Æ Goyle shook his head. ØHeís still in Mungoís. And I think heíd be happy if he got out and it was over. Me and Pansy, we were done with this by end of first term.Æ ØYeah?Æ Draco hesitated. ØI saw Crabbe earlier.Æ Goyle let out a grunt and faced the battlement again. ØHeís off his head, after what happened to his dad at Azkaban. Wonít talk to anyone except Millicent anymore. He called Blaise and Theo blood traitors. Just because Blaise wouldnít tell his mum to take the Mark and Theo wouldnít curse a second-year in detention.Æ ØTheo and Blaise have evacuated, I suppose?Æ ØTheyíre in the Room of Requirement,Æ said Pansy, who was leaning against the rampart, wand held so tightly that her knuckles glowed in the starlight. ØBlaise said he and his mum have been thinking about leaving the country. Apparently theyíve got houses in the States and Nigeria and Greece that nobody else knows about. Paid for in cash and Unplottable. So, if all thisÆîshe waved out at the Hogwarts groundsîØcollapses, heís said we can all go into hiding together. Howís that for an offer?Æ Draco couldnít help a smile, drawing the Elder Wand from his pocket. ØThree secret houses ready for evacuation. Iíve never heard anything less surprising about the Zabini family.Æ Goyle and Pansy both laughed, and Draco settled against the rampart beside his friends. Their smiles couldnít last long. The sea of wandlight beyond Flitwickís enchantments had grown to an ocean, encircling the castle. Draco glanced over to see Hermione, Harry, and Ron filling in the gap to his left. As the ring of students stood facing outward, their houses were indiscernible among the dark ranks. The last of the murmurs faded. Something was happening near the Hogwarts gates. The glow was intensifying, spreading, turning to a deep purple. Then an almighty crack rang out as if from a thunderhead. Flitwickís enchantments had broken open. The enemy spilled forward. ØReady, everyone?Æ called Remus. ØAim your jinxes and hexes high! AndîNOW!Æ Dozens of wands aimed up into the night, and a firework of spells erupted from the top of the tower. All around the castle Draco could see the same happening, cued by their attack. Flashes exploded down the ramparts like hundreds of cameras going off in sequence, and the noise of shuddering blasts echoed up from their sites of contact in the grounds. At first Draco thought that the deep booms were all from spellwork. Then he saw the towering figures looming at the edge of the grounds. The hair on the back of his neck rose. Giants, a dozen or more, twenty-five feet tall, were moving slowly past the lake. ØWinky,Æ said Tonks from somewhere behind Draco, Øtell Kingsley weíve got eyes on fourteen giants. Theyíll each need a dedicated team.Æ ØRight away, miss,Æ said Winky, and with a crack the elf had gone. ØBrooms!Æ yelled someoneîDean Thomas, Draco thoughtîand every head craned upward. Flyers were pouring out of the moon like black rain, spells cascading down. A flurry of Shield Charms rose up, including two hazy barriers conjured by a pair of grizzled-looking witches Draco didnít recognise. Most of the attacks struck the shields and ricocheted backward, scattering the flyers, but several spells penetrated, causing everyone on the tower to dive behind the rampart for cover. ØCounter-attacks,Æ came Tonksís voice, Ønow!Æ They sprang to their feet and volleyed back, taking aim into the night. Dracoís spells issued out of the Elder Wand, as powerful as any spells heíd ever duelled with, but aiming was nearly impossible. Draco couldnít be sure what kinds of brooms the attackers were flying, but from the hairpin turns they were taking, these were no family brooms generations old. They had been hand-selected for aerial assault. Another wave of attacks. As they ducked behind the ramparts, Draco seized Hermione at the elbow. ØHermione! Potter, Weasley! We need to get back inside the castle.Æ ØWhat?Æ said Ron, looking outraged. ØButîÆ Draco held up the Elder Wand. ØWe need to keep control of the wand, if Potterís going to use it against Voldemort. We canít risk losing ownership of it in a fly-by attack.Æ ØAnd we need to target Nagini,Æ said Harry vaguely. ØI can see him moving.Æ He was staring into the distance, the glow of spells crisscrossing the lenses of his glasses. His eyes slipped shut for only a moment before he returned to himself. ØHeís crossing the grounds. He and Naginiîtheyíre not following the rest of the troops.Æ Scanning the grounds, trying to think of how they would get through the melee, an idea struck Draco. ØPotter. The Room of Requirement. When you see where heís settled, we can open up a passage just near the snake èÆ ØThen we pull the snake through!Æ Ron said. ØOr just reach out with the fang andîÆ He slashed out with his wand, sending a Stunning spell into the night. They had begun to dart for the exit when Remus yelled, ØStudents, fall back into the castle and cast through windows instead! And you fourîÆ He ran toward them, deflecting a spell with an aerobic swipe as though wielding a Bludgerís bat. ØI need you to get a message to Kingsley, and not through the portraits. It could get lost. With this many flyers, it looks like theyíre trying to make their main breach through the aerial entrances. The dozen older fighters closed in around the entrance, Transfiguring a blockade. Draco darted one look back as a piece of wood thudded into place, shutting out the stars. # Hermione glanced through every passing window as they ran. One of the structures in the lower courtyards had caught fire, black smoke billowing like hundreds of feet of cloth. ØDo we know where Kingsleyís stationed?Æ she panted. ØYeah,Æ said Ron. ØHe set up a team of ex-Aurors in the Entrance Hall. Theyíve been barricading the windows on the ground floor.Æ Overall, the state of the halls reassured Hermione. In contrast to the chaos outside, the castleís interior was still tightly ordered. Each window had one or more fighters firing spells out onto the encroaching forces of Death Eaters and the Ministry. Tightly clustered groups could be seen charging toward areas sustaining heavier attacks. While they dashed back to the Grand Staircase, Hermione ran through the plan to entrap Nagini in her mind. It could work, if they could catch Voldemort unawares. But the risk of accidentally allowing Voldemort access to the Room of Requirement was a terrifying one. Could they organise a diversion? Distract Voldemort for a crucial moment while opening a passageway to steal Nagini from behind? She supposed it all depended on where Voldemort and the snake ended up è They pelted down the first-floor corridor, passing Fred and George, who had gathered a group around the statue of Gregory the Smarmy. Sounds of an explosion rang through the open windows, and everyone in the halls flinched for cover before returning to their posts. Hermione wheeled around the corner with the others. They emerged at the top of the sweeping stairs to the Entrance Hall. Kingsleyís imposing figure was stationed at the front door, flanked by a score of other hardened-looking witches and wizards. Behind them stood two dozen house-elves, hands outstretched, ready to perform wandless incantations. Immense bangs and thuds rang upon the door as the Death Eatersí forces tried to break through. They pelted down the steps. ØKingsley!Æ Harry yelled when they had reached the bottom. But when the Aurors and elves turned away from the door, they did not look at Harry, Hermione, Draco, or Ron. Their horrified gazes fixed on the ground floor passageways flanking the stairs. Hermione whirled around with the others just in time to dive for safety. Scores of Death Eaters in robes and masks were pouring out of the ground floor corridors, sending spells toward the Auror guard at the front door. Hermione, throwing herself behind the marble banister, felt an exploding fragment of stone sear across the back of her wrist, leaving a bloody line in its wake. ØBreach!Æ Kingsley roared. A second contingent of Aurors and elves poured out of the Great Hall, and as spells began to volley in earnest, Hermione realised that she had been separated from Harry, Ron, and Draco, who had dived forward for cover into the Great Hall, while she had flung herself backward. She shot a wild look up the broad stairway. It was still empty, and they needed reinforcements. She flung herself up the steps, sprinting pell-mell out of the fray. Only when sheíd safely reached the landing and cover of a statue did she spare a glance back, thinking, How? How had the Death Eaters gotten inside? But the answer came to her as she saw a Death Eater's robes dragging across the stones, leaving a glistening trail of water behind. The attackers had come in through the lake and the Slytherin Common Room. It was the very way that they had escaped Hogwarts last summer. Hermione gritted her teeth and shoved through the massive doors at the end of the landing, back into the first floor corridor. She lifted her wand to her throat, amplifying her voice, and yelled, ØEverybody!Æ All down the hall, the fighters at the windows looked up. ØThereís a breach in the dungeons. Theyíve come into the Entrance Hall!Æ Hermione stabbed her finger toward the landing. ØWe have to close off the dungeons and hold the front doors!Æ The speed of the reaction was startling. Every single person in sight sprinted toward her. Hermione flattened herself against the walls as scores of fighters tore by, wands outstretched. Professor McGonagall burst out of the nearby Transfiguration classroom, a dozen fully-grown lions springing out into the hall at her wandtip. Hermione could tell they had once been desks; the talons of the last were made from graffitied oak. ØComing, Hermione?Æ Fred said with a hungry grin as he passed, the light of battle in his eyes. ØI canít promise Iíll leave any Death Eaters for you.Æ ØAll yours,Æ Hermione panted. ØI need to get more reinforcements.
