Take the hall near Filchís office instead.Æ Hermione nodded her thanks and sprinted through the castle, sending group after group toward the Entrance Hall or up toward the towers as Remus had asked. Beyond the windows, the sight grew grimmer and grimmer. The top of Ravenclaw Tower was surrounded by a malevolent yellow glow. One of the giants had reached the south face of the castle and was hurling boulders into the wall, each landing with a cataclysmic boom. Triple the guard, triple the guard, she thought frantically. Had she sent enough people up to the towers? She had to get back to Draco, Harry, and Ron. Getting to the Room of Requirement would be no use without Harryís knowledge of where Voldemort might be, and she had no idea if theyíd seen her leave the Entrance Hall. They might still be looking for her there. She turned on her heel and ran. But halfway there, she stopped dead. A deep, splintering crash had come from ahead. Shouting had turned into screams. Hermione knew instinctively that the front doors had fallen. Terror flooded her. They had to fall back, get out of the Entrance Hall. All the Death Eatersí forces would be pouring through that single route. Hermione wheeled around a bend. Up ahead was the landing to the Entrance Hallîbut the door leading out to it was already barricaded. She searched the cluster of Order fighters and found them. Harry, Ron, and Draco in the middle of a heated debate with Fred and George. Øîmight still be down there!Æ Draco was snapping as Hermione sprinted toward them. ØSheís not,Æ said Fred. ØHow do you know?Æ ØBecause sheís right there,Æ said George, pointing to Hermione. Draco, Harry, and Ron spun and saw her, relief breaking over their expressions. ØTheyíve broken through?Æ she gasped as she skidded to a stop. ØYeah,Æ said Harry. ØKingsley and the others are barricading the other side of the landing.Æ A great boom shook their barricade. Shards of wood went flying. Half a dozen voices yelled, ØReparo!Æ and the hole sealed over, but Hermione could hear spell after spell tearing into the door on the other side, chipping away enchantments as quickly as the Order could erect them. ØWe need to regroup somewhere,Æ Ron panted. ØThereís too many of them.Æ Fred and George traded a nod. ØPassage behind the tapestry of Urg the Unclean,Æ said George. ØItíll take us out on the sixth floor,Æ said Fred. ØPut some distance between us and them. All right?Æ ØPerfect,Æ said Ron. Fred nodded and gave Ron a clap on the shoulder. ØOi,Æ Fred yelled, turning to the Order. ØYou lot!Æ The Order members shoring up the barricade turned. ØWeíre regrouping on the sixth floor,Æ Fred went on. ØFollow my brother Ron, heíllîÆ The barricade split down the middle in a blast of blinding light. Hermione squeezed her eyes shut and felt herself become weightless, rising from the reverberating ground as a deafening roar filled the air. She clutched to her wand with one hand, the other moving toward the back of her neck, her body folding instinctively into a protective position, and she waited to feel heat and agony, waited for the explosion to devour her. Then the sound ended, and she forced her eyes open, knowing there was no time to waste. She dragged herself out from a pile of rubble, hot liquid trickling down the side of her face. ØReparo!Æ she was croaking, her ears ringing; she was trying to move in a straight line and staggering sideways. ØR-repîÆ Her voice failed. Nearer the landing, Harry and Draco and three red-headed men were grouped on the ground. It was as though a hand had seized her around the neck. ØNoînoîno!Æ someone was shouting. ØNo! Fred! No!Æ ØWeíve got to go,Æ Draco was insisting, Øget up, weíve got to run!Æ He and Harry had seized Ron and Georgeís arms, trying to yank them down the hall, and Hermione finally reached them, but for a moment she could do nothing, neither think nor speak nor move. Fred lay sprawled before them, his face limp and expressionless as it had never been in life. Another gut-shaking blast brought her to her senses. Behind them, the Order were conjuring planks and stones and magical shields, trying to cobble the barricade back together, even as the Death Eatersí spells thudded one after another into the obstruction. Hermione tore her eyes from Fred and heaved at Ronís arm, and finally he was overpowered, dragged back, senseless sounds tearing out of him. But George was struggling too forcefully. They had hardly made it five steps when he wrenched free, threw himself back to Fred, and huddled over his brotherís body. ØGo,Æ he yelled, twisting around and pointing down the hall. ØGO!Æ A splintering sound as the last of the barricade began to wrench apart. The rest of their allies were retreating now, pelting down the corridor, clutching their injuries. Hermione was dimly aware of dragging Ron backward with Harry and Draco, although Ron was red in the face, trying to fight, trying to get back to his brothers. ØFred!Æ he howled, his voice breaking in a way that Hermione felt deep in her chest. ØGeorge!Æ But George was beyond hearing. As the barricade exploded open before him, he shot spell after spell into the crush of Death Eaters, on his knees before Fredís body, one hand held over his brotherís chest as though to protect him from harm. The last thing Hermione saw before they pulled Ron around the corner was George transfixed by a jet of green light, rigid for a moment, then falling beside Fred, not to be parted from him. Chapter End Notes tumblr | instagram Chapter 31: TheCaptive in the Open Cell Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes Ron was still struggling when they dragged him into the narrow stairwell behind the tapestry. Draco knew that Ronís arms would bruise, so tightly were he and Harry clutching to keep him back from the threshold. ØLet me goîI want to fight themîÆ ØRon, listenîÆ Harry grunted. ØLET GO OF ME!Æ ØPlease, Ron, please!Æ Hermione seized the fist that clutched his wand. ØWe canít go back out there! We need to get to the snake, we need t-to kill Nagini!Æ ØI donít careîI want to do something, I want to kill Death EatersîÆ Shouts rang down the hall. Draco, Harry, and Hermione aimed their wands at the tapestry and began to brick up the entrance, but Ron continued to twist in their grasp. ØWeíll have to fight them to get to the Room of Requirement!Æ Hermione said. The tears in her eyes spilled over, and she wiped her face with her torn sleeve. ØWe will fight, but we canít lose sight of what weíre supposed to be d-doing!Æ Draco forced his agreement through numb lips, hardly aware of what he was saying. Waves of shock still hummed through him. He knew that if he lived a hundred and fifty years, he would never forget the sight of the Weasley twins lying in the ruin of the barricade. Now you know, said a small, blank voice in his mind. Now they knew exactly how outmatched they were. The entryway closed, Draco lowered the Elder Wand. He remembered thirsting for this weapon back in winter, really believing that if heíd had it, heíd have shown the Death Eaters who was in charge. But when it had come down to it, the wand hadnít transformed him into some legendary duellist. Heíd had to throw himself out of the way of curse after curse, not knowing whether Protego or Parasalvus had the power to block them. In those last chaotic moments, heíd been trying with the rest to fortify the barricade with elementary spells like Reparo and Duro. Draco felt anew what all four of them were: teenagers armed with the kind of defensive knowledge that teachers like Gilderoy Lockhart and Dolores Umbridge had imparted to them. They couldnít fight like soldiers, and so they would die like children. ØDo it, Harry,Æ Hermione was saying. ØLook inside him! Where is he?Æ Harryís eyes slid shut, and a minute later he gasped out, ØHeís in the Shrieking Shack. The snakeís with him, itís got some sort of magical protection around it.Æ ØWhat kind of protection?Æ Draco said. Harry wiped a trickle of blood from the side of his face. ØI donít know. An enchantment. Itís like a glowing cage.Æ Draco closed his eyes. Even their plan seemed ridiculously childish now. Opening a secret door in the Room of Requirement and pulling Nagini through? What had they been thinking? Of course she would be under the strongest protections Lord Voldemort could conjure. Nothing would distract the Dark Lord from his final Horcrux anymore. ØItís not good enough,Æ he said. ØWe canít just burst into that place. Heíll kill us all before we can figure out how to break the enchantment.Æ ØIt has to be good enough,Æ said Hermione with desperation. ØItís our only plan.Æ But just then, as Draco wiped a scrape on his cheek and his robes pulled up his arm, he caught sight of the Dark Mark on his left forearmîand an idea struck him hard. ØItís not our only plan,Æ he breathed. There was a secret way, a back door, something they hadnít considered. They didnít need to get anywhere near Voldemort. Nor did they need to stand on the front lines and watch their allies die. They only needed to cast one spell. He started up the stairs at a run. ØWe need to get to the Headís office.Æ ØWhat?Æ said Harry, hot on his heels with the others. ØButîthe snakeîÆ ØWe can get to the snake without risking our necks. And we can order the Death Eaters to do whatever we want.Æ ØHow?Æ said Hermione. ØBy putting Snape under an Imperius Curse.Æ They broke out of their run at the top of the passage. Draco addressed Ron now, looking into the blue eyes still full of rage and grief. ØYou wanted a strategic advantage, Weasley. Everyone knows that Snapeís the second-in-command. Weíll give orders through him and divert their forces somewhere, then order a concentrated ambush.Æ Draco wondered if any of the Gryffindors would protest the use of the Imperius. None did. In the light of battle, the lines had blurred around what was forgivable. ØOf course,Æ Hermione said quietly. ØThen weíll send Snape to Voldemort with a Basilisk fang.Æ Harry frowned. ØBut if we donít know how to break the protective enchantments on the snake, how can SnapeîÆ ØIt doesnít matter what we know,Æ said Draco. ØIf I tell someone under Imperius to break down a door, I donít need to give them an exact spell. Theyíll be forced to find a way. If we tell Snape to get through the enchantment, heíll have to try everything he knows.Æ ØAnd if anyone knows how,Æ said Hermione, ØIím sure itís Snape.Æ ØI thought you said the Imperius was really advanced, though,Æ said Harry. Draco held up the Elder Wand. ØI have this. Itíll work, Potter.Æ ØHow do you know?Æ ØBecause weíve got no other choice.Æ A momentís silence. Nobody protested again. Neither did anyone voice what they all knew: that to kill Nagini would almost certainly cost Snapeís life. ØAll right. We need to hurry,Æ said Harry. ØWe canít let them find Snape first.Æ They emerged through the exit on the sixth floor. Ranks of their allies were still stationed here, firing spells out through windows. It seemed unfathomable that these people had continued on with their defensive strategy as though nothing had happened. But nearby, a girl Draco recognised as Cho Chang stopped casting at the sight of them. ØHarry! Whatís going on? Whatís happened to you four?Æ ØTheyíve broken into the castle,Æ Harry panted. ØTell everyone you can to get ready. Theyíre on the lower floors for now, but they could be here any time.Æ Cho dashed off to relay the message. As word spread down the hall, Draco ran with the others in the opposite direction. The Headmasterís office was halfway across the castle, but if they could reach it before the tide of Death Eaters rose to meet them, Snape would be all the armour they needed. They sprinted past aerial responders kicking off on brooms through windows, past Professor Sprout and a dozen students carrying armfuls of writhing Venomous Tentacula, past Peeves bouncing along and cackling as he prepared a huge sack full of Dungbombs. Their luck ran out as they passed by the Charms classroom. Up ahead, screams and yells ignited, the sounds of open battle. ØWeíre almost there,Æ Harry panted. ØIf we can just get pastîÆ His eyes widened, fixing through the nearest window. He bellowed, ØGET OUT OF THE WAY!Æ Draco threw himself headlong down the hall, not a second too soon. An enormous boulder crashed through the castle wall. The ancient stones of Hogwarts shuddered, then began to crumble out from beneath Dracoís feet, too. He seized a portrait frame on the wall and hoisted himself out of the way as the floor gave out where he had just lain. Cold wind howled into the hallway now laid open to the elements. Draco looked back and saw Ron and Hermione on the other side of the gulf, separated from him and Harry. ØJump!Æ Harry called over the wind. ØItís too far,Æ Ron yelled back. Draco lifted his wand to conjure a bridge from one section of floor to another, but Hermione screamed, ØDraco! MOVE!Æ He looked up to see a hand the size of a table plunging toward him as the giant thrust his forearm into the castleís faade. Draco threw himself flat to dodge the gigantic fingers, bloodying his palms. From the floor, he took aim at the giantís eye and fired off a Conjunctivitis Curse from the Elder Wand. It shot onto the glazed surface of one gigantic eyeball. The giant howled with agony and clutched at his face, staggering back with footsteps that made yet more stones crumble away from the damaged floor. But as the giant pulled away, Draco saw something worse approaching. Acromantulas were swarming up the side of a nearby tower, a teeming mass of hairy legs and bodies, and out of the night, the Ministryís airborne forces were zooming toward the fresh breach in the castle wall. Ron had leapt back from the sight of the oncoming spiders. ØGo!Æ he yelled. ØGet to the Headís office. Weíll find another way around!Æ ØWear the Cloak!Æ Hermione shouted before they sprinted in opposite directions. As they ran, Harry did as Hermione said and pulled fistfuls of silvery material out of his mokeskin pouch. He threw the Cloak over them both. The sounds of battle that theyíd heard earlier grew louder. They turned a corner and saw clouds of dust and spell light. Screamed incantations shot out of the melee. Death Eaters were facing off against the Orderís allies, Amycus Carrow battling Mr. Weasley while a pair of sisters from Azkaban volleyed hexes at two masked figures stationed behind a suit of armour. Draco and Harry ran for it. The hallway was wide enough for them to dodge the duellists beneath the Cloak, and with so many jets of light flying, no one noticed when they deflected curses that came their way. At the end of the corridor, a huddle of students faced down a pair of Aurors, Padma Patil and Michael Corner standing guard over a heavily bleeding Colin Creevey. ØHeís sixteen,Æ snarled Padma, Øyou evilîÆ ØWands down! Put your wands down!Æ an Auror shrieked, blasting unrelenting Stunners toward the students. Harry let out a sound of fury, his steps lagging, but Draco dragged him forward by the arm. They mustnít be noticed, mustnít be followed. Then they were out of the fray. A minute later, they came to the stretch of hallway where the gargoyle guarded the entrance to the Headís office. The gargoyle had been shattered. It lay in pieces before the exposed doorway. They both stopped dead. Harry swore. ØDo you reckon Bellatrix came back here?Æ Draco studied the open entrance. ØCanít be sure,Æ he murmured. On one hand, Bellatrix wouldnít have needed to shatter the gargoyle, knowing the password, but then, when had his aunt ever stopped herself destroying whatever she wanted? Dracoís hand tightened on his wand. Part of him wanted her to be inside the office. He wanted to face Bellatrix down one on one, to get revenge for what sheíd done to Hermioneî even to get revenge for goading his father. Of course your son could never have been anything but weak è Bellatrix had loomed like a shadow over his family for too long. They slipped out from under the Cloak to raise their wands. As they stepped into the dark, silent doorway, Draco focused on the warmth of the Elder Wand. His aunt was one question, but Snape was another. Soon he would need to cast the Imperius Curse on a Severus Snape who might no longer be bound and defenceless. They reached the door, which hung ajar on its hinges. The room beyond was darkened. Ready? Harry mouthed. Draco nodded, lifting his wand. They had just burst through the door when a dazzling silver shape materialised in the darkness of the Headís office. Two shields erupted from the tips of Dracoís and Harryís wands. In one instant, Draco discerned the shape behind the translucent barriers. In the next, his shield flickered and died. It was the silver doe, standing beside the empty chair where they had tied Snape. The bindings lay slashed on the ground, a large, burly figure sprawled unconscious beside the chair: Vincent Crabbe, relieved of his wand. Shock and realisation mingled in Draco. He remembered the silvery mist that had shimmered in front of Snape as theyíd Stunned him in the Chamber of Secrets. Heíd been thinking of Pettigrewís hand, which had so recently killed its owner in such a gruesome way, but of courseîit had been the beginnings of a Patronus. This Patronus. The doe dissipated, and Severus Snape stepped out from the shadows of a bookcase. Neither Draco nor Harry lowered their wands. ØIt was you?Æ said Harry in an unsteady voice. ØYou left us Dumbledoreís bequests? You passed our message to McGonagall?Æ Draco remembered Snape helping Dumbledore down the hall in Grimmauld Place the previous summer. Heíd thought Snape had complicated motives for keeping Dumbledore alive, when in fact all his actions were bound by one perfectly simple truth. ØYou and Dumbledore planned this together,Æ said Draco. ØHis hand è that curse was going to kill him anyway. So, you both planned his death in a way that would be of some use.Æ ØYes,Æ said Snape. There was satisfaction in his sibilant voice, but when he looked at Draco, his mouth contracted as though he had tasted something sour. Draco stiffened defensively, not understanding. Snape had never looked at him that wayîso bitterly that he might have been looking at Harry. As for Harry, a series of difficult calculations seemed to be taking place inside him. He lowered his wand, and when he spoke, every word sounded like a struggle. ØThen help us.Æ Snape did not reply. ØIf youíre on our side, we need your help,Æ said Harry more urgently. ØThereís something Dumbledore told us to do. Weíve got to kill Voldemortís snake, but heís put it under a protection that we donít know how to break.Æ There was a flicker in Snapeís black eyes. Harry had seen it too. ØSo, Dumbledore told you about it?Æ he said at once. ØHow much did he explain?Æ ØVery little, Potter,Æ Snape replied. ØHe said only that there would come a time when Voldemort would keep Nagini near him. è He chose not to furnish me with any more detail.Æ ØThe snake is keeping Voldemort alive. Itís got to be killed, or he canít be killed.Æ Draco drew the box from his pocket; Harry took it and flicked the catch to reveal the array of Basilisk fangs. ØAnd it needs to be done with one of these,Æ Harry finished. Snape considered this information. Then his eyes travelled from the fangs onto Harry. His expression was somehow different than the malevolence that had characterised six yearsí interaction between the two. He seemed to be watching Harry through a thick barrier, very far away. Finally Snape murmured, ØDumbledore, too, was cavalier with my life.Æ ØIím not being cavalier withîÆ ØScarcely one minuteís knowledge of my loyalties, and you wish to return me to the Dark Lord. You ask me to place myself in mortal danger with precious little information in return; you see that to be my use. Yes, you are more like Dumbledore than even you dare to hope.Æ Harryís face grew mutinous. ØYeah, well, I notice youíre still here. Dumbledoreís not around to give you orders anymore. If youíre not interested in ending the war, then why not just leave?Æ Snape drew his wand so suddenly that both Harry and Draco flinched, but in an instant he had conjured a small crystal bottle and begun to draw silvery strands of thought from his temple. ØBecause, Potter,Æ said Snape, still with that strange remoteness, ØDumbledore told me that when this time came, and when the Dark Lord sought to protect Nagini, I must relay a message to you. That is why, as you so astutely acknowledge, I am still here.Æ ØA message? What message?Æ Snape made no answer except to continue extracting memories. When the bottle was full, he remained before the desk for a moment, regarding first its empty chair, next the snoozing portrait of Dumbledore himself on the wall. The portrait he studied for a long while. Then he strode toward Harry and Draco. ØThere,Æ he hissed. The remoteness had gone, and in its place was cold vehemence. He forced the bottle into Harryís hand. ØTake it, Potter. Dumbledoreís last message. If you are so eager to finish what he startedîyou may as well know why.Æ He raised his wand once more. A Basilisk fang rose from the box in Harryís hand, enfolded itself in a black velvet sleeve, and landed in Snapeís outstretched hand. He pocketed it, then went for the window, his black cloak billowing behind him. ØSir,Æ said Draco. ØSir, wait. The Death Eaters are taking over the school. You could stop them.Æ Snape paused before the window. ØThe Granger girl,Æ he said, voice jerky, without facing Draco. ØShe is alive?Æ Draco didnít understand. ØYes.Æ Snape looked back, thenînot at Draco but at Harry. Draco had never seen Snapeís face that way, contorted with pain. He stared into Harryís eyes for a long moment. Then he was thrusting Crabbeís wand outward and hoisting himself through the window. He dropped. He sailed out, silent as an owl, into the night. # ØMerlinís bloody buggeringîÆ Ron dove for cover, knocking Hermione backward. ØItís Death Eaters all the way down that hall, go back!Æ ØWhat are we going to do?Æ Hermione panted, spinning around. ØWeíll have to double back all the way to the Grand Staircase if we want to try another floor, and thatís sure to be swarming with them, too.Æ ØWeíve got to. Theyíll cut us off otherwise.Æ Hermione nodded. They dashed around the corner, sprinted corridor after corridor, and emerged into the Grand Staircase. The sounds of duelling made a deafening cacophony. The immense vertical space was filled with a rain of spells, ghosts twisting and twirling and divebombing, trying to nudge the bolts of light away from the Hogwarts defenders. Peeves shot Dungbombs every which way. ØOUT! OUT!Æ a wild-eyed Professor Trelawney shrieked, causing steaming pots of tea and crystal balls to plummet storeys downward toward her targets. ØThere!Æ Hermione pointed to a section of staircase. They leapt onto it, but they had hardly made it halfway to the next landing when Ron let out a horrified shout. On a nearby staircase, Fenrir Greyback was crouching over a fallen, twitching Lavender Brown. ØObdurata!Æ Hermione screamed, aiming her wand at Greyback. He was blasted backward, but he had hardly hit the wall when he was running anew, bolting up a set of steps toward Ron and Hermione now, his grizzled mouth smeared with blood. They both cast spells at Greyback, but he parried them, wand held in his fist like a hammerîand one of the spells rebounded into Ronís shoulder. Ron spun off-balance toward the edge of the staircase. Hermione let out a small scream and grappled Ron back from the edge, trying not to look down at six storeys of empty space below them. Her wand slipped from her sweaty fingers and clattered to the steps. ØNo!Æ Hermione cried, Ron still off-balance. She dove for her wand, but they had already reached the landing, and Greyback was bulling onto their section of staircase. He was larger than Hermione would have thought possible. The reek of blood washed over her. He tore up the steps toward her, and she wasnít going to reach her wand in timeîRonís hex went wild; Greyback was inches from herî ØNO, YOU DONíT!Æ yelled another voice from behind her. A jet of blue light struck Greyback square in the face. Poleaxed, he fell backward. Hermione watched, transfixed, as his limp body rolled from step to step. She watched as he toppled out into open space and plummeted seventy feet. She didnít hear him hit the ground. Screams from below were the only sign of his impact. Ronís mouth was wide open with shock as they secured their wands and scrambled onto the next landing. Hermione twisted around to see who had cast the killing blow and saw a grim-faced Tonks on a broom. Tonks didnít look triumphant. Her face was as hollow as a mask mounted on a wall. She flew down through the staircase, bobbing through obstacles, and landed at their side. Remus followed closely behind, looking less certain on a broom, stepping off on shaky ankles. ØWhat are you two still doing here?Æ Tonks yelled over the exclamations of hundreds of portraits. ØWeíve fallen back to the library!Æ ØWe need to get to the Headmasterís office!Æ Hermione yelled back as they ducked out of the Grand Staircase. ØWe keep getting detoured!Æ ØRight, well, weíre not letting you wander off by yourselves again,Æ said Tonks. ØRemusî Headís office?Æ Remus nodded. ØThereís a passage not many know about, over by the Arithmancy classroom. This way.Æ # Draco stood at the window, watching Snapeís dark figure shrink as he flew over the grounds toward the Forbidden Forest. Why had Snape asked about Hermione? Draco remembered with a prickle of fury that Snape had stood by while Bellatrix had tortured Hermione earlier that night. He knew Snape must have seen hundreds of people tortured or murdered on Voldemortís orders by nowîyet how could Snape have watched it happen, not intervening? ØDraco,Æ said Harry. Draco turned around. Harry had set the Pensieve on Dumbledoreís desk, and now he poured Snapeís memories into it. Shimmering light emanated from its surface. Draco approached the desk. He and Harry traded one glance before lowering their heads toward the surface. Moments later, they were spinning, falling, twisting into a sea of memory. Draco landed hard on a patch of grass beside Harry. They both looked around beneath a dome of blue sky, and moments later they saw him. Severus Snape, not even Hogwarts age, was creeping up the lane of this factory town in an absurdly oversized coat. Draco watched Snape linger in the bushes, peering out at the sisters on the swing-set. He watched Snape step out into the open, then exchange his first words with a Muggle-born girl named Lily Evans. Itís obvious, isnít it? è I know what you are. And Draco watched, too, as Snape returned from that ill-fated first encounter to his life of deprivation: starved for food, for affection, for the feeling of consequence. So when Lily did, eventually, turn her eyes on Snape, when she softened, when she spoke to him with curiosity rather than affront and agreed that they were the same, Draco understood the way the young Snapeís life was sunlit. And when Snape told Lily that her birth didnít matter, it was what Snape felt. You could see it all around in the memory, in the way the trees shook perfectly in the breeze. Then to Hogwartsîto striving for recognition as a scrawny, sour-faced half-blood in a house of well-loved pure-bloods. School was a world of newness for Snape, everything from his teachersí approval to three square meals, but these new elements rotated always around a single fixed point. Every humiliation by James Potter and Sirius Black and their cronies, every word of approval from an older Slytherin, every Outstanding on every assignment è it all orbited around the one friendship. Lily Evans infused flavour into every petty event of Snapeís life like salt into water. Yet Draco could see Snapeís world transforming before his eyes, and he felt each stage intimately. It was Dracoís own life played in reverse. He began to hear insistence after insistence of the Slytherins around Snape that it was a humiliation to be seen with that Mudblood girl, yes, best to get what he wanted from her and drop her. And to avoid ostracization, Snape murmured coldly indifferent remarks and pretended they understood what Lily was to himîpretended that she was a decoration upon his life rather than its entire substance. He had to pretend. He didnít want the other Slytherins to cast him out. Lily didnít understand (how could she, sunlight Lily, breeze-spun Lily), but the other Slytherins made him feel special for the first time. Even more than that, they made him feel powerful; the first Dark curse he cast felt like control. Lily was everything to him, but she was the opposite of the way power felt. To love her was to kick through the air in freefall, and to watch her slowly turning toward James Potter like a sunflower toward the sky was unbearable. So Draco watched as Severus groped for anything else that might provide stability. He reached out and his fingers found the Dark Arts, and the other Slytherins who would reassure him that he was unique, that he had a place beside the most powerful wizard in history, and all he needed to do was place himself firmly on the right side of the lines that had begun to darken around him, separating pure-blood from half-blood from Muggle-born. So Draco watched with the feeling of nightmare as Snape was hoisted up by his ankle so that his robes fell over his headîas Lily defended himîas he tumbled back to the earthîas he said the words that could not be unsaid to the only person he had ever loved. Draco watched Snapeís world come apart again and again and again for twenty years. He watched Snape, his whole body contorted with pain, vow to protect Lilyís son. The Granger girl. She is alive? Draco understood the question now, the same way he understood the bitter way that Snape had looked at him. Some part of Snape must loathe him for walking the path that Snape had destroyed for himself. Then, at last, Snape and Dumbledore sat in the latterís office, circling the events of the previous two years. Draco heard his own death sentence discussed, the task that Voldemort had set him, and to hear his task discussed by Dumbledore was nearly surreal. But it was not until they mentioned Nagini that Draco remembered, quite abruptly, that Snape had been meant to deliver a message to Harryîthat this was the very point of this torrent of memories. He had to force his mind away from Lily Potter, although even here, something of her memory infused the scene, shot through every moment of Snapeís thoughts. Draco forced himself to listen to the two men speaking. ØIf there comes a time,Æ said Dumbledore, Øwhen Lord Voldemort stops sending that snake forth to do his bidding, but keeps it safe beside him under magical protection, then, I think, it will be safe to tell Harry èÆ ØTell him what?Æ Dumbledoreís eyes shut tight. Dracoís mouth went strangely dry. ØTell him that on the night Lord Voldemort tried to kill him, when Lily cast her own life between them as a shield, the Killing Curse rebounded upon Lord Voldemort, and a fragment of Voldemortís soul was blasted apart from the whole, and latched itself onto the only living soul left in that collapsed building. Part of Lord Voldemort lives inside Harry, and it is that which gives him the power of speech with snakes, and a connection with Lord Voldemortís mind that he has never understood. And while that fragment of soul, unmissed by Voldemort, remains attached to and protected by Harry, Lord Voldemort cannot die.Æ ØSo the boy è the boy must die?Æ asked Snape quite calmly. ØAnd Voldemort himself must do it, Severus. That is essential.Æ The two men continued to talk, but Dracoís hearing seemed to have broken. Surely he had misunderstood. Surely somewhere in the rush of memory there had been something to soften what he had just heard, to change its meaning. Draco turned. Harry was standing quite still, his green eyes like glass. So much like his motherís, Draco had heard so many people say all their lives, and now he too understood how exactly the two resembled each other. Draco looked back toward the desk just as Snape cast the doe Patronus. She landed softly, lightly, in the office, and though the memory faded, though it changed, her silver form seemed to swirl over Dracoís vision as the last scenes played out before them: Snapeís engineering of the Seven Potters plan, and finally the planting of artifacts in the Potter Cottage. Severus lowered himself to his knees before the ruined crib, where Lily had died, and slid the box beneath. He knelt there a long time, as if before an altar. The scene dissolved into darkness. Draco landed back on the floor of the Headís office. He swayed, his legs unsteady. He stood in silence beside Harry, distant sounds of battle swimming around them. Of course, the fight raged on. But the conversation with Dumbledore was still echoing in his ears. Upon the wall, the clock began to strike midnight. It brought Draco back into himself with a jolt. His hand tightened around the Elder Wand. He spun toward Harry, realizing in that instant what Harry would doîwhat Harry must intend to do. Draco began to think a Shield Charm, began to lift the Elder Wand. Harryís Full-Body Bind blasted into Draco. He flew back and landed, rigid as a board, on the office floor. No, he thought. He tried to force sound out from between his clenched teeth, but managed only a strangled noise. Harry knelt beside Draco. He was very pale but looked calm; that expression made Draco feel a kind of slow horror. No, you idiot, he thought, wishing he could yell the words, donít do itîundo the charm, weíll find another wayîhavenít the four of us found another way for everything so far? Harry reached into his mokeskin pouch and withdrew the Snitch. ØI am about to die,Æ he whispered to the tiny golden ball. It split open in his hand to reveal the shattered Resurrection Stone. Dracoís heart thudded harder, faster, at the sight of the final Hallow. And as Harry looked into the pieces of the Stone, Draco could have sworn that he saw Harry unmooring from himself little by little, already bent on joining the people he had once wished to bring back to the world of the living. ØThere,Æ Harry said quietly. He slid the Elder Wand from Dracoís rigid fingers, then the Invisibility Cloak from his pocket, and held the Deathly Hallows united in his handsîMaster of Death. ØIím leaving this with you,Æ said Harry in that same quiet voice, placing the Elder Wand on Dumbledoreís desk, well away from Draco. ØSince Iím giving myself up, I should be its final master, but I donít want to take any chances.Æ There was a long silence. Finite Incantatem, Draco thought, thinking of Harryís wand, of the wand on the desk. Finite Incantatem è He tried to move a finger, a toe, anything, but his body was as inaccessible to him as a foreign object. Harry had straightened up. He was halfway under the Cloak already, a slice of him gone. ØYou remember first year?Æ he said suddenly, looking back down at Draco. ØHow you challenged me to a midnight duel?Æ Draco felt real panic, then. There was no way to stop it. He would be forced to lie here and watch Harry Potter walk out of this room, into Voldemortís hands. ØItís a long time since then,Æ Harry went on, smiling a strange, distant smile. ØThanks for this year, Draco.Æ He brought one hand absently to his forehead, then to his chest, as though checking the basic functions of his body, which he was about to depart. ØTell Ron and Hermione, and Ginny è tell everyone I love them, and goodbye.Æ Draco forced another strangled yell between his teeth. It hurt horribly, but he forced out another, and another. But Harry was stooping beneath the Invisibility Cloak. He was gone. # ØYou have fought valiantly. Lord Voldemort knows how to value bravery.Æ Halfway up the cramped set of steps, Ron startled so violently that he nearly tripped Tonks. Voldemortís voice continued, ØYet you have sustained heavy losses. If you continue to resist me, you will all die, one by one. I do not wish this to happen. Every drop of magical blood spilled is a loss and a waste.Æ ØIgnore it,Æ gasped Hermione, a stitch throbbing in her side, the cut on her scalp leaking new blood. But there was no ignoring the voice. It echoed around them as they climbed. ØLord Voldemort is merciful. I command my forces to retreat immediately. You have one hour. Dispose of your dead with dignity. Treat your injured.Æ They stopped in their tracks. ØRetreat?Æ said Tonks. ØWhatís he doing?Æ ØI speak now, Harry Potter, directly to you. If, at the end of that hour, you have not come to me, have not given yourself up, then battle recommences. This time, I shall enter the fray myself, Harry Potter, and I shall find you, and I shall punish every last man, woman, and child who has tried to conceal you from me. One hour.Æ Ron had gone as white as a sheet. Though Hermione felt a surge of fear, she said, ØDraco wonít let him do it. This is goodîif the Death Eaters have cleared out, we can get to the office more quickly!Æ And indeed, within a matter of minutes, they had broken out into the hallway of the Headís office. They piled up the spiral steps and into the Headís office. Hermioneís heart dropped into her stomach. Draco and Vincent Crabbe lay rigid on the ground, Dracoís eyes wide open. ØIt was Potter.Æ ØWhat?Æ Draco forced himself to his feet, leaning heavily on the Headís desk.
