ØOf course è and Lily è I remember, yes, for the first few years of school, we would occasionally see her with Severus. But I never knew that he èÆ ØBut Harry,Æ Ron broke in urgently. ØWhereís Harry?Æ ØYouíre too late,Æ said Draco hoarsely. ØHe left a quarter of an hour ago. Heís gone.Æ Chapter End Notes tbh this one gave me real trouble... i think The Prince's Tale is probably the single best chapter of canon, and whenever this fic bumps into something i REALLY love, i'm like, "well, why aren't we just rereading canon, then." but them's the breaks with a rewrite .one more plus epilogue!! :) tumblr | instagram Chapter 32: TheLove ofthe Dead Chapter Notes hello! as a heads-up, a number of passages and/or sentences in this chapter are sourced directly from Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows. if it feels familiar, it may be because i didn't write it. as the DH remix comes to a close, we converge! See the end of the chapter for more notes As Draco ran through Hogwarts at Hermioneís side, everything about the castle seemed off-balance. All the portraits had emptied of their inhabitants. Comfortable nooks once devoted to study had caved in, and space bled into ruined space. When Draco cast a look into a classroom whose desks lay on their backs, smouldering, he could see clean through a collapsed wall into the splintered tiles of the Prefectsí Bathroom. It was all wrong, and yet none of the wreckage felt as wrong as what Draco knew to be true: they were too late. Harry had had more than enough time to get down to the Forbidden Forest. He must already be dead. Yet how could he be? Draco felt that sense of imbalance again, like water was pushing at his inner ear. How could Harry Potter, as foundational to Dracoís conception of Hogwarts as the stones of the castle, have died? Hermione was crying openly as they ran; Ron was white-faced; Lupin and Tonks urged them on faster, faster. Draco stopped only to snatch a wand from the hand of a fallen Death Eater and hide the Elder Wand deep in his robes. As they pelted through the first-floor corridor, they passed dozens of injured allies lying on the floor or propped against the wall, Madam Pomfrey attending to them. ØHarryÆîthey stopped to askîØhave you seen Harry Potter?Æ No one had, but Draco hadnít expected him to come out from the Cloak again. They ran onward. Low moans of pain filled the hall, but the worst sounds came from ahead. They tore down the steps in the Entrance Hall and froze in the threshold to the Great Hall. Hermione went silent, shocked out of tears. The four house tables stood against the wall, and laid out upon the plain of flagstones were rows upon rows of the fallen. Feet away, Padma Patil and Michael Corner knelt by the tiny body of Colin Creevey, the living as blank-faced as the dead. Dobby and Winky wailed quietly with the other house-elves at the end of the hall, where several elves lay upon bedsheets and towels. In one corner, Fred and George were nearly invisible in the cluster of their mourning family. Molly Weasley had prostrated herself between them, her mouth wide open but no sound emerging, tears pouring down her cheeks. My mother, Draco thought numbly. He scanned the rows of bodies for a blonde head, dreading that he would find her. Instead he saw other familiar faces. His gaze caught on Sturgis Podmore, then on Lavender Brown. Parvati Patil was wracked with such violent sobs over her best friendís body that it resembled sickness. è But perhaps the worst were those whose bodies lay alone and unrecognised, with no one left to grieve them. Hermione clasped his shoulder wordlessly. Draco turned and saw his mother standing in the Entrance Hall, not ten feet away. Narcissa looked ghostly, almost translucent. The first thing Draco felt was relief, but he didnít move. He didnít know whether to step closer or gird himself for what might come out of her mouth. Her terrible scream on the Gringotts steps seemed to build in his ears. Would she look at him and see his father? Did she blame him for the fact that Lucius numbered among the death toll of this endless day and night? Then Narcissa swept toward him and took his face in her hands. ØMy son,Æ she whispered. She wrapped her arms around him in a crushing embrace. Eyes tight shut, Draco placed his hands on her shivering back. He was returned to his senses seconds later by the great crack of the front doors opening, and Remusís and Tonksís magnified voices ringing down the grounds. ØHarry! Harry!Æ Draco pulled back from his mother as Ron, with a torn look at his grieving family, pelted after Remus and Tonks. Hermione hesitated. ØGo,Æ Draco told her with a squeeze of her hand. She didnít need telling twice. ØHominem revelio!Æ she cried as she sprinted over the threshold, wand held high. ØThis way, Draco,Æ Narcissa said, turning toward the steps. ØWe must leave the castle before the Dark Lord launches his second attack. We must get to safety.Æ ØSafety? Mother, if the Order fall tonight, thereís nowhere weíll be safe.Æ ØAnywhere will be better than this castle. Come!Æ She took his wrist. ØYou and I will not change the course of this war.Æ She tried to tug him forward, but Draco was half a head taller than Narcissa now, and she could not move him by force. He set his hand on his motherís and, gently but firmly, worked his wrist free. ØI already have,Æ he told her. There was a pause. ØYes,Æ she murmured at last. ØI know.Æ She looked up into his face with a strained, curious expression. ØWhat is it?Æ A minute, graceful movement of her head. ØYou became a man without my noticing.Æ The tightness in Dracoís chest intensified. His mother would never say such a thing for sentimentís sake; after seventeen years of seeking to shelter him, she must really see him as a man grown. Yet Draco did not feel sleek and complete, the way he had always imagined manhood must feel when he looked at his father. He felt sickened from the sight of the Great Hall, from the residual fear that he might spot his mother or Pansy or Goyle among the dead. No, he was no longer a boy, and yet he felt as though his boyhood were near his surface in this moment, beating its terrified wings against his chest. ØYou go to the evacuation point,Æ he said to his mother. ØThe Death Eaters canít get inside. Itís on the seventh floor opposite the tapestry of Barnabas the Barmy. If things go wrong, Iíll meet you inîÆ ØNo.Æ Her tone was clipped, resolved. ØWhatever we do, it will be together.Æ Before Draco could reply, another voice began to speak. Voldemortís words echoed through the halls of Hogwarts one more time, causing even the sobs in the Great Hall to go silent. ØHarry Potter is dead.Æ Cries of shock rang throughout the Great Hall. The Weasleys tottered to their feet, their faces stricken. ØHe was killed as he ran away, trying to save himself while you lay down your lives for him. We bring you his body as proof that your hero is gone.Æ Ginny pelted wild-eyed into the Entrance Hall, followed by Padma, Cho, Ernie Macmillan, Dobby and Winky, all the Hogwarts Professors, as Voldemort continued: ØThe battle is won. You have lost half of your fighters. Our forces outnumber you, and the Boy Who Lived is finished. There must be no more war. For the good of the Wizarding World, anybody who continues to fight in the name of Harry Potter, man, woman, or child, will be eliminated, as will every member of their family. Come out of the castle now, give your allegiance to the Ministry, and you shall be spared. Your parents and children, your brothers and sisters will live and be forgiven, and you will join me in the new world we shall build together.Æ Everyone else in the Great Hall was flooding out into the Entrance Hall now, fearful and disbelieving, and those in the first-floor corridor were pouring down the steps; even the wounded were hobbling out, leaning on their allies, whispering that it could not be real. Draco stood unmoving with his mother as the crowd milled around them, looking through the thrown-open front doors down to the black ridge of the Forbidden Forest. He already knew it was real. Harry had done what heíd promised to do again and again. Not even a day ago, although it felt like a lifetime, he had told the Order at headquarters, I can decide what kind of world I want to live inîor die for. I made that choice years ago. Now Draco found himself moving toward the door with the rest of the crowd, his mother at his shoulder. We bring you his body as proof è he had to see it, had to be sure è had to find Hermione. If Harryís sacrifice had destroyed the Horcrux within his body, and if Snape had succeeded in killing the snake, then Lord Voldemort was mortal for the first time in half a century. And he must be killed now, here, because if he were allowed to leave the battle alive, he might rend his soul again, conceal new Horcruxes where they might never be found. They stepped down into the dark grass. Draco saw Hermione at the front of their alliesí ranks and pushed through the crowd until he had reached her. She was ashen-faced, her eyes filled with fear and disbelief, but neither could speak. She slipped her hand into his and squeezed so tightly that it hurt. The Death Eatersí forces pushed out of the woods, a slow tide of shadow creeping up the grounds. A hush spread among the Hogwarts fighters. Voldemort had not lied: they were badly outnumbered. There were two Death Eaters or Ministry members for every one of the Orderís allies. ØNO!Æ came a raw scream halfway down the Orderís line. Draco didnít recognise the voice as Professor McGonagallís until he saw her swaying in the grass, a step out from the front line. Then Draco saw it, too. Voldemort was gliding up the grounds, tall and skeletal. Bellatrix Lestrange strode at his left side, a mark like a blistered hand wrapped over her face. Draco knew immediately that this had not been the Orderís doing, that it had been Voldemortís punishment for losing the Cup of Hufflepuff. At Voldemortís right hand walked Severus Snape, face impassive, wand outstretched. Harryís dead body hung suspended eerily in the air at Snapeís wandtip, his glasses clinging to his thin nose. Cries burst from the crowd. ØHARRY!Æ Hermione half-sobbed, half-screamed, and Ron was shouting, ØNO!Æ and Ginny was yelling something incomprehensible in the Orderís clamour. But Draco watched in silence, ice spreading through him. Harry Potter limp and lifeless. The dead in the Great Hall. His own father on the Gringotts steps. è It was all so senseless. Months ago, Draco had sat in a chip shop in Muggle London and seen the hollowness of the Death Eatersí cause. Now the lives of hundreds had been poured into that hollow place. He knew that the others around him would never stop fighting, although in light of their numbers, it seemed impossible that they would reach Voldemort. So many more would be slaughteredîand for what? The tide of the enemy rose up the grounds and stopped twenty paces from the Orderís lines. For a while, Voldemort did not speak. He seemed to be enjoying the sounds of rage and anguish washing down toward him, his eyes lingering lovingly on those who wept and screamed. And when Draco tore his eyes at last from Harryís body, he saw that it was even worse than heíd feared. The shape of the great snake still moved at Voldemortís heels, stirring in her starry, enchanted cage. ØSnape hasnít done it,Æ Ron whispered, sounding numb. ØWhy?Æ ØTh-that enchantment,Æ Hermione said, wiping her cheeks. ØIt must be something that only V-Voldemort can alter.Æ ØThen itís impossible for anyone to get to her.Æ ØNo. Itís an active enchantment. If heís going to fight us all, heíll have to disperse it.Æ Hermione let go of Dracoís hand to draw her wand, rage mingling with the grief on her face. ØThe snake mustnít get away.Æ ØIt wonít,Æ Draco murmured. His hand went to the box in his pocket, which held three Basilisk fangs. Now Voldemort spoke in his magnified voice. ØSilence.Æ And with a flash and a bang from his wand, silence fell. ØIt is over. Set him down, Snape, at my feet, where he belongs.Æ Snape lowered his wand, and Harryís body drifted down to the grass. Voldemort began to pace slowly behind him, back and forth, his robes hushing through the grass. His bloodless face was luminescent in the gloom. ØYou see?Æ said Voldemort. ØHarry Potter is dead. You must act reasonably. Think of your families. Would you squander your own lives and theirs for a killer raised among Muggles, who sought to destroy the Wizarding WîÆ ØLiar!Æ yelled Ron. The Order began to scream, too, a flood of outrage washing down the grounds. The spell had brokenîbut how? Draco wondered suddenly. Voldemort thrust his wand forward again. With a second, louder bang, silence fell once more. ØSurely not all of you are so foolhardy?Æ said Voldemortís amplified voice. ØCome, now! è Even if you believe somehow that the boy is innocent of the crimes that have followed him year after year, accident after tragic accident è would you really charge into death for the memory of a boy of no particular talent, who allowed so many to die for him?Æ He stopped pacing at Harryís shoulder. ØThere must be someone among you who will see reason! There must be at least one èÆ His crimson eyes roved the Orderís ranks. Draco knew an instant before it happened that Voldemortís eyes would lock onto his own. The snakelike face affected surprise, but Draco knew this was no coincidence. ØWhat is this?Æ said Voldemort. ØDraco Malfoy, one of my own house è one of my own Death Eaters, the fearless few who have tried for so many years to preserve our Wizarding customs è Draco Malfoy, of such noble lineage, seduced into the Orderís seditious ways? Surely not. Come, Draco è return to stand at my side, and all will be forgiven.Æ He lifted his wandless hand and beckoned. Protego! Draco thought, brandishing his wand, but whichever wandless spell Voldemort had used penetrated the Shield Charm. Draco was a fish on a reel, snared and yanked suddenly forward. He flew out of the crowd to cries from Hermione and Narcissa, and then he was on his knees on the grass, feet away from Harryís body. A hush fell over the Order behind him. Night wind whipped through the grass as Draco looked up into the face of Lord Voldemort. There was no mercy in those red eyes, no pity, no hint of the interest that he had pantomimed for the crowd. ØI wondered if I might see you here, Draco,Æ said Voldemort in that soft hiss, which nonetheless echoed over the grounds. ØBut I was certain that you would ally yourself with wizardkind. Your aunt has told me that your father was loyal to his last breath, whatever his failings è I know you to be a devoted son. Surely you will stand alongside Luciusís memory?Æ It was a taunt. Draco tried to suppress the heated stab of rage he felt. He must think; he must form a plan. The snake, he thought without allowing his eyes to stray to Nagini. Could he pretend to accept Voldemortís offer, and in doing so gain access to the snake? She was right there, undulating in her enchanted cage è yet even if he managed to stab her with one of the fangs in his pocket, how would he escape with his life? Could he devise a plan with Snape somehow; could the Order provide some distraction? He glanced over his shoulder. Most of the crowd seemed frozen, afraid to move lest Voldemort strike Draco dead upon the spot. But at the front, Ron and Ginny were restraining Hermione, Mr. and Mrs. Weasley holding his mother back. Both of their mouths were open in silent yells. Understanding began to move slowly through Draco. His thoughts of plots and schemes unwound, leaving his mind very quiet. They were being held back because the place where he stood was ground marked for death, as certainly as a grave. He was kneeling alone on the dark grass. No help would come to him here. Of course, Voldemort did not expect him to return to the side of the Death Eaters. He had invited him out into the open for torture and humiliation before his murder, to show what happened to those who were disloyal. There would be no escaping with his life. Not this time. ØWell?Æ said Voldemort. Draco considered. Then he stood, straightened his robes, and swept back his hair. Once he had grovelled at the Dark Lordís feet and pleaded for mercy. But Voldemort would never take his pride, nor his dignity, again. ØKill me, then, if thatís what youíre planning to do,Æ he said, as cold and scathing as ever he had been at Hogwarts when speaking to somebody he considered beneath him. ØBut donít make me stand here and listen to you talk.Æ Voldemortís eyes widened infinitesimally. A few stunned, fearful laughs could be heard scurrying through the ranks of the Order behind Draco, and he felt a strange, twisting leap in his stomach as though he were plunging through the air on his Nimbus 2001. ØYou dareîyou dareîÆ spat Bellatrix. Around the burned handprint on her face, her skin had flushed a humiliated red. ØLet me kill him myself, my lord! Let me wipe this stain from my family tree!Æ Voldemort looked at Bellatrix with icy disdain. Draco saw her flinch. Then Voldemort turned his red eyes back onto Draco, and he knew that impossible pain awaited him. The lunacy of having spoken to the Dark Lord as he just hadîand yet he would be dead within the hour. Why should he hold his tongue; why shouldnít he speak exactly as he wished? With a rush of painful feeling, Draco remembered the way Hermione had used to laugh at his dry retorts, turning over onto her back on the library floor, a cushion beneath the spirals of her hair, a book hugged to her chestîbut he must not think of Hermione, or he would think of the life they might have lived è The future heíd longed for with her seemed cruelly close in that moment, lying in a thousand glittering pieces just out of armís reach, millimetres from coalescing into something whole. In the end, heíd had his motherís love. Heíd had a family, Tonks and Remus and little Teddy; heíd had Pansy, Goyle, and the allies he had made in the Order. Heíd had, above all, Hermione. And they had been so close to breaking through the dark clouds into a world that would have them. Maybe, Draco thought, this was what it meant to become a man: to feel the kind of yearning that was larger than himself. He stood very still, waiting for the Cruciatus. Instead he heard the silky voice of Severus Snape. ØMy lord è might I suggest that you take this opportunity to remind the boy of his loyalties? It seems only fitting to purge Slytherin House of a blood traitor with the symbol of our house.Æ Snape turned his black eyes to Nagini. Draco understood the plan at once. He wondered if Voldemort would see through itîif he would sense dangerîbut the malice in Voldemortís face was transforming into excitement. The Dark Lordís arrogance reigned, and he saw only a delightfully symbolic piece of sadism from a servant who had never failed him. ØGood,Æ Voldemort whispered to Snape. ØYes è very good, Severus.Æ Voldemort raised his voice so that it echoed off the Hogwarts faade. ØYoung Draco has volunteered to demonstrate what happens to those who continue to fight when the battle is lost.Æ He raised his wand. Draco unlatched the box of Basilisk fangs inside his pocket. ØNo!Æ Dracoís stomach twisted. It was Hermioneís voice. Again Voldemortís Silencing Charm had broken, and he heard the Orderís cries, and footsteps striking the dewy grass, and he knew even before he turned that Hermione had broken freeîthat she would collide with him, seizing his arm at the elbow, her tear-streaked face defiant. He wanted to tell her to go back, to push her away to safety, but it was too late. She was already looking around at the massed Ministry workers and raising her voice. ØDonít you see?Æ she demanded. ØCanít you understand why they want to hurt Draco? Itís because he used to believe all the lies theyíve told you about Muggle-borns, andîand house-elves, and werewolves, and the rest! And if a Malfoy realised none of it matters, anybody can! Itís not too late for all of you to see the truth, too!Æ Another voice rang out. Ron was pelting across the no-manís-land to join them. ØVoldemort was never trying to save the Wizarding World,Æ he shouted. ØHeís done this to take over the Ministry, and youíre just going to stand there and let him!Æ Shouts of agreement from the Orderís ranks. There were stirrings in the groups of Ministry workers. No one dared to speak, but Draco saw a few heads turn uncertainly. ØThe truth?Æ Voldemortís high, mirthless voice soared over the crowd. ØA Mudblood and a blood traitor, responsible for hundreds of deaths at Malfoy Manor and Azkaban, seek to tell you all the truth! Of course, we already know what they will do if allowed to encroach on an orderly Wizarding society. We have seen the toll theyíve taken on innocent magical families. How considerate, then, of these two criminals to offer themselves up so freely è it is time for justice to be served.Æ Draco, Hermione, and Ron drew their wands, but Disarming Charms shot out of the mass of Death Eaters, too many to block. Their wands flew from their hands. Draco didnít dare rearm himself with the Elder Wandîwhat if, despite what Harry had guessed, it recognised Voldemort as its new master?îand so he stood defenceless with Hermione and Ron. Voldemort had raised his wand again. Hermioneís hand tightened on Dracoís arm, and he heard her whisper, scarcely a breath, ØI love you,Æ and he whispered it in return, knowing it would be the last time. Their eyes met for a shivering instant, and if he was going to die here, at least this was the last of it: her face in the starlight. ØLook,Æ Voldemort called, Øand see how the blood of Salazar Slytherin protects the Wizarding World from those who would defile it!Æ Voldemortís wand slashed downward. A fissure opened in Naginiís enchanted cage, and Voldemort hissed a command in Parseltongue. The dark, heavy body of the immense snake lunged forward. Her jaws were wide, her fangs gleaming. She was ten feet awayîfiveî Yells from their allies split the air, and then Draco heard the thundering of feet as the Order charged. Draco thrust his hand into his pocket and withdrew the box. Hermione and Ron were ready. They all seized Basilisk fangs, but hardly had they raised their weapons when the snake was upon them. They scattered, dodging her deadly strike, and as the first spells began to crisscross between the Order and the Death Eaters, they were forced to throw themselves flat to the ground. Almost at once, the jets of spells grew into a deluge of light, so many curses and hexes whizzing toward Voldemort that he was impossible to see. Draco brought his fang down again and again, but the snakeís tail slithered and wriggled with frantic speed. Ron threw himself onto the back of Naginiís thrashing body, trying to hold her in place long enough to pierce her hideîbut she was powerful, twelve feet of muscle. The scaled tail lashed the side of Ronís head, knocking him away. He reeled, temporarily stunned, and Nagini shot toward him like dark lightning. A vengeful shriek came from the crowd behind them. As Naginiís jaws gaped wide, Pansy knocked Ron out of the way. The Orderís front line had reached them. Now two more figures were diving onto the snake, pinning its midsection in place far more effectively than Ron had îGregory Goyle and Neville Longbottom. The others piled on. Hermione was keeping Naginiís tail in place, Pansy and Ron crushing the diamond-shaped head down into the grass. Draco lifted his fang high and sank it into the base of the snakeís neck. Voldemort, who was now duelling half a dozen of their front-line allies at once, let out a furious scream. Draco could see the realisation on his face at the sight of the Basilisk fangs, the knowledge of the betrayal. A brilliant crimson light ricocheted from Voldemortís wand, blasting the duellists back one after the other, and he whirled toward his right-hand side, wand held high, ready to smite Snapeîbut Snape was no longer there. The Potions Master had dived out of the Death Eatersí front line toward a spot several paces before Voldemort. He was not alone. From the alliesí front line, Remus was bolting toward the same spot, his eyes wide and stunned in a way that Draco didnít understand until he followed Lupinís line of sight. Both men were hurling themselves toward Harryís lifeless body è except that Harryís body was no longer lifeless. Draco saw with a shock like a physical blow that Harry was thrusting his hands into his robes, withdrawing his wand. He was alive. And Snape and Remus were not a moment too soon, because Voldemort, too, had seen. He screamed a Killing Curse, but spells erupted from Snapeís and Remusís wandtips at the same time. Snapeís spell drew the ground up in front of Harry like a cresting wave of earth, so that the green jet of light sank into the grass. Remus had cast a glittering barrier between Harry and Voldemort. By the time Voldemort had dispersed it with a dark smoke that ate away the points of light, Harry was on his feet, and Snape and Remus had closed ranks before him, standing as one between Voldemort and the son of Lily and James. A great shout built in the crowd as the Order turned toward Harry. ØHEíS ALIVE!ÆîØHARRY!Æ ØWait,Æ Harry yelled, wand outstretched, but hands were grappling him back into the safety of the masses, and Voldemort was crying a spell whose incantation made Dracoís very teeth ache, which made a crack like lightning split the air in two. A dark, roiling mass appeared before Voldemort and split in two. One stream crashed into Snapeís chest, the second into Remusís. The two men fell side by side and moved no more. Uproar. The crowd crushed inward, and their front lines collided with the Death Eatersí. Draco was still clutching the Basilisk fang in one nerveless hand, the other splayed over Naginiís scaly skin. His ears rang. He knew that Remus and Snape were dead, but he could not look away from their bodies. He saw Remus bursting into the front room of headquarters, declaring Teddyís name; he saw the boy Snape crouching in the bushes on that playground decades ago. Then Hermione was tugging him to his feet, a bruise purpling on her cheek, thrusting a wand into his hand. But as they stood back to back, preparing to fight, Draco heard a bizarre sound like a thousand baying hounds echoing down from the sky. He tilted his head upward and felt his heart stop. Charlie Weasley was swooping in at the head of fifty dragonet riders, yelling in French and then Bulgarian to a hundred broom-riders who flanked the dragonets. Thin, powerful bursts of flame issued toward the Death Eatersí back ranks, and those caught in the fires sprinted toward the lake, screaming. Another noise like thunder resounded from the reaches of the grounds. Centaurs, Thestrals, and Hippogriffs were galloping out of the Forbidden Forest. And at the Hogwarts gate, Aberforth Dumbledore was leading a flood of reinforcements on foot, the families of the students, the hundreds upon hundreds who had been sustaining the Orderís activities from across the nation for months. ØWhereís Harry gone?Æ Hermione was yelling. At the other end of the snakeís fallen body, Ron was looking wildly around, strainingîstill not on his feet. Only then did Draco see that Pansy was lying limply against Ron, her eyes half-shut. Two dark stains had spread across her side. When she had pushed Ron out of the way, Nagini had bitten her. Cold flooded through Draco. He tried to move toward them, but his mother caught him by the shoulder and yanked him back just as a bright orange curse tore by. It struck a Death Eater close behind. He heard the crack of bones and the howl of pain. Draco cast a look back, he and his mother clutching each otherís forearms, and saw Bellatrix ten steps away, her eyes brilliant with bloodlust. He saw her lips form the word Finally, a silent snarl lost in the chaos. She raised her wand, and though she cast no spell, fighters threw themselves out of the way, no one daring to stand between Bellatrix Lestrange and her prey. As the last bystanders cleared the space between them, flying into duels in their periphery, Draco, Narcissa, and Hermione faced Bellatrix shoulder to shoulder. They began to duel. Bellatrix moved so quickly that she seemed inhuman, her robes a dark whirl. Barely had Draco thought the spellsîIncarcerous, Stupefy, Levicorpusîwhen she was deflecting them. Her own curses gouged deep wounds into the earth at their feet; they set swathes of grass aflame; one sliced a hot, painful cut into Dracoís side. He strangled a pained sound in his throat. He knew that if Hermione or his mother took the time to look toward him, they were dead. Then Bella swept her wand toward the fallen body of the snake. Draco staggered backward in horror as its twelve-foot length reanimated, its lifeless jaws gaping and dripping and snapping, fangs thrust toward them. ØSectumsempra!Æ Draco yelled, bringing his wand downward. The snakeís body came apart with the force of the slashing curse. Barely had her puppet fallen when Bellatrix was shooting new curses toward them. But now two others had seen openings in the volleying spells and darted into the duel: Bill and Ginny Weasley, livid with grief. Under their onslaught, Bellatrix was beginning to lose ground. Hermioneís hex singed her sleeve; Billís spell caught her left arm in a twist of conjured rope. ØNo more, Bella!Æ Narcissa shouted, her thin face twisted. ØItís done!Æ Bellaís wild eyes landed on her sister. Her teeth were bared, loathing and despair etched in every inch of her expression. With the Dark Lordís handprint burned upon her face, Draco knew she had lost his favour foreverîand so she had lost everything. She aimed her wand not at Narcissa but at Draco. ØAvadaîÆ she snarled. Draco lifted his wand to summon some obstruction, but before Bellatrix could finish the incantation, a jet of dark red flame shot from Narcissaís wand and seared across her sisterís chest. Bellatrix spun backward. She toppled upon the grass, steps from the place that Voldemort was duelling McGonagall, Kingsley, and Slughorn. Draco stared at his mother. Narcissaís face was lined with cold fury, her eyes bright as she lowered her wand. Draco heard, rather than saw, Voldemort encounter Bellatrixís body. The Dark Lordís rage exploded with the force of dragonfire. A crater blasted around him into the grounds, shreds of dirt and bloodied grass showering outward, McGonagall and Kingsley and Slughorn thrown backward through the air. Even as it cleared, and Voldemort looked around for victims, a voice said, ØEnough!Æ Harry stepped out into the crater, onto the dark, soft earth, and Draco knew right away what to do. He drew the Elder Wand from the depths of his robes and moved through the crowd, who were shifting to form an immense ring around the blast site. ØSo,Æ Voldemort said. ØThe Boy Who Lived, come to die. è Who will you hide behind this time, Potter?Æ ØNo one,Æ said Harry. ØNo more shields, no more lifelines. It has to end like this.Æ ØOh, I donít think anyone here will believe that.Æ Voldemort turned his head as though to let the crowd in on a joke. ØWe all know that wherever the Boy Who Lived walks, others fall around him! Who will it be this time, Potter? Who will you allow me to kill next for your own survival?Æ ØYouíre not going to kill any of these people.Æ Harryís voice rose. ØYouíre not going to touch them ever again. Itís just you and me, Riddle. You, meîand this.Æ He glanced back as Draco stepped up to the rim of the crater and placed the Elder Wand into Harry's hand. Voldemort regarded the wand a long moment. Then his slash of a mouth drew outward in a terrible smile, and he began to laugh. His laughter pealed down the grounds, rang over the lake. ØThe Elder Wand, at last,Æ he hissed. ØFate favours Lord Voldemort today! Yes, Potter. Duel me with the Elder Wand, and see how it holds allegiance to its true master.Æ ØIím counting on that,Æ said Harry. Voldemortís smile contorted. ØYou foolish boy. Did you not see me strike down Severus Snape, the last master of the Elder Wand? Do you not seeîÆ He broke off. He had pointed one skeletal finger up the slope of the grounds toward the place where Snape and Remus lay, steps from the ruined form of Nagini. But the bodies of the two men were no longer there. ØLike I said, Riddle,Æ said Harry quietly. He turned his head infinitesimally, and as Draco looked that way, too, his mouth drifted open. There they were, stepping between the ranks of the crowd: Remus Lupin and Severus Snape, supported by the Hogwarts teachers, each man bandaged across his chestîbut each unquestionably alive. Voldemortís blood-red eyes widened. He looked back to Harry, waiting for the explanation that must come, and began to circle the crater, Harry moving opposite him. ØDidnít you notice your Silencing Charms couldnít hold them?Æ said Harry. ØDidnít you know what that meant? Iíve done what my mother did for me. I walked into that forest to die for them, andîÆ ØBut you did not!Æ ØI did,Æ said Harry quietly. ØIíve been there, the place youíre so afraid of. Iíve been right up to the door of death. And Iíve seen what youíll become.Æ Voldemort aimed his wand at Harry almost convulsively. It was clear that not even Snapeís and Remusís reappearance had disturbed him like these words. ØWhat I will become? You believe that you, a boy with no exceptional magical talent, can overcome me, when I am the master of the wand in your hand? Perhaps Snape survived, but I cast the curse that struck him!Æ ØYou still donít understand, Riddle.Æ ØYou dareîÆ ØYes, I dare! I know things you donít know, Tom Riddle. I know lots of important things that you donít. Want to hear some, before you make another big mistake?Æ Voldemort continued to circle. ØThere is no need to tell me that Snape proved himself a traitor and a fool in the end.Æ He shot a look toward the edge of the craterîbut Snape had melted into the crowd. ØOf course he sought to deceive me, to take more power for himself. Such things are not difficult to grasp, Potter èÆ ØPower had nothing to do with it. You lost Snapeís loyalty the moment you started hunting down my mother, my Muggle-born mother, because he loved her nearly all of his life, since the time when they were children. Donít you remember him asking you to spare her life?Æ ØHe desired her, that was all,Æ sneered Voldemort, Øbut when she had gone, he agreed that there were other women, and of purer blood, worthier of himîÆ ØOf course he told you that,Æ said Harry, Øbut he was Dumbledoreís spy from the moment you threatened her. Heís been working against you ever since, because Dumbledore understood what you couldnít.Æ ØDumbledore,Æ Voldemort spat. ØDumbledore lies dead upon these very grounds!Æ Voldemort cast a contemptuous look toward the edge of the lake, where the white tomb was a grey shadow in the gloaming. ØSee what good Dumbledoreís understanding did when Snape killed himÆîhe seemed unable to keep the truth from spilling outîØon my command!Æ ØWrong again. Snape didnít kill Dumbledore on your orders, Riddle. They planned his death together.Æ Harry raised the Elder Wand. ØDo you see, yet?Æ Voldemort prowled along the edge of the crater, his attention now riveted on the Elder Wand. ØVery well. è Then you suggest that Dumbledore died the final master of the Elder Wand. But that will do you no good, Potter. That means we duel on skill alone.Æ ØYouíre forgetting something,Æ said Harry, stopping in his tracks beside Draco; he had come full circle. ØAm I?Æ said Voldemort. ØWhat might that be?Æ ØMe,Æ said Draco. Satisfaction coursed through him to see Voldemortís steps slow, then stop. His face had filled with suspicion. Harry glanced back at Draco, and Draco stepped forward into the crater, too. ØRemember the task you set me, back when you gave me this?Æ Draco rolled up the sleeve of his robe to reveal the Dark Mark stripped of its power. ØWell, I almost went through with it. I disarmed Dumbledore. I became the master of the Elder Wand.Æ ØAnd still you failed to kill him?Æ Voldemort hissed. ØYou failed to kill Dumbledore, when I would have rewarded you beyond all otherîÆ ØI chose Dumbledore because I saw the way you reward your followers,Æ said Draco, his voice sharp and clear. ØLook at my father. Look at Bellatrix. She lived to please you!Æ Faces turned toward Bellatrixís broken body on the ridge of the crater, the red mark stamped across her face. ØLook at me,Æ Draco said, staring into Voldemortís ghostly face. ØYou sent me, a sixteen.year-old who would have been loyal to you, to die. And now youíre going to pay the price, because earlier tonight, Potter got control of that wand, and he did it through me.Æ Voldemort had remained impassive through Dracoís words. Now, however, he smiled a thin smile. ØA childish fantasy. If Potter came to me in the forest as the master of the Elder Wand è then its loyalty is, indeed, to me.Æ Voldemort raised his own wand aloft. ØWith this wand I cast the Killing Curse upon him! It is clear that you had one last trick up your sleeve to cling to life, Potter, but it comes down to this: I struck a blow against you. I showed myself to be your better. You could not defend yourself against me, you were cast to the earth by my force, and the wand will recognise my power!Æ Harry shook his head. ØYou donít learn from your mistakes, do you, Riddle? Itís not that I couldnít defend myself. Itís that I chose not to.Æ ØYou think that makes any difference?" Voldemort spat. "It is all the same kind of weakness!Æ ØIt makes all the difference in the world. My mum dying to save me, Dumbledore dying the way he and Snape planned, me walking into that forest. è These arenít accidents. It means you didnít master any of us. It means that weíve chosen how we live or die, and what we live or die for.Æ Harry raised the Elder Wand and pointed it at Voldemort. ØIt means you never overpowered me tonight, Riddle. You did exactly what I meant you to do. Iím not afraid of death. Are you?Æ A great wind swept over the darkened grounds then, whipping Dracoís hair off his forehead, making robes flap and limbs shake in the chill. Harryís yell and Voldemortís shriek mingled in the dark air, and the spells that blasted from their wandtips illuminated them, Harryís afire with focus and certainty, Voldemortís face a greenish blurî ØAvada Kedavra!Æ ØExpelliarmus!Æ The two spells collided with an eruption of golden flame, scorching the earth in the centre of the crater. The crowd flinched back from the brilliant light, but between his fingers, Draco saw the green jet ricochet backward, rebounding against the power of the Elder Wand, which had recognised not force but intentînot the power of domination but of self-possession. Tom Riddle struck the earth with a mundane finality, his body feeble and shrunken, the white hands empty, the snakelike face vacant and unknowing. Voldemort was dead, killed by his own curse. One shivering second of silence, the shock of the moment suspended, and then the tumult broke as the roars of the watchers rent the air. The crowd flooded forward into the crater to surround Harry, and Draco stepped back, overwhelmed by the very sight, the way they reached and groped for Harry, the way they screamed and cried for him. On the edge of the crater nearby, an elderly man had sunk to his knees, sobbing, one hand splayed across his face. Others were embracing. Draco craned his neck, trying to search in the confusionî where was Hermione? Farther from the epicentre, screams and flashes of spell light still punctuated the night as the Death Eatersí forces tried to blast their way free, but they were the ones outnumbered now, and many of the Ministry had dropped their wands at the truth of Voldemortís treachery, hands held high. Then he heard her. ØDraco!Æ Hermioneís voice pealed through the crowd from where the snake layîwhere, he remembered with a jolt of fear, Ron and Pansy still lay. ØHarry!Æ Draco looked back to see if he might drag Harry with him, but a teeming knot of Order members still surrounded him, shouting, pressing in. The Weasleys and Hagrid, the Hogwarts professors, Luna and Neville and the rest of Dumbledoreís Army. Draco knew better than to try and pull them off him. So he turned alone and forced his way against the crowd toward Hermioneís voice. Their allies were yelling their last battle cries as they chased the Death Eaters back down the grounds, Stunning many, pursuing others who were trying to flee. Draco reached the body of the snake and saw her. Hermione was standing with wand in hand, her hair whipping back and forth in the wind. Her expression was almost wild. His mouth was as dry as parchment. They collided in a painful tangle of limbs. Draco could feel her shaking, and tears were falling from his eyes, too, and they sank to the earth, breaking apart to huddle with Ron and Goyle over Pansy. She was limp and pallid. ØIs sheîÆ Draco said. ØDonít touch her,Æ Ron said fiercely, trying and failing to blink back tears. ØDonít èÆ ØThis way!Æ said a sharp voice overhead. They all looked up to see Narcissa hurrying down from the front doors, Madam Pomfrey bustling after her. The nurse had a bandage plastered over the side of her own neck. The two women sank to Pansyís side. Only after a minuteís insistence did Ron let go, allowing Madam Pomfrey to shift Pansy onto the grass. The nurse touched her wand to Pansyís neck, then to the wound. She cut away Pansyís robes over the bite mark and applied a potion that glowed a pale violet, then returned her wandtip to the injury. They all watched in silence, dreading what she might say. Finally Madam Pomfrey lowered her wand. ØShe will need treatment right away to ensure that there is no lasting damage to the nerves.Æ Draco closed his eyes and felt his fear drain. She was alive, she was going to be all right. His heart beat shallowly, the pain in his side from Bellatrixís cut on a sluggish, pulsing delay. Ron made a croaking sound but couldnít seem to manage words. Madam Pomfrey rose. ØI will return shortly; it may take some time to find a mediwizard who can assist me with transporting her. The potion should hold the symptoms at bay for now. Please move her as little as possible, Mr. Weasley.Æ She bustled off. They watched her go, then, as one, looked out over the grounds without speaking. The bodies of the fallen were being transported up to the Great Hall, their forces and the enemyís alike. Fires were being extinguished, the clutter of Transfigurations being melted back into earth and stone and grass. Here and there, Draco saw clusters like their own, groups of three or six or twelve sitting, shivering, on the grounds. They were groups of loved ones, families and friends, huddled in the oncoming dawn of the new world. After a number of minutes, Draco realised that the cluster around Harry in the crater had finally dissipated. He hardly had time to wonder where heíd gone when Harry was stepping out from beneath the Invisibility Cloak before them, dirt-streaked and exhausted-looking. ØGot rid of the fan club, Potter?Æ said Draco. ØBut I suppose you never will, after this.Æ ØYou might wind up with one, yourself,Æ Harry said. They all stood. Ron and Hermione embraced Harry, and then he shook hands with Draco, Goyle, and Narcissa. But they were too tired to stay on their feet. They sank back down to the slope of the grounds. ØLetís do something about this,Æ Draco said, indicating the body of the great snake that lay before them. Hermione lifted her wand as he raised his. They began to make smooth motions with their wands, back and forth, over the body. The earth shifted beneath the snakeís remains, turning, rolling, and the last Horcrux sifted down into the ground, submerged deeper and deeper with every pass. Soon all that was left was a streak of dark, freshly turned earth, as though it had just been tilled. Draco and Hermione lowered their wands. They all drew a deep breath. Draco hadnít realised until the snake was buried, but the scent of blood had been hanging upon the air. Now the wind rushed in, fresh and cold and sweet, whisking it away. ØIt doesnít feel real, does it?Æ said Hermione quietly. ØNo,Æ said Draco, Ron, Harry, and Goyle all at the same time. Laughter rippled among them, but it was subdued. The battle still lay upon them all like a spell. And yet there was relief, too, the kind of relief that touched Draco to the bone: an absence of fear that he had not felt since fifth year, and which he had not known then how to appreciate. The exhaustion in Dracoís body grew so profound that he felt on the verge of lying down right here on the grounds and slipping into unconsciousness. All that kept him upright was Hermioneís hand in his, and the need to watch the coming sunrise. For dawn was definitely on its way now. Though the landscape around them remained shadowed, colour was seeping back into the grass, into the lake, into the stones of Hogwarts behind them. ØIt is real, though,Æ Draco murmured to Hermione. ØItís over.Æ ØNo.Æ She lifted his hand to her mouth and pressed her lips to his knuckles, her eyes shut. ØItís just about to start.Æ Chapter End Notes i figured i should put Harry James Jesus-Allegory Potter to good use and spare some people for a change. epilogue up next Friday (and it will be a healthy length) (and it will not be Like That). love yíall x tumblr | instagram Chapter33: Epilogue: Two Months Later Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes ØParkinson è Parkinson è yes, Creature-Induced Injuries,Æ said the harassed-looking Welcome Witch at the reception area of St. Mungoís. ØFirst floor, Hippolyta Morton Ward.Æ She jabbed her quill toward the floor guide, then looked up for the first time since Hermione and Draco had stopped before the desk. The blonde witchís quill froze in mid-air. ØHold on. Are you èÆ ØThank you,Æ said Hermione quickly, tugging Draco toward the double doors. Dracoís eyes glimmered with amusement. ØBored of the hero-worship already?Æ ØDraco,Æ said Hermione in her most dignified tone, ØIíd like to at least try to keep to our schedule. Weíre already late, and itís bound to be packed. Yesîsee?Æ The corridor beyond the double doors was chaos. Visitors swirled among Healers in lime-green robes, the crowd so thick that the portraits on the walls were nearly invisible. It took several long minutes of excuse-mes and trodden-on toes for them to reach the stairs, and by the time they got there, a current of murmurs had started to follow them. Heads turned. Necks craned. Hermione ducked into the stairwell with relief. The stairs were just as busy, mostly with people on their way to the fourth floorîSpell Damageîbut when they slipped out into the Creature-Induced Injuries floor, they found a clearer path. Soon they were entering the Hippolyta Morton Ward, a mostly empty hall of beds partitioned by gauzy, hovering curtains. ØHermione, Malfoy,Æ called Ronís voice from halfway down the ward. ØOver here.Æ They approached the bed in question and found Ron, Harry, and Goyle gathered around Pansyís bedside. Pansy herself was sitting up in bed and looking artfully bored, a copy of Witch Weekly splayed open on her lap. After they'd all exchanged hugs and greetings, Draco and Hermione drew up two more seats at the end of the bed. ØWhat happened, Pansy?Æ Hermione said, trying not to sound too worried. Draco eyed her bandages. ØI thought they were supposed to discharge you on Monday.Æ ØThey were.Æ Pansy aimed a sullen look at the bandaging, too. ØThen a lot of bruising showed up out of nowhere, so theyíre doing more tests. Apparently Iíll be in here for at least another week.Æ ØAh, well,Æ said Ron with deliberate carelessness. ØMore time to learnîwhat was it?Æ He craned his neck to read the cover of Witch Weekly. ØSeven Wand-Free Ways to Put That Special Wizard Under Your Spell.Æ ØItís a worthless list,Æ Pansy said. ØThey donít even mention getting an Azkaban sentence.Æ Everyone around the bed laughed, Ronís ears took on a pleased red tint, and Pansy looked slightly cheered. Hermione snuck another look at Ron, who had dark circles like Pansyís under his eyes. After the battle, Pansy had remained in the Hogwarts Infirmary for two weeks. St. Mungoís had been so full of injured people that anything non-life-threatening had been designated to other, less well-equipped hospitals across the country. And while Naginiís bite hadnít worsened at Hogwarts, neither had Madam Pomfrey had the resources to heal it. Eventually, in mid-June, after experiencing worrisome side effects, sheíd been transferred to St. Mungoís. Throughout it all, Ron had visited nearly every day, even spending some nights at Pansyís bedside. A clutter of both their possessions had accumulated at the bedside table: hand-drawn get-well cards from Pansyís younger brothers, Ronís beaten-down chess set, two imposing bouquets from Theo Nott and Blaise Zabini, a stack of Quidditch Quarterlies that might have belonged to either of themîPansy was a staunch fan of the Falmouth Falcons, to Ronís outrageîand a jar full of opaline dragon scales that Charlie had given to Ron, which Pansy had been crafting into jewellery. They heard the door at the end of the ward open and close. ØOh, blimey,Æ said Ron, startling to his feet. ØThatíll be them.Æ Pansyís expression barely changed, but the muscles of her throat tightened as though she were trying and failing to swallow. ØYour parents?Æ Draco said, low and quick. Pansy gave one curt nod. ØOoh, no,Æ Hermione said, leaping to her feet. ØI wish we hadnít been lateîweíd better give you some privacy.Æ They all knew that Pansy hadnít spoken to her parents since moving into Order headquarters in early March. Even the cards from her brothers had come from post owls rather than the Parkinson family owl. Ron and Goyle stood, too. ØDonít move, you two,Æ Pansy warned, grabbing at Ronís and Goyleís hands, but they dodged. ØRonîGreg!Æ ØItíll be better on your own,Æ said Goyle in his solemn way. Hermione was still getting used to hearing Goyle speak in full sentences. Every word was deliberate, as though requiring his utmost concentration, and there was a way he held eye contact while he spoke that made his words feel heavy. He, too, was still in the process of reconciling with his parents. His father remained on the Fourth Floor, recovering from the last effects of the Fiendfyre. Hermione heard Ron say in a quiet voice as she hurried away, ØWeíre just outside, all right?Æ Hermione stammered out a ØhelloÆ to the Parkinsons as they passed, not meeting their eyes. She didnít think sheíd ever be able to look at Pansyís parents again without thinking of herself and Draco in the Ministry of Magic. In the hall, they dodged a Healer accompanying a patient with an elephantís trunk for a nose. ØI reckon theyíll be a while,Æ said Ron apologetically. ØThatís all right,Æ said Harry, checking his watch. ØI should get to Diagon Alley, anyway. Hermione, Draco, were you two going that way, too?Æ Draco nodded. ØWeíll come back tomorrow, Weasley.Æ He gave the door of the ward a wary look. Øè to witness the aftermath.Æ ØItíll be all right,Æ said Ron bracingly, although he half-sounded as though he were trying to convince himself. ØGo on. And take the back exitîyou get less swarmed that way.Æ They Disapparated in a nearby side street. Soon Hermione, Draco, and Harry were walking up Diagon Alley. It was shaping up to be a warm, steady summer, with days-long spells of sun, and they treated themselves to ice creams from Fortescueís. The shop now had a memorial plaque to Florean Fortescue hung on the wall. They all hesitated before it a moment before continuing on their way. ØI hear they finally found him last week,Æ said Hermione in a small voice. ØFlorean Fortescue, I mean. I read in the Prophet that his è his remains were found on the grounds of the Lestrange House.Æ ØTook them long enough,Æ said Harry. ØHe vanished start of sixth year.Æ ØIím surprised they found anything, the way the Ministryís being run right now,Æ said Draco, casting a dismissive look toward a stand of Daily Prophets. ØShacklebolt canít carry the whole thing on his shoulders.Æ Harry and Draco began to debate the way the Auror Office had reformed, and what Kingsleyís appointment as Acting Minister had done to the Department of Magical Law Enforcement, but Hermione was still thinking about that small silver plaque on the wall with Fortescueís name on it. Her eyes stung. It was odd, the things that reached beneath her skin these days, while others passed over her like a breeze. Inevitably, they were stopped in the middle of Diagon Alley along the way. Tearful witches and wizards shook Harryís hand, and almost always went on to do the same to Hermione and Draco. Especially while accompanying Harry, there was no way that they could be mistaken for anyone other than two of the most prominent members of the Order of the Phoenix: Hermione Granger, former Undesirable No. 2; and Draco Malfoy, one of only two people to betray the Mark and live. ØItís really not fair, you know,Æ Hermione huffed after the fifth or sixth such encounter. ØYouíll see our names in the Prophet again and again, but youíll notice they havenít interviewed Remus or Hagrid or Dobby.Æ She pursed her lips. ØI wonder what could possibly be keeping the Wizarding World from taking their contributions seriously.Æ ØLet me guess,Æ said Harry with a sigh. ØIím serious, Harry! Iíve read the Prophet front to back every day since they fired half their staff and started publishing again. There isnít one mention of house-elvesí, goblinsí, or part-humansí contributions to the Order. Not one!Æ she seethed. ØWrite an editorial,Æ Draco said. ØThe Prophet will bend over backward to be the first publisher of war heroine Hermione Granger.Æ ØNot a bad idea,Æ said Harry. Hermione sighed. ØNo, itís not enough. I tried for years to get people to listen with S.P.E.W. è I really think the only way to make some proper change is to go back to Hogwarts for N.E.W.T.s and make a career of it.Æ Hermione knew that Draco, too, intended to return to Hogwarts for a seventh yearís education, not least because he was interested in Ministry work and the Ministry would need a good long while to reform. Theyíd spoken about it often in the months since the battle, in calm afternoons at the Zabinisí house, where Draco and his mother had been staying while they searched for a new home. Mr. and Mrs. Weasley had also insisted on having Hermione, Draco, and Harry around for breakfasts every other weekend, where she pestered them relentlessly about their future plans. But they were halfway through summer now, and Harry still hadnít made any indication of whether he meant to return to Hogwarts. Hermione and Draco both glanced over at him; he looked pensive. ØWell?Æ said Draco. ØWhat are you going to do, Potter? Write a bestselling memoir?Æ Harry rubbed the back of his neck. ØActually, Iíve been owling Kingsley. The Auror Office have been trying to track down the rest of the Death Eaters. Hundreds of Voldemortís supporters have gone to other countries, or gone into hiding. Kingsleyís asked me if I want to join the effort, and I told him a few days ago that I would.Æ ØHarry!Æ Hermione tried to affix enthusiasm onto her face, but something like dismay was spreading through her. ØThatísîwell, thatís èÆ Draco wasnít feigning excitement. ØRight back into it again, then?Æ He sighed. ØYou arenít tired of putting your neck on the line?Æ ØItís different now,Æ Harry argued. ØWeíre not up against the whole world anymore. And weíve got to finish the job.Æ ØI suppose,Æ Hermione said slowly. ØWe have to undo all the damage that the Death Eaters did with their propaganda, too.Æ Harry nodded. ØBesides, Iíll visit Hogwarts. Luna had agreed to be his assistant over the summer, which suited her, as she could visit her father in St. Mungoís much more easily from Diagon Alley. Both she and Ollivander were waving goodbye to a figure who now ducked through the doorway, beaming. ØHarry!Æ boomed Hagrid, waving with the long, thin, dusty box in his hand. ØHermione! Draco, good to see yeh, good to see yeh.Æ He gave Draco a clap on the shoulder that made him stagger. ØCan we see it, Hagrid?Æ Hermione said eagerly, pointing to the box. Hagrid lifted the lid to reveal a wand whose handle was as thick around as Hermioneís wrist. ØCourse yeh can. Oak, like my first! Eighteen inches, aní a dragon heartstring core.Æ ØItís fantastic,Æ said Harry with a grin. Hagrid sniffed loudly as he slipped the box into the depths of his overcoat. ØNever thought Iíd be allowed ter finish up at Hogwarts è Dumbledore put in a petition after yeh cleared my name in yer second year, Harry, but the Ministry kepí puttiní it off è didní want to admit theyíd blamed the wrong person for openiní the Chamber, see? è But Iím guessiní Kingsley aní Minerva had somethiní ter do with this.Æ Hermione beamed. Minervaís still got åer Transfiguration classes, and sheís keepiní Slughorn on fer Potions, as well. ØHave you been to the Apothecary?Æ Hagrid was bouncing on the tips of his toes, making a nearby bench shudder on the cobblestones. ØItís going to be strange, though. What about if one of us feels overwhelmed, we just è just work a certain word into conversation?Æ Draco nodded. ØArithmancy?Æ ØPerfect.Æ Hermione swallowed. ØButîno, I think weíll be all right. Weíve already been to the Burrow half a dozen times, after all.Æ Still, as Weasleysí Wizard Wheezes came into view ahead, Hermione felt her pulse quicken. The last time sheíd been inside that building, they had Disapparated into Fredís and Georgeís office from Hogsmeade on the day of Dumbledoreís funeral. She pictured the flat above the shop stuffed with Weasley photographs and too-fine furnishings. The twinsí own funeral had been small and private, taking place in the countryside near the Burrow where the Weasleys had so often flown on brooms in childhood, skimming over ponds and grasses. The weeks after the battle had been so filled with mourning that it had felt best to keep the twinsí memorial close to the family and the Order. Still, hundreds of letters and packages of condolence had arrived at the Burrow over the course of weeks. Some had come from Hogwarts students and teachers, others from fans of the store, still others from Order allies who had listened to The Daily Potter, drawing strength and consolation from Fredís and Georgeís broadcasts. Hermioneís throat tightened as they stopped in front of Weasleysí Wizard Wheezes. The display was as violent in colour as ever, orange clashing brilliantly with green and purple. GRAND RE-OPENING! read a sign whose letters popped and crackled. ØI hope theyíre both in,Æ Hermione said as they stepped into the shop, which was even more crowded than St. Mungoís had been. ØThey are,Æ said Draco, pointing toward the back. Behind the register, Ginny Weasley spotted them and waved, grinning. Beside her, scribbling on a hovering clipboard with one hand and pressing up his horn-rimmed glasses with the other, was Percy. Ron had told Hermione how all this had come to happen. After the funeral, there had been a family-wide argument about what to do with the shop, which the twins had left to the family. Arthur had suggested they offer the premises and the twinsí ideas to Zonkoís, which Fred and George had admired for so much of their lives. Molly had thought the store should be closed and sold, with its proceeds donated instead to a memorial to the twins. But it was Percy who had insisted with startling fervour that Weasleysí Wizard Wheezes must stay open. Eventually he had shouted at his parents, ØThey left Hogwarts for this! They risked being killed to keep it open! TheyîÆ Mrs. Weasley had dissolved into tears, ending the argument. But Ginny and Ron had sided with Percy, and eventually the rest of the family had come to agree, too. The question was how to run the storeîa question that was answered when the family had finally brought themselves to look through the twinsí tent and flat. There, they had discovered scores of notebooks filled with minuscule writing, jam-packed with yearsí if not decadesí worth of plans. Percy had gathered up the books and, with the obsessive meticulousness that had given him his twelve O.W.L.s, set about bringing his brothersí plans into reality. ØOf course, thereís some things he canít do on his own,Æ Ginny had remarked to Hermione at one of the Weasleysí breakfasts. ØHe can actually be quite funny in writing, Percy, but heís got a less-than-zero sense of mayhem. Iím trying to help him on that front.Æ Now Hermione and Draco shouldered through to the desk, and Ginny and Percy stepped over to the side to greet them. ØHermione,Æ said Percy, finishing a sentence on his clipboard. ØDraco. Lovely to see you both.Æ ØYou too,Æ said Hermione, speaking loudly to be heard over the shopís vibrant buzz. ØHow is the reopening going?Æ ØFrankly, itís very stressful.Æ Percy dotted his parchment with such force that the quill nib bent. He sighed and flicked his wand; the hovering clipboard landed on the countertop. ØItís fine, Hermione,Æ Ginny said, smiling and shaking back her long red hair. ØTrue, we nearly drowned those first couple of daysîthe Galleons are flooding in. But things have been a lot better since we hired Lee on as manager.Æ She nodded to the corner, where Lee Jordan was demonstrating the usage of a Drinkable Hover Charm to a giddy crowd. The labels on the iridescent bottles read Add some lift to any party! ØThatís all very well for now,Æ Percy said irritably, Øbut once you go back to Hogwarts, GinnyîÆ Øîweíll have Lee handle most of it and hire someone else to fill in,Æ said Ginny patiently. ØOr, you know, I could justîÆ ØNo,Æ Percy said. ØYouíre going back for your seventh year and thatís final.Æ ØMerlin. You sound like Mum. Harry didnít go back for his seventh year.Æ ØTo be fair,Æ Draco pointed out, ØPotter was busy being the figurehead of a revolution.Æ Ginny shrugged. ØSuppose Iíll have to find another revolution to spearhead, then.Æ Hermione laughed. ØWeíll be back at Hogwarts, too, Ginny. And weíve just heard Remus is coming back to teach Defence.Æ Ginny perked up. ØWell, thatís one good reason to go back.Æ ØAnd,Æ said Percy, Øyouíll need N.E.W.T.-level magic if weíre going to develop the rest of these ideas, Ginny.Æ He glanced to the stack of notebooks teetering in the corners. ØSome of it is far more advanced than Iíd haveîhave anticipated.Æ A note of pain had entered his voice. He busied himself with his clipboard again. Ginny looked from Draco to Hermione and smiled. ØYeah,Æ she said more quietly. ØItís going fine. Mostly itís how I thought itíd be.Æ She didnít elaborate, but Hermione knew what she meant. They emerged blinking into the sunlight, Hermioneís eyes stinging again. She glanced up at Draco and could see the flutters of disturbance in the small movements of his mouth, in the way his gaze moved over the shopfront and away. She felt a warm squeeze in her stomach as he met her eyes. ØGood?Æ Draco said quietly. ØYes, Iím fine. Are you?Æ He lifted his shoulders. ØItís just è do you ever è?Æ Hermione swallowed as they began to walk. ØNever mind. Itís silly.Æ ØSomehow, I doubt that.Æ She managed a weak smile. ØDo I ever what?Æ Draco asked. ØI donít know. Sometimes I wake up and I think Iím still there in headquarters. And I donít want that to be true, but itís almost èÆ As they walked up Diagon Alley, Hermione struggled to articulate it. In those confused dawn hours, thinking she was in the Potter Cottage, Fred and George clanking about in the attic and the Cup of Hufflepuff still out of reach è Hermione didnít miss it. It would have been absurd to miss it. But there had been something to the idea of everyone emerging unscathed somehowîsomething to the impossible hopes sheíd allowed herself to cultivate in that pressurised incubator. ØI know,Æ Draco said. ØIf it were true, there are things we could try again.Æ Hermione knew that he was speaking about his father. They continued in pensive silence for a moment before she squeezed his hand and said, ØWell, that was the last thing we had to do today. What if we circled around to Flourish and Blotts?Æ ØThere are a few more things, actually.Æ Draco was composed again. ØReally? I could have swornîÆ ØYour perfect memory isnít failing you, Granger, donít worry.Æ He slipped his arm around her shoulders. ØJust follow me.Æ # Beside Quality Quidditch Supplies stood a pale blue door. Draco rested his wandtip against the silver knocker for several seconds, and the door melted into nothingness. ØOh!Æ Hermione exclaimed, whirling to face him. ØIs thisîhave you finallyîÆ ØI have no idea what you could possibly mean.Æ Draco led her over the threshold into a foyer filled with light. A stained-glass ceiling cast droplets of coloured light onto the leaves of hanging plants, as well as onto the mosaic floor. Directly opposite stood a wall with a row of seven doors. Draco approached the seventh, drew a heavy brass key from his pocket, and placed it into the keyhole. ØWe moved in last night,Æ said Draco. ØDecided to get out from under Zabiniís feet.Æ He opened the door onto the flat. Hermione let out a little laugh. ØWhat?Æ he said. ØNothing,Æ she said, stepping inside. ØBut itís no surprise, is it?Æ Draco supposed she had a point. The apartment was traditional. This building had been constructed sometime in the late 1700s and enchanted in the 1890s. Dark wooden floors gleamed with age and polish. Heavily curtained French windows opened out onto small balconettes that overlooked Diagon Alley from their seventh-storey vantage. The rugs were imports, the furniture domestic. His mother had commented favourably on each of these aspects as theyíd walked through. ØI donít understand.Æ Hermione ran one hand over the sofaís tufted back. ØI thought you wanted to live out in the countryside.Æ ØI did. My mother thinks itís best to live closer to the heart of things, for now.Æ ØWhy?Æ Draco straightened one of the portraits by a millimetre. Its inhabitant, a black stallion, reared and snorted at him for daring to touch the gilt frame. ØShe thinks we should take an active role in polishing the Malfoy reputation. Which, in practice, means donating thousands of Galleons to everything that moves.Æ ØAh.Æ Hermione glanced down the long hallway. ØWhere is she?Æ ØAt the Manor site. Apparently theyíre planning to reconstruct it as a historical monument. Walk through and see the ballroom where they hosted the International Confederation of Wizards in 1824, please mind the guard-ropes, that sort of thing.Æ ØDo you think youíll go back?Æ Draco hesitated. He still felt a twist in his chest when he thought of the blackened ruin. ØMaybe after school,Æ Draco saidîbut Hermione was looking sombre now. This wasnít what heíd meant to do by showing her the apartment; heíd hoped she would be excited. ØHere,Æ he said, beckoning her toward the door. ØThereís one more thing we need to do.Æ # Half an hour later, they were seated at a table for two at Erialo. The restaurant lay near the top of Diagon Alley, and its space-expansion charms had required more than fifty separate permits from the Office of Spatial Stability. What appeared from the outside to be a third-floor studio flat in a brick apartment building became, upon entrance, a four-tiered wooden terrace overlooking a glittering lake. The terraceís top level dripped with silvery Octavines whose flowers occasionally rang against each other in the breeze like chimes. Draco behaved in the way his mother and father had taught him. He took Hermioneís bag to hand to the wait staff. He withdrew her chair from the table before she sat. Throughout these procedures there was an airlessness between them that evoked gentle irony, laughter on its way. As Hermione thanked him and perched on the edge of her chair, he felt they were playing pretend somehow. Two monthsí freedom and there were still moments like these: unreality folded into the new reality. There were people looking their way, as heíd once imagined they might, but not because he was a Malfoy and Hermione was Muggle-born, and not because they were at imminent risk of attack. Pleasure rolled through Draco as he sat down, luxuriating in every ounce of sensation: the coolness of the charmed air, the scents of bread and olive oil, the evening light on Hermioneís cheeks and hair. ØNot the same table where you sat with Flint, I hope,Æ said Draco. Hermione made a face. ØNo, that was on the first level of the terrace. He kept complaining about it, too.Æ She glanced around. The terraceís top level held only five tables. Her brow furrowed. ØOh, Draco, please tell me you didnít bribe the restaurant to get us one of these seats. There are so many better uses for your money thanîÆ ØYou think I needed to bribe anyone?Æ Draco said. ØYouíre Hermione Granger. Iím Draco Malfoy. They offered us any table at any time.Æ ØOh. Well. In that case.Æ She picked up the menu with a somewhat sly smile. ØIt is nice sometimes, I suppose, the recognition.Æ ØWe should capitalise on it at Hogwarts,Æ said Draco. ØIím so sorry, Professor Vector, I didnít finish that calculation chart about sequential casting degradation. è But we saved the Wizarding World three months ago, which Iím sure youíll agree deserves at least an åEí.Æ Hermione laughed. ØSequential casting degradation is rubbish, anyway.Æ ØFinished reading the textbook, then?Æ ØYes, and I honestly donít know why they devote half a chapter to degradation. I thought the principles seemed shaky, so I picked up a supplementary journal, and apparently, once you correct for every variable in the casterís technique, thereís hardly any evidence that spell power actually degrades with repetition.Æ Hermione sipped from her water. ØI have a hypothesis that Lamnick and Velter were just self-conscious about their lack of casting endurance. And now generations of arithmancers have to deal with their projected insecurities.Æ Draco leaned back in his seat, basking in her academic affront. ØMaybe thatís the editorial that needs publishing in the Prophet. An expos on the shoddy methods of two dead arithmancers from the 1950s.Æ ØIíll start writing it now,Æ said Hermione, smiling. They spoke about classes a while longerîtheir schedule for N.E.W.T.s promised to be similarîand about who they suspected would be chosen as Prefects among the fifth- and sixth-years. They spoke about Remus and Tonks moving to Hogsmeade; the young parents had invited Draco to visit their new apartment above the Hogís Head to see little Teddy. The evening deepened. Over flutes of shimmering wine that slowly shifted colour from lavender to burgundy, they spoke about Hermioneís parents, who had readjusted well to the reappearance of their memories, for the most part. Initially furious with Hermione for having meddled with their minds, they had softened when sheíd explained the circumstances of the previous year. Now they journeyed to St. Mungoís once every few weeks for therapy treatments, as sometimes, in dreams, they still thought of themselves not as Dominic and Celia Granger but as Wendell and Monica Wilkins. ØYouíll meet them at Harryís birthday party,Æ said Hermione. ØIíve told them everything about you, of course.Æ ØEverything?Æ ØMost things.Æ ØSo, thereís nothing left for me to say.Æ ØYou can stand there and look mysterious.Æ Draco looked out at the crystalline waters of the lake. ØLike this?Æ ØYes, perfect.Æ Abruptly, as he looked into the shifting light on the water, Draco remembered his father telling him how heíd proposed to his mother at Erialo. He wondered if it had happened at this very table. The proposal had been quiet: a soft question and a ring brought out from the depths of Luciusís robes. No shriek or exclamation from Narcissa, just the joining of hands and the knowledge that they wanted to braid their two lives into one. It was the first memory of his father since Luciusís death that did not pain Draco. He cupped it inside himself and wondered if he could replicate this later, this painlessness. Draco came back to himself at the sound of whispers. The table beside them had noticed who, exactly, their neighbours were. A witch in bottle-green dress robes was whispering excitedly behind her hand to several companions who were staring openly at Draco and Hermione. ØDo you want to move somewhere more private?Æ Hermione said quietly. Draco shrugged. ØLet them watch.Æ He luxuriated in taking her hand then and there, and in the way she smiled, gripping a bit too tightly as always. It wasnít until the voices at the next table broke off that Draco glanced down to see that his robe had caught on the table, exposing a slice of his forearm. The Dark Mark stood out upon his skin. Draco studied his arm, as he knew the others were. He thought about Dumbledoreís copy of himself lying atop the Astronomy Tower, the Mark glaring out from the unmoving body. He thought about that boy dwindling at his back, fading forever into the night. These days Draco awoke every morning and passed his wand over the Mark as Remus had instructed. In the months since he had begun the exercise, there had been a definite if slight improvement. Jet-black had transformed to the soft darkness of a thunderhead. Every day the skull and snake would grow fainter. Somedayîmaybe in five years, maybe in fiftyîit would appear as though the mark had never been there. At Dracoís back, the whispers had restarted, tense with new doubt, but he made no move to cover his arm. There was no need. He lifted his eyes to Hermione and saw her regard. The End Chapter End Notes good lord, I can't believe it's done... thank you so, so much to everyone who commented and/or left kudos on this fic - i owe finishing it to you. it has been so wonderful to have such supportive, engaged, and perceptive readers boosting my morale along the way to the end. sending love, sw Works inspired by this one [Restricted Work] by Beatificbean, ETL_Echo_Audiobooks Please drop by the Archive and comment to let the creator know if you enjoyed their work!
